assessments.title: The Music in Words: What Makes a Poem a Poem?

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Secret Music Inside a Poem

Words That Look Different on the Page

Clap your hands twice. Now stomp your foot. Clap, clap, stomp. Clap, clap, stomp. You just made a pattern with sound — and that is exactly what a poem does with words.

A poem is not written in big, blocky paragraphs like a story. And here is something that might surprise you: a line of poetry does not have to be a full sentence. One sentence can stretch across two or three lines, and a line can stop right in the middle of a thought. The poet decides where each line breaks, almost like choosing where to take a breath.

A group of lines that belong together is called a stanza — a section of a poem, the way a paragraph is a section of a story. You can spot a new stanza when you see a blank space between groups of lines. Some poems have two stanzas. Others have ten.
stimulus.content_text:
The Secret Music Inside a PoemWords That Look Different on the Page
Clap your hands twice. Now stomp your foot. Clap, clap, stomp. Clap, clap, stomp. You just made a pattern with sound — and that is exactly what a poem does with words.
A poem is not written in big, blocky paragraphs like a story. And here is something that might surprise you: a line of poetry does not have to be a full sentence. One sentence can stretch across two or three lines, and a line can stop right in the middle of a thought. The poet decides where each line breaks, almost like choosing where to take a breath.
A group of lines that belong together is called a stanza — a section of a poem, the way a paragraph is a section of a story. You can spot a new stanza when you see a blank space between groups of lines. Some poems have two stanzas. Others have ten.

items.prompt: According to the passage, how is a poem different from a story?

choices.text: A poem is written in big, blocky paragraphs
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage says a poem is NOT written in big, blocky paragraphs — that describes a story, not a poem.

choices.text: A poem has stanzas instead of paragraphs
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage explains that poems use stanzas instead of paragraphs, and even compares them directly: 'a stanza is a section of a poem, the way a paragraph is a section of a story.'

choices.text: A poem always has ten stanzas in it
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage says some poems have two stanzas and others have ten, meaning ten is just an example, not something every poem must have.

choices.text: A poem must rhyme at the end of each line
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage never mentions rhyming — it only talks about how poems make patterns with words and how lines are arranged on the page.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Rhyme: Cracking the Code

Many poems use rhyme — words that share the same ending sound, like "cat" and "hat" or "moon" and "June." When those rhyming words follow a pattern, poets call it a rhyme scheme, which is like a map that shows which lines match up in sound. We mark the pattern with letters. If line one rhymes with line two, we write AA. If line one rhymes with line three, and line two rhymes with line four, we write ABAB. Try it yourself with any short poem, and you will feel like you are cracking a secret code.

You might think every real poem has to rhyme. Actually, some poems skip rhyme on purpose. These are called free verse poems — poems with no set rhyme pattern at all. They are still real poems. They just use other tools, like vivid pictures or surprising word choices, to grab your attention.
stimulus.content_text:
Rhyme: Cracking the Code
Many poems use rhyme — words that share the same ending sound, like "cat" and "hat" or "moon" and "June." When those rhyming words follow a pattern, poets call it a rhyme scheme, which is like a map that shows which lines match up in sound. We mark the pattern with letters. If line one rhymes with line two, we write AA. If line one rhymes with line three, and line two rhymes with line four, we write ABAB. Try it yourself with any short poem, and you will feel like you are cracking a secret code.
You might think every real poem has to rhyme. Actually, some poems skip rhyme on purpose. These are called free verse poems — poems with no set rhyme pattern at all. They are still real poems. They just use other tools, like vivid pictures or surprising word choices, to grab your attention.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, why do free verse poems use tools like vivid pictures or surprising word choices?

choices.text: To grab the reader's attention without using rhyme
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage says free verse poems use vivid pictures and surprising word choices to 'grab your attention' since they don't have rhyme to do that job.

choices.text: To help the reader crack a secret code in the text
choices.feedback: B is wrong because 'cracking a secret code' is how the passage describes figuring out rhyme schemes, which is the opposite of free verse.

choices.text: To show the reader which lines match up in sound
choices.feedback: C is wrong because showing which lines match up in sound describes a rhyme scheme, not the tools used in free verse poems.

choices.text: To teach the reader how to write a free verse poem
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the passage explains what free verse poems use these tools for, but it never says the goal is to teach you how to write one.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
A Poet Who Made Readers Laugh

A poet named Ogden Nash loved to play with rhyme in wild, silly ways. He squeezed and stretched words — even invented new ones — just to make them rhyme. His humorous poems, meaning poems written to be funny, made readers laugh out loud. Nash proved that poetry does not always have to be serious. Here is one of his most famous tiny poems:

If called by a panther,
Don't anther.
stimulus.content_text:
A Poet Who Made Readers Laugh
A poet named Ogden Nash loved to play with rhyme in wild, silly ways. He squeezed and stretched words — even invented new ones — just to make them rhyme. His humorous poems, meaning poems written to be funny, made readers laugh out loud. Nash proved that poetry does not always have to be serious. Here is one of his most famous tiny poems:

If called by a panther,
Don't anther.

items.prompt: According to Ogden Nash's tiny poem in the text, what should a person do after being called by a panther?

choices.text: They should not answer.
choices.feedback: A is correct because 'Don't anther' is Nash's funny way of writing 'Don't answer,' so the poem is telling us not to answer a panther's call.

choices.text: They should run away.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the poem never says anything about running away — that idea is not in the poem at all.

choices.text: They should invent words.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because while the passage tells us Nash invented words, the poem itself is not giving us instructions to invent words.

choices.text: They should laugh loudly.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because laughing loudly is something Nash's poems make readers do, but it is not what the tiny poem tells a person to do.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
A Poet Who Painted with Words

Christina Rossetti lived in the 1800s and wrote about nature, the seasons, and faith. She described something invisible by showing what it does.

Who has seen the wind?
Neither I nor you:
But when the leaves hang trembling,
The wind is passing through.

Who has seen the wind?
Neither you nor I:
But when the trees bow down their heads,
The wind is passing by.

Her quiet words painted a picture you could feel on your skin.
stimulus.content_text:
A Poet Who Painted with Words
Christina Rossetti lived in the 1800s and wrote about nature, the seasons, and faith. She described something invisible by showing what it does.

Who has seen the wind?
Neither I nor you:
But when the leaves hang trembling,
The wind is passing through.

Who has seen the wind?
Neither you nor I:
But when the trees bow down their heads,
The wind is passing by.

Her quiet words painted a picture you could feel on your skin.

items.prompt: In the poem, the phrase "the trees bow down their heads" compares the trees to what?

choices.text: people bending low
choices.feedback: This is correct because 'bow down their heads' describes what people do when they bend low, so the poet is comparing the trees to humans.

choices.text: animals lying still
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because animals lying still are not bowing or bending, which is the action described in the poem.

choices.text: flowers opening wide
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because flowers opening wide is the opposite of bowing down — it's a spreading-out movement, not a bending-low one.

choices.text: clouds moving fast
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because clouds moving fast have nothing to do with bowing or bending down like the trees in the poem.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Rhythm Is Not the Same as Rhyme

Here is where many readers get tripped up. Rhythm and rhyme sound like they could mean the same thing, but they are very different. Rhyme is about matching sounds at the ends of words. Rhythm is the beat of a poem — the pattern of strong and soft sounds, like a drum keeping time. Say "PUMP-kin" out loud. Strong beat, then soft. Poets arrange words so those beats form a pattern, and your voice almost sings along without trying.

Tap out the rhythm on your desk and hunt for the rhyme scheme. A poem is not just words sitting quietly on paper. It is music waiting for your voice to bring it to life.
stimulus.content_text:
Rhythm Is Not the Same as Rhyme
Here is where many readers get tripped up. Rhythm and rhyme sound like they could mean the same thing, but they are very different. Rhyme is about matching sounds at the ends of words. Rhythm is the beat of a poem — the pattern of strong and soft sounds, like a drum keeping time. Say "PUMP-kin" out loud. Strong beat, then soft. Poets arrange words so those beats form a pattern, and your voice almost sings along without trying.
Tap out the rhythm on your desk and hunt for the rhyme scheme. A poem is not just words sitting quietly on paper. It is music waiting for your voice to bring it to life.

items.prompt: According to the text, what is rhythm in a poem?

choices.text: the matching of sounds at the ends of words
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says matching sounds at the ends of words describes RHYME, not rhythm — the passage even points out that people mix these two up!

choices.text: the pattern of strong and soft sounds
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly says 'Rhythm is the beat of a poem — the pattern of strong and soft sounds,' which is exactly what answer B says.

choices.text: the words that a poet writes on paper
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never says rhythm means the words a poet writes — in fact, it says a poem is 'music waiting for your voice to bring it to life,' not just words sitting on paper.

choices.text: the way a poem looks on the page
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage does not describe rhythm as having anything to do with how a poem looks on the page.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: Based on the article, how are Ogden Nash's poems different from Christina Rossetti's poems?

choices.text: Nash wrote funny poems with invented words, but Rossetti wrote quiet poems about nature.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the article clearly says Nash wrote funny, humorous poems with invented words, while Rossetti wrote quiet poems about nature like wind and trees.

choices.text: Nash wrote poems without any rhyming words, but Rossetti wrote poems with secret codes.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because Nash actually used lots of rhyme — that was his whole style — and neither poet wrote poems described as 'secret codes.'

choices.text: Nash wrote long poems in big paragraphs, but Rossetti wrote short poems in small stanzas.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the article never says Nash wrote long poems in paragraphs — his famous example poem is only two lines long!

choices.text: Nash wrote poems about invisible things, but Rossetti wrote poems about wild animals.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because it's Rossetti, not Nash, who wrote about something invisible like the wind, and Nash wrote silly poems, not poems about wild animals.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: According to the article, why do poets use tools like rhyme schemes and rhythm?

choices.text: To teach the reader true facts about wild animals.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the article never says poets use rhyme or rhythm to teach facts about animals — that idea is not in the passage at all.

choices.text: To build sound patterns that feel like music.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the article directly compares poetry's rhyme and rhythm to music and a drumbeat, saying a poem is 'music waiting for your voice to bring it to life.'

choices.text: To turn every single line into a complete sentence.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the article says the opposite — a line of poetry does NOT have to be a full sentence.

choices.text: To describe invisible things like the blowing wind.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because describing invisible things like wind is a technique used by one poet, Christina Rossetti, and is not the reason poets use rhyme schemes and rhythm.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: Based on the headings, which list shows the correct order of topics in the article?

choices.text: How lines look, how words rhyme, how beats sound
choices.feedback: A is correct because the headings go in this exact order: lines on the page first, then rhyme in the middle, then rhythm and beats at the end.

choices.text: How beats sound, how lines look, how words rhyme
choices.feedback: B is wrong because beats (rhythm) are explained last in the article, not first.

choices.text: How words rhyme, how beats sound, how lines look
choices.feedback: C is wrong because rhyme is not the first topic — the article starts by talking about how lines look on the page.

choices.text: How lines look, how beats sound, how words rhyme
choices.feedback: D is wrong because it puts beats before rhyme, but the article explains rhyme first and saves the section about beats and rhythm for the very end.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: Which sentence best states the main idea of the article?

choices.text: Poems use special patterns of sound and words to share ideas.
choices.feedback: A is correct because it covers the whole article — poems use many sound and word patterns, like rhyme, rhythm, and stanzas, all to share ideas.

choices.text: Poems use matching ending sounds to create a code for readers.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because it only talks about rhyme, which is just one tool poems use, not the main idea of the whole article.

choices.text: Poems are divided into stanzas so the poet knows where to breathe.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because stanzas and breathing are only a small detail mentioned near the beginning, not what the whole article is about.

choices.text: Poems are written to be humorous so that they make readers laugh.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because only one section talks about humorous poems, and the article covers many other kinds of poems too.

syllabus_metadata.title: The Music in Words: What Makes a Poem a Poem?

assessments.title: Painting Pictures with Words: Simile and Metaphor

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Secret Comparisons Hiding in Poems

A Blanket Made of Snow

A poet might write, "The snow was like a blanket covering the earth." You can almost feel the warmth of that blanket.

That comparison is called a simile — a comparison between two different things using the word "like" or "as." Think of a simile as a bridge. "Her smile was as bright as the sun" is a simile.
stimulus.content_text:
The Secret Comparisons Hiding in PoemsA Blanket Made of Snow
A poet might write, "The snow was like a blanket covering the earth." You can almost feel the warmth of that blanket.
That comparison is called a simile — a comparison between two different things using the word "like" or "as." Think of a simile as a bridge. "Her smile was as bright as the sun" is a simile.

items.prompt: According to the text, what makes a simile different from other kinds of comparisons?

choices.text: It compares two things that are exactly the same
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says a simile compares two *different* things, not things that are exactly the same.

choices.text: It uses the word "like" or "as" to compare
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage clearly states that a simile is a comparison that uses the word 'like' or 'as,' and both examples in the text show this.

choices.text: It only describes things found in nature
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because similes can compare all kinds of things — like a smile and the sun — not just things found in nature.

choices.text: It tells a story about two different people
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because a simile is a special kind of comparison using 'like' or 'as,' not a story about two different people.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
When "Like" and "As" Disappear

A metaphor works like a simile's bold cousin. Instead of saying one thing is like another, a metaphor says one thing is another. A simile says, "The snow was like a blanket." A metaphor says, "The snow was a blanket on the earth." Same idea, but the metaphor speaks with more force. It asks you to see the snow as a blanket, not just similar to one.

When someone says "the world is a stage," they do not mean the planet is built from wood and curtains. They mean people act out roles in life, the way actors perform in a play. Similes and metaphors are types of figurative language — words that do not mean exactly what they say but paint a picture in your mind instead.
stimulus.content_text:
When "Like" and "As" Disappear
A metaphor works like a simile's bold cousin. Instead of saying one thing is like another, a metaphor says one thing is another. A simile says, "The snow was like a blanket." A metaphor says, "The snow was a blanket on the earth." Same idea, but the metaphor speaks with more force. It asks you to see the snow as a blanket, not just similar to one.
When someone says "the world is a stage," they do not mean the planet is built from wood and curtains. They mean people act out roles in life, the way actors perform in a play. Similes and metaphors are types of figurative language — words that do not mean exactly what they say but paint a picture in your mind instead.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, why is the phrase "the world is a stage" a type of figurative language?

choices.text: Because it describes a planet made out of real wood and curtains.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because the passage says the phrase does NOT mean the planet is made of wood and curtains — that's the literal meaning we are supposed to look past.

choices.text: Because it paints a picture instead of meaning exactly what it says.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage says figurative language paints a picture instead of meaning exactly what it says, which is exactly what 'the world is a stage' does.

choices.text: Because it uses the words like and as to compare two different things.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because 'the world is a stage' is a metaphor, and metaphors do not use 'like' or 'as' — only similes do.

choices.text: Because it explains how actors perform their roles in a stage play.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because explaining how actors perform is just used to help us understand the phrase's meaning, not to explain why it counts as figurative language.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Emily Dickinson's Singing Bird

Emily Dickinson was a poet who lived in the 1800s. She spent most of her life in one house in Massachusetts, but her imagination traveled everywhere. She wrote hundreds of poems, and she loved metaphors. Dickinson used them to describe big feelings that are hard to see or touch. She compared hope to a bird that perches — sits and grips with its feet, the way a robin rests on a branch — in the soul and never stops singing. She described death as a kind gentleman who took her on a carriage ride, a slow journey in a horse-drawn cart. Her metaphors turned invisible feelings into pictures anyone could see.

"Hope" is the thing with feathers —
That perches in the soul —
And sings the tune without the words —
And never stops — at all —

And sweetest — in the Gale — is heard —
And sore must be the storm —
That could abash the little Bird
That kept so many warm —

I've heard it in the chillest land —
And on the strangest Sea —
Yet — never — in Extremity,
It asked a crumb — of me.
stimulus.content_text:
Emily Dickinson's Singing Bird
Emily Dickinson was a poet who lived in the 1800s. She spent most of her life in one house in Massachusetts, but her imagination traveled everywhere. She wrote hundreds of poems, and she loved metaphors. Dickinson used them to describe big feelings that are hard to see or touch. She compared hope to a bird that perches — sits and grips with its feet, the way a robin rests on a branch — in the soul and never stops singing. She described death as a kind gentleman who took her on a carriage ride, a slow journey in a horse-drawn cart. Her metaphors turned invisible feelings into pictures anyone could see.


"Hope" is the thing with feathers —
That perches in the soul —
And sings the tune without the words —
And never stops — at all —

And sweetest — in the Gale — is heard —
And sore must be the storm —
That could abash the little Bird
That kept so many warm —

I've heard it in the chillest land —
And on the strangest Sea —
Yet — never — in Extremity,
It asked a crumb — of me.

items.prompt: According to the poem, what does the bird do right after it perches in the soul?

choices.text: It asks for a little crumb.
choices.feedback: Asking for a crumb comes at the very end of the poem, not right after the bird perches — and the poem actually says the bird never asks for a crumb!

choices.text: It sings a tune without words.
choices.feedback: This is correct! The poem says the bird 'perches in the soul' and then right away 'sings the tune without the words,' so singing is what happens next.

choices.text: It flies to a faraway sea.
choices.feedback: The poem mentions a strange sea, but the bird never flies there — the speaker just says they've heard hope even in faraway places.

choices.text: It hides from the winter storm.
choices.feedback: The poem says the bird's song is actually sweetest during a storm, so it doesn't hide — it keeps on singing!

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Robert Frost's Fork in the Road

Robert Frost also filled his poems with figurative language. His famous poem "The Road Not Taken" describes a traveler walking through a yellow wood. The traveler reaches a fork where the path splits in two. The fork in the road is a metaphor for the choices you face in life. Every choice sends you in a new direction. Frost did not need to say, "Life is full of hard decisions." He showed it instead, with dirt paths and autumn leaves. Frost wrote another short poem called "The Pasture" that also invites you into a real place.

I'm going out to clean the pasture spring;
I'll only stop to rake the leaves away
(And wait to watch the water clear, I may):
I sha'n't be gone long. — You come too.

I'm going out to fetch the little calf
That's standing by the mother. It's so young,
It totters when she licks it with her tongue.
I sha'n't be gone long. — You come too.
stimulus.content_text:
Robert Frost's Fork in the Road
Robert Frost also filled his poems with figurative language. His famous poem "The Road Not Taken" describes a traveler walking through a yellow wood. The traveler reaches a fork where the path splits in two. The fork in the road is a metaphor for the choices you face in life. Every choice sends you in a new direction. Frost did not need to say, "Life is full of hard decisions." He showed it instead, with dirt paths and autumn leaves. Frost wrote another short poem called "The Pasture" that also invites you into a real place.


I'm going out to clean the pasture spring;
I'll only stop to rake the leaves away
(And wait to watch the water clear, I may):
I sha'n't be gone long. — You come too.

I'm going out to fetch the little calf
That's standing by the mother. It's so young,
It totters when she licks it with her tongue.
I sha'n't be gone long. — You come too.

items.prompt: According to the passage, how is a fork in the road like a choice you face in life?

choices.text: Both send a person in a new direction.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage directly says every choice sends you in a new direction, just like a fork in the road sends a traveler in a new direction.

choices.text: Both are found inside of a yellow wood.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the yellow wood is only part of the poem's setting — it has nothing to do with how a fork is like a life choice.

choices.text: Both are made of dirt and autumn leaves.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because dirt and autumn leaves describe the path in the poem, not what a fork in the road and a life choice have in common.

choices.text: Both are very hard for a person to see.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the passage never mentions that forks in the road or life choices are hard to see.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Reading with New Eyes

If you spot a line that says one thing is something completely different, you have found a metaphor.
stimulus.content_text:
Reading with New Eyes
If you spot a line that says one thing is something completely different, you have found a metaphor.

items.prompt: According to the section "Reading with New Eyes," what is a metaphor?

choices.text: A line that says one thing is something completely different.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage says a metaphor is 'a line that says one thing is something completely different,' which matches this answer word for word.

choices.text: A line that says one thing looks exactly like something else.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because saying something 'looks like' something else describes a simile, not a metaphor, and the passage never uses the words 'looks like.'

choices.text: A line that says a word means exactly what it normally says.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because a metaphor gives words a new, creative meaning — it does not use words in their normal, everyday way.

choices.text: A line that says a picture shows something that is very real.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the passage never mentions pictures or anything being 'very real' when explaining what a metaphor is.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: Based on the passage, how are Emily Dickinson's metaphors different from Robert Frost's metaphors?

choices.text: Dickinson describes invisible feelings, while Frost describes choices using real places.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage says Dickinson used metaphors for invisible feelings like hope, while Frost used real places like a forked road to show the choices in life.

choices.text: Dickinson uses the words "like" or "as," while Frost says that one thing is another.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because using 'like' or 'as' describes a simile, not a metaphor, and both Dickinson and Frost use metaphors in the examples given.

choices.text: Dickinson writes about animals in the woods, while Frost writes about gentlemen in carts.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because it swaps the poets — Dickinson is the one who writes about a bird and a carriage ride, not Frost.

choices.text: Dickinson paints pictures in your mind, while Frost means exactly what his words say.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage explains that Frost's figurative language, just like Dickinson's, paints pictures in your mind and does not mean exactly what the words say.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: Based on the passage, why do poets choose to use metaphors instead of just saying exactly what they mean?

choices.text: Because they want to teach readers true facts about science and history.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage never says poets use metaphors to teach facts about science or history — metaphors are about expressing feelings and ideas through pictures in your mind.

choices.text: Because they want to turn hard-to-see ideas into pictures in your mind.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage says Emily Dickinson used metaphors to turn 'invisible feelings into pictures anyone could see,' which means making hard-to-see ideas easier to understand.

choices.text: Because they want to use the words "like" and "as" to compare things.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because using the words 'like' and 'as' to compare things describes a simile, not a metaphor — metaphors leave those words out.

choices.text: Because they want to hide secret messages that are impossible to figure out.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage explains that metaphors actually help readers understand ideas more clearly, not hide secret messages that can't be figured out.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: Based on the article's headings, in what sequence does the author organize the ideas to teach you about figurative language?

choices.text: First sharing poems, next explaining metaphors, and last explaining similes.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the author explains similes before metaphors, not the other way around, and the poems come last, not first.

choices.text: First explaining similes, next explaining metaphors, and last sharing poems.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the headings show the author starts with similes, moves to metaphors, and then shares poems by Dickinson and Frost.

choices.text: First explaining metaphors, next sharing poems, and last explaining similes.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the author explains similes first, not metaphors, and similes are not saved for last.

choices.text: First sharing poems, next explaining similes, and last explaining metaphors.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the poems appear near the end of the article, not at the beginning.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: Based on the whole article, what is the main idea about why poets use comparisons?

choices.text: Poets use comparisons to turn ideas into pictures you can see.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the article says poets use comparisons to turn invisible feelings and ideas into pictures anyone can see, like hope becoming a singing bird.

choices.text: Poets use comparisons to teach readers about nature and animals.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because while some poems mention nature and animals, the article explains those are used as comparisons for feelings and life choices, not to teach lessons about nature.

choices.text: Poets use comparisons to hide secret messages in their writing.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the article never says poets are hiding secret messages — it actually explains that comparisons help make ideas clearer and easier to understand.

choices.text: Poets use comparisons to explain how to travel to new places.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because even though Frost's poem uses a road, the article explains the road is a metaphor for life choices, not a guide about how to travel anywhere.

syllabus_metadata.title: Painting Pictures with Words: Simile and Metaphor

assessments.title: Hear the Buzz! Onomatopoeia and Sound in Poetry

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Sounds Hiding Inside Poems

Words That Sound Like What They Mean

A snake slides through dry leaves. You hear it before you see it: hissssss. That sound is so perfect that we turned it into a word. "Hiss" doesn't just describe the noise a snake makes. It copies the noise. Say it slowly and listen to yourself. Your mouth becomes the snake.

Words like "hiss," "buzz," "crash," and "pop" have a special name. They are called onomatopoeia — say it like this: on-uh-MAT-uh-PEE-uh. Onomatopoeia means a word that copies the sound it describes, the way a parrot copies your voice. Poets love these words because they pull real sounds right off the page and into your ears.

You might think onomatopoeia only means loud, obvious words like "bang" or "boom." Actually, some of the best examples are quieter. "Murmur" sounds like people talking softly. "Sizzle" sounds like butter melting in a hot pan. "Rustle" sounds like paper shifting in the wind. These gentle words copy sounds too — they just do it with a whisper instead of a shout.
stimulus.content_text:
The Sounds Hiding Inside PoemsWords That Sound Like What They Mean

Words like "hiss," "buzz," "crash," and "pop" have a special name. They are called onomatopoeia — say it like this: on-uh-MAT-uh-PEE-uh. Onomatopoeia means a word that copies the sound it describes, the way a parrot copies your voice. Poets love these words because they pull real sounds right off the page and into your ears.

You might think onomatopoeia only means loud, obvious words like "bang" or "boom." Actually, some of the best examples are quieter. "Murmur" sounds like people talking softly. "Sizzle" sounds like butter melting in a hot pan. "Rustle" sounds like paper shifting in the wind. These gentle words copy sounds too — they just do it with a whisper instead of a shout.

items.prompt: According to the passage, how are quiet onomatopoeia words different from loud onomatopoeia words?

choices.text: They describe feelings instead of sounds.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage says quiet onomatopoeia words copy sounds, not feelings — 'murmur' copies soft talking and 'sizzle' copies a cooking sound.

choices.text: They copy sounds softly instead of loudly.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage says these quiet words 'copy sounds too — they just do it with a whisper instead of a shout,' meaning they copy sounds softly instead of loudly.

choices.text: They are used in stories instead of poems.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage never says quiet words belong in stories — it only mentions that poets love onomatopoeia words in general.

choices.text: They are harder to say instead of easier.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage never compares how easy or hard any of these words are to say.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
When Beginning Sounds Repeat

Poets have another trick for making words sound musical. It is called alliteration — the repetition, or repeating, of the same beginning sound in words that sit close together. Think of the tongue twister "Peter Piper picked a peck of pickled peppers." Many of those words start with the same "P" sound. That is alliteration, and it gives the line a beat, like a drum.

Here is a surprise: alliteration is about matching sounds, not matching letters. "Cool cat" is alliteration because both words start with the "K" sound. But "phone photo" is alliteration too — even though those words start with different letters, they both begin with the "F" sound. Your ears catch alliteration, not your eyes.
stimulus.content_text:
When Beginning Sounds Repeat

Think of the tongue twister "Peter Piper picked a peck of pickled peppers." Many of those words start with the same "P" sound. That is alliteration, and it gives the line a beat, like a drum.

Here is a surprise: alliteration is about matching sounds, not matching letters. "Cool cat" is alliteration because both words start with the "K" sound. But "phone photo" is alliteration too — even though those words start with different letters, they both begin with the "F" sound. Your ears catch alliteration, not your eyes.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, why does the author say 'phone photo' is an example of alliteration?

choices.text: because both words are spelled with the letters p-h
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage clearly says alliteration is about matching sounds, not matching letters or spelling.

choices.text: because both words begin with the same sound
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage explains that 'phone' and 'photo' both start with the 'F' sound, which is what makes them alliteration.

choices.text: because the words rhyme with each other
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because rhyming means words have the same ending sound, but alliteration is about the beginning sound.

choices.text: because the words sit next to each other
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because words need to share the same beginning sound to be alliteration — just being close together is not enough.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
A Poet Who Played With Sound

Edward Lear was a poet who lived in the 1800s. He wrote limericks, which are short, bouncy, five-line poems. He also wrote nonsense poems — poems full of silly ideas and made-up words that don't exist in any dictionary. One of his most famous poems tells the story of the Owl and the Pussycat, who sailed away "in a beautiful pea-green boat." They danced "by the light of the moon, the moon." That repeated "moon" rolls off your tongue like a song. Lear's playful rhythms show that poetry can make you giggle out loud.
stimulus.content_text:
A Poet Who Played With Sound
Edward Lear was a poet who lived in the 1800s. He wrote limericks, which are short, bouncy, five-line poems. He also wrote nonsense poems — poems full of silly ideas and made-up words that don't exist in any dictionary. One of his most famous poems tells the story of the Owl and the Pussycat, who sailed away "in a beautiful pea-green boat." They danced "by the light of the moon, the moon." That repeated "moon" rolls off your tongue like a song. Lear's playful rhythms show that poetry can make you giggle out loud.

items.prompt: According to the text, what is the correct order of events in the story of the Owl and the Pussycat?

choices.text: First they sailed in a boat, and then they danced.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage says they sailed away first and then danced by the light of the moon, which matches this order.

choices.text: First they danced by the moon, and then they sailed.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because it flips the order — the sailing happened first, and the dancing came after.

choices.text: First they sang a loud song, and then they danced.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the passage never says the Owl and the Pussycat sang a loud song.

choices.text: First they made up new words, and then they sailed.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because making up silly words describes Edward Lear's poetry style, not events in the Owl and Pussycat story.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
How Sound Creates Feeling

Poets choose sounds carefully to create a mood — the feeling a poem gives you, the way a dark room feels different from a sunny one. These tools for shaping sound are called sound devices, and they work like colors on a painter's palette. A poem about a quiet forest might use soft words like "whisper" and "rustle." A poem about a thunderstorm might use sharp words like "crack" and "roar." The sounds themselves change how you feel.

But rhyming is only one tool in a poet's collection. Onomatopoeia and alliteration can make a poem sound musical even when nothing rhymes at all. A line like "the slow, silver stream slipped past the stones" has no rhyme — but say it aloud and hear how it flows.
stimulus.content_text:

These tools for shaping sound are called sound devices, and they work like colors on a painter's palette. A poem about a quiet forest might use soft words like "whisper" and "rustle." A poem about a thunderstorm might use sharp words like "crack" and "roar." The sounds themselves change how you feel.

But rhyming is only one tool in a poet's collection. Onomatopoeia and alliteration can make a poem sound musical even when nothing rhymes at all. A line like "the slow, silver stream slipped past the stones" has no rhyme — but say it aloud and hear how it flows.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, how does the author explain what a "mood" is in a poem?

choices.text: By comparing it to how a dark room feels different from a sunny room.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage uses the exact words 'the way a dark room feels different from a sunny one' to explain what mood means.

choices.text: By comparing it to how a quiet forest sounds different from a loud storm.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the quiet forest and thunderstorm examples are used to show how sound devices work, not to define mood.

choices.text: By comparing it to how a rhyming word sounds different from a musical word.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage never compares rhyming words to musical words when explaining mood.

choices.text: By comparing it to how a silver stream looks different from a hard stone.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the silver stream and stones are part of an alliteration example, not an explanation of mood.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Poetry Lives in Your Voice

Try something the next time you pick up a poem. Listen for words that copy real sounds. Notice where beginning sounds repeat. Feel how those choices change the mood inside you. Poetry was never meant to sit silently on paper. It was built for your voice — the way a song was built for singing.
stimulus.content_text:
Poetry Lives in Your Voice
Try something the next time you pick up a poem. Listen for words that copy real sounds. Notice where beginning sounds repeat. Feel how those choices change the mood inside you. Poetry was never meant to sit silently on paper. It was built for your voice — the way a song was built for singing.

items.prompt: According to the section "Poetry Lives in Your Voice," how does the author define the purpose of poetry?

choices.text: It is built for your voice.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage uses almost these exact words: 'It was built for your voice,' telling us the author's definition of poetry's purpose.

choices.text: It is built for silent paper.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the passage says the opposite — poetry was 'never meant to sit silently on paper.'

choices.text: It is built for real sounds.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because real sounds are a feature to listen for in poems, not the main purpose the author describes.

choices.text: It is built for inside moods.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because changing your mood is an effect poetry can have on you, not the purpose the author defines for it.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: Based on the passage, how is onomatopoeia different from alliteration?

choices.text: One copies real noises, while the other repeats starting sounds.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage clearly explains that onomatopoeia copies real sounds (like 'buzz' or 'hiss') while alliteration repeats the same beginning sound in nearby words (like 'Peter Piper picked').

choices.text: One uses silly made-up words, while the other uses normal words.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because made-up words are mentioned when talking about Edward Lear's nonsense poems, not when explaining onomatopoeia or alliteration.

choices.text: One creates a very happy mood, while the other creates a sad mood.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the passage never says either sound device creates only a happy or only a sad mood — both tools can help create many different feelings.

choices.text: One uses only loud noises, while the other uses only soft noises.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the passage specifically says onomatopoeia includes soft, quiet words like 'murmur,' 'sizzle,' and 'rustle,' not just loud noises.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: Based on the passage, why do poets use sound devices like onomatopoeia and alliteration?

choices.text: Because they help create a special mood or feeling for the reader.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage directly says poets use sound devices to create a mood, or feeling, in the reader — just like colors help a painter create a certain look.

choices.text: Because they help make every single line in the poem rhyme perfectly.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the passage actually says onomatopoeia and alliteration can make a poem musical 'even when nothing rhymes at all,' so they are NOT just about making lines rhyme.

choices.text: Because they help teach the reader true facts about animals and nature.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage never says sound devices are used to teach facts about animals or nature — they are used to create feelings and music in poems.

choices.text: Because they help readers learn how to spell difficult new vocabulary.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage never mentions that sound devices help readers learn to spell; their purpose is about sound and feeling, not spelling.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: Which sequence of steps best supports the main idea that poetry is meant to be heard?

choices.text: First find the rhyming words, then read the poem silently, and finally look up the words.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage says poetry was never meant to sit silently on paper, so reading silently goes against the main idea.

choices.text: First listen for copied sounds, then notice repeating sounds, and finally feel the mood.
choices.feedback: B is correct because it follows the exact three steps the passage gives: listen for copied sounds, notice repeating beginning sounds, and feel the mood — all of which show poetry is meant to be heard.

choices.text: First learn about Edward Lear, then write a bouncy limerick, and finally sail in a boat.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the details about Edward Lear are just one example in the passage and don't describe the steps for experiencing poetry through sound.

choices.text: First shout the loud words, then whisper the quiet words, and finally guess the letters.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage never instructs readers to shout loud words, whisper quiet words, or guess letters — those steps are made up and miss the point.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: Use the bold key words to find the sections about onomatopoeia and alliteration. Based on the text, how are these two poetry tools similar?

choices.text: They both focus on how words sound to your ears rather than how they look.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage says onomatopoeia copies sounds you hear, and alliteration is caught by your ears, not your eyes — both tools are all about sound.

choices.text: They both use silly, made-up words that cannot be found in a dictionary.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because made-up, silly words describe Edward Lear's nonsense poems, not onomatopoeia or alliteration.

choices.text: They both require words to start with the exact same letter of the alphabet.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the passage clearly explains that alliteration matches beginning *sounds*, not letters — 'phone' and 'photo' are an example of this.

choices.text: They both are used to make a poem sound like a loud, crashing thunderstorm.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the passage shows both tools can be used in soft, quiet poems, like 'the slow, silver stream slipped past the stones,' not just loud thunderstorm poems.

assessments.title: Longfellow's Song: Rhythm and Storytelling in Verse

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Poet Who Made Words Gallop

A Horse in the Dark

Two lanterns glow from a church tower in Boston. A rider leaps onto his horse and thunders through the night. But the person who made his ride thunder inside the minds of millions was a poet named Henry Wadsworth Longfellow. In the 1800s, Longfellow was one of the most popular poets in America.
stimulus.content_text:
The Poet Who Made Words GallopA Horse in the Dark
Two lanterns glow from a church tower in Boston. A rider leaps onto his horse and thunders through the night. But the person who made his ride thunder inside the minds of millions was a poet named Henry Wadsworth Longfellow. In the 1800s, Longfellow was one of the most popular poets in America.

items.prompt: According to the text, what is the difference between the rider on the horse and Henry Wadsworth Longfellow?

choices.text: The rider held the lanterns, but Longfellow rode the fast horse.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage never says the rider held the lanterns — it only says two lanterns glowed from a church tower.

choices.text: The rider rode the horse, but Longfellow made the ride famous.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage shows the rider actually rode the horse, while Longfellow was the poet who made that ride famous to millions of people.

choices.text: The rider lived in the 1800s, but Longfellow lived much earlier.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage says Longfellow lived in the 1800s, so he did not live at a different time than the rider.

choices.text: The rider was a famous poet, but Longfellow was a church leader.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage clearly calls Longfellow a poet, and nothing in the passage says the rider was a poet or that Longfellow was a church leader.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Poems That Tell Stories

Longfellow wrote a type of poem called a narrative poem. The word narrative means "telling a story," the way a friend might narrate what happened at recess. A narrative poem has characters, a setting, and a sequence of events, just like a chapter book.

You might think all poems are only a few lines long. Some narrative poems are even book-length, running for hundreds of pages. Actually, a narrative poem can be as big as a novel — it just happens to use the tools of poetry to tell its tale.

Rhythm is the pattern of strong and weak beats in a line, like the steady boom-tap-tap of a drum. Rhyme is when words share the same ending sound, like "night" and "light." And verse is simply another word for lines of a poem grouped together, the way sentences form a paragraph. Poets mix all three to create something that sounds almost like music. Many poems do rhyme, but some narrative poems skip rhyme entirely and still pull you into a story using rhythm and vivid pictures made of words.
stimulus.content_text:
Poems That Tell Stories
Longfellow wrote a type of poem called a narrative poem. The word narrative means "telling a story," the way a friend might narrate what happened at recess. A narrative poem has characters, a setting, and a sequence of events, just like a chapter book.
You might think all poems are only a few lines long. Some narrative poems are even book-length, running for hundreds of pages. Actually, a narrative poem can be as big as a novel — it just happens to use the tools of poetry to tell its tale.
Rhythm is the pattern of strong and weak beats in a line, like the steady boom-tap-tap of a drum. Rhyme is when words share the same ending sound, like "night" and "light." And verse is simply another word for lines of a poem grouped together, the way sentences form a paragraph. Poets mix all three to create something that sounds almost like music. Many poems do rhyme, but some narrative poems skip rhyme entirely and still pull you into a story using rhythm and vivid pictures made of words.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, why can some narrative poems still pull readers into a story even without rhyme?

choices.text: because they are written in hundreds of pages like a novel
choices.feedback: Being hundreds of pages long like a novel is just talking about how big a poem can be, not about what keeps readers interested without rhyme.

choices.text: because they use rhythm and vivid word pictures instead
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says poems without rhyme can still pull you in by using rhythm and vivid word pictures instead.

choices.text: because they have characters and a setting like a book
choices.feedback: Having characters and a setting is what makes a poem a narrative poem in the first place, but it doesn't explain what replaces rhyme.

choices.text: because they share the same ending sounds in each line
choices.feedback: This is actually describing rhyme — words with the same ending sounds — which is the exact thing these poems are NOT using.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Hoofbeats You Can Hear

Longfellow's most famous poem is "Paul Revere's Ride." In it, Revere waits for a signal from the Old North Church in Boston. His friend will hang one lantern if the British army is coming by land. Two lanterns mean they are coming by sea. When two lanterns appear, Revere leaps onto his horse and rides through the night, warning the colonists — the people living in America under British rule — that danger is near.

Read these lines from the poem out loud: "And the spark struck out by that steed, in his flight, / Kindled the land into flame with its light." Can you hear the quick, galloping beat?
stimulus.content_text:
Hoofbeats You Can Hear
Longfellow's most famous poem is "Paul Revere's Ride." In it, Revere waits for a signal from the Old North Church in Boston. His friend will hang one lantern if the British army is coming by land. Two lanterns mean they are coming by sea. When two lanterns appear, Revere leaps onto his horse and rides through the night, warning the colonists — the people living in America under British rule — that danger is near.
Read these lines from the poem out loud: "And the spark struck out by that steed, in his flight, / Kindled the land into flame with its light." Can you hear the quick, galloping beat?

items.prompt: According to the text, which event happens after two lanterns appear?

choices.text: Revere waits for a signal
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because Revere waits for the signal before the lanterns appear, not after.

choices.text: Revere rides through the night
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage clearly says that after two lanterns appear, Revere leaps on his horse and rides through the night.

choices.text: His friend hangs one lantern
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because one lantern is a different signal meaning the British are coming by land — it is not what happens after two lanterns appear.

choices.text: The British come by land
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because two lanterns mean the British are coming by sea, not by land.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Stories People Carried in Their Heads

Longfellow wrote other famous narrative poems as well. One tells the story of Hiawatha, a figure from Native American legend. The poem about Hiawatha is called The Song of Hiawatha, published in 1855. In each poem, Longfellow used strong rhythm and sharp details to make readers feel they were inside the story.

By the shores of Gitche Gumee,
By the shining Big-Sea-Water,
Stood the wigwam of Nokomis,
Daughter of the Moon, Nokomis.
Dark behind it rose the forest,
Rose the black and gloomy pine-trees,
Rose the firs with cones upon them;
Bright before it beat the water,
Beat the clear and sunny water,
Beat the shining Big-Sea-Water.

Notice how the same words repeat — "Beat the water," "shining Big-Sea-Water" — like waves rolling in again and again.

People in the 1800s loved his poems so much that they would memorize them and recite them — speak them aloud from memory — at family gatherings. His work appeared in newspapers and schoolrooms across the country. He showed that poetry belonged to everyone, not just to scholars, people who spend their lives studying books.

His work appeared in newspapers and schoolrooms across the country. He showed that poetry belonged to everyone, not just to scholars, people who spend their lives studying books.

items.prompt: When the passage says the repeated words are 'like waves rolling in again and again,' what is this language comparing the poem's words to?

choices.text: trees growing taller in a dark forest
choices.feedback: Trees are described in the poem, but the passage never compares the repeated words to trees growing taller.

choices.text: water moving onto shore over and over
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage directly says the repeated words are 'like waves rolling in again and again,' which means water moving onto shore over and over.

choices.text: people speaking loudly at family gatherings
choices.feedback: People reciting poems at family gatherings is mentioned in the passage, but that has nothing to do with the wave comparison.

choices.text: birds singing the same song each morning
choices.feedback: Birds singing is never mentioned in the passage at all, so this can't be what the comparison means.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Words That Still Ride

Close your eyes and read "Paul Revere's Ride" out loud. No screen can do what those words do — they turn your own voice into a galloping horse on a dark road in 1775.
stimulus.content_text:
Words That Still Ride
Close your eyes and read "Paul Revere's Ride" out loud. No screen can do what those words do — they turn your own voice into a galloping horse on a dark road in 1775.

items.prompt: In the section "Words That Still Ride," what does the author mean by saying the words "turn your own voice into a galloping horse on a dark road in 1775"?

choices.text: Your voice changes into a real horse sound
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the author is using a fun, imaginative way of saying the poem feels exciting — your voice doesn't actually sound like a horse.

choices.text: The words make you feel like you are riding
choices.feedback: B is correct because the author says the words make you feel like you are galloping on a dark road, which means the poem makes you feel like you are right there in the story.

choices.text: The poem teaches you facts about horses
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage never says anything about learning facts about horses.

choices.text: Reading out loud makes your voice go faster
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the author is describing the feeling of being inside the poem's story, not saying that reading out loud makes your voice speed up.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: Based on the article, how is the story of "Paul Revere's Ride" different from the story of "The Song of Hiawatha"?

choices.text: One is about a rider warning colonists, and the other is about a Native American legend.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the article clearly states that 'Paul Revere's Ride' is about warning colonists of danger, while 'The Song of Hiawatha' is about a figure from Native American legend.

choices.text: One is about a dark and gloomy forest, and the other is about a clear and sunny ocean.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the dark forest and the shining water are both images from the same poem, 'The Song of Hiawatha,' not from two different poems.

choices.text: One is about a long and exciting novel, and the other is about a short and simple song.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the article never calls either poem a novel or a simple song — it just says narrative poems can sometimes be as long as a novel.

choices.text: One is about a quiet church building, and the other is about a loud and busy schoolroom.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the church and the schoolroom are small details mentioned in the article, not what either poem is about.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: Why did people in the 1800s memorize Longfellow's poems and recite them at family gatherings?

choices.text: because the poems were short and had only a few lines to learn
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says narrative poems can be very long — even book-length — so they were not short and easy to memorize because of their length.

choices.text: because the poems used strong rhythm and told exciting stories
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage says Longfellow used strong rhythm and sharp details to pull readers into exciting stories, which is exactly why people loved and memorized his poems.

choices.text: because the poems were printed in books that cost too much money
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says his poems appeared in newspapers and schoolrooms across the country, which means people could easily get them without spending a lot of money.

choices.text: because the poems skipped rhyme and used only vivid word pictures
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage explains that Longfellow did use rhyme in his poems — rhyme was one of the poetry tools he mixed together to make his poems sound like music.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: Which choice best recounts the key details of the article?

choices.text: First the author explains narrative poems, next gives an example, and finally shows why people loved them.
choices.feedback: A is correct because it follows the real order of the article: explaining narrative poems, giving examples, and showing why people loved them.

choices.text: First the author describes a horse ride, next explains how to write a book, and finally tells a legend.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the author never explains how to write a book — that's not in the article at all.

choices.text: First the author shows people memorizing poems, next explains rhythm, and finally introduces Longfellow.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the order is backwards — the article introduces Longfellow near the beginning, not at the end.

choices.text: First the author tells about a church tower, next compares poems to books, and finally lists scholars.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because scholars are only mentioned briefly at the end as a small detail, not as a main idea of the article.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: Based on the article, how are the poems "Paul Revere's Ride" and "The Song of Hiawatha" similar?

choices.text: They both use rhythm to make readers feel inside the story.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the article directly says Longfellow used 'strong rhythm and sharp details' in each poem to make readers feel inside the story.

choices.text: They both tell stories about colonists fighting the British army.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because only 'Paul Revere's Ride' is about colonists and the British — 'The Song of Hiawatha' is about a figure from Native American legend.

choices.text: They both use rhyme to help families memorize the verses.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the article never says rhyme helped people memorize the poems, and the lines shown from 'The Song of Hiawatha' don't even rhyme.

choices.text: They both repeat words to sound like rolling ocean waves.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the repeating words that sound like rolling waves are only described for 'The Song of Hiawatha,' not for 'Paul Revere's Ride.'

syllabus_metadata.title: Longfellow's Song: Rhythm and Storytelling in Verse

assessments.title: Frosty Mornings and Snowy Woods: Nature in Poetry

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Poet Who Stopped in the Snow

A Dark Road, a Quiet Forest

This is the scene Robert Frost painted — not with a brush, but with words — in his famous poem "Stopping by Woods on a Snowy Evening."

Frost lived in New England, the part of the United States that includes Vermont, New Hampshire, and Massachusetts. He wrote about stone walls, birch trees, country roads, and farms. He was a rural poet, meaning he wrote about life in the countryside, far from busy cities. He watched how morning light stretched across a field. He noticed how snow buried the fences at night.
stimulus.content_text:
The Poet Who Stopped in the SnowA Dark Road, a Quiet Forest
This is the scene Robert Frost painted — not with a brush, but with words — in his famous poem "Stopping by Woods on a Snowy Evening."

Frost lived in New England, the part of the United States that includes Vermont, New Hampshire, and Massachusetts. He wrote about stone walls, birch trees, country roads, and farms. He was a rural poet, meaning he wrote about life in the countryside, far from busy cities. He watched how morning light stretched across a field. He noticed how snow buried the fences at night.

items.prompt: According to the text, a rural poet writes about the countryside instead of what kind of place?

choices.text: a snowy forest
choices.feedback: A snowy forest is incorrect because the passage describes snowy forests as something a rural poet like Frost DOES write about, not something he writes about instead of the countryside.

choices.text: a busy city
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer! The passage says a rural poet writes about 'life in the countryside, far from busy cities,' so a busy city is the opposite of what a rural poet writes about.

choices.text: a country road
choices.feedback: A country road is incorrect because the passage actually lists country roads as one of the things Frost wrote about as a rural poet.

choices.text: a farm field
choices.feedback: A farm field is incorrect because farms and fields are part of the countryside, which is exactly what rural poets like Frost write about.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Seeing, Hearing, and Feeling a Poem

In his snowy poem, a traveler stops his horse to watch the woods grow white and still. Frost helps you experience this moment through imagery — descriptive words that wake up your five senses. Imagery is like a movie made of words. It makes you see, hear, smell, taste, or feel something that isn't really in front of you.

The poem begins:

Whose woods these are I think I know.
His house is in the village though;
He will not see me stopping here
To watch his woods fill up with snow.

My little horse must think it queer
To stop without a farmhouse near
Between the woods and frozen lake
The darkest evening of the year.

Listen to these lines from the poem: "The only other sound's the sweep / Of easy wind and downy flake." Can you hear that soft wind?

Frost's poem also rhymes — "know" matches with "snow," and "here" matches with "near." You might think all poems have to rhyme. Actually, many wonderful poems do not rhyme at all. Rhyme is just one tool in a poet's toolbox. Some poets use rhythm, repeated sounds, or surprising word choices instead.
stimulus.content_text:
Seeing, Hearing, and Feeling a Poem
In his snowy poem, a traveler stops his horse to watch the woods grow white and still. Frost helps you experience this moment through imagery — descriptive words that wake up your five senses. Imagery is like a movie made of words. It makes you see, hear, smell, taste, or feel something that isn't really in front of you.

The poem begins:


Whose woods these are I think I know.
His house is in the village though;
He will not see me stopping here
To watch his woods fill up with snow.
My little horse must think it queer
To stop without a farmhouse near
Between the woods and frozen lake
The darkest evening of the year.


Listen to these lines from the poem: "The only other sound's the sweep / Of easy wind and downy flake." Can you hear that soft wind?

Frost's poem also rhymes — "know" matches with "snow," and "here" matches with "near." You might think all poems have to rhyme. Actually, many wonderful poems do not rhyme at all. Rhyme is just one tool in a poet's toolbox. Some poets use rhythm, repeated sounds, or surprising word choices instead.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, why does imagery help a reader experience a poem?

choices.text: Because it uses words to wake up the senses
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage directly says imagery uses descriptive words to 'wake up your five senses,' helping readers experience the poem.

choices.text: Because it makes the poem's lines rhyme well
choices.feedback: B is wrong because rhyming is a separate tool — the passage talks about rhyme in a different paragraph from imagery.

choices.text: Because it tells a story about a small horse
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the little horse is just part of the poem's story, not an explanation of how imagery works.

choices.text: Because it uses rhythm and repeated sounds
choices.feedback: D is wrong because rhythm and repeated sounds are listed as other tools a poet can use, not what imagery does.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
More Than Just Snow

Here is something surprising about poems: they can mean more than what they say on the surface. Poets often give their words a deeper meaning — a hidden message tucked inside the lines, like a gift inside a wrapped box.

The traveler in Frost's poem wants to stay and enjoy the peaceful woods. But he says, "I have miles to go before I sleep." He has responsibilities — jobs to finish and promises to keep. Frost is thinking about duty, the feeling that you must do what you said you would do, even when you want to rest. The snowy woods stand for peace and quiet. The miles ahead stand for the hard work of life.

It tells a tiny story, shows a winter night, and makes you think about your own promises.
stimulus.content_text:
More Than Just Snow
Here is something surprising about poems: they can mean more than what they say on the surface. Poets often give their words a deeper meaning — a hidden message tucked inside the lines, like a gift inside a wrapped box.

The traveler in Frost's poem wants to stay and enjoy the peaceful woods. But he says, "I have miles to go before I sleep." He has responsibilities — jobs to finish and promises to keep. Frost is thinking about duty, the feeling that you must do what you said you would do, even when you want to rest. The snowy woods stand for peace and quiet. The miles ahead stand for the hard work of life.

It tells a tiny story, shows a winter night, and makes you think about your own promises.

items.prompt: According to the text, what is the correct sequence of events for the traveler?

choices.text: He goes to sleep, and then he travels for miles.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because it reverses the order — the traveler says he must travel the miles BEFORE he sleeps, not after.

choices.text: He travels for miles, and then he goes to sleep.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the traveler's own words, 'I have miles to go before I sleep,' clearly show he travels first and sleeps second.

choices.text: He wraps a gift, and then he travels for miles.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because wrapping a gift is just an example the author uses to explain deeper meaning in poems — it is not part of the traveler's story.

choices.text: He rests in the woods, and then he finishes jobs.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the traveler does not actually rest in the woods — he feels the pull to stay but chooses to keep going and finish his responsibilities.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Nature as a Mirror

Many poets find meaning in the natural world. Some write about ocean waves crashing on rocks. Others describe flowers opening in spring or leaves turning gold in autumn. A sunset can become a poem about endings. A seedling pushing through dirt can become a poem about hope.

Nature works like a mirror for human feelings. A stormy sky can match a feeling of anger. A calm lake can match a feeling of peace. Poets see these connections and pick exact words so you can feel what they felt.

Robert Frost once said that a poem "begins in delight and ends in wisdom." His snowy poem starts with a beautiful picture and ends with a truth about life. That is what the best nature poems do. They let you stand in a forest or beside an ocean — and then they show you something about yourself you didn't see before.
stimulus.content_text:
Nature as a Mirror
Many poets find meaning in the natural world. Some write about ocean waves crashing on rocks. Others describe flowers opening in spring or leaves turning gold in autumn. A sunset can become a poem about endings. A seedling pushing through dirt can become a poem about hope.

Nature works like a mirror for human feelings. A stormy sky can match a feeling of anger. A calm lake can match a feeling of peace. Poets see these connections and pick exact words so you can feel what they felt.

Robert Frost once said that a poem "begins in delight and ends in wisdom." His snowy poem starts with a beautiful picture and ends with a truth about life. That is what the best nature poems do. They let you stand in a forest or beside an ocean — and then they show you something about yourself you didn't see before.

items.prompt: The author says that nature "works like a mirror." Based on the passage, what does this comparison mean?

choices.text: Nature looks shiny and clear like real glass.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage uses 'mirror' to talk about feelings being reflected, not about nature being shiny or made of glass.

choices.text: Nature matches the feelings that a person has.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly says nature 'works like a mirror' by matching human feelings, like a stormy sky matching anger or a calm lake matching peace.

choices.text: Nature teaches people how to write good poems.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage never says nature teaches people how to write poems — it says nature matches feelings.

choices.text: Nature causes people to feel angry about storms.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage says a stormy sky can match anger someone already feels, not that storms make people feel angry.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: Based on the passage, how is what the traveler wants to do different from what he has to do?

choices.text: He wants to rest in the quiet woods, but he has to finish his jobs.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage clearly says the traveler wants to stay in the peaceful woods, but he has 'miles to go' and responsibilities he must take care of first.

choices.text: He wants to visit the nearby village, but he has to stay in the forest.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the traveler never mentions wanting to visit the village — the village is only mentioned as where the woods owner lives.

choices.text: He wants to feed his little horse, but he has to walk in the snow.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage never says anything about the traveler wanting to feed his horse.

choices.text: He wants to sleep in a farmhouse, but he has to cross a frozen lake.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the frozen lake is just part of the scenery in the poem, not something the traveler has to cross or a farmhouse he wants to sleep in.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: Based on the article, why does Robert Frost use descriptions of nature, like the snowy woods, in his poem?

choices.text: To teach readers how to travel safely through the cold woods.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because Frost's poem is not giving travel safety tips — it's sharing feelings and life lessons through nature.

choices.text: To help readers understand human feelings and important truths.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the article says nature acts like a mirror for human feelings, and Frost uses the snowy woods to show ideas about duty, rest, and keeping promises.

choices.text: To show readers that all good poems must have matching sounds.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the article clearly says many wonderful poems do not rhyme, so matching sounds is not the reason Frost uses nature.

choices.text: To prove that living in the city is better than the countryside.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because Frost was a rural poet who loved the countryside, and the article never says city life is better.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: According to the text, what is the correct order of how a good nature poem shares its main idea?

choices.text: It begins with a pretty picture and ends with a truth about life.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage directly says Frost's poem 'starts with a beautiful picture and ends with a truth about life,' which is exactly what answer A says.

choices.text: It begins with a truth about life and ends with a pretty picture.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because it has the order backwards — the pretty picture comes first and the truth about life comes at the end, not the other way around.

choices.text: It begins with a rhyming sound and ends with a quiet winter wood.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage never says a poem begins with a rhyming sound and ends with a quiet winter wood — those details are not connected that way.

choices.text: It begins with a quiet forest and ends with a busy city street.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage never mentions a busy city street, and Frost's poem is actually about staying away from busy places, not ending at one.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: Based on the headings, how is the information in "Seeing, Hearing, and Feeling a Poem" different from the information in "More Than Just Snow"?

choices.text: The first explains how words build a picture, while the second explains the hidden message.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the first section teaches how imagery builds a picture using your senses, while the second section explains the deeper, hidden meaning about duty and responsibility.

choices.text: The first describes a busy city setting, while the second describes a quiet forest setting.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because neither section describes a busy city — both sections talk about the quiet, snowy forest setting in Frost's poem.

choices.text: The first tells why all poems must rhyme, while the second tells why poems use rhythm.
