choices.feedback: C is wrong because the passage actually tells us that poems do NOT all have to rhyme, and neither section is focused on rules about rhyme or rhythm.

choices.text: The first shows the ending of the poem, while the second shows the start of the poem.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the two sections are not about different parts of the poem — they each explain a different way to understand the whole poem.

syllabus_metadata.title: Frosty Mornings and Snowy Woods: Nature in Poetry

assessments.title: Down the Rabbit Hole: Alice's Wild Adventure Begins

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Down the Rabbit Hole: The Story Behind Alice's Adventures in Wonderland

A Rabbit with a Pocket Watch

Have you ever been so bored that you would follow a rabbit into a hole? That's exactly what Alice does! A bored girl named Alice spots it and jumps up. She chases it to a big hole under a hedge — a thick wall of bushes, like a green fence made of leaves — and falls right in after it.

Alice was getting very tired of sitting with her sister. She had nothing to do. She looked at her sister's book, but it had no pictures or conversations in it. "What is the use of a book without pictures or conversations?" thought Alice. It was a hot day and she felt sleepy. She was thinking about whether to get up and pick daisies when suddenly a White Rabbit with pink eyes ran past her. That wasn't so strange. But then the Rabbit said, "Oh dear! Oh dear! I shall be too late!" Alice didn't think that was too odd either. But then the Rabbit took a watch out of its pocket and looked at it! Alice had never seen a rabbit with a pocket or a watch before. She jumped up and ran after it. She was just in time to see it pop down a large rabbit-hole under the hedge.
stimulus.content_text:
Down the Rabbit Hole: The Story Behind Alice's Adventures in WonderlandA Rabbit with a Pocket Watch
Have you ever been so bored that you would follow a rabbit into a hole? That's exactly what Alice does! A bored girl named Alice spots it and jumps up. She chases it to a big hole under a hedge — a thick wall of bushes, like a green fence made of leaves — and falls right in after it.


Alice was getting very tired of sitting with her sister. She had nothing to do. She looked at her sister's book, but it had no pictures or conversations in it. "What is the use of a book without pictures or conversations?" thought Alice. It was a hot day and she felt sleepy. She was thinking about whether to get up and pick daisies when suddenly a White Rabbit with pink eyes ran past her. That wasn't so strange. But then the Rabbit said, "Oh dear! Oh dear! I shall be too late!" Alice didn't think that was too odd either. But then the Rabbit took a watch out of its pocket and looked at it! Alice had never seen a rabbit with a pocket or a watch before. She jumped up and ran after it. She was just in time to see it pop down a large rabbit-hole under the hedge.

items.prompt: According to the passage, which detail shows a contrast between the White Rabbit and an ordinary rabbit?

choices.text: a rabbit with pink eyes
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because pink eyes can actually be found in real rabbits, so that doesn't make this rabbit different from an ordinary one.

choices.text: a rabbit with a pocket watch
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage says Alice had never seen a rabbit with a pocket or a watch before, which shows this is very different from a normal rabbit.

choices.text: a rabbit under a hedge
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because real rabbits often live near bushes and hedges, so that is not unusual at all.

choices.text: a rabbit in a big hole
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because rabbits in the real world do dig and live in holes, so that is something an ordinary rabbit might do too.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
A Book That Broke the Rules

Lewis Carroll published Alice's Adventures in Wonderland in 1865. He filled his story with nonsense — silly, impossible things that don't follow normal rules. For example, there is a cat that disappears but leaves its smile behind! The book is full of crazy ideas that will make you scratch your head in the best possible way!
stimulus.content_text:
A Book That Broke the Rules
Lewis Carroll published Alice's Adventures in Wonderland in 1865. He filled his story with nonsense — silly, impossible things that don't follow normal rules. For example, there is a cat that disappears but leaves its smile behind! The book is full of crazy ideas that will make you scratch your head in the best possible way!

items.prompt: Based on the section, why does the author say that Lewis Carroll's story will make readers 'scratch your head in the best possible way'?

choices.text: because the story was published a very long time ago
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the year the book was published (1865) has nothing to do with why it makes readers scratch their heads.

choices.text: because the story is full of silly, impossible ideas
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly says the story is full of 'silly, impossible things' like a cat that leaves its smile behind, which is what makes readers scratch their heads in a fun way.

choices.text: because the story teaches readers important lessons
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage never says anything about the story teaching important lessons.

choices.text: because the story has a cat as a main character
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the cat is just one small example of the silly ideas in the book, not the main reason the story is so head-scratching.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
The Real Alice

Here's something cool you might not know — Lewis Carroll wasn't even his real name! It was a pen name — a made-up name that an author puts on a book, like a costume for a writer. His real name was Charles Dodgson. In real life, he was a math teacher in England.

One summer day in 1862, Dodgson went on a boat ride with three young sisters named Liddell. He told them the story of Alice on that boat ride. The real Alice Liddell loved it so much she asked him to write it down. Dodgson spent two years turning his story into a full book. He added even more silly and strange scenes along the way.
stimulus.content_text:
The Real Alice
Here's something cool you might not know — Lewis Carroll wasn't even his real name! It was a pen name — a made-up name that an author puts on a book, like a costume for a writer. His real name was Charles Dodgson. In real life, he was a math teacher in England.

One summer day in 1862, Dodgson went on a boat ride with three young sisters named Liddell. He told them the story of Alice on that boat ride. The real Alice Liddell loved it so much she asked him to write it down. Dodgson spent two years turning his story into a full book. He added even more silly and strange scenes along the way.

items.prompt: According to the text, what happened right after Alice Liddell heard the story on the boat ride?

choices.text: Dodgson chose the pen name Lewis Carroll.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage never says that choosing the pen name 'Lewis Carroll' happened right after the boat ride.

choices.text: She asked Dodgson to write the story down.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage says Alice Liddell loved the story so much that she asked Dodgson to write it down — and that happened right after she heard it on the boat.

choices.text: Dodgson added silly and strange scenes to it.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because adding silly and strange scenes happened later, when Dodgson was spending two years writing the full book.

choices.text: Dodgson spent two years teaching math in England.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because Dodgson was already a math teacher before the boat ride — the passage doesn't say he started teaching math after the story.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
A Story That Never Got Old

Can you believe people are still talking about the Cheshire Cat and the Mad Hatter today — more than 150 years later? That's how amazing this story is!

Sometimes the wildest stories don't start on a page. They start with someone telling a tale out loud. So next time you hear a great story, pay close attention — you never know where it could lead!
stimulus.content_text:
A Story That Never Got Old
Can you believe people are still talking about the Cheshire Cat and the Mad Hatter today — more than 150 years later? That's how amazing this story is!

Sometimes the wildest stories don't start on a page. They start with someone telling a tale out loud. So next time you hear a great story, pay close attention — you never know where it could lead!

items.prompt: Based on the passage, how does the author compare how Alice's story started with how most stories start?

choices.text: It started as a written book instead of a spoken tale
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because it has it backwards — Alice's story started as a spoken tale, not a written book.

choices.text: It started as a story told out loud instead of on a page
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage clearly says stories like Alice's start with 'someone telling a tale out loud' instead of starting 'on a page.'

choices.text: It started as a movie instead of a written story
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage never mentions movies anywhere.

choices.text: It started as a school lesson instead of a fun tale
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage never says anything about school lessons.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: Based on the article, how is the book Alice's sister reads different from the book Lewis Carroll wrote?

choices.text: The sister's book has no pictures, but Carroll's book is full of crazy nonsense.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage says the sister's book had no pictures or conversations, while Carroll's book was full of nonsense and impossible, silly ideas.

choices.text: The sister's book is about a boat, but Carroll's book is about a math teacher.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because the boat ride is how Dodgson first told the Alice story to the Liddell sisters — it has nothing to do with what the sister's book was about.

choices.text: The sister's book has a magic cat, but Carroll's book has a normal white rabbit.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because it mixes things up — the disappearing cat is in Carroll's book, and the White Rabbit is also in Carroll's story, not the sister's book.

choices.text: The sister's book is told out loud, but Carroll's book is only written on pages.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because the passage never says the sister's book was told out loud — Alice was sitting and looking at it, so it was a regular written book.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: Based on the article, why does the author say that "the wildest stories don't start on a page"?

choices.text: Because Charles Dodgson first told the story out loud on a boat ride.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the article says Dodgson told the Alice story out loud on a boat ride before it was ever written down, which is exactly what 'stories don't start on a page' means.

choices.text: Because Alice was bored while sitting outside with a pictureless book.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because Alice being bored is something that happens inside the story, not a clue about how the real story was created.

choices.text: Because Lewis Carroll used a fake name to hide his job as a teacher.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because using a pen name is just about hiding his real name, and has nothing to do with where the story started.

choices.text: Because the story was filled with silly ideas like a disappearing cat.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the silly ideas describe what kind of story it is, not how or where the story first began.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: The main idea of the article is that a famous book began as a simple story. Which sequence of key details supports this main idea?

choices.text: Dodgson told a story out loud, wrote it into a book, and shared it with the world.
choices.feedback: A is correct because it traces the key steps — Dodgson told the story aloud, wrote it into a book, and shared it with the world — which directly supports the main idea that a famous book began as a simple story.

choices.text: Alice chased a fast white rabbit, fell down a deep dark hole, and started a wild adventure.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because it describes what happens to Alice inside the story, not how the book itself was created.

choices.text: Dodgson taught math at a school, went on a long boat ride, and changed his real name.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because Dodgson's math job and pen name are interesting details, but they don't show how the book grew from a simple spoken story.

choices.text: A writer made up silly rules, added crazy nonsense ideas, and confused readers in a good way.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because it focuses on the nonsense and silly ideas in the book, not on the journey of how the story became a famous book.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: How does the information under the heading "A Rabbit with a Pocket Watch" compare to the information under "The Real Alice"?

choices.text: The first section describes the made-up character, while the second section describes the actual girl.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the first section introduces Alice as a made-up story character, while the second section tells us about the real girl named Alice Liddell who inspired the book.

choices.text: The first section describes the book's silly rules, while the second section describes the book's author.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the section about the book's silly rules is in 'A Book That Broke the Rules,' not in these two sections.

choices.text: The first section describes how the story started, while the second section describes why it is still read.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the second section is about the real Alice and the author, not about why people still read the story today.

choices.text: The first section describes the talking animals, while the second section describes the boat ride's path.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the first section is about more than just talking animals, and the second section never describes the path of the boat ride.

syllabus_metadata.title: Down the Rabbit Hole: Alice's Wild Adventure Begins

assessments.title: Curiouser and Curiouser: Alice Meets Wonderland's Characters

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Impossible Characters of Wonderland

A Story Born on a River

That image comes from one of the most famous children's books ever written: Alice's Adventures in Wonderland.

A math teacher named Lewis Carroll wrote this story in 1865. You might think Alice in the book is a real person, but she is not. Carroll invented her — created her from his own imagination — the way a painter invents a picture from colors and brushstrokes.
stimulus.content_text:
The Impossible Characters of WonderlandA Story Born on a River
That image comes from one of the most famous children's books ever written: Alice's Adventures in Wonderland.
A math teacher named Lewis Carroll wrote this story in 1865. You might think Alice in the book is a real person, but she is not. Carroll invented her — created her from his own imagination — the way a painter invents a picture from colors and brushstrokes.

items.prompt: According to the section "A Story Born on a River," which of the following correctly contrasts Lewis Carroll and Alice?

choices.text: Carroll is a real person, but Alice is an invented character.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage clearly states Carroll was a real math teacher who invented Alice from his imagination, making her a fictional character.

choices.text: Carroll is a famous painter, but Alice is a smart math teacher.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the passage says Carroll was a math teacher, not a painter — painting is just used as an example to explain what 'invented' means.

choices.text: Carroll is a written story, but Alice is a living human child.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because Carroll is a real person who wrote a book, not a written story himself.

choices.text: Carroll is a young student, but Alice is a grown adult woman.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the passage never mentions Carroll's age or describes Alice as a grown adult woman.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
A Cat That Breaks the Rules

One of the hardest-to-forget characters is the Cheshire Cat. He sits in a tree and gives Alice directions, but his advice never quite makes sense. Here is what makes him truly strange: he can disappear. He vanishes — fades away like fog in sunlight — starting with his tail and ending with his grin. His smile stays behind, floating in the air all by itself. "Well, I've often seen a cat without a grin," Alice says, "but a grin without a cat is the most curious thing!"

Carroll wanted to surprise you, make you laugh, and make you picture something that could never happen.
stimulus.content_text:
A Cat That Breaks the Rules
One of the hardest-to-forget characters is the Cheshire Cat. He sits in a tree and gives Alice directions, but his advice never quite makes sense. Here is what makes him truly strange: he can disappear. He vanishes — fades away like fog in sunlight — starting with his tail and ending with his grin. His smile stays behind, floating in the air all by itself. "Well, I've often seen a cat without a grin," Alice says, "but a grin without a cat is the most curious thing!"
Carroll wanted to surprise you, make you laugh, and make you picture something that could never happen.

items.prompt: Based on the article, what causes Alice to say the Cheshire Cat is "the most curious thing"?

choices.text: He sits in a tree and gives her directions.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because Alice never says sitting in a tree and giving directions is 'the most curious thing.'

choices.text: His smile stays behind and floats in the air.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage shows Alice saying a grin floating without a cat is 'the most curious thing,' which happens after the cat's smile stays behind in the air.

choices.text: He fades away like fog in the warm sunlight.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because fading away describes how the cat disappears, but Alice's surprise is about the smile that stays floating, not the fading itself.

choices.text: His advice to her never quite makes any sense.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the confusing advice is mentioned in the passage, but Alice's 'most curious thing' quote is only about the floating grin.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
A Tea Party That Never Ends

Next, Alice finds the Mad Hatter at a long table set for tea. The March Hare and a sleepy little Dormouse sit with him. Time has stopped for the Hatter. He asks riddles with no answers. "Why is a raven like a writing desk?" he says. Alice tries to follow the conversation, but it goes in circles. Nothing he says follows the rules of logic — the normal way things connect and make sense, like how 1 + 1 always equals 2.

In the original text, you can see just how strange their manners really are:

"Have some wine," the March Hare said in an encouraging tone.

Alice looked all round the table, but there was nothing on it but tea. "I don't see any wine," she remarked.

"There isn't any," said the March Hare.

"Then it wasn't very civil of you to offer it," said Alice angrily.

"It wasn't very civil of you to sit down without being invited," said the March Hare.

"I didn't know it was your table," said Alice; "it's laid for a great many more than three."

"Your hair wants cutting," said the Hatter. He had been looking at Alice for some time with great curiosity, and this was his first speech.

"You should learn not to make personal remarks," Alice said with some severity; "it's very rude."

The Hatter opened his eyes very wide on hearing this; but all he said was, "Why is a raven like a writing-desk?"

Carroll flipped those rules upside down to show that some of them are actually pretty silly.
stimulus.content_text:
A Tea Party That Never Ends
Next, Alice finds the Mad Hatter at a long table set for tea. The March Hare and a sleepy little Dormouse sit with him. Time has stopped for the Hatter. He asks riddles with no answers. "Why is a raven like a writing desk?" he says. Alice tries to follow the conversation, but it goes in circles. Nothing he says follows the rules of logic — the normal way things connect and make sense, like how 1 + 1 always equals 2.
In the original text, you can see just how strange their manners really are:


"Have some wine," the March Hare said in an encouraging tone.
Alice looked all round the table, but there was nothing on it but tea. "I don't see any wine," she remarked.
"There isn't any," said the March Hare.
"Then it wasn't very civil of you to offer it," said Alice angrily.
"It wasn't very civil of you to sit down without being invited," said the March Hare.
"I didn't know it was your table," said Alice; "it's laid for a great many more than three."
"Your hair wants cutting," said the Hatter. He had been looking at Alice for some time with great curiosity, and this was his first speech.
"You should learn not to make personal remarks," Alice said with some severity; "it's very rude."
The Hatter opened his eyes very wide on hearing this; but all he said was, "Why is a raven like a writing-desk?"


Carroll flipped those rules upside down to show that some of them are actually pretty silly.

items.prompt: According to the text, what does Alice do right after the March Hare tells her to have some wine?

choices.text: She looks all round the table.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage says 'Alice looked all round the table' immediately after the March Hare offered her wine.

choices.text: She sits down without being invited.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because Alice sitting down without an invitation happened before the wine was mentioned, not after.

choices.text: She tells the Hatter he is rude.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because Alice calls the Hatter rude later in the scene, after he makes a comment about her hair.

choices.text: She asks a riddle about a raven.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the Hatter is the one who asks the raven riddle, not Alice.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
A Queen Who Is Not Really a Villain

The scariest character Alice meets is the Queen of Hearts. She rules Wonderland with a terrible temper. She constantly shouts, "Off with their heads!" Her playing-card soldiers rush to obey. But here is the funny secret: no one is ever actually harmed. The King of Hearts quietly pardons everyone — forgives them and sets them free, like a teacher who tears up a detention slip when no one is looking. The Queen's anger is loud but harmless, like thunder without lightning.

Carroll made the Queen big and loud and bossy to poke fun at people who act important but do not really do much.
stimulus.content_text:
A Queen Who Is Not Really a Villain
The scariest character Alice meets is the Queen of Hearts. She rules Wonderland with a terrible temper. She constantly shouts, "Off with their heads!" Her playing-card soldiers rush to obey. But here is the funny secret: no one is ever actually harmed. The King of Hearts quietly pardons everyone — forgives them and sets them free, like a teacher who tears up a detention slip when no one is looking. The Queen's anger is loud but harmless, like thunder without lightning.
Carroll made the Queen big and loud and bossy to poke fun at people who act important but do not really do much.

items.prompt: In the sentence "The Queen's anger is loud but harmless, like thunder without lightning," what does the comparison show about the Queen's anger?

choices.text: It sounds scary but does not hurt anyone.
choices.feedback: A is correct because thunder is loud and scary-sounding but doesn't hurt anyone, just like the Queen's shouting sounds frightening but the King forgives everyone so no one is harmed.

choices.text: It is quiet but still hurts people.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the passage says the Queen is loud, not quiet, and it clearly tells us no one is ever actually hurt.

choices.text: It is bright and easy to see.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the thunder comparison is about sound and harmlessness, not about light or being easy to see.

choices.text: It is strong and lasts a long time.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the passage never says her anger is powerful or lasts a long time — it actually shows her anger doesn't do anything at all.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
What Alice Learned — and What You Can, Too

"We're all mad here," the Cheshire Cat tells her.

Carroll wrote this story over 150 years ago, but readers your age still love it.
stimulus.content_text:
What Alice Learned — and What You Can, Too
"We're all mad here," the Cheshire Cat tells her.
Carroll wrote this story over 150 years ago, but readers your age still love it.

items.prompt: In the section, the Cheshire Cat says, 'We're all mad here.' What does the word 'mad' mean as the Cheshire Cat uses it?

choices.text: feeling angry or upset
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because even though 'mad' can sometimes mean angry, the Cheshire Cat is describing the silly, crazy world of Wonderland, not anger.

choices.text: being crazy or silly
choices.feedback: B is correct because the Cheshire Cat is saying everyone in Wonderland is a little crazy or silly, which fits the fun and imaginative world of the story.

choices.text: feeling sad or lonely
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the Cat's words are about being wild and wacky, not about feeling sad or lonely.

choices.text: being tired or sleepy
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because there is nothing in the passage that connects the Cat's words to being tired or sleepy.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: Based on the article, how is Alice different from the Wonderland characters she meets?

choices.text: She expects things to make sense, but they break normal rules.
choices.feedback: A is correct because Alice expects normal, logical things while the Wonderland characters — like the Hatter and March Hare — break all the rules of sense and good manners.

choices.text: She asks riddles with no answers, but they speak very clearly.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because Alice is the one trying to make sense of things, and it's the Mad Hatter who asks riddles with no answers.

choices.text: She has a very terrible temper, but they are quiet and sleepy.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the Queen of Hearts is the one with the terrible temper, not Alice — Alice stays calm and tries to be polite.

choices.text: She gives advice to the animals, but they ignore what she says.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the Cheshire Cat gives advice to Alice, not the other way around.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: Based on evidence from more than one section, why did Lewis Carroll make the Wonderland characters act in impossible or silly ways?

choices.text: He wanted to teach children the correct rules of logic and manners.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because Carroll did the opposite — he flipped rules 'upside down' to show that some rules are 'actually pretty silly,' not to teach kids the correct rules.

choices.text: He wanted to surprise readers and poke fun at rules people take seriously.
choices.feedback: B is correct because two different sections tell us Carroll wanted to surprise and make readers laugh, and to poke fun at people who act important but don't really do much.

choices.text: He wanted to scare readers with dangerous and harmful characters.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage tells us no one is ever actually harmed in Wonderland, so the characters are not truly dangerous or scary.

choices.text: He wanted to show that Alice was smarter than everyone in Wonderland.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage never says Carroll's goal was to show Alice was smarter than everyone else — his goal was to surprise readers and have fun with silly rules.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: If you want to read about the characters Alice meets right after the Cheshire Cat, which section heading should you look under?

choices.text: The heading "A Story Born on a River"
choices.feedback: This section is about how Lewis Carroll wrote the book back in 1865 — it doesn't talk about characters Alice meets at all.

choices.text: The heading "A Cat That Breaks the Rules"
choices.feedback: This section is about the Cheshire Cat, who Alice meets before the tea party characters, not after.

choices.text: The heading "A Tea Party That Never Ends"
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because this section comes right after the Cheshire Cat section and introduces the Mad Hatter and his friends, who are the next characters Alice meets.

choices.text: The heading "A Queen Who Is Not Really a Villain"
choices.feedback: The Queen of Hearts appears later in the story, not right after the Cheshire Cat, so this section comes too late.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: How are the Cheshire Cat and the Mad Hatter alike in showing the main idea of the article?

choices.text: They both show that Wonderland is a very dangerous place for a young girl.
choices.feedback: A is wrong because the article tells us no one is ever actually harmed in Wonderland — it's weird, not truly dangerous.

choices.text: They both show that Wonderland is a place where normal rules do not work.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the Cheshire Cat vanishes against the rules of nature, and the Mad Hatter ignores the rules of logic, both showing that normal rules don't apply in Wonderland.

choices.text: They both show that animals in Wonderland are much smarter than humans.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because neither character is shown to be smarter than Alice — they mostly say things that don't make sense.

choices.text: They both show that people in Wonderland always have excellent manners.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the tea party shows the opposite of good manners, like offering nonexistent wine and making rude comments about Alice's hair.

syllabus_metadata.title: Curiouser and Curiouser: Alice Meets Wonderland's Characters

assessments.title: Messing About in Boats: The Wind in the Willows

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Wind in the Willows: A Story Born at Bedtime

Up Through the Earth

A mole shoves his tiny paws through the dirt. He has been sweeping, dusting, and whitewashing — painting his walls with a thick white coating to make them bright and clean. Warm air drifts down from above. The smell of fresh grass fills his nose. He scrambles upward, breaks through the soil, and tumbles into the sunshine.

The Mole had been working very hard all the morning, spring-cleaning his little home. First with brooms, then with dusters; then on ladders and steps and chairs, with a brush and a pail of whitewash; till he had dust in his throat and eyes, and splashes of whitewash all over his black fur, and an aching back and weary arms. Spring was moving in the air above and in the earth below and around him, penetrating even his dark and lowly little house with its spirit of divine discontent and longing. It was small wonder, then, that he suddenly flung down his brush on the floor, said "Bother!" and "O blow!" and also "Hang spring-cleaning!" and bolted out of the house without even waiting to put on his coat.
stimulus.content_text:
The Wind in the Willows: A Story Born at BedtimeUp Through the Earth
A mole shoves his tiny paws through the dirt. He has been sweeping, dusting, and whitewashing — painting his walls with a thick white coating to make them bright and clean. Warm air drifts down from above. The smell of fresh grass fills his nose. He scrambles upward, breaks through the soil, and tumbles into the sunshine.


The Mole had been working very hard all the morning, spring-cleaning his little home. First with brooms, then with dusters; then on ladders and steps and chairs, with a brush and a pail of whitewash; till he had dust in his throat and eyes, and splashes of whitewash all over his black fur, and an aching back and weary arms. Spring was moving in the air above and in the earth below and around him, penetrating even his dark and lowly little house with its spirit of divine discontent and longing. It was small wonder, then, that he suddenly flung down his brush on the floor, said "Bother!" and "O blow!" and also "Hang spring-cleaning!" and bolted out of the house without even waiting to put on his coat.

items.prompt: According to the text, what does the Mole do at the end that contrasts with his hard work of spring-cleaning?

choices.text: He decides to clean his dark house even more.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because Mole does the opposite — he stops cleaning and runs away instead of doing even more cleaning.

choices.text: He throws down his brush and runs outside.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage says Mole 'flung down his brush' and 'bolted out of the house,' which means he threw down his brush and ran outside.

choices.text: He puts on his coat to go work in the yard.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage specifically says he left 'without even waiting to put on his coat,' so he did NOT put on his coat.

choices.text: He climbs up a ladder to paint the ceiling.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because climbing a ladder to paint happened earlier in the story, not at the very end when Mole gives up and runs outside.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
A Father's Gift

Grahame first told these stories to his young son, Alastair. Others came in letters Grahame mailed when he traveled. Grahame published The Wind in the Willows in 1908, and the story he had shared at bedtime became a book that children everywhere could enjoy.
stimulus.content_text:
A Father's Gift
Grahame first told these stories to his young son, Alastair. Others came in letters Grahame mailed when he traveled. Grahame published The Wind in the Willows in 1908, and the story he had shared at bedtime became a book that children everywhere could enjoy.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, why were children everywhere able to enjoy the stories about the Wind in the Willows?

choices.text: because Grahame mailed letters to children in many countries
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage says Grahame mailed letters to his son Alastair when he traveled, not to children in many countries.

choices.text: because Grahame published the stories as a book in 1908
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage clearly states that Grahame published the stories as a book in 1908, which is what allowed children everywhere to enjoy them.

choices.text: because Alastair shared the bedtime stories with his friends
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage never mentions Alastair sharing the bedtime stories with his friends.

choices.text: because Grahame traveled to many places to tell the stories
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because even though Grahame traveled, the passage does not say he traveled to tell the stories to children in different places.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Animals Who Act Like People

The story follows animal characters who live along a riverbank in the English countryside — a green, rolling stretch of fields, hedges, and winding rivers far from any big city. They walk on two legs, wear clothes, and live in cozy homes with furniture and fireplaces. Writers call this technique personification — giving human qualities to animals or objects, the way a cartoon dog might talk or a teacup might dance. Grahame's animals live like people, but they also love the wild outdoors. That mix is part of the book's magic.

When Mole reaches the river, he meets Water Rat. "There is nothing, absolutely nothing, half so much worth doing as simply messing about in boats," Rat tells him.
stimulus.content_text:
Animals Who Act Like People
The story follows animal characters who live along a riverbank in the English countryside — a green, rolling stretch of fields, hedges, and winding rivers far from any big city. They walk on two legs, wear clothes, and live in cozy homes with furniture and fireplaces. Writers call this technique personification — giving human qualities to animals or objects, the way a cartoon dog might talk or a teacup might dance. Grahame's animals live like people, but they also love the wild outdoors. That mix is part of the book's magic.

When Mole reaches the river, he meets Water Rat. "There is nothing, absolutely nothing, half so much worth doing as simply messing about in boats," Rat tells him.

items.prompt: According to the passage, what does Mole do before he meets Water Rat?

choices.text: He messes about in boats on the river
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because 'messing about in boats' is something Rat says he loves to do — it's not what Mole does before they meet.

choices.text: He reaches the river in the countryside
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage clearly says 'When Mole reaches the river, he meets Water Rat,' showing that reaching the river comes first.

choices.text: He puts on clothes and walks on two legs
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because walking on two legs and wearing clothes describes all the animal characters in general, not a specific thing Mole does right before meeting Rat.

choices.text: He sits by a fireplace in a cozy home
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage never says Mole was sitting by a fireplace before he met Water Rat.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Four Very Different Friends

Each character has a personality all his own. Mole is timid — shy and easily scared, like a kid on the first day at a new school. He is gentle and eager to learn about the world above ground. He always shows up when his friends need him. Badger lives deep in the Wild Wood. He is wise, strong, and a little grumpy, but he has a good heart. Then there is Toad. Toad of Toad Hall is wealthy, cheerful, and very, very reckless — he rushes into danger without thinking, like someone who jumps off a high dive before checking if the pool has water. Actually, it is the differences between Mole, Rat, Badger, and Toad that make their friendship so interesting.
stimulus.content_text:
Four Very Different Friends
Each character has a personality all his own. Mole is timid — shy and easily scared, like a kid on the first day at a new school. He is gentle and eager to learn about the world above ground. He always shows up when his friends need him. Badger lives deep in the Wild Wood. He is wise, strong, and a little grumpy, but he has a good heart. Then there is Toad. Toad of Toad Hall is wealthy, cheerful, and very, very reckless — he rushes into danger without thinking, like someone who jumps off a high dive before checking if the pool has water. Actually, it is the differences between Mole, Rat, Badger, and Toad that make their friendship so interesting.

items.prompt: The passage compares Toad to 'someone who jumps off a high dive before checking if the pool has water.' How is Toad similar to that person?

choices.text: They are both wealthy and cheerful about danger.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the comparison to the high dive is about acting without thinking, not about being wealthy or cheerful.

choices.text: They both rush into danger without thinking first.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly says Toad 'rushes into danger without thinking,' and the high dive example shows exactly that same risky, thoughtless behavior.

choices.text: They are both shy and easily scared of new things.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because being shy and easily scared describes Mole, not Toad.

choices.text: They both live near water and enjoy swimming.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the pool and high dive are just used as an example to explain recklessness — the passage never says Toad swims or lives near water.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Adventure and Stillness, Side by Side

Some chapters roar with excitement. Toad steals a motorcar. He escapes from prison. He disguises himself as a washerwoman. These wild escapades — daring, sometimes foolish adventures — will make you laugh out loud. Other chapters are quiet and gentle. Grahame paints the feeling of snow falling softly over the Wild Wood. These slower moments are just as important as the exciting ones — they teach you how to pay attention to the world around you — the river, the snow, the friend sitting next to you.
stimulus.content_text:
Adventure and Stillness, Side by Side
Some chapters roar with excitement. Toad steals a motorcar. He escapes from prison. He disguises himself as a washerwoman. These wild escapades — daring, sometimes foolish adventures — will make you laugh out loud. Other chapters are quiet and gentle. Grahame paints the feeling of snow falling softly over the Wild Wood. These slower moments are just as important as the exciting ones — they teach you how to pay attention to the world around you — the river, the snow, the friend sitting next to you.

items.prompt: In the section 'Adventure and Stillness, Side by Side,' the author uses the word escapades and explains it. What does the word escapades mean as defined in this section?

choices.text: quiet moments spent near a river
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because 'quiet moments near a river' describes the calm, peaceful parts of the story, which is the opposite of the wild, exciting escapades.

choices.text: daring, sometimes foolish adventures
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly defines escapades as 'daring, sometimes foolish adventures' right after using the word.

choices.text: clever disguises worn by animals
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because disguises are just one example of something Toad does in his adventures, not the meaning of the word escapades.

choices.text: exciting stories written in chapters
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because 'exciting stories written in chapters' describes the book itself, not the meaning of the word escapades.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: Based on the article, how are Mole's actions different from Toad's actions?

choices.text: Mole does quiet chores at home, while Toad goes on wild and daring adventures.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage shows Mole doing quiet chores like sweeping and whitewashing, while Toad is described as reckless and goes on wild adventures like stealing a motorcar and escaping from prison.

choices.text: Mole likes to steal fast motorcars, while Toad likes to sweep and dust his house.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because it has the characters switched around — the article says Toad is the one who steals a motorcar, and Mole is the one who sweeps and dusts his home.

choices.text: Mole lives deep in the Wild Wood, while Toad lives along the winding riverbank.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the article says Badger, not Mole, is the one who lives deep in the Wild Wood.

choices.text: Mole writes letters to his family, while Toad tells bedtime stories in the dark.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because writing letters and telling bedtime stories are things the author Kenneth Grahame did, not things Mole or Toad did in the story.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: A student wants to find out what caused the author to write the book and what causes the animals' friendship to be so interesting. Which two headings should the student look under to find these causes?

choices.text: "Up Through the Earth" and "Animals Who Act Like People"
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because 'Up Through the Earth' is just about Mole cleaning his house, and 'Animals Who Act Like People' explains personification — neither one tells us why the book was written or why the friendship is interesting.

choices.text: "A Father's Gift" and "Four Very Different Friends"
choices.feedback: B is correct because 'A Father's Gift' explains what caused Grahame to write the book, and 'Four Very Different Friends' explains that the characters' differences are what cause their friendship to be so interesting.

choices.text: "Animals Who Act Like People" and "A Father's Gift"
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because while 'A Father's Gift' is the right heading for why the book was written, 'Animals Who Act Like People' talks about personification and the riverbank setting, not about what makes the friendship interesting.

choices.text: "Four Very Different Friends" and "Up Through the Earth"
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because 'Four Very Different Friends' is the right heading for the friendship question, but 'Up Through the Earth' is just the opening scene with Mole and doesn't explain why the book was written.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: To recount the key details of Mole's story, which sequence of events shows his actions in the correct order?

choices.text: Mole finishes his spring-cleaning, tumbles into the sunshine, and meets Water Rat at the river.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage shows Mole cleaning first, then bursting into the sunshine, and then meeting Water Rat at the river — in that exact order.

choices.text: Mole meets Water Rat at the river, finishes his spring-cleaning, and tumbles into the sunshine.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because Mole meets Water Rat at the river after he comes outside, not before he finishes cleaning.

choices.text: Mole finishes his spring-cleaning, tumbles into the sunshine, and goes back underground for a nap.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the passage never says Mole goes back underground for a nap — that detail was made up.

choices.text: Mole finishes his spring-cleaning, tumbles into the sunshine, and steals a motorcar for an adventure.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because it is Toad, not Mole, who steals a motorcar in the story.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: According to the article "Messing About in Boats," which statement shows how the animals in the story are similar to human beings?

choices.text: They do spring-cleaning and live in homes with furniture.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the article says the animals live in homes with furniture and fireplaces and do spring-cleaning, which are all things humans do.

choices.text: They sleep deep in the woods and eat fresh green grass.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the article never mentions the animals eating grass or sleeping in the woods like wild animals.

choices.text: They build large wooden boats and swim in the winding river.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the passage does not say the animals build boats or spend their time swimming in the river.

choices.text: They hide under the dark dirt and stay away from the sunshine.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because Mole actually rushes up out of the dirt and into the sunshine — he loves being outside, not hiding underground.

syllabus_metadata.title: Messing About in Boats: The Wind in the Willows

assessments.title: Toad's Wild Ride: Adventures and Misadventures

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Toad's Wild Ride: A Story About a Terrible Hero and His Wonderful Friends

"Poop-Poop!" Goes the Horn

This scene comes from a famous novel called The Wind in the Willows, written by Kenneth Grahame over a hundred years ago. A novel is a long story told in chapters. In the book, Grahame describes the moment like this:

They heard a soft humming sound, like a faraway bee. They looked back and saw a small cloud of dust coming toward them very fast. From inside the dust came a faint "Poop-poop!" sound. Then, in a flash, everything changed. A huge motorcar roared past them! It blasted them with wind and dust. They jumped out of the way. For just a second, they saw shiny glass and fancy seats inside the car. Then it was gone, shrinking into a tiny dot far down the road.

Toad sat right down in the middle of the dusty road. He stretched his legs out in front of him and stared after the car. He breathed quickly. He had a calm, happy look on his face. Every now and then he whispered softly, "Poop-poop!"
stimulus.content_text:
Toad's Wild Ride: A Story About a Terrible Hero and His Wonderful Friends"Poop-Poop!" Goes the Horn
This scene comes from a famous novel called The Wind in the Willows, written by Kenneth Grahame over a hundred years ago. A novel is a long story told in chapters. In the book, Grahame describes the moment like this:


They heard a soft humming sound, like a faraway bee. They looked back and saw a small cloud of dust coming toward them very fast. From inside the dust came a faint "Poop-poop!" sound. Then, in a flash, everything changed. A huge motorcar roared past them! It blasted them with wind and dust. They jumped out of the way. For just a second, they saw shiny glass and fancy seats inside the car. Then it was gone, shrinking into a tiny dot far down the road.
Toad sat right down in the middle of the dusty road. He stretched his legs out in front of him and stared after the car. He breathed quickly. He had a calm, happy look on his face. Every now and then he whispered softly, "Poop-poop!"

items.prompt: According to the passage, how was Toad's reaction to the motorcar different from the other characters' reaction?

choices.text: Toad ran away while the others sat still
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because Toad was the one who sat still in the road, while the others jumped out of the way.

choices.text: Toad sat calmly while the others jumped away
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage clearly shows the others jumped away from the car while Toad calmly sat down in the road with a happy look on his face.

choices.text: Toad felt scared while the others felt excited
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because Toad looked calm and happy, not scared — it was the others who reacted with alarm by jumping aside.

choices.text: Toad drove the car while the others watched
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because no one in the passage drove the car — it sped past all the characters, including Toad.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
A Main Character Who Makes Terrible Choices

Toad is selfish, wild, and reckless — that means he does things without thinking about danger. Before motorcars, Toad had been obsessed with a canary-yellow gypsy caravan — a bright, fancy wagon pulled by a horse. Once he spotted a motorcar, though, nothing else mattered to him.
stimulus.content_text:
A Main Character Who Makes Terrible Choices
Toad is selfish, wild, and reckless — that means he does things without thinking about danger. Before motorcars, Toad had been obsessed with a canary-yellow gypsy caravan — a bright, fancy wagon pulled by a horse. Once he spotted a motorcar, though, nothing else mattered to him.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, what caused Toad to stop caring about his gypsy caravan?

choices.text: He lost his pulling horse.
choices.feedback: The passage never says Toad lost his horse — that detail is not in the story.

choices.text: He spotted a motorcar.
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says 'once he spotted a motorcar, nothing else mattered to him,' which tells us the motorcar is what made him stop caring about the caravan.

choices.text: He broke his fancy wagon.
choices.feedback: The passage never says Toad broke his wagon — that never happened in the story.

choices.text: He wanted to be reckless.
choices.feedback: Wanting to be reckless is part of Toad's personality, but the passage does not say that is why he stopped caring about the caravan.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Friends Who Refused to Give Up

Toad's three best friends were Rat, Mole, and Badger. They watched him make bad choices. They worried about him. But they always saw the good in Toad, even when Toad could not see it himself.
stimulus.content_text:
Friends Who Refused to Give Up
Toad's three best friends were Rat, Mole, and Badger. They watched him make bad choices. They worried about him. But they always saw the good in Toad, even when Toad could not see it himself.

items.prompt: According to the passage, what did Toad's friends do after they watched him make bad choices?

choices.text: They stopped talking to Toad
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage never says the friends stopped talking to Toad — they stayed close to him.

choices.text: They worried about him
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage uses the exact words 'They worried about him' right after saying Toad made bad choices.

choices.text: They made bad choices too
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage only says Toad made bad choices, not his friends.

choices.text: They gave up on Toad
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the title even says 'Friends Who Refused to Give Up,' which means they kept believing in Toad.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Toad Faces the Consequences

Toad stole a motorcar that did not belong to him. A judge was angry. Toad was sent to a dark, cold dungeon — an underground jail made of stone. These were the consequences of his choices. Consequences are what happens because of your actions. It is like getting a stomachache after eating too much candy.

But Toad did not stay locked up for long. He wore disguises — costumes that hide who you are. He had one wild adventure after another, all to get back home to Toad Hall.
stimulus.content_text:
Toad Faces the Consequences
Toad stole a motorcar that did not belong to him. A judge was angry. Toad was sent to a dark, cold dungeon — an underground jail made of stone. These were the consequences of his choices. Consequences are what happens because of your actions. It is like getting a stomachache after eating too much candy.

But Toad did not stay locked up for long. He wore disguises — costumes that hide who you are. He had one wild adventure after another, all to get back home to Toad Hall.

items.prompt: How is getting a stomachache after eating too much candy similar to Toad being sent to the dungeon?

choices.text: Both involve being trapped in a dark place
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because a stomachache from eating candy has nothing to do with being trapped in a dark place — that only describes the dungeon.

choices.text: Both are consequences of making a choice
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage explains that consequences are what happens because of your actions, and both the stomachache and the dungeon are examples of consequences from making a choice.

choices.text: Both happen because someone stole something
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because eating too much candy is not stealing — only Toad's situation involved stealing something.

choices.text: Both are punishments given by a judge
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because a stomachache is not a punishment given by a judge; it just happens naturally when you eat too much candy.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
The Battle for Toad Hall

When Toad finally got home, he found a nasty surprise. Weasels and stoats — small, sneaky animals from the Wild Wood — had taken over his house. His friends were ready to help. Rat, Mole, Badger, and Toad crept through a secret tunnel into Toad Hall. They fought a great battle and took it back. Kenneth Grahame hints that Toad behaved better after that — at least for a while.
stimulus.content_text:
The Battle for Toad Hall
When Toad finally got home, he found a nasty surprise. Weasels and stoats — small, sneaky animals from the Wild Wood — had taken over his house. His friends were ready to help. Rat, Mole, Badger, and Toad crept through a secret tunnel into Toad Hall. They fought a great battle and took it back. Kenneth Grahame hints that Toad behaved better after that — at least for a while.

items.prompt: According to the text, what are "stoats"?

choices.text: Small, sneaky animals from the wood
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage says stoats are 'small, sneaky animals from the Wild Wood' — the author explains it right in the sentence!

choices.text: Brave, helpful friends of Mr. Toad
choices.feedback: B is wrong because stoats are the bad guys who took over Toad Hall, not Toad's brave, helpful friends like Rat, Mole, and Badger.

choices.text: Secret, dark tunnels under the hall
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the secret tunnel is something different — it's how Toad and his friends sneaked into the house to fight back.

choices.text: Large, angry creatures in the house
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the passage clearly says stoats are small animals, not large ones.

items.title: Section 6 - Guiding Question 5

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
What the Story Is Really About

The plot is what happens in a story. In this story, the plot includes stolen cars, a prison escape, and a battle with weasels.

But the theme is different from the plot. A theme is the deeper meaning hiding inside the story. The theme of this story is about friendship and loyalty.
stimulus.content_text:
What the Story Is Really About
The plot is what happens in a story. In this story, the plot includes stolen cars, a prison escape, and a battle with weasels.

But the theme is different from the plot. A theme is the deeper meaning hiding inside the story. The theme of this story is about friendship and loyalty.

items.prompt: Which detail from "What the Story Is Really About" best supports the idea that a theme is different from a plot?

choices.text: The plot tells the deeper meaning of the story.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage says the plot is what happens in the story, not the deeper meaning — the deeper meaning belongs to the theme.

choices.text: The story has action events, but its theme is friendship and loyalty.
choices.feedback: B is correct because it shows the difference clearly — the plot has action events like stolen cars and a prison escape, while the theme is the deeper idea of friendship and loyalty.

choices.text: The theme is a list of exciting things that happen.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because a list of exciting things that happen describes the plot, not the theme.

choices.text: The plot and the theme mean exactly the same thing.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage directly tells us that the theme is different from the plot, so they do not mean the same thing.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: Based on the article, how is Toad different from his friends Rat, Mole, and Badger?

choices.text: Toad likes to stay safely at home, but his friends like to go on wild adventures.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because Toad is the reckless one who goes on wild adventures, not the one who stays safely at home.

choices.text: Toad does things without thinking, but his friends worry about him and help him.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the article says Toad acts without thinking about danger, while his friends worry about him and always help him.

choices.text: Toad wants to fight the weasels alone, but his friends want to hide underground.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because all four friends — Toad, Rat, Mole, and Badger — worked together and fought the weasels as a team.

choices.text: Toad cares only about the old caravan, but his friends care about the motorcars.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because Toad forgot all about the caravan once he saw the motorcar — the motorcar is what he became obsessed with.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: Why did Toad get his home back at the end of the story?

choices.text: Because the judge let him keep Toad Hall
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the judge actually punished Toad by sending him to a dungeon — he had nothing to do with getting Toad Hall back.

choices.text: Because his loyal friends helped him fight for it
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage clearly says Rat, Mole, Badger, and Toad crept through a secret tunnel and fought a great battle together to take Toad Hall back from the weasels.

choices.text: Because the motorcar carried him home quickly
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the motorcar was never used to bring Toad home — he escaped from jail by wearing disguises and going on wild adventures.

choices.text: Because the weasels decided to leave on their own
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the weasels and stoats did not leave on their own — Toad and his friends had to fight them in a battle to win the house back.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: Which event from the story best shows the theme of friendship and loyalty?

choices.text: Toad steals a fast motorcar and is sent to a dark, cold dungeon.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because Toad stealing a car and being sent to a dungeon shows the consequences of his bad choices, not friendship or loyalty.

choices.text: Rat, Mole, and Badger creep through a secret tunnel to help Toad.
choices.feedback: This is correct because Rat, Mole, and Badger fighting through a secret tunnel to help Toad get his home back is a perfect example of loyal, caring friendship.

choices.text: Toad puts on a disguise and has wild adventures to get back home.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because Toad escaping in disguises is about his own wild adventures, not about his friends being loyal to him.

choices.text: Toad sits in the dusty road and watches a huge motorcar roar past.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because Toad sitting in the road watching a motorcar shows how obsessed he is with cars, which has nothing to do with friendship or loyalty.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: Based on the article, how is Toad's action with the motorcar similar to the weasels' actions at Toad Hall?

choices.text: They both took something that belonged to someone else.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the article says Toad stole a motorcar and the weasels took over Toad Hall — both took something that belonged to someone else.

choices.text: They both wore sneaky costumes to hide who they were.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because only Toad wore disguises to hide who he was; the article never mentions the weasels wearing any costumes.

choices.text: They both ended up locked inside a cold stone dungeon.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because Toad was the one sent to the dungeon, and the article never says the weasels were locked up.

choices.text: They both fought a great battle to win their home back.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because Toad and his friends — Rat, Mole, and Badger — were the ones who fought the battle to take back Toad Hall, not the weasels.

syllabus_metadata.title: Toad's Wild Ride: Adventures and Misadventures

assessments.title: How Authors Build Worlds: Setting and Imagination in Fiction

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Two Worlds Made of Words

A Girl Falls Down a Rabbit Hole

A deck of playing cards marches like an army. A caterpillar sits on a mushroom, smoking a pipe and asking rude questions. This is Wonderland, the world Lewis Carroll created in Alice's Adventures in Wonderland. Every strange thing you see tells you what kind of place this is.

The place and time where a story happens is called the setting. Think of it like a box that holds everything in the story. Setting is not just a place on a map. It also includes the time of year, the time of day, and even what the weather is like. A story set on a snowy winter night feels very different from one set on a sunny summer morning, even if both happen in the same town.
stimulus.content_text:
Two Worlds Made of WordsA Girl Falls Down a Rabbit Hole
A deck of playing cards marches like an army. A caterpillar sits on a mushroom, smoking a pipe and asking rude questions. This is Wonderland, the world Lewis Carroll created in Alice's Adventures in Wonderland. Every strange thing you see tells you what kind of place this is.

The place and time where a story happens is called the setting. Think of it like a box that holds everything in the story. Setting is not just a place on a map. It also includes the time of year, the time of day, and even what the weather is like. A story set on a snowy winter night feels very different from one set on a sunny summer morning, even if both happen in the same town.

items.prompt: According to the text, how does a story set on a snowy winter night feel compared to one set on a sunny summer morning?

choices.text: They feel the same because they happen in the same town.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage says the two stories feel very different, even when they happen in the same town.

choices.text: They feel very different even if they happen in the same town.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly states that a snowy winter night and a sunny summer morning feel very different, even in the same town.

choices.text: They feel different only if they happen in different towns.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage says the stories feel different even if they are in the same town, so the town doesn't matter.

choices.text: They feel the same because setting is just a place on a map.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage clearly says setting is NOT just a place on a map — it also includes weather, time of day, and time of year.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Carroll's World of Surprise

Carroll built Wonderland in a fun way. He did not write long paragraphs about what things looked like. Instead, he filled the world with wild things happening. You learn about Wonderland the same way Alice does — one weird moment at a time.

Even the dialogue — the words characters say to each other — sounds like nonsense in Wonderland. Characters talk in circles and riddles. Their silly conversations show you that this place has no normal rules. Carroll used action and speech, not long descriptions, to make his world feel real.
stimulus.content_text:
Carroll's World of Surprise
Carroll built Wonderland in a fun way. He did not write long paragraphs about what things looked like. Instead, he filled the world with wild things happening. You learn about Wonderland the same way Alice does — one weird moment at a time.

Even the dialogue — the words characters say to each other — sounds like nonsense in Wonderland. Characters talk in circles and riddles. Their silly conversations show you that this place has no normal rules. Carroll used action and speech, not long descriptions, to make his world feel real.

items.prompt: According to the passage, what causes the reader to realize that Wonderland has no normal rules?

choices.text: The characters having silly conversations
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage directly says the characters' silly conversations 'show you that this place has no normal rules.'

choices.text: The author writing very long paragraphs
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the passage says Carroll did NOT write long paragraphs — he used action and speech instead.

choices.text: The reader learning things before Alice
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the passage says readers learn about Wonderland the same way Alice does, not before her.

choices.text: The story having normal, quiet moments
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the passage describes Wonderland as full of 'wild things happening,' not normal or quiet moments.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Grahame's World of Feeling

Kenneth Grahame did something very different in The Wind in the Willows. He used sensory details — words that help you see, hear, smell, taste, or touch what the characters feel. When Mole first comes above ground, Grahame describes warm sun on fur, a soft breeze, and birds singing. You can almost hear water splashing against the bank.

The story moves from a warm, sunny riverbank to a dark, freezing forest in winter. The cold, the shadows, and the scary sounds of the forest are not just background. They make Mole feel very scared. He panics, and Rat has to rescue him. The setting pushed the story forward, like a hand on a character's back.

Grahame also used warm, friendly dialogue between his characters. Their happy conversations made the riverbank feel cozy and safe. Compare that to Carroll's silly nonsense talk, and you can see how dialogue helps set the mood of a story.
stimulus.content_text:
Grahame's World of Feeling
Kenneth Grahame did something very different in The Wind in the Willows. He used sensory details — words that help you see, hear, smell, taste, or touch what the characters feel. When Mole first comes above ground, Grahame describes warm sun on fur, a soft breeze, and birds singing. You can almost hear water splashing against the bank.

The story moves from a warm, sunny riverbank to a dark, freezing forest in winter. The cold, the shadows, and the scary sounds of the forest are not just background. They make Mole feel very scared. He panics, and Rat has to rescue him. The setting pushed the story forward, like a hand on a character's back.

Grahame also used warm, friendly dialogue between his characters. Their happy conversations made the riverbank feel cozy and safe. Compare that to Carroll's silly nonsense talk, and you can see how dialogue helps set the mood of a story.

items.prompt: According to the section "Grahame's World of Feeling," where does the story move after the warm, sunny riverbank?

choices.text: To a cozy, safe underground
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage does not mention a cozy underground place in this section — that detail simply isn't there.

choices.text: To a dark, freezing forest
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly says the story moves 'from a warm, sunny riverbank to a dark, freezing forest in winter.'

choices.text: To a loud, splashing river
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the splashing water is described at the start near the riverbank, not as a new place the story travels to.

choices.text: To a silly, nonsense world
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because silly nonsense talk is used to describe Carroll's writing style, not a place in Grahame's story.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Why Authors Choose Every Detail

When authors create a setting, they build a world from scratch. They choose the weather, the sounds, the buildings, and the time period. A spooky story might have a dark forest and a creaky old house. A happy story might have a sunny meadow and a cozy kitchen. These details tell you what kind of story you are reading before anyone even speaks.

Carroll wanted a silly, upside-down world, so he used surprise and impossible events. Grahame wanted a world that felt as real as your own backyard, so he used lots of sensory details. The kind of setting an author builds matches the kind of story the author wants to tell.
stimulus.content_text:
Why Authors Choose Every Detail
When authors create a setting, they build a world from scratch. They choose the weather, the sounds, the buildings, and the time period. A spooky story might have a dark forest and a creaky old house. A happy story might have a sunny meadow and a cozy kitchen. These details tell you what kind of story you are reading before anyone even speaks.

Carroll wanted a silly, upside-down world, so he used surprise and impossible events. Grahame wanted a world that felt as real as your own backyard, so he used lots of sensory details. The kind of setting an author builds matches the kind of story the author wants to tell.

items.prompt: According to the passage, how are the authors Carroll and Grahame similar?

choices.text: They both include surprises and impossible events.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because only Carroll uses surprises and impossible events — the passage does not say Grahame does this.

choices.text: They both build settings that match their stories.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage tells us both Carroll and Grahame built settings that matched the kind of story they wanted to tell.

choices.text: They both write about dark forests and old houses.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because dark forests and old houses are mentioned as examples of spooky stories, not as something Carroll or Grahame wrote about.

choices.text: They both create worlds that feel like a backyard.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because only Grahame created a world that felt like a real backyard — Carroll's world was silly and upside-down.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
A World Waiting on the Page

Listen for sounds the author puts in your ears. Watch how the setting changes from chapter to chapter. Notice how characters feel when it does. A rabbit hole and a riverbank have nothing in common, except this: a writer chose every single detail to put you there. The best settings do not just surround a story. They become part of it.
stimulus.content_text:
A World Waiting on the Page
Listen for sounds the author puts in your ears. Watch how the setting changes from chapter to chapter. Notice how characters feel when it does. A rabbit hole and a riverbank have nothing in common, except this: a writer chose every single detail to put you there. The best settings do not just surround a story. They become part of it.

items.prompt: According to the text, how does the author define the best settings?

choices.text: They are places that change in every single chapter.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage mentions settings changing from chapter to chapter, but it never says the best settings must change in every single chapter.

choices.text: They are places that become a part of the story.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly states that the best settings 'become part of' the story, not just a background around it.

choices.text: They are places that include a hole and a river.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the rabbit hole and riverbank are just two examples the author uses, not the definition of what makes a setting great.

choices.text: They are places that put loud sounds in your ears.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because sounds are mentioned as one detail a writer can use, but the passage never says loud sounds are what define the best settings.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: Based on the article, how is the dialogue in Lewis Carroll's story different from the dialogue in Kenneth Grahame's story?

choices.text: Carroll's characters speak in riddles, but Grahame's characters have friendly chats.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the article directly says Carroll's characters speak in 'circles and riddles' as nonsense, while Grahame's characters have warm, friendly conversations.

choices.text: Carroll's characters tell scary stories, but Grahame's characters share funny jokes.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the article never says Carroll's characters tell scary stories or that Grahame's characters share funny jokes — those details are not in the passage.

choices.text: Carroll's characters use very long words, but Grahame's characters use short words.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the article never compares the length of words used by characters in either story.

choices.text: Carroll's characters talk about the weather, but Grahame's characters talk about rules.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the article never mentions characters in either story talking about the weather or about rules.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: According to the article, what effect does a changing setting, like a dark and freezing forest, have on a story?

choices.text: It pushes the story forward by changing how the characters feel.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the article says the dark, freezing forest made Mole feel scared and panic, showing that a changing setting pushes the story forward by changing how characters feel.

choices.text: It forces the characters to speak in silly riddles and nonsense.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because silly riddles and nonsense dialogue are part of Wonderland, not something caused by a changing setting like a dark forest.

choices.text: It requires the author to write long paragraphs about the map.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the article says Carroll did NOT write long paragraphs describing things — he used wild events and action instead.

choices.text: It makes the reader learn about the world using only magic.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the article never mentions magic as a way readers learn about the world in either story.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: According to the article, in what order does a reader usually experience a story's setting?

choices.text: First you learn what kind of story it is from details, and next you watch the setting change.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the article says setting details tell you what kind of story you are reading before anyone speaks, and then the setting keeps changing to push the story forward.

choices.text: First you read long paragraphs that describe the story's map, and next you hear characters talk normally.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the article says Carroll did NOT use long paragraphs to describe his world, and the dialogue in Wonderland is silly nonsense, not normal talk.

choices.text: First you see the characters panic in a dark forest, and next you read about a warm riverbank.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because it puts the events backwards — the warm riverbank comes first in the story, and the dark forest comes later.

choices.text: First you learn all the normal rules of the new place, and next you figure out the weather.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the article never says readers learn all the rules of a place first, and Wonderland actually has no normal rules at all.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
