items.prompt: What do the headings "Carroll's World of Surprise" and "Grahame's World of Feeling" tell the reader about how the two stories compare?

choices.text: One story is full of unexpected surprises, while the other focuses on characters' emotions.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the headings directly signal that Carroll's story is built on surprising, impossible events, while Grahame's story is built on feelings and sensory details that connect to characters' emotions.

choices.text: One story takes place in a sunny meadow, while the other takes place in a dark freezing forest.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because those are just two specific settings mentioned in the passage — they don't explain what the headings are telling us about how the two stories compare overall.

choices.text: One story uses long paragraphs to describe things, while the other uses short conversations.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because it gets it backwards — the passage actually says Carroll did NOT use long paragraphs, but this choice doesn't match what the headings 'Surprise' and 'Feeling' are telling us.

choices.text: One story is about a real place on a map, while the other is about a completely fake place.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the headings say nothing about real versus fake places — they are comparing the style and mood of the two stories, not whether the settings exist in real life.

syllabus_metadata.title: How Authors Build Worlds: Setting and Imagination in Fiction

assessments.title: Gods of the North: Odin, Thor, and the World of the Norse

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Gods Behind the Thunder: Norse Myths

A Tree That Held the Universe

In the cold, dark lands of northern Europe, every crack of thunder had a story behind it. The grandmother speaks: "That is Thor, swinging his hammer at the giants again."

It was a god protecting the world. Before science could explain thunder, winter, or the stars, myths gave people answers. These stories are called Norse myths — traditional tales told by the Scandinavian people, meaning the people who lived in present-day Norway, Sweden, and Denmark.

Norse myths grew out of a harsh, freezing place. Long winters. Icy seas. Dark skies for months. So Norse stories are full of ice giants, bitter cold, and heroes who had to be brave just to survive. Myths from warmer places, like ancient Greece, look different. Each culture's stories match the world that culture lived in.
stimulus.content_text:
The Gods Behind the Thunder: Norse MythsA Tree That Held the Universe
In the cold, dark lands of northern Europe, every crack of thunder had a story behind it. The grandmother speaks: "That is Thor, swinging his hammer at the giants again."

It was a god protecting the world. Before science could explain thunder, winter, or the stars, myths gave people answers. These stories are called Norse myths — traditional tales told by the Scandinavian people, meaning the people who lived in present-day Norway, Sweden, and Denmark.

Norse myths grew out of a harsh, freezing place. Long winters. Icy seas. Dark skies for months. So Norse stories are full of ice giants, bitter cold, and heroes who had to be brave just to survive. Myths from warmer places, like ancient Greece, look different. Each culture's stories match the world that culture lived in.

items.prompt: According to the text, how are Norse myths different from myths from warmer places?

choices.text: They are full of ice giants and bitter cold.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage says Norse stories are 'full of ice giants, bitter cold' due to the freezing world Norse people lived in, which made their myths different from those in warmer places.

choices.text: They are full of thunder and bright stars.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because while thunder is mentioned in the passage, it is not described as what makes Norse myths different from myths in warmer places.

choices.text: They are full of heroes and brave people.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because heroes appear in many kinds of myths, not just Norse ones, so having heroes is not what makes Norse myths different.

choices.text: They are full of stories from Greece and Denmark.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because Greece and Denmark are from different cultures with different myths, and the passage never groups their stories together.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Nine Worlds on One Giant Tree

At the heart of Norse mythology — a whole collection of myths that fit together like pieces of one big puzzle — stood a giant ash tree called Yggdrasil (say "IG-druh-sill"). The Norse believed this enormous tree held the entire universe together, the way a trunk holds up its branches. Everything in the Norse universe was connected through this one mighty tree.
stimulus.content_text:
Nine Worlds on One Giant Tree
At the heart of Norse mythology — a whole collection of myths that fit together like pieces of one big puzzle — stood a giant ash tree called Yggdrasil (say "IG-druh-sill"). The Norse believed this enormous tree held the entire universe together, the way a trunk holds up its branches. Everything in the Norse universe was connected through this one mighty tree.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, what caused the entire Norse universe to stay together?

choices.text: A giant tree that held it up like branches.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage directly says Yggdrasil, the giant ash tree, held the entire Norse universe together like a trunk holds up its branches.

choices.text: A large puzzle that locked the worlds in place.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the puzzle in the passage is used to describe how the myths fit together, not something that locked the worlds in place.

choices.text: A group of myths that tied the pieces together.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the myths are the stories the Norse people believed, not the force that held the universe together.

choices.text: A mighty forest that grew all around the edges.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the passage only talks about one single mighty tree, Yggdrasil, and never mentions a forest around the edges.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Odin: The One-Eyed God of Wisdom

The most powerful Norse god was Odin, chief of all the gods. Odin wanted to know everything — every secret, every hidden truth. His hunger for knowledge was so strong that he sacrificed one of his own eyes. To sacrifice means to give up something precious on purpose. Odin gave his eye so he could drink from the Well of Wisdom. After that, he could see things no one else could see.

Writers have long tried to capture Odin's majesty in words. The passage below comes from an older retelling of the myths. Note that when it calls Odin "the personification of the sky," most scholars today describe him differently — as the god of wisdom, war, and poetry, and as the Allfather of gods and humans.

Odin, the greatest and most divine of all the gods, was the personification of the sky, and the All-father of gods and men. He was the god of wisdom, poetry, and war. His most famous attributes were his gray cloak, his broad-brimmed hat drawn down to conceal his missing eye, and his spear Gungnir, which never missed its mark. He rode through the skies upon his eight-footed steed Sleipnir, and his two ravens flew out each day across the wide world, returning to whisper all they had seen and heard into his ear.

Those ravens' names mean "Thought" and "Memory." Every day, they flew across all nine worlds and brought back news.
stimulus.content_text:
Odin: The One-Eyed God of Wisdom
The most powerful Norse god was Odin, chief of all the gods. Odin wanted to know everything — every secret, every hidden truth. His hunger for knowledge was so strong that he sacrificed one of his own eyes. To sacrifice means to give up something precious on purpose. Odin gave his eye so he could drink from the Well of Wisdom. After that, he could see things no one else could see.

Writers have long tried to capture Odin's majesty in words. The passage below comes from an older retelling of the myths. Note that when it calls Odin "the personification of the sky," most scholars today describe him differently — as the god of wisdom, war, and poetry, and as the Allfather of gods and humans.


Odin, the greatest and most divine of all the gods, was the personification of the sky, and the All-father of gods and men. He was the god of wisdom, poetry, and war. His most famous attributes were his gray cloak, his broad-brimmed hat drawn down to conceal his missing eye, and his spear Gungnir, which never missed its mark. He rode through the skies upon his eight-footed steed Sleipnir, and his two ravens flew out each day across the wide world, returning to whisper all they had seen and heard into his ear.


Those ravens' names mean "Thought" and "Memory." Every day, they flew across all nine worlds and brought back news.

items.prompt: According to the text, what happened right after Odin gave up one of his eyes?

choices.text: He rode across the sky on Sleipnir.
choices.feedback: Riding Sleipnir through the sky is something Odin did, but the passage never says it happened right after he gave up his eye.

choices.text: He drank from the Well of Wisdom.
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says Odin gave his eye 'so he could drink from the Well of Wisdom,' meaning that drinking from the well happened right after.

choices.text: He sent his ravens across the worlds.
choices.feedback: Sending his ravens out each day is something Odin did regularly, but the passage doesn't connect it to the moment he gave up his eye.

choices.text: He hid his eye under a broad hat.
choices.feedback: Odin did wear a hat to hide his missing eye, but that detail comes from a description of how he looked — not from what happened right after he made his sacrifice.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Thor: The Hammer-Swinging Protector

Odin's son Thor was the god of thunder and the strongest of all the gods. Thor carried a mighty hammer called Mjolnir (say "MYOL-neer"). When he threw it, the hammer always hit its target and flew right back to his hand. When Thor swung Mjolnir, lightning flashed and thunder boomed. The Norse believed every thunderstorm meant Thor was battling giants who threatened the world. While Odin was wise and serious, Thor was bold and full of energy.
stimulus.content_text:
Thor: The Hammer-Swinging Protector
Odin's son Thor was the god of thunder and the strongest of all the gods. Thor carried a mighty hammer called Mjolnir (say "MYOL-neer"). When he threw it, the hammer always hit its target and flew right back to his hand. When Thor swung Mjolnir, lightning flashed and thunder boomed. The Norse believed every thunderstorm meant Thor was battling giants who threatened the world. While Odin was wise and serious, Thor was bold and full of energy.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, how does the author compare Thor and Odin?

choices.text: By explaining that Thor was bold and energetic, while Odin was wise and serious.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage uses the exact words 'wise and serious' for Odin and 'bold and full of energy' for Thor when comparing them.

choices.text: By explaining that Thor was wise and serious, while Odin was bold and energetic.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because it has the descriptions backwards — the passage says Thor was bold and energetic, not Odin.

choices.text: By explaining that Thor threw a magical hammer, while Odin fought giant monsters.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never says Odin fought giants — it says the Norse people believed Thor battled giants.

choices.text: By explaining that Thor created bright lightning, while Odin made the loud thunder.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says Thor's hammer caused both lightning and thunder, and it never mentions Odin making thunder.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Why These Stories Lasted

The Norse people took their myths seriously. Warriors who died bravely in battle hoped to reach Valhalla — Odin's great golden hall, like a palace for heroes. There, they would feast and fight alongside the gods forever.

Norse myths are still with us today in a surprising place: your weekly calendar. Wednesday comes from "Woden's day" — Woden was another name for Odin. Thursday comes from "Thor's day." So every Wednesday and Thursday, your calendar is quietly telling a Norse myth.
stimulus.content_text:
Why These Stories Lasted
The Norse people took their myths seriously. Warriors who died bravely in battle hoped to reach Valhalla — Odin's great golden hall, like a palace for heroes. There, they would feast and fight alongside the gods forever.

Norse myths are still with us today in a surprising place: your weekly calendar. Wednesday comes from "Woden's day" — Woden was another name for Odin. Thursday comes from "Thor's day." So every Wednesday and Thursday, your calendar is quietly telling a Norse myth.

items.prompt: According to the text, what is Valhalla?

choices.text: a golden crown worn by Odin
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage says Valhalla is a great golden hall, not a crown.

choices.text: a great golden hall for heroes
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage describes Valhalla as 'Odin's great golden hall' where heroes feast and fight forever.

choices.text: a day of the week named for Thor
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because Thursday is the day named for Thor — Valhalla is a place, not a day of the week.

choices.text: a brave warrior who fought in battle
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because Valhalla is the special hall that brave warriors hoped to go to, not a warrior itself.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: Based on the article, how is Thor different from Odin?

choices.text: Thor is a god who lives in Valhalla, but Odin does not
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because Valhalla is described as a hall for brave warriors who died in battle, not as Thor's home.

choices.text: Thor is bold and full of energy, but Odin is wise and serious
choices.feedback: B is correct because the article states directly that 'Odin was wise and serious' while 'Thor was bold and full of energy,' showing their key difference.

choices.text: Thor uses ravens to learn secrets, but Odin uses a hammer
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because it has it backwards — Odin is the one whose ravens bring him secrets, and Thor is the one who uses a hammer.

choices.text: Thor wants to know everything, but Odin wants to fight giants
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because it is also backwards — the article says Odin wanted to know everything, and Thor is the one who battles giants.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: Why does the author first describe the harsh, freezing lands and then tell about brave gods like Thor and Odin?

choices.text: to show that the myths were written down before science existed
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage never says anything about the myths being written down before science — it only says myths gave people answers before science could explain things.

choices.text: to show that the tough world caused people to create tough heroes
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly connects the harsh, freezing world to the creation of brave, tough heroes, using the word 'so' to show that one caused the other.

choices.text: to show that Thor and Odin caused the cold weather in the North
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because Thor and Odin are characters inside the myths — the passage never says they actually caused the cold weather in real life.

choices.text: to show that all myths from every culture have the same stories
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage actually says the opposite — it explains that myths from different places look different because each culture's stories match the world they lived in.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: Which choice best recounts the main ideas of the article in the order they are told?

choices.text: First, it explains why the people created myths, next it describes the gods and their world, and finally it shows how the stories lasted.
choices.feedback: A is correct because it follows the article's real order: why myths were made, who the gods were and their world, and then how the stories live on today.

choices.text: First, it shows how the stories lasted, next it explains why the people created myths, and finally it describes the gods and their world.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because it puts the ending — how the stories lasted — first, but that part actually comes at the end of the article.

choices.text: First, it explains how the gods created winter, next it describes the humans fighting the giants, and finally it shows how the universe fell apart.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the article never says the gods created winter or that the universe fell apart — those things just didn't happen in the story.

choices.text: First, it explains how science answers our questions, next it describes the weather in northern Europe, and finally it shows how to read a calendar.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the article says myths came before science to explain things, and the calendar is only a small detail at the very end, not a main idea.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: Which detail shows a way Odin and Thor are alike in Norse myths?

choices.text: Both are linked to things people notice in the sky.
choices.feedback: A is correct because Odin rode through the skies and Thor controlled thunder and lightning, so both gods were connected to things people noticed in the sky.

choices.text: Both gave up something precious to gain power.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because only Odin sacrificed something precious — his eye — to gain wisdom; the passage never says Thor gave up anything.

choices.text: Both are remembered only in the names of weekdays.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the passage shows both gods are remembered in myths, stories, and other ways, not only in the names of weekdays.

choices.text: Both traveled daily through all nine worlds.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because it was Odin's two ravens, not both gods, that traveled through the worlds each day to bring back news.

syllabus_metadata.title: Gods of the North: Odin, Thor, and the World of the Norse

assessments.title: The Trickster and the Gentle God: Loki and Balder

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Trickster and the Bright God: A Norse Myth of Loki and Balder

A Tiny Plant That Killed a God

A small green sprig of mistletoe — a plant so tiny it grows by clinging to tree branches — once brought down the most beloved god in the world. That is how the Norse people told it, and they believed every word.

You might think that all mythology comes from ancient Greece. But mythology — the collection of sacred stories a group of people tells to explain the world — belongs to many cultures. The Norse myths come from the Vikings and other peoples of Scandinavia, a cold, mountainous part of northern Europe. They used myths to explain why seasons changed, why storms raged, and what would happen when the world ended.

Two of their most famous characters were Loki and Balder. Their story is one of the saddest ever told.
stimulus.content_text:
The Trickster and the Bright God: A Norse Myth of Loki and BalderA Tiny Plant That Killed a God
A small green sprig of mistletoe — a plant so tiny it grows by clinging to tree branches — once brought down the most beloved god in the world. That is how the Norse people told it, and they believed every word.

You might think that all mythology comes from ancient Greece. But mythology — the collection of sacred stories a group of people tells to explain the world — belongs to many cultures. The Norse myths come from the Vikings and other peoples of Scandinavia, a cold, mountainous part of northern Europe. They used myths to explain why seasons changed, why storms raged, and what would happen when the world ended.

Two of their most famous characters were Loki and Balder. Their story is one of the saddest ever told.

items.prompt: According to the passage, Norse myths come from Scandinavia, but where does the author say many people think all mythology comes from?

choices.text: ancient Rome
choices.feedback: Ancient Rome is incorrect because the passage never mentions Rome anywhere.

choices.text: ancient Greece
choices.feedback: Ancient Greece is correct because the passage says 'You might think that all mythology comes from ancient Greece,' showing that is what many people believe.

choices.text: northern Europe
choices.feedback: Northern Europe is incorrect because that is where Norse myths come from, not where people think all mythology comes from.

choices.text: the Viking lands
choices.feedback: The Viking lands is incorrect because Vikings are connected to Norse myths specifically, not to where people think all mythology comes from.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Loki: Not Good, Not Evil, Just Trouble

Loki was a shape-shifter — someone who could change form the way you change clothes. One moment he buzzed around a room as a fly. The next, he darted through a river as a salmon. He could gallop across a field as a horse. No other god had this power.

It's easy to think of Loki as a villain, but Norse myths often have characters who are not purely good or purely bad. He helped the gods escape danger many times. His father was a giant, though, so the gods never fully trusted him. He and Odin, the chief god, shared a special bond as blood brothers. But because of his giant blood, the other gods always kept one eye on him.

Still, Loki loved causing trouble. Some of his tricks made the gods laugh. Others caused real harm. And as years passed, his tricks grew darker.
stimulus.content_text:
Loki: Not Good, Not Evil, Just Trouble
Loki was a shape-shifter — someone who could change form the way you change clothes. One moment he buzzed around a room as a fly. The next, he darted through a river as a salmon. He could gallop across a field as a horse. No other god had this power.

It's easy to think of Loki as a villain, but Norse myths often have characters who are not purely good or purely bad. He helped the gods escape danger many times. His father was a giant, though, so the gods never fully trusted him. He and Odin, the chief god, shared a special bond as blood brothers. But because of his giant blood, the other gods always kept one eye on him.

Still, Loki loved causing trouble. Some of his tricks made the gods laugh. Others caused real harm. And as years passed, his tricks grew darker.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, why did the gods never fully trust Loki?

choices.text: Because Loki could change into many forms
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage says shape-shifting was Loki's special power, but it never says that's why the gods didn't trust him.

choices.text: Because Loki's father was a giant
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly says 'His father was a giant, though, so the gods never fully trusted him.'

choices.text: Because Odin wanted the gods to watch him
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because Odin was Loki's blood brother and had a special bond with him — the passage doesn't say Odin told the gods to watch Loki.

choices.text: Because Loki's tricks made the gods laugh
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the gods laughing at Loki's tricks shows they sometimes enjoyed them, which is not a reason to distrust someone.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Balder: The God Everyone Loved

Balder was Loki's opposite. Everyone loved him — gods, humans, even animals and plants. His mother was Frigg, queen of the gods. Frigg could sometimes see into the future, and what she saw terrified her.

So Frigg traveled across the entire world. She asked every single thing to promise never to hurt Balder. Every metal, every stone, every animal gave its word. Balder seemed perfectly safe.

But Frigg skipped one thing. She passed right by the tiny mistletoe plant. It looked too small and too weak to matter.

That was the mistake that changed everything.
stimulus.content_text:
Balder: The God Everyone Loved
Balder was Loki's opposite. Everyone loved him — gods, humans, even animals and plants. His mother was Frigg, queen of the gods. Frigg could sometimes see into the future, and what she saw terrified her.

So Frigg traveled across the entire world. She asked every single thing to promise never to hurt Balder. Every metal, every stone, every animal gave its word. Balder seemed perfectly safe.

But Frigg skipped one thing. She passed right by the tiny mistletoe plant. It looked too small and too weak to matter.

That was the mistake that changed everything.

items.prompt: According to the passage, what did Frigg do right after she saw something terrifying in the future?

choices.text: She skipped over the mistletoe plant
choices.feedback: Skipping the mistletoe happened later during her travels — it was not the first thing she did after seeing the scary future.

choices.text: She traveled across the entire world
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage tells us that right after Frigg saw something terrifying, she traveled across the entire world to keep Balder safe.

choices.text: She asked Balder to protect himself
choices.feedback: The passage never says Frigg asked Balder to protect himself — that didn't happen in the story at all.

choices.text: She told the other gods about her fear
choices.feedback: The passage doesn't mention Frigg telling the other gods about her fear — she went straight to traveling the world instead.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
The Darkest Day

Loki, always searching for trouble, discovered Frigg's secret. Then he carried it to where the gods were playing a game — tossing things at Balder and laughing as each object bounced off without leaving a scratch.

Hodr, Balder's blind brother, stood nearby. He couldn't play because he couldn't see. Loki placed the mistletoe dart in Hodr's hand and aimed it for him. Hodr threw. The dart struck Balder, and the brightest god fell dead.

It was the saddest day in all of Norse mythology.
stimulus.content_text:
The Darkest Day
Loki, always searching for trouble, discovered Frigg's secret. Then he carried it to where the gods were playing a game — tossing things at Balder and laughing as each object bounced off without leaving a scratch.

Hodr, Balder's blind brother, stood nearby. He couldn't play because he couldn't see. Loki placed the mistletoe dart in Hodr's hand and aimed it for him. Hodr threw. The dart struck Balder, and the brightest god fell dead.

It was the saddest day in all of Norse mythology.

items.prompt: How are Loki and Hodr similar in what happens when the mistletoe dart is thrown at Balder?

choices.text: They both feel sad about what happens to Balder
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage never says Loki or Hodr felt sad — Loki was actually stirring up trouble on purpose.

choices.text: They both play a part in causing Balder to fall
choices.feedback: B is correct because Loki aimed the dart and placed it in Hodr's hand, while Hodr threw it, so they both played a part in causing Balder to fall.

choices.text: They both know the secret about the mistletoe
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because only Loki discovered Frigg's secret about the mistletoe — Hodr didn't know it.

choices.text: They both want to join the gods' throwing game
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because Hodr couldn't play the throwing game because he was blind, and the passage doesn't say Loki wanted to join the game either.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
The End — and a New Beginning

The Norse people believed Balder's death set something terrible in motion: Ragnarok, the final battle at the end of the world. Ragnarok means "doom of the gods." In this last fight, the gods and the giants would clash. Fire and floods would destroy everything.

The Norse believed a new, better world would rise from the ashes.

Think about that the next time you see mistletoe hanging in a doorway during the holidays. Somewhere behind that little plant sits one of the oldest, saddest stories humans ever told — a reminder that even the smallest thing can carry the greatest power.
stimulus.content_text:
The End — and a New Beginning
The Norse people believed Balder's death set something terrible in motion: Ragnarok, the final battle at the end of the world. Ragnarok means "doom of the gods." In this last fight, the gods and the giants would clash. Fire and floods would destroy everything.

The Norse believed a new, better world would rise from the ashes.

Think about that the next time you see mistletoe hanging in a doorway during the holidays. Somewhere behind that little plant sits one of the oldest, saddest stories humans ever told — a reminder that even the smallest thing can carry the greatest power.

items.prompt: According to the text, what does the word "Ragnarok" mean?

choices.text: The doom of the gods
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage directly states 'Ragnarok means doom of the gods.'

choices.text: A new and better world
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because 'a new and better world' is what the Norse believed would come after Ragnarok, not what the word means.

choices.text: A very old, sad story
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because 'a very old, sad story' describes the tale about Balder, not the meaning of the word Ragnarok.

choices.text: The fire and the floods
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because fire and floods are things that happen during Ragnarok, not the definition of the word.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: What is the main lesson of this text?

choices.text: A very tiny thing can carry great power.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the tiny mistletoe plant caused the death of a god, and the passage directly tells us that 'even the smallest thing can carry the greatest power.'

choices.text: A very good person can do awful things.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the story is about Loki, a tricky troublemaker, not a good person who does something awful.

choices.text: A very smart god can make silly choices.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the passage never shows a smart god making silly choices — that idea doesn't match what the story is about.

choices.text: A very fun game can start angry fights.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because even though a game is happening when Balder is hurt, the story's lesson is about the power of small things, not about games causing fights.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: Based on the passage, why was Loki able to use the mistletoe to hurt Balder?

choices.text: Because Frigg decided the plant was too small to ask for a promise.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage says Frigg skipped the mistletoe plant, thinking it was too small to matter, so it never made a promise not to hurt Balder.

choices.text: Because Hodr was blind and could not see the dangerous plant he threw.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because Hodr's blindness explains how Loki tricked someone into throwing the dart, but it doesn't explain why the mistletoe could hurt Balder in the first place.

choices.text: Because the gods were playing a game and forgot to protect him from it.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the gods weren't forgetting to protect Balder — they just didn't know Frigg had missed asking the mistletoe for a promise.

choices.text: Because Loki used his magic powers to turn himself into the tiny plant.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage never says Loki turned into the mistletoe — he simply found the real plant and used it as a dart.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: Which event happened right before Balder fell dead?

choices.text: Frigg asked the world to protect Balder
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because Frigg asking the world to protect Balder happened long before Balder died — it was one of the first things in the story.

choices.text: Hodr threw the mistletoe dart
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage says Hodr threw the mistletoe dart, and right after that, Balder fell dead — making it the event that happened right before.

choices.text: Loki learned Frigg's secret
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because Loki learning Frigg's secret happened before the game, not right before Balder fell.

choices.text: The gods laughed at Loki's tricks
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the gods laughed when things bounced off Balder during the game, not specifically at Loki's tricks.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: Based on the passage, how do Loki's actions in the section "Loki: Not Good, Not Evil, Just Trouble" compare to his actions in "The Darkest Day"?

choices.text: Earlier he plays tricks that cause laughter, but later he causes the saddest death.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage says Loki's early tricks made gods laugh, but later he caused Balder's death, which was the saddest day in Norse mythology.

choices.text: Earlier he uses magic to help the gods, but later he uses magic to help the giants.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because the passage never says Loki uses magic to help the giants — in fact, he helps the gods escape danger in the earlier section.

choices.text: Earlier he hides from the wild animals, but later he hides from the blind god Hodr.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because Loki never hides from wild animals or from Hodr anywhere in the passage.

choices.text: Earlier he changes his shape to run away, but later he changes his shape to fight.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because the passage never shows Loki changing his shape to run away or to fight — his shape-shifting is mentioned as a special power, but not used in those ways in the story.

syllabus_metadata.title: The Trickster and the Gentle God: Loki and Balder

assessments.title: Gods Around the World: Comparing Norse and Greek Myths

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Same Thunder, Different Gods: How Norse and Greek Myths Match

A Crash in the Sky

Thunder shakes your house and the sky flashes white. Now imagine you live thousands of years ago, with no science books or weather reports. You look up and think: someone powerful must be doing that. In ancient Greece, people believed a god named Zeus was hurling lightning bolts from the top of Mount Olympus. Far away in Scandinavia — the cold, icy lands we now call Norway, Sweden, and Denmark — people believed a god named Thor was swinging his mighty hammer across the clouds.

These two groups of people lived thousands of miles apart. They never met. Neither group copied the other's stories. Yet their myths — the sacred stories a culture tells to explain how the world works — sound surprisingly alike.
stimulus.content_text:
Same Thunder, Different Gods: How Norse and Greek Myths MatchA Crash in the Sky
Thunder shakes your house and the sky flashes white. Now imagine you live thousands of years ago, with no science books or weather reports. You look up and think: someone powerful must be doing that. In ancient Greece, people believed a god named Zeus was hurling lightning bolts from the top of Mount Olympus. Far away in Scandinavia — the cold, icy lands we now call Norway, Sweden, and Denmark — people believed a god named Thor was swinging his mighty hammer across the clouds.
These two groups of people lived thousands of miles apart. They never met. Neither group copied the other's stories. Yet their myths — the sacred stories a culture tells to explain how the world works — sound surprisingly alike.

items.prompt: According to the text, what is one difference between what the Greeks believed and what the people of Scandinavia believed?

choices.text: Greeks believed Zeus swung a hammer, but Scandinavians believed Thor threw lightning bolts.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because it has the details switched around — it was Zeus who threw lightning bolts and Thor who swung a hammer, not the other way around.

choices.text: Greeks believed Zeus threw lightning bolts, but Scandinavians believed Thor swung a hammer.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage clearly states Zeus threw lightning bolts and Thor swung his hammer, which are two different things.

choices.text: Greeks believed Zeus lived in a cold land, but Scandinavians believed Thor lived on a mountain.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage never says anything about where Zeus or Thor lived in the way this answer describes.

choices.text: Greeks believed Zeus and Thor were friends, but Scandinavians believed they were enemies.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage never mentions Zeus and Thor having any kind of relationship at all.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Kings of the Gods

Both cultures created a chief god who ruled over all the others, like a king rules a country. The Greeks chose Zeus. He sat on a high throne and watched over the world below. The Norse chose Odin, a one-eyed god of wisdom. Odin also sat on a high throne and looked down on everything. Both were powerful fathers who led whole families of gods.

People everywhere see the same sky, feel the same storms, and watch the same seasons change.
stimulus.content_text:
Kings of the Gods
Both cultures created a chief god who ruled over all the others, like a king rules a country. The Greeks chose Zeus. He sat on a high throne and watched over the world below. The Norse chose Odin, a one-eyed god of wisdom. Odin also sat on a high throne and looked down on everything. Both were powerful fathers who led whole families of gods.
People everywhere see the same sky, feel the same storms, and watch the same seasons change.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, why did ancient Greek and Norse people imagine similar chief gods?

choices.text: Because they wanted their gods to have the same families
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage never says people chose similar gods because they wanted them to share the same families.

choices.text: Because people everywhere experience the same natural world
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly says people everywhere experience the same sky, storms, and seasons, which explains why they imagined similar gods.

choices.text: Because Odin copied Zeus from the Greek stories
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage never says Odin was copied from Zeus — both gods came from separate cultures.

choices.text: Because kings learned to rule from the gods above
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage does not say anything about kings learning how to rule by watching the gods.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Not Just Bedtime Stories

It's easy to think of myths as fun tales, like fairy tales told before sleep. Actually, myths were much more serious than that. They were the science and religion of their time. Whole cities built temples to these gods and truly believed these stories explained real events. When the Greeks told the story of Persephone, they were explaining winter itself.

Persephone was taken to the underworld — a dark place beneath the earth where the dead were believed to go. Her mother, Demeter, was the goddess of the harvest. Demeter missed her daughter so much that she let every plant die. Nothing grew. That was winter. When Persephone returned each year, Demeter rejoiced, and flowers bloomed again. That was spring.

The Norse explained winter differently. They told of frost giants — huge, icy creatures who battled the gods. When the frost giants grew strong, cold and darkness spread across the land. When the gods fought them back, warmth returned. Different characters, same big question: why do seasons change?
stimulus.content_text:
Not Just Bedtime Stories
It's easy to think of myths as fun tales, like fairy tales told before sleep. Actually, myths were much more serious than that. They were the science and religion of their time. Whole cities built temples to these gods and truly believed these stories explained real events. When the Greeks told the story of Persephone, they were explaining winter itself.
Persephone was taken to the underworld — a dark place beneath the earth where the dead were believed to go. Her mother, Demeter, was the goddess of the harvest. Demeter missed her daughter so much that she let every plant die. Nothing grew. That was winter. When Persephone returned each year, Demeter rejoiced, and flowers bloomed again. That was spring.
The Norse explained winter differently. They told of frost giants — huge, icy creatures who battled the gods. When the frost giants grew strong, cold and darkness spread across the land. When the gods fought them back, warmth returned. Different characters, same big question: why do seasons change?

items.prompt: According to the passage, what happened right after Persephone returned each year?

choices.text: Demeter let every plant die
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because Demeter letting plants die happened when Persephone was gone, not when she came back.

choices.text: Demeter rejoiced and flowers bloomed
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage says 'When Persephone returned each year, Demeter rejoiced, and flowers bloomed again.'

choices.text: Persephone was taken to the underworld
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because being taken to the underworld is what happened at the beginning of the story, not after Persephone returned.

choices.text: Frost giants grew strong across the land
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because frost giants are part of the Norse myth, not the Greek story about Persephone and Demeter.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
New Names, Borrowed Stories

Centuries later, the Romans conquered Greece. They admired Greek stories so much that they adopted them — took them in and made them their own. They kept the stories mostly the same but changed the names. Zeus became Jupiter. Ares, the god of war, became Mars. Aphrodite, the goddess of love, became Venus. The Greek gods and the Roman gods are not the same gods. They are the same stories retold by a different people.
stimulus.content_text:
New Names, Borrowed Stories
Centuries later, the Romans conquered Greece. They admired Greek stories so much that they adopted them — took them in and made them their own. They kept the stories mostly the same but changed the names. Zeus became Jupiter. Ares, the god of war, became Mars. Aphrodite, the goddess of love, became Venus. The Greek gods and the Roman gods are not the same gods. They are the same stories retold by a different people.

items.prompt: The text says the Roman and Greek myths are 'the same stories retold by a different people.' What does this phrase mean about how the Roman gods compare to the Greek gods?

choices.text: They have mostly the same adventures but are called by different names.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage says the Romans kept the stories mostly the same but changed the names, which means the same adventures happened — just with new names like Jupiter instead of Zeus.

choices.text: They have mostly the same names but go on completely new adventures.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because it's the names that changed, not the adventures — the passage says the stories stayed mostly the same.

choices.text: They have mostly the same homes but fight against completely new enemies.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the passage never mentions anything about where the gods lived or who they fought against.

choices.text: They have mostly the same powers but are ruled by different leaders.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the passage doesn't say anything about the gods' powers or who was in charge of them.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Myths Hiding in Your Week

Open any calendar, and ancient gods are staring back at you. Thursday means "Thor's Day." Wednesday comes from Woden, another name for Odin. Tuesday comes from Tyr, a Norse god of war. Saturday comes from Saturn, a Roman god.

Studying myths from around the world helps us see what people have in common. Ancient people faced the same storms, the same dark winters, the same big questions about life and death. They answered with stories — and often, without ever meeting, they told the same kinds of stories. Myths don't just tell you about gods and heroes. They tell you what it means to be human, no matter where on Earth you live.
stimulus.content_text:
Myths Hiding in Your Week
Open any calendar, and ancient gods are staring back at you. Thursday means "Thor's Day." Wednesday comes from Woden, another name for Odin. Tuesday comes from Tyr, a Norse god of war. Saturday comes from Saturn, a Roman god.
Studying myths from around the world helps us see what people have in common. Ancient people faced the same storms, the same dark winters, the same big questions about life and death. They answered with stories — and often, without ever meeting, they told the same kinds of stories. Myths don't just tell you about gods and heroes. They tell you what it means to be human, no matter where on Earth you live.

items.prompt: In the article 'Gods Around the World,' the first paragraph of the section 'Myths Hiding in Your Week' defines the days of the week. How does the second paragraph build on this to define what myths are?

choices.text: It defines myths as stories about what it means to be human.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage directly says myths 'tell you what it means to be human,' which is the author's definition of myths.

choices.text: It defines myths as guides that help people survive the dark winters.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because dark winters are just one example of a challenge ancient people faced, not a definition of what myths are.

choices.text: It defines myths as calendars that list the names of ancient gods.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because calendars are only used as a fun example in the first paragraph, not as a way to define myths.

choices.text: It defines myths as records of how ancient people met each other.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the passage says ancient people told similar stories without ever meeting, so myths are not records of people meeting.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: Based on the article, how were the Romans different from the Norse in the way they got their myths?

choices.text: The Romans borrowed their stories from the Greeks, while the Norse made up their own.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the article clearly says the Romans took Greek stories and changed the names, while the Norse invented their own original myths without copying anyone.

choices.text: The Romans used their stories to explain the winter, while the Norse used them for calendars.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because it was the Greeks who explained winter with Persephone's story, and the Norse also used their stories to explain the world, not just for calendars.

choices.text: The Romans created brand new stories about gods, while the Norse copied Greek stories.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because it has it backwards — the Romans borrowed from the Greeks, not the other way around, and the Norse created their own stories independently.

choices.text: The Romans told their stories as bedtime tales, while the Norse used them for science.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the article says myths were serious science and religion for all ancient people, not bedtime tales, and this was true for both Romans and Norse.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: Why does the author end the article by naming days like Thursday and Wednesday?

choices.text: To show that myths caused all weather to happen
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the article explains that myths were stories people used to explain weather, not that myths actually caused weather.

choices.text: To show that old myths still affect our lives today
choices.feedback: B is correct because the author shows that names like Thursday (Thor's Day) and Wednesday (Woden's Day) come from ancient gods, proving old myths still show up in our everyday lives.

choices.text: To show that Romans invented the Norse gods first
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage says Norse and Greek myths developed separately, and Romans adopted Greek stories — they did not invent the Norse gods.

choices.text: To show that calendars caused people to write myths
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because people created myths long before calendars used those names — the myths came first, and the days were named after the gods later.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: Which sequence best shows the main idea that myths changed over time but still stayed part of people's lives?

choices.text: First people made myths to explain nature, then Romans retold Greek myths, and later god names stayed in the days of the week.
choices.feedback: A is correct because it matches the passage's real order: myths started to explain nature, Romans retold Greek myths with new names, and god names still live in our days of the week today.

choices.text: First Romans named the days of the week, then Greeks copied them, and later Norse people made storm stories.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because Romans did not name the days of the week first, and the Greeks did not copy the Romans — it was the other way around.

choices.text: First people stopped believing myths, then Romans invented new gods, and later calendars replaced the old stories.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the passage never says people stopped believing myths, and it says Romans adopted Greek gods rather than inventing completely new ones.

choices.text: First Norse and Greek people met and shared myths, then Romans changed the weather stories, and later winter myths disappeared.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the passage specifically says the Norse and Greek people never met each other and did not share their stories directly.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: Based on the sections "A Crash in the Sky" and "Not Just Bedtime Stories," how are the Greek and Norse myths alike?

choices.text: They both use stories about gods to explain weather and seasons.
choices.feedback: A is correct because both cultures used god stories to explain weather like thunder AND seasons like winter and spring.

choices.text: They both say that winter happens when a goddess goes underground.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because only the Greeks explained winter with a goddess going underground — the Norse used frost giants.

choices.text: They both borrowed their stories about the natural world from the Romans.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the Romans borrowed stories from the Greeks, not the other way around, and the Norse myths were not borrowed from Romans.

choices.text: They both explain that thunder is made by a god swinging a magical hammer.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because only Thor uses a hammer — Zeus makes thunder by throwing lightning bolts, not swinging a hammer.

syllabus_metadata.title: Gods Around the World: Comparing Norse and Greek Myths

assessments.title: The Boot-Shaped Land: Italy and the Mediterranean

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Ancient Rome

A Boot in the Sea

Picture a boot about to kick a ball. That is exactly what Italy looks like on a map. The "ball" is the island of Sicily, and the boot stretches right out into the bright blue Mediterranean Sea. Italy is a peninsula — a piece of land with water on three sides, like a finger poking into the ocean.

But the land around it held secrets that would help it grow into one of the greatest cities the world has ever known. About 2,700 years ago, a tiny village of mud huts sat on a few hills along a river in this boot-shaped land.
stimulus.content_text:
Ancient RomeA Boot in the Sea
Picture a boot about to kick a ball. That is exactly what Italy looks like on a map. The "ball" is the island of Sicily, and the boot stretches right out into the bright blue Mediterranean Sea. Italy is a peninsula — a piece of land with water on three sides, like a finger poking into the ocean.

But the land around it held secrets that would help it grow into one of the greatest cities the world has ever known. About 2,700 years ago, a tiny village of mud huts sat on a few hills along a river in this boot-shaped land.

items.prompt: According to the passage, how was the village from 2,700 years ago different from what Italy would later become?

choices.text: a tiny village that sat beside the ocean
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage says the village sat along a river, not beside the ocean.

choices.text: a tiny village compared to a great city
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly contrasts the tiny mud-hut village with what it would grow into — one of the greatest cities in the world.

choices.text: a tiny village that was shaped like a boot
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the boot shape describes Italy's land on a map, not the village.

choices.text: a tiny village built on top of a peninsula
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage describes Italy as a peninsula, but never says the village was built on top of one.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
The Sea in the Middle

The Mediterranean Sea sits between three continents: Europe, Africa, and Asia. Its name comes from Latin, the language the ancient Romans spoke. In Latin, "Mediterranean" means "middle of the land." That name fits perfectly.

Because Italy sticks out into the middle of the Mediterranean, Roman traders could reach places in every direction. Ships crossed this sea carrying olive oil, wine, pottery, and cloth. Merchants — people who buy and sell goods for a living, like shopkeepers who travel by boat — sailed from port to port. Traders from North Africa brought grain. Ships from Greece carried fine pottery. Rome sat like a spider at the center of a web of sea roads.
stimulus.content_text:
The Sea in the Middle
The Mediterranean Sea sits between three continents: Europe, Africa, and Asia. Its name comes from Latin, the language the ancient Romans spoke. In Latin, "Mediterranean" means "middle of the land." That name fits perfectly.

Because Italy sticks out into the middle of the Mediterranean, Roman traders could reach places in every direction. Ships crossed this sea carrying olive oil, wine, pottery, and cloth. Merchants — people who buy and sell goods for a living, like shopkeepers who travel by boat — sailed from port to port. Traders from North Africa brought grain. Ships from Greece carried fine pottery. Rome sat like a spider at the center of a web of sea roads.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, why could Roman traders reach many places?

choices.text: Because the sea was named by the ancient Romans
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because giving the sea a name didn't help Roman traders travel — it's just where the name came from.

choices.text: Because Italy reached into the middle of the sea
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly says Italy sticks into the middle of the sea, letting Roman traders sail in every direction.

choices.text: Because merchants brought goods back to Rome
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because merchants bringing goods describes what traders did, not the reason they could reach so many different places.

choices.text: Because Rome was built on three continents
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because Rome was one city in one place, not something spread across three continents.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Rich Soil, Full Bellies

Italy also had fertile soil — soil so rich and full of nutrients that crops grew tall and strong, the way plants grow in the best garden you have ever seen. The weather helped, too. Summers were warm, and winters were gentle.

Roman farmers grew three main crops: wheat for bread, grapes for wine, and olives to press into oil. These foods filled Roman tables every single day. Without good farmland, Rome could never have fed enough people to become a great city.
stimulus.content_text:
Rich Soil, Full Bellies
Italy also had fertile soil — soil so rich and full of nutrients that crops grew tall and strong, the way plants grow in the best garden you have ever seen. The weather helped, too. Summers were warm, and winters were gentle.

Roman farmers grew three main crops: wheat for bread, grapes for wine, and olives to press into oil. These foods filled Roman tables every single day. Without good farmland, Rome could never have fed enough people to become a great city.

items.prompt: According to the text, what is the correct sequence of events that helped Rome become a great city?

choices.text: Have good farmland, feed enough people, and become a great city.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage clearly states that good farmland came first, then feeding people, and that is what allowed Rome to become a great city.

choices.text: Become a great city, have good farmland, and feed enough people.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because it puts becoming a great city first, but the passage says Rome needed good farmland and food before it could become great.

choices.text: Plant a large garden, change the weather, and become a great city.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage never mentions planting a large garden or changing the weather as steps to becoming a great city.

choices.text: Grow tall crops, press olives into oil, and become a great city.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because growing tall crops and pressing olives are farming details from the passage, not the sequence of events that made Rome a great city.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Seven Hills and a River

Rome was not a country. It was a city — the most powerful city in the Roman Empire, a huge area of land ruled by one government, stretching across many countries. That city was built on seven hills along the Tiber River, about fifteen miles inland from the coast. Inland means away from the sea, like walking from the beach toward the middle of town.

The hills made Rome easier to defend. Enemies had to fight uphill to attack, which is exhausting and dangerous. Cities built right on the shore were easy targets for pirates and raiders who struck fast and escaped by sea. Rome was close enough to trade by water but far enough away to sleep safely at night.

The Tiber River was just as important. It brought fresh water for drinking, cooking, and bathing. Small boats carried grain and heavy stone blocks up the river right to the city.
stimulus.content_text:
Seven Hills and a River
Rome was not a country. It was a city — the most powerful city in the Roman Empire, a huge area of land ruled by one government, stretching across many countries. That city was built on seven hills along the Tiber River, about fifteen miles inland from the coast. Inland means away from the sea, like walking from the beach toward the middle of town.

The hills made Rome easier to defend. Enemies had to fight uphill to attack, which is exhausting and dangerous. Cities built right on the shore were easy targets for pirates and raiders who struck fast and escaped by sea. Rome was close enough to trade by water but far enough away to sleep safely at night.

The Tiber River was just as important. It brought fresh water for drinking, cooking, and bathing. Small boats carried grain and heavy stone blocks up the river right to the city.

items.prompt: Which comparison does the author use to help explain the meaning of the word **inland**?

choices.text: Walking from the beach toward the middle of town.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the author directly uses the comparison of walking from the beach toward the middle of town to explain what inland means.

choices.text: Fighting uphill to attack a city on seven hills.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because fighting uphill explains why Rome's hills were good for defense, not what the word inland means.

choices.text: Sailing small boats up the river to the city.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because sailing boats up the river is a detail about how Rome got supplies, not an explanation of the word inland.

choices.text: Carrying heavy stone blocks across a huge land area.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because carrying stone blocks is mentioned as something boats helped with, but it has nothing to do with explaining the word inland.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
The Right Place at the Right Time

The Mediterranean Sea connected Rome to distant lands. Hills protected it, and the Tiber River supplied it. The land itself gave Rome advantages that even the smartest leaders could not have invented.

Today, modern Italy sits on the same peninsula, and people still live in the city of Rome. But the ancient Romans who built that city lived thousands of years ago. They spoke a different language, wore different clothes, and saw the world in ways very different from Italians today.

Then remember: a handful of farmers once built mud huts on a few hills there — and the ground beneath their feet helped them build an empire.
stimulus.content_text:
The Right Place at the Right Time
The Mediterranean Sea connected Rome to distant lands. Hills protected it, and the Tiber River supplied it. The land itself gave Rome advantages that even the smartest leaders could not have invented.

Today, modern Italy sits on the same peninsula, and people still live in the city of Rome. But the ancient Romans who built that city lived thousands of years ago. They spoke a different language, wore different clothes, and saw the world in ways very different from Italians today.

Then remember: a handful of farmers once built mud huts on a few hills there — and the ground beneath their feet helped them build an empire.

items.prompt: According to the section 'The Right Place at the Right Time', which statement defines who the original builders of Rome were?

choices.text: They were a handful of farmers who built mud huts.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage directly says 'a handful of farmers once built mud huts on a few hills there,' which is exactly what this answer says.

choices.text: They were the smartest leaders from distant lands.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the passage mentions smart leaders only to say that even they could not have invented the land advantages Rome had — they are not described as Rome's builders.

choices.text: They were modern people who live in Italy today.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because modern people in Italy live there now, but the passage says the ancient Romans who built the city lived thousands of years ago.

choices.text: They were travelers who sailed the Mediterranean Sea.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the Mediterranean Sea is described as something that connected Rome to distant lands, not as a place where Rome's builders came from.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: Based on the passage, how was the Mediterranean Sea different from the seven hills in the way it helped Rome?

choices.text: The sea brought traders to the city, but the hills kept dangerous enemies away.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage says the Mediterranean Sea helped traders reach faraway places, while the seven hills protected Rome by forcing enemies to fight uphill.

choices.text: The sea gave people fresh drinking water, but the hills gave farmers rich soil.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the passage says the Tiber River provided fresh drinking water, not the sea, and the rich soil came from Italy's fertile land, not the hills.

choices.text: The sea helped farmers grow their wheat, but the hills helped the merchants sail.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the passage says merchants used the sea for sailing and trading, not farmers, and the hills were for defense, not sailing.

choices.text: The sea protected the city from pirates, but the hills connected Rome to Africa.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the passage says the hills protected Rome from raiders and pirates, while the sea connected Rome to distant lands like Africa — the opposite of what this answer says.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: If you want to find out how the land and soil caused Rome to grow into a strong city, which two headings should you read?

choices.text: "A Boot in the Sea" and "The Sea in the Middle"
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because 'A Boot in the Sea' just describes what Italy looks like on a map, and 'The Sea in the Middle' is about trading by sea, not about land or soil.

choices.text: "Rich Soil, Full Bellies" and "Seven Hills and a River"
choices.feedback: B is correct because 'Rich Soil, Full Bellies' explains how good soil grew food for Rome, and 'Seven Hills and a River' explains how the hills and river helped Rome stay safe and supplied.

choices.text: "A Boot in the Sea" and "The Right Place at the Right Time"
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because 'A Boot in the Sea' only describes Italy's shape, and 'The Right Place at the Right Time' is a summary section, not a detailed explanation of land and soil.

choices.text: "The Sea in the Middle" and "Rich Soil, Full Bellies"
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because 'The Sea in the Middle' focuses on sea trading routes and merchants, not on the land or soil that helped Rome grow.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: Which sequence of key details best explains the main idea of the whole article?

choices.text: Farmers started a tiny village, used the rich land, and built an empire.
choices.feedback: A is correct because it follows the real order of events in the article: tiny village first, then using the rich land, then building a great empire.

choices.text: Leaders ruled a huge empire, found the rich land, and started a village.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the order is backwards — Rome started as a small village long before it became a big empire with leaders ruling it.

choices.text: Merchants sailed the middle sea, bought sweet oil, and sold fine clay pottery.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because it only picks out one small detail about merchants trading, which does not explain the main idea of the whole article.

choices.text: Pirates attacked the steep hills, stole the fresh water, and escaped the city.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the article says the hills actually protected Rome from pirates, so pirates never stole the water or took over the city.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: How were the Mediterranean Sea and the Tiber River alike in the way they helped Rome?

choices.text: Both protected Rome from enemies attacking the city.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because it was Rome's seven hills that made it hard for enemies to attack, not the Mediterranean Sea or the Tiber River.

choices.text: Both were water routes that carried goods to Rome.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage says merchant ships carried goods across the Mediterranean and small boats carried grain and stone up the Tiber River — both were water routes that brought supplies to Rome.

choices.text: Both gave Romans fresh water for drinking and cooking.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because only the Tiber River provided fresh water for drinking and cooking — the passage never says the Mediterranean Sea did this.

choices.text: Both connected Rome to Africa, Europe, and Asia.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because only the Mediterranean Sea connected Rome to Africa, Europe, and Asia — the Tiber was an inland river, not a route to other continents.

syllabus_metadata.title: The Boot-Shaped Land: Italy and the Mediterranean

assessments.title: Romulus, Remus, and the She-Wolf: Rome's Founding Legend

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The She-Wolf and the Twins: How Rome Got Its Story

Babies in the River

A basket bobs in the muddy water of the Tiber River. Inside, two baby boys cry in the cold. Their own uncle put them there. He wanted them dead. But the river carried them to shore — and into one of the most famous stories ever told.

Their uncle, the king, feared the boys would one day take his power. Their mother was a princess, and their father was Mars, the god of war. This is a legend, a story passed down over many years that may not be true but that people told to explain something important. According to Rome's legend, the twin brothers were named Romulus and Remus.

The twins did not drown. Their basket washed up on a muddy bank. A she-wolf found them there. Instead of hurting them, she nursed them — she fed them her own milk, the same way a mother dog feeds her puppies. Later, a kind shepherd discovered the boys and raised them as his own sons. Romulus and Remus grew up strong. They had no idea they were really princes.
stimulus.content_text:
The She-Wolf and the Twins: How Rome Got Its StoryBabies in the River
A basket bobs in the muddy water of the Tiber River. Inside, two baby boys cry in the cold. Their own uncle put them there. He wanted them dead. But the river carried them to shore — and into one of the most famous stories ever told.

Their uncle, the king, feared the boys would one day take his power. Their mother was a princess, and their father was Mars, the god of war. This is a legend, a story passed down over many years that may not be true but that people told to explain something important. According to Rome's legend, the twin brothers were named Romulus and Remus.

The twins did not drown. Their basket washed up on a muddy bank. A she-wolf found them there. Instead of hurting them, she nursed them — she fed them her own milk, the same way a mother dog feeds her puppies. Later, a kind shepherd discovered the boys and raised them as his own sons. Romulus and Remus grew up strong. They had no idea they were really princes.

items.prompt: The uncle wanted the twin babies to die, but according to the text, what did the she-wolf do instead?

choices.text: She carried them back to the king
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage never says the wolf took the babies to the king — that never happened in the story.

choices.text: She nursed them with her own milk
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage says the she-wolf 'nursed them' and 'fed them her own milk,' which means she took care of them instead of hurting them.

choices.text: She left them alone on the bank
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the wolf did not leave them alone — she stayed and fed them her milk.

choices.text: She brought them to a shepherd
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the shepherd found the boys on his own later; the she-wolf did not bring them to him.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
A City Born from an Argument

When the twins finally learned the truth about their royal family, they made a bold plan. They would build a brand-new city near the spot where the she-wolf had saved them. But they hit a problem right away. They could not agree on which hill to build it on.

The argument between the brothers turned ugly. It grew worse and worse until Romulus killed Remus. It was a violent, sad beginning for what would become a great city.

Romulus became the first king of Rome in 753 BC. That was about 2,700 years ago. He named the city after himself. He built walls around his small settlement, a place where a group of people set up homes together, like a tiny village. Then he invited people from the countryside to move in.
stimulus.content_text:
A City Born from an Argument
When the twins finally learned the truth about their royal family, they made a bold plan. They would build a brand-new city near the spot where the she-wolf had saved them. But they hit a problem right away. They could not agree on which hill to build it on.

The argument between the brothers turned ugly. It grew worse and worse until Romulus killed Remus. It was a violent, sad beginning for what would become a great city.

Romulus became the first king of Rome in 753 BC. That was about 2,700 years ago. He named the city after himself. He built walls around his small settlement, a place where a group of people set up homes together, like a tiny village. Then he invited people from the countryside to move in.

items.prompt: Based on the text, what caused the terrible argument between Romulus and Remus?

choices.text: They could not agree on which hill to build the city on.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage says the brothers 'could not agree on which hill to build it on,' and that disagreement is what started their terrible argument.

choices.text: They could not agree on what to name their brand-new city.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never says they argued about the city's name — in fact, Romulus named the city himself after he became king.

choices.text: They could not agree on who should be the very first king.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage does not mention them fighting over who would be king — that is never given as a reason for their argument.

choices.text: They could not agree on how to build the settlement's walls.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never says they disagreed about how to build the walls — Romulus built the walls later, after the argument was already over.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
What Really Happened?

The Romans told the legend of Romulus and Remus with great pride. They loved the idea that a god's son started their city. The image of the she-wolf feeding the twin babies became a famous symbol of Rome. You can still see statues of it in Rome today.

But historians — people who study evidence from the past — believe something different. Rome probably grew from several small farming villages along the Tiber River. Picture a handful of tiny communities scattered across seven hills. Over time, these villages merged, which means they joined together the way drops of water combine into a puddle. There was no single founder. There was no single moment when Rome was born.

You might think Rome was always huge and powerful. But early Rome was no bigger than your school neighborhood. It took hundreds of years for that cluster of villages to grow into a mighty empire.
stimulus.content_text:
What Really Happened?
The Romans told the legend of Romulus and Remus with great pride. They loved the idea that a god's son started their city. The image of the she-wolf feeding the twin babies became a famous symbol of Rome. You can still see statues of it in Rome today.

But historians — people who study evidence from the past — believe something different. Rome probably grew from several small farming villages along the Tiber River. Picture a handful of tiny communities scattered across seven hills. Over time, these villages merged, which means they joined together the way drops of water combine into a puddle. There was no single founder. There was no single moment when Rome was born.

You might think Rome was always huge and powerful. But early Rome was no bigger than your school neighborhood. It took hundreds of years for that cluster of villages to grow into a mighty empire.

items.prompt: According to the text, what happened first as Rome began to grow?

choices.text: Rome became a mighty empire
choices.feedback: Rome becoming a mighty empire happened much later — the passage says it took hundreds of years, so this was not the first step.

choices.text: Small villages joined together
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says small farming villages along the Tiber River merged, or joined together, and that was how Rome first began to grow.

choices.text: A she-wolf fed twin babies
choices.feedback: The she-wolf feeding the twins is part of the Roman legend, but historians don't believe that's what really happened when Rome started.

choices.text: Statues appeared in Rome
choices.feedback: The statues in Rome are something people can still see today, so they are not part of what happened first when Rome was just beginning.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Why the Story Still Matters

So if the legend isn't true, why did Romans keep telling it? Because they wanted their city to feel destined for greatness — meaning they believed the gods had chosen Rome for big things from the very start, the way a seed is destined to become a tree.

The story of the she-wolf and the twins gave Romans something facts alone could not: a dramatic beginning that made their city feel special. People all over the world still do the same thing. That is what legends are for — not to record exactly what happened, but to show what people believed about themselves.
stimulus.content_text:
Why the Story Still Matters
So if the legend isn't true, why did Romans keep telling it? Because they wanted their city to feel destined for greatness — meaning they believed the gods had chosen Rome for big things from the very start, the way a seed is destined to become a tree.

The story of the she-wolf and the twins gave Romans something facts alone could not: a dramatic beginning that made their city feel special. People all over the world still do the same thing. That is what legends are for — not to record exactly what happened, but to show what people believed about themselves.

items.prompt: In the article, the author compares Rome to a seed that grows into a tree. What does this comparison help the reader understand about the word 'destined'?

choices.text: something that is very small at first
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the seed in the comparison isn't about being small — it's about how a seed will definitely grow into a tree, which tells us about the future, not the size.

choices.text: something that is sure to happen
choices.feedback: B is correct because just like a seed is sure to become a tree, the passage shows that 'destined' means something is certain to happen.

choices.text: something that happened a long time ago
