choices.feedback: C is incorrect because 'destined' is about what will happen in the future, not about something that already happened long ago.

choices.text: something that is hard to believe
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because something that is destined is actually very certain and believable — it's the opposite of hard to believe.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: According to the article, how is the legend of Rome's beginning different from what historians believe?

choices.text: The legend says that one man started the city, but historians say that there was no single founder.
choices.feedback: This is correct — the legend says Romulus alone founded Rome, but historians say there was no single founder, just villages that merged over time.

choices.text: The legend says that small villages joined together, but historians say that twin brothers built the city.
choices.feedback: This is backwards — it's the legend that talks about twin brothers building the city, and historians who say villages joined together, not the other way around.

choices.text: The legend says that the city was always huge, but historians say that it started as a tiny neighborhood.
choices.feedback: The article never says the legend claims Rome was always huge — in fact, the article says early Rome was no bigger than a school neighborhood, which is what historians believe.

choices.text: The legend says that a kind shepherd built the city, but historians say that the god of war built it.
choices.feedback: The article never says the shepherd built the city — the shepherd just raised the twins — and historians say villages merged together, not that the god of war built anything.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: Which section would best help you figure out why Romans kept telling the story of Romulus and Remus even if it was not exactly true?

choices.text: the section called "Babies in the River"
choices.feedback: Section A, 'Babies in the River,' tells the beginning of the legend about the twins and the she-wolf, but it doesn't explain why Romans kept telling the story.

choices.text: the section called "A City Born from an Argument"
choices.feedback: Section B, 'A City Born from an Argument,' tells how Romulus and Remus fought and how Rome was built, but it doesn't explain why people kept sharing the legend.

choices.text: the section called "What Really Happened?"
choices.feedback: Section C, 'What Really Happened?,' tells us what historians believe about Rome's real history, but it doesn't focus on why Romans loved telling the legend.

choices.text: the section called "Why the Story Still Matters"
choices.feedback: Section D, 'Why the Story Still Matters,' is correct because it directly explains that Romans told the legend to make their city feel special and chosen by the gods for greatness.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: The first half of the article shares the famous legend of Rome's beginning. Which sequence of key details best recounts this main story?

choices.text: First, a she-wolf saved the twin babies, and later, the brothers planned to build a city.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage clearly shows the she-wolf saving the babies came first, and the brothers planning their city came later after they grew up.

choices.text: First, the brothers planned to build a city, and later, they learned about their royal family.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because it flips the order — the twins learned about their royal family first, and then they made their plan to build a city.

choices.text: First, small farming villages sat by a river, and later, they joined together into an empire.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because it describes the historians' explanation of how Rome really grew, not the legend the question is asking about.

choices.text: First, a kind uncle saved the twin babies, and later, a she-wolf raised them in town.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the uncle was the villain who put the babies in the river to hurt them, and the she-wolf found them by the riverbank, not in a town.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: How does the way the legend says Rome began compare to the way historians say Rome began?

choices.text: The legend says Rome started from farming villages, but historians say a king built it
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because it has the two stories switched — the legend is the one about a king (Romulus) building Rome, while historians talk about farming villages joining together.

choices.text: The legend says a god created Rome, but historians say the twins created it
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the legend says Mars the god was the twins' father, but it was still Romulus the human twin who founded the city, not a god directly.

choices.text: The legend says one person founded Rome, but historians say villages joined together
choices.feedback: C is correct because the passage clearly says the legend credits one founder, Romulus, while historians say several villages merged together over time with no single founder.

choices.text: The legend says Rome grew over many years, but historians say it happened quickly
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because it's the historians who say Rome grew slowly over many years, while the legend describes a specific moment when Romulus founded the city in 753 BC.

syllabus_metadata.title: Romulus, Remus, and the She-Wolf: Rome's Founding Legend

assessments.title: Power to the People: The Roman Republic

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Roman Republic: When a City Kicked Out Its King

The Night Rome Said "No More"

In the year 509 BC, angry Romans marched through their city's streets and forced their king to leave. His name was Tarquin the Proud, and he had been cruel. He ignored what the people wanted. He punished anyone who disagreed with him. So the Romans overthrew him — they kicked him out of power, the way you might vote someone off a team that they were ruining. Then they made a bold promise: Rome would never have a king again.

So the Romans built a brand-new kind of government called a republic. The word comes from the Latin phrase res publica, meaning "the people's affair." In a republic, citizens vote for leaders to speak and act for them.
stimulus.content_text:
The Roman Republic: When a City Kicked Out Its KingThe Night Rome Said "No More"
In the year 509 BC, angry Romans marched through their city's streets and forced their king to leave. His name was Tarquin the Proud, and he had been cruel. He ignored what the people wanted. He punished anyone who disagreed with him. So the Romans overthrew him — they kicked him out of power, the way you might vote someone off a team that they were ruining. Then they made a bold promise: Rome would never have a king again.
So the Romans built a brand-new kind of government called a republic. The word comes from the Latin phrase res publica, meaning "the people's affair." In a republic, citizens vote for leaders to speak and act for them.

items.prompt: According to the text, how was the new republic different from King Tarquin's rule?

choices.text: The republic let one strong leader make all choices
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because letting one strong leader decide everything is what King Tarquin did, not what a republic does.

choices.text: The republic let citizens vote for their leaders
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly says that in a republic, citizens vote for leaders to speak and act for them.

choices.text: The republic gave the king even more power
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the Romans removed the king's power entirely and promised Rome would never have a king again.

choices.text: The republic kept the same rules as before
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the Romans built a brand-new government called a republic, which was a big change from before.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Two Leaders Instead of One

Instead of one king, Rome elected two leaders each year called consuls. Why two? Because the Romans were clever. If one consul made a bad choice, the other could say, "No, stop." They worked like a buddy system for running a country. And here is the key part: after just one year, both consuls had to step down. New ones took their place. No one got to hold power for too long.

Alongside the consuls, the most powerful group in the republic was the Senate, about three hundred wealthy men who made laws and decided how Rome spent its money. Unlike consuls, senators served for life. They met in a grand building in the center of the city called the Senate House, where they debated and argued over every big decision Rome faced.
stimulus.content_text:
Two Leaders Instead of One
Instead of one king, Rome elected two leaders each year called consuls. Why two? Because the Romans were clever. If one consul made a bad choice, the other could say, "No, stop." They worked like a buddy system for running a country. And here is the key part: after just one year, both consuls had to step down. New ones took their place. No one got to hold power for too long.
Alongside the consuls, the most powerful group in the republic was the Senate, about three hundred wealthy men who made laws and decided how Rome spent its money. Unlike consuls, senators served for life. They met in a grand building in the center of the city called the Senate House, where they debated and argued over every big decision Rome faced.

items.prompt: Based on the section, what is the main reason the Romans set up their republic with rules like two consuls, one-year terms, and a Senate?

choices.text: They wanted to make sure the Senate could meet in a grand building.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the Senate building is just a place where senators met to talk — it has nothing to do with why Rome made rules about sharing power.

choices.text: They wanted to keep any one person from having too much power.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage explains that having two consuls, short one-year terms, and a Senate were all ways to make sure no single person could hold too much power for too long.

choices.text: They wanted to copy the way their old kings had ruled Rome.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because Rome set up the republic to be different from kings, not to copy them — kings had too much power, and that's exactly what Rome was trying to avoid.

choices.text: They wanted the consuls to serve for life like the senators did.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage says the opposite — consuls only served for one year, while senators were the ones who served for life.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Not Everyone Had a Voice

You might think the Roman Republic let everyone vote and have a say. Actually, it was far from fair. At first, only rich men from old, powerful families could become senators or consuls. These wealthy families were called patricians — picture them as the people who sat in the front row and made all the rules. Ordinary citizens, called plebeians, had almost no power at all. Women, enslaved people, and non-citizens could never vote, no matter what.

The plebeians fought back. They went on strikes, refusing to work or fight in Rome's army until the government listened. Over time, they won the right to elect their own special leaders called tribunes. A tribune could stand up in the Senate and shout "Veto!" — a Latin word meaning "I forbid it" — to block any law that hurt ordinary people. That was real power.
stimulus.content_text:
Not Everyone Had a Voice
You might think the Roman Republic let everyone vote and have a say. Actually, it was far from fair. At first, only rich men from old, powerful families could become senators or consuls. These wealthy families were called patricians — picture them as the people who sat in the front row and made all the rules. Ordinary citizens, called plebeians, had almost no power at all. Women, enslaved people, and non-citizens could never vote, no matter what.
The plebeians fought back. They went on strikes, refusing to work or fight in Rome's army until the government listened. Over time, they won the right to elect their own special leaders called tribunes. A tribune could stand up in the Senate and shout "Veto!" — a Latin word meaning "I forbid it" — to block any law that hurt ordinary people. That was real power.

items.prompt: According to the text, what did the plebeians do first, before they got their own leaders called tribunes?

choices.text: They voted for new consuls to lead Rome.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the text tells us only rich patricians had that kind of power — plebeians couldn't vote for consuls.

choices.text: They refused to work or fight for Rome.
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage clearly says the plebeians went on strikes and refused to work or fight before they earned the right to have their own leaders called tribunes.

choices.text: They moved away from Rome for good.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never says the plebeians left Rome — they stayed and fought for their rights.

choices.text: They asked the patricians to share money.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage doesn't mention the plebeians asking for money from the patricians at all.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
A Republic Grows — Then Falls

The republic lasted almost five hundred years, from 509 BC to 27 BC. During that time, Rome grew from a small city into a huge empire stretching across the Mediterranean Sea — the large body of water surrounded by Europe, Africa, and Asia. The republic's system of shared power kept Rome strong and steady for centuries. But in 27 BC, the republic ended, and Rome began to be ruled by emperors instead.
stimulus.content_text:
A Republic Grows — Then Falls
The republic lasted almost five hundred years, from 509 BC to 27 BC. During that time, Rome grew from a small city into a huge empire stretching across the Mediterranean Sea — the large body of water surrounded by Europe, Africa, and Asia. The republic's system of shared power kept Rome strong and steady for centuries. But in 27 BC, the republic ended, and Rome began to be ruled by emperors instead.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, how was the way Rome was ruled during the republic different from the way it was ruled after 27 BC?

choices.text: Rome was a small city during the republic but became an empire after 27 BC
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage says Rome grew into a huge empire while the republic was still going, not after 27 BC.

choices.text: Rome had shared power during the republic but had emperors after 27 BC
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage clearly states the republic had 'shared power,' and after 27 BC Rome was ruled by emperors — two very different ways of governing.

choices.text: Rome was weak during the republic but became strong after 27 BC
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage says the republic's shared power kept Rome 'strong and steady,' meaning Rome was already strong during the republic.

choices.text: Rome was near the Mediterranean during the republic but moved after 27 BC
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage never says anything about Rome moving away from the Mediterranean Sea.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Rome's Idea Lives On

The United States borrowed several ideas from Rome. America has a Senate. American presidents serve set terms and then step down, just like Roman consuls did. However, the two systems are not copies of each other. Roman senators were wealthy men who were never elected by regular people, while American senators are chosen by all citizens who can vote. Even the word "republic" itself connects us to ancient Rome — that Latin phrase res publica has been in use for over two thousand years.
stimulus.content_text:
Rome's Idea Lives On
The United States borrowed several ideas from Rome. America has a Senate. American presidents serve set terms and then step down, just like Roman consuls did. However, the two systems are not copies of each other. Roman senators were wealthy men who were never elected by regular people, while American senators are chosen by all citizens who can vote. Even the word "republic" itself connects us to ancient Rome — that Latin phrase res publica has been in use for over two thousand years.

items.prompt: In the section "Rome's Idea Lives On," how does the text define American senators?

choices.text: As people chosen by citizens who vote
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage says 'American senators are chosen by all citizens who can vote,' which is exactly what this answer says.

choices.text: As wealthy men who are never elected
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because 'wealthy men who are never elected' describes Roman senators, not American senators.

choices.text: As presidents who step down from power
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because stepping down from power describes presidents, not senators.

choices.text: As leaders who completely copy the Romans
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage clearly says the two systems are NOT copies of each other.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: Based on the article, how were the Roman consuls different from the tribunes?

choices.text: Consuls were chosen by the ordinary citizens, while tribunes were chosen by the wealthy families.
choices.feedback: This is backwards — the article says plebeians (ordinary citizens) elected the tribunes, while consuls were elected by Rome's government, not specifically by ordinary citizens.

choices.text: Consuls served for their entire lives, while tribunes had to step down after one year.
choices.feedback: This is the wrong way around — the article says consuls stepped down after one year, and it was senators (not tribunes) who served for life.

choices.text: Consuls acted as leaders for the country, while tribunes worked to protect the ordinary people.
choices.feedback: This is correct — the article shows consuls led the whole country, while tribunes had the special job of protecting ordinary people by blocking unfair laws with a 'Veto!'

choices.text: Consuls wanted to keep the king in power, while tribunes wanted to build a large empire.
choices.feedback: The article never says anything like this — consuls and tribunes both existed after the king was already gone, and neither one was connected to building an empire.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: Based on the sections "The Night Rome Said 'No More'" and "Not Everyone Had a Voice," what do the events in both parts of the text have in common?

choices.text: The people of Rome fought back against leaders who were cruel and unfair.
choices.feedback: A is correct because in both sections, Romans pushed back against leaders who were cruel or unfair — first by overthrowing King Tarquin, and then by striking until plebeians got their own leaders called tribunes.

choices.text: The people of Rome voted for new leaders to conquer lands across the sea.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because neither section talks about voting for leaders to conquer lands across the sea — that idea isn't in either of these two sections at all.

choices.text: The people of Rome refused to fight in the army to get a brand-new king.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the plebeians refused to fight in the army to gain more rights, not to get a new king — and the first section doesn't mention the army at all.

choices.text: The people of Rome took away the power of leaders who ran out of wealth.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage never says leaders lost power because they ran out of wealth — Tarquin was removed for being cruel, and patricians were challenged for being unfair, not for losing money.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: Which sequence of events best tells the main story of the Roman Republic?

choices.text: The Romans removed their king, formed a shared government, and later gave the people a voice.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage tells us Romans removed their king in 509 BC, built a shared government with consuls and a Senate, and later gave ordinary people more power through tribunes.

choices.text: The Romans built a huge empire, elected emperors to rule, and later asked a king to return.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because Romans never invited a king back — in fact, they promised Rome would never have a king again, and they ended up ruled by emperors, not kings.

choices.text: The Romans elected two consuls, built a grand Senate House, and later made rules just for the rich.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because while consuls and the Senate House are mentioned in the passage, these details don't capture the full main story of the republic from start to finish.

choices.text: The Romans copied American ideas, voted for new presidents, and later ended their republic.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because it has the history backwards — Rome existed long before America, and it was America that borrowed ideas like the Senate from ancient Rome.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: Based on the article, how was the job of a Roman consul different from the job of King Tarquin?

choices.text: Consuls shared their power for one year, while the king ruled all by himself.
choices.feedback: This is correct — the article clearly says two consuls shared power and stepped down after one year, while King Tarquin ruled alone and held power until the Romans kicked him out.

choices.text: Consuls made laws for their whole lives, while the king served for a short time.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect — the article says senators served for life, not consuls, and the king ruled for a long time, not a short time.

choices.text: Consuls were chosen by ordinary people, while the king was chosen by the Senate.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect — the article says only wealthy patricians could become consuls at first, so ordinary people did not choose them.

choices.text: Consuls ruled over a huge empire, while the king ruled over a small city.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect — both the king and the early consuls ruled over Rome as a city, and the empire came hundreds of years later.

syllabus_metadata.title: Power to the People: The Roman Republic

assessments.title: Julius Caesar: The General Who Changed Rome Forever

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Julius Caesar and the End of the Roman Republic

A Dangerous Walk to the Senate

On the morning of March 15, 44 BC, Julius Caesar walked into the Roman Senate building.

Rome was a republic — a type of government where citizens choose leaders to make decisions for them, like a school where students elect a class president instead of letting one kid boss everyone around. No single person was supposed to control everything. Romans were proud of this system. It had worked for nearly five hundred years.
stimulus.content_text:
Julius Caesar and the End of the Roman RepublicA Dangerous Walk to the Senate
On the morning of March 15, 44 BC, Julius Caesar walked into the Roman Senate building.
Rome was a republic — a type of government where citizens choose leaders to make decisions for them, like a school where students elect a class president instead of letting one kid boss everyone around. No single person was supposed to control everything. Romans were proud of this system. It had worked for nearly five hundred years.

items.prompt: According to the text, what do citizens do in a republic instead of letting one person control everything?

choices.text: They walk into the Senate building.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because walking into the Senate building is what Julius Caesar did on March 15, not what citizens do in a republic.

choices.text: They choose leaders to make decisions.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly says a republic is a government where citizens choose leaders to make decisions for them.

choices.text: They boss all the other kids around.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because bossing everyone around is the opposite of a republic — a republic is designed so no single person can do that.

choices.text: They rule for five hundred long years.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because five hundred years describes how long Rome's republic had been working, not an action that citizens take.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Caesar's Rise to Power and Military Conquests

Caesar was born into a noble family — a family with high rank and old connections — around 100 BC. Even as a boy, he was ambitious, meaning he burned with desire for power and fame. He climbed through Roman politics by making speeches, winning friends, and outsmarting rivals.

But Caesar built his real fame on the battlefield. From 58 to 50 BC, he conquered Gaul, a huge region covering what is now France, Belgium, and parts of Germany and Switzerland. For eight years, he fought fierce tribes, crossed dangerous rivers, and even invaded Britain twice. He marched beside his soldiers, slept on the same cold ground, and shared their hunger. Victory after victory made Rome's territory much larger — and made Caesar the most famous man alive.
stimulus.content_text:
Caesar's Rise to Power and Military Conquests
Caesar was born into a noble family — a family with high rank and old connections — around 100 BC. Even as a boy, he was ambitious, meaning he burned with desire for power and fame. He climbed through Roman politics by making speeches, winning friends, and outsmarting rivals.
But Caesar built his real fame on the battlefield. From 58 to 50 BC, he conquered Gaul, a huge region covering what is now France, Belgium, and parts of Germany and Switzerland. For eight years, he fought fierce tribes, crossed dangerous rivers, and even invaded Britain twice. He marched beside his soldiers, slept on the same cold ground, and shared their hunger. Victory after victory made Rome's territory much larger — and made Caesar the most famous man alive.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, which detail best explains why Caesar became the most famous man alive?

choices.text: He made speeches and won friends in politics.
choices.feedback: Making speeches and winning friends helped Caesar in politics, but the passage says his fame came from his battle victories, not his political skills.

choices.text: He won many battles and grew Rome's territory.
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage directly says that 'victory after victory' made Rome bigger and made Caesar 'the most famous man alive.'

choices.text: He was born into a family with old connections.
choices.feedback: Being born into a noble family gave Caesar connections, but the passage doesn't say that's what made him the most famous man alive.

choices.text: He slept on the cold ground with his soldiers.
choices.feedback: Sleeping on the cold ground showed Caesar was a caring leader, but the passage points to his many victories — not this detail — as the reason for his great fame.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Too Much Power for One Man

When Caesar returned to Rome, the senators grew nervous. Then, in 44 BC, Caesar was declared dictator for life. A dictator was a leader with total control — imagine a teacher who could make every rule and never be questioned. In the past, Rome had only chosen dictators during emergencies, and only for a few months. "Dictator for life" meant Caesar planned to rule forever.

You might think this made Caesar an emperor or a king. Actually, he was never either one. But the senators feared he was heading that way. A king was exactly what the republic had been built to prevent.
stimulus.content_text:
Too Much Power for One Man
When Caesar returned to Rome, the senators grew nervous. Then, in 44 BC, Caesar was declared dictator for life. A dictator was a leader with total control — imagine a teacher who could make every rule and never be questioned. In the past, Rome had only chosen dictators during emergencies, and only for a few months. "Dictator for life" meant Caesar planned to rule forever.
You might think this made Caesar an emperor or a king. Actually, he was never either one. But the senators feared he was heading that way. A king was exactly what the republic had been built to prevent.

items.prompt: According to the section "Too Much Power for One Man," what happened to Caesar after the senators grew nervous?

choices.text: He was declared dictator for life.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage says 'Caesar was declared dictator for life' right after mentioning the senators grew nervous.

choices.text: He was crowned as the Roman king.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the passage clearly states Caesar was never a king.

choices.text: He was chosen to be the emperor.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the passage says Caesar was never an emperor either.

choices.text: He was hired as a strict teacher.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the teacher was just a fun example to explain what a dictator is — it was not something that really happened to Caesar.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
The Ides of March and Caesar's Assassination

A group of senators decided to stop Caesar by force. Among them were Brutus and Cassius — men Caesar trusted. These conspirators, people who make a secret plan together, surrounded Caesar on March 15, a date called the Ides of March. They assassinated him, meaning they killed him on purpose for political reasons. According to legend, Caesar looked at Brutus and whispered, "Et tu, Brute?" — Latin for "You too, Brutus?"
stimulus.content_text:
The Ides of March and Caesar's Assassination
A group of senators decided to stop Caesar by force. Among them were Brutus and Cassius — men Caesar trusted. These conspirators, people who make a secret plan together, surrounded Caesar on March 15, a date called the Ides of March. They assassinated him, meaning they killed him on purpose for political reasons. According to legend, Caesar looked at Brutus and whispered, "Et tu, Brute?" — Latin for "You too, Brutus?"

items.prompt: Based on the passage, how were Brutus and Cassius alike?

choices.text: They both were men whom Caesar trusted.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage uses the exact words 'men Caesar trusted' to describe both Brutus and Cassius.

choices.text: They both tried to keep Caesar very safe.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because Brutus and Cassius were part of the group that attacked Caesar, not protected him.

choices.text: They both warned Caesar about the danger.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage never mentions either of them warning Caesar about any danger.

choices.text: They both wanted to rule the whole empire.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage does not say anything about Brutus or Cassius wanting to rule the empire.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
The Fall of the Republic and Caesar's Lasting Legacy

The senators who killed Caesar believed they were saving the republic. But their plan backfired completely. Caesar's death sparked a civil war — a war fought between groups inside the same country. Different leaders battled for control. The republic that had lasted almost five hundred years fell apart. Caesar's adopted son, Augustus, eventually took power and became Rome's first emperor. The republic was gone for good.

Caesar's legacy — the lasting impact a person leaves behind, like footprints pressed deep into wet concrete — still surrounds you. He reformed the Roman calendar, and the month of July carries his name. The word "Caesar" itself became a title for rulers. In German, "Kaiser" means emperor. In Russian, "Tsar" means emperor. Both words grew from Caesar's name.

So the next time you flip your calendar to July, remember: you are reading a word that is over two thousand years old, left behind by a man whose life ended Rome's republic and began something entirely new.

The Fall of the Republic and Caesar's Lasting Legacy

The republic was gone for good.
Caesar's legacy — the lasting impact a person leaves behind, like footprints pressed deep into wet concrete — still surrounds you. He reformed the Roman calendar, and the month of July carries his name. The word "Caesar" itself became a title for rulers. In German, "Kaiser" means emperor. In Russian, "Tsar" means emperor. items.prompt: According to the passage, what definition does the bold key word **civil war** help the reader locate?

choices.text: A war fought between groups inside the same country
choices.feedback: A is correct because right after the bold word 'civil war,' the passage says 'a war fought between groups inside the same country,' which is the definition.

choices.text: A lasting impact that a person leaves behind them
choices.feedback: B is wrong because 'a lasting impact a person leaves behind' is the definition of 'legacy,' the other bold word in the passage.

choices.text: A strong leader who takes control of the government
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the passage never defines 'civil war' as a strong leader taking control — that describes Augustus, not the meaning of the word.

choices.text: A secret plan made by senators to save the republic
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the senators' plan is part of the story, not the definition of 'civil war.'

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: Based on the passage, how is a dictator different from the leaders of a republic?

choices.text: A dictator has total control, while republic leaders are chosen by the citizens.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage clearly says a dictator has 'total control,' while a republic lets citizens choose their leaders.

choices.text: A dictator is born into nobility, while republic leaders are famous generals.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because being born into a noble family describes Caesar personally, and the passage never says republic leaders are famous generals.

choices.text: A dictator fights in fierce wars, while republic leaders stay in the Senate.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the passage never separates dictators and republic leaders by who fights in wars — Caesar was both a general and a political leader.

choices.text: A dictator rules for a few months, while republic leaders rule for their whole lives.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the passage says dictators in the past ruled for only a few months, not republic leaders — it's the opposite of what this answer says.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: Which heading would best help a reader quickly find why Caesar's death led to a big change in Rome's government?

choices.text: Caesar's victories in Gaul made Rome larger
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because Caesar's victories in Gaul made Rome's territory bigger, but that is not about why his death changed Rome's government.

choices.text: Caesar's killing helped end the republic
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage explains that Caesar's assassination sparked a civil war and ended the republic, which is the big change in Rome's government.

choices.text: Caesar's friends planned a secret attack
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the secret plan tells us how Caesar was killed, but not what big change happened to Rome's government after his death.

choices.text: Caesar's name became words for rulers
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because Caesar's name becoming words like 'Kaiser' and 'Tsar' is about his legacy in language, not about the change in Rome's government.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: Which list shows the main events of Caesar's life in the order they happened?

choices.text: He won many battles, he gained total power, and he was killed.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage shows Caesar won battles from 58–50 BC, became dictator for life in 44 BC, and was then assassinated — battles, then power, then death.

choices.text: He gained total power, he won many battles, and he was killed.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because Caesar won his famous battles in Gaul before he ever gained total power as dictator.

choices.text: He became an emperor, he won many battles, and he was killed.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the passage tells us Caesar was never made an emperor — that was Augustus, who came after him.

choices.text: He was killed, he gained total power, and he won many battles.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because Caesar's assassination happened at the very end of his story, not at the beginning.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: Based on the passage, how were Julius Caesar and his adopted son Augustus alike?

choices.text: They both held total power over Rome.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage says Caesar was 'dictator for life' with total control, and Augustus became Rome's first emperor — so both held total power over Rome.

choices.text: They both fought fierce tribes in Gaul.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because only Caesar spent eight years fighting in Gaul; the passage never mentions Augustus fighting there.

choices.text: They both were killed by the senators.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because Caesar was killed by senators, but the passage says Augustus took power after the civil war and was never assassinated.

choices.text: They both were chosen by the citizens.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because neither man was chosen by citizens — Caesar was declared dictator and Augustus took power after a war, not through a vote.

syllabus_metadata.title: Julius Caesar: The General Who Changed Rome Forever

assessments.title: Augustus and the Golden Age: The Roman Empire Begins

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Augustus: The Teenager Who Built an Empire

An Eighteen-Year-Old Steps Forward

A man lay dead on the marble floor of the Roman Senate. Julius Caesar had been stabbed by his own allies. Across the city, an eighteen-year-old named Octavian heard the news. Caesar had adopted him and named him as his heir. Now Octavian had a choice: hide, or fight for control of Rome.

For thirteen years, Octavian battled one rival after another. But he was patient and smart, and he outlasted them all.
stimulus.content_text:
Augustus: The Teenager Who Built an EmpireAn Eighteen-Year-Old Steps Forward
A man lay dead on the marble floor of the Roman Senate. Julius Caesar had been stabbed by his own allies. Across the city, an eighteen-year-old named Octavian heard the news. Caesar had adopted him and named him as his heir. Now Octavian had a choice: hide, or fight for control of Rome.
For thirteen years, Octavian battled one rival after another. But he was patient and smart, and he outlasted them all.

items.prompt: According to the text, what choice did Octavian face after Caesar was killed?

choices.text: stay in Rome or move to a new city
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage never says anything about Octavian thinking about moving to a new city.

choices.text: hide or fight for control of Rome
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage uses those exact words — 'hide, or fight for control of Rome' — to describe Octavian's choice.

choices.text: join the Senate or lead the army
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage never mentions Octavian choosing between joining the Senate or leading the army.

choices.text: run away from Rome or help Caesar
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because Caesar was already dead when Octavian heard the news, so helping Caesar was not an option.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
A New Kind of Leader

In 27 BC, Octavian became the first emperor of Rome — a single ruler with power over the entire government. The Senate gave him a new name: Augustus, meaning "the deeply respected one."

Actually, Romans hated the very word "king." Hundreds of years earlier, they had thrown out their last king and promised never to have another. He also remembered that Caesar had been killed partly because people feared he wanted to be king. So Augustus called himself the "first citizen" instead. He kept the Senate meeting as usual. But everyone knew he held the real power.

This moment ended the Roman Republic — the old system where elected leaders shared power, like a team making decisions together. In its place rose the Roman Empire, where one person ruled alone.
stimulus.content_text:
A New Kind of Leader
In 27 BC, Octavian became the first emperor of Rome — a single ruler with power over the entire government. The Senate gave him a new name: Augustus, meaning "the deeply respected one."
Actually, Romans hated the very word "king." Hundreds of years earlier, they had thrown out their last king and promised never to have another. He also remembered that Caesar had been killed partly because people feared he wanted to be king. So Augustus called himself the "first citizen" instead. He kept the Senate meeting as usual. But everyone knew he held the real power.
This moment ended the Roman Republic — the old system where elected leaders shared power, like a team making decisions together. In its place rose the Roman Empire, where one person ruled alone.

items.prompt: Based on the text, why did Augustus call himself the "first citizen"?

choices.text: He wanted to share his power with a team.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because Augustus did not share his power — the passage says 'everyone knew he held the real power,' even while the Senate kept meeting.

choices.text: He knew the people were afraid of kings.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage explains that Romans hated kings and that Caesar was killed for seeming like one, so Augustus used 'first citizen' to avoid frightening people.

choices.text: He wanted the Senate to give him a name.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the Senate gave Augustus the name 'Augustus,' not 'first citizen' — Augustus chose that title himself for a different reason.

choices.text: He wanted to be the only person in charge.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because being the only person in charge describes what Augustus actually did, but it is not the reason he chose the title 'first citizen.'

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Two Hundred Years of Peace

Before Augustus, Romans had suffered through decades of bloody civil wars. Augustus brought order. The long calm that followed is called the Pax Romana, a Latin phrase meaning "Roman Peace." It lasted about two hundred years, across the reigns of many emperors after Augustus.

Peace did not mean nothing bad happened. There were still battles along the empire's borders. But most people living inside the empire went their whole lives without seeing a major war. Merchants traveled safely on Roman roads. Trade grew. Cities buzzed with activity.
stimulus.content_text:
Two Hundred Years of Peace
Before Augustus, Romans had suffered through decades of bloody civil wars. Augustus brought order. The long calm that followed is called the Pax Romana, a Latin phrase meaning "Roman Peace." It lasted about two hundred years, across the reigns of many emperors after Augustus.
Peace did not mean nothing bad happened. There were still battles along the empire's borders. But most people living inside the empire went their whole lives without seeing a major war. Merchants traveled safely on Roman roads. Trade grew. Cities buzzed with activity.

items.prompt: According to the text, what happened before Augustus brought order to the Roman Empire?

choices.text: Romans fought in bloody civil wars.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage directly says 'Before Augustus, Romans had suffered through decades of bloody civil wars.'

choices.text: Merchants traveled on Roman roads.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because merchants traveling safely on Roman roads happened after Augustus brought peace, not before.

choices.text: Emperors ruled for two hundred years.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the two hundred years of emperors ruling describes the Pax Romana that came after Augustus, not what happened before him.

choices.text: Battles happened along the borders.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the passage says border battles happened during the peaceful Pax Romana period, not specifically before Augustus.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
A City of Marble

Augustus poured energy into rebuilding Rome itself. He once bragged that he "found Rome a city of brick and left it a city of marble." He built grand temples, theaters, and public buildings. Under his rule, Rome grew to over one million people — the largest city in the ancient world.

Augustus also cared about art and writing. He supported poets like Virgil, who wrote the Aeneid — a long, grand story in poem form about how Rome was first founded. Another poet, Horace, wrote verses that people still read today, over two thousand years later. Augustus believed a great civilization needed great stories, not just great armies.
stimulus.content_text:
A City of Marble
Augustus poured energy into rebuilding Rome itself. He once bragged that he "found Rome a city of brick and left it a city of marble." He built grand temples, theaters, and public buildings. Under his rule, Rome grew to over one million people — the largest city in the ancient world.
Augustus also cared about art and writing. He supported poets like Virgil, who wrote the Aeneid — a long, grand story in poem form about how Rome was first founded. Another poet, Horace, wrote verses that people still read today, over two thousand years later. Augustus believed a great civilization needed great stories, not just great armies.

items.prompt: Based on the article, how were Virgil and Horace alike?

choices.text: They both built grand temples out of marble.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because building grand temples out of marble was something Augustus did, not the poets Virgil or Horace.

choices.text: They both were poets supported by Augustus.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage clearly says Augustus supported both Virgil and Horace, and describes both of them as poets.

choices.text: They both ruled the largest city in the world.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because Augustus was the ruler of Rome, not Virgil or Horace.

choices.text: They both wrote stories about great armies.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because Virgil wrote about how Rome was founded, and the passage does not say either poet wrote about great armies.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
An Empire That Stretched Across the World

Under Augustus, the Roman Empire reached from Spain in the west to the deserts of the Middle East in the east. It stretched from the forests of Germany in the north to the sands of Africa in the south. Later emperors expanded it even further, adding lands like Britain. Millions of people used Roman coins, walked on Roman roads, and followed Roman law. Most of them spoke Latin, the Roman language.

Augustus ruled for over forty years until his death in AD 14. He had taken a broken, war-torn government and turned it into the most powerful empire the world had ever seen. He did it not through cruelty, but through careful planning and patience.
stimulus.content_text:
An Empire That Stretched Across the World
Under Augustus, the Roman Empire reached from Spain in the west to the deserts of the Middle East in the east. It stretched from the forests of Germany in the north to the sands of Africa in the south. Later emperors expanded it even further, adding lands like Britain. Millions of people used Roman coins, walked on Roman roads, and followed Roman law. Most of them spoke Latin, the Roman language.
Augustus ruled for over forty years until his death in AD 14. He had taken a broken, war-torn government and turned it into the most powerful empire the world had ever seen. He did it not through cruelty, but through careful planning and patience.

items.prompt: According to the passage, what is Latin?

choices.text: a type of Roman coin
choices.feedback: Roman coins are mentioned in the passage, but Latin is not a coin — the passage clearly says Latin is a language.

choices.text: the Roman language
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says 'Most of them spoke Latin, the Roman language,' which tells us Latin is the Roman language.

choices.text: a kind of Roman road
choices.feedback: Roman roads are mentioned in the passage, but Latin is not a road — it is a language that people spoke.

choices.text: a set of Roman laws
choices.feedback: Roman law is mentioned in the passage, but Latin is not a set of laws — it is the language Romans spoke.

items.title: Section 6 - Guiding Question 5

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
What Peace Can Build

What Augustus showed is what peace can create — marble cities, epic poems, safe roads stretching thousands of miles. The next time you drive on a highway connecting two faraway cities, you are using an idea the Romans practiced first, on stone roads built during the peace Augustus began.
stimulus.content_text:
What Peace Can Build
What Augustus showed is what peace can create — marble cities, epic poems, safe roads stretching thousands of miles. The next time you drive on a highway connecting two faraway cities, you are using an idea the Romans practiced first, on stone roads built during the peace Augustus began.

items.prompt: Which detail from the passage best supports the idea that peace helped the Roman Empire grow?

choices.text: Augustus was a powerful leader of Rome
choices.feedback: The passage doesn't say Augustus was a powerful leader — it focuses on what peace created, not on Augustus's power.

choices.text: Romans built stone roads during peacetime
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly says stone roads were built during the peace Augustus began, showing how peace helped Rome grow and connect.

choices.text: People today still drive on long highways
choices.feedback: This talks about highways today, which is a modern example the author uses to help us relate, but it doesn't show the Roman Empire growing.

choices.text: The Roman Empire lasted for many years
choices.feedback: The passage never mentions how long the Roman Empire lasted, so this detail isn't in the text at all.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: Based on the article, how was the time before Augustus different from the time he ruled?

choices.text: Before him, leaders shared power and fought wars; under him, one ruler brought lasting peace.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the article clearly says Rome had civil wars and shared leadership before Augustus, and then he became one ruler who brought lasting peace.

choices.text: Before him, poets wrote long and grand stories; under him, citizens only cared about armies.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the article says Augustus supported poets like Virgil during his rule, not before it.

choices.text: Before him, builders made the city from marble; under him, workers used only red bricks.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the article says Augustus found Rome as a city of brick and turned it into marble, so marble came during his rule, not before.

choices.text: Before him, merchants traveled safely on stone roads; under him, soldiers closed borders to trade.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the article says merchants traveled safely on Roman roads during the peace Augustus created, not before his rule.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: Based on the article, why did the Pax Romana help cities like Rome grow bigger and busier?

choices.text: Augustus forced all people to move into cities and work on buildings.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage never says Augustus forced anyone to move into cities — people came because of peace and opportunity.

choices.text: Peace let merchants trade safely and leaders build new things.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly says merchants traveled safely on Roman roads, trade grew, and Augustus built grand buildings, all of which made cities grow and buzz with activity.

choices.text: The Senate gave Augustus money to pay soldiers to guard cities.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage does not mention the Senate giving Augustus money to pay soldiers to guard cities.

choices.text: Poets wrote stories that made people want to visit Roman cities.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because while the passage mentions poets like Virgil and Horace, it never says their stories caused people to visit or move to Roman cities.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: Based on the article, which of these events happened first?

choices.text: Augustus rebuilt Rome with marble buildings.
choices.feedback: Augustus rebuilt Rome with marble buildings during his reign, which happened many years after Caesar was killed.

choices.text: The Pax Romana brought two hundred years of peace.
choices.feedback: The Pax Romana started after Augustus became emperor, so it came after Caesar's death and after Octavian got his new name.

choices.text: Julius Caesar was killed in the Roman Senate.
choices.feedback: This is correct! Caesar's death is the very first event in the article — it's what set everything else in motion.

choices.text: The Senate gave Octavian the name Augustus.
choices.feedback: The Senate gave Octavian the name Augustus in 27 BC, which was thirteen years after Caesar was killed and Octavian started fighting for power.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: The main idea of the passage is that Augustus built a great and lasting empire. How does the author compare the Roman Empire to our world today to support this main idea?

choices.text: By showing that we still read their poems and build roads like they did.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage directly says we still read Roman poems and that our highways come from the same idea as Roman roads, connecting ancient Rome to our lives today.

choices.text: By showing that we still use their old coins to buy things in our towns.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the passage mentions Roman coins were used inside the empire long ago, but never says people use them to buy things in our towns today.

choices.text: By showing that we still have powerful emperors who rule over the whole world.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the passage never says the world has emperors today — that was something special to ancient Rome.

choices.text: By showing that we still fight the exact same wars along our country's borders.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the passage never says we fight the same wars the Romans did along their borders.

syllabus_metadata.title: Augustus and the Golden Age: The Roman Empire Begins

assessments.title: Patricians and Plebeians: Rich and Poor in Rome

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Two Romes: The Rich, the Poor, and the Fight for Fairness

A City Walks Out

One morning around 494 BCE, the streets of Rome went silent. Thousands of ordinary workers packed up and marched out of the city. They climbed a hill outside Rome's walls, sat down, and refused to come back.

Why would an entire city's workers just leave? To understand, you need to know that Rome was really two worlds packed inside one city.
stimulus.content_text:
Two Romes: The Rich, the Poor, and the Fight for FairnessA City Walks Out
One morning around 494 BCE, the streets of Rome went silent. Thousands of ordinary workers packed up and marched out of the city. They climbed a hill outside Rome's walls, sat down, and refused to come back.

Why would an entire city's workers just leave? To understand, you need to know that Rome was really two worlds packed inside one city.

items.prompt: According to the text, even though Rome was only one city, what was it really like inside?

choices.text: It held two different worlds.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage directly says Rome was 'two worlds packed inside one city,' meaning it had two very different worlds inside even though it was just one city.

choices.text: It had one very large hill.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the hill mentioned in the passage is where the workers marched outside Rome's walls, not something that describes what Rome was like inside.

choices.text: It was a totally silent place.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the streets only went silent on that one morning when the workers left — the passage does not say Rome was always a silent place.

choices.text: It kept all workers outside.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the workers started out living and working inside Rome and then chose to march out themselves — no one was keeping them outside.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Patricians: The Powerful Few

At the top sat the patricians — wealthy families who held almost all of Rome's power. Think of them as a small club whose members made every rule. Patrician families could trace their relatives all the way back to Rome's first senators. They owned huge estates, which were stretches of land bigger than many farms put together. They wore fine clothes and ate expensive food. They believed they were born to lead.

Actually, that life belonged to very few people. Most Romans lived nothing like that.
stimulus.content_text:
Patricians: The Powerful Few
At the top sat the patricians — wealthy families who held almost all of Rome's power. Think of them as a small club whose members made every rule. Patrician families could trace their relatives all the way back to Rome's first senators. They owned huge estates, which were stretches of land bigger than many farms put together. They wore fine clothes and ate expensive food. They believed they were born to lead.

Actually, that life belonged to very few people. Most Romans lived nothing like that.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, why did patricians believe they were born to lead?

choices.text: because they ate expensive food every day
choices.feedback: Eating expensive food showed they were wealthy, but the passage never says that's why they believed they were born to lead.

choices.text: because most Romans lived nothing like them
choices.feedback: The passage does say most Romans lived differently, but that fact doesn't explain why patricians felt they were born to lead.

choices.text: because they came from Rome's first senators
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage says patrician families traced their relatives back to Rome's very first senators, which made them feel they were meant to rule.

choices.text: because they made friends with other leaders
choices.feedback: The passage never mentions patricians making friends with other leaders, so this answer has no support from the text.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Plebeians: The Working Majority

The rest of Rome's free citizens were called plebeians — the common people. Plebeians were farmers, merchants (people who bought and sold goods), craftsmen, bakers, and laborers. They made up most of the population. They paid taxes. They fought in Rome's army. But for a long time, they had almost no voice in how Rome was run. Patricians wrote the laws and filled every important office.

But you have no say in the rules you must follow. The plebeians thought this was deeply unfair — and they decided to do something about it.
stimulus.content_text:
Plebeians: The Working Majority
The rest of Rome's free citizens were called plebeians — the common people. Plebeians were farmers, merchants (people who bought and sold goods), craftsmen, bakers, and laborers. They made up most of the population. They paid taxes. They fought in Rome's army. But for a long time, they had almost no voice in how Rome was run. Patricians wrote the laws and filled every important office.

But you have no say in the rules you must follow. The plebeians thought this was deeply unfair — and they decided to do something about it.

items.prompt: According to the text, what did the patricians do before the plebeians decided the rules were unfair?

choices.text: They wrote the laws and filled offices.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage directly states that 'Patricians wrote the laws and filled every important office,' which is exactly what this answer says.

choices.text: They decided to change the unfair rules.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because it was the plebeians who decided to do something about the unfair rules, not the patricians.

choices.text: They fought as soldiers in Rome's army.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage says the plebeians paid taxes and fought in Rome's army, not the patricians.

choices.text: They asked the common people for advice.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage says the plebeians had almost no voice in how Rome was run, meaning the patricians did not ask them for advice.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
The Walkout That Changed Rome

That is exactly what happened on that quiet morning. The plebeians left Rome and camped on a hill. Without workers, soldiers, and craftsmen, the patricians could not run the city.

The patricians gave in. Plebeians won the right to elect their own leaders, called tribunes — officials whose only job was to protect ordinary people. A tribune could stand up in the Senate and shout "Veto!" which meant "I forbid it!" That single word could stop an unfair law cold.
stimulus.content_text:
The Walkout That Changed Rome
That is exactly what happened on that quiet morning. The plebeians left Rome and camped on a hill. Without workers, soldiers, and craftsmen, the patricians could not run the city.

The patricians gave in. Plebeians won the right to elect their own leaders, called tribunes — officials whose only job was to protect ordinary people. A tribune could stand up in the Senate and shout "Veto!" which meant "I forbid it!" That single word could stop an unfair law cold.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, how are workers, soldiers, and craftsmen alike?

choices.text: They were all needed to run the city.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage directly says that without workers, soldiers, and craftsmen, the patricians could not run the city — so all three were needed.

choices.text: They were all chosen to lead the Senate.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the passage never says workers, soldiers, or craftsmen were chosen to lead the Senate — that was not their role.

choices.text: They were all trusted to stop the laws.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because stopping unfair laws was the job of the tribunes, not workers, soldiers, or craftsmen.

choices.text: They were all allowed to shout the word.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because shouting 'Veto!' was something tribunes could do, not workers, soldiers, or craftsmen.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Two Very Different Homes

Daily life looked completely different depending on your place in Roman society. Wealthy families lived in a domus, a private house built around an open courtyard with gardens, fountains, and painted walls. Enslaved people cooked, cleaned, and cared for the children inside these homes. Enslaved people were not the same as plebeians. Plebeians were free citizens with rights. Enslaved people were not citizens at all and had no rights — they were treated as property.

Many plebeians lived in insulae, which were crowded apartment buildings stacked five or six stories high — like a tower of shoeboxes. The rooms were small and dark. People cooked over open flames inside wooden walls, so fires broke out often. Upper floors had no running water. The noise never stopped.

Wealthy families lived in a domus, a private house built around an open courtyard with gardens, fountains, and painted walls. Enslaved people cooked, cleaned, and cared for the children inside these homes. Enslaved people were not the same as plebeians. Plebeians were free citizens with rights. Enslaved people were not citizens at all and had no rights — they were treated as property.

Many plebeians lived in insulae, which were crowded apartment buildings stacked five or six stories high — like a tower of shoeboxes. The rooms were small and dark. People cooked over open flames inside wooden walls, so fires broke out often. Upper floors had no running water. The noise never stopped.

items.prompt: According to the text, what is a domus?

choices.text: a crowded apartment building for plebeians
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because a crowded apartment building is called an insulae — that's where plebeians lived, not wealthy families.

choices.text: a private house built around an open courtyard
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly says a domus is 'a private house built around an open courtyard,' which matches this answer word for word.

choices.text: a tall tower stacked five or six stories high
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the building stacked five or six stories high describes the insulae, not the domus.

choices.text: a garden with fountains and painted walls
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because gardens and fountains were things found inside the domus, but they describe features of the house, not what the domus actually is.

items.title: Section 6 - Guiding Question 5

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
One City, Shared and Divided

Still, patricians and plebeians shared parts of life. They worshipped the same gods and celebrated the same festivals. They cheered together at gladiator contests, public fights where trained fighters battled in giant arenas while huge crowds watched. They shopped at the same markets and walked the same stone streets.

Over the centuries, the sharp line between the two groups began to blur. Some plebeian families grew wealthy and gained power.

The next time someone tells you that regular people can't change unfair rules, remember those Roman bakers, farmers, and soldiers sitting on a hill outside their own city — waiting, together, until the powerful had no choice but to listen.
stimulus.content_text:
One City, Shared and Divided
Still, patricians and plebeians shared parts of life. They worshipped the same gods and celebrated the same festivals. They cheered together at gladiator contests, public fights where trained fighters battled in giant arenas while huge crowds watched. They shopped at the same markets and walked the same stone streets.

Over the centuries, the sharp line between the two groups began to blur. Some plebeian families grew wealthy and gained power.

The next time someone tells you that regular people can't change unfair rules, remember those Roman bakers, farmers, and soldiers sitting on a hill outside their own city — waiting, together, until the powerful had no choice but to listen.

items.prompt: Which detail from the passage best supports the idea that patricians and plebeians shared parts of their lives?

choices.text: They celebrated the same festivals together.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage directly says they 'celebrated the same festivals,' which is a clear example of them sharing parts of life.

choices.text: They lived in the same large houses.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the passage never mentions anything about the kinds of houses patricians and plebeians lived in.

choices.text: They fought together in the giant arenas.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage says trained gladiators did the fighting while the crowds watched — patricians and plebeians didn't fight together.

choices.text: They waited together on a hill outside the city.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because sitting on the hill was something the plebeians did as a protest, not something both groups did together.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: Based on the article, what was one major difference between patricians and plebeians?

choices.text: Patricians made the rules, while plebeians did the everyday work.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the article clearly says patricians made all the rules and held every important office, while plebeians were the workers, farmers, and soldiers who had almost no voice in how Rome was run.

choices.text: Patricians fought in the army, while plebeians stayed on the farms.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the article says plebeians fought in Rome's army, not patricians.

choices.text: Patricians lived in tall buildings, while plebeians had large gardens.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect and actually backwards — plebeians lived in tall apartment buildings called insulae, while patricians had private homes with gardens and fountains.

choices.text: Patricians were treated as property, while plebeians were free citizens.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the article says enslaved people were treated as property, not patricians — and plebeians were already described as free citizens with rights.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: Which heading would help you find why the plebeians won new rights after they left Rome?

choices.text: Patricians: The Powerful Few
choices.feedback: Section A is about how powerful and wealthy the patricians were, not about the plebeians winning new rights.

choices.text: The Walkout That Changed Rome
choices.feedback: This is correct! Section B tells us that after the walkout, the patricians gave in and plebeians won the right to elect tribunes who could stop unfair laws.

choices.text: Two Very Different Homes
choices.feedback: Section C describes what homes looked like for rich and poor Romans, not about the rights plebeians gained.

choices.text: One City, Shared and Divided
choices.feedback: Section D talks about things both groups shared, like festivals and markets, not about what changed after the walkout.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: Which detail best supports the main idea that the common people fought for fairness?

choices.text: The plebeians left Rome and camped on a hill.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the walkout was the main action the plebeians took to fight for fairness, and it worked — they won new rights as a result.

choices.text: Wealthy families lived in private houses with painted walls.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because painted walls and gardens describe a wealthy family's home, which is about how rich people lived, not about fighting for fairness.

choices.text: Trained fighters battled in giant arenas while crowds watched.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because gladiator contests were something both groups watched for fun together, not an example of common people fighting for their rights.

choices.text: Patrician families wore fine clothes and ate expensive food.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because patricians wearing fine clothes and eating expensive food shows their wealth, not anything about the plebeians' fight for fairness.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: How were the lives of patricians and plebeians different in the way they lived at home?

choices.text: Patricians lived in houses with gardens, while plebeians lived in small, crowded rooms.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage clearly says patricians had private houses with gardens and fountains, while plebeians lived in small, crowded apartment rooms.

choices.text: Patricians lived on farms outside Rome, while plebeians lived inside the city walls.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the passage never says patricians lived on farms outside Rome — they lived inside the city in fancy houses called a domus.

choices.text: Patricians lived in tall buildings with fountains, while plebeians lived in painted houses.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because it mixes up the details — patricians had the fountains and painted walls, while plebeians lived in the tall, crowded buildings.

choices.text: Patricians lived in rooms with no water, while plebeians lived in homes with courtyards.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because it has it backwards — the passage says plebeians on upper floors had no running water, not the patricians, who had comfortable homes with courtyards.

syllabus_metadata.title: Patricians and Plebeians: Rich and Poor in Rome

assessments.title: The Forum and the Colosseum: Heart of Roman Life

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Forum and the Colosseum: Two Hearts of Ancient Rome

A Morning in the Roman Forum

A man shouts the price of fresh fish. Somewhere nearby, two leaders argue about a new law. You are standing in the Roman Forum on a busy morning, nearly two thousand years ago.

The Forum was a large open plaza — a public square, like a town park where everyone gathers — surrounded by government buildings, temples, and shops. It sat in a valley between two of Rome's seven hills. For hundreds of years, the Forum was the center of Roman life. Elections happened here. Laws were announced here. Victory parades marched through its streets. If you wanted to know what was happening in Rome, you went to the Forum.

At one end stood the Senate House. Senators — leaders chosen to help make laws, like members of Congress today — met inside to debate and vote. You might think Rome was always ruled by one powerful emperor. Actually, for hundreds of years before that, Rome was a republic, a type of government where citizens voted for their leaders. Only later did emperors take control. Over time, different emperors added new buildings to the Forum, each one grander than the last.

Grand temples to the gods rose on every side of the Forum. The Forum was not just a place for business. It was a symbol of Roman pride and power.
