The Forum and the Colosseum: Two Hearts of Ancient RomeA Morning in the Roman Forum
A man shouts the price of fresh fish. Somewhere nearby, two leaders argue about a new law. You are standing in the Roman Forum on a busy morning, nearly two thousand years ago.

The Forum was a large open plaza — a public square, like a town park where everyone gathers — surrounded by government buildings, temples, and shops. It sat in a valley between two of Rome's seven hills. For hundreds of years, the Forum was the center of Roman life. Elections happened here. Laws were announced here. Victory parades marched through its streets. If you wanted to know what was happening in Rome, you went to the Forum.

At one end stood the Senate House. Senators — leaders chosen to help make laws, like members of Congress today — met inside to debate and vote. You might think Rome was always ruled by one powerful emperor. Actually, for hundreds of years before that, Rome was a republic, a type of government where citizens voted for their leaders. Only later did emperors take control. Over time, different emperors added new buildings to the Forum, each one grander than the last.

Grand temples to the gods rose on every side of the Forum. The Forum was not just a place for business. It was a symbol of Roman pride and power.

items.prompt: According to the text, how was Rome's government different before emperors took control?

choices.text: Citizens voted for their leaders.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage says Rome was a republic where 'citizens voted for their leaders' before emperors took over.

choices.text: Senators made laws without voting.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because senators actually did vote on laws — that was an important part of how the republic worked.

choices.text: Two leaders argued to be the king.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the passage never says two leaders argued to become king — that detail is not in the text.

choices.text: Gods chose the rules for the city.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the passage never says gods chose the rules; gods were worshipped in temples, but people voted for their leaders.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
The Colosseum: A Giant Arena

Romans did not only gather for politics. They loved entertainment. And the greatest entertainment building in the ancient world was the Colosseum. This enormous oval-shaped arena could hold about 50,000 spectators — people who come to watch an event, like fans filling a football stadium.

The Colosseum was an engineering marvel, a building so cleverly designed that it amazed everyone who saw it. It had four levels of arched entrances. Each gate was numbered so people could find their seats fast, just like a modern sports stadium. A huge cloth awning — a cover like a giant shade umbrella — could be stretched over the top to block the hot sun. Beneath the arena floor, a maze of underground tunnels and rooms hid animals and fighters. Trapdoors in the floor let animals burst up into the arena, surprising the crowd.
stimulus.content_text:
The Colosseum: A Giant Arena
Romans did not only gather for politics. They loved entertainment. And the greatest entertainment building in the ancient world was the Colosseum. This enormous oval-shaped arena could hold about 50,000 spectators — people who come to watch an event, like fans filling a football stadium.

The Colosseum was an engineering marvel, a building so cleverly designed that it amazed everyone who saw it. It had four levels of arched entrances. Each gate was numbered so people could find their seats fast, just like a modern sports stadium. A huge cloth awning — a cover like a giant shade umbrella — could be stretched over the top to block the hot sun. Beneath the arena floor, a maze of underground tunnels and rooms hid animals and fighters. Trapdoors in the floor let animals burst up into the arena, surprising the crowd.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, why were trapdoors built into the arena floor of the Colosseum?

choices.text: so workers could clean the arena after each event
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage never mentions anything about cleaning the arena — that idea isn't in the text at all.

choices.text: so animals could burst up and surprise the crowd
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly states that 'trapdoors in the floor let animals burst up into the arena, surprising the crowd.'

choices.text: so spectators could enter the arena more quickly
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because it was the numbered gates, not the trapdoors, that helped spectators find their seats quickly.

choices.text: so fighters could escape from the animals below
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage says the trapdoors let animals come up into the arena, not that fighters used them to escape.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
The Gladiators

Gladiators were fighters who battled each other or wild animals while thousands watched. You might picture gladiators as brave warriors who chose to fight for fame. Here's the surprising part: most gladiators were enslaved people or prisoners. They did not choose this life freely. The fights were bloody and dangerous, and their life was harsh and short.

Not everyone approved. As centuries passed, many Romans began to question whether such violent entertainment was right. A religion called Christianity — a faith based on the teachings of Jesus — spread across Rome. Its followers believed all human life was precious. Eventually, the games were stopped for good.
stimulus.content_text:
The Gladiators
Gladiators were fighters who battled each other or wild animals while thousands watched. You might picture gladiators as brave warriors who chose to fight for fame. Here's the surprising part: most gladiators were enslaved people or prisoners. They did not choose this life freely. The fights were bloody and dangerous, and their life was harsh and short.

Not everyone approved. As centuries passed, many Romans began to question whether such violent entertainment was right. A religion called Christianity — a faith based on the teachings of Jesus — spread across Rome. Its followers believed all human life was precious. Eventually, the games were stopped for good.

items.prompt: According to the text, which event happened after many Romans began to question the violent games?

choices.text: Gladiators fought wild animals in the arena.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because gladiators fighting wild animals is described at the start of the passage, long before Romans began questioning the games.

choices.text: The games were stopped for good.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage says that after Romans questioned the violent games and Christianity spread, 'the games were stopped for good' — that is exactly what happened next.

choices.text: Thousands watched the bloody fights.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because thousands watching the bloody fights happened at the beginning of the story, not as a result of people questioning the games.

choices.text: Most gladiators became famous warriors.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage says most gladiators were enslaved people or prisoners, not warriors who became famous by choice.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Still Standing

Today, the Roman Forum is a field of ruins. Broken columns and crumbling walls are all that remain. The Colosseum still rises in the center of Rome — partly broken, partly standing — and about seven million people visit it every year.

But look closely at those ruins. The Senate House where Romans debated laws? Your own government has a Senate that works in a similar way. The numbered gates at the Colosseum? Every stadium you have ever visited borrowed that idea. Ancient Rome didn't just disappear. Pieces of it are hiding in your world right now.
stimulus.content_text:
Still Standing
Today, the Roman Forum is a field of ruins. Broken columns and crumbling walls are all that remain. The Colosseum still rises in the center of Rome — partly broken, partly standing — and about seven million people visit it every year.

But look closely at those ruins. The Senate House where Romans debated laws? Your own government has a Senate that works in a similar way. The numbered gates at the Colosseum? Every stadium you have ever visited borrowed that idea. Ancient Rome didn't just disappear. Pieces of it are hiding in your world right now.

items.prompt: Based on the text, how are stadiums today similar to the ancient Colosseum?

choices.text: They both use numbers on their gates.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage says every stadium borrowed the idea of numbered gates from the Colosseum.

choices.text: They both have crumbling stone walls.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because modern stadiums are not crumbling — only the ancient ruins look that way.

choices.text: They both are used to debate new laws.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because stadiums are for sports and shows, not for debating laws like a Senate.

choices.text: They both sit in the center of Rome.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because modern stadiums are built all around the world, not in the center of Rome.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: Based on the article, how were senators different from gladiators?

choices.text: Senators were enslaved, but gladiators were chosen leaders.
choices.feedback: This is backwards — the article says most gladiators were enslaved people or prisoners, not senators.

choices.text: Senators made the laws, but gladiators fought in the games.
choices.feedback: This is correct! The article clearly says senators met to debate and vote on laws, while gladiators were fighters who battled in the arena.

choices.text: Senators built the temples, but gladiators built the arenas.
choices.feedback: The article never says senators built temples or that gladiators built arenas — that's not in the passage at all.

choices.text: Senators lived long ago, but gladiators live in Rome today.
choices.feedback: Both senators and gladiators lived in ancient Rome long ago — gladiators do not live in Rome today.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: A student wants to find out why the Romans built a giant arena and why the violent fights finally ended. Which two headings should the student look under to find these causes?

choices.text: "A Morning in the Roman Forum" and "Still Standing"
choices.feedback: Option A is wrong because 'A Morning in the Roman Forum' is about politics and laws, not why the arena was built, and 'Still Standing' only describes ruins and modern connections.

choices.text: "The Colosseum: A Giant Arena" and "The Gladiators"
choices.feedback: Option B is correct because 'The Colosseum: A Giant Arena' explains Romans built it for entertainment, and 'The Gladiators' explains the fights ended because of the spread of Christianity and the belief that human life is precious.

choices.text: "A Morning in the Roman Forum" and "The Gladiators"
choices.feedback: Option C is wrong because 'A Morning in the Roman Forum' is about Roman government and daily life, not about why the Colosseum was built.

choices.text: "The Colosseum: A Giant Arena" and "Still Standing"
choices.feedback: Option D is wrong because 'Still Standing' talks about what the ruins look like today and how Rome influenced modern life, but it does not explain why the violent fights ended.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: Which list of key details summarizes the events in the passage in the order they happened?

choices.text: Citizens voted for leaders, emperors took control, the violent games ended, and the buildings became ruins.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage clearly shows this order: republic with voting came first, then emperors, then the games ended, then the buildings became ruins.

choices.text: Emperors took control, citizens voted for leaders, the buildings became ruins, and the violent games ended.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because it has the order backwards — citizens voted for leaders first, and emperors came to power later, not the other way around.

choices.text: Citizens voted for leaders, gladiators chose to fight, the violent games ended, and the buildings became ruins.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the passage says most gladiators did NOT choose to fight — they were enslaved people or prisoners who had no choice.

choices.text: Animals surprised the crowd, gladiators fought each other, the violent games ended, and the buildings became ruins.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because it only includes events from inside the Colosseum and leaves out the important parts about Rome's government and how it changed over time.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: Based on the article, what is one way the Roman Forum and the Colosseum are similar?

choices.text: Both places were built by emperors to hold wild animal fights.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the Forum was used for laws, elections, and business — not animal fights — and not everything was built by emperors.

choices.text: Both places gave us ideas that are still used in the world today.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the article says both places gave us ideas still used today, like our Senate from the Forum and numbered stadium gates from the Colosseum.

choices.text: Both places were used mostly for citizens to vote for new leaders.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because only the Forum was used for voting — the Colosseum was built for entertainment like gladiator fights, not for citizens to vote.

choices.text: Both places were completely destroyed when the games were stopped.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the article tells us the Colosseum is still partly standing and millions of people visit it, and the Forum still has ruins you can see today.

syllabus_metadata.title: The Forum and the Colosseum: Heart of Roman Life

assessments.title: Roads, Aqueducts, and Arches: Roman Engineering Marvels

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Built to Last: How Rome's Builders Changed the World

Roads That Outlived an Empire

Two thousand years ago, a Roman soldier stepped onto a stone road and marched all day long. That road is still there. You can walk on it today.

They built over 50,000 miles of them — enough to wrap around the earth twice.

These were not simple dirt paths. Roman workers dug deep trenches and filled them with layers of gravel and sand. Then they placed heavy, flat paving stones on top, like puzzle pieces locked together. The roads were straight, smooth, and tough enough to survive centuries of rain and heat.

Why go through all that trouble? Speed. Roman armies needed to march quickly across the empire. Straight, paved roads let soldiers move fast instead of slogging through mud. Merchants used the roads to carry food, cloth, and tools to distant cities. The saying "All roads lead to Rome" comes from this time. Every road in the empire connected back to the capital city.
stimulus.content_text:
Built to Last: How Rome's Builders Changed the WorldRoads That Outlived an Empire
Two thousand years ago, a Roman soldier stepped onto a stone road and marched all day long. That road is still there. You can walk on it today.

They built over 50,000 miles of them — enough to wrap around the earth twice.

These were not simple dirt paths. Roman workers dug deep trenches and filled them with layers of gravel and sand. Then they placed heavy, flat paving stones on top, like puzzle pieces locked together. The roads were straight, smooth, and tough enough to survive centuries of rain and heat.

Why go through all that trouble? Speed. Roman armies needed to march quickly across the empire. Straight, paved roads let soldiers move fast instead of slogging through mud. Merchants used the roads to carry food, cloth, and tools to distant cities. The saying "All roads lead to Rome" comes from this time. Every road in the empire connected back to the capital city.

items.prompt: According to the text, how were Roman roads different from simple dirt paths?

choices.text: They were built only for Roman soldiers
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says merchants also used the roads to carry food, cloth, and tools — not just soldiers.

choices.text: They were made with layers and stones
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage describes layers of gravel, sand, and heavy flat paving stones, which made Roman roads very different from simple dirt paths.

choices.text: They were used to carry food to Rome
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because carrying food describes how people used the roads, not how the roads were built differently from dirt paths.

choices.text: They were built in just a few months
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never mentions how long it took to build the roads.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Carrying Water Across the Sky

A huge city needs a lot of water. Rome's rivers and wells were not enough. So Roman builders created aqueducts — long channels that carried fresh water from mountain springs to the city, sometimes traveling more than fifty miles.

Most of the channel ran underground, like a buried garden hose. The water flowed gently downhill the whole way. But when the channel reached a valley, builders constructed tall, arched bridges to carry the water straight across. These bridges are the parts you see in photographs today, and some still stand.
stimulus.content_text:
Carrying Water Across the Sky
A huge city needs a lot of water. Rome's rivers and wells were not enough. So Roman builders created aqueducts — long channels that carried fresh water from mountain springs to the city, sometimes traveling more than fifty miles.

Most of the channel ran underground, like a buried garden hose. The water flowed gently downhill the whole way. But when the channel reached a valley, builders constructed tall, arched bridges to carry the water straight across. These bridges are the parts you see in photographs today, and some still stand.

items.prompt: The passage explains that Romans built amazing aqueducts to bring water to their city. Why does the author include the detail that Rome's rivers and wells were not enough?

choices.text: It explains why the city needed to build the aqueducts.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage uses the word 'so' right after mentioning rivers and wells weren't enough, showing that this is exactly the reason Romans decided to build aqueducts.

choices.text: It shows why the water flowed gently downhill the whole way.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because water flowing gently downhill explains how aqueducts worked, not why Rome's rivers and wells being insufficient matters.

choices.text: It describes why the builders made tall bridges over valleys.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the tall arched bridges were built to cross valleys, which is a completely separate detail from why Rome needed aqueducts at all.

choices.text: It proves why the mountain springs were fifty miles away.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the fifty miles describes how long the aqueducts were, and the passage never connects that distance to why the mountain springs were located there.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
The Secret of the Arch

Hold a ruler flat between two stacks of books. Press down in the middle. It bends and snaps. Now imagine curving that ruler into a half-circle shape. That curve is an arch — a rounded structure that spreads weight evenly across all its stones instead of pushing it straight down.

The Romans perfected arches and used them everywhere: in bridges, buildings, and aqueducts. The keystone — the wedge-shaped stone at the very top — locked every other stone in place, the way the last piece of a jigsaw puzzle holds the picture together. Pull out the keystone, and the whole arch falls.
stimulus.content_text:
The Secret of the Arch
Hold a ruler flat between two stacks of books. Press down in the middle. It bends and snaps. Now imagine curving that ruler into a half-circle shape. That curve is an arch — a rounded structure that spreads weight evenly across all its stones instead of pushing it straight down.

The Romans perfected arches and used them everywhere: in bridges, buildings, and aqueducts. The keystone — the wedge-shaped stone at the very top — locked every other stone in place, the way the last piece of a jigsaw puzzle holds the picture together. Pull out the keystone, and the whole arch falls.

items.prompt: According to the text, what happens to the whole arch if you pull out the keystone?

choices.text: The arch becomes stronger than before
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage says the arch falls apart without the keystone — it does NOT get stronger.

choices.text: The whole arch falls down
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly states 'Pull out the keystone, and the whole arch falls.'

choices.text: The other stones lock into place
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the keystone is what locks the other stones in place — without it, they cannot lock together on their own.

choices.text: The arch spreads weight more evenly
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because spreading weight evenly is what the arch does when it is whole and working, not what happens when the keystone is removed.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
A Recipe Stronger Than Stone

The Romans greatly improved concrete, a building material made by mixing volcanic ash (the powdery gray dust left behind by erupting volcanoes), a chalky powder called lime, and water. Earlier peoples had used simple forms of concrete, but the Romans made it much stronger by adding volcanic ash. This mixture dried harder than rock. Here's the surprising part: it could even harden underwater. That meant Romans could build strong walls for harbors and ports right in the sea.

Their most famous concrete building is the Pantheon, a temple in Rome with a massive rounded roof called a dome. It was built nearly two thousand years ago. It is not a ruin. People still walk inside it today, look up at its dome, and feel small.
stimulus.content_text:
A Recipe Stronger Than Stone
The Romans greatly improved concrete, a building material made by mixing volcanic ash (the powdery gray dust left behind by erupting volcanoes), a chalky powder called lime, and water. Earlier peoples had used simple forms of concrete, but the Romans made it much stronger by adding volcanic ash. This mixture dried harder than rock. Here's the surprising part: it could even harden underwater. That meant Romans could build strong walls for harbors and ports right in the sea.

Their most famous concrete building is the Pantheon, a temple in Rome with a massive rounded roof called a dome. It was built nearly two thousand years ago. It is not a ruin. People still walk inside it today, look up at its dome, and feel small.

items.prompt: Based on the article, how are volcanic ash and lime similar?

choices.text: Both are powders that are mixed to make concrete.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage describes volcanic ash as a powdery dust and lime as a chalky powder, and both are ingredients mixed together to make concrete.

choices.text: Both are ashes that are left by erupting volcanoes.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because only volcanic ash comes from erupting volcanoes — the passage says lime is a chalky powder, not a volcano ash.

choices.text: Both are liquids that are used to build sea ports.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because volcanic ash and lime are both powders, not liquids, and the passage never says they are used to build sea ports on their own.

choices.text: Both are stones that are shaped to build tall roofs.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because neither volcanic ash nor lime is a stone — they are both powders that are mixed with water to create concrete.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Stone, Ash, Water, and Clever Thinking

Thousands of workers made these wonders real — soldiers, paid workers, and enslaved people who hauled stones, mixed concrete, and carved arches under the hot sun. The emperors got the credit, but the hands that did the building belonged to ordinary people.

Next time you drive on a highway, turn on a faucet, or walk under a bridge, think about this: the Romans solved those same problems two thousand years ago — with no machines, no electricity, and no computers. Just stone, ash, water, and clever thinking.
stimulus.content_text:
Stone, Ash, Water, and Clever Thinking
Thousands of workers made these wonders real — soldiers, paid workers, and enslaved people who hauled stones, mixed concrete, and carved arches under the hot sun. The emperors got the credit, but the hands that did the building belonged to ordinary people.

Next time you drive on a highway, turn on a faucet, or walk under a bridge, think about this: the Romans solved those same problems two thousand years ago — with no machines, no electricity, and no computers. Just stone, ash, water, and clever thinking.

items.prompt: According to the text, who were the workers that made the Roman wonders real?

choices.text: Soldiers, paid workers, and enslaved people
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage directly states 'soldiers, paid workers, and enslaved people' were the ones who hauled stones, mixed concrete, and carved arches.

choices.text: Emperors, rich leaders, and clever thinkers
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the text says emperors got the credit but were not the ones doing the actual building work.

choices.text: Drivers, bridge walkers, and faucet makers
choices.feedback: C is wrong because drivers, bridge walkers, and faucet makers are examples of people today, not the workers who built Roman structures.

choices.text: People with machines, power, and computers
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the passage clearly says the Romans built their wonders with no machines, no electricity, and no computers.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: Based on the text, how were Roman roads built differently than Roman aqueducts?

choices.text: Roads were laid straight and flat, while aqueducts were angled to flow downhill.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage says roads were built flat and straight, while aqueducts were sloped so water could flow gently downhill the whole way.

choices.text: Roads were raised on tall arches, while aqueducts were buried in deep trenches.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because it has things backwards — aqueducts used tall arches, and roads were built in deep trenches, not the other way around.

choices.text: Roads were covered by round roofs, while aqueducts were locked with wedge stones.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because round roofs (domes) and wedge stones (keystones) describe buildings and arches, not how roads were built.

choices.text: Roads were shaped into half circles, while aqueducts were shaped like puzzle pieces.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because half-circle shapes describe arches used in aqueducts, and puzzle pieces describe road paving stones — so the two details are mixed up.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: Which heading explains what causes a rounded structure to be strong, and why?

choices.text: "The Secret of the Arch", because it explains how a curved shape spreads out heavy weight.
choices.feedback: A is correct because 'The Secret of the Arch' explains that a curved shape spreads heavy weight evenly across all its stones, which is what makes a rounded structure strong.

choices.text: "A Recipe Stronger Than Stone", because it describes how volcanic ash hardens underwater.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because 'A Recipe Stronger Than Stone' is about how Romans made super-strong concrete using volcanic ash, not about why a rounded shape is strong.

choices.text: "Carrying Water Across the Sky", because it shows how water flowed downhill to the city.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because 'Carrying Water Across the Sky' explains how aqueducts carried water downhill from mountains to the city, not how a rounded structure holds up weight.

choices.text: "Roads That Outlived an Empire", because it tells how paving stones locked together like puzzle pieces.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because 'Roads That Outlived an Empire' is about how Roman roads were built with layers of gravel and flat stones, not about rounded structures or arches.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: One main idea of the text is that Romans used clever engineering to bring water to their city. Which sequence of key details shows how this water traveled?

choices.text: First, water gathered at mountain springs, next it flowed through underground channels, and finally it crossed valleys on arched bridges locked by keystones.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage clearly says water came from mountain springs, traveled through underground channels, and crossed valleys on arched bridges — in that exact order.

choices.text: First, water gathered at city rivers, next it flowed through deep gravel trenches, and finally it crossed valleys on flat paving stones locked together.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because city rivers and gravel trenches are details about roads, not about how aqueducts carried water.

choices.text: First, water gathered at mountain springs, next it flowed through buried garden hoses, and finally it crossed valleys on strong walls made of volcanic ash.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because 'buried garden hose' was just a comparison in the passage, not a real part of the aqueduct, and volcanic ash was used in concrete, not to carry water.

choices.text: First, water crossed valleys on arched bridges locked by keystones, next it flowed through underground channels, and finally it gathered at mountain springs.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because it lists the steps in the wrong order — water started at the mountain springs first, it didn't end there.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: Based on the article, how are Roman roads and the Pantheon similar?

choices.text: They are still used by people today.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the article says you can still walk on Roman roads today, and people still walk inside the Pantheon today too.

choices.text: They were built to carry fresh water.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because roads were built for marching and travel, not for carrying water — that was the job of aqueducts.

choices.text: They were made using only flat stones.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the Pantheon was built using concrete, not flat stones, so this can't describe both structures.

choices.text: They were built entirely underwater.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because neither roads nor the Pantheon were built underwater — the article only mentions that Roman concrete could harden underwater, which was used for harbor walls.

syllabus_metadata.title: Roads, Aqueducts, and Arches: Roman Engineering Marvels

assessments.title: Jupiter, Mars, and Neptune: Gods of Ancient Rome

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Gods of Ancient Rome

Thunder Over Rome and the Gods Romans Worshipped

A flash of lightning split the sky above the city of Rome. A farmer looked up from his field. He didn't think about electricity or storms the way you do. He thought a god was angry.

The ancient Romans worshipped many gods and goddesses. They didn't pick just one. Instead, they prayed to different gods for different needs — one god before a battle, another before a sea voyage, and yet another at planting time. Each god controlled a different part of life and nature. Romans took their gods seriously. They believed that prayers and offerings — gifts given to please a god — could bring good luck, victory in war, and a large harvest.

You might think the Romans made up all their gods on their own. Actually, they borrowed most of them from the Greeks. They kept the gods but gave them new Roman names and sometimes changed their stories a little.
stimulus.content_text:
The Gods of Ancient RomeThunder Over Rome and the Gods Romans Worshipped
A flash of lightning split the sky above the city of Rome. A farmer looked up from his field. He didn't think about electricity or storms the way you do. He thought a god was angry.
The ancient Romans worshipped many gods and goddesses. They didn't pick just one. Instead, they prayed to different gods for different needs — one god before a battle, another before a sea voyage, and yet another at planting time. Each god controlled a different part of life and nature. Romans took their gods seriously. They believed that prayers and offerings — gifts given to please a god — could bring good luck, victory in war, and a large harvest.
You might think the Romans made up all their gods on their own. Actually, they borrowed most of them from the Greeks. They kept the gods but gave them new Roman names and sometimes changed their stories a little.

items.prompt: According to the text, what was one difference between the Greek gods and the Roman gods?

choices.text: The Roman gods were given new names.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage directly says the Romans kept the Greek gods but gave them new Roman names.

choices.text: The Roman gods were given new powers.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the passage never mentions the Roman gods receiving new powers — that detail isn't in the text.

choices.text: The Roman gods were given new harvests.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because harvests are crops that farmers grow, and the passage never says gods were given new harvests.

choices.text: The Roman gods were given new offerings.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because offerings were gifts Romans gave to please their gods, but the passage never says offerings were a difference between Greek and Roman gods.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Jupiter, Mars, Neptune, and Other Important Gods

The most powerful Roman god was Jupiter. He ruled the sky and thunder. The Greeks called him Zeus, but the Romans made him their own. When Jupiter was angry, he hurled lightning bolts down from the heavens. The Romans built their grandest temple to Jupiter on the Capitoline Hill, one of Rome's famous seven hills. Roman leaders visited Jupiter's temple before going to war. They wanted his blessing before a single soldier marched.

Mars was the god of war, and the Romans respected him deeply. They valued military strength above almost everything else. According to legend, Mars was the father of Romulus, the legendary — meaning famous from old stories, not proven to be real — founder of Rome. That made Mars feel almost like a family member to the Roman people. Even today, the month of March carries his name.

Neptune ruled the sea. He carried a trident, a long spear with three sharp points. Roman sailors prayed to Neptune before long voyages, asking for calm waters. When Neptune was angry, Romans believed he struck the ocean floor with his trident and caused terrible storms and earthquakes.

Other important gods included Venus, goddess of love and beauty; Mercury, the speedy messenger who carried words between gods; and Minerva, goddess of wisdom and crafts. Every god had a personality and a job to do.

When Jupiter was angry, he hurled lightning bolts down from the heavens. The Romans built their grandest temple to Jupiter on the Capitoline Hill, one of Rome's famous seven hills. Roman leaders visited Jupiter's temple before going to war. They wanted his blessing before a single soldier marched.
Mars was the god of war, and the Romans respected him deeply. They valued military strength above almost everything else. According to legend, Mars was the father of Romulus, the legendary — meaning famous from old stories, not proven to be real — founder of Rome. That made Mars feel almost like a family member to the Roman people. Even today, the month of March carries his name.
Neptune ruled the sea. He carried a trident, a long spear with three sharp points. Roman sailors prayed to Neptune before long voyages, asking for calm waters. When Neptune was angry, Romans believed he struck the ocean floor with his trident and caused terrible storms and earthquakes.
Other important gods included Venus, goddess of love and beauty; Mercury, the speedy messenger who carried words between gods; and Minerva, goddess of wisdom and crafts. Every god had a personality and a job to do.

items.prompt: The text describes Romans asking Jupiter for blessings and Neptune for calm waters. Based on the main idea, what caused them to ask different gods for help?

choices.text: Because each god had a special personality and a job.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage clearly says every god had a personality and a job, which is exactly why Romans asked Jupiter for war blessings and Neptune for calm seas.

choices.text: Because the Greeks told the Romans to change their names.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the passage says the Romans made Jupiter their own from the Greeks — it never says the Greeks told Romans who to ask for help.

choices.text: Because the leaders wanted to build large temples on hills.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because building temples on hills is just one thing Romans did to honor Jupiter, not the reason they asked different gods for help.

choices.text: Because Mars was the famous father of the city's founder.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because Mars being the father of Rome's founder is an interesting detail, but it doesn't explain why Romans asked different gods for different kinds of help.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Temples, Priests, Festivals, and Gods in Everyday Life

Romans built grand temples with tall stone columns and decorated roofs. Inside each temple stood a statue of the god it honored. Priests and priestesses led ceremonies there. They burned incense — dried plants that release sweet-smelling smoke — as a gift to the gods. They also made sacrifices, which meant giving something valuable to a god. Often this meant offering an animal, because Romans believed giving up something important showed respect.

Romans also held big festivals throughout the year. One of the most popular was Saturnalia, a celebration honoring Saturn, the god of farming and time. Saturnalia happened in late December. People exchanged gifts, ate special meals, and took days off from work. It was the most joyful time of the Roman year.

Romans didn't just think about their gods on special days. They prayed at temples. They prayed before battles, before voyages, and before planting seeds. Their gods gave them a way to explain thunder, storms, and earthquakes — all the things they couldn't yet understand through science. A Roman child heard that same thunder and pictured Jupiter reaching for a lightning bolt.

Temples, Priests, Festivals, and Gods in Everyday Life

They burned incense — dried plants that release sweet-smelling smoke — as a gift to the gods. They also made sacrifices, which meant giving something valuable to a god. Often this meant offering an animal, because Romans believed giving up something important showed respect.
Romans also held big festivals throughout the year. One of the most popular was Saturnalia, a celebration honoring Saturn, the god of farming and time. Saturnalia happened in late December. People exchanged gifts, ate special meals, and took days off from work. It was the most joyful time of the Roman year.
Romans didn't just think about their gods on special days. They prayed at temples. They prayed before battles, before voyages, and before planting seeds. Their gods gave them a way to explain thunder, storms, and earthquakes — all the things they couldn't yet understand through science. A Roman child heard that same thunder and pictured Jupiter reaching for a lightning bolt.

items.prompt: According to the text, what did priests do during ceremonies after they burned sweet-smelling plants?

choices.text: They gave something valuable as a sacrifice.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage directly says priests burned incense AND made sacrifices during ceremonies, so sacrificing came right after burning the sweet-smelling plants.

choices.text: They built temples with tall stone columns.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because building temples with tall stone columns is something Romans did to create the temples, not something priests did during ceremonies inside them.

choices.text: They shared gifts and ate special meals.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because exchanging gifts and eating special meals were part of the Saturnalia festival celebration, not what priests did during temple ceremonies.

choices.text: They pictured Jupiter holding a lightning bolt.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage says a Roman child pictured Jupiter reaching for a lightning bolt when hearing thunder — this has nothing to do with what priests did in ceremonies.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: According to the article, how was an ancient Roman's understanding of a storm different from ours today?

choices.text: Romans thought storms were special gifts, but we know they are natural events.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because Romans did not think storms were special gifts — they believed storms meant a god like Jupiter was angry.

choices.text: Romans thought angry gods caused storms, but we use science to explain them.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly says Romans thought angry gods caused storms, while we now use science to understand them.

choices.text: Romans thought the sea created storms, but we know they form in the sky.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never says Romans believed the sea created storms — that idea is not in the article at all.

choices.text: Romans thought festivals prevented storms, but we use weather reports to track them.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never connects festivals to preventing storms, and weather reports are never mentioned.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: According to the article, why did the ancient Romans make sacrifices and give offerings to the gods?

choices.text: They wanted to show respect and bring good luck to their lives.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage directly says offerings could bring good luck and victory, and that sacrifices showed the gods respect.

choices.text: They needed to use up the extra animals and crops on their farms.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the article never says anything about Romans having leftover animals or crops they needed to get rid of.

choices.text: They wanted to copy the traditions of the ancient Greek people.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because borrowing gods from the Greeks is a separate idea in the article and has nothing to do with why Romans made sacrifices.

choices.text: They needed to prepare for the joyful festival of Saturnalia.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because Saturnalia was a festival for celebrating Saturn, not the reason Romans gave offerings and sacrifices to the gods.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: Which sequence of events best supports the main idea that Romans relied heavily on their gods?

choices.text: First they built grand stone temples, and then they stopped believing in Greek gods.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because Romans didn't stop believing in Greek gods — they actually borrowed the Greek gods and gave them Roman names.

choices.text: First they prayed and gave offerings, and then they went to war or planted seeds.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage clearly shows Romans prayed and gave offerings before battles, voyages, and planting, proving they relied on their gods for important events.

choices.text: First they celebrated big festivals, and then they gave the gods their Roman names.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never connects festivals to the moment when gods received their Roman names — those are two separate ideas.

choices.text: First they saw lightning in the sky, and then they learned about science and storms.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says Romans never understood storms through science — learning about science is something people do today, not what ancient Romans did.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: How does the information under the heading "Jupiter, Mars, Neptune, and Other Important Gods" compare to the information under "Temples, Priests, Festivals, and Gods in Everyday Life"?

choices.text: The first explains who the gods were, but the second shows how people honored them.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the first section tells us who gods like Jupiter, Mars, and Neptune were, while the second section explains how Romans honored gods through temples, festivals, and daily prayers.

choices.text: The first describes the old Greek gods, but the second describes the new Roman gods.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the first section describes Roman gods like Jupiter and Mars, not just the old Greek gods.

choices.text: The first tells why the gods were angry, but the second tells how to calm them down.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because neither section focuses on why gods were angry or how to calm them down — those are just small details, not the main ideas.

choices.text: The first lists the oldest Roman beliefs, but the second lists the newest Roman beliefs.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the passage never says that one section has older beliefs and the other has newer beliefs.

syllabus_metadata.title: Jupiter, Mars, and Neptune: Gods of Ancient Rome

assessments.title: A New Faith: The Rise of Christianity in Rome

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
From Forbidden Faith to Official Religion: Christianity in Ancient Rome

A World of Many Gods

A Roman father kneels before a small statue of Jupiter in his home, whispering a prayer for good weather, while his daughter places a tiny cake at the feet of Juno's statue. Every morning, in homes across Rome, families prayed to different gods for different things.

For hundreds of years, worshipping many gods was a normal part of Roman life. Romans built beautiful temples to gods like Mars, the god of war, and Venus, the goddess of love. This tradition shaped their holidays, their meals, and their daily habits. Then a new faith began to grow — quietly, and far from Rome.
stimulus.content_text:
From Forbidden Faith to Official Religion: Christianity in Ancient RomeA World of Many Gods
A Roman father kneels before a small statue of Jupiter in his home, whispering a prayer for good weather, while his daughter places a tiny cake at the feet of Juno's statue. Every morning, in homes across Rome, families prayed to different gods for different things.

For hundreds of years, worshipping many gods was a normal part of Roman life. Romans built beautiful temples to gods like Mars, the god of war, and Venus, the goddess of love. This tradition shaped their holidays, their meals, and their daily habits. Then a new faith began to grow — quietly, and far from Rome.

items.prompt: According to the text, how was the new faith different from the normal Roman traditions?

choices.text: It grew quietly and far away from Rome.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage uses these exact words: the new faith 'began to grow quietly, and far from Rome,' which shows it was very different from the loud, public Roman traditions.

choices.text: It started loudly in the center of Rome.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the passage says the new faith grew quietly and far away — the opposite of loudly and in the center of Rome.

choices.text: It involved building big temples in Rome.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because building beautiful temples in Rome was part of the old Roman traditions, not something connected to the new faith.

choices.text: It was practiced only by families in Rome.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because it was Roman families who practiced the old traditions of praying to many gods, not the new faith.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
A Teacher in a Distant Province

Christianity started in a Roman province — a region controlled by Rome, the way a state is part of a larger country. This province was called Judea, in the area we now call Israel and Palestine. There, a man named Jesus of Nazareth began to teach. He said there was one God who loved all people equally. He told people to love one another, forgive their enemies, and help the poor. His message of compassion — deep care for others' suffering, like the ache you feel when a friend cries — drew many followers. Most of them were poor and struggling.

After Jesus died, his followers called themselves Christians. They carried his teachings across the empire, walking along Roman roads from city to city. Small groups of Christians formed in towns everywhere.
stimulus.content_text:
A Teacher in a Distant Province
Christianity started in a Roman province — a region controlled by Rome, the way a state is part of a larger country. This province was called Judea, in the area we now call Israel and Palestine. There, a man named Jesus of Nazareth began to teach. He said there was one God who loved all people equally. He told people to love one another, forgive their enemies, and help the poor. His message of compassion — deep care for others' suffering, like the ache you feel when a friend cries — drew many followers. Most of them were poor and struggling.

After Jesus died, his followers called themselves Christians. They carried his teachings across the empire, walking along Roman roads from city to city. Small groups of Christians formed in towns everywhere.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, why did small groups of Christians form in towns across the Roman Empire?

choices.text: because Roman leaders invited Christians to move to new towns
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage never mentions Roman leaders inviting Christians to move to new towns.

choices.text: because followers carried Jesus's teachings along Roman roads
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly says followers carried Jesus's teachings along Roman roads from city to city.

choices.text: because Jesus traveled to every city in the Roman Empire
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage says Jesus taught in one province, and it was his followers — not Jesus — who traveled across the empire.

choices.text: because poor people in each town wrote down the teachings
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage says followers spread the teachings by walking and traveling, not by writing them down in each town.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Danger and Growth

Rome's government did not welcome this new religion. Roman leaders expected everyone to honor the emperor and the traditional gods. But Christians believed in only one God and refused to worship the emperor. This made Roman leaders suspicious and angry.

Early Christians were persecuted — punished and treated cruelly for what they believed, the way a student might be bullied just for being different. Some were arrested or killed. But Christianity kept growing. Christians cared for the sick, shared food with the hungry, and welcomed anyone — soldiers, merchants, or enslaved people. These acts of kindness drew more and more people to the faith.
stimulus.content_text:
Danger and Growth
Rome's government did not welcome this new religion. Roman leaders expected everyone to honor the emperor and the traditional gods. But Christians believed in only one God and refused to worship the emperor. This made Roman leaders suspicious and angry.

Early Christians were persecuted — punished and treated cruelly for what they believed, the way a student might be bullied just for being different. Some were arrested or killed. But Christianity kept growing. Christians cared for the sick, shared food with the hungry, and welcomed anyone — soldiers, merchants, or enslaved people. These acts of kindness drew more and more people to the faith.

items.prompt: According to the text, what happened after early Christians were arrested or killed?

choices.text: The new religion continued to grow.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage clearly says 'Christianity kept growing' even after Christians were arrested or killed, thanks to their acts of kindness.

choices.text: The Roman leaders honored the gods.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because Roman leaders honoring the traditional gods was already happening before — it was not something that happened as a result of the persecutions.

choices.text: The government welcomed the people.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage says the opposite — Rome's government did NOT welcome Christians and was suspicious and angry toward them.

choices.text: The soldiers became very suspicious.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because it was the Roman leaders who became suspicious, and that happened before the persecutions, not as a result of them.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Constantine and a Vision in the Sky

By the time any emperor decided to support Christianity, it had already been spreading for almost 300 years. More and more Romans had become Christians. Then, in AD 313, Emperor Constantine issued the Edict of Milan — an official order, like a new law announced by the president — that allowed Christians to worship freely. According to an old story, Constantine saw a glowing cross in the sky before a great battle. He won that battle and became a strong supporter of Christianity. He built churches, supported church leaders, and moved his capital to a new city he named Constantinople.

Plenty of Romans still prayed to Jupiter and Mars and kept visiting the old temples.
stimulus.content_text:
Constantine and a Vision in the Sky
By the time any emperor decided to support Christianity, it had already been spreading for almost 300 years. More and more Romans had become Christians. Then, in AD 313, Emperor Constantine issued the Edict of Milan — an official order, like a new law announced by the president — that allowed Christians to worship freely. According to an old story, Constantine saw a glowing cross in the sky before a great battle. He won that battle and became a strong supporter of Christianity. He built churches, supported church leaders, and moved his capital to a new city he named Constantinople.

Plenty of Romans still prayed to Jupiter and Mars and kept visiting the old temples.

items.prompt: What does the author compare the Edict of Milan to?

choices.text: A glowing cross seen in the sky
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the glowing cross is something Constantine saw in the sky before a battle — it is not what the author compares the Edict of Milan to.

choices.text: A new law made by a president
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly says the Edict of Milan was 'like a new law announced by the president,' which is exactly the comparison the author makes.

choices.text: A great battle won by a leader
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the great battle is a separate event in the story about Constantine — it has nothing to do with explaining what the Edict of Milan is.

choices.text: An old temple used by a Roman
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because old temples are mentioned at the very end of the passage about Romans who still prayed to old gods, not as a comparison to the Edict of Milan.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
An Empire Transformed

By AD 380, Emperor Theodosius made Christianity the official religion of the entire Roman Empire. The empire that once punished Christians was now led by them.

Then something surprising happened. The Roman Empire itself eventually fell apart. Its armies scattered. Its roads crumbled. But Christianity survived. It spread across Europe and, centuries later, across the world. Today, if you see a church in your town, you are looking at a legacy — something handed down from the past, like a family recipe from your great-grandmother. That legacy stretches all the way back to a small group of believers walking dusty Roman roads, sharing a message they refused to let die.
stimulus.content_text:
An Empire Transformed
By AD 380, Emperor Theodosius made Christianity the official religion of the entire Roman Empire. The empire that once punished Christians was now led by them.

Then something surprising happened. The Roman Empire itself eventually fell apart. Its armies scattered. Its roads crumbled. But Christianity survived. It spread across Europe and, centuries later, across the world. Today, if you see a church in your town, you are looking at a legacy — something handed down from the past, like a family recipe from your great-grandmother. That legacy stretches all the way back to a small group of believers walking dusty Roman roads, sharing a message they refused to let die.

items.prompt: In the article 'A New Faith: The Rise of Christianity in Rome,' what does the word legacy mean?

choices.text: A thing handed down from the past.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage directly defines legacy as 'something handed down from the past,' which matches this answer perfectly.

choices.text: An official religion of the empire.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because 'official religion of the empire' describes Christianity under Emperor Theodosius, not the meaning of the word legacy.

choices.text: A dusty road built by the Romans.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because dusty Roman roads are just a detail in the story about early Christians, not the definition of legacy.

choices.text: A family recipe for a large meal.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because a family recipe is only used as a fun example to help explain what legacy means — it is not the full definition.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: Based on the article, how were the beliefs of the early Christians different from the traditional beliefs of the Romans?

choices.text: Christians prayed to only one God, but Romans worshipped many different gods.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the article says Romans prayed to many gods like Jupiter and Venus, while Christians believed in only one God and refused to worship the emperor.

choices.text: Christians built large stone temples, but Romans prayed quietly inside their homes.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because it's actually the Romans who prayed at home to small statues, while Christians traveled along roads spreading their faith in many towns.

choices.text: Christians cared mostly for the rich, but Romans shared their food with the poor.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the article says Christians helped the poor and shared food with the hungry — they did not care mostly for the rich.

choices.text: Christians worshipped the emperor, but Romans refused to honor him as a god.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because it has things backwards — Romans expected everyone to worship the emperor, but Christians refused to do so.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: Based on the article, what caused Christianity to keep spreading even when Christians were being punished?

choices.text: Roman leaders told people to follow the new faith
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because Roman leaders were suspicious and angry at Christians, not encouraging people to join the new faith.

choices.text: Christians helped the sick and shared with the poor
choices.feedback: This is correct because the article says Christians helped the sick, shared food, and welcomed everyone, and those acts of kindness kept drawing new people to the faith.

choices.text: Emperor Constantine built churches across the empire
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because Constantine built churches much later, after Christianity had already been spreading for nearly 300 years on its own.

choices.text: Christians worshipped many gods like other Romans did
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because Christians only believed in one God and refused to worship the Roman gods, which is actually what caused them trouble with Roman leaders.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: Which list of events best shows the main idea of the article in the order that they happened?

choices.text: Jesus taught in Judea, leaders punished his followers, an emperor allowed the faith, and it became the official religion.
choices.feedback: A is correct because it puts the key events in the right order and shows how Christianity grew from a small, punished faith into the official religion of Rome.

choices.text: Leaders punished his followers, Jesus taught in Judea, it became the official religion, and an emperor allowed the faith.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because it puts the events in the wrong order — leaders punished followers before Jesus even taught, which is backwards.

choices.text: A father prayed to Jupiter, families built temples to Mars, Christians walked on roads, and the Roman armies scattered.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because it lists small details like a father praying and roads, not the big important events that show the main idea of the article.

choices.text: Romans worshipped many gods, the Roman Empire fell apart, Jesus taught in Judea, and an emperor built new churches.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because it puts the events out of order — the Roman Empire fell apart near the end of the story, not before Jesus taught.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: Based on the article, how does the Roman government's treatment of Christians in 'Danger and Growth' compare to its treatment of them in 'An Empire Transformed'?

choices.text: The government punished them at first, but later it was led by them.
choices.feedback: This is correct — the article clearly says Christians were persecuted (punished) early on, but by AD 380 the empire was led by Christians.

choices.text: The government ignored them at first, but later it forced them to leave.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the government did not ignore Christians — it actively punished them — and it never forced them to leave the empire.

choices.text: The government helped them at first, but later it destroyed their churches.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the government never helped Christians at first; it actually hurt them, and later it supported them by building churches, not destroying them.

choices.text: The government hired them at first, but later it made them into slaves.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the government never hired Christians or made them into slaves — those things are not mentioned anywhere in the article.

syllabus_metadata.title: A New Faith: The Rise of Christianity in Rome

assessments.title: The Fall of the Mighty Empire: Why Rome Collapsed

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Slow Fall of the Mightiest Empire

A Teenager Loses an Empire

In the year AD 476, a boy named Romulus Augustulus sat on the throne of the Western Roman Empire. He was just a teenager. A Germanic leader named Odoacer marched into Rome and removed him from power — overthrew him, the way you might knock a chess piece off the board. Odoacer did not even want to call himself emperor. The western half of Rome was finished.

It was more like a campfire slowly dying than a candle being blown out.
stimulus.content_text:
The Slow Fall of the Mightiest EmpireA Teenager Loses an Empire
In the year AD 476, a boy named Romulus Augustulus sat on the throne of the Western Roman Empire. He was just a teenager. A Germanic leader named Odoacer marched into Rome and removed him from power — overthrew him, the way you might knock a chess piece off the board. Odoacer did not even want to call himself emperor. The western half of Rome was finished.
It was more like a campfire slowly dying than a candle being blown out.

items.prompt: According to the text, how was the fall of Rome different from a candle being blown out?

choices.text: It was like a campfire slowly dying over time
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage uses the exact words 'a campfire slowly dying' to describe how Rome fell — gradually, not all at once.

choices.text: It was like a chess piece knocked off a board
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because knocking a chess piece off a board describes how Odoacer removed Romulus from power, not how the whole empire fell.

choices.text: It was like a big fire burning down quickly
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because a big fire burning down quickly still sounds fast and sudden, but the passage says Rome fell slowly over time.

choices.text: It was like a strong wind ending things at once
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because a strong wind ending things at once is sudden and fast, which is exactly how the passage says Rome did NOT fall.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Too Big to Control and Running Out of Money

At its largest, the Roman Empire stretched from Britain to North Africa, from Spain to the Middle East. Imagine trying to run a school where the classrooms are spread across an entire country. That was Rome's problem. Messages took weeks to travel from one end to the other.

In AD 285, Emperor Diocletian (say: dye-oh-KLEE-shun) split the empire into two halves — Western and Eastern. Each half got its own emperor. This made governing easier for a while. But it also divided Rome's strength in two. Each piece was weaker than the whole.

Borders needed guards. When money ran low, the government raised taxes and made cheaper coins with less silver in them. Prices shot up. Farmers left their fields. The wealth that once made Rome powerful was draining away.
stimulus.content_text:
Too Big to Control and Running Out of Money
At its largest, the Roman Empire stretched from Britain to North Africa, from Spain to the Middle East. Imagine trying to run a school where the classrooms are spread across an entire country. That was Rome's problem. Messages took weeks to travel from one end to the other.
In AD 285, Emperor Diocletian (say: dye-oh-KLEE-shun) split the empire into two halves — Western and Eastern. Each half got its own emperor. This made governing easier for a while. But it also divided Rome's strength in two. Each piece was weaker than the whole.
Borders needed guards. When money ran low, the government raised taxes and made cheaper coins with less silver in them. Prices shot up. Farmers left their fields. The wealth that once made Rome powerful was draining away.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, why did prices go up in Rome?

choices.text: because messages took weeks to travel
choices.feedback: Slow messages made it hard to run the empire, but the passage never connects that to prices going up.

choices.text: because coins had less silver
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says Rome made cheaper coins with less silver, and right after that it says prices shot up — one caused the other.

choices.text: because the empire was split in two
choices.feedback: Splitting the empire weakened Rome, but the passage points to the cheaper coins as the reason prices rose.

choices.text: because farmers guarded the borders
choices.feedback: The passage actually says farmers left their fields, which is the opposite of guarding borders.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Newcomers at the Border and Weak Leaders

Germanic tribes — groups of people from northern and central Europe — began pushing across Rome's borders. Many admired Roman culture and wanted land, food, and a safer life. They wanted to live inside the empire, not just tear it apart.

Still, the Roman army could not hold them all back. Groups like the Visigoths, Vandals, Ostrogoths, and Franks crossed into Roman territory. Here is the surprising part: many soldiers in the later Roman army were themselves Germanic warriors hired to fight for Rome.

In AD 410, the Visigoths captured the city of Rome and looted it — stealing treasure and valuables. This had not happened in eight hundred years. People across the empire were stunned. Rome was supposed to be the eternal city.

Rome also suffered from political instability — meaning its government kept falling apart. Generals fought each other for the throne. Some emperors ruled for only a few months before being killed or removed. No leader stayed long enough to fix the empire's deep problems.
stimulus.content_text:
Newcomers at the Border and Weak Leaders
Germanic tribes — groups of people from northern and central Europe — began pushing across Rome's borders. Many admired Roman culture and wanted land, food, and a safer life. They wanted to live inside the empire, not just tear it apart.
Still, the Roman army could not hold them all back. Groups like the Visigoths, Vandals, Ostrogoths, and Franks crossed into Roman territory. Here is the surprising part: many soldiers in the later Roman army were themselves Germanic warriors hired to fight for Rome.
In AD 410, the Visigoths captured the city of Rome and looted it — stealing treasure and valuables. This had not happened in eight hundred years. People across the empire were stunned. Rome was supposed to be the eternal city.
Rome also suffered from political instability — meaning its government kept falling apart. Generals fought each other for the throne. Some emperors ruled for only a few months before being killed or removed. No leader stayed long enough to fix the empire's deep problems.

items.prompt: According to the text, what is the correct order of events for the Visigoths in AD 410?

choices.text: They captured the city of Rome, and then they stole the city's treasure.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage clearly states the Visigoths captured Rome first and then looted it, meaning they stole the treasure after taking the city.

choices.text: They stole the city's treasure, and then they captured the city of Rome.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because it flips the order — the passage tells us capturing the city happened before the looting, not after.

choices.text: They captured the city of Rome, and then they fixed the empire's problems.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the Visigoths never fixed the empire's problems; fixing problems was something no leader managed to do, according to the passage.

choices.text: They fought for the throne, and then they captured the city of Rome.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage says Roman generals fought each other for the throne, not the Visigoths.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Half an Empire Survives and Why It Still Matters

You might think the whole Roman Empire ended in AD 476. It did not. Only the western half fell. The Eastern Roman Empire — called the Byzantine Empire (say: BIZ-un-teen) — kept going for nearly one thousand more years. Its capital was Constantinople (say: kon-stan-tih-NO-pul), built by Emperor Constantine. The Byzantines preserved Roman laws, books, and knowledge long after the western cities crumbled.

Rome did not fall because of one battle or one bad emperor. It fell because too many problems piled up — money troubles, weak leaders, a military that could no longer protect its people, and newcomers the empire could not manage. Each problem made the others worse, like cracks spreading across a wall. The warning still echoes: even the strongest things break when no one fixes the cracks.
stimulus.content_text:
Half an Empire Survives and Why It Still Matters
You might think the whole Roman Empire ended in AD 476. It did not. Only the western half fell. The Eastern Roman Empire — called the Byzantine Empire (say: BIZ-un-teen) — kept going for nearly one thousand more years. Its capital was Constantinople (say: kon-stan-tih-NO-pul), built by Emperor Constantine. The Byzantines preserved Roman laws, books, and knowledge long after the western cities crumbled.
Rome did not fall because of one battle or one bad emperor. It fell because too many problems piled up — money troubles, weak leaders, a military that could no longer protect its people, and newcomers the empire could not manage. Each problem made the others worse, like cracks spreading across a wall. The warning still echoes: even the strongest things break when no one fixes the cracks.

items.prompt: In the sentence "Each problem made the others worse, like cracks spreading across a wall," what does the word "cracks" help compare the problems to?

choices.text: small breaks that spread
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage uses cracks as a comparison to show how problems start small and spread, just like cracks spreading across a wall.

choices.text: strong stones that last
choices.feedback: B is wrong because cracks are weak and break things apart — they are the opposite of strong stones that last.

choices.text: new cities that grow
choices.feedback: C is wrong because cracks spreading has nothing to do with new cities growing — that idea is not in the sentence at all.

choices.text: safe walls that protect
choices.feedback: D is wrong because a wall with cracks is not safe or protective — the whole point is that the cracks make it weaker.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: How was the Eastern Roman Empire different from the Western Roman Empire after AD 476?

choices.text: It moved its capital to Rome and kept growing stronger.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the Eastern Empire's capital was Constantinople, not Rome, and the passage does not say it kept growing stronger.

choices.text: It survived and preserved Roman laws and knowledge.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage says the Byzantine Empire survived for nearly a thousand more years and preserved Roman laws, books, and knowledge.

choices.text: It fell apart because of the same money problems.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the Eastern Empire did not fall apart from money problems — it lasted almost one thousand more years.

choices.text: It was taken over by Germanic tribes right away.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because it was the Western Roman Empire, not the Eastern one, that was taken over by Germanic tribes.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: Which choice best explains why the Western Roman Empire became easier for outside groups to take over?

choices.text: It had fewer strong leaders and less money to protect its borders.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage clearly shows that weak leaders and running out of money left Rome's borders unprotected and easy for outside groups to cross.

choices.text: It had more silver coins and more soldiers from faraway lands.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the passage says Rome made coins with LESS silver when money ran low, not more, and having Germanic soldiers actually showed the army was struggling.

choices.text: It had one young emperor and one attack on the city of Rome.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because pointing to just one young emperor and one attack doesn't explain the full picture — the passage says many problems piled up over a long time.

choices.text: It had two equal halves and two emperors who worked together well.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage says splitting into two halves divided Rome's strength and made each piece weaker, not that the two emperors worked together well.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: The first section of the article tells how the Western Roman Empire ended in the year AD 476. What does the author do in the next section, "Too Big to Control and Running Out of Money"?

choices.text: Goes back in time to explain problems that weakened the empire.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the second section goes back in time to AD 285 and earlier to explain problems like the empire being too big, running out of money, and splitting in two — all things that weakened Rome long before it fell.

choices.text: Tells what happened to the teenage emperor after he lost his power.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the article never mentions what happened to the teenage emperor Romulus Augustulus after Odoacer removed him from power.

choices.text: Explains how the Eastern Roman Empire survived for many more years.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the Eastern Roman Empire and the Byzantine Empire are explained in the last section of the article, not the second section.

choices.text: Describes how a group called the Visigoths captured the city of Rome.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the story of the Visigoths capturing Rome is in the third section, called 'Newcomers at the Border and Weak Leaders,' not the second section.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: The author compares Rome's problems to cracks spreading across a wall. What main idea does this comparison help explain?

choices.text: One sudden battle quickly destroyed the whole empire.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage clearly says Rome did NOT fall from one sudden event — it fell slowly from many problems building up over a long time.

choices.text: Many different problems slowly weakened the empire.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage lists many different problems like money troubles, weak leaders, and border attacks, and the crack comparison shows how all those problems slowly spread and weakened the empire together.

choices.text: Two separate halves fought and ruined the empire.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because while the empire was split into two halves, the passage does not say the two halves fought each other to cause Rome's fall.

choices.text: One strong army easily defeated the entire empire.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because no single army easily defeated Rome — the passage explains it was a slow process caused by many different problems piling up.

syllabus_metadata.title: The Fall of the Mighty Empire: Why Rome Collapsed

assessments.title: Rome's Lasting Legacy: How Ancient Rome Shaped Our World

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Still Standing: How Ancient Rome Lives in Your World

A City That Took Centuries to Build — and Centuries to Fade
