Two thousand years ago, a farmer outside Rome woke before sunrise, fed his goats, and walked a stone road to the market. He was one of millions of ordinary people — farmers, shopkeepers, builders, and enslaved people with no freedom at all — who kept the Roman world running every single day.

You might picture Rome as a place of only emperors and sword fights. Actually, most Romans lived simple, hardworking lives. Together, they built one of the largest empires the world has ever seen, stretching across Europe, North Africa, and parts of the Middle East. Rome did not vanish overnight, like a candle blown out. It weakened slowly over hundreds of years. But even after Rome's government ended, its legacy — the things it left behind for future people — kept going. That legacy is all around you right now.
stimulus.content_text:
Still Standing: How Ancient Rome Lives in Your WorldA City That Took Centuries to Build — and Centuries to Fade
Two thousand years ago, a farmer outside Rome woke before sunrise, fed his goats, and walked a stone road to the market. He was one of millions of ordinary people — farmers, shopkeepers, builders, and enslaved people with no freedom at all — who kept the Roman world running every single day.

You might picture Rome as a place of only emperors and sword fights. Actually, most Romans lived simple, hardworking lives. Together, they built one of the largest empires the world has ever seen, stretching across Europe, North Africa, and parts of the Middle East. Rome did not vanish overnight, like a candle blown out. It weakened slowly over hundreds of years. But even after Rome's government ended, its legacy — the things it left behind for future people — kept going. That legacy is all around you right now.

items.prompt: According to the article, how was the end of Rome different from a candle being blown out?

choices.text: It weakened slowly over hundreds of years.
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says Rome 'weakened slowly over hundreds of years,' which is the opposite of a candle being blown out all at once.

choices.text: It stopped working before the sun came up.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect — the passage never says Rome stopped working before sunrise; that detail is about the farmer, not about Rome ending.

choices.text: It vanished quickly during a large sword fight.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect — the passage actually says Rome did NOT vanish quickly like a candle; it faded slowly, not during a sword fight.

choices.text: It broke apart suddenly on a busy market day.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect — the passage never says Rome broke apart suddenly on a market day; it weakened gradually over a very long time.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Words You Speak Every Day

Romans spoke a language called Latin. When the empire faded, Latin evolved — changed slowly over time, the way a seed grows into different kinds of trees. It grew into French, Spanish, Italian, Portuguese, and Romanian.

Even English borrowed thousands of Latin words. A tiny piece of ancient Rome sits on your tongue each time you use them.
stimulus.content_text:
Words You Speak Every Day
Romans spoke a language called Latin. When the empire faded, Latin evolved — changed slowly over time, the way a seed grows into different kinds of trees. It grew into French, Spanish, Italian, Portuguese, and Romanian.

Even English borrowed thousands of Latin words. A tiny piece of ancient Rome sits on your tongue each time you use them.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, why do French, Spanish, Italian, Portuguese, and Romanian exist as languages today?

choices.text: Because people in those countries decided to stop speaking English
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage never says anything about people in those countries stopping the use of English.

choices.text: Because Latin slowly changed into different languages over time
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly says Latin evolved and grew into French, Spanish, Italian, Portuguese, and Romanian over time.

choices.text: Because the Roman Empire forced everyone to speak new languages
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage says Latin changed slowly on its own, not that the Roman Empire forced people to speak new languages.

choices.text: Because English borrowed thousands of words from Latin
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because English borrowing Latin words is a separate fact in the passage and does not explain why those five languages were created.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
A Government by the People

Rome grew up in what is now Italy, separate from ancient Greece farther east. One of Rome's biggest ideas was the republic — a type of government where citizens vote for leaders instead of one king holding all the power. Think of it like a classroom where students elect a class president instead of the teacher picking one.

Those elected leaders were called representatives — people chosen to speak and act for a larger group. Romans created rules so no single person had too much power. America's founders loved this system. The U.S. Senate is even named after the Roman Senate.
stimulus.content_text:
A Government by the People
Rome grew up in what is now Italy, separate from ancient Greece farther east. One of Rome's biggest ideas was the republic — a type of government where citizens vote for leaders instead of one king holding all the power. Think of it like a classroom where students elect a class president instead of the teacher picking one.

Those elected leaders were called representatives — people chosen to speak and act for a larger group. Romans created rules so no single person had too much power. America's founders loved this system. The U.S. Senate is even named after the Roman Senate.

items.prompt: According to the text, what did America's founders do after they learned about Rome's republic?

choices.text: They picked one king to rule the country
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the whole point of a republic is that no single king holds all the power — people vote for their leaders instead.

choices.text: They created the U.S. Senate from it
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage says America's founders loved Rome's system and even named the U.S. Senate after the Roman Senate.

choices.text: They moved their government to Italy
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage never says the founders moved anywhere — they just borrowed Rome's ideas for their own country.

choices.text: They ended the Roman Senate forever
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage never mentions anyone ending the Roman Senate — it only talks about how it inspired America.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Buildings You Can Recognize

Look at the U.S. Capitol building in Washington, D.C. Its grand dome and tall columns look just like a Roman temple. Courthouses and libraries across the world still copy that style.
stimulus.content_text:
Buildings You Can Recognize
Look at the U.S. Capitol building in Washington, D.C. Its grand dome and tall columns look just like a Roman temple. Courthouses and libraries across the world still copy that style.

items.prompt: How does the author connect the U.S. Capitol building to a Roman temple?

choices.text: By explaining that they were built at the exact same time.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage never mentions when the buildings were built or says they were built at the same time.

choices.text: By comparing the way their domes and tall columns look.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the author directly compares the dome and tall columns of the Capitol building to those of a Roman temple to show they look alike.

choices.text: By showing that one was built to replace the other one.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage never says one building replaced the other — it only says they look similar.

choices.text: By listing the steps needed to build both of the buildings.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage never lists any steps for building either structure.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Fair Laws and Everyday Inventions

Roman law gave us a powerful principle — a basic rule that guides how people act. That rule says a person is innocent until proven guilty. Courts in countries everywhere still follow this idea.

Romans also left behind practical inventions. Their straight, sturdy roads became models for modern highways. Julius Caesar reformed — fixed and improved — the Roman calendar, creating what we call the Julian calendar. Hundreds of years later, Pope Gregory XIII updated it again to make it more accurate. That newer version, called the Gregorian calendar, is the one hanging on your wall today. Roman engineers even built pipes that carried fresh water into cities, an early version of the plumbing in your home.
stimulus.content_text:
Fair Laws and Everyday Inventions
Roman law gave us a powerful principle — a basic rule that guides how people act. That rule says a person is innocent until proven guilty. Courts in countries everywhere still follow this idea.

Romans also left behind practical inventions. Their straight, sturdy roads became models for modern highways. Julius Caesar reformed — fixed and improved — the Roman calendar, creating what we call the Julian calendar. Hundreds of years later, Pope Gregory XIII updated it again to make it more accurate. That newer version, called the Gregorian calendar, is the one hanging on your wall today. Roman engineers even built pipes that carried fresh water into cities, an early version of the plumbing in your home.

items.prompt: In the sentence "Roman law gave us a powerful principle — a basic rule that guides how people act," what does the word "principle" mean?

choices.text: a person who leads others
choices.feedback: A person who leads others is called a 'principal' — that word sounds like 'principle' but means something completely different.

choices.text: a basic rule to follow
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage uses a dash right after the word 'principle' to explain it means 'a basic rule that guides how people act.'

choices.text: a building used for court
choices.feedback: The passage never says a principle is a building — a court is a place where laws are used, not what the word 'principle' means.

choices.text: a tool for carrying water
choices.feedback: Pipes that carry water describe plumbing, which is mentioned later in the passage, but that has nothing to do with the word 'principle.'

items.title: Section 6 - Guiding Question 5

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
One Big Idea That Still Matters

Rome held together people from many different lands, languages, and traditions under one shared set of laws. That was not easy, and Rome did not always do it fairly. But the idea that diversity — having many different kinds of people in one place — can make a group stronger, not weaker? That idea traveled forward through time, all the way to you.

The farmer who walked that stone road two thousand years ago is long gone. But the road he walked on shaped the roads you ride on today. Ancient Rome is not just history — it is the ground beneath your feet.
stimulus.content_text:
One Big Idea That Still Matters
Rome held together people from many different lands, languages, and traditions under one shared set of laws. That was not easy, and Rome did not always do it fairly. But the idea that diversity — having many different kinds of people in one place — can make a group stronger, not weaker? That idea traveled forward through time, all the way to you.

The farmer who walked that stone road two thousand years ago is long gone. But the road he walked on shaped the roads you ride on today. Ancient Rome is not just history — it is the ground beneath your feet.

items.prompt: Which detail from the section best supports the idea that ancient Rome's influence has lasted a very long time?

choices.text: Rome held together people from many different lands and traditions.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because it describes what Rome did with different people, but it doesn't show how Rome's influence reached all the way to today.

choices.text: The farmer who walked that road two thousand years ago is long gone.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because saying the farmer is 'long gone' actually suggests things have disappeared, not that they have lasted.

choices.text: Rome did not always treat all of its people fairly.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because Rome being unfair is about how it treated people, not about how long its influence has lasted.

choices.text: The road he walked on shaped the roads you ride on today.
choices.feedback: D is correct because it directly connects a road from two thousand years ago to the roads you ride on today, showing Rome's influence has lasted all the way to the present.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: Based on the article, how are the ancient Roman people different from the ideas and inventions they created?

choices.text: The people are gone today, but their creations are still used.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage clearly shows the Roman people are gone but their inventions, laws, roads, and ideas are still part of our everyday lives today.

choices.text: The people were very famous, but their creations are forgotten.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the article says the opposite — Roman creations like the calendar, roads, and laws are still remembered and used all over the world.

choices.text: The people stayed in one city, but their creations moved away.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the article never says the people stayed in one city; Rome actually spread across Europe, North Africa, and the Middle East.

choices.text: The people liked to fight, but their creations brought peace.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the article does not describe Romans as people who liked to fight, and it does not say their creations brought peace.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: Why does the author say ancient Rome is still part of your world today?

choices.text: Because Roman ideas still shape things people use and follow today
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage gives many examples — laws, government, calendars, roads, and language — that show Roman ideas still shape things people use and follow today.

choices.text: Because Roman emperors still rule many countries today
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the passage clearly says Rome's government ended long ago, and no Roman emperors are ruling any countries today.

choices.text: Because Roman buildings are the only model for modern cities today
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the passage only says some buildings copy Roman style — it never says Roman buildings are the only model for modern cities.

choices.text: Because Roman farmers taught everyone how to grow food today
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the passage never mentions Roman farmers teaching people how to grow food — that idea is not in the passage at all.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: Based on the article, which of these happened in the correct order over time?

choices.text: America's founders created the Senate, then Romans invented the republic.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because it has the order backwards — Romans invented the republic thousands of years before America's founders created their Senate.

choices.text: Romans created a republic, then America's founders named their Senate after Rome's.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the article clearly shows Romans built their republic first, and then America's founders later named their Senate after Rome's.

choices.text: Julius Caesar made the Gregorian calendar, then Pope Gregory changed it to the Julian calendar.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because it mixes up the names — Julius Caesar created the Julian calendar, not the Gregorian calendar.

choices.text: Pope Gregory updated the calendar, then Julius Caesar reformed it to be more accurate.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because it has the order backwards — Julius Caesar reformed the calendar first, and Pope Gregory updated it later.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: Which two headings help you find comparisons between ancient Rome and the United States?

choices.text: "Words You Speak Every Day" and "Fair Laws and Everyday Inventions"
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because 'Words You Speak Every Day' is about Latin words in English, not comparisons to the United States.

choices.text: "A Government by the People" and "Buildings You Can Recognize"
choices.feedback: B is correct because both sections directly compare ancient Rome to the United States, mentioning the U.S. Senate and the U.S. Capitol building.

choices.text: "A City That Took Centuries to Build" and "One Big Idea That Still Matters"
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because those sections talk about Rome's history and the idea of diversity, not specific comparisons to America.

choices.text: "Words You Speak Every Day" and "One Big Idea That Still Matters"
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because 'Words You Speak Every Day' discusses Latin and English words, not connections to the United States.

syllabus_metadata.title: Rome's Lasting Legacy: How Ancient Rome Shaped Our World

assessments.title: People of the North: Where the Vikings Came From

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Vikings: Sailors from the Frozen North

A Ship on the Horizon

A long wooden ship cuts through gray waves. Its carved dragon head stares forward with hollow eyes. Thirty men pull at the oars, their breath turning to mist in the cold air. They have sailed for days from their homeland, and the green coast of England is finally in sight.
stimulus.content_text:
The Vikings: Sailors from the Frozen NorthA Ship on the Horizon
A long wooden ship cuts through gray waves. Its carved dragon head stares forward with hollow eyes. Thirty men pull at the oars, their breath turning to mist in the cold air. They have sailed for days from their homeland, and the green coast of England is finally in sight.

items.prompt: The text says the coast is green. In contrast, what color are the waves?

choices.text: The waves are gray.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage says 'gray waves' in the very first sentence — that's your evidence!

choices.text: The waves are blue.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the passage never says the waves are blue — that's just what waves sometimes look like in pictures or other stories.

choices.text: The waves are white.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage does not mention white waves anywhere in the text.

choices.text: The waves are brown.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage never describes the waves as brown — that word is not used at all.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
A Hard Land to Call Home

The Vikings came from Scandinavia, the part of northern Europe that includes Norway, Sweden, and Denmark today. Picture a place where winter lasts most of the year. Snow buries the ground for months. Mountains cover much of Norway, and thick forests stretch across Sweden. Along the coast, the sea has carved deep, narrow inlets called fjords. A fjord is like a river valley that the ocean flooded — steep cliffs rise on both sides, and cold water fills the bottom.

With so many mountains and so much snow, there was not much good farmland to go around. When a father's land passed to his oldest son, the younger sons had nothing. Many looked to the sea for a new life.
stimulus.content_text:
A Hard Land to Call Home
The Vikings came from Scandinavia, the part of northern Europe that includes Norway, Sweden, and Denmark today. Picture a place where winter lasts most of the year. Snow buries the ground for months. Mountains cover much of Norway, and thick forests stretch across Sweden. Along the coast, the sea has carved deep, narrow inlets called fjords. A fjord is like a river valley that the ocean flooded — steep cliffs rise on both sides, and cold water fills the bottom.
With so many mountains and so much snow, there was not much good farmland to go around. When a father's land passed to his oldest son, the younger sons had nothing. Many looked to the sea for a new life.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, why did many younger sons in Scandinavia look to the sea for a new life?

choices.text: They wanted to explore the deep fjords along the coast.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage mentions fjords to describe what Scandinavia looks like, not as a reason why younger sons left home.

choices.text: They had no farmland because it went to the oldest son.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly says that farmland passed to the oldest son, leaving younger sons with nothing, so they turned to the sea instead.

choices.text: They were tired of living in thick forests in Sweden.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage never says anyone was tired of living in the forests — that detail is just part of describing the land.

choices.text: They needed to find warmer places with less snow.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage never mentions younger sons leaving to find warmer places or escape the snow.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Masters of the Sea

They learned to fish, swim, and sail in the sheltered fjords. The rough northern seas made them skillful and brave. By the time a Viking boy became a man, he could steer a ship through fierce storms.

Their secret weapon was the longship. Viking longships were light, fast, and narrow. They sat so low in the water that Vikings could sail them up shallow rivers. Other ships could only stay in deep water, but longships could go almost anywhere. A dragon head or snake head was often carved at the front.
stimulus.content_text:
Masters of the Sea
They learned to fish, swim, and sail in the sheltered fjords. The rough northern seas made them skillful and brave. By the time a Viking boy became a man, he could steer a ship through fierce storms.
Their secret weapon was the longship. Viking longships were light, fast, and narrow. They sat so low in the water that Vikings could sail them up shallow rivers. Other ships could only stay in deep water, but longships could go almost anywhere. A dragon head or snake head was often carved at the front.

items.prompt: According to the text, what did Viking boys learn to do before they became men who could steer ships through storms?

choices.text: They learned to build longships.
choices.feedback: The passage never says Viking boys learned to build longships — that detail isn't in the text at all.

choices.text: They learned to carve dragon heads.
choices.feedback: Carving dragon heads is mentioned as a feature of longships, but the passage doesn't say boys learned to carve them.

choices.text: They learned to sail in the fjords.
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage clearly says they learned to fish, swim, and sail in the sheltered fjords before becoming skilled enough to handle fierce storms.

choices.text: They learned to travel on deep rivers.
choices.feedback: This is tricky, but the passage says longships could sail up shallow rivers — not deep rivers — so this answer has it backwards.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Where the Vikings Went

The Viking Age lasted from about AD 793 to AD 1066. During those years, Vikings burst out of Scandinavia in every direction. They sailed west to Iceland and Greenland. They sailed east along the rivers of Russia. They sailed south to raid the coasts of England, France, and Spain. They even reached the Mediterranean Sea, the warm body of water between Europe and Africa, and traveled to Constantinople, one of the largest cities in the world at that time.

Vikings also reached North America about 500 years before Columbus, landing on the coast of what is now Canada. So the next time someone says Columbus was the first European in the Americas — you will know better.
stimulus.content_text:
Where the Vikings Went
The Viking Age lasted from about AD 793 to AD 1066. During those years, Vikings burst out of Scandinavia in every direction. They sailed west to Iceland and Greenland. They sailed east along the rivers of Russia. They sailed south to raid the coasts of England, France, and Spain. They even reached the Mediterranean Sea, the warm body of water between Europe and Africa, and traveled to Constantinople, one of the largest cities in the world at that time.
Vikings also reached North America about 500 years before Columbus, landing on the coast of what is now Canada. So the next time someone says Columbus was the first European in the Americas — you will know better.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, how does the Vikings' trip to the Americas compare to Columbus's trip?

choices.text: The Vikings reached the Americas long before Columbus did.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage clearly says Vikings reached North America about 500 years before Columbus, which means they arrived long before he did.

choices.text: The Vikings reached the Americas long after Columbus did.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the passage says the Vikings came first — Columbus actually came about 500 years AFTER the Vikings.

choices.text: The Vikings reached the Americas at the same time Columbus did.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because 500 years is a very long time apart, so they definitely did not arrive at the same time.

choices.text: The Vikings reached the Americas by following Columbus's path.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because Columbus came after the Vikings, so the Vikings could not have followed a path that Columbus hadn't even traveled yet.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
More Than Warriors

Not all Vikings were raiders. Most of them never raided anyone. They were farmers who kept cattle and grew barley. They were fishermen who pulled cod from icy waters. They were craftsmen — skilled workers who built ships and shaped metal. They were traders who swapped furs and walrus ivory for silver and silk. The raiders got all the fame, but they were only one part of a much larger Norse society. In fact, the famous horned helmets most people picture on Vikings were never really worn by them — that idea came from costumes and paintings made hundreds of years after the Viking Age ended.
stimulus.content_text:
More Than Warriors
Not all Vikings were raiders. Most of them never raided anyone. They were farmers who kept cattle and grew barley. They were fishermen who pulled cod from icy waters. They were craftsmen — skilled workers who built ships and shaped metal. They were traders who swapped furs and walrus ivory for silver and silk. The raiders got all the fame, but they were only one part of a much larger Norse society. In fact, the famous horned helmets most people picture on Vikings were never really worn by them — that idea came from costumes and paintings made hundreds of years after the Viking Age ended.

items.prompt:
Read this sentence from the text: "They were craftsmen — skilled workers who built ships and shaped metal."

According to this sentence, what is the definition of the word "craftsmen"?

choices.text: Skilled people who grow crops.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage says craftsmen built ships and shaped metal — growing crops is what farmers do.

choices.text: Skilled people who build things.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage defines craftsmen as 'skilled workers who built ships and shaped metal,' which means they are skilled people who build things.

choices.text: Skilled people who trade goods.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because trading goods describes traders, who the passage mentions as a completely different group from craftsmen.

choices.text: Skilled people who wear helmets.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because wearing helmets is never part of the definition of craftsmen given in the passage.

items.title: Section 6 - Guiding Question 5

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
From Frozen Shores to the Wider World

The Vikings turned every disadvantage into a tool. A shortage of farmland became a reason to explore. Harsh weather made them tough enough to survive anywhere. In their light, fast longships, people from a frozen corner of Europe reached lands thousands of miles away. The next time you see a map of the North Atlantic, trace a line from Norway to Canada. That is a Viking road, drawn a thousand years ago by people who refused to stay put.
stimulus.content_text:
From Frozen Shores to the Wider World
The Vikings turned every disadvantage into a tool. A shortage of farmland became a reason to explore. Harsh weather made them tough enough to survive anywhere. In their light, fast longships, people from a frozen corner of Europe reached lands thousands of miles away. The next time you see a map of the North Atlantic, trace a line from Norway to Canada. That is a Viking road, drawn a thousand years ago by people who refused to stay put.

items.prompt: Based on the section "From Frozen Shores to the Wider World," which detail provides evidence that the Vikings were determined people who did not give up easily?

choices.text: They lived in a very cold corner of Europe.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because living in a cold place tells us about their home, not about how determined or tough they were.

choices.text: They turned every disadvantage into a tool.
choices.feedback: B is correct because turning every disadvantage into a tool shows the Vikings kept going and found solutions even when things were hard.

choices.text: They built longships that were fast and light.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because building fast, light ships tells us they were good builders, but it doesn't show they refused to give up when facing challenges.

choices.text: They drew a long road from Norway to Canada.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage uses 'road' as a comparison to describe their sailing route — the Vikings didn't actually draw a road on a map.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: How is what most people believe about Viking helmets different from what the text says is true?

choices.text: People think Vikings wore horned helmets, but they never really did
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage directly says people picture Vikings in horned helmets, but Vikings never really wore them — it was just an idea from old costumes and paintings.

choices.text: People think Vikings wore metal helmets, but they wore leather ones
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never mentions leather helmets at all — it only talks about the horned helmet myth.

choices.text: People think Vikings had no helmets, but they actually wore some
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never says people think Vikings had no helmets — the mix-up is about horns, not whether helmets existed.

choices.text: People think Vikings wore plain helmets, but they had dragon heads
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never compares plain helmets to dragon-head helmets — dragon heads are mentioned on ships, not helmets.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: The section 'A Hard Land to Call Home' explains that a lack of farmland caused the Vikings to look to the sea. Which heading helps you find the section that explains what caused them to be so successful at sailing?

choices.text: "A Ship on the Horizon"
choices.feedback: 'A Ship on the Horizon' is a short opening scene that describes a Viking ship arriving near England — it doesn't explain what made Vikings successful at sailing.

choices.text: "Masters of the Sea"
choices.feedback: 'Masters of the Sea' is correct because it explains how growing up near the fjords and sailing in rough northern seas made Vikings skilled and brave, and how their special longships helped them succeed.

choices.text: "Where the Vikings Went"
choices.feedback: 'Where the Vikings Went' lists all the faraway places Vikings traveled to, but it doesn't explain the reasons they were so good at sailing.

choices.text: "More Than Warriors"
choices.feedback: 'More Than Warriors' talks about Vikings being farmers, fishermen, and traders, but it doesn't explain what caused them to be successful sailors.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: Which sequence of actions best summarizes what the Vikings did in the article?

choices.text: They survived a harsh land, learned to sail, and explored the world.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the article shows Vikings going from surviving a harsh land, to learning to sail, to exploring the whole world — that is the main story from beginning to end.

choices.text: They built wooden ships, fought many wars, and wore horned helmets.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the article says most Vikings were farmers and traders, not warriors, and it clearly states they never actually wore horned helmets.

choices.text: They sailed to England, found good farmland, and stayed in Europe.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the Vikings sailed far beyond Europe, reaching places like Russia, North America, and Constantinople — not just England.

choices.text: They found new farmland, built fast ships, and learned to catch fish.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the Vikings learned to fish early on while growing up near the fjords, so fishing was something they already knew, not something that came after building ships.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: Based on the passage, how were the Viking raiders and the Viking traders similar?

choices.text: Both traveled far away to get new things.
choices.feedback: A is correct because both raiders and traders left Scandinavia and traveled far away — raiders to raid coasts and traders to swap goods like furs for silver and silk.

choices.text: Both stayed in Scandinavia to plant crops.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the passage says younger sons had no farmland and looked to the sea, so they did NOT stay in Scandinavia to plant crops.

choices.text: Both fished for cod in the icy ocean water.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because fishing for cod is only mentioned as something Viking fishermen did, not something raiders and traders both did.

choices.text: Both built the fast longships out of wood.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the passage says craftsmen were the skilled workers who built the ships, not the raiders and traders.

syllabus_metadata.title: People of the North: Where the Vikings Came From

assessments.title: Masters of the Sea: Viking Longships

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Viking Longship: A Boat That Could Go Anywhere

A Ship Pulled from the Mud

In 1904, workers in Norway dug into a mound of earth and found a wooden ship buried underground. A Viking chief had been placed inside it after death, surrounded by weapons, treasures, and tools. Its curved wooden body swept upward like a stretched-out smile. This was a longship — and it carried the Vikings across the world.

Vikings lived during the medieval period, roughly 793 to 1066 CE. Most were farmers and traders from Scandinavia — the part of Europe that includes Norway, Sweden, and Denmark.
stimulus.content_text:
The Viking Longship: A Boat That Could Go AnywhereA Ship Pulled from the Mud
In 1904, workers in Norway dug into a mound of earth and found a wooden ship buried underground. A Viking chief had been placed inside it after death, surrounded by weapons, treasures, and tools. Its curved wooden body swept upward like a stretched-out smile. This was a longship — and it carried the Vikings across the world.
Vikings lived during the medieval period, roughly 793 to 1066 CE. Most were farmers and traders from Scandinavia — the part of Europe that includes Norway, Sweden, and Denmark.

items.prompt: According to the text, what did most Vikings do, even though they are often known for sailing longships?

choices.text: They were warriors and kings
choices.feedback: The passage mentions a Viking chief, but it never says most Vikings were warriors and kings.

choices.text: They were farmers and traders
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says 'most were farmers and traders,' which is exactly what option B says.

choices.text: They were builders and sailors
choices.feedback: The passage talks about longships and sailing, but it never says most Vikings were builders and sailors.

choices.text: They were chiefs and explorers
choices.feedback: The passage mentions a chief and hints at exploration, but it does not say most Vikings were chiefs and explorers.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Built for Speed and Surprise

A typical longship stretched about 75 feet long but only 17 feet wide. That narrow shape sliced through waves the way a blade cuts through tall grass. A carved dragon or serpent head rose from the front. Vikings believed these carvings scared off enemies and evil spirits.

The longship's most clever trick was its shallow draft — the depth of the ship sitting below the water, like how deep your feet sink into a puddle. A longship's draft was less than three feet. That meant it could float in water barely deeper than a bathtub. Vikings could land right on a beach or glide up a narrow river. Other nations built heavy ships that needed deep harbors, or sheltered areas of deep water where ships dock. Viking ships could go almost anywhere water flowed.
stimulus.content_text:
Built for Speed and Surprise
A typical longship stretched about 75 feet long but only 17 feet wide. That narrow shape sliced through waves the way a blade cuts through tall grass. A carved dragon or serpent head rose from the front. Vikings believed these carvings scared off enemies and evil spirits.
The longship's most clever trick was its shallow draft — the depth of the ship sitting below the water, like how deep your feet sink into a puddle. A longship's draft was less than three feet. That meant it could float in water barely deeper than a bathtub. Vikings could land right on a beach or glide up a narrow river. Other nations built heavy ships that needed deep harbors, or sheltered areas of deep water where ships dock. Viking ships could go almost anywhere water flowed.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, why could Viking ships land right on a beach?

choices.text: because they had dragon heads on the front
choices.feedback: The dragon heads were for scaring enemies and evil spirits, not for helping the ship land on a beach.

choices.text: because they sat very shallow in the water
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says the shallow draft meant the ship could float in very shallow water, letting Vikings land right on a beach.

choices.text: because they were longer than other ships
choices.feedback: The passage talks about the ship's length, but being long is not the reason it could land on a beach.

choices.text: because they docked in deep harbors
choices.feedback: This is actually the opposite — the passage says OTHER nations needed deep harbors, but Viking ships did NOT need them.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Sails and Oars Together

When the wind blew strong, Vikings raised a large square sail to catch it. When the wind died, the crew grabbed oars and rowed. A longship could carry sixty warriors or more. This mix of sail and oar power meant the ship was never stuck waiting for weather.
stimulus.content_text:
Sails and Oars Together
When the wind blew strong, Vikings raised a large square sail to catch it. When the wind died, the crew grabbed oars and rowed. A longship could carry sixty warriors or more. This mix of sail and oar power meant the ship was never stuck waiting for weather.

items.prompt: According to the text, what did the crew do right after the wind died?

choices.text: They raised a large square sail.
choices.feedback: Raising the sail is what the Vikings did when the wind was blowing strong, not after it died.

choices.text: They grabbed oars and rowed.
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says 'When the wind died, the crew grabbed oars and rowed' — that's exactly what the question asks.

choices.text: They waited for better weather.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because the passage says the ship was 'never stuck waiting for weather,' meaning they always had another way to keep moving.

choices.text: They picked up sixty warriors.
choices.feedback: Sixty warriors is how many people the longship could carry, not something the crew did when the wind stopped.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Overlapping Planks, Flexible Strength

Building a longship took great skill. Shipbuilders split tall, straight oak trees into planks and shaped each one by hand with axes. They used a method called clinker-building, where each plank overlapped the one below it — like how you might stack playing cards so each one peeks out beneath the next. Iron rivets, small metal bolts hammered tight, held the planks together. The overlapping design kept water out. It also let the hull — the body of the ship — bend and flex with the waves instead of cracking apart.
stimulus.content_text:
Overlapping Planks, Flexible Strength
Building a longship took great skill. Shipbuilders split tall, straight oak trees into planks and shaped each one by hand with axes. They used a method called clinker-building, where each plank overlapped the one below it — like how you might stack playing cards so each one peeks out beneath the next. Iron rivets, small metal bolts hammered tight, held the planks together. The overlapping design kept water out. It also let the hull — the body of the ship — bend and flex with the waves instead of cracking apart.

items.prompt: Based on the author's comparison to stacking playing cards, what does the word clinker-building mean?

choices.text: Placing wooden planks so they partly cover one another.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage explains clinker-building as planks overlapping each other, just like playing cards that peek out beneath the next one.

choices.text: Fastening wooden planks tightly with small metal bolts.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because using metal bolts describes rivets, which is a different word explained separately in the passage.

choices.text: Shaping wooden planks so they can bend in the big waves.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because bending with the waves is what the overlapping design allows the hull to do, not the definition of clinker-building itself.

choices.text: Splitting tall oak trees into perfectly straight planks.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because splitting oak trees into planks is how shipbuilders prepared the wood, which happens before clinker-building starts.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
More Than a Warship

The longship was built for speed and battle. But Vikings also built wider, deeper ships called knarrs for trading voyages. Knarrs moved slower, but their big open bellies could hold heavy cargo — furs, dried fish, and walrus ivory, the valuable white tusks of walruses. Knarrs carried these goods hundreds of miles across the ocean.

Thanks to both kinds of ships, Vikings reached places no other Europeans had been. They crossed the North Atlantic to Iceland and Greenland. They sailed up the rivers of Russia. They raided the coasts of England and France and pushed into the Mediterranean Sea.
stimulus.content_text:
More Than a Warship
The longship was built for speed and battle. But Vikings also built wider, deeper ships called knarrs for trading voyages. Knarrs moved slower, but their big open bellies could hold heavy cargo — furs, dried fish, and walrus ivory, the valuable white tusks of walruses. Knarrs carried these goods hundreds of miles across the ocean.
Thanks to both kinds of ships, Vikings reached places no other Europeans had been. They crossed the North Atlantic to Iceland and Greenland. They sailed up the rivers of Russia. They raided the coasts of England and France and pushed into the Mediterranean Sea.

items.prompt: According to the text, what is a knarr?

choices.text: a fast ship used for battle
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage says longships were built for speed and battle, not knarrs.

choices.text: a wider ship used for trading
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly says Vikings built 'wider, deeper ships called knarrs for trading voyages.'

choices.text: a white tusk from a walrus
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because walrus ivory refers to the valuable white tusks of walruses, which were goods carried inside knarrs.

choices.text: a river route through Russia
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the rivers of Russia are a place Vikings sailed to, not a kind of ship.

items.title: Section 6 - Guiding Question 5

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
Buried with Their Ships

Vikings treated their ships with deep respect. Some chiefs were buried inside their longships along with everything they might need in the afterlife — swords, jewelry, food, even animals. These ship burials tell us that Vikings believed life continued after death and that a good ship would carry its owner into the next world, too.

Today, you can visit a real Viking ship in a museum in Oslo, Norway. Its dark wood still curves gracefully after a thousand years. A single boat, built with axes and iron bolts, once gave its owners the open ocean as a road.
stimulus.content_text:
Buried with Their Ships
Vikings treated their ships with deep respect. Some chiefs were buried inside their longships along with everything they might need in the afterlife — swords, jewelry, food, even animals. These ship burials tell us that Vikings believed life continued after death and that a good ship would carry its owner into the next world, too.
Today, you can visit a real Viking ship in a museum in Oslo, Norway. Its dark wood still curves gracefully after a thousand years. A single boat, built with axes and iron bolts, once gave its owners the open ocean as a road.

items.prompt: You might think a very old boat would look ugly and ruined. Which detail from the passage gives evidence that the author thinks the Viking ship is still beautiful?

choices.text: The wood still curves gracefully.
choices.feedback: This is correct because 'curves gracefully' are words the author uses to describe how the ship looks, and 'gracefully' means beautiful and smooth — that's evidence the author thinks it's still pretty.

choices.text: The ships were built with axes.
choices.feedback: This tells us how the ship was built, but it doesn't say anything about whether the ship looks beautiful or ugly today.

choices.text: The Vikings respected their ships.
choices.feedback: This tells us how Vikings felt about their ships long ago, but it doesn't give us any clue about what the ship looks like now.

choices.text: The chiefs were buried inside them.
choices.feedback: This is an interesting fact about Viking burial customs, but it doesn't describe the ship's appearance or tell us the author thinks it's beautiful.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: How does the author connect the information in 'Built for Speed and Surprise' to the information in 'More Than a Warship'?

choices.text: By explaining how longships slowly changed into knarrs over time.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage never says longships changed or turned into knarrs over time — they were just two different kinds of ships.

choices.text: By comparing how the two ships were built for very different jobs.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the author connects the two sections by showing that longships were built for speed and battle, while knarrs were built to carry heavy cargo on trading trips.

choices.text: By showing that the heavy knarrs were the reason longships were made.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage never says knarrs were the reason longships were made — the two ships were built separately for different purposes.

choices.text: By describing the exact steps Vikings took to build both kinds of ships.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage only describes how longships were built in detail, and does not give step-by-step building instructions for knarrs.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: If you want to find out what caused Viking ships to float in shallow rivers and what caused them to be able to carry heavy cargo, which two headings should you read?

choices.text: "A Ship Pulled from the Mud" and "Sails and Oars Together"
choices.feedback: Wrong — 'A Ship Pulled from the Mud' is about a buried ship being discovered, and 'Sails and Oars Together' is about how Vikings used wind and oars, so neither one answers both parts of the question.

choices.text: "Built for Speed and Surprise" and "More Than a Warship"
choices.feedback: Correct — 'Built for Speed and Surprise' explains the shallow draft that let ships float in rivers, and 'More Than a Warship' explains how knarr ships carried heavy cargo.

choices.text: "Overlapping Planks, Flexible Strength" and "Buried with Their Ships"
choices.feedback: Wrong — 'Overlapping Planks, Flexible Strength' is about how ships were built with wood and iron bolts, and 'Buried with Their Ships' is about Viking burial customs, not shallow water or cargo.

choices.text: "A Ship Pulled from the Mud" and "Buried with Their Ships"
choices.feedback: Wrong — 'A Ship Pulled from the Mud' is about discovering a buried ship, and 'Buried with Their Ships' is about Viking burial traditions, so neither section answers the question.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: The main idea of the text is that longships were important to Vikings from the time they were made until after they died. Which sequence of key details is in the correct order to support this main idea?

choices.text: Builders shaped oak trees into ships, crews sailed them across oceans, and chiefs were buried in them.
choices.feedback: A is correct because it puts the details in the right order — building, sailing, and burial — which shows how longships were important to Vikings from beginning to end.

choices.text: Crews sailed ships across oceans, chiefs were buried in them, and builders shaped oak trees into ships.
choices.feedback: B has the right details but in the wrong order, since ships had to be built before they could be sailed.

choices.text: Workers dug ships out of the mud, museums put them on display, and people visit them in Norway.
choices.feedback: C talks about modern-day events like museums and visitors, which doesn't support the main idea about why longships mattered to Vikings.

choices.text: Builders hammered iron bolts tight, crews waited for the wind to die, and chiefs traded dried fish.
choices.feedback: D picks small, unrelated details that don't work together to show how longships were important throughout a Viking's life and death.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: How does the author compare the longship's job while a Viking was alive to its job after a Viking died?

choices.text: It carried Vikings across the ocean in life, and it carried them to the next world in death.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage says longships carried Vikings across the ocean in life, and chiefs were buried in their ships so the ship could carry them into the next world after death.

choices.text: It carried heavy trading goods in life, and it carried tools for building ships in death.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because heavy trading goods were carried by knarrs, not longships, and the passage never mentions tools for building ships being buried.

choices.text: It scared away evil spirits in life, and it kept buried treasures safe from thieves in death.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because while dragon carvings did scare away spirits, the passage never says the buried ship's job was to protect treasures from thieves.

choices.text: It floated in shallow river water in life, and it rested in deep ocean harbors in death.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the passage says longships floated in shallow water, but it never says they rested in deep ocean harbors after a Viking died.

syllabus_metadata.title: Masters of the Sea: Viking Longships

assessments.title: Leif Erikson and the Discovery of Vinland

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Viking Who Beat Columbus by 500 Years

A Stranger Spotted Land

Somewhere around the year 1000, a wooden ship rocked through icy waves in the North Atlantic. Their leader, a young man named Leif Erikson, stared west into fog. He was looking for a place no European had ever touched.

Leif was Norse — a word that means he came from the Viking people of Scandinavia, the cold northern countries we now call Norway, Sweden, and Denmark. You might picture Vikings as fierce warriors who raided villages. But they were also skilled sailors, traders, and farmers. Leif Erikson's story shows that side of them.
stimulus.content_text:
The Viking Who Beat Columbus by 500 YearsA Stranger Spotted Land
Somewhere around the year 1000, a wooden ship rocked through icy waves in the North Atlantic. Their leader, a young man named Leif Erikson, stared west into fog. He was looking for a place no European had ever touched.

Leif was Norse — a word that means he came from the Viking people of Scandinavia, the cold northern countries we now call Norway, Sweden, and Denmark. You might picture Vikings as fierce warriors who raided villages. But they were also skilled sailors, traders, and farmers. Leif Erikson's story shows that side of them.

items.prompt: According to the text, people might picture Vikings as fierce warriors, but what else were they?

choices.text: clever builders, hunters, and fishermen
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage never mentions builders, hunters, or fishermen — those words don't appear anywhere in the text.

choices.text: skilled sailors, traders, and farmers
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage uses these exact words: 'they were also skilled sailors, traders, and farmers.'

choices.text: famous kings, princes, and soldiers
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage doesn't say anything about Vikings being kings, princes, or soldiers.

choices.text: young leaders, guides, and explorers
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because even though Leif Erikson is a young leader and explorer, the passage doesn't list those words when describing what Vikings were.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
A Family of Adventurers

Leif grew up in Greenland, surrounded by ice and treeless hills. His father, Erik the Red, had been the first European to settle there — after getting kicked out of Iceland for starting fights.

In Greenland, Leif heard a wild story. A trader named Bjarni Herjolfsson had been sailing and spotted unknown land to the west. Leif wanted to do what Bjarni would not.
stimulus.content_text:
A Family of Adventurers
Leif grew up in Greenland, surrounded by ice and treeless hills. His father, Erik the Red, had been the first European to settle there — after getting kicked out of Iceland for starting fights.

In Greenland, Leif heard a wild story. A trader named Bjarni Herjolfsson had been sailing and spotted unknown land to the west. Leif wanted to do what Bjarni would not.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, why did Erik the Red end up settling in Greenland?

choices.text: He wanted to find land west of Iceland.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never says Erik wanted to find land west of Iceland — that was Leif's goal.

choices.text: He was forced to leave Iceland for fighting.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly says Erik was 'kicked out of Iceland for starting fights,' which is why he ended up in Greenland.

choices.text: He heard a wild story about unknown land.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the wild story about unknown land was something Leif heard in Greenland, and it had nothing to do with why Erik moved there.

choices.text: He was trying to follow Bjarni's route.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because Bjarni's route is something Leif learned about later — Erik had already settled in Greenland before any of that happened.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Reaching Vinland

Leif and his crew sailed west in a longship — a long, narrow boat with a big square sail, built for crossing open ocean. With no maps, they watched the sun and stars to find their way.

After days at sea, they spotted land. They explored the coastline and found different places. One was flat and covered with rocks. Another was thick with trees. But the place Leif liked best had mild weather, green grass, and streams full of salmon. He called it Vinland. Some scholars — people who study a subject deeply, like detectives of history — think "vin" meant grapes. Others think it meant "meadow" in Old Norse.

When spring came, they loaded their ship with timber and sailed home. Wood was precious in treeless Greenland.
stimulus.content_text:
Reaching Vinland
Leif and his crew sailed west in a longship — a long, narrow boat with a big square sail, built for crossing open ocean. With no maps, they watched the sun and stars to find their way.

After days at sea, they spotted land. They explored the coastline and found different places. One was flat and covered with rocks. Another was thick with trees. But the place Leif liked best had mild weather, green grass, and streams full of salmon. He called it Vinland. Some scholars — people who study a subject deeply, like detectives of history — think "vin" meant grapes. Others think it meant "meadow" in Old Norse.

When spring came, they loaded their ship with timber and sailed home. Wood was precious in treeless Greenland.

items.prompt: According to the text, how are the season of spring and the crew's journey related?

choices.text: When spring came, they loaded their ship and sailed home.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage says 'When spring came, they loaded their ship with timber and sailed home' — those are the exact same words!

choices.text: Before spring came, they built a longship and sailed home.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the crew built and used their longship before they ever reached Vinland, not right before sailing home in spring.

choices.text: After spring ended, they watched the stars and sailed home.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because watching the stars helped them find their way TO Vinland, and the passage never connects star-watching to their trip home.

choices.text: While spring lasted, they explored the coast and sailed home.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the crew explored the coastline when they first arrived, not during spring — spring is when they packed up and left.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Proof in the Ground

For hundreds of years, people thought Vinland was just a legend. The story survived in sagas — long adventure tales that Norse families passed down by telling them aloud, the way you might retell your favorite movie scene by scene. Were the sagas true, or just good stories?

Then, in the 1960s, two archaeologists — scientists who dig up old objects to learn about the past — found something astonishing. At a place called L'Anse aux Meadows in Newfoundland, Canada, they uncovered the remains of a Viking settlement. They found building foundations, iron nails, and other artifacts, which are objects made by people long ago, like clues left behind at a crime scene. Vikings really had reached North America about one thousand years ago.
stimulus.content_text:
Proof in the Ground
For hundreds of years, people thought Vinland was just a legend. The story survived in sagas — long adventure tales that Norse families passed down by telling them aloud, the way you might retell your favorite movie scene by scene. Were the sagas true, or just good stories?

Then, in the 1960s, two archaeologists — scientists who dig up old objects to learn about the past — found something astonishing. At a place called L'Anse aux Meadows in Newfoundland, Canada, they uncovered the remains of a Viking settlement. They found building foundations, iron nails, and other artifacts, which are objects made by people long ago, like clues left behind at a crime scene. Vikings really had reached North America about one thousand years ago.

items.prompt: The author compares artifacts to clues at a crime scene. Based on this comparison, how are artifacts and clues similar?

choices.text: Both are objects that help solve a mystery about the past.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage directly compares artifacts to clues at a crime scene, and both help you solve a mystery — artifacts solve mysteries about the past.

choices.text: Both are items made out of iron nails and pieces of wood.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because iron nails and wood describe some artifacts found at L'Anse aux Meadows, but not all artifacts or clues are made of those materials.

choices.text: Both are stories discovered by reading long adventure tales.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because artifacts are real physical objects that people dig up, not stories or adventure tales.

choices.text: Both are foundations buried deep underneath the cold ground.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because foundations are just one type of artifact found at that site, and crime scene clues are not buried deep underground.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Why Didn't the Vikings Stay?

Leif Erikson reached North America nearly five hundred years before Christopher Columbus sailed in 1492. So why don't we hear about it as often?

The Viking settlement lasted only a few years. Old stories say the Vikings clashed with the Indigenous peoples — the original people — who had already been living on that land for thousands of years. The Vikings were far from home with few supplies. Without more help, the small colony could not survive. They left, and Europeans forgot about the land across the sea.

Columbus's later voyage led to lasting contact between Europe and the Americas. But Columbus was not the first European to arrive.
stimulus.content_text:
Why Didn't the Vikings Stay?
Leif Erikson reached North America nearly five hundred years before Christopher Columbus sailed in 1492. So why don't we hear about it as often?

The Viking settlement lasted only a few years. Old stories say the Vikings clashed with the Indigenous peoples — the original people — who had already been living on that land for thousands of years. The Vikings were far from home with few supplies. Without more help, the small colony could not survive. They left, and Europeans forgot about the land across the sea.

Columbus's later voyage led to lasting contact between Europe and the Americas. But Columbus was not the first European to arrive.

items.prompt: The word Indigenous is printed in bold in this section. How does this text feature help a reader find the definition of that word?

choices.text: It shows the word is the title of the section
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the section title is the heading 'Why Didn't the Vikings Stay?' at the top, not a bolded word inside the text.

choices.text: It signals that the word is explained nearby in the text
choices.feedback: B is correct because the bold word 'Indigenous' is immediately followed by a definition — 'the original people' — showing that bold signals a nearby explanation.

choices.text: It means the word is repeated many times in the section
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the word 'Indigenous' only appears one time in the passage, not many times.

choices.text: It tells the reader to look in a different section
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the reader doesn't need to look anywhere else — the meaning of Indigenous is explained right next to the bold word in the same sentence.

items.title: Section 6 - Guiding Question 5

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
A Day on the Calendar

Every October 9, the United States celebrates Leif Erikson Day. A young man from icy Greenland sailed a wooden boat across one of the roughest oceans on Earth — using just wind, waves, and nerve. And a thousand years later, your calendar still remembers his name.
stimulus.content_text:
A Day on the Calendar
Every October 9, the United States celebrates Leif Erikson Day. A young man from icy Greenland sailed a wooden boat across one of the roughest oceans on Earth — using just wind, waves, and nerve. And a thousand years later, your calendar still remembers his name.

items.prompt: Which detail from the text best shows that Leif Erikson's trip was dangerous?

choices.text: He sailed across a very rough ocean.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage uses the words 'roughest oceans on Earth,' which tells us the water was very dangerous to sail across.

choices.text: He received a day on the calendar.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because getting a day on the calendar is an honor, not something that shows his trip was dangerous.

choices.text: He traveled away from icy Greenland.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because leaving Greenland just tells us where he started — it doesn't tell us the trip was dangerous.

choices.text: He was remembered a thousand years later.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because being remembered a thousand years later shows he was famous, not that his journey was dangerous.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: Based on the text, how was Vinland different from the land where Leif Erikson grew up?

choices.text: Vinland had mild weather and many trees, while his home had ice and no trees.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage clearly says Greenland had ice and no trees, while Vinland had mild weather, green grass, and trees (the crew even brought timber home).

choices.text: Vinland had thick ice and cold snow, while his home had green grass and fish.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because it flips the descriptions — Greenland was the icy place, and Vinland was the one with green grass, not the other way around.

choices.text: Vinland had fierce Viking warriors, while his home had quiet little villages.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the passage never says Vinland had fierce warriors or that Greenland had quiet villages — those details simply aren't in the text.

choices.text: Vinland had large mountains and oceans, while his home had flat green meadows.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the passage never mentions large mountains in Vinland or flat green meadows in Greenland — those details were made up and don't come from the story.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: Based on the article, what caused Leif Erikson to sail west into the fog?

choices.text: He wanted to step on the unknown land that a trader had spotted.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the article clearly says Leif heard about unknown land spotted by a trader and wanted to sail there and explore it himself.

choices.text: He wanted to find a new home after being forced to leave Iceland.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because it was Leif's father, Erik the Red, who got kicked out of Iceland — not Leif.

choices.text: He wanted to live in a place with mild weather and many salmon.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because Leif didn't know about the mild weather or salmon before he sailed; he discovered those things after he arrived.

choices.text: He wanted to trade iron nails and other artifacts with the new people.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the iron nails were artifacts the Vikings left behind at their settlement, not items they planned to trade.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: Which section should a reader look at next to find out what happened after Leif and his crew sailed home in spring?

choices.text: the section about Leif growing up in Greenland
choices.feedback: Section A is about Leif growing up in Greenland, which happened before the voyage — not after it.

choices.text: the section about proof found in the ground
choices.feedback: Section B is correct because it tells what happened next: people wondered if Vinland was real, and scientists later found Viking ruins in Canada that proved the story was true.

choices.text: the section about reaching Vinland
choices.feedback: Section C is the section about reaching Vinland, which is the part we already read — it doesn't tell us what happened after they sailed home.

choices.text: the section about a day on the calendar
choices.feedback: Section D jumps all the way to Leif Erikson Day and skips over important events like the proof found by archaeologists.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: Which statement best compares Leif Erikson and Christopher Columbus to show the main idea of the article?

choices.text: Both men started colonies that lasted for many years in North America.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the article says the Viking settlement lasted only a few years, and there is no mention of Columbus starting a long-lasting colony either.

choices.text: Leif reached North America earlier, but Columbus's trip led to lasting contact.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the article clearly states Leif arrived nearly 500 years before Columbus, but Columbus's voyage — not Leif's — led to lasting contact between Europe and the Americas.

choices.text: Leif and Columbus were both looking for wood to bring back to Europe.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because only Leif's crew collected wood to bring back to Greenland — the article never says Columbus was looking for wood.

choices.text: Leif reached Vinland later, but people remembered his voyage right away.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the article says it was Leif who reached North America earlier (not later), and Europeans actually forgot about his voyage for hundreds of years.

syllabus_metadata.title: Leif Erikson and the Discovery of Vinland

assessments.title: Farmers, Craftsmen, and Storytellers: Daily Viking Life

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Vikings: Farmers, Craftsmen, and Storytellers

A Smoky Room, a Thousand Years Ago

Smoke curls up from a fire pit in the middle of a dirt floor. A whole family sits together in one long, dark room. Cows breathe softly behind a wooden wall at one end of the building. A grandmother begins to tell a story about a hero who sailed across a wild sea. This is a winter night in a Viking home, about a thousand years ago.

The Vikings lived in Scandinavia — Norway, Sweden, and Denmark — from about 793 to 1066 CE. You might picture Vikings as fierce warriors in horned helmets. But real Viking helmets had no horns at all. That idea comes from movies, not history. Most Vikings were actually farmers.
stimulus.content_text:
The Vikings: Farmers, Craftsmen, and StorytellersA Smoky Room, a Thousand Years Ago
Smoke curls up from a fire pit in the middle of a dirt floor. A whole family sits together in one long, dark room. Cows breathe softly behind a wooden wall at one end of the building. A grandmother begins to tell a story about a hero who sailed across a wild sea. This is a winter night in a Viking home, about a thousand years ago.

The Vikings lived in Scandinavia — Norway, Sweden, and Denmark — from about 793 to 1066 CE. You might picture Vikings as fierce warriors in horned helmets. But real Viking helmets had no horns at all. That idea comes from movies, not history. Most Vikings were actually farmers.

items.prompt: According to the text, people might picture Vikings as fierce warriors, but what were most Vikings instead?

choices.text: They were mostly heroes.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because 'heroes' only appear in a made-up story told by the grandmother — the passage never says most Vikings were heroes.

choices.text: They were mostly farmers.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage clearly states 'most Vikings were actually farmers,' right after explaining that the warrior image comes from movies.

choices.text: They were mostly sailors.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because even though a story about sailing is mentioned, the passage never says most Vikings were sailors.

choices.text: They were mostly storytellers.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because only one grandmother is shown telling a story — the passage does not say most Vikings were storytellers.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Working the Land from Dawn to Dark

They grew barley and oats in small fields near their homes. Summers in Scandinavia are short, so every sunny day mattered. Families plowed, planted, and harvested grain. They milked cows and made butter and cheese. They cut and dried hay so their animals could eat during the long winter.

When winter came, the ground froze hard. Farming stopped. Families stayed indoors, fixing tools and mending clothes. Their homes were called longhouses — big buildings shaped like upside-down boats, made of wood, stone, or turf, a layer of earth held together by grass roots. One large room held the whole family. A fire pit gave heat and light, but there were no windows. On the coldest nights, animals came inside too.
stimulus.content_text:
Working the Land from Dawn to Dark
They grew barley and oats in small fields near their homes. Summers in Scandinavia are short, so every sunny day mattered. Families plowed, planted, and harvested grain. They milked cows and made butter and cheese. They cut and dried hay so their animals could eat during the long winter.

When winter came, the ground froze hard. Farming stopped. Families stayed indoors, fixing tools and mending clothes. Their homes were called longhouses — big buildings shaped like upside-down boats, made of wood, stone, or turf, a layer of earth held together by grass roots. One large room held the whole family. A fire pit gave heat and light, but there were no windows. On the coldest nights, animals came inside too.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, why did Viking families cut and dry hay during the summer?

choices.text: because they wanted to build roofs for their longhouses
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage says longhouses were made of wood, stone, or turf — not hay.

choices.text: because their animals needed food during the long winter
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly says they cut and dried hay so their animals could eat during the long winter.

choices.text: because the ground froze and farming had to stop
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the ground freezing is why farming stopped, not why they cut hay — they cut hay before winter arrived.

choices.text: because they needed hay to start fires for heat
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage says a fire pit gave heat and light, and it never mentions using hay to start fires.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Three Kinds of People

Viking society had three groups. At the top were the jarls, or nobles — like the richest landowners in a town. Jarls owned huge farms and had many workers. In the middle were the karls, free people who owned smaller farms or worked as craftsmen. Most Vikings were karls. At the bottom were the thralls, enslaved people who had no freedom. Thralls did the hardest jobs. Some were captured during raids. Others were born into slavery or sold to pay debts. This system was deeply unfair, but it shaped everyday Viking life.
stimulus.content_text:
Three Kinds of People
Viking society had three groups. At the top were the jarls, or nobles — like the richest landowners in a town. Jarls owned huge farms and had many workers. In the middle were the karls, free people who owned smaller farms or worked as craftsmen. Most Vikings were karls. At the bottom were the thralls, enslaved people who had no freedom. Thralls did the hardest jobs. Some were captured during raids. Others were born into slavery or sold to pay debts. This system was deeply unfair, but it shaped everyday Viking life.

items.prompt: According to the text, when did some people become enslaved thralls?

choices.text: Right when they were born
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage says 'Others were born into slavery,' meaning some people became thralls right at birth.

choices.text: After they bought a farm
choices.feedback: B is wrong because buying or owning a farm is something karls (free people) did, not thralls.

choices.text: Before they went on raids
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the passage says some thralls were captured during raids, meaning it happened after a raid, not before.

choices.text: While they worked as craftsmen
choices.feedback: D is wrong because working as a craftsman was a job for karls, the free people in the middle group.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Skilled Hands

Viking craftsmen — people who made things by hand — were some of the best in Europe. Metalworkers heated iron, silver, and gold and hammered them into swords, axes, and brooches, decorative pins used to hold a cloak closed the way you might use a zipper. Woodcarvers cut detailed patterns of animals and swirling lines into ships, doorways, and furniture. Women spun sheep's wool into thread and wove it into cloth on tall wooden frames called looms — imagine a giant standing harp, but with thread stretched across it instead of strings.
stimulus.content_text:
Skilled Hands
Viking craftsmen — people who made things by hand — were some of the best in Europe. Metalworkers heated iron, silver, and gold and hammered them into swords, axes, and brooches, decorative pins used to hold a cloak closed the way you might use a zipper. Woodcarvers cut detailed patterns of animals and swirling lines into ships, doorways, and furniture. Women spun sheep's wool into thread and wove it into cloth on tall wooden frames called looms — imagine a giant standing harp, but with thread stretched across it instead of strings.

items.prompt: Based on the author's comparison to a zipper, what is the meaning of the word brooches?

choices.text: Pins used to keep clothing fastened together
