choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage says brooches are decorative pins that hold a cloak closed, just like a zipper holds your jacket closed.

choices.text: Tools used to cut patterns into solid wood
choices.feedback: B is wrong because cutting detailed patterns into wood is what woodcarvers do, not what brooches are used for.

choices.text: Frames used to stretch thread to make cloth
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the passage calls the tall wooden frames used to weave cloth 'looms,' not brooches.

choices.text: Weapons used to fight enemies during battles
choices.feedback: D is wrong because brooches are clothing pins, and the passage lists swords and axes — not brooches — as the weapons Vikings made.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Stories Around the Fire

Vikings did not write books. Instead, they remembered their history by telling stories out loud. These spoken tales were called sagas — long, exciting stories about heroes, gods, and ocean voyages. A skilled storyteller could hold an entire longhouse spellbound. People respected great storytellers the way you might admire a star athlete or musician.

For hundreds of years, sagas lived only in people's memories. Then, scholars in Iceland wrote them down on parchment, a writing material made from stretched animal skin. Because of those scholars, you can still read Viking sagas today.
stimulus.content_text:
Stories Around the Fire
Vikings did not write books. Instead, they remembered their history by telling stories out loud. These spoken tales were called sagas — long, exciting stories about heroes, gods, and ocean voyages. A skilled storyteller could hold an entire longhouse spellbound. People respected great storytellers the way you might admire a star athlete or musician.

For hundreds of years, sagas lived only in people's memories. Then, scholars in Iceland wrote them down on parchment, a writing material made from stretched animal skin. Because of those scholars, you can still read Viking sagas today.

items.prompt: In the section titled 'Stories Around the Fire,' which keyword would you look for to find the definition of a writing material made from stretched animal skin?

choices.text: sagas
choices.feedback: Sagas is a bold word, but it means long spoken stories about heroes, gods, and voyages — not a writing material.

choices.text: scholars
choices.feedback: Scholars are the people in Iceland who wrote the sagas down, not a material used for writing.

choices.text: parchment
choices.feedback: Parchment is correct because the passage defines it as 'a writing material made from stretched animal skin,' which matches the question exactly.

choices.text: longhouse
choices.feedback: Longhouse is mentioned in the passage, but it is not a bold keyword and the passage does not give a definition for it.

items.title: Section 6 - Guiding Question 5

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
More Than Raiders

The next time you see a Viking in a movie, remember that smoky longhouse. Remember the grandmother telling a story so good that no one wrote it down for centuries, and it survived anyway. The Vikings kept their whole world alive with strong hands and long memories.
stimulus.content_text:
More Than Raiders
The next time you see a Viking in a movie, remember that smoky longhouse. Remember the grandmother telling a story so good that no one wrote it down for centuries, and it survived anyway. The Vikings kept their whole world alive with strong hands and long memories.

items.prompt: Which detail from the text shows how Vikings kept their stories alive?

choices.text: They watched movies about smoky longhouses.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because Vikings never watched movies — movies are a modern invention that didn't exist in Viking times.

choices.text: They used strong hands and long memories.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly says 'The Vikings kept their whole world alive with strong hands and long memories,' showing they remembered and told their stories out loud.

choices.text: They built smoky longhouses to keep warm.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the smoky longhouse is just where people gathered, not the reason their stories survived.

choices.text: They wrote their stories down in big books.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage says the stories were NOT written down for centuries — they survived through memory and storytelling instead.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: Based on the passage, how was Viking life in the summer different from Viking life in the winter?

choices.text: In summer they worked outside on farms, but in winter they stayed inside fixing tools and telling stories.
choices.feedback: This is correct — the passage clearly says Vikings farmed outside in summer and stayed inside fixing things and telling stories in winter.

choices.text: In summer they sailed across the wild ocean, but in winter they fought as fierce warriors in battles.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never says Vikings sailed or fought battles in summer versus winter — those ideas are mixed up from the movie version of Vikings, not real life.

choices.text: In summer they lived in large wooden ships, but in winter they moved into upside-down boats made of dirt.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because Vikings lived in longhouses all year long, not in ships during summer — the passage says longhouses were shaped like upside-down boats, but they were real homes, not actual boats.

choices.text: In summer they ruled as rich nobles called jarls, but in winter they became enslaved workers called thralls.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because jarls and thralls were different groups of people in Viking society, not the same people switching roles between summer and winter.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: Why were Viking sagas able to survive for hundreds of years before anyone wrote them down?

choices.text: because Vikings had a writing system but chose not to use it
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says Vikings did not write books at all, not that they had a writing system and chose not to use it.

choices.text: because skilled storytellers memorized and retold the tales
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage says Vikings kept their sagas alive by telling them out loud, and skilled storytellers passed them down through memory for hundreds of years.

choices.text: because Vikings carved all their stories into wooden ships
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage only mentions woodcarvers making decorative patterns on ships, not carving stories into them.

choices.text: because families wrote the stories on stretched animal skin
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because scholars in Iceland wrote the sagas on parchment much later — it was not something Viking families did to keep the stories alive.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: Look under the heading "Stories Around the Fire." What happened to Viking stories after they were told out loud for hundreds of years?

choices.text: People in Iceland wrote them down.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage clearly states that scholars in Iceland wrote the sagas down on parchment, saving them for us to read today.

choices.text: Woodcarvers carved them into ships.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because woodcarvers made decorative animal patterns on ships and doorways — they did not carve stories.

choices.text: The Vikings printed them in books.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the passage tells us directly that Vikings did not write books.

choices.text: Families forgot them during the winter.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the stories were remembered and kept alive in people's memories for hundreds of years before being written down.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: How does the author compare real Vikings to movie Vikings to explain the main idea of the passage?

choices.text: Real Vikings were farmers and skilled creators, while movie Vikings are just fierce warriors.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the author contrasts the movie image of fierce warriors with the real Vikings who were farmers, craftsmen, and storytellers — which is exactly the main idea of the whole passage.

choices.text: Real Vikings wore helmets with very large horns, while movie Vikings wore helmets with no horns.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect and backwards — the passage says real Vikings had NO horns on their helmets, and the horned helmet idea comes from movies, not real history.

choices.text: Real Vikings sailed across the very wild seas, while movie Vikings stayed inside the longhouses.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage never says anything about movie Vikings staying inside longhouses — that detail is simply made up.

choices.text: Real Vikings wrote long books about heroes, while movie Vikings only told their stories out loud.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect and backwards — it was the real Vikings who told stories out loud and did NOT write books, while scholars (not Vikings) later wrote the sagas down on parchment.

assessments.title: Raiders and Traders: The Vikings' Impact on Europe

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Vikings: Raiders, Traders, and Settlers

Terror from the Sea

On a quiet summer morning in the year 793, monks knelt in prayer on a tiny island off the coast of England. They lived at the monastery of Lindisfarne — a building where religious people lived, worked, and studied together, like a school and church combined. Then ships appeared on the horizon. Warriors leaped ashore with swords and axes. They smashed through the doors, grabbed gold and silver, and seized the monks. The Viking Age had begun.

Churches across Europe added a new prayer: "From the fury of the Northmen, deliver us, O Lord." For the next 250 years, Viking raiders struck coastal towns and cities.
stimulus.content_text:
The Vikings: Raiders, Traders, and SettlersTerror from the Sea
On a quiet summer morning in the year 793, monks knelt in prayer on a tiny island off the coast of England. They lived at the monastery of Lindisfarne — a building where religious people lived, worked, and studied together, like a school and church combined. Then ships appeared on the horizon. Warriors leaped ashore with swords and axes. They smashed through the doors, grabbed gold and silver, and seized the monks. The Viking Age had begun.
Churches across Europe added a new prayer: "From the fury of the Northmen, deliver us, O Lord." For the next 250 years, Viking raiders struck coastal towns and cities.

items.prompt: According to the text, instead of praying quietly like the monks, what did the Viking warriors do?

choices.text: They studied inside the church.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because studying inside the church is what the monks did at the monastery, not what the Viking warriors did.

choices.text: They smashed through the doors.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage clearly states the warriors 'smashed through the doors' when they attacked the monastery.

choices.text: They prayed for the Northmen.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the prayer about the Northmen was said by churches across Europe who were afraid of the Vikings, not by the Vikings themselves.

choices.text: They sailed away on ships.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the Vikings actually jumped off their ships to attack — the passage says 'warriors leaped ashore.'

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Who Were the Vikings?

Vikings came from the cold, rocky lands we now call Norway, Sweden, and Denmark — a region called Scandinavia. You might picture Vikings as one group from one country, but they actually came from many different communities spread across these three lands.

Back home, Vikings farmed, fished, and raised animals. They were also astonishing shipbuilders. Their longships — narrow wooden boats with tall, curved fronts — could cross deep oceans and slide up shallow rivers. These fast, light ships made everything else possible: the raids, the trade, and the exploration.

And no, Vikings never wore horned helmets. That image comes from movies and cartoons. Real Viking helmets were plain metal or leather.
stimulus.content_text:
Who Were the Vikings?
Vikings came from the cold, rocky lands we now call Norway, Sweden, and Denmark — a region called Scandinavia. You might picture Vikings as one group from one country, but they actually came from many different communities spread across these three lands.
Back home, Vikings farmed, fished, and raised animals. They were also astonishing shipbuilders. Their longships — narrow wooden boats with tall, curved fronts — could cross deep oceans and slide up shallow rivers. These fast, light ships made everything else possible: the raids, the trade, and the exploration.
And no, Vikings never wore horned helmets. That image comes from movies and cartoons. Real Viking helmets were plain metal or leather.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, why were Vikings able to raid, trade, and explore so many places?

choices.text: They came from three different countries in Scandinavia
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because coming from three countries in Scandinavia tells us where Vikings lived, not why they could raid, trade, and explore.

choices.text: They built fast, light ships that could travel many waters
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly says their fast, light longships could travel oceans and rivers, and that these ships 'made everything else possible' — including the raids, trade, and exploration.

choices.text: They were skilled at farming, fishing, and raising animals
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because farming, fishing, and raising animals were things Vikings did at home, not the reason they could travel to so many places.

choices.text: They wore strong helmets made of plain metal or leather
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage mentions helmets only to bust a myth about horns — helmets had nothing to do with how Vikings traveled or explored.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Traders and Explorers

Vikings were not just raiders. They were also smart traders and bold explorers. They built trade routes stretching from Baghdad in the east to Newfoundland in the west. This was one of the largest trading networks in the medieval world — the period of history between about 500 and 1500, sometimes called the Middle Ages. Viking merchants carried furs, amber (a golden, gem-like material made from hardened tree sap), and walrus ivory. They traded these goods for silver, silk, and spices from distant empires. Viking trading posts grew into busy cities.
stimulus.content_text:
Traders and Explorers
Vikings were not just raiders. They were also smart traders and bold explorers. They built trade routes stretching from Baghdad in the east to Newfoundland in the west. This was one of the largest trading networks in the medieval world — the period of history between about 500 and 1500, sometimes called the Middle Ages. Viking merchants carried furs, amber (a golden, gem-like material made from hardened tree sap), and walrus ivory. They traded these goods for silver, silk, and spices from distant empires. Viking trading posts grew into busy cities.

items.prompt: According to the passage, what happened after Viking trading posts were set up?

choices.text: They became places for building ships
choices.feedback: The passage never mentions building ships at trading posts, so this is not supported by what you read.

choices.text: They grew into busy cities
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says 'Viking trading posts grew into busy cities,' which are the exact words from the text.

choices.text: They were attacked by raiders
choices.feedback: The passage does not say anything about trading posts being attacked by raiders.

choices.text: They stopped trading with others
choices.feedback: This is the opposite of what happened — trading posts grew and became busy cities, which means trade kept going strong!

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Settling New Lands

Over time, many Vikings stopped raiding and started settling. In England, they controlled a large region called the Danelaw, where Norse customs and laws were followed. In France, a Viking chief named Rollo received land from the French king. That area became known as Normandy, meaning "land of the Northmen." Rollo's descendants — the family members born after him, like his grandchildren and great-grandchildren — eventually spoke French. But they kept their Viking boldness. In 1066, William of Normandy, one of those descendants, conquered all of England.

Vikings also settled in Iceland, Greenland, and briefly in North America. In Iceland, settlers created one of the world's first parliaments — a group of people chosen to make laws for everyone, like a very early version of Congress. They called it the Althing, and it began in the year 930.
stimulus.content_text:
Settling New Lands
Over time, many Vikings stopped raiding and started settling. In England, they controlled a large region called the Danelaw, where Norse customs and laws were followed. In France, a Viking chief named Rollo received land from the French king. That area became known as Normandy, meaning "land of the Northmen." Rollo's descendants — the family members born after him, like his grandchildren and great-grandchildren — eventually spoke French. But they kept their Viking boldness. In 1066, William of Normandy, one of those descendants, conquered all of England.
Vikings also settled in Iceland, Greenland, and briefly in North America. In Iceland, settlers created one of the world's first parliaments — a group of people chosen to make laws for everyone, like a very early version of Congress. They called it the Althing, and it began in the year 930.

items.prompt: Based on the text, how were Rollo's descendants similar to the Vikings who came before them?

choices.text: They spoke the same language.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says Rollo's descendants eventually spoke French, not the same language as the original Vikings.

choices.text: They kept their bold spirit.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly says Rollo's descendants 'kept their Viking boldness,' showing they shared that brave, daring spirit with earlier Vikings.

choices.text: They lived in the same region.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because Rollo's descendants lived in Normandy, France, which was a new region, not the same lands as the earlier Vikings.

choices.text: They followed the same laws.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage does not say Rollo's descendants followed Viking laws — in fact, they adopted French customs and language over time.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
A Name That Tells a Story

The word "Viking" likely comes from the Old Norse word "vik," meaning a narrow bay or inlet. Old Norse was the language Vikings spoke. A Viking was someone who sailed out from the bays to seek fortune — some through raiding, others through trade.

In less than three hundred years, Vikings founded cities and shaped the cultures of many countries. They mixed their language with the languages of people they lived among. English itself contains many words borrowed from Old Norse, including "sky," "window," "egg," and "husband." So the next time you look up at the sky or crack open an egg at breakfast, you are speaking a little bit of Viking.
stimulus.content_text:
A Name That Tells a Story
The word "Viking" likely comes from the Old Norse word "vik," meaning a narrow bay or inlet. Old Norse was the language Vikings spoke. A Viking was someone who sailed out from the bays to seek fortune — some through raiding, others through trade.
In less than three hundred years, Vikings founded cities and shaped the cultures of many countries. They mixed their language with the languages of people they lived among. English itself contains many words borrowed from Old Norse, including "sky," "window," "egg," and "husband." So the next time you look up at the sky or crack open an egg at breakfast, you are speaking a little bit of Viking.

items.prompt: According to the text, what was Old Norse?

choices.text: A narrow bay or a small inlet
choices.feedback: A narrow bay or inlet is the meaning of the Old Norse word 'vik,' not what Old Norse itself is.

choices.text: The language spoken by the Vikings
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says directly, 'Old Norse was the language Vikings spoke.'

choices.text: A person who sails to seek fortune
choices.feedback: That describes a Viking person, not Old Norse — the passage says a Viking was someone who sailed out to seek fortune.

choices.text: A city founded by the early traders
choices.feedback: The passage says Vikings founded cities, but Old Norse was their language, not a city they built.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: Based on the article, how were the Vikings' actions at Lindisfarne different from their actions in Iceland?

choices.text: At Lindisfarne they built ships, but in Iceland they farmed the land.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the article never mentions Vikings building ships at Lindisfarne or farming in Iceland — those details come from different parts of the story.

choices.text: At Lindisfarne they traded goods, but in Iceland they fought in wars.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the article never says the Vikings traded at Lindisfarne or fought wars in Iceland.

choices.text: At Lindisfarne they stole gold, but in Iceland they made new laws.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the article clearly shows Vikings stealing gold at Lindisfarne during a raid, while in Iceland they created the Althing, one of the world's first law-making groups.

choices.text: At Lindisfarne they spoke French, but in Iceland they spoke Old Norse.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because it was Rollo's descendants in Normandy, France who spoke French — not the Vikings at Lindisfarne.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: If you wanted to find information that explains why Vikings could affect places far away, which two section headings would help you most?

choices.text: "Terror from the Sea" and "A Name That Tells a Story"
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because 'Terror from the Sea' only describes one raid and 'A Name That Tells a Story' explains where the word Viking comes from — neither one explains how Vikings could travel so far away.

choices.text: "Who Were the Vikings?" and "Traders and Explorers"
choices.feedback: B is correct because 'Who Were the Vikings?' explains the powerful longships that made long-distance travel possible, and 'Traders and Explorers' shows how Vikings used those ships to reach places as far apart as Baghdad and Newfoundland.

choices.text: "Settling New Lands" and "A Name That Tells a Story"
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because 'Settling New Lands' talks about where Vikings moved to, and 'A Name That Tells a Story' is about the word Viking — neither section explains the tools or skills that let them travel far.

choices.text: "Terror from the Sea" and "Settling New Lands"
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because both 'Terror from the Sea' and 'Settling New Lands' describe things Vikings did after they arrived somewhere, not what made it possible for them to reach faraway places.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: Based on the article, which sequence of events best tells the main idea of how the Vikings' actions changed over time?

choices.text: First they built fast wooden ships, and later they made metal helmets.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because building ships and making helmets are small details from the article, not the main story of how Vikings changed over time.

choices.text: First they prayed in quiet churches, and later they farmed rocky lands.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the monks praying and Vikings farming are two separate details from the article that don't show how Viking actions changed over time.

choices.text: First they raided coastal towns, and later they settled in new lands.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the article clearly shows Vikings first raided for 250 years, and then over time many of them stopped raiding and began settling in new lands.

choices.text: First they settled in new lands, and later they raided coastal towns.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because it has the order backwards — according to the article, raiding happened first, and settling in new lands came later.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: Based on the article, how was the Vikings' behavior at Lindisfarne different from their behavior as merchants?

choices.text: At Lindisfarne they traded for silver, but as merchants they took treasures by force.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because it has the two behaviors switched — it was at Lindisfarne where Vikings took things by force, not where they traded for silver.

choices.text: At Lindisfarne they took items by force, but as merchants they traded for goods.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the article shows Vikings grabbing treasures by force at Lindisfarne, while as merchants they traded goods like furs and amber for silver and spices.

choices.text: At Lindisfarne they built large new cities, but as merchants they destroyed small towns.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the article never says Vikings destroyed towns as merchants or that they built cities during the Lindisfarne raid.

choices.text: At Lindisfarne they shared all their gold, but as merchants they hid their wealth.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the article never mentions Vikings sharing all their gold or hiding their wealth at any point.

assessments.title: Rocky Shores and Town Meetings: The New England Colonies

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The New England Colonies

A Cold, Rocky Start

Picture a boy standing on a frozen shore in Massachusetts in 1635.

They had hilly, rocky ground, thick forests, harbors and bays along the coast, and long, bitter winters. The thirteen colonies that England built along the coast of North America were not all alike. The four New England colonies — Massachusetts, Connecticut, Rhode Island, and New Hampshire — sat in the cold northeast.
stimulus.content_text:
The New England ColoniesA Cold, Rocky Start
Picture a boy standing on a frozen shore in Massachusetts in 1635.

They had hilly, rocky ground, thick forests, harbors and bays along the coast, and long, bitter winters. The thirteen colonies that England built along the coast of North America were not all alike. The four New England colonies — Massachusetts, Connecticut, Rhode Island, and New Hampshire — sat in the cold northeast.

items.prompt: According to the section "A Cold, Rocky Start," how were the New England colonies different from some other colonies?

choices.text: They had warm weather all year.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage describes long, bitter winters in the New England colonies, which is the opposite of warm weather all year.

choices.text: They sat in the cold northeast.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly states the New England colonies 'sat in the cold northeast,' setting them apart from the other colonies.

choices.text: They were the only colonies on the coast.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage says all thirteen colonies were built along the coast of North America, not only the New England ones.

choices.text: They had no forests or bays.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage clearly mentions that the New England colonies had thick forests and harbors and bays along the coast.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Searching for Freedom to Worship

So why did people come here? Many came for their faith. A group called the Puritans — English people who wanted to make the Church of England simpler and stricter — faced punishment back home for their beliefs. "Purify" means to clean something out, and that is exactly what the Puritans wanted to do to their church. When the English government cracked down on them, thousands of Puritans sailed across the Atlantic to build communities where they could worship freely.

You may have heard of the Pilgrims, too. It's easy to assume Pilgrims and Puritans were the same group. They were not. The Pilgrims landed at Plymouth in 1620 and had broken away from the Church of England completely. The Puritans arrived later, in larger numbers, still hoping to fix the church from the inside.
stimulus.content_text:
Searching for Freedom to Worship
So why did people come here? Many came for their faith. A group called the Puritans — English people who wanted to make the Church of England simpler and stricter — faced punishment back home for their beliefs. "Purify" means to clean something out, and that is exactly what the Puritans wanted to do to their church. When the English government cracked down on them, thousands of Puritans sailed across the Atlantic to build communities where they could worship freely.

You may have heard of the Pilgrims, too. It's easy to assume Pilgrims and Puritans were the same group. They were not. The Pilgrims landed at Plymouth in 1620 and had broken away from the Church of England completely. The Puritans arrived later, in larger numbers, still hoping to fix the church from the inside.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, why did thousands of Puritans sail across the Atlantic Ocean?

choices.text: They wanted to break away from the Church of England completely.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because breaking away from the Church of England completely is what the Pilgrims did, not the Puritans — the Puritans wanted to fix the church from the inside.

choices.text: They wanted to build communities where they could worship freely.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly states that thousands of Puritans sailed across the Atlantic to build communities where they could worship freely.

choices.text: They wanted to punish the English government for its rules.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the Puritans were running away from the English government's punishment, not trying to punish the government.

choices.text: They wanted to join the Pilgrims who were already at Plymouth.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never mentions the Puritans wanting to join the Pilgrims, and the passage even points out they were two different groups.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Schools, Ships, and Codfish

But the rocky soil and freezing winters made growing large fields of crops almost impossible. New Englanders had to find other work. Luckily, the Atlantic Ocean was packed with cod and other fish. Fishing became a major industry. The thick forests gave colonists timber — wood cut from trees for building. They used that timber to build ships, and New England shipyards grew famous. Merchants — people who buy and sell goods — loaded those ships with fish, lumber, and other products to trade with England, the Caribbean, and Africa.

Education mattered deeply in New England as well. The Puritans believed every person should be able to read the Bible. In 1636 — just sixteen years after the Pilgrims landed — they founded Harvard College.
stimulus.content_text:
Schools, Ships, and Codfish
But the rocky soil and freezing winters made growing large fields of crops almost impossible. New Englanders had to find other work. Luckily, the Atlantic Ocean was packed with cod and other fish. Fishing became a major industry. The thick forests gave colonists timber — wood cut from trees for building. They used that timber to build ships, and New England shipyards grew famous. Merchants — people who buy and sell goods — loaded those ships with fish, lumber, and other products to trade with England, the Caribbean, and Africa.

Education mattered deeply in New England as well. The Puritans believed every person should be able to read the Bible. In 1636 — just sixteen years after the Pilgrims landed — they founded Harvard College.

items.prompt: According to the text, what happened sixteen years after the Pilgrims landed?

choices.text: They built many wooden ships.
choices.feedback: Building wooden ships is mentioned in the passage, but the text never says it happened sixteen years after the Pilgrims landed.

choices.text: They founded Harvard College.
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says 'In 1636 — just sixteen years after the Pilgrims landed — they founded Harvard College,' which matches this answer perfectly.

choices.text: They planted fields of crops.
choices.feedback: The passage actually says growing large fields of crops was almost impossible in New England, so this doesn't fit.

choices.text: They traded goods with Africa.
choices.feedback: Trading goods with Africa is mentioned in the passage, but it is never connected to the sixteen-year detail about the Pilgrims.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
A Voice for Some, but Not All

One of the most unusual things about New England was the town meeting. Imagine a big room full of people arguing, listening, and raising their hands to vote. Colonists gathered to decide how to spend the town's money, which roads to build, how much to pay the minister, and how to solve local problems. Decisions were made by majority vote — meaning whichever side got more than half the votes would win, just like picking a class game by show of hands.

This was a form of self-government — people making rules for themselves instead of a faraway king making rules for them. In England at that time, most ordinary people had zero say in government. But here is the hard truth: not everyone in New England got a voice, either. Only men who owned property could vote. Women, enslaved people, and Native Americans could not. It was a step toward fairness, but only a small one.
stimulus.content_text:
A Voice for Some, but Not All
One of the most unusual things about New England was the town meeting. Imagine a big room full of people arguing, listening, and raising their hands to vote. Colonists gathered to decide how to spend the town's money, which roads to build, how much to pay the minister, and how to solve local problems. Decisions were made by majority vote — meaning whichever side got more than half the votes would win, just like picking a class game by show of hands.

This was a form of self-government — people making rules for themselves instead of a faraway king making rules for them. In England at that time, most ordinary people had zero say in government. But here is the hard truth: not everyone in New England got a voice, either. Only men who owned property could vote. Women, enslaved people, and Native Americans could not. It was a step toward fairness, but only a small one.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, how were town meetings in New England and government in England similar?

choices.text: Both let all people vote on important decisions
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage tells us that women, enslaved people, and Native Americans were NOT allowed to vote in New England town meetings.

choices.text: Both kept some people from having a voice
choices.feedback: B is correct because both England and New England left many people out — England shut out ordinary people, and New England shut out women, enslaved people, and Native Americans.

choices.text: Both used a faraway king to make all rules
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because town meetings were actually created so people could govern themselves instead of being ruled by a faraway king.

choices.text: Both gave women and children the right to vote
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage specifically says women could not vote in New England town meetings.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Hard Ground, Big Ideas

Life in New England was not easy, and it was not always peaceful. Relations with Native American peoples, including the Wampanoag and Pequot, were often tense and sometimes turned violent.

Yet the struggles of New England left a lasting mark on history. The idea that ordinary people deserve a voice in their own government spread — into the Declaration of Independence, into the Constitution, into every voting booth you have ever seen on Election Day.
stimulus.content_text:
Hard Ground, Big Ideas
Life in New England was not easy, and it was not always peaceful. Relations with Native American peoples, including the Wampanoag and Pequot, were often tense and sometimes turned violent.

Yet the struggles of New England left a lasting mark on history. The idea that ordinary people deserve a voice in their own government spread — into the Declaration of Independence, into the Constitution, into every voting booth you have ever seen on Election Day.

items.prompt: According to the text, what does the word 'tense' mean when the author says relations with Native American peoples 'were often tense'?

choices.text: friendly and welcoming
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage says relations 'sometimes turned violent,' which is the opposite of friendly and welcoming.

choices.text: uneasy and strained
choices.feedback: B is correct because 'tense' means uneasy and strained — the passage shows things were difficult and sometimes became violent, which matches this meaning.

choices.text: boring and slow
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage is describing a serious conflict between people, not something boring or slow.

choices.text: secret and hidden
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage openly talks about these relations, so there is nothing secret or hidden about them.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: Based on the article, how was living in New England different from living in England?

choices.text: In New England, colonists worshiped freely and voted on their own local rules.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage says Puritans came to worship freely and that New Englanders used town meetings to vote on their own local rules, both of which were different from life in England.

choices.text: In New England, colonists enjoyed warm weather and planted large fields of crops.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the article says New England had long, bitter winters and rocky soil that made growing large fields of crops almost impossible.

choices.text: In New England, colonists followed the king and stayed in the Church of England.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the Puritans actually left England to escape the king's control over their church and to worship in their own way.

choices.text: In New England, colonists treated everyone fairly and gave every person a vote.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the article explains that only men who owned property could vote, and women, enslaved people, and Native Americans could not.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: How do the headings "A Cold, Rocky Start" and "Schools, Ships, and Codfish" help you locate information about a cause and its effect?

choices.text: They help you find how the freezing weather caused the colonists to build warmer schools.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage never says cold weather caused colonists to build warmer schools — schools were built so Puritans could read the Bible.

choices.text: They help you find how the bad farming land caused the colonists to work at sea.
choices.feedback: B is correct because 'A Cold, Rocky Start' shows the cause (bad land for farming) and 'Schools, Ships, and Codfish' shows the effect (colonists turned to fishing and shipbuilding instead).

choices.text: They help you find how the thick forests caused the colonists to leave the rocky coast.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the thick forests didn't make colonists leave the coast — they used the timber from those forests to build ships right there.

choices.text: They help you find how the deep ocean caused the colonists to plant new crop seeds.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the deep ocean didn't lead to planting new crops — it actually replaced farming by giving colonists fish to catch and trade.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: Which sequence of events best supports the main idea that the Puritans built a new life for themselves?

choices.text: First, they faced punishment in England; next, they sailed across the ocean; last, they held town meetings.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage clearly shows the Puritans were punished in England, then sailed to America, and then held town meetings to govern themselves — each step shows them building a new life.

choices.text: First, they landed at Plymouth; next, they left their church completely; last, they built Harvard College.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because landing at Plymouth and leaving the church completely describes the Pilgrims, not the Puritans — the passage tells us these were two different groups.

choices.text: First, they cut down thick forests; next, they traded goods with Africa; last, they grew large fields of crops.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the passage says the rocky soil made growing large fields of crops almost impossible, so that last step never happened.

choices.text: First, they made rules for the king; next, they fought with Native Americans; last, they wrote the Constitution.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the colonists made rules for themselves instead of a faraway king, and the Constitution came much later — these steps don't describe the Puritans building their new life.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: Based on the article, how were the early New England town meetings similar to the United States Constitution?

choices.text: Both allowed every single person in the towns to vote.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the article clearly says women, enslaved people, and Native Americans could NOT vote — so not everyone got a voice.

choices.text: Both shared the idea that ordinary people deserve a voice.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the article says the idea that ordinary people deserve a voice in government spread from town meetings all the way into the Constitution.

choices.text: Both were created to help merchants trade goods with England.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because town meetings were used to make local decisions like spending money and building roads, not to help merchants trade with England.

choices.text: Both were sets of rules written by the English king.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because both town meetings and the Constitution were created so people could govern themselves — the whole point was to NOT be ruled by a faraway king.

syllabus_metadata.title: Rocky Shores and Town Meetings: The New England Colonies

assessments.title: The Breadbasket Colonies: New York, Pennsylvania, and Their Neighbors

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Breadbasket Colonies

A Land Between Two Worlds

The Middle colonies included New York, New Jersey, Pennsylvania, and Delaware. They sat right between New England and the Southern colonies. New England had rocky soil and long winters. The Middle colonies had something else entirely: rich soil, rolling hills, wide rivers, and mild weather perfect for growing grain.

This special geography shaped daily life in the Middle colonies in ways that made them unlike anywhere else. The land was green and full of life, and the rivers nearby helped farmers send their crops to faraway places.
stimulus.content_text:
The Breadbasket ColoniesA Land Between Two Worlds
The Middle colonies included New York, New Jersey, Pennsylvania, and Delaware. They sat right between New England and the Southern colonies. New England had rocky soil and long winters. The Middle colonies had something else entirely: rich soil, rolling hills, wide rivers, and mild weather perfect for growing grain.

This special geography shaped daily life in the Middle colonies in ways that made them unlike anywhere else. The land was green and full of life, and the rivers nearby helped farmers send their crops to faraway places.

items.prompt: According to the text, how were the Middle colonies different from New England?

choices.text: They had rich soil and mild weather.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage directly says the Middle colonies had 'rich soil' and 'mild weather,' which made them different from New England.

choices.text: They had rocky soil and long winters.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because rocky soil and long winters describe New England, not the Middle colonies.

choices.text: They had dry dirt and short summers.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage never mentions dry dirt or short summers anywhere.

choices.text: They had tall trees and deep oceans.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage does not talk about tall trees or deep oceans at all.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
The Breadbasket of the Colonies

Farmers here grew enormous amounts of wheat, barley, and rye. They grew so much grain that people gave these colonies a nickname: the breadbasket colonies. Think of a basket overflowing with fresh loaves of bread. That is the picture the name was meant to create.

Middle colony farmers did not just feed their own families. They exported their grain — sent it far away to be sold — to other colonies and across the ocean to Europe. Ships loaded with flour and wheat left busy ports along the Hudson and Delaware Rivers. The Middle colonies fed a huge part of the colonial world.
stimulus.content_text:
The Breadbasket of the Colonies
Farmers here grew enormous amounts of wheat, barley, and rye. They grew so much grain that people gave these colonies a nickname: the breadbasket colonies. Think of a basket overflowing with fresh loaves of bread. That is the picture the name was meant to create.

Middle colony farmers did not just feed their own families. They exported their grain — sent it far away to be sold — to other colonies and across the ocean to Europe. Ships loaded with flour and wheat left busy ports along the Hudson and Delaware Rivers. The Middle colonies fed a huge part of the colonial world.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, what caused the ports along the rivers to be so busy?

choices.text: Farmers needed to buy fresh bread from far away.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the farmers were sending their grain away to be sold, not buying bread from somewhere else.

choices.text: People wanted to visit the new breadbasket colonies.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the passage never mentions people traveling to visit the breadbasket colonies.

choices.text: Farmers sent their extra grain away to be sold.
choices.feedback: C is correct because the passage says farmers exported their grain by ship from the ports along the Hudson and Delaware Rivers, which is what made those ports so busy.

choices.text: People moved to the colonies to grow more wheat.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage does not say anything about people moving to the colonies to grow more wheat.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
People from Everywhere

Here is the surprising part: the Middle colonies were home to settlers from England, the Netherlands, Germany, Sweden, and Finland. New York had originally been a Dutch colony called New Amsterdam. The English took control of it in 1664, but Dutch, Swedish, and Finnish families still lived there. German immigrants settled in Pennsylvania and became known as some of the best farmers in the colonies.

All these different groups brought their own languages, foods, and customs. This mix of people did not happen by accident. A man named William Penn made it happen on purpose.
stimulus.content_text:
People from Everywhere
Here is the surprising part: the Middle colonies were home to settlers from England, the Netherlands, Germany, Sweden, and Finland. New York had originally been a Dutch colony called New Amsterdam. The English took control of it in 1664, but Dutch, Swedish, and Finnish families still lived there. German immigrants settled in Pennsylvania and became known as some of the best farmers in the colonies.

All these different groups brought their own languages, foods, and customs. This mix of people did not happen by accident. A man named William Penn made it happen on purpose.

items.prompt: According to the text, what happened after the English took control of New York in 1664?

choices.text: All Dutch families moved back to the Netherlands
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never says Dutch families moved away — it actually says they still lived in New York.

choices.text: Dutch, Swedish, and Finnish families still lived there
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly states that Dutch, Swedish, and Finnish families still lived in New York after the English took control in 1664.

choices.text: William Penn started a new colony called New Amsterdam
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because William Penn is connected to Pennsylvania, not New Amsterdam, and New Amsterdam was already the old name for New York before the English arrived.

choices.text: German immigrants took over the farms in New York
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says German immigrants settled in Pennsylvania, not in New York.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
William Penn's "Holy Experiment"

Penn was a Quaker. Quakers were a Christian group that believed in peace, equality, and simple worship. In England, Quakers had been persecuted — punished and treated cruelly — just for practicing their faith. Imagine being thrown in jail simply for the way you pray. That is what happened to Quakers in England, including Penn himself.

So Penn founded Pennsylvania as a place of tolerance, meaning a place where people accepted others even when they were different. He called it a "holy experiment." Penn welcomed Quakers, Mennonites, Amish, Catholics, Jews, and many others who had been mistreated because of their beliefs. People who had been turned away in other places found a haven — a safe shelter — in Pennsylvania.

But tolerance did not mean everything was perfect. Some groups still faced unfair treatment, and arguments between different groups did happen. Penn's experiment was real, but it was messy and incomplete.
stimulus.content_text:
William Penn's "Holy Experiment"
Penn was a Quaker. Quakers were a Christian group that believed in peace, equality, and simple worship. In England, Quakers had been persecuted — punished and treated cruelly — just for practicing their faith. Imagine being thrown in jail simply for the way you pray. That is what happened to Quakers in England, including Penn himself.

So Penn founded Pennsylvania as a place of tolerance, meaning a place where people accepted others even when they were different. He called it a "holy experiment." Penn welcomed Quakers, Mennonites, Amish, Catholics, Jews, and many others who had been mistreated because of their beliefs. People who had been turned away in other places found a haven — a safe shelter — in Pennsylvania.

But tolerance did not mean everything was perfect. Some groups still faced unfair treatment, and arguments between different groups did happen. Penn's experiment was real, but it was messy and incomplete.

items.prompt: Based on the text, how were the Quakers, Mennonites, and Amish similar?

choices.text: They all helped to start the new colony.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage only says Penn founded Pennsylvania — it doesn't say the Quakers, Mennonites, and Amish all helped start the colony.

choices.text: They all faced mistreatment for their beliefs.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage clearly says Penn welcomed these groups because they had all 'been mistreated because of their beliefs.'

choices.text: They all experienced perfect peace in the end.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage actually says tolerance didn't mean everything was perfect, and that unfair treatment still happened.

choices.text: They all punished people who prayed differently.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because it was the Quakers (and others) who were being punished, not doing the punishing — they were the ones treated cruelly for how they prayed.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Trade, Rivers, and Busy Cities

The Middle colonies were also centers of trade and manufacturing — the making of goods by hand or with tools. Rivers like the Hudson and the Delaware made it easy to move heavy loads. Merchants shipped grain, flour, and lumber from the ports. In the cities, craftsmen built iron tools, furniture, and barrels. Philadelphia grew to become one of the largest and most important cities in all the colonies.

A child walking through Philadelphia might hear three languages on a single street and smell German rye bread baking in one kitchen and Dutch spice cake in the next.
stimulus.content_text:
Trade, Rivers, and Busy Cities
The Middle colonies were also centers of trade and manufacturing — the making of goods by hand or with tools. Rivers like the Hudson and the Delaware made it easy to move heavy loads. Merchants shipped grain, flour, and lumber from the ports. In the cities, craftsmen built iron tools, furniture, and barrels. Philadelphia grew to become one of the largest and most important cities in all the colonies.

A child walking through Philadelphia might hear three languages on a single street and smell German rye bread baking in one kitchen and Dutch spice cake in the next.

items.prompt: In the section 'Trade, Rivers, and Busy Cities,' what does the word manufacturing mean?

choices.text: the shipping of goods to faraway ports
choices.feedback: Shipping goods to ports is what merchants did, not what manufacturing means.

choices.text: the making of goods by hand or tools
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly defines manufacturing as 'the making of goods by hand or with tools.'

choices.text: the growing of crops on large farms
choices.feedback: Growing crops on farms is farming, not manufacturing, and the passage does not connect those two ideas.

choices.text: the selling of goods at a busy market
choices.feedback: Selling goods at a market is trading, not manufacturing, and the passage treats these as different activities.

items.title: Section 6 - Guiding Question 5

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
A Different Kind of Strength

People who were different from each other lived side by side, grew food together, and traded with each other. It was not always easy, but it worked — and the idea that different kinds of people can build something together did not stay in the 1700s.
stimulus.content_text:
A Different Kind of Strength
People who were different from each other lived side by side, grew food together, and traded with each other. It was not always easy, but it worked — and the idea that different kinds of people can build something together did not stay in the 1700s.

items.prompt: Which detail from the text provides evidence that different groups of people worked together?

choices.text: They found it very easy to live side by side.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says 'it was not always easy,' so living side by side was actually hard sometimes.

choices.text: They grew food and traded with one another.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly says people 'grew food together and traded with each other,' which shows real teamwork between different groups.

choices.text: They stayed in the 1700s to build new towns.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says the idea did NOT stay in the 1700s, meaning it kept going beyond that time period.

choices.text: They built large ships to travel the ocean.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never mentions ships or the ocean anywhere in the text.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: According to the article, how were farmers different from craftsmen in the Middle colonies?

choices.text: Farmers grew huge amounts of grain, while craftsmen built tools and barrels.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the article clearly says farmers grew huge amounts of grain, and craftsmen in the cities made things like iron tools and barrels.

choices.text: Farmers shipped heavy lumber on the rivers, while craftsmen grew fresh food.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because it switches the jobs around — merchants shipped lumber on the rivers, and farmers grew food, not the other way around.

choices.text: Farmers built strong iron tools, while craftsmen fed the whole colonial world.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because it has the jobs completely backwards — it was the farmers who fed the colonial world, and the craftsmen who built iron tools.

choices.text: Farmers worked in the busy port cities, while craftsmen worked on rolling hills.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the article says farmers worked on the land outside the cities, while craftsmen worked inside the busy port cities like Philadelphia.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: How does the section 'William Penn's Holy Experiment' help explain why the section 'People from Everywhere' describes so many different groups living in the Middle colonies?

choices.text: Penn forced people from other countries to move there
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because Penn invited and welcomed people freely — the passage never says he forced anyone to move there.

choices.text: Penn created a place that welcomed different people
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage clearly says Penn created Pennsylvania as a place of tolerance that welcomed people of many different beliefs who had been mistreated elsewhere.

choices.text: Penn grew more grain than any other colony leader
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage never says Penn was a great grain farmer — his important work was about welcoming different people, not growing crops.

choices.text: Penn built rivers so that ships could bring settlers
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because rivers already existed naturally in the Middle colonies — Penn did not build them, and rivers were not the reason so many different groups came to live there.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: Which sequence of key details best explains the main idea of the article?

choices.text: First, the rich soil helped farmers grow grain; next, different people arrived; last, they built busy trading cities.
choices.feedback: A is correct because it follows the article's main ideas in the right order — rich soil led to farming, different people arrived, and together they built busy trading cities.

choices.text: First, they built busy trading cities; next, the rich soil helped farmers grow grain; last, many different people arrived.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the cities came last, not first — farming and the arrival of many different people happened before the cities grew big.

choices.text: First, New England had rocky soil; next, William Penn was put in jail; last, craftsmen made heavy wooden barrels.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because those are small details from different parts of the article, not the key ideas that explain what the Middle colonies were all about.

choices.text: First, the English took over New York; next, the Dutch moved away; last, the Quakers stopped all of their trading.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the article says Dutch families stayed in New York even after the English took over, and nothing in the article says the Quakers stopped trading.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: How does the author show that farmers and merchants were similar in the Middle colonies?

choices.text: By explaining that both groups used the rivers to send their goods away.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage shows both farmers and merchants used the Hudson and Delaware Rivers to send their grain and goods far away.

choices.text: By describing how both groups built iron tools and wooden barrels for towns.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the passage says craftsmen — not farmers or merchants — were the ones building iron tools and barrels.

choices.text: By showing that both groups traveled from England to find a safe new home.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the passage never says both farmers and merchants came from England looking for a safe home.

choices.text: By stating that both groups worked hard to make the rocky soil grow crops.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the Middle colonies had rich soil, not rocky soil — the passage says rocky soil was a problem in New England, not here.

assessments.title: Plantations and Cash Crops: The Southern Colonies

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Southern Colonies: Warm Soil, Hard Labor

A Struggling Settlement

In 1607, a group of English settlers stepped off their ships onto the muddy banks of a river in Virginia. They called their tiny settlement Jamestown. Within months, many of them were dying. Disease spread fast. Food ran out. The Powhatan people — Native Americans who had built towns and farmed this land for generations — watched these newcomers struggle to survive. Conflicts broke out between the two groups. The colony nearly disappeared more than once.

Then the settlers discovered something. Tobacco grew beautifully in Virginia's warm, damp soil. They could ship it to England and sell it for high prices. That single plant saved Jamestown and changed the South forever.
stimulus.content_text:
The Southern Colonies: Warm Soil, Hard LaborA Struggling Settlement
In 1607, a group of English settlers stepped off their ships onto the muddy banks of a river in Virginia. They called their tiny settlement Jamestown. Within months, many of them were dying. Disease spread fast. Food ran out. The Powhatan people — Native Americans who had built towns and farmed this land for generations — watched these newcomers struggle to survive. Conflicts broke out between the two groups. The colony nearly disappeared more than once.
Then the settlers discovered something. Tobacco grew beautifully in Virginia's warm, damp soil. They could ship it to England and sell it for high prices. That single plant saved Jamestown and changed the South forever.

items.prompt: According to the text, what had the Powhatan people already been doing on the land that the English settlers had not?

choices.text: shipping tobacco to England
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage says the English settlers were the ones who shipped tobacco to England — the Powhatan people are never described doing this.

choices.text: building towns and farming
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly says the Powhatan people 'had built towns and farmed this land for generations,' which the English settlers had not done.

choices.text: struggling to find food
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because struggling to find food describes the English settlers, not the Powhatan people.

choices.text: sailing across the ocean
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because sailing across the ocean is what the English settlers did to arrive in Virginia — the Powhatan people already lived there.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Five Colonies, One Warm Climate

Jamestown was just the beginning. Over time, five Southern colonies grew along the Atlantic coast: Virginia, Maryland, North Carolina, South Carolina, and Georgia. Each colony had its own reason for being founded. Georgia, the last of the thirteen colonies, was started in 1733 — more than a hundred years after Jamestown.

While colonists in New England built ships and caught fish in cold waters, Southern colonists farmed. The warm sun, long summers, and rich soil made this region perfect for growing crops.
stimulus.content_text:
Five Colonies, One Warm Climate
Jamestown was just the beginning. Over time, five Southern colonies grew along the Atlantic coast: Virginia, Maryland, North Carolina, South Carolina, and Georgia. Each colony had its own reason for being founded. Georgia, the last of the thirteen colonies, was started in 1733 — more than a hundred years after Jamestown.
While colonists in New England built ships and caught fish in cold waters, Southern colonists farmed. The warm sun, long summers, and rich soil made this region perfect for growing crops.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, what is the main reason the section describes the warm sun, long summers, and rich soil of the Southern colonies?

choices.text: To explain why Southern colonists focused on farming instead of fishing
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage directly connects the warm sun, long summers, and rich soil to why Southern colonists farmed instead of doing things like fishing.

choices.text: To show why each colony was founded at a different time
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the passage talks about founding dates separately and never connects the warm climate to when colonies were started.

choices.text: To prove that the Southern colonies were larger than New England
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the passage never mentions or compares the sizes of the Southern colonies and New England.

choices.text: To describe why colonists preferred warm weather over cold weather
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the passage is explaining why the land was great for farming, not saying that colonists simply liked warm weather better.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Cash Crops and Big Farms

Southern farmers grew cash crops — crops raised not to feed their own families, but to sell for money. Tobacco was the biggest cash crop in Virginia and Maryland. Rice grew well in the wet, swampy ground of South Carolina. Indigo, a plant used to make bright blue dye for clothing, brought in good money too. Ships carried all of these products across the ocean to England.

Growing cash crops on a large scale took huge amounts of land. Wealthy colonists set up plantations — enormous farms that could stretch across thousands of acres, bigger than many towns. You might think most Southerners lived on plantations like these. Actually, most white Southern colonists were small farmers who worked their own fields with their families. But plantation owners held the most wealth and the most power in the South.
stimulus.content_text:
Cash Crops and Big Farms
Southern farmers grew cash crops — crops raised not to feed their own families, but to sell for money. Tobacco was the biggest cash crop in Virginia and Maryland. Rice grew well in the wet, swampy ground of South Carolina. Indigo, a plant used to make bright blue dye for clothing, brought in good money too. Ships carried all of these products across the ocean to England.
Growing cash crops on a large scale took huge amounts of land. Wealthy colonists set up plantations — enormous farms that could stretch across thousands of acres, bigger than many towns. You might think most Southerners lived on plantations like these. Actually, most white Southern colonists were small farmers who worked their own fields with their families. But plantation owners held the most wealth and the most power in the South.

items.prompt: According to the section "Cash Crops and Big Farms," what happened to the cash crops after the farmers grew them?

choices.text: Ships carried them across the ocean to England.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage directly states 'Ships carried all of these products across the ocean to England.'

choices.text: Farmers served them at home to their families.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the passage says cash crops were raised to sell for money, not to feed the farmers' own families.

choices.text: Colonists traded them for land in small towns.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the passage never mentions trading crops for land in small towns.

choices.text: Workers turned them into bright blue clothing.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because while indigo was used to make blue dye for clothing, the passage does not say the crops were turned into clothing — they were shipped to England.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
The Cruelty of Slavery

Running a plantation took many, many workers. And here the story turns dark. Starting in the early 1600s, plantation owners forced enslaved Africans to do this work. These people did not choose to come to the colonies. They were captured, torn from their families, and carried across the Atlantic Ocean on ships in horrible conditions. They were treated as property — bought and sold like tools or animals — not as human beings.

Enslaved people worked in the fields from sunrise to sunset, planting, weeding, and harvesting the crops that made their owners rich. But even under this brutal system, enslaved people found ways to keep their cultures alive. They told stories, sang songs, and held onto traditions from their homelands.
stimulus.content_text:
The Cruelty of Slavery
Running a plantation took many, many workers. And here the story turns dark. Starting in the early 1600s, plantation owners forced enslaved Africans to do this work. These people did not choose to come to the colonies. They were captured, torn from their families, and carried across the Atlantic Ocean on ships in horrible conditions. They were treated as property — bought and sold like tools or animals — not as human beings.
Enslaved people worked in the fields from sunrise to sunset, planting, weeding, and harvesting the crops that made their owners rich. But even under this brutal system, enslaved people found ways to keep their cultures alive. They told stories, sang songs, and held onto traditions from their homelands.

items.prompt: The author helps readers understand the word "property" by comparing enslaved people to tools and animals. Based on this comparison, what do property, tools, and animals have in common?

choices.text: They are things that owners buy and sell.
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says enslaved people were 'bought and sold like tools or animals,' which tells us that property, tools, and animals are all things that can be owned and traded by people.

choices.text: They are things that grow in the fields.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because tools and animals don't grow in fields — crops like tobacco and cotton grow in fields.

choices.text: They are things that travel on large ships.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because tools and animals don't travel on large ships — it was the enslaved people who were forced onto ships.

choices.text: They are things that tell many stories.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because tools and animals cannot tell stories — it was the enslaved people who kept their cultures alive by telling stories.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
A Different Way of Life

Life in the Southern colonies looked nothing like life in the North. Southern towns sat far apart. Families lived spread out across the countryside instead of clustered in villages. Nearly everything depended on farming.

The plantation system created enormous wealth for a small number of people. But that wealth was built on stolen labor and stolen lives. The pain of slavery did not end when the colonies became a nation. It echoed through centuries — through a civil war, through long struggles for justice, through conversations your own family might have today. The Southern colonies gave America tobacco, rice, and Jamestown. They also gave it a debt of fairness that is still being paid.
stimulus.content_text:
A Different Way of Life
Life in the Southern colonies looked nothing like life in the North. Southern towns sat far apart. Families lived spread out across the countryside instead of clustered in villages. Nearly everything depended on farming.
The plantation system created enormous wealth for a small number of people. But that wealth was built on stolen labor and stolen lives. The pain of slavery did not end when the colonies became a nation. It echoed through centuries — through a civil war, through long struggles for justice, through conversations your own family might have today. The Southern colonies gave America tobacco, rice, and Jamestown. They also gave it a debt of fairness that is still being paid.

items.prompt: According to the text, which detail describes the way families lived in the Southern colonies?

choices.text: Families lived spread out across the countryside.
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage uses almost the exact same words, saying families 'lived spread out across the countryside instead of clustered in villages.'

choices.text: Families lived clustered together inside villages.
choices.feedback: This is the opposite of what the passage says — families were spread out, not clustered together in villages.

choices.text: Families lived in towns that sat close to each other.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because the passage says Southern towns 'sat far apart,' meaning they were not close to each other.

choices.text: Families lived in places that looked like the North.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because the passage starts by saying Southern life 'looked nothing like life in the North.'

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: Based on the article, how was life in the Southern colonies different from life in the North?

choices.text: Southerners lived in close villages, while Northerners farmed on huge plantations.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because it has things backwards — Southerners lived spread out on farms, and it was Northerners who lived in closer villages.

choices.text: Southerners farmed spread-out land, while Northerners lived in villages and fished.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the article says Southerners farmed on spread-out land across the countryside, while Northerners built ships, caught fish, and lived in villages.

choices.text: Southerners built ships in cold waters, while Northerners grew cash crops for money.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because it has things mixed up — it was Northerners who built ships in cold waters, while Southerners were the ones farming and growing crops.

choices.text: Southerners grew food just for their families, while Northerners grew crops for money.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the article clearly says Southern farmers grew cash crops to sell for money, not just to feed their own families.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: Which two headings help you find the effects (the results) of the large farming system in the Southern colonies?

choices.text: "A Struggling Settlement" and "Five Colonies, One Warm Climate"
choices.feedback: Both of these headings are about the early beginnings of the colonies, not the results of the large farming system.

choices.text: "Five Colonies, One Warm Climate" and "Cash Crops and Big Farms"
choices.feedback: These headings explain what the colonies were like and what crops were grown, but they don't focus on the effects or results of the big farming system.

choices.text: "A Struggling Settlement" and "Cash Crops and Big Farms"
choices.feedback: 'A Struggling Settlement' is about the early days of Jamestown, which happened before the large farming system even got going.

choices.text: "The Cruelty of Slavery" and "A Different Way of Life"
choices.feedback: This is correct because 'The Cruelty of Slavery' shows how enslaved people were forced to work as a result of the plantation system, and 'A Different Way of Life' shows how the farming system changed how people lived — both are effects of large-scale farming.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: Which order of events best tells the main story of the Southern colonies?

choices.text: Settlers struggled to survive, grew cash crops for money, and then used enslaved labor.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage shows settlers struggling first at Jamestown, then growing cash crops like tobacco to sell, and then using enslaved labor to run the big plantations.

choices.text: Settlers built enormous plantations, grew cash crops for money, and then struggled to survive.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because settlers were struggling to survive first — they didn't start by building enormous plantations.

choices.text: Settlers arrived in Virginia, moved to the colony of Georgia, and then caught fish.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because catching fish was what Northern colonists did, and the passage never says Southern settlers moved from Virginia to Georgia in that way.

choices.text: Settlers learned to farm, built small villages close together, and then stopped planting tobacco.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because Southern colonists kept growing tobacco and lived spread far apart across the countryside, not in villages close together.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: Which detail best shows a way Jamestown and the later Southern colonies were alike?

choices.text: Both depended on cold ocean fishing to earn money.
choices.feedback: A is wrong because the passage says cold ocean fishing was done by colonists in New England, not in the Southern colonies.

choices.text: Both grew crops in warm soil to make money.
choices.feedback: B is correct because both Jamestown and the later Southern colonies grew crops in warm soil to sell for money, which the passage clearly describes.

choices.text: Both were founded at the same time by the same people.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the Southern colonies were founded at very different times by different groups of people — Georgia alone came more than 100 years after Jamestown.

choices.text: Both had most families living together in large towns.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the passage says Southern families lived spread out across the countryside, not together in large towns.

assessments.title: Governing Ourselves: Colonial Assemblies and Charters

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Governing Themselves: How the Colonists Learned to Lead

A Ship Full of Decisions
