In November 1620, forty-one men crowded into the cabin of a wooden ship called the Mayflower. They had not yet stepped onto land. But before anyone left the ship, they picked up a pen. They signed a short agreement promising to make laws together and follow those laws. This document was the Mayflower Compact — one of the first written plans for self-government in America. Its big idea was simple: the people themselves would decide how to be governed. It spread across thirteen colonies over the next 150 years.
stimulus.content_text:
Governing Themselves: How the Colonists Learned to LeadA Ship Full of Decisions
In November 1620, forty-one men crowded into the cabin of a wooden ship called the Mayflower. They had not yet stepped onto land. But before anyone left the ship, they picked up a pen. They signed a short agreement promising to make laws together and follow those laws. This document was the Mayflower Compact — one of the first written plans for self-government in America. Its big idea was simple: the people themselves would decide how to be governed. It spread across thirteen colonies over the next 150 years.

items.prompt: According to the text, how was the Mayflower Compact different from having a king or ruler tell people what to do?

choices.text: A single leader wrote all the laws alone
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage shows the men worked together by all signing the agreement, not one person writing laws alone.

choices.text: The people themselves decided how to be governed
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly says 'the people themselves would decide how to be governed,' which is the opposite of a king making all the decisions.

choices.text: The ship's captain made the rules for everyone
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage never mentions the ship's captain making any rules for the group.

choices.text: Leaders from England created the agreement
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the agreement was created by the people on the Mayflower, not by leaders back in England.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Why Colonists Had to Lead Themselves

England was more than three thousand miles away. A letter sent across the Atlantic Ocean could take weeks or even months to arrive. The king and Parliament — England's group of lawmakers, like a faraway Congress — could not manage every detail of daily life in the colonies. So the colonists built their own systems of government, right from the start. Actually, they had been practicing self-government for a very long time.
stimulus.content_text:
Why Colonists Had to Lead Themselves
England was more than three thousand miles away. A letter sent across the Atlantic Ocean could take weeks or even months to arrive. The king and Parliament — England's group of lawmakers, like a faraway Congress — could not manage every detail of daily life in the colonies. So the colonists built their own systems of government, right from the start. Actually, they had been practicing self-government for a very long time.

items.prompt: In the section "Why Colonists Had to Lead Themselves," which detail explains why the colonists built their own government?

choices.text: The faraway king could not manage their daily lives.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage directly says the king and Parliament could not manage every detail of daily life in the colonies since England was so far away.

choices.text: The lawmakers sent a faraway Congress to govern them.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because Parliament stayed in England — it was not sent to the colonies as a Congress.

choices.text: The colonists wanted to start a brand new country.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage never mentions the colonists wanting to start a new country.

choices.text: The king sent letters to help them practice leading.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage says slow letters made it hard for the king to help lead, not that he sent letters to teach them.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Governors and Assemblies

Each colony had a governor, the most powerful leader in the colony. In royal colonies like Virginia, the king chose the governor. In other colonies, the colonists picked their own. The governor could approve or reject laws. He could call meetings and command the colony's militia — a group of ordinary citizens who served as soldiers when needed.

But the governor did not rule alone. Every colony had a colonial assembly, a group of elected people who helped make laws and decided how tax money was spent. Think of it like a school council, where students vote for classmates to speak for them. However, not everyone could vote. In most colonies, only white men who owned land had that right. Women, enslaved people, and Native Americans were all left out. Self-government was real, but it did not include everyone.

It's easy to assume all thirteen colonies worked the same way, but they did not. Each colony had its own rules and its own style of government.
stimulus.content_text:
Governors and Assemblies
Each colony had a governor, the most powerful leader in the colony. In royal colonies like Virginia, the king chose the governor. In other colonies, the colonists picked their own. The governor could approve or reject laws. He could call meetings and command the colony's militia — a group of ordinary citizens who served as soldiers when needed.
But the governor did not rule alone. Every colony had a colonial assembly, a group of elected people who helped make laws and decided how tax money was spent. Think of it like a school council, where students vote for classmates to speak for them. However, not everyone could vote. In most colonies, only white men who owned land had that right. Women, enslaved people, and Native Americans were all left out. Self-government was real, but it did not include everyone.
It's easy to assume all thirteen colonies worked the same way, but they did not. Each colony had its own rules and its own style of government.

items.prompt: According to the text, what happens in a school council after students vote for their classmates?

choices.text: The classmates speak for them.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage directly says students vote for classmates 'to speak for them' in a school council.

choices.text: The classmates pick the leader.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the passage never mentions classmates picking a leader as part of the school council.

choices.text: The classmates spend the money.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because deciding how to spend money is what the colonial assembly does, not what the passage says about the school council.

choices.text: The classmates serve as soldiers.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because serving as soldiers describes the militia, which has nothing to do with the school council comparison.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
The Power of the Purse

Governors and assemblies often disagreed. Governors wanted to follow the king's orders. Assemblies wanted to protect the colonists. Over time, the assemblies discovered their strongest tool: money. Since the assembly controlled taxes and spending, the governor needed their help to pay for anything. This was called the "power of the purse." Imagine holding the only key to the piggy bank — that gives you a lot of say in what happens next.
stimulus.content_text:
The Power of the Purse
Governors and assemblies often disagreed. Governors wanted to follow the king's orders. Assemblies wanted to protect the colonists. Over time, the assemblies discovered their strongest tool: money. Since the assembly controlled taxes and spending, the governor needed their help to pay for anything. This was called the "power of the purse." Imagine holding the only key to the piggy bank — that gives you a lot of say in what happens next.

items.prompt: The author compares the "power of the purse" to holding the key to a piggy bank. Based on this comparison, what does the phrase mean?

choices.text: Having control of the money
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage says the assembly controlled taxes and spending, and the piggy bank key comparison shows that controlling money gives you power over what happens.

choices.text: Having a safe place for coins
choices.feedback: A piggy bank can store coins, but the passage isn't talking about keeping money safe — it's talking about who gets to be in charge of the money.

choices.text: Having the right to make laws
choices.feedback: Making laws is a different kind of power; the passage specifically talks about controlling taxes and spending, not writing laws.

choices.text: Having to obey the governor
choices.feedback: It's actually the opposite — the assembly had power over the governor because he needed their help with money, so the governor had to listen to them!

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Charters and Town Meetings

Each colony also had a charter — an official paper from the king that gave the colony permission to exist and listed rules for its government. A charter was like a permission slip that also spelled out your rights. Some charters gave colonists great freedom. Others kept tight royal control. Colonists treasured their charters because those papers protected them.

In New England, many towns held town meetings where ordinary people gathered to discuss local problems and vote on decisions together. Town meetings gave everyday colonists a direct voice in their communities.
stimulus.content_text:
Charters and Town Meetings
Each colony also had a charter — an official paper from the king that gave the colony permission to exist and listed rules for its government. A charter was like a permission slip that also spelled out your rights. Some charters gave colonists great freedom. Others kept tight royal control. Colonists treasured their charters because those papers protected them.
In New England, many towns held town meetings where ordinary people gathered to discuss local problems and vote on decisions together. Town meetings gave everyday colonists a direct voice in their communities.

items.prompt: According to the text, what is a charter?

choices.text: a meeting where people voted on local problems
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage says town meetings are where people voted on local problems — that's a different thing from a charter.

choices.text: an official paper that gave a colony permission to exist
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly says a charter is 'an official paper from the king that gave the colony permission to exist.'

choices.text: a list of towns that were allowed to hold meetings
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage never mentions any list of towns allowed to hold meetings — that detail doesn't exist in the text.

choices.text: a letter that colonists wrote to ask the king for help
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because a charter was a paper the king gave to the colonists, not a letter the colonists wrote to the king.

items.title: Section 6 - Guiding Question 5

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
Seeds That Grew

So when the British government later tried to take away those rights, the colonists refused to give them up. They had been running their own lives for over 150 years.

The next time you raise your hand to vote on something in class, you are using the same idea those forty-one men signed onto aboard a rocking ship — the idea that people have a right to govern themselves.
stimulus.content_text:
Seeds That Grew
So when the British government later tried to take away those rights, the colonists refused to give them up. They had been running their own lives for over 150 years.
The next time you raise your hand to vote on something in class, you are using the same idea those forty-one men signed onto aboard a rocking ship — the idea that people have a right to govern themselves.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, which detail best supports the idea that the colonists refused to give up their rights?

choices.text: They had just signed a document on a ship.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because signing a document on a ship is something that happened earlier and the passage doesn't connect it to why the colonists refused to give up their rights.

choices.text: They had been governing themselves for over 150 years.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly states the colonists had been governing themselves for over 150 years, which is the reason they refused to give up their rights.

choices.text: They wanted the British government to help them.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage says the colonists refused the British government's control — they did not want help from them.

choices.text: They had never made any decisions on their own.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because it is the opposite of what the passage says — the colonists had been making their own decisions for a very long time.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: Based on the text, what is one difference between the Mayflower Compact and a colony's charter?

choices.text: The Compact was an agreement made by people, but a charter was a paper from the king.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage clearly says the Mayflower Compact was signed by the colonists themselves, while a charter was an official paper that came from the king.

choices.text: The Compact was a group of chosen lawmakers, but a charter was a list of new colony taxes.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because a charter was not a list of taxes — that describes something closer to what the colonial assembly handled, not a charter.

choices.text: The Compact was a way to control the money, but a charter was a way to lead the soldiers.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the Mayflower Compact was not about controlling money — the 'power of the purse' belonged to the colonial assemblies, which is a completely different thing.

choices.text: The Compact was a letter sent to England, but a charter was a rule signed on a big ship.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the Mayflower Compact was not a letter sent to England — it was signed on the ship before the colonists even stepped onto land, and it was an agreement among themselves.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: Why were the colonists able to resist when Britain later tried to take away their rights?

choices.text: They had already practiced self-government for many years.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the colonists had practiced self-government for over 150 years, so they were ready and willing to fight to keep those rights.

choices.text: They had already sailed across the ocean on the Mayflower.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because sailing on the Mayflower was just the very start of colonial history, not the reason they could stand up to Britain later.

choices.text: They had already let every person vote in the colonies.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the passage clearly says that most colonists could NOT vote — only white men who owned land had that right.

choices.text: They had already given governors all the power.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because governors did not have all the power — assemblies controlled the money and shared in making laws.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: Which heading helps the reader find information about the first written plan for self-government?

choices.text: "A Ship Full of Decisions"
choices.feedback: A is correct because the section under 'A Ship Full of Decisions' describes the Mayflower Compact, which the passage calls one of the first written plans for self-government in America.

choices.text: "Governors and Assemblies"
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because 'Governors and Assemblies' is about the different leaders who helped run each colony, not about the first written plan for self-government.

choices.text: "The Power of the Purse"
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because 'The Power of the Purse' is about how colonial assemblies used control over money to gain influence, not about the first written plan for self-government.

choices.text: "Charters and Town Meetings"
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because 'Charters and Town Meetings' is about official permission papers from the king and local community meetings, not about the first written plan for self-government.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: The main idea of the text is that colonists learned to govern themselves. How are the Mayflower Compact and town meetings alike in showing this idea?

choices.text: Both show colonists following the king's orders.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because both the Mayflower Compact and town meetings were about colonists making their own decisions, not following orders from the king.

choices.text: Both show colonists making choices together.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the Mayflower Compact had colonists signing an agreement together, and town meetings had colonists voting on decisions together — both show people governing themselves as a group.

choices.text: Both show colonists picking a governor to lead.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because neither the Mayflower Compact nor town meetings were about choosing a governor — that was a separate part of how colonies were run.

choices.text: Both show colonists using money to get their way.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because using money to get their way describes the 'power of the purse' that assemblies used, which is a different idea from the passage.

syllabus_metadata.title: Governing Ourselves: Colonial Assemblies and Charters

assessments.title: The Town Meeting: Democracy Begins in America

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Town Meetings: How New England Colonists Governed Themselves

The Bell Rings

An iron bell clangs across a small New England town on a cold fall morning. A farmer sets down his hoe. A shopkeeper locks his door. A minister closes his book. They all walk toward the same wooden building in the center of town. Inside, rows of benches fill up fast. Today, these men will make the rules for their own community. Today is town meeting day.
stimulus.content_text:
Town Meetings: How New England Colonists Governed ThemselvesThe Bell Rings
An iron bell clangs across a small New England town on a cold fall morning. A farmer sets down his hoe. A shopkeeper locks his door. A minister closes his book. They all walk toward the same wooden building in the center of town. Inside, rows of benches fill up fast. Today, these men will make the rules for their own community. Today is town meeting day.

items.prompt: According to the text, even though the men in the town have different jobs, what do they all do on the morning of the town meeting?

choices.text: They walk to the same wooden building.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage clearly says the farmer, shopkeeper, and minister all walk toward the same wooden building, which is the one thing they all do together.

choices.text: They ring the large iron bell together.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the bell rings on its own to call the men — no one in the story rings it together.

choices.text: They work together on the town's farm.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage never mentions the men working together on a farm.

choices.text: They read from the new minister's book.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the minister closes his book and walks away — he does not read from it with the others.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
A Different Kind of Government

In England, powerful lords and members of Parliament — the group of leaders who made laws for the country — decided the rules. Ordinary people had almost no say. But in colonial New England, something different happened. Male property owners could gather in one room and vote on laws, taxes, and community plans themselves.

This kind of government is called direct democracy. Direct democracy means that citizens vote on issues themselves instead of picking someone else to vote for them. Imagine your class votes on what game to play at recess — that is direct democracy. In a town meeting, every person who was allowed to attend got to speak and got one equal vote.

A farmer could stand up and argue against a new tax. A shopkeeper could suggest building a road. A minister could ask the town to raise money for a school. Each voice counted the same.
stimulus.content_text:
A Different Kind of Government
In England, powerful lords and members of Parliament — the group of leaders who made laws for the country — decided the rules. Ordinary people had almost no say. But in colonial New England, something different happened. Male property owners could gather in one room and vote on laws, taxes, and community plans themselves.

This kind of government is called direct democracy. Direct democracy means that citizens vote on issues themselves instead of picking someone else to vote for them. Imagine your class votes on what game to play at recess — that is direct democracy. In a town meeting, every person who was allowed to attend got to speak and got one equal vote.

A farmer could stand up and argue against a new tax. A shopkeeper could suggest building a road. A minister could ask the town to raise money for a school. Each voice counted the same.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, why was a farmer able to stand up and argue against a new tax at a colonial town meeting?

choices.text: Powerful lords in England made the new rules.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because powerful lords making rules was how things worked in England, not at colonial New England town meetings.

choices.text: Every person allowed to attend got to speak.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly says every person allowed to attend got to speak and had one equal vote, which is why the farmer could stand up.

choices.text: The town needed to raise money for a school.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because raising money for a school was just an example of what a minister suggested, not the reason everyone could speak.

choices.text: Citizens picked new leaders to vote for them.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because direct democracy means citizens vote on issues themselves — they do NOT pick someone else to vote for them.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
What They Decided

Town meetings covered everything — big and small. Citizens debated how much to pay the schoolteacher. They argued about when to fix the bridge. They figured out what to do about stray animals wandering through gardens. They even voted on whether to build a new fence around the cemetery. They set tax rates and chose how to spend the money. They settled disputes — disagreements between neighbors, like two people arguing over where a property line sits.

Between meetings, the town still needed someone to handle daily business. So colonists elected selectmen, trusted community members who carried out the decisions the town meeting had made. Selectmen were like team captains. The whole team decided the plan, but the captains made sure it actually happened. They kept roads in good shape, collected taxes, and enforced the town's rules until the next meeting.
stimulus.content_text:
What They Decided
Town meetings covered everything — big and small. Citizens debated how much to pay the schoolteacher. They argued about when to fix the bridge. They figured out what to do about stray animals wandering through gardens. They even voted on whether to build a new fence around the cemetery. They set tax rates and chose how to spend the money. They settled disputes — disagreements between neighbors, like two people arguing over where a property line sits.

Between meetings, the town still needed someone to handle daily business. So colonists elected selectmen, trusted community members who carried out the decisions the town meeting had made. Selectmen were like team captains. The whole team decided the plan, but the captains made sure it actually happened. They kept roads in good shape, collected taxes, and enforced the town's rules until the next meeting.

items.prompt: According to the section 'What They Decided,' what happened after citizens made decisions at the town meeting?

choices.text: Citizens held another meeting right away
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage does not say citizens held another meeting right away — selectmen handled things in between meetings.

choices.text: Selectmen carried out those decisions
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage clearly states that selectmen 'carried out the decisions the town meeting had made,' like team captains following the team's plan.

choices.text: Selectmen changed the decisions on their own
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because selectmen followed the decisions citizens made — the passage never says they changed those decisions on their own.

choices.text: Citizens fixed the roads by themselves
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage says selectmen kept roads in good shape, not the citizens themselves.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Who Was Left Out

Actually, most people could not. Only men who owned property were allowed to speak and vote. Women could not participate. People who did not own land were shut out. Enslaved people and Native Americans had no voice at all. So while town meetings gave some colonists real power, many people in the very same community had none.
stimulus.content_text:
Who Was Left Out
Actually, most people could not. Only men who owned property were allowed to speak and vote. Women could not participate. People who did not own land were shut out. Enslaved people and Native Americans had no voice at all. So while town meetings gave some colonists real power, many people in the very same community had none.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, how were women and Native Americans similar during colonial town meetings?

choices.text: They were both allowed to listen but not vote
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage never mentions women or Native Americans being allowed to listen — it simply says they could not participate or had no voice.

choices.text: They both had no voice in the meetings
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage clearly states women 'could not participate' and Native Americans 'had no voice at all,' showing both groups were shut out.

choices.text: They both owned property in the town
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage says only men who owned property could take part, so women and Native Americans were not described as property owners with power.

choices.text: They both chose to stay away from meetings
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage shows these groups were excluded by the rules, not that they made a personal choice to stay away.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Not Every Colony Worked This Way

They did not. Town meetings were mainly a New England tradition — in colonies like Massachusetts, Connecticut, and New Hampshire. The Middle Colonies used elected assemblies, and the Southern Colonies ran local government through county courts. New England's town meetings gave ordinary citizens the most direct power.
stimulus.content_text:
Not Every Colony Worked This Way
They did not. Town meetings were mainly a New England tradition — in colonies like Massachusetts, Connecticut, and New Hampshire. The Middle Colonies used elected assemblies, and the Southern Colonies ran local government through county courts. New England's town meetings gave ordinary citizens the most direct power.

items.prompt: According to the text, what were town meetings mainly a tradition in?

choices.text: the Middle Colonies
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage says the Middle Colonies used elected assemblies, not town meetings.

choices.text: New England colonies
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly states that town meetings were 'mainly a New England tradition' in colonies like Massachusetts, Connecticut, and New Hampshire.

choices.text: the Southern Colonies
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage says the Southern Colonies ran their local government through county courts, not town meetings.

choices.text: all the colonies
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage makes clear that different colonies used different systems, so town meetings were not a tradition in all of them.

items.title: Section 6 - Guiding Question 5

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
A Tradition That Survived

Here is something surprising: town meetings never disappeared. Today, citizens in many New England towns still gather in town halls to debate local issues and vote. The format looks a little different now, but the core idea is the same.

When American colonists later fought for independence from Britain, they were not reaching for something new. They were holding on to something they already practiced every time that bell rang and they walked into the meetinghouse together. Self-government was not just an idea to them. It was something they had already done with their own hands, their own voices, and their own votes.
stimulus.content_text:
A Tradition That Survived
Here is something surprising: town meetings never disappeared. Today, citizens in many New England towns still gather in town halls to debate local issues and vote. The format looks a little different now, but the core idea is the same.

When American colonists later fought for independence from Britain, they were not reaching for something new. They were holding on to something they already practiced every time that bell rang and they walked into the meetinghouse together. Self-government was not just an idea to them. It was something they had already done with their own hands, their own voices, and their own votes.

items.prompt: Which detail from "A Tradition That Survived" best shows that the colonists already knew how to govern themselves?

choices.text: They fought a hard war for independence from the country of Britain.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because fighting a war shows the colonists wanted independence, but it doesn't show that they already had experience governing themselves.

choices.text: They practiced making choices when they went to the meetinghouse.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage says the colonists practiced self-government every time they went to the meetinghouse, showing they already knew how to make decisions together.

choices.text: They changed the format of the town meetings to look a little different.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because changing the format of town meetings happened later and has nothing to do with what the colonists already knew.

choices.text: They made sure that the town meetings never completely disappeared today.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because town meetings surviving today is a fact about the present, not evidence of what the colonists had already practiced long ago.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: Based on the article, what was the difference between how rules were made in England and New England?

choices.text: In England, powerful lords made the rules, but in New England, men with land voted.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the article clearly states that powerful lords ruled in England, while men who owned property got to vote in New England.

choices.text: In England, ordinary people made the rules, but in New England, powerful lords voted.
choices.feedback: B is backwards — in England, ordinary people had almost no say, and it was the powerful lords who made the rules, not the other way around.

choices.text: In England, all the people made the rules, but in New England, only farmers voted.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because ordinary people did not make the rules in England, and in New England it wasn't only farmers — shopkeepers and ministers voted too.

choices.text: In England, team captains made the rules, but in New England, every person voted.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because 'team captains' refers to selectmen in New England who carried out decisions, and they had nothing to do with how England made its rules.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: Based on the article, why did the colonists already know how to govern themselves when they later fought for independence from Britain?

choices.text: because Parliament had taught them how to make laws
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because Parliament was in England and actually gave ordinary people almost no say in making laws.

choices.text: because they had practiced voting at town meetings
choices.feedback: B is correct because colonists had been voting and making decisions at town meetings for years, so they already knew how to govern themselves.

choices.text: because selectmen made all the decisions for them
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because selectmen only carried out the town's decisions — the whole group of voters made the actual choices together.

choices.text: because every person in the colony was allowed to vote
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the article explains that most people, like women, people without land, and enslaved people, were not allowed to vote at town meetings.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: Which order of events best summarizes how colonists ran their town meetings?

choices.text: Men gathered in town, citizens voted on rules, and then selectmen carried out the plans.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage shows men gathered first, then voted on rules together, and then selectmen carried out the plans between meetings.

choices.text: Selectmen carried out plans, men gathered in town, and then citizens voted on the rules.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because selectmen only acted *after* the town voted, not before people even gathered.

choices.text: A loud bell rang, a farmer argued about taxes, and then a minister wanted money.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because it lists a few small details from the passage instead of summarizing the main steps of how town meetings worked.

choices.text: Men gathered in town, powerful lords made the rules, and then selectmen carried out plans.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because powerful lords made rules in England, not in New England — colonial town meetings were set up so ordinary men could make the rules themselves.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: Based on the article, how were town meetings in colonial New England and town meetings in New England today similar?

choices.text: Both allowed only men who owned property to vote on issues.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because that old rule only applied to colonial times — today, the article says the format is 'a little different now,' meaning those unfair limits are gone.

choices.text: Both let citizens gather together to debate and vote on local issues.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the article says colonists debated and voted in meetings long ago, and today people in New England towns 'still gather in town halls to debate local issues and vote' — the core idea stayed the same.

choices.text: Both used selectmen instead of letting citizens speak for themselves.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because selectmen were team captains who carried out the town's decisions, but citizens still spoke and voted for themselves at the meetings.

choices.text: Both were held in every colony across America.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because the article clearly says town meetings were 'mainly a New England tradition' and other colonies used different kinds of government.

syllabus_metadata.title: The Town Meeting: Democracy Begins in America

assessments.title: The House of Burgesses: Virginia's First Lawmakers

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Hot Day That Started American Democracy

A Church Full of Lawmakers

Twenty-two men squeezed into a tiny wooden church in Jamestown, Virginia, in the summer of 1619. The air was so thick with heat that one man fainted right where he sat. But nobody left. They were not there to pray. They were there to do something no colonists in America had ever done before: make their own laws.

This was the first meeting of the House of Burgesses. A burgess was a person chosen to speak and vote for the people in a town — like a team captain who carries the whole team's ideas to a big meeting. Male landowners in each Virginia settlement picked two burgesses to send to Jamestown. Those burgesses then discussed problems, argued about solutions, and voted on new rules for the whole colony.

You might think American democracy started with the Declaration of Independence or the Constitution. Actually, it began more than 150 years earlier, in that sweltering church.
stimulus.content_text:
The Hot Day That Started American DemocracyA Church Full of Lawmakers
Twenty-two men squeezed into a tiny wooden church in Jamestown, Virginia, in the summer of 1619. The air was so thick with heat that one man fainted right where he sat. But nobody left. They were not there to pray. They were there to do something no colonists in America had ever done before: make their own laws.

This was the first meeting of the House of Burgesses. A burgess was a person chosen to speak and vote for the people in a town — like a team captain who carries the whole team's ideas to a big meeting. Male landowners in each Virginia settlement picked two burgesses to send to Jamestown. Those burgesses then discussed problems, argued about solutions, and voted on new rules for the whole colony.

You might think American democracy started with the Declaration of Independence or the Constitution. Actually, it began more than 150 years earlier, in that sweltering church.

items.prompt: According to the passage about the House of Burgesses, how did the men's purpose for visiting the Jamestown church differ from the typical reason people go to church?

choices.text: They went to make laws, but people usually go to pray.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage says the men 'were not there to pray' but were there to make laws, which is exactly what this answer says.

choices.text: They went to buy land, but people usually go to pray.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the passage never mentions buying land as the reason the men came to the church.

choices.text: They went to write the Constitution, but people usually go to pray.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the passage says democracy started over 150 years before the Constitution, so they could not have been there to write it.

choices.text: They went to sign the Declaration, but people usually go to pray.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the Declaration of Independence also came much later than 1619, so the men were not there to sign it.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Why It Was a Big Deal

Before the House of Burgesses, colonists had no say in how Virginia was run. The Virginia Company — a business group back in England that paid to start the colony — chose a governor and a small council. These few men made every decision. Colonists just had to follow along.

The House of Burgesses created something called representative government. That means people choose someone to speak for them when laws are made — the way your class might elect a student to bring your ideas to the principal. The burgesses were those chosen voices. They formed a legislative assembly, which is a group of elected people who meet to write and vote on laws.

Only white men who owned land could vote. So this early democracy left most people out. It was a small start — but it was a start.
stimulus.content_text:
Why It Was a Big Deal
Before the House of Burgesses, colonists had no say in how Virginia was run. The Virginia Company — a business group back in England that paid to start the colony — chose a governor and a small council. These few men made every decision. Colonists just had to follow along.

The House of Burgesses created something called representative government. That means people choose someone to speak for them when laws are made — the way your class might elect a student to bring your ideas to the principal. The burgesses were those chosen voices. They formed a legislative assembly, which is a group of elected people who meet to write and vote on laws.

Only white men who owned land could vote. So this early democracy left most people out. It was a small start — but it was a start.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, why was the creation of the House of Burgesses important for the colonists?

choices.text: It let the Virginia Company choose better governors for the colony.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage says the Virginia Company already chose governors before the House of Burgesses, and nothing in the passage says the House of Burgesses helped them pick better ones.

choices.text: It gave colonists a way to help make the laws they followed.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage explains that the House of Burgesses created representative government, giving colonists chosen voices — called burgesses — to help make the laws they had to follow.

choices.text: It allowed every person living in Virginia to vote on new laws.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage specifically says only white men who owned land could vote, so most people in Virginia were actually left out.

choices.text: It stopped England from sending any more leaders to Virginia.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage never mentions England stopping the sending of leaders to Virginia — that idea isn't in the passage at all.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Growing Stronger Over Time

At that first short meeting, the burgesses talked about farming rules, relations with Native Americans, and keeping order in the colony. But the assembly kept meeting, year after year.

As Virginia grew, the House of Burgesses grew too. It moved from Jamestown to Williamsburg, Virginia's new colonial capital. It took on bigger questions — how to collect taxes (money people pay to the government to keep things running), how to trade goods, who got which land, and how to defend the colony.

Each of the thirteen colonies built its own kind of government over time. Some had assemblies like the House of Burgesses. But Virginia's idea — that colonists should help make their own laws — spread.
stimulus.content_text:
Growing Stronger Over Time
At that first short meeting, the burgesses talked about farming rules, relations with Native Americans, and keeping order in the colony. But the assembly kept meeting, year after year.

As Virginia grew, the House of Burgesses grew too. It moved from Jamestown to Williamsburg, Virginia's new colonial capital. It took on bigger questions — how to collect taxes (money people pay to the government to keep things running), how to trade goods, who got which land, and how to defend the colony.

Each of the thirteen colonies built its own kind of government over time. Some had assemblies like the House of Burgesses. But Virginia's idea — that colonists should help make their own laws — spread.

items.prompt: According to the text, which event happened first — the House of Burgesses meeting in Jamestown or the House of Burgesses moving to Williamsburg?

choices.text: It moved to Williamsburg before it ever met in Jamestown.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage says the assembly moved FROM Jamestown TO Williamsburg, which means Jamestown came first.

choices.text: It met in Jamestown first and then moved to Williamsburg.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage says the assembly met in Jamestown and then later 'moved from Jamestown to Williamsburg' as Virginia grew.

choices.text: It met in Williamsburg first and then moved to Jamestown.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage says the opposite — the assembly was in Jamestown first and then moved to Williamsburg, not the other way around.

choices.text: It met in both Jamestown and Williamsburg at the same time.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage says the assembly moved from one city to the other, meaning it left Jamestown and went to Williamsburg, not both at once.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Famous Burgesses You Already Know

Some of America's most famous leaders got their start in the House of Burgesses. George Washington served there and learned how to lead and work with other lawmakers. Thomas Jefferson sharpened his thinking about freedom and government as a burgess — ideas he later poured into the Declaration of Independence. Patrick Henry gave fiery speeches there defending the colonists' rights, and people across the colonies heard about his bold words.

The House of Burgesses kept meeting until the American Revolution, when Virginia replaced it with a state legislature.
stimulus.content_text:
Famous Burgesses You Already Know
Some of America's most famous leaders got their start in the House of Burgesses. George Washington served there and learned how to lead and work with other lawmakers. Thomas Jefferson sharpened his thinking about freedom and government as a burgess — ideas he later poured into the Declaration of Independence. Patrick Henry gave fiery speeches there defending the colonists' rights, and people across the colonies heard about his bold words.

The House of Burgesses kept meeting until the American Revolution, when Virginia replaced it with a state legislature.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, what did George Washington, Thomas Jefferson, and Patrick Henry have in common?

choices.text: They all wrote the Declaration of Independence.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage only says Thomas Jefferson's ideas went into the Declaration of Independence — it does not say Washington or Henry wrote it.

choices.text: They all began as leaders in the House.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage tells us all three men served in the House of Burgesses, making that the one thing they all had in common.

choices.text: They all gave fiery speeches to the colonists.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage only says Patrick Henry gave fiery speeches — it does not say Washington or Jefferson did the same.

choices.text: They all fought in the American Revolution.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage does not mention any of these three men fighting in the American Revolution.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Still Happening Today

Today, when members of Congress vote on a law in Washington, D.C., they are doing what those twenty-two sweating burgesses did in 1619. Every time Americans choose someone to represent them, they are using an idea that started on a blistering summer day in a small wooden church — an idea that the people who live under the laws should have a voice in making them.
stimulus.content_text:
Still Happening Today
Today, when members of Congress vote on a law in Washington, D.C., they are doing what those twenty-two sweating burgesses did in 1619. Every time Americans choose someone to represent them, they are using an idea that started on a blistering summer day in a small wooden church — an idea that the people who live under the laws should have a voice in making them.

items.prompt: According to the text, what does the author mean by the idea that 'the people who live under the laws should have a voice in making them'?

choices.text: people should speak loudly at government meetings
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because 'having a voice' in the passage means choosing someone to represent you, not about how loudly you speak at meetings.

choices.text: people should follow all the laws in their town
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because following laws is something different — the passage is about people having a say in creating the laws, not just obeying them.

choices.text: people should choose representatives to help make laws
choices.feedback: C is correct because the passage says Americans 'choose someone to represent them,' which means picking representatives to help make the laws they live under.

choices.text: people should write the laws down by themselves
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage explains that people choose representatives to make laws for them, not that every person writes the laws on their own.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: Based on the article, how was the Virginia Company's council different from the House of Burgesses?

choices.text: The council allowed all people to vote, but the House of Burgesses allowed only landowners to vote.
choices.feedback: This is backwards — the article says the House of Burgesses only allowed male landowners to vote, not all people.

choices.text: The council was chosen by a group in England, but the House of Burgesses was chosen by colonists.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the article says the Virginia Company in England chose the council, while colonists in each settlement chose their own burgesses.

choices.text: The council met in the new capital city, but the House of Burgesses met in a small wooden church.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because it was the House of Burgesses that started in the small wooden church and later moved to Williamsburg, not the council.

choices.text: The council made the rules about farming, but the House of Burgesses made the rules about collecting taxes.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because the article never says the council only made farming rules — the council made every single decision before the House of Burgesses was created.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: Based on the article, why did the idea of representative government spread to other colonies?

choices.text: because England forced all colonies to copy Virginia's laws
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the article never mentions England forcing other colonies to copy Virginia's laws — the idea spread on its own.

choices.text: because Virginia showed that colonists could help make laws
choices.feedback: This is correct because the article directly says Virginia's idea that colonists should help make their own laws spread to the other colonies.

choices.text: because famous leaders moved from Virginia to other colonies
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the article does not say that famous leaders moved away from Virginia to bring the idea to other colonies.

choices.text: because the other colonies wanted to collect more taxes
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because taxes were just one topic the burgesses discussed, not the reason other colonies wanted their own representative governments.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: Which sentence best tells the main idea about how American lawmaking changed over time?

choices.text: It started with a small meeting in a church and grew into our government today.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage shows lawmaking grew from a tiny 1619 church meeting all the way into the Congress Americans use today.

choices.text: It started with the Declaration of Independence and became a small council in Jamestown.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the Declaration of Independence came after the House of Burgesses, not before it, and there was no small council in Jamestown at the start of the story.

choices.text: It started with the Virginia Company's rules and ended as a completely forgotten idea.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the passage clearly shows the idea of representative government was not forgotten — it spread to all thirteen colonies and is still used today.

choices.text: It started with a group of famous leaders and turned into a business in England.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the story starts with colonists making laws, not famous leaders starting a business, and the Virginia Company was the business group, not the burgesses.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: Based on the article, how are the burgesses from 1619 and today's members of Congress alike?

choices.text: Both meet in a small wooden church in Virginia.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the article says Congress meets in Washington, D.C., not in a small wooden church in Virginia — that was only where the very first burgesses met in 1619.

choices.text: Both are chosen by a business group in England.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the Virginia Company chose the governor and council, but the burgesses were chosen by the colonists themselves, not by any business group in England.

choices.text: Both are chosen by the people to vote on laws.
choices.feedback: C is correct because the article explains that burgesses were chosen by the people to speak and vote on laws, and that members of Congress today are doing that same thing.

choices.text: Both allow every single person to vote on rules.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the article clearly states that in 1619 only white men who owned land could vote, so most people were left out.

syllabus_metadata.title: The House of Burgesses: Virginia's First Lawmakers

assessments.title: Two Empires, One Continent: The Causes of the French and Indian War

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The French and Indian War

A Young Soldier in the Wilderness

Can you imagine being just 21 years old and riding alone through snowy, dark forests on a dangerous mission? That is exactly what George Washington did! In 1753, this young soldier from Virginia rode deep into the forests of western Pennsylvania. The governor had sent him to tell the French to get off British land. Washington found the French fort and bravely delivered his message. The French commander just smiled, said no, and sent him home. But this was only the beginning of a much bigger story!
stimulus.content_text:
The French and Indian WarA Young Soldier in the Wilderness
Can you imagine being just 21 years old and riding alone through snowy, dark forests on a dangerous mission? That is exactly what George Washington did! In 1753, this young soldier from Virginia rode deep into the forests of western Pennsylvania. The governor had sent him to tell the French to get off British land. Washington found the French fort and bravely delivered his message. The French commander just smiled, said no, and sent him home. But this was only the beginning of a much bigger story!

items.prompt: Washington was sent to tell the French to leave. According to the text, what did the French commander do instead?

choices.text: He agreed to leave and packed up the fort
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the French commander said no instead of agreeing to leave.

choices.text: He said no and refused to leave the land
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage says the commander 'said no and sent him home,' which means he refused to leave the land.

choices.text: He asked Washington to stay at the fort
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the commander sent Washington away rather than asking him to stay.

choices.text: He rode back to Virginia with Washington
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage never says the French commander traveled anywhere with Washington.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Two Nations Want the Same Land

Have you ever argued with someone over who gets to use something? Now imagine two whole countries doing that — but with an entire continent! By the mid-1700s, Britain and France both claimed huge parts of North America. Britain held the thirteen colonies along the Atlantic coast. France controlled land stretching from Canada through the Great Lakes and down the Mississippi River to Louisiana. Both countries wanted the Ohio River Valley, a stretch of land covered in thick forests and full of animals like beaver. Beaver fur sold for big money back in Europe, so you can see why everyone wanted it!
stimulus.content_text:
Two Nations Want the Same Land
Have you ever argued with someone over who gets to use something? Now imagine two whole countries doing that — but with an entire continent! By the mid-1700s, Britain and France both claimed huge parts of North America. Britain held the thirteen colonies along the Atlantic coast. France controlled land stretching from Canada through the Great Lakes and down the Mississippi River to Louisiana. Both countries wanted the Ohio River Valley, a stretch of land covered in thick forests and full of animals like beaver. Beaver fur sold for big money back in Europe, so you can see why everyone wanted it!

items.prompt: In the section 'Two Nations Want the Same Land,' how does the detail about beaver fur support the main idea that Britain and France wanted the Ohio River Valley?

choices.text: It shows why the animals needed to live in thick forests.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage mentions thick forests to describe the Ohio River Valley, not to explain why animals need forests.

choices.text: It proves that the two nations liked to argue with each other.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the beaver fur detail explains the land's value, not that Britain and France enjoyed arguing.

choices.text: It explains why the land was so valuable to both countries.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage says beaver fur sold for big money in Europe, which shows why both countries wanted that land so much.

choices.text: It describes how the thirteen colonies were built by Britain.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the beaver fur detail is about the Ohio River Valley's value, not about how Britain built its thirteen colonies.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Native American Nations Choose Sides

Here is something important to think about: Native American nations had lived on this land long before either European country arrived. Now they faced a hard choice. The British wanted to cut down trees and plant crops, which pushed Native peoples off their land. The French mostly wanted to trade, not to clear forests for farms. So most Native American nations in the Ohio Valley became allies of France — partners who agreed to help each other.
stimulus.content_text:
Native American Nations Choose Sides
Here is something important to think about: Native American nations had lived on this land long before either European country arrived. Now they faced a hard choice. The British wanted to cut down trees and plant crops, which pushed Native peoples off their land. The French mostly wanted to trade, not to clear forests for farms. So most Native American nations in the Ohio Valley became allies of France — partners who agreed to help each other.

items.prompt: According to the text, who lived on the land long before the European countries arrived?

choices.text: The French trading groups
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the French trading groups were one of the European groups who arrived later — they were not living on the land before the Europeans came.

choices.text: The British farming groups
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the British farming groups also arrived later as Europeans, and the passage even says they pushed Native peoples off their land.

choices.text: The Native American nations
choices.feedback: C is correct because the passage clearly states that 'Native American nations had lived on this land long before either European country arrived.'

choices.text: The European country leaders
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because European country leaders were part of the European groups who came to the land later, not before the Europeans arrived.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
The First Shots

Remember young George Washington? Well, he was back! In 1754, Washington returned to the Ohio Valley with a small group of soldiers. In a quick skirmish — a short, sudden fight — his men killed a French officer. Washington then built a tiny fort called Fort Necessity. But the French came back with way more soldiers, surrounded the fort, and forced Washington to surrender, which means to give up a fight because you cannot win. The young Virginian had to march home, defeated.
stimulus.content_text:
The First Shots
Remember young George Washington? Well, he was back! In 1754, Washington returned to the Ohio Valley with a small group of soldiers. In a quick skirmish — a short, sudden fight — his men killed a French officer. Washington then built a tiny fort called Fort Necessity. But the French came back with way more soldiers, surrounded the fort, and forced Washington to surrender, which means to give up a fight because you cannot win. The young Virginian had to march home, defeated.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, how did the number of soldiers in Washington's group compare to the number in the French group?

choices.text: Washington's group had a smaller number of soldiers than the French group.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage says the French came back with 'way more soldiers,' which means Washington's group was smaller.

choices.text: Washington's group had a larger number of soldiers than the French group.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because it was the French who had more soldiers, not Washington's group.

choices.text: Washington's group had the exact same number of soldiers as the French group.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the passage says the French had 'way more' soldiers, so the two groups were definitely not equal.

choices.text: Washington's group had an unknown number of soldiers compared to the French group.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the passage does tell us about the difference — it says the French had way more soldiers than Washington.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
War Breaks Out — and Britain Wins

British colonists called it the French and Indian War. Here is a surprising part: that name does not mean France fought against Native Americans. It means the British fought against the French and their Native American allies together. Tricky name, right? In the end, Britain won the war. The Treaty of Paris gave Britain control of nearly all of France's land in North America.
stimulus.content_text:
War Breaks Out — and Britain Wins
British colonists called it the French and Indian War. Here is a surprising part: that name does not mean France fought against Native Americans. It means the British fought against the French and their Native American allies together. Tricky name, right? In the end, Britain won the war. The Treaty of Paris gave Britain control of nearly all of France's land in North America.

items.prompt: According to the text, what does the name "French and Indian War" mean about who fought in the war?

choices.text: The British fought against the French and Native Americans.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage clearly states the British fought against the French and their Native American allies together.

choices.text: The French fought against the British and Native Americans.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the French and British were on opposite sides — they were enemies, not partners.

choices.text: The French fought against the Native Americans for the land.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the passage says France and Native Americans were allies, meaning they fought together, not against each other.

choices.text: The Native Americans fought against the French and the British.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because Native Americans were helping the French fight the British, not fighting against both of them.

items.title: Section 6 - Guiding Question 5

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
Why This War Still Matters

So why should you care about a war that ended hundreds of years ago? Britain won enormous new territory — but paying for the war left the British government nearly broke. To raise money, Britain started taxing the American colonists. Those colonists were not happy about it at all. And do you remember the young soldier who surrendered Fort Necessity? George Washington would one day lead those angry colonists in a revolution against Britain itself. That first snowy ride through the forest was just the beginning of one of history's greatest stories!
stimulus.content_text:
Why This War Still Matters
So why should you care about a war that ended hundreds of years ago? Britain won enormous new territory — but paying for the war left the British government nearly broke. To raise money, Britain started taxing the American colonists. Those colonists were not happy about it at all. And do you remember the young soldier who surrendered Fort Necessity? George Washington would one day lead those angry colonists in a revolution against Britain itself. That first snowy ride through the forest was just the beginning of one of history's greatest stories!

items.prompt: Which detail from the text provides evidence that winning the war caused a problem for Britain?

choices.text: The British government was left nearly broke.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage directly says paying for the war left Britain 'nearly broke,' which is the problem that winning caused.

choices.text: Britain won an enormous amount of territory.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because winning lots of new territory was actually a good result for Britain, not a problem.

choices.text: George Washington surrendered Fort Necessity.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because George Washington surrendering Fort Necessity is about a battle event, not about a problem Britain had after winning the war.

choices.text: The colonists rode through the snowy forest.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the snowy forest ride is about George Washington's journey and has nothing to do with Britain's problems.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: According to the article, how did George Washington's actions toward the British change over time?

choices.text: He helped the British claim land early on, but later he fought against them.
choices.feedback: This is correct because Washington started out serving the British as a soldier and messenger, but the article tells us he later led a revolution against Britain.

choices.text: He fought against the British early on, but later he helped them claim land.
choices.feedback: This is backwards — Washington helped the British first and fought against them later, not the other way around.

choices.text: He traded with the British early on, but later he cleared their forest land.
choices.feedback: Washington never traded with the British or cleared their forest land — those details are about the French and British settlers, not Washington.

choices.text: He surrendered to the British early on, but later he defeated them in battle.
choices.feedback: Washington did surrender Fort Necessity, but he surrendered to the FRENCH, not the British, so this answer has the wrong enemy.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: To find out why the French and Indian War started and why the colonists later started a revolution, which two headings should you look under?

choices.text: "A Young Soldier in the Wilderness" and "The First Shots"
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because 'A Young Soldier in the Wilderness' and 'The First Shots' tell us about George Washington's missions, not the main reasons the war started or why the colonists revolted.

choices.text: "Two Nations Want the Same Land" and "Why This War Still Matters"
choices.feedback: B is correct because 'Two Nations Want the Same Land' explains why the war started, and 'Why This War Still Matters' explains how the war led to the colonists starting a revolution.

choices.text: "Native American Nations Choose Sides" and "War Breaks Out — and Britain Wins"
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because these sections tell us which side Native Americans chose and who won the war, but not the root cause of the war or why the colonists later rebelled.

choices.text: "The First Shots" and "Native American Nations Choose Sides"
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because 'The First Shots' and 'Native American Nations Choose Sides' describe events and alliances during the war, not the reasons the war began or why the revolution happened.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: The main idea of the article is that an argument over land grew into a major conflict. Which sequence of key details best supports this main idea?

choices.text: A small fight happened, two countries wanted the same land, a big war began, and Washington delivered a warning.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because it puts the small fight before the two countries wanting the same land, which is the wrong order — the land dispute came first.

choices.text: Two countries wanted the same land, Washington delivered a warning, a small fight happened, and a big war began.
choices.feedback: B is correct because it lists the key events in the right order, showing step by step how an argument over land grew into a major war.

choices.text: Native Americans traded animal furs, Washington rode through the snow, the British planted crops, and the colonists paid taxes.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because those details — fur trading, riding through snow, planting crops, and paying taxes — are not the main steps that show how the conflict grew.

choices.text: Britain won the new territory, the government lost its money, the colonists paid new taxes, and a revolution started.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because it only covers what happened after the war was already over, not how the argument over land turned into a war in the first place.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: How were Britain and France similar in what they wanted in North America?

choices.text: Both countries wanted to trade beaver fur with Native Americans.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says France wanted to trade, but Britain wanted to clear forests and plant crops — they did not have the same goal of trading beaver fur.

choices.text: Both countries wanted to claim the Ohio River Valley.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage clearly states 'Both countries wanted the Ohio River Valley,' which is exactly what made them enemies.

choices.text: Both countries wanted to build farms along the Atlantic coast.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because Britain had the thirteen colonies along the Atlantic coast, but France controlled land through Canada and the Mississippi River, not the Atlantic coast.

choices.text: Both countries wanted to be allies with Native American nations.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because most Native American nations chose to side with France, not with both countries — so Britain did not share that alliance.

syllabus_metadata.title: Two Empires, One Continent: The Causes of the French and Indian War

assessments.title: Battles in the Wilderness: Key Events of the War

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The French and Indian War

Red Coats in the Woods

In 1755, a long line of British soldiers marched through the Pennsylvania forest. Their bright red uniforms stood out against the dark green trees. They had no idea they were walking into a disaster.

These soldiers served General Edward Braddock. They were headed toward Fort Duquesne, a French fort built where the city of Pittsburgh stands today. The name "French and Indian War" can trick you. You might think the French fought against Native Americans. Actually, the French and many Native American nations fought together — against the British. The war's name tells you who the British were fighting, not who was fighting each other.

Both France and Britain wanted the Ohio River Valley. This land had rich soil, thick forests full of animals for the fur trade, and rivers for moving goods.
stimulus.content_text:
The French and Indian WarRed Coats in the Woods
In 1755, a long line of British soldiers marched through the Pennsylvania forest. Their bright red uniforms stood out against the dark green trees. They had no idea they were walking into a disaster.

These soldiers served General Edward Braddock. They were headed toward Fort Duquesne, a French fort built where the city of Pittsburgh stands today. The name "French and Indian War" can trick you. You might think the French fought against Native Americans. Actually, the French and many Native American nations fought together — against the British. The war's name tells you who the British were fighting, not who was fighting each other.

Both France and Britain wanted the Ohio River Valley. This land had rich soil, thick forests full of animals for the fur trade, and rivers for moving goods.

items.prompt: The name 'French and Indian War' might make people think the French fought against Native Americans. According to the passage, what actually happened instead?

choices.text: The French and British fought together against Native Americans.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage says the French and British were on opposite sides, not the same side.

choices.text: The French and many Native Americans fought together against the British.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly states that the French and many Native American nations fought together against the British.

choices.text: The Native Americans fought alone against both the French and British.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because Native Americans were not fighting alone — they were fighting alongside the French.

choices.text: The French and Native Americans fought against each other over land.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the French and Native Americans did not fight each other; they teamed up and fought on the same side.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Braddock's Terrible Defeat

General Braddock's soldiers marched in neat rows, the way armies fought in Europe. But this was not Europe. The French and their Native American allies — groups who had agreed to fight on the same side, like teammates — hid behind trees and rocks. They used tactics, or battle plans, that worked perfectly in thick woods. They fired from cover. They launched ambushes, attacking suddenly from hidden spots.

Braddock's soldiers made easy targets in their red uniforms. The general himself was killed. His army fell apart. A young officer named George Washington — yes, the future first president — helped lead the survivors to safety.

You might think the mighty British Empire won this war quickly. But for years, Britain kept losing. The French knew how to fight on the frontier, the wild land at the edge of European settlements. Different Native American nations chose different sides. Many joined France. Some joined Britain. Others stayed neutral. Each nation made its own decision based on what was best for its people.
stimulus.content_text:
Braddock's Terrible Defeat
General Braddock's soldiers marched in neat rows, the way armies fought in Europe. But this was not Europe. The French and their Native American allies — groups who had agreed to fight on the same side, like teammates — hid behind trees and rocks. They used tactics, or battle plans, that worked perfectly in thick woods. They fired from cover. They launched ambushes, attacking suddenly from hidden spots.

Braddock's soldiers made easy targets in their red uniforms. The general himself was killed. His army fell apart. A young officer named George Washington — yes, the future first president — helped lead the survivors to safety.

You might think the mighty British Empire won this war quickly. But for years, Britain kept losing. The French knew how to fight on the frontier, the wild land at the edge of European settlements. Different Native American nations chose different sides. Many joined France. Some joined Britain. Others stayed neutral. Each nation made its own decision based on what was best for its people.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, what caused the French and Native American allies to be successful in the battle?

choices.text: They marched in neat rows like European armies.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because marching in neat rows was what Braddock's soldiers did, and it hurt them — not the French and their allies.

choices.text: They used battle plans meant for thick woods.
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says the French and their allies used battle plans that worked perfectly in thick woods, like hiding behind trees and launching surprise attacks.

choices.text: They were led to safety by George Washington.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because George Washington helped Braddock's surviving soldiers get to safety — he was on the other side from the French and their allies.

choices.text: They decided to stay neutral to protect people.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because staying neutral means not fighting for either side, but the French allies chose to fight alongside France.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Climbing the Cliffs of Quebec

After years of defeat, Britain changed its plan. Slowly, the British began to win.

The biggest moment came in 1759 at Quebec, the capital of French Canada. Quebec sat on top of steep cliffs above the St. Lawrence River. Attacking it seemed impossible — like trying to storm a castle in the sky. But British General James Wolfe found a hidden path. In the darkness of night, his soldiers climbed the cliffs. By morning, they stood on the flat plains above the city.

French General Montcalm rushed his troops out to fight. The battle was short and fierce. Both generals were mortally wounded — hurt so badly that they died from their injuries. But the British won. With Quebec captured, France could no longer hold Canada. One by one, their remaining forts fell.
stimulus.content_text:
Climbing the Cliffs of Quebec
After years of defeat, Britain changed its plan. Slowly, the British began to win.

The biggest moment came in 1759 at Quebec, the capital of French Canada. Quebec sat on top of steep cliffs above the St. Lawrence River. Attacking it seemed impossible — like trying to storm a castle in the sky. But British General James Wolfe found a hidden path. In the darkness of night, his soldiers climbed the cliffs. By morning, they stood on the flat plains above the city.
