French General Montcalm rushed his troops out to fight. The battle was short and fierce. Both generals were mortally wounded — hurt so badly that they died from their injuries. But the British won. With Quebec captured, France could no longer hold Canada. One by one, their remaining forts fell.

items.prompt: According to the passage, which list shows the correct order of events during the battle at Quebec?

choices.text: Montcalm rushed out to fight, then Wolfe climbed the cliffs, then Quebec was captured
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because it has the order backwards — Wolfe climbed the cliffs first, and then Montcalm rushed out to fight.

choices.text: Wolfe's soldiers climbed the cliffs, then Montcalm rushed out to fight, then Quebec fell
choices.feedback: B is correct because it matches the order in the passage: soldiers climbed the cliffs, then Montcalm fought back, then Quebec fell.

choices.text: Quebec was captured, then both generals were wounded, then soldiers climbed the cliffs
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because it puts Quebec being captured at the beginning, but that actually happened at the end of the battle.

choices.text: Both generals were wounded, then Wolfe found a hidden path, then Montcalm rushed out
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because Wolfe found the hidden path and climbed the cliffs before the battle even started, not after the generals were wounded.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
A War That Wrapped Around the World

This war was part of an even larger conflict — a serious fight between groups — called the Seven Years' War. Britain and France battled each other in India, the Caribbean, West Africa, and across Europe. It was one of the first wars fought on nearly every continent.

The war ended in 1763 with the Treaty of Paris. France handed over almost all of its North American land to Britain. The map of the continent looked completely different overnight.

But Britain had spent enormous amounts of money. To pay its debts, the British government started taxing the American colonists. Those taxes made the colonists furious. Within twelve years, those same colonists would be fighting Britain for their own independence. The war that made Britain stronger in America also planted the seeds of a revolution.
stimulus.content_text:
A War That Wrapped Around the World
This war was part of an even larger conflict — a serious fight between groups — called the Seven Years' War. Britain and France battled each other in India, the Caribbean, West Africa, and across Europe. It was one of the first wars fought on nearly every continent.

The war ended in 1763 with the Treaty of Paris. France handed over almost all of its North American land to Britain. The map of the continent looked completely different overnight.

But Britain had spent enormous amounts of money. To pay its debts, the British government started taxing the American colonists. Those taxes made the colonists furious. Within twelve years, those same colonists would be fighting Britain for their own independence. The war that made Britain stronger in America also planted the seeds of a revolution.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, how were Britain and the American colonists similar after the war ended?

choices.text: Both wanted to sign the Treaty of Paris right away
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage never says anything about Britain or the colonists wanting to sign the Treaty of Paris quickly.

choices.text: Both ended up unhappy because of the war's costs
choices.feedback: B is correct because Britain went deep into debt from the war, and the colonists became furious over the taxes Britain used to pay off that debt — both ended up unhappy.

choices.text: Both lost all of their land in North America
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because Britain actually gained land after the war — France gave up almost all of its North American land to Britain.

choices.text: Both fought on the same side against France
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage doesn't describe the colonists fighting alongside Britain against France — it only talks about what happened after the war ended.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: How was the British approach to fighting at Quebec DIFFERENT from their approach at Fort Duquesne?

choices.text: They wore different uniforms to blend in with the trees
choices.feedback: The passage never mentions the British changing their uniforms at Quebec — that detail doesn't appear anywhere in the story.

choices.text: They used a surprise plan instead of marching openly
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says General Wolfe found a hidden path and climbed the cliffs at night as a surprise, which is the opposite of Braddock's open marching at Fort Duquesne.

choices.text: They asked Native Americans to fight for them instead
choices.feedback: The passage doesn't say the British asked Native Americans to fight for them at Quebec — that's not how they won that battle.

choices.text: They brought more soldiers than they had before
choices.feedback: The passage doesn't mention the British bringing more soldiers to Quebec; the key difference was their clever surprise plan, not the number of troops.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: To find out what caused the British to lose early battles and what caused them to finally win, which two headings should you look under?

choices.text: 'Red Coats in the Woods' and 'A War That Wrapped Around the World'
choices.feedback: A is wrong because 'Red Coats in the Woods' only introduces the soldiers marching to the fort, and 'A War That Wrapped Around the World' talks about the war spreading to other continents and the peace treaty — neither one fully explains why Britain kept losing or how they finally won.

choices.text: 'Braddock's Terrible Defeat' and 'Climbing the Cliffs of Quebec'
choices.feedback: B is correct because 'Braddock's Terrible Defeat' explains why the British lost early battles, and 'Climbing the Cliffs of Quebec' explains the turning point when Britain finally started winning.

choices.text: 'Red Coats in the Woods' and 'Braddock's Terrible Defeat'
choices.feedback: C is wrong because both 'Red Coats in the Woods' and 'Braddock's Terrible Defeat' are mostly about early British losses, so they don't help you find out what caused Britain to finally win.

choices.text: 'Climbing the Cliffs of Quebec' and 'A War That Wrapped Around the World'
choices.feedback: D is wrong because 'Climbing the Cliffs of Quebec' and 'A War That Wrapped Around the World' don't explain why Britain lost the early battles — you need 'Braddock's Terrible Defeat' for that part.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: Which list shows the main events of the passage in the order they happened?

choices.text: Britain lost early battles, captured Quebec, and taxed the colonists.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage shows Britain losing early, then winning at Quebec, then taxing colonists — in that exact order.

choices.text: Britain taxed the colonists, lost early battles, and captured Quebec.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the taxes happened at the very end of the war, not at the beginning.

choices.text: Britain won the first battle, lost Quebec, and signed a treaty.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because Britain lost the first battle and captured Quebec — it did not lose Quebec.

choices.text: Britain marched in red coats, hid behind trees, and climbed cliffs.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because hiding behind trees was a tactic used by the French and their Native American allies, not the British.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: Based on the passage, how were General Braddock, General Wolfe, and General Montcalm similar?

choices.text: They all fought for the British army.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because Montcalm was a French general, not a British one — only Braddock and Wolfe fought for Britain.

choices.text: They all died from battle injuries.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage says Braddock was killed in battle and that both Wolfe and Montcalm were 'mortally wounded,' meaning they all died from their battle injuries.

choices.text: They all used hidden surprise attacks.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because it was the French and Native American allies who hid and used surprise attacks — Braddock actually marched his soldiers in open, neat rows.

choices.text: They all survived the long, hard war.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because all three generals died during the war, so none of them survived it.

syllabus_metadata.title: Battles in the Wilderness: Key Events of the War

assessments.title: A New Map of North America: The Treaty of Paris 1763

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The War That Won an Empire — and Started a Revolution

A New Map for North America

In February 1763, leaders from Britain and France met in the city of Paris. They signed a paper called the Treaty of Paris — a written agreement to end a war. After they signed it, the map of North America changed forever.

The war they ended was called the French and Indian War. The name can be confusing. France and many Native American nations actually fought together against Britain. The war got its name from Britain's enemies, not from two sides fighting each other.

The people living in America at this time were not their own country yet. They were British, and American soldiers fought alongside British troops for about seven years.
stimulus.content_text:
The War That Won an Empire — and Started a RevolutionA New Map for North America
In February 1763, leaders from Britain and France met in the city of Paris. They signed a paper called the Treaty of Paris — a written agreement to end a war. After they signed it, the map of North America changed forever.

The war they ended was called the French and Indian War. The name can be confusing. France and many Native American nations actually fought together against Britain. The war got its name from Britain's enemies, not from two sides fighting each other.

The people living in America at this time were not their own country yet. They were British, and American soldiers fought alongside British troops for about seven years.

items.prompt: According to the text, how is the name 'French and Indian War' different from what actually happened?

choices.text: France and the Native Americans fought against each other in the war
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage says the opposite — France and Native Americans fought together, not against each other.

choices.text: France and many Native Americans actually fought on the same side
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage clearly states that France and many Native American nations fought on the same side against Britain.

choices.text: Britain and the Native Americans worked together to defeat France
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage says Britain fought against France and the Native Americans, not with them.

choices.text: France and Britain signed the treaty before the war even started
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the treaty was signed to end the war, not before it started.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Winners, Losers, and a Whole Lot of Land

Before the war, France controlled a huge piece of land. It went from the St. Lawrence River in the north all the way down to the Gulf of Mexico. After the treaty, almost all of that land belonged to Britain. Britain now had Canada and all French land east of the Mississippi River.

France kept only a few small islands near Canada. It had already given its land west of the Mississippi to Spain. France's dream of owning a big part of North America was over.

Spain had fought on France's side, so it lost land too. Spain gave up Florida to Britain. In return, Spain got the French land called Louisiana. Three empires had traded land back and forth like cards in a game.
stimulus.content_text:
Winners, Losers, and a Whole Lot of Land
Before the war, France controlled a huge piece of land. It went from the St. Lawrence River in the north all the way down to the Gulf of Mexico. After the treaty, almost all of that land belonged to Britain. Britain now had Canada and all French land east of the Mississippi River.

France kept only a few small islands near Canada. It had already given its land west of the Mississippi to Spain. France's dream of owning a big part of North America was over.

Spain had fought on France's side, so it lost land too. Spain gave up Florida to Britain. In return, Spain got the French land called Louisiana. Three empires had traded land back and forth like cards in a game.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, why did Spain lose land to Britain?

choices.text: It gave away land west of the Mississippi.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because giving away land west of the Mississippi was something France did, not Spain — and it wasn't the reason Spain lost land.

choices.text: It fought on the same side as France.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly says Spain fought on France's side, and that is why it lost Florida to Britain.

choices.text: It wanted to trade Florida for Louisiana.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because Spain didn't choose to give up Florida — it lost Florida as a consequence of being on the losing side of the war.

choices.text: It dreamed of owning all of North America.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because it was France, not Spain, that dreamed of owning a big part of North America, and that dream had nothing to do with why Spain lost land.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Victory with a Giant Bill

Britain won the war, but the war cost a lot of money. Britain owed almost twice as much money as before the war. Someone had to pay it back.

British leaders in Parliament — the group that makes laws in Britain, like Congress in America today — looked at the colonies. They decided the colonists should help pay. After all, the war had helped keep the colonists safe. Parliament made one new tax after another. The colonists got angrier each time.
stimulus.content_text:
Victory with a Giant Bill
Britain won the war, but the war cost a lot of money. Britain owed almost twice as much money as before the war. Someone had to pay it back.

British leaders in Parliament — the group that makes laws in Britain, like Congress in America today — looked at the colonies. They decided the colonists should help pay. After all, the war had helped keep the colonists safe. Parliament made one new tax after another. The colonists got angrier each time.

items.prompt: According to the text, what happened first — Britain owing a lot of money or Parliament making new taxes for the colonists?

choices.text: Parliament made new taxes first.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because Parliament only made new taxes after Britain already owed a lot of money from the war.

choices.text: Britain owed a lot of money first.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage clearly shows Britain had a huge debt first, and then Parliament created taxes to help pay it back.

choices.text: Both things happened at the same time.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because these two things did not happen at the same time — the debt came first and caused the taxes to happen later.

choices.text: The colonists asked for taxes first.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the colonists never asked for taxes — the passage actually says they got angrier every time Parliament made a new tax.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
A Line No One Wanted to Obey

Many Native American nations — like the Shawnee, the Delaware, and the Cherokee — lived west of the Appalachian Mountains. They were their own peoples with their own ways of life. Some had fought with France, and some had fought with Britain.

King George III made an official order called the Proclamation of 1763. He drew an imaginary line along the Appalachian Mountains. Colonists were not allowed to move west of that line. He did this to stop more fighting with Native nations.

The colonists were angry. Many had fought in the war because they wanted that western land. Soldiers, farmers, and people who wanted to buy and sell land all felt cheated. Many people just ignored the king's order and crossed the mountains anyway.
stimulus.content_text:
A Line No One Wanted to Obey
Many Native American nations — like the Shawnee, the Delaware, and the Cherokee — lived west of the Appalachian Mountains. They were their own peoples with their own ways of life. Some had fought with France, and some had fought with Britain.

King George III made an official order called the Proclamation of 1763. He drew an imaginary line along the Appalachian Mountains. Colonists were not allowed to move west of that line. He did this to stop more fighting with Native nations.

The colonists were angry. Many had fought in the war because they wanted that western land. Soldiers, farmers, and people who wanted to buy and sell land all felt cheated. Many people just ignored the king's order and crossed the mountains anyway.

items.prompt: According to the text, how were the soldiers and the farmers alike after King George III made his official order?

choices.text: They both felt cheated by the king's official order.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage directly says soldiers and farmers both 'felt cheated' by the king's order that stopped them from moving west.

choices.text: They both lived west of the Appalachian Mountains.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the king's order actually prevented colonists from living west of the Appalachian Mountains.

choices.text: They both made official orders to stop the fighting.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because soldiers and farmers didn't make any official orders — only King George III made the official order called the Proclamation of 1763.

choices.text: They both fought with France during the recent war.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the passage says some people fought with France and some with Britain, but doesn't say all soldiers and farmers fought with France.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
From Friends to Enemies

Think about this: in 1763, colonists cheered for Britain's win. They were proud to be British. But then came new taxes to pay for the war. Then came the king's order blocking western land. The colonists grew more and more angry. In just twelve years, those same colonists would be fighting against British soldiers to become their own country.

People who had once fought on the same side were about to become enemies.
stimulus.content_text:
From Friends to Enemies
Think about this: in 1763, colonists cheered for Britain's win. They were proud to be British. But then came new taxes to pay for the war. Then came the king's order blocking western land. The colonists grew more and more angry. In just twelve years, those same colonists would be fighting against British soldiers to become their own country.

People who had once fought on the same side were about to become enemies.

items.prompt: According to the section "From Friends to Enemies," what was the "king's order"?

choices.text: An order that paid for the long war
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because paying for the war was done through new taxes, not the king's order.

choices.text: An order that blocked western land
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage says 'the king's order blocking western land,' which tells us exactly what the order was about.

choices.text: An order that started a new country
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because starting a new country was something the colonists did on their own later, not something the king ordered.

choices.text: An order that cheered for a big win
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because cheering for a big win is what the colonists did after Britain won the war, not what the king's order said.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: According to the article, how did the relationship between American colonists and British soldiers change from the time of the French and Indian War to twelve years later?

choices.text: They went from fighting together to fighting each other.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage clearly shows colonists and British soldiers fought together during the war, but twelve years later the colonists were fighting against Britain to become their own country.

choices.text: They went from trading land to sharing the western land.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the article talks about countries trading land, not colonists and British soldiers sharing western land together.

choices.text: They went from fighting each other to fighting together.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because it has the story backwards — the colonists and British soldiers started out fighting together, not against each other.

choices.text: They went from ignoring the king to obeying his new laws.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the article says colonists ignored the king's Proclamation of 1763, which is the opposite of obeying his orders.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: Under which two headings should a reader look to find the events that caused the colonists to become angry with Britain?

choices.text: "A New Map for North America" and "Winners, Losers, and a Whole Lot of Land"
choices.feedback: Both of these sections explain what happened during and after the war, like who got what land, but they don't talk about the colonists being angry with Britain.

choices.text: "Winners, Losers, and a Whole Lot of Land" and "Victory with a Giant Bill"
choices.feedback: 'Victory with a Giant Bill' does talk about the colonists getting angry over taxes, but 'Winners, Losers, and a Whole Lot of Land' is about land changing hands between Britain, France, and Spain — not about the colonists being upset.

choices.text: "Victory with a Giant Bill" and "A Line No One Wanted to Obey"
choices.feedback: This is correct because 'Victory with a Giant Bill' explains how new taxes made colonists angry, and 'A Line No One Wanted to Obey' explains how the king's order blocking western land also made colonists angry.

choices.text: "A New Map for North America" and "A Line No One Wanted to Obey"
choices.feedback: 'A New Map for North America' just introduces the Treaty of Paris and explains the war's name — it doesn't describe anything that made the colonists angry with Britain.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: Which order of events best tells the main idea of the article?

choices.text: The colonists get angry, Britain wins a war, and Britain makes new rules.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the colonists didn't get angry first — they were actually proud and happy when Britain won the war, and the anger came later after the new rules.

choices.text: Britain wins a war, Britain makes new rules, and the colonists get angry.
choices.feedback: B is correct because it matches the order in the article: Britain wins the war, then makes new taxes and rules, and then the colonists grow angry enough to want to fight for independence.

choices.text: Britain makes new rules, the colonists get angry, and Britain wins a war.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because Britain couldn't make rules about the war debt or western land until after the war was already won.

choices.text: Britain wins a war, the colonists get angry, and Britain makes new rules.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the colonists got angry as a result of the new rules, so the anger happened last, not before the rules were made.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: Based on the article, how were France and Spain alike after the Treaty of Paris of 1763 was signed?

choices.text: They both gained new land from Britain
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because France and Spain did not gain land from Britain — Britain was the one who gained land from both of them.

choices.text: They both lost land to Britain
choices.feedback: B is correct because the article says Britain took land east of the Mississippi from France and took Florida from Spain, meaning both countries lost land to Britain.

choices.text: They both kept all of their land
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because both France and Spain lost land after the treaty, so they definitely did not keep all of their land.

choices.text: They both started fighting against Britain
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the article does not say France or Spain started fighting Britain after the treaty was signed.

syllabus_metadata.title: A New Map of North America: The Treaty of Paris 1763

assessments.title: No Taxation Without Representation: The Stamp Act Crisis

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Road to Revolution

A Stamp That Started a Fight

Picture a newspaper seller in Boston in 1765. He picks up his stack of papers to sell, but something new is stuck on every copy — a small stamp. That stamp means a tax has been paid to the king of England. And the colonists who live in America? They are furious.
stimulus.content_text:
The Road to RevolutionA Stamp That Started a Fight
Picture a newspaper seller in Boston in 1765. He picks up his stack of papers to sell, but something new is stuck on every copy — a small stamp. That stamp means a tax has been paid to the king of England. And the colonists who live in America? They are furious.

items.prompt: According to the section "A Stamp That Started a Fight," which choice shows a contrast between the king and the colonists?

choices.text: The king gets the tax, but the colonists are furious.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage shows a contrast — the king benefits by getting the tax money, while the colonists are angry about having to pay it.

choices.text: The king sells the papers, but the colonists read them.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never says anything about the king selling newspapers — that's the newspaper seller's job.

choices.text: The king lives in Boston, but the colonists live in America.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says the king lives in England, not Boston, so this gets the king's location wrong.

choices.text: The king pays the tax, but the colonists get the stamp.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because it has it backwards — the colonists are the ones paying the tax, not the king.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
A War and a Big Bill

To understand why, you need to go back a few years. Britain and France had just finished fighting a long, expensive war in North America called the French and Indian War. You might think that war was between France and India, but it wasn't. It was between Britain and France, with Native American groups fighting on both sides.

Britain won, but the war cost a fortune. British leaders decided the colonists should help pay the bill. So in 1765, the British Parliament — the group of leaders who made laws for Britain, like a congress — passed the Stamp Act.
stimulus.content_text:
A War and a Big Bill
To understand why, you need to go back a few years. Britain and France had just finished fighting a long, expensive war in North America called the French and Indian War. You might think that war was between France and India, but it wasn't. It was between Britain and France, with Native American groups fighting on both sides.

Britain won, but the war cost a fortune. British leaders decided the colonists should help pay the bill. So in 1765, the British Parliament — the group of leaders who made laws for Britain, like a congress — passed the Stamp Act.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, why did British leaders pass the Stamp Act?

choices.text: They wanted to punish the colonists for fighting
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage never says Britain was trying to punish the colonists — the colonists hadn't done anything wrong yet.

choices.text: They needed the colonists to help pay war costs
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly says the war 'cost a fortune' and British leaders wanted the colonists to 'help pay the bill.'

choices.text: They wanted the colonists to start a new war
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the whole reason for the Stamp Act was to deal with the costs of a war that had already ended, not to start a new one.

choices.text: They needed the colonists to join the Parliament
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage describes Parliament as Britain's group of leaders, and never mentions wanting colonists to become part of it.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Stamps on Everything

The Stamp Act put a tax on printed materials. Newspapers, legal documents, even playing cards — all had to carry an official stamp.

The colonists were angry. But here is the surprising part: it wasn't really about the money. It was about a principle — a deep belief about right and wrong. The government could only tax people who had representatives — people they voted for to speak for them in Parliament. No colonists had a seat in Parliament. So they believed this tax was unfair and against British law.

A powerful phrase captured their anger: "No taxation without representation." These words did not mean colonists refused to pay any taxes at all. They meant colonists wanted a say in how their money was spent.
stimulus.content_text:
Stamps on Everything
The Stamp Act put a tax on printed materials. Newspapers, legal documents, even playing cards — all had to carry an official stamp.

The colonists were angry. But here is the surprising part: it wasn't really about the money. It was about a principle — a deep belief about right and wrong. The government could only tax people who had representatives — people they voted for to speak for them in Parliament. No colonists had a seat in Parliament. So they believed this tax was unfair and against British law.

A powerful phrase captured their anger: "No taxation without representation." These words did not mean colonists refused to pay any taxes at all. They meant colonists wanted a say in how their money was spent.

items.prompt: According to the text, what must happen before representatives can speak for people in Parliament?

choices.text: The people must vote for them.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage says representatives are 'people they voted for to speak for them in Parliament,' so voting comes first.

choices.text: The people must pay taxes to them.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the passage never says paying taxes is how people get a representative — it's actually the other way around: you need a representative before you can be fairly taxed.

choices.text: The people must buy stamps from them.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because buying stamps is about the Stamp Act tax, not about choosing someone to speak for you in Parliament.

choices.text: The people must write laws for them.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage says representatives speak for the people in Parliament, not that the people write laws for the representatives.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Fighting Back

Most colonists still thought of themselves as loyal British citizens. They were not asking for their own country — not yet. They simply wanted the same rights as British people living in England.

But they fought hard for those rights. Colonists organized boycotts — they refused to buy British goods, the way you might refuse to shop at a store that treated you unfairly. They burned stamps in the streets. They made straw dummies that looked like tax collectors and hung them from trees. Some protests turned violent. Real tax collectors were threatened and chased out of town.

A secret group called the Sons of Liberty organized much of the resistance. They planned protests, enforced boycotts, and spread the word from colony to colony.

Then nine colonies did something they had never done before. They sent representatives to a meeting in New York called the Stamp Act Congress. Together, they wrote a petition — a formal letter asking for change — and sent it to the king. They asked him to repeal, or cancel, the Stamp Act.
stimulus.content_text:
Fighting Back
Most colonists still thought of themselves as loyal British citizens. They were not asking for their own country — not yet. They simply wanted the same rights as British people living in England.

But they fought hard for those rights. Colonists organized boycotts — they refused to buy British goods, the way you might refuse to shop at a store that treated you unfairly. They burned stamps in the streets. They made straw dummies that looked like tax collectors and hung them from trees. Some protests turned violent. Real tax collectors were threatened and chased out of town.

A secret group called the Sons of Liberty organized much of the resistance. They planned protests, enforced boycotts, and spread the word from colony to colony.

Then nine colonies did something they had never done before. They sent representatives to a meeting in New York called the Stamp Act Congress. Together, they wrote a petition — a formal letter asking for change — and sent it to the king. They asked him to repeal, or cancel, the Stamp Act.

items.prompt: The article compares a boycott to refusing to shop at a store that treated you unfairly. Based on this comparison, what does the word 'boycotts' mean?

choices.text: writing formal letters to ask for change
choices.feedback: Writing formal letters is what a petition is, not a boycott — that's a different word from the passage.

choices.text: refusing to buy from someone as a protest
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says colonists refused to buy British goods, and compares it to not shopping at an unfair store — both mean refusing to buy as a protest.

choices.text: burning things in the streets to show anger
choices.feedback: Burning things in the streets is another protest the colonists did, but that's not what the word 'boycotts' means.

choices.text: making secret plans to start a new country
choices.feedback: The passage actually says the colonists were NOT trying to start a new country yet, and making secret plans is not what a boycott is.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
A Victory That Wasn't

British merchants were losing money because colonists had stopped buying their goods. In 1766, Parliament repealed the Stamp Act. Colonists celebrated in the streets.

Parliament also passed the Declaratory Act. This new law said Parliament could make any law it wanted for the colonies — any law at all. Britain had taken back the stamp tax, but it had not given up its power.

For the first time, people in Massachusetts, Virginia, New York, and beyond had worked together against a law they believed was wrong. They had discovered something powerful: apart, each colony was small. Together, they were strong. That feeling — that shared anger, that shared voice — would only grow louder in the years ahead.
stimulus.content_text:
A Victory That Wasn't
British merchants were losing money because colonists had stopped buying their goods. In 1766, Parliament repealed the Stamp Act. Colonists celebrated in the streets.

Parliament also passed the Declaratory Act. This new law said Parliament could make any law it wanted for the colonies — any law at all. Britain had taken back the stamp tax, but it had not given up its power.

For the first time, people in Massachusetts, Virginia, New York, and beyond had worked together against a law they believed was wrong. They had discovered something powerful: apart, each colony was small. Together, they were strong. That feeling — that shared anger, that shared voice — would only grow louder in the years ahead.

items.prompt: According to the text, what was the Declaratory Act?

choices.text: A law that allowed the colonies to work together.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because no law allowed the colonies to work together — they chose to do that on their own to fight the Stamp Act.

choices.text: A law that said Parliament could make any law.
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says the Declaratory Act was a law that said Parliament could make any law it wanted for the colonies.

choices.text: A law that stopped colonists from buying British goods.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because it was the colonists who stopped buying British goods — that was something they did, not a law called the Declaratory Act.

choices.text: A law that forced Britain to give up its power.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says Britain had NOT given up its power — the Declaratory Act actually kept Britain's power strong.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: How were the colonists' feelings about Britain different before and after the Stamp Act?

choices.text: They liked British goods before but hated all British people after
choices.feedback: A is wrong because the passage never says colonists hated all British people — they were angry about an unfair law, not about British people themselves.

choices.text: They felt loyal to Britain before but grew angry and united after
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage says colonists felt loyal to Britain at first, but the Stamp Act made them angry and brought them together like never before.

choices.text: They wanted their own country before but changed their minds after
choices.feedback: C is wrong because it's backwards — the passage says colonists were NOT asking for their own country yet, meaning they didn't want independence before the Stamp Act.

choices.text: They refused to pay any taxes before but agreed to pay them after
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the passage says colonists were never refusing to pay all taxes — their complaint was about being taxed without having a representative in Parliament to speak for them.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: How do the headings "Stamps on Everything" and "Fighting Back" help a reader find the causes and effects of the colonists' anger?

choices.text: They show where to find what caused the colonists' anger and the actions they took to stop the taxes.
choices.feedback: A is correct because 'Stamps on Everything' explains what caused the colonists' anger, and 'Fighting Back' shows the actions they took in response, like boycotts and petitions.

choices.text: They show where to find what caused the British king's anger and the actions he took to start a war.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the passage is about the colonists being angry, not the British king, and no war starts in these sections.

choices.text: They show where to find what caused the colonists' happiness and the actions they took to buy more stamps.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the colonists were angry and refused to buy stamps — they were definitely not happy about them.

choices.text: They show where to find what caused the tax collectors' anger and the actions they took to burn straw dummies.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the tax collectors were the ones being chased and threatened, not the ones who were angry and taking action.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: Which order of events best tells the main story of the article?

choices.text: Britain needs money, leaders pass a tax, people fight back, the tax ends.
choices.feedback: A is correct because it follows the real order: Britain needed money, passed the Stamp Act, colonists fought back, and then the tax was canceled.

choices.text: People fight back, Britain needs money, leaders pass a tax, the tax ends.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the fighting back happened AFTER the tax was passed, not before Britain even needed money.

choices.text: A war is fought, people buy papers, groups write letters, a law passes.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because it focuses on small details like buying papers and writing letters, not the main story of the whole article.

choices.text: Leaders pass a tax, people pay the money, the tax ends, a war starts.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because colonists refused to pay the tax and fought against it, and no new war started at the end of this story.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: Which statement best compares what the colonists wanted with what Parliament wanted?

choices.text: The colonists wanted lower war costs, while Parliament wanted more newspaper sellers.
choices.feedback: A is wrong because the colonists were upset about unfair taxes, not war costs, and nothing in the passage mentions Parliament wanting more newspaper sellers.

choices.text: The colonists wanted a voice in taxes, while Parliament wanted full power over the colonies.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage shows the colonists wanted a voice in taxes ('no taxation without representation') while Parliament's Declaratory Act showed they wanted full power over the colonies.

choices.text: The colonists wanted to start a new country, while Parliament wanted to end all taxes forever.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the passage says the colonists still thought of themselves as loyal British citizens and were not asking for their own country yet.

choices.text: The colonists wanted to punish French soldiers, while Parliament wanted to help the Sons of Liberty.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the colonists were not trying to punish French soldiers, and Parliament was actually trying to stop the Sons of Liberty, not help them.

syllabus_metadata.title: No Taxation Without Representation: The Stamp Act Crisis

assessments.title: Shots on King Street: The Boston Massacre

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Boston Massacre

Snowballs and Soldiers on King Street

A chunk of ice flew through the cold night air and smacked a British soldier in the chest. Then another. Then a whole shower of snowballs. It was the evening of March 5, 1770, and a crowd of angry colonists had surrounded a soldier standing guard outside Boston's Custom House, where the British collected taxes the colonists believed were deeply unfair.

British soldiers had been living in Boston for months. The king sent them to keep order after colonists protested new taxes on things like glass, paper, and tea. Colonists hated having soldiers march through their streets. They called the soldiers "lobsterbacks" because of their bright red coats. Fights and angry words broke out almost every day.
stimulus.content_text:
The Boston MassacreSnowballs and Soldiers on King Street
A chunk of ice flew through the cold night air and smacked a British soldier in the chest. Then another. Then a whole shower of snowballs. It was the evening of March 5, 1770, and a crowd of angry colonists had surrounded a soldier standing guard outside Boston's Custom House, where the British collected taxes the colonists believed were deeply unfair.

British soldiers had been living in Boston for months. The king sent them to keep order after colonists protested new taxes on things like glass, paper, and tea. Colonists hated having soldiers march through their streets. They called the soldiers "lobsterbacks" because of their bright red coats. Fights and angry words broke out almost every day.

items.prompt: According to the text, the British collected taxes, but how did the colonists feel about these taxes?

choices.text: They believed the taxes were deeply unfair.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage uses these exact words: the colonists believed the taxes were 'deeply unfair.'

choices.text: They wanted to pay the new taxes on the tea.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the colonists did NOT want to pay the new taxes — they were angry and protested against them.

choices.text: They thought the taxes would help keep order.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the colonists did not think the taxes would help keep order — it was actually the soldiers who were sent to keep order.

choices.text: They hoped the taxes would pay for red coats.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never says anything about colonists wanting taxes to pay for red coats — that detail is made up and not in the story.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
A Night That Turned Deadly

People shouted insults and dared the soldier to fight back. Someone rang a church bell, and even more colonists poured into the street. The soldiers gripped their muskets. They were surrounded, outnumbered, and scared.

Then someone yelled "Fire!" No one knows who. The soldiers shot into the crowd. When the smoke cleared, five colonists lay dead or dying. But five people were killed that night — not five hundred. The word "massacre" was chosen on purpose, and you'll soon see why.

Among the dead was Crispus Attucks, a man of African and Native American background. He is often called the first casualty — a person killed or hurt in a conflict — of the American Revolution. The other victims were Samuel Gray, James Caldwell, Samuel Maverick, and Patrick Carr.
stimulus.content_text:
A Night That Turned Deadly
People shouted insults and dared the soldier to fight back. Someone rang a church bell, and even more colonists poured into the street. The soldiers gripped their muskets. They were surrounded, outnumbered, and scared.

Then someone yelled "Fire!" No one knows who. The soldiers shot into the crowd. When the smoke cleared, five colonists lay dead or dying. But five people were killed that night — not five hundred. The word "massacre" was chosen on purpose, and you'll soon see why.

Among the dead was Crispus Attucks, a man of African and Native American background. He is often called the first casualty — a person killed or hurt in a conflict — of the American Revolution. The other victims were Samuel Gray, James Caldwell, Samuel Maverick, and Patrick Carr.

items.prompt: According to the passage about the Boston Massacre, what caused more colonists to come into King Street?

choices.text: The soldiers fired into the crowd.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the soldiers fired their muskets later in the story, after the extra colonists had already come into the street.

choices.text: Someone rang a church bell.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly tells us that someone rang a church bell and that caused even more colonists to pour into the street.

choices.text: Five colonists were killed.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the five colonists were killed after the crowd had already gathered, not before.

choices.text: Crispus Attucks was named first.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because Crispus Attucks being called the first casualty is a fact the passage shares with us, but it has nothing to do with why people came outside that night.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
A Picture That Told a Different Story

What happened after the shooting mattered just as much as the shooting itself. A Boston silversmith named Paul Revere created a famous engraving — a picture carved into metal and printed on paper, like a stamp pressed into ink. Revere's engraving showed British soldiers standing in a neat line, calmly firing into a helpless crowd.

It's easy to look at that picture and think it shows exactly what happened. Actually, the real scene was confused and messy. The crowd had been throwing ice and pressing in on the soldiers. But Revere's image told a simple, powerful story: the British were shooting innocent Americans.

Colonial leader Samuel Adams spread this story everywhere. He used the engraving and the shooting as propaganda — information shaped to make people feel a certain way, like a poster designed to make you angry. Adams called the event the "Boston Massacre." He published articles in newspapers and held memorial events every year. He wanted colonists across America to turn against British rule.
stimulus.content_text:
A Picture That Told a Different Story
What happened after the shooting mattered just as much as the shooting itself. A Boston silversmith named Paul Revere created a famous engraving — a picture carved into metal and printed on paper, like a stamp pressed into ink. Revere's engraving showed British soldiers standing in a neat line, calmly firing into a helpless crowd.

It's easy to look at that picture and think it shows exactly what happened. Actually, the real scene was confused and messy. The crowd had been throwing ice and pressing in on the soldiers. But Revere's image told a simple, powerful story: the British were shooting innocent Americans.

Colonial leader Samuel Adams spread this story everywhere. He used the engraving and the shooting as propaganda — information shaped to make people feel a certain way, like a poster designed to make you angry. Adams called the event the "Boston Massacre." He published articles in newspapers and held memorial events every year. He wanted colonists across America to turn against British rule.

items.prompt: According to the text, what did Samuel Adams do AFTER Paul Revere created his engraving?

choices.text: He carved the picture into metal and printed it on paper.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because carving the picture into metal and printing it is what Paul Revere did, not Samuel Adams.

choices.text: He spread the story and published articles in newspapers.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage clearly says Adams spread the story, published newspaper articles, and held memorial events after Revere created the engraving.

choices.text: He threw ice at the British soldiers on King Street.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage says the crowd threw ice at the soldiers, but it never says Samuel Adams was there doing that.

choices.text: He created the engraving before the shooting happened.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the engraving was made after the shooting happened, and Adams used it afterward — nothing happened before the shooting.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
A Surprising Lawyer

Here's the surprising part. The British soldiers got a fair trial in a Boston courtroom. And their lawyer was John Adams — Samuel Adams's own cousin and a future president. John Adams believed every person deserved a fair defense, even people he disagreed with. Most of the soldiers were found not guilty. Two were convicted — found guilty by a court — of lesser charges.
stimulus.content_text:
A Surprising Lawyer
Here's the surprising part. The British soldiers got a fair trial in a Boston courtroom. And their lawyer was John Adams — Samuel Adams's own cousin and a future president. John Adams believed every person deserved a fair defense, even people he disagreed with. Most of the soldiers were found not guilty. Two were convicted — found guilty by a court — of lesser charges.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, how were the British soldiers and other people the same in John Adams's mind?

choices.text: They all deserved a fair defense in court
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage says John Adams believed 'every person deserved a fair defense,' meaning soldiers and all other people were the same to him in that way.

choices.text: They all fought on the same side in Boston
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the British soldiers and the colonists were on opposite sides, not the same side.

choices.text: They all agreed with John Adams's ideas
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the passage says Adams defended people he 'disagreed with,' so they did not all share his ideas.

choices.text: They were all found not guilty at the trial
choices.feedback: D is wrong because two of the soldiers were actually convicted, or found guilty, so not everyone was found not guilty.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Why Five Deaths Shook Thirteen Colonies

Five people died on King Street that night. It was not a big battle. But the image of British soldiers firing on unarmed townspeople burned into the colonists' minds. Many people who had not yet picked a side now sided against the British. Samuel Adams made sure no one forgot. Paul Revere made sure everyone could see it.

Sometimes the biggest changes don't start with armies or cannons. Sometimes they start with snowballs, a shout in the dark, and a picture that made a whole country angry enough to fight for something new.
stimulus.content_text:
Why Five Deaths Shook Thirteen Colonies
Five people died on King Street that night. It was not a big battle. But the image of British soldiers firing on unarmed townspeople burned into the colonists' minds. Many people who had not yet picked a side now sided against the British. Samuel Adams made sure no one forgot. Paul Revere made sure everyone could see it.

Sometimes the biggest changes don't start with armies or cannons. Sometimes they start with snowballs, a shout in the dark, and a picture that made a whole country angry enough to fight for something new.

items.prompt: According to the text, what does the author mean by calling the event something that 'shook thirteen colonies'?

choices.text: it caused earthquakes across the land
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage never mentions earthquakes — 'shook' here is used as a figure of speech meaning a big impact on people.

choices.text: it made colonists feel cold and scared
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the passage uses 'shook' to describe how the event changed people's minds and actions, not how cold or scared they felt.

choices.text: it had a powerful effect on many people
choices.feedback: C is correct because the passage shows the event changed many people's opinions and turned them against the British, which is a powerful effect on many people.

choices.text: it knocked down buildings in the colonies
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage says nothing about buildings falling — it describes how people's thoughts and loyalties changed after the event.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: Based on the article, how were the actions of John Adams different from the actions of Samuel Adams?

choices.text: John defended the soldiers, while Samuel angered the colonists.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the article clearly shows John Adams defended the soldiers in court while Samuel Adams used the shooting as propaganda to make colonists angry at the British.

choices.text: John wanted to start a war, while Samuel tried to keep the peace.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because it's backwards — Samuel Adams wanted to make colonists angry and turn them against Britain, not keep the peace.

choices.text: John drew a famous picture, while Samuel wrote newspaper stories.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because it was Paul Revere, not John Adams, who made the famous engraving, and Samuel Adams wrote newspaper articles, not drew pictures.

choices.text: John fought in the streets, while Samuel stayed in the courtroom.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because neither Adams fought in the streets — John worked in the courtroom and Samuel worked through newspapers and speeches.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: Why did the author include the section "A Picture That Told a Different Story"?

choices.text: To explain how an image caused colonists to turn against British rule.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the section explains how Revere's engraving spread a powerful story that made colonists turn against British rule, which is exactly what the section is about.

choices.text: To describe how the British soldiers caused the crowd to become angry.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the section focuses on how the picture shaped people's opinions, not on what the soldiers did to make the crowd angry.

choices.text: To show why John Adams decided to defend the soldiers in court.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because John Adams and the trial are discussed in a separate section called 'A Surprising Lawyer,' not in this one.

choices.text: To tell how the colonists caused the soldiers to fire their muskets.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because even though the passage mentions the crowd throwing snowballs and ice, that detail is not the main reason the author included this section.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: Which list of events best tells the main story of the article in the order they happened?

choices.text: The king sent soldiers, a church bell rang, and a lawyer went to a trial.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because ringing a church bell and going to a trial are small details, not the main events that the whole article is about.

choices.text: Colonists threw snowballs, soldiers fired guns, and leaders spread a story.
choices.feedback: B is correct because it names the three most important events — snowballs, shooting, and spreading the story — in the exact order they happened in the passage.

choices.text: Leaders spread a story, colonists threw snowballs, and soldiers fired guns.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the order is backwards — leaders spread the story after the snowballs and shooting, not before.

choices.text: Soldiers attacked a crowd, colonists threw ice, and the king collected taxes.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the soldiers did not attack first; the colonists were the ones throwing ice and snowballs at the soldiers.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: Which statement best compares the real shooting on King Street with Paul Revere's picture of it?

choices.text: The real event was calm, but the picture made it look confusing.
choices.feedback: A is wrong because it flips the truth — the real event was the messy, chaotic one, not the picture.

choices.text: The real event was messy, but the picture made it look orderly.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage says the real scene was 'confused and messy,' while Revere's picture showed soldiers in a neat, calm line.

choices.text: The real event was small, but the picture made it look friendly.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the picture didn't make the event look friendly — it made the British soldiers look like they were attacking innocent people.

choices.text: The real event was fair, but the picture made it look unfair.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the passage never says the real event was fair — it describes it as chaotic and scary, not orderly or just.

syllabus_metadata.title: Shots on King Street: The Boston Massacre

assessments.title: Tea in the Harbor: The Boston Tea Party

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Boston Tea Party: A Protest in Disguise

Cold Water, Hot Anger

On the night of December 16, 1773, the water in Boston Harbor turned brown. About 150 men cracked open 342 wooden chests with hatchets and poured tea into the cold, dark sea.

You might think the name "Boston Tea Party" means people were celebrating. The name came later. That night was dangerous and illegal. The men who dumped the tea could have been arrested or even killed. They took the risk because they believed something important was worth fighting for.
stimulus.content_text:
The Boston Tea Party: A Protest in DisguiseCold Water, Hot Anger
On the night of December 16, 1773, the water in Boston Harbor turned brown. About 150 men cracked open 342 wooden chests with hatchets and poured tea into the cold, dark sea.

You might think the name "Boston Tea Party" means people were celebrating. The name came later. That night was dangerous and illegal. The men who dumped the tea could have been arrested or even killed. They took the risk because they believed something important was worth fighting for.

items.prompt: The author notes that the name "Boston Tea Party" sounds like a celebration. According to the passage, what was the night actually like instead?

choices.text: It was dangerous and illegal.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage uses those exact words — 'dangerous and illegal' — to describe what the night was really like.

choices.text: It was cold and very boring.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because while the water was cold, the passage never says the night was boring — in fact, it was very risky and serious.

choices.text: It was loud and very crowded.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage never mentions the night being loud or crowded, so we can't pick details that aren't there.

choices.text: It was safe and very peaceful.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage says the opposite — the men could have been arrested or killed, so it was definitely not safe or peaceful.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Why Tea Caused So Much Trouble

Tea was the most popular drink in the American colonies. People sipped it every day, the way many people drink coffee now. So when the British government passed the Tea Act of 1773, colonists paid close attention.

The Tea Act gave the British East India Company a monopoly on tea in the colonies. A monopoly means only one company is allowed to sell something — imagine if only one store in your whole town could sell food, and you had no other choice.

But the real anger went deeper than the price of tea. The tea tax was chosen by the British Parliament, the group of lawmakers who made decisions for Britain. Colonists had no voice in Parliament. No one there spoke for them. The colonists called this "taxation without representation" — being forced to pay taxes decided by people you never chose and cannot vote out. That felt deeply unfair.
stimulus.content_text:
Why Tea Caused So Much Trouble
Tea was the most popular drink in the American colonies. People sipped it every day, the way many people drink coffee now. So when the British government passed the Tea Act of 1773, colonists paid close attention.

The Tea Act gave the British East India Company a monopoly on tea in the colonies. A monopoly means only one company is allowed to sell something — imagine if only one store in your whole town could sell food, and you had no other choice.

But the real anger went deeper than the price of tea. The tea tax was chosen by the British Parliament, the group of lawmakers who made decisions for Britain. Colonists had no voice in Parliament. No one there spoke for them. The colonists called this "taxation without representation" — being forced to pay taxes decided by people you never chose and cannot vote out. That felt deeply unfair.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, what was the main reason the colonists were angry about the tea tax?

choices.text: They had to pay more money for tea than people in Britain did.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage never compares tea prices between colonists and people in Britain.

choices.text: They had no voice in choosing the lawmakers who created the tax.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly says colonists had no voice in Parliament and felt that 'taxation without representation' was deeply unfair.

choices.text: They were upset that only British people were allowed to drink tea.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage never says anything about who was allowed to drink tea.

choices.text: They did not want to buy tea from the British East India Company.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage says the 'real anger went deeper' than the monopoly — the bigger issue was having no say in the tax.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Showdown at the Harbor

In December 1773, three ships loaded with British tea sailed into Boston Harbor. Colonial leaders told the ships to turn around. The royal governor of Massachusetts refused. The colonists had to decide what to do next.

The men who boarded the ships that night painted their faces and dressed in Mohawk clothing. You might think they did this to trick the British into blaming Native Americans. But the disguises were a symbol. The colonists were saying, "We are not British anymore. We are something new." No actual Mohawk people took part, and many participants hid their real names for years.

The destroyed tea was worth about one million dollars in today's money. It was the boldest act of protest — a public action against something you believe is wrong — that the colonies had ever seen.
stimulus.content_text:
Showdown at the Harbor
In December 1773, three ships loaded with British tea sailed into Boston Harbor. Colonial leaders told the ships to turn around. The royal governor of Massachusetts refused. The colonists had to decide what to do next.

The men who boarded the ships that night painted their faces and dressed in Mohawk clothing. You might think they did this to trick the British into blaming Native Americans. But the disguises were a symbol. The colonists were saying, "We are not British anymore. We are something new." No actual Mohawk people took part, and many participants hid their real names for years.

The destroyed tea was worth about one million dollars in today's money. It was the boldest act of protest — a public action against something you believe is wrong — that the colonies had ever seen.

items.prompt: According to the text, what happened right after colonial leaders told the ships to turn around?

choices.text: The men painted their faces and boarded the ships.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the men painting their faces and boarding the ships happened later that night, after the governor refused.

choices.text: The royal governor refused to send the ships away.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage clearly states that after colonial leaders told the ships to leave, the royal governor refused to send them away.

choices.text: Three ships loaded with tea sailed into the harbor.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the ships sailing into the harbor was the very first event in the story, before the leaders said anything.

choices.text: The colonists destroyed one million dollars of tea.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the tea was destroyed much later, after the governor refused and the colonists decided what to do.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Britain Strikes Back

King George III was furious. He wanted to punish Massachusetts so harshly that no other colony would dare resist. Parliament passed a set of laws the colonists called the Intolerable Acts — meaning laws so unfair they could not be endured. These laws closed Boston Harbor completely. No ships could enter or leave. Trade stopped. Parliament also banned most town meetings, gave the royal governor more power, and forced colonists to house British soldiers in their own homes.

The British expected fear. They got the opposite. Colonies up and down the coast sent food and supplies to Boston. People who had never cared about politics suddenly chose sides. Instead of dividing the colonists, the punishment pulled them together. Leaders from twelve colonies sent representatives to a great meeting called the First Continental Congress — a gathering to decide, together, how to respond to Britain.
stimulus.content_text:
Britain Strikes Back
King George III was furious. He wanted to punish Massachusetts so harshly that no other colony would dare resist. Parliament passed a set of laws the colonists called the Intolerable Acts — meaning laws so unfair they could not be endured. These laws closed Boston Harbor completely. No ships could enter or leave. Trade stopped. Parliament also banned most town meetings, gave the royal governor more power, and forced colonists to house British soldiers in their own homes.

The British expected fear. They got the opposite. Colonies up and down the coast sent food and supplies to Boston. People who had never cared about politics suddenly chose sides. Instead of dividing the colonists, the punishment pulled them together. Leaders from twelve colonies sent representatives to a great meeting called the First Continental Congress — a gathering to decide, together, how to respond to Britain.

items.prompt:
Read these two sentences from the text:

"The British expected fear. They got the opposite."

How are these two sentences connected?

choices.text: They compare what the British expected with what happened.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the first sentence tells us what the British expected (fear), and the second sentence tells us the opposite actually happened — the colonists united instead of giving up.

choices.text: They explain the reasons the king punished the colonists.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because these two sentences don't explain why the king punished the colonists — that information is found earlier in the passage.

choices.text: They list the unfair new rules the colonists had to follow.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the unfair rules like closing Boston Harbor are listed in a different part of the passage, not in these two sentences.

choices.text: They describe the ways the colonies shared food and supplies.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because sharing food and supplies is mentioned later in the passage, not in these two short sentences.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
One Night, One Message

Those 342 chests of tea sinking into Boston Harbor carried more than dried leaves. They carried an idea: no one should have to obey rules they had no part in making. That principle — a core belief that guides your choices — still echoes every time someone says that government should listen to the people it governs.
stimulus.content_text:
One Night, One Message
Those 342 chests of tea sinking into Boston Harbor carried more than dried leaves. They carried an idea: no one should have to obey rules they had no part in making. That principle — a core belief that guides your choices — still echoes every time someone says that government should listen to the people it governs.

items.prompt: According to the text, what is a principle?

choices.text: a rule that the government makes for people
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage says a principle is a core belief that guides your choices, not a rule made by the government.

choices.text: a core belief that guides your choices
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly defines principle as 'a core belief that guides your choices' right in the text.

choices.text: a message that is sent across the ocean
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage never connects the word principle to a message being sent across the ocean.

choices.text: a person who leads a group of people
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because a principle is a belief, not a person — you might be thinking of a school principal, which is a different word!

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: The British passed the Intolerable Acts to make the colonists afraid, but instead the colonists responded by doing what?

choices.text: hiding their tea so the British could not find it
choices.feedback: Hiding tea is never mentioned anywhere in the passage — that didn't happen.

choices.text: working together and sending help to Boston
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says other colonies sent food and supplies to Boston, and twelve colonies met together at the First Continental Congress to decide how to respond.

choices.text: agreeing to follow the new British laws quietly
choices.feedback: This is wrong because the colonists did the opposite — they refused to back down and came together to fight back.

choices.text: dressing up as Mohawk people to fool the British
choices.feedback: This is wrong because the Mohawk disguises happened on the night of the Tea Party, which was before the Intolerable Acts were passed.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: To find out what happened as a result of the Boston Tea Party, a student looks under the heading "Britain Strikes Back." Why is this the correct heading to use?

choices.text: It explains how the destroyed tea caused the king to close the Boston Harbor.
choices.feedback: A is correct because 'Britain Strikes Back' describes what King George III did in response to the Tea Party, including closing Boston Harbor — that is the result the question is asking about.

choices.text: It explains how the unfair taxes caused the colonists to feel very deep anger.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the colonists' deep anger about unfair taxes happened before the Tea Party, not as a result of it.

choices.text: It explains how the governor's refusal caused the men to board the British ships.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the governor's refusal and the men boarding the ships are events that led up to the Tea Party, not consequences that came after it.

choices.text: It explains how the bold protest caused people today to want a fair government.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because people's beliefs about fair government today are a long-term legacy, not an immediate result described under 'Britain Strikes Back.'

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: Which summary best tells the main story of the article from beginning to end?

choices.text: First, Britain taxed tea; next, colonists dumped it; finally, the colonies united.
choices.feedback: A is correct because it matches the article's order: Britain taxed tea first, colonists dumped it next, and the colonies united at the end.

choices.text: First, the colonies united; next, colonists dumped tea; finally, Britain taxed it.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because it puts the events in backwards order — the colonies uniting happened last, not first.

choices.text: First, colonists drank tea; next, Mohawk people dumped it; finally, the ships left.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the article clearly says no actual Mohawk people took part in dumping the tea.

choices.text: First, the king became angry; next, the harbor closed; finally, soldiers moved in.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because it only covers what happened after the tea was dumped, leaving out the most important parts of the story.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: In the article "Tea in the Harbor: The Boston Tea Party," how does the author compare the colonists who protested to people living today?

choices.text: Both feel that leaders must listen to the people they govern.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the author directly connects the colonists' belief — that government should listen to the people — to people living today, saying that idea 'still echoes' in our world.

choices.text: Both think that drinking hot tea is better than drinking coffee.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the article only uses coffee as a comparison to show how popular tea was back then, not to say people today prefer tea over coffee.

choices.text: Both wear special disguises to hide their names from lawmakers.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because wearing disguises was something only the colonists did that night in 1773, and the article never says people today hide their names from lawmakers.

choices.text: Both want to start a brand new country with their own new rules.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the article focuses on the colonists protesting unfair taxation, not on a shared goal between colonists and people today of starting a new country.

syllabus_metadata.title: Tea in the Harbor: The Boston Tea Party

assessments.title: Spreading the Word: Committees of Correspondence

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Letters That Linked a Revolution

A Rider in the Dark

A man on horseback gallops down a muddy road in 1772, a sealed letter tucked inside his coat. The letter holds urgent news from Boston about British soldiers and unfair taxes. By the time he reaches Virginia, the news will already be weeks old. This was life in colonial America. There were no phones, no computers, no way to send a quick message. Important news crawled from colony to colony on horseback, by ship, or simply by people talking to each other.

The thirteen colonies needed a faster way to share what they knew.
stimulus.content_text:
Letters That Linked a RevolutionA Rider in the Dark
A man on horseback gallops down a muddy road in 1772, a sealed letter tucked inside his coat. The letter holds urgent news from Boston about British soldiers and unfair taxes. By the time he reaches Virginia, the news will already be weeks old. This was life in colonial America. There were no phones, no computers, no way to send a quick message. Important news crawled from colony to colony on horseback, by ship, or simply by people talking to each other.
The thirteen colonies needed a faster way to share what they knew.

items.prompt: According to the text, even though the letter held urgent news, what was true by the time it reached Virginia?

choices.text: It was dropped in the mud.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage never says the letter was dropped in the mud — it stayed tucked safely inside the rider's coat.

choices.text: It was already weeks old.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage clearly states that by the time the rider reached Virginia, the news would already be weeks old.

choices.text: It was taken by the soldiers.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage never mentions soldiers taking the letter — that detail does not appear anywhere in the story.

choices.text: It was placed on a ship.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage says the letter was carried by a man on horseback, not placed on a ship.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
A Web of Letters

You might think the colonies already acted like one team. Actually, they did not. A farmer in Virginia had little reason to care about a tax problem in Massachusetts. Most colonists thought of themselves as Virginians or New Yorkers, not as Americans.

In 1772, a Boston leader named Samuel Adams helped create one of the first Committees of Correspondence — groups of trusted citizens who wrote letters to similar groups in other colonies, like a chain of friends passing notes across a giant classroom. Each committee described what the British were doing in their area. They reported unfair actions. They planned how to respond together. When people read letters about faraway colonies, they began to see a pattern. The same unfair treatment was showing up everywhere. The committees changed that.
stimulus.content_text:
A Web of Letters
