You might think the colonies already acted like one team. Actually, they did not. A farmer in Virginia had little reason to care about a tax problem in Massachusetts. Most colonists thought of themselves as Virginians or New Yorkers, not as Americans.
In 1772, a Boston leader named Samuel Adams helped create one of the first Committees of Correspondence — groups of trusted citizens who wrote letters to similar groups in other colonies, like a chain of friends passing notes across a giant classroom. Each committee described what the British were doing in their area. They reported unfair actions. They planned how to respond together. When people read letters about faraway colonies, they began to see a pattern. The same unfair treatment was showing up everywhere. The committees changed that.

items.prompt: Which statement best explains the main idea of the section "A Web of Letters"?

choices.text: Reading letters from other colonies caused the people to see a pattern of unfairness.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage directly says that reading letters helped colonists see a pattern of unfair treatment happening across all the colonies, which is the main point of the whole section.

choices.text: Having a tax problem in Massachusetts caused the farmers in Virginia to become angry.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the passage says the opposite — a Virginia farmer had little reason to care about a tax problem in Massachusetts.

choices.text: Passing written notes in a giant classroom caused the trusted citizens to be friends.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the 'giant classroom' was just a comparison used to explain how letters were passed around, not something that actually happened.

choices.text: Creating a new committee in the city of Boston caused Samuel Adams to become a leader.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because Samuel Adams was already described as a Boston leader before he helped create the committee, so the committee did not cause him to become a leader.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Not Just Famous Names

It's easy to imagine that only famous leaders ran these committees. But ordinary people also sat down with ink and paper to keep their neighbors informed. Adams was passionate about standing up to British tyranny — a word that means a government using its power in cruel and unfair ways, like a bully who makes all the rules. He knew that sharing news quickly would help colonists organize better protests. Everyday citizens shared that same goal.
stimulus.content_text:
Not Just Famous Names
It's easy to imagine that only famous leaders ran these committees. But ordinary people also sat down with ink and paper to keep their neighbors informed. Adams was passionate about standing up to British tyranny — a word that means a government using its power in cruel and unfair ways, like a bully who makes all the rules. He knew that sharing news quickly would help colonists organize better protests. Everyday citizens shared that same goal.

items.prompt: According to the section "Not Just Famous Names" in "Spreading the Word: Committees of Correspondence," what happened first?

choices.text: Neighbors were kept informed.
choices.feedback: Keeping neighbors informed (A) happened after people sat down to write, so it came second, not first.

choices.text: People sat down with ink and paper.
choices.feedback: This is correct! Sitting down with ink and paper was the very first step — people had to write before they could share any news.

choices.text: Colonists organized better protests.
choices.feedback: Organizing better protests (C) happened last, after the news was already written and shared with neighbors.

choices.text: British leaders changed the rules.
choices.feedback: The passage never says British leaders changed any rules, so this answer doesn't fit the story at all.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
From Letters to Action

The idea spread fast. By 1774, nearly every colony had its own committee. Letters traveled up and down the coast from Massachusetts to Georgia. But the committees did more than share news. They organized boycotts — agreements to stop buying British goods, the way you might refuse to visit a store that treated your friend badly. They also planned protests together.

When Britain passed the Intolerable Acts, the committees proved their worth. These harsh laws punished Massachusetts for the Boston Tea Party by shutting down Boston's port and taking away many of the colony's rights. "Intolerable" means impossible to put up with — and that is exactly how the colonists felt. The Committees of Correspondence spread the word. Other colonies sent food, money, and supplies to help Boston survive. Without the letter network, Boston might have faced that punishment alone.
stimulus.content_text:
From Letters to Action
The idea spread fast. By 1774, nearly every colony had its own committee. Letters traveled up and down the coast from Massachusetts to Georgia. But the committees did more than share news. They organized boycotts — agreements to stop buying British goods, the way you might refuse to visit a store that treated your friend badly. They also planned protests together.
When Britain passed the Intolerable Acts, the committees proved their worth. These harsh laws punished Massachusetts for the Boston Tea Party by shutting down Boston's port and taking away many of the colony's rights. "Intolerable" means impossible to put up with — and that is exactly how the colonists felt. The Committees of Correspondence spread the word. Other colonies sent food, money, and supplies to help Boston survive. Without the letter network, Boston might have faced that punishment alone.

items.prompt: The author compares a boycott to refusing to visit a store that treated your friend badly. Based on this comparison, what is a boycott?

choices.text: Agreeing to stop buying things from someone.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage says boycotts are agreements to stop buying British goods, and the store comparison shows that refusing to visit means stopping your purchases.

choices.text: Agreeing to write angry letters to a business.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the passage never says boycotts involve writing angry letters — that's what the Committees of Correspondence did.

choices.text: Agreeing to punish a friend who behaved badly.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because a boycott is about refusing to buy from someone, not about punishing a friend.

choices.text: Agreeing to send food and supplies to Boston.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because sending food and supplies to Boston was how other colonies helped Massachusetts, which is a different action described later in the passage.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Thinking Like Americans

If Britain could punish Massachusetts, it could punish any colony. Your colony could be next. That fear pulled people together. Slowly, colonists stopped thinking of themselves as just Virginians or Pennsylvanians. They started thinking of themselves as Americans.

This did not happen overnight. Years of writing, reading, and organizing built the connections that made the Revolution possible. The committees eventually led to the Continental Congress — a meeting where leaders from the colonies gathered to make decisions as one group. That Congress would one day declare independence from Britain.

All of it started with people picking up a pen. Today you can text a friend across the country in seconds. In the 1770s, a handwritten letter riding in a saddlebag did the same work — just slower, and with much higher stakes.
stimulus.content_text:
Thinking Like Americans
If Britain could punish Massachusetts, it could punish any colony. Your colony could be next. That fear pulled people together. Slowly, colonists stopped thinking of themselves as just Virginians or Pennsylvanians. They started thinking of themselves as Americans.
This did not happen overnight. Years of writing, reading, and organizing built the connections that made the Revolution possible. The committees eventually led to the Continental Congress — a meeting where leaders from the colonies gathered to make decisions as one group. That Congress would one day declare independence from Britain.
All of it started with people picking up a pen. Today you can text a friend across the country in seconds. In the 1770s, a handwritten letter riding in a saddlebag did the same work — just slower, and with much higher stakes.

items.prompt: According to the text, what is the Continental Congress?

choices.text: a letter sent between colonies to share news
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because a letter sent between colonies describes how colonists shared news, not what the Continental Congress was.

choices.text: a meeting where colonial leaders made decisions together
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly says the Continental Congress was 'a meeting where leaders from the colonies gathered to make decisions as one group.'

choices.text: a group that punished colonies for breaking rules
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage says Britain was the one punishing colonies, not the Continental Congress.

choices.text: a committee that delivered messages on horseback
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because delivering messages on horseback describes how letters were carried, not what the Continental Congress was.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: Based on the article, how did the colonists' view of themselves change after the committees were formed?

choices.text: They stopped caring about their own towns and started worrying only about Boston.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because colonists didn't stop caring about their own towns — the committees helped them care about both their own colony and others at the same time.

choices.text: They stopped feeling like just Virginians and started feeling like Americans.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the article directly says colonists stopped thinking of themselves as just Virginians or Pennsylvanians and started thinking of themselves as Americans after the committees connected them.

choices.text: They stopped trusting their neighbors and started trusting only famous leaders.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the article says ordinary people helped run the committees alongside famous leaders, so trust in neighbors actually grew.

choices.text: They stopped acting like one big team and started acting like separate groups.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because it describes the opposite of what happened — the committees brought colonies together as one team instead of keeping them apart.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: Which heading explains the problem that caused the colonists to create the letter network?

choices.text: "A Rider in the Dark"
choices.feedback: A is correct because this section describes the problem — news traveled too slowly — and ends with the colonies needing a faster way to share information, which is what led to the letter network.

choices.text: "A Web of Letters"
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because 'A Web of Letters' explains how the Committees of Correspondence worked, not the problem that made them necessary.

choices.text: "Not Just Famous Names"
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because 'Not Just Famous Names' focuses on who participated in the committees, not the problem that caused them to be created.

choices.text: "From Letters to Action"
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because 'From Letters to Action' describes what the committees did and achieved, which happened after the network was already set up.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: Which choice best tells the order of key details that supports the article's main idea?

choices.text: Colonists met in Congress, wrote letters, and then saw they were Americans.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the article shows colonists wrote letters first, and the Continental Congress came much later, near the end of the story.

choices.text: Colonists shared letters, worked together, and then felt more like Americans.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the article shows colonists shared letters first, then worked together to help each other, and finally began to feel like Americans — in that exact order.

choices.text: Colonists felt like Americans, shared letters, and then learned about unfair taxes.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because feeling like Americans was the last thing that happened, not the first.

choices.text: Colonists stopped British taxes, sent letters, and then formed committees.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the colonists did not stop British taxes, and committees were formed before letters were widely shared across all colonies.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: Based on the article, how does the author compare sending messages today to sending messages in the 1770s?

choices.text: Both methods share information, but messages in the 1770s traveled much slower.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the author directly compares texting today to letters in the 1770s, saying both share information but the old way was much slower.

choices.text: Both methods use technology, but messages in the 1770s only went to famous leaders.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the 1770s method didn't use technology, and the article clearly says ordinary people — not just famous leaders — wrote and read the letters.

choices.text: Both methods rely on horses, but messages today travel across the entire country.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the article says we use texting today, not horses, so horses are only part of the old method.

choices.text: Both methods organize protests, but messages in the 1770s caused the unfair taxes.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the letters helped colonists organize against unfair treatment — they didn't cause the unfair taxes.

syllabus_metadata.title: Spreading the Word: Committees of Correspondence

assessments.title: Coming Together: The First Continental Congress

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Road to Revolution

A City Under Watch

British soldiers marched through the streets of Boston in the summer of 1774. No ships could enter or leave. Town meetings — where neighbors had gathered to solve problems for years — were now against the law.

The British government had passed harsh new laws to punish Massachusetts for the Boston Tea Party. Colonists called these the Intolerable Acts. The word intolerable means "impossible to put up with." The port of Boston was shut down. Local leaders lost the power to make decisions. British soldiers could move into people's homes. These punishments scared colonists in every colony. If Britain could do this to Massachusetts, it could do this to anyone.
stimulus.content_text:
The Road to RevolutionA City Under Watch
British soldiers marched through the streets of Boston in the summer of 1774. No ships could enter or leave. Town meetings — where neighbors had gathered to solve problems for years — were now against the law.

The British government had passed harsh new laws to punish Massachusetts for the Boston Tea Party. Colonists called these the Intolerable Acts. The word intolerable means "impossible to put up with." The port of Boston was shut down. Local leaders lost the power to make decisions. British soldiers could move into people's homes. These punishments scared colonists in every colony. If Britain could do this to Massachusetts, it could do this to anyone.

items.prompt: According to the section 'A City Under Watch,' neighbors used to gather at town meetings to solve problems, but what was true about these meetings in the summer of 1774?

choices.text: They were held in people's homes.
choices.feedback: The passage never says town meetings were moved to people's homes — that detail isn't in the story at all.

choices.text: They were made against the law.
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says town meetings 'were now against the law,' which means they were not allowed anymore.

choices.text: They were moved to other colonies.
choices.feedback: The passage doesn't say town meetings were moved to other colonies — there's no evidence for that in the story.

choices.text: They were run by British soldiers.
choices.feedback: The passage never says British soldiers ran the town meetings — in fact, the meetings were banned, not taken over.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Twelve Colonies, One Room

One colony alone could not stand up to the mighty British Empire. So in September 1774, delegates — people chosen to speak for a larger group, like a class representative at a school meeting — traveled to Philadelphia. They gathered inside Carpenter's Hall for the First Continental Congress. Twelve of the thirteen colonies sent delegates. Georgia did not attend, though it supported the cause.

Actually, they argued — a lot. A rice planter from South Carolina lived a very different life than a shipbuilder from Massachusetts. Some wanted to take a strong stand against Britain right away. Others hoped for a peaceful solution. But they all believed the British government had gone too far.

Famous leaders like George Washington, John Adams, Samuel Adams, Patrick Henry, and John Jay were among the delegates. This was the first time so many colonies had come together to face a shared problem.
stimulus.content_text:
Twelve Colonies, One Room
One colony alone could not stand up to the mighty British Empire. So in September 1774, delegates — people chosen to speak for a larger group, like a class representative at a school meeting — traveled to Philadelphia. They gathered inside Carpenter's Hall for the First Continental Congress. Twelve of the thirteen colonies sent delegates. Georgia did not attend, though it supported the cause.

Actually, they argued — a lot. A rice planter from South Carolina lived a very different life than a shipbuilder from Massachusetts. Some wanted to take a strong stand against Britain right away. Others hoped for a peaceful solution. But they all believed the British government had gone too far.

Famous leaders like George Washington, John Adams, Samuel Adams, Patrick Henry, and John Jay were among the delegates. This was the first time so many colonies had come together to face a shared problem.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, why did delegates from twelve colonies travel to Philadelphia in 1774?

choices.text: because they wanted to start a war with Britain right away
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says some delegates wanted peace, not that everyone wanted to start a war right away.

choices.text: because one colony alone could not stand up to Britain
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage says 'one colony alone could not stand up to the mighty British Empire,' which is exactly why they came together in Philadelphia.

choices.text: because Georgia asked the other colonies to meet there
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because Georgia didn't even attend the meeting, so it couldn't have asked the others to gather there.

choices.text: because George Washington invited them to Carpenter's Hall
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never mentions George Washington inviting anyone — the colonies came together on their own to face a shared problem.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
A Plan Without Weapons

The Congress finally agreed on a plan. The colonies would boycott British goods — refuse to buy them, the way you might stop going to a store that treated you unfairly. They also stopped exporting products to Britain, which means they stopped sending American goods across the ocean.

This would hurt everyone. Colonial merchants would lose money. British merchants would lose customers. But the colonists hoped the pressure would force Parliament to repeal the Intolerable Acts — to cancel them, the way you might erase a mistake.

The Congress also sent a petition to King George III. A petition is a formal, written request asking someone in charge to fix a problem. The petition was polite but firm. Here is the surprising part: the colonists did not want independence. Not yet. They still thought of themselves as loyal British subjects. They simply wanted the king to take back the unjust laws.
stimulus.content_text:
A Plan Without Weapons
The Congress finally agreed on a plan. The colonies would boycott British goods — refuse to buy them, the way you might stop going to a store that treated you unfairly. They also stopped exporting products to Britain, which means they stopped sending American goods across the ocean.

This would hurt everyone. Colonial merchants would lose money. British merchants would lose customers. But the colonists hoped the pressure would force Parliament to repeal the Intolerable Acts — to cancel them, the way you might erase a mistake.

The Congress also sent a petition to King George III. A petition is a formal, written request asking someone in charge to fix a problem. The petition was polite but firm. Here is the surprising part: the colonists did not want independence. Not yet. They still thought of themselves as loyal British subjects. They simply wanted the king to take back the unjust laws.

items.prompt: According to the text, what did the colonists do after they agreed to stop buying and selling British goods?

choices.text: They asked the king to cancel the unfair laws.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage says the Congress sent a petition to King George III asking him to repeal, or cancel, the unfair laws.

choices.text: They decided they wanted to be independent.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the passage clearly states the colonists did NOT want independence yet and still saw themselves as loyal British subjects.

choices.text: They started selling more goods across the ocean.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the colonists actually stopped exporting, which means they stopped sending goods across the ocean.

choices.text: They went back to buying things from Britain.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the colonists' whole plan was to boycott, or refuse to buy, British goods — the opposite of going back to buying them.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Hope Fades

Before leaving Philadelphia, the delegates made one more decision. If the king did not address their grievances — their complaints about unfair treatment — they would meet again in May 1775.

They hoped the king would listen. He did not. King George III and Parliament ignored the petition. British troops stayed in Boston. Tensions kept rising.

By the time the delegates gathered again in May 1775, shots had already been fired at Lexington and Concord. The Second Continental Congress now faced a much harder question: not how to make peace with Britain, but how to fight a war against it.
stimulus.content_text:
Hope Fades
Before leaving Philadelphia, the delegates made one more decision. If the king did not address their grievances — their complaints about unfair treatment — they would meet again in May 1775.

They hoped the king would listen. He did not. King George III and Parliament ignored the petition. British troops stayed in Boston. Tensions kept rising.

By the time the delegates gathered again in May 1775, shots had already been fired at Lexington and Concord. The Second Continental Congress now faced a much harder question: not how to make peace with Britain, but how to fight a war against it.

items.prompt: The text states, 'They hoped the king would listen. He did not. King George III and Parliament ignored the petition.' Based on this detail, how was Parliament similar to King George III?

choices.text: Both refused to listen to the delegates.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage directly says both King George III and Parliament ignored the petition, meaning neither one listened to the delegates.

choices.text: Both decided to keep the troops in Boston.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the passage doesn't say Parliament made the decision to keep troops in Boston — it only says the troops stayed there.

choices.text: Both wanted to make peace with the delegates.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because ignoring the petition shows they did NOT want to make peace with the delegates.

choices.text: Both fired the first shots during the war.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the shots at Lexington and Concord were fired during a battle, not by the king or Parliament.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Stronger Together

Twelve colonies with different lives and different opinions sat in one room and chose to act as one. The First Continental Congress did not fire a single shot. It wrote letters and made promises. But those promises proved something powerful: when the colonies worked together, even the British Empire had to pay attention. The people who argued in Carpenter's Hall in 1774 would soon lead a revolution.
stimulus.content_text:
Stronger Together
Twelve colonies with different lives and different opinions sat in one room and chose to act as one. The First Continental Congress did not fire a single shot. It wrote letters and made promises. But those promises proved something powerful: when the colonies worked together, even the British Empire had to pay attention. The people who argued in Carpenter's Hall in 1774 would soon lead a revolution.

items.prompt: According to the text, what was the First Continental Congress?

choices.text: A group of twelve colonies acting as one
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says twelve colonies chose to act as one, which is exactly what the First Continental Congress was.

choices.text: An army of soldiers firing their weapons
choices.feedback: This is wrong because the passage clearly says the First Continental Congress 'did not fire a single shot' — they wrote letters instead of fighting.

choices.text: A team of builders at Carpenter's Hall
choices.feedback: This is wrong because Carpenter's Hall was just the building where they met, not what the group was — they were colony leaders, not builders.

choices.text: A group of leaders from the British Empire
choices.feedback: This is wrong because the passage says the colonies were standing up to the British Empire, not leading it — they were on opposite sides!

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: Based on the article, how was the First Continental Congress different from the Second Continental Congress?

choices.text: The First Congress wanted to fight a war, but the Second Congress wanted to make peace.
choices.feedback: A is backwards — the First Congress wanted peace, and the Second Congress had to deal with war, not the other way around.

choices.text: The First Congress tried to make peace, but the Second Congress had to plan for a war.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the article shows the First Congress sent a peaceful petition to the king, but by the Second Congress, battles had already started and they had to plan for war.

choices.text: The First Congress created new laws, but the Second Congress decided to cancel them.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because neither Congress made or canceled laws — the article says the colonists wanted King George III and Parliament to repeal the Intolerable Acts.

choices.text: The First Congress met in Massachusetts, but the Second Congress met in Pennsylvania.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the article says both the First and Second Continental Congress met in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, not in Massachusetts.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: The author organizes the article to show how one event caused the next. Which sentence best describes how the events in the article are connected?

choices.text: Britain passed harsh laws, which caused the colonies to work together on a plan.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage clearly shows that Britain's Intolerable Acts scared the colonies into working together and making a boycott plan — one event caused the other.

choices.text: The colonies wrote a letter, which caused the king to cancel the harsh laws.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the passage tells us King George III ignored the petition and did NOT repeal the harsh laws.

choices.text: The colonies stopped buying goods, which caused Britain to shut the Boston port.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because it has the order backwards — Britain shut down Boston's port first, and the colonies' boycott came after that.

choices.text: Britain sent soldiers to Boston, which caused the colonies to want a new leader.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the colonists did not want a new leader; the passage says they still thought of themselves as loyal British subjects who just wanted unfair laws removed.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: Which list recounts the most important events of the article in the correct order?

choices.text: Soldiers march in Boston, delegates argue in a hall, and merchants lose their money.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because merchants losing money is just a small detail, not one of the most important events the article is about.

choices.text: Britain passes harsh laws, colonies meet to make a plan, and the king ignores the plan.
choices.feedback: B is correct because it lists the three biggest events — harsh laws, the colonies' plan, and the king ignoring them — in the exact order they happened in the article.

choices.text: Colonies meet to make a plan, Britain passes harsh laws, and the king ignores the plan.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because it has the order backwards — the colonies could only meet to make a plan after Britain had already passed the harsh laws.

choices.text: The king ignores the colonies, Britain passes harsh laws, and colonies meet to make a plan.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the king ignoring the colonies happened at the end of the story, not at the beginning.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: How were the colonists in the section 'A City Under Watch' similar to the delegates in the section 'Twelve Colonies, One Room'?

choices.text: Both groups wanted to fight a war against Britain right away.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the delegates did NOT all want to fight right away — some wanted a peaceful solution, and the colonists in Boston were being punished, not planning a war.

choices.text: Both groups believed the British government had been unfair.
choices.feedback: B is correct because both the colonists in Boston and the delegates in Philadelphia shared the belief that the British government had treated them unfairly.

choices.text: Both groups decided to stop buying goods from Britain.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the boycott of British goods was decided by the delegates at the Congress, not by the colonists described in 'A City Under Watch.'

choices.text: Both groups sent letters to King George III for help.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because sending a petition to King George III was something only the Congress did, and it happened later — it is not mentioned in 'A City Under Watch' at all.

syllabus_metadata.title: Coming Together: The First Continental Congress

assessments.title: We Hold These Truths: The Declaration of Independence

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The American Revolution

A Young Writer in a Rented Room

In the summer of 1776, a thirty-three-year-old man from Virginia sat alone in a rented room in Philadelphia. Thomas Jefferson dipped his pen in ink and began to write. His job was enormous: explain to the whole world why the American colonies were breaking free from Britain.

The fighting had already started more than a year earlier. Soldiers had already died at battles in Lexington, Concord, and Bunker Hill. July 4, 1776, was not the start of the war. It was the day the colonies finally said out loud, on paper, that they were done being ruled by a king.
stimulus.content_text:
The American RevolutionA Young Writer in a Rented Room
In the summer of 1776, a thirty-three-year-old man from Virginia sat alone in a rented room in Philadelphia. Thomas Jefferson dipped his pen in ink and began to write. His job was enormous: explain to the whole world why the American colonies were breaking free from Britain.

The fighting had already started more than a year earlier. Soldiers had already died at battles in Lexington, Concord, and Bunker Hill. July 4, 1776, was not the start of the war. It was the day the colonies finally said out loud, on paper, that they were done being ruled by a king.

items.prompt: According to the text, July 4, 1776, was not the start of the war, but instead it was the day the colonies did what?

choices.text: They fought a major battle at Bunker Hill.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage says the Battle of Bunker Hill happened before July 4, 1776, not on that day.

choices.text: They began to fight against the British army.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the passage clearly says the fighting had already started more than a year before July 4, 1776.

choices.text: They said they were done with the king.
choices.feedback: C is correct because the passage says July 4th was the day the colonies 'said out loud, on paper, that they were done being ruled by a king.'

choices.text: They rented a private room in Philadelphia.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because Thomas Jefferson rented the room to write, but that is not what July 4, 1776 is remembered for.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Colonies, Kings, and Complaints

To understand the Declaration, you need to know what a colony is. A colony is a place controlled by a faraway country. For over a hundred years, Britain had ruled thirteen colonies along the eastern coast of America. The colonists followed British laws and paid British taxes. But they had no voice in the British government. They could not vote on those laws or taxes.

King George III made life harder. He taxed the colonists without their consent — their agreement or permission. Imagine someone taking coins from your piggy bank without asking. He also quartered soldiers in colonists' homes, forcing families to let British soldiers live and eat in their own houses. He blocked their trade and took away their right to self-government, the power of a community to make its own decisions. The colonists tried to solve these problems peacefully. The king refused to listen.
stimulus.content_text:
Colonies, Kings, and Complaints
To understand the Declaration, you need to know what a colony is. A colony is a place controlled by a faraway country. For over a hundred years, Britain had ruled thirteen colonies along the eastern coast of America. The colonists followed British laws and paid British taxes. But they had no voice in the British government. They could not vote on those laws or taxes.

King George III made life harder. He taxed the colonists without their consent — their agreement or permission. Imagine someone taking coins from your piggy bank without asking. He also quartered soldiers in colonists' homes, forcing families to let British soldiers live and eat in their own houses. He blocked their trade and took away their right to self-government, the power of a community to make its own decisions. The colonists tried to solve these problems peacefully. The king refused to listen.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, why did the colonists feel that King George III's taxes were unfair?

choices.text: because the taxes were higher than what people in Britain paid
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage never mentions how much people in Britain paid in taxes or compares it to what the colonists paid.

choices.text: because the colonists had not given their agreement or permission
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly says King George taxed the colonists without their 'consent — their agreement or permission,' meaning they never got to agree to the taxes.

choices.text: because the money from taxes was used to pay for British soldiers
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage does not say that tax money was used to pay for British soldiers.

choices.text: because the colonists had already voted against the tax laws
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage says the colonists had no vote at all — they could not vote on laws or taxes, so they never had a chance to vote against them.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Writing the Declaration

The Continental Congress — a group of leaders from all thirteen colonies who met to make big decisions together — chose Jefferson to write the Declaration. You might think he wrote every word by himself. Actually, he worked with a small committee that included Benjamin Franklin and John Adams. Jefferson wrote for about seventeen days, scratching out lines and adding new ones. Then Congress debated his draft and changed parts of it.

The Declaration says that "all men are created equal" and have unalienable rights — rights that can never be taken away, like a birthmark you are born with — to "life, liberty, and the pursuit of happiness." Jefferson argued that governments exist to protect these rights. When a government fails, the people can change it.

When the Declaration said "all men are created equal," it did not include everyone. Enslaved African Americans were not given these rights. The words pointed toward fairness, but the country had a long way to go.
stimulus.content_text:
Writing the Declaration
The Continental Congress — a group of leaders from all thirteen colonies who met to make big decisions together — chose Jefferson to write the Declaration. You might think he wrote every word by himself. Actually, he worked with a small committee that included Benjamin Franklin and John Adams. Jefferson wrote for about seventeen days, scratching out lines and adding new ones. Then Congress debated his draft and changed parts of it.

The Declaration says that "all men are created equal" and have unalienable rights — rights that can never be taken away, like a birthmark you are born with — to "life, liberty, and the pursuit of happiness." Jefferson argued that governments exist to protect these rights. When a government fails, the people can change it.

When the Declaration said "all men are created equal," it did not include everyone. Enslaved African Americans were not given these rights. The words pointed toward fairness, but the country had a long way to go.

items.prompt: According to the text, what happened right after Jefferson finished writing his draft of the Declaration?

choices.text: Jefferson chose Franklin and Adams to help him write it.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because Jefferson worked with Franklin and Adams before and during the writing, not after he finished his draft.

choices.text: Congress debated his draft and changed parts of it.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage clearly states that after Jefferson wrote his draft, Congress debated it and changed parts of it.

choices.text: The colonies decided to form the Continental Congress.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the Continental Congress was already meeting before Jefferson was even chosen to write the Declaration.

choices.text: Jefferson started scratching out lines and adding new ones.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because scratching out lines and adding new ones describes what Jefferson did while he was writing for seventeen days, not what happened after he finished.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Courage on Paper

On July 2, 1776, Congress voted for independence. Two days later, on July 4, they officially adopted the Declaration. That date became the birthday of the United States.

Signing was no celebration. It was an act of treason — the crime of turning against your own country. The punishment was death. Fifty-six men signed anyway. They were lawyers, merchants — people who buy and sell goods — farmers, and doctors. Every single one risked being hanged if the colonies lost the war. Benjamin Franklin reportedly joked, "We must, all hang together, or most assuredly we shall all hang separately."
stimulus.content_text:
Courage on Paper
On July 2, 1776, Congress voted for independence. Two days later, on July 4, they officially adopted the Declaration. That date became the birthday of the United States.

Signing was no celebration. It was an act of treason — the crime of turning against your own country. The punishment was death. Fifty-six men signed anyway. They were lawyers, merchants — people who buy and sell goods — farmers, and doctors. Every single one risked being hanged if the colonies lost the war. Benjamin Franklin reportedly joked, "We must, all hang together, or most assuredly we shall all hang separately."

items.prompt: According to the passage, what did the lawyers, merchants, farmers, and doctors who signed the Declaration have in common?

choices.text: They all bought and sold goods.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage says merchants are the ones who buy and sell goods — not the lawyers, farmers, and doctors who also signed.

choices.text: They all risked losing their lives.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage clearly states that every single person who signed risked being hanged if the colonies lost the war.

choices.text: They all expected a large celebration.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage says signing was 'no celebration' — it was a dangerous and serious act, not a party.

choices.text: They all voted on the exact same day.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage does not say all the signers voted on the same day — it only mentions when Congress voted for independence.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Words That Kept Working

Those fifty-six signatures did not just create a new country. Since 1776, people on every continent have borrowed those same words — "all men are created equal" — to demand their own freedom. The Declaration did not finish its work in one summer. It is still working now, every time someone stands up and says that fairness belongs to everyone.
stimulus.content_text:
Words That Kept Working
Those fifty-six signatures did not just create a new country. Since 1776, people on every continent have borrowed those same words — "all men are created equal" — to demand their own freedom. The Declaration did not finish its work in one summer. It is still working now, every time someone stands up and says that fairness belongs to everyone.

items.prompt: According to the text, what does the author mean by saying the Declaration 'is still working now'?

choices.text: People are still writing new copies of the document
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage never mentions anyone writing new copies of the Declaration.

choices.text: People still use its words to stand up for fairness
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage says the Declaration is 'still working now' every time someone uses its words to stand up for fairness.

choices.text: People are still adding more signatures to it
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage only mentions the original fifty-six signatures and says nothing about adding new ones.

choices.text: People still read it aloud every single summer
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage never says anything about people reading the Declaration aloud every summer.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: Based on the article, how was the idea of government in the Declaration different from how King George III ruled the colonies?

choices.text: The Declaration stated governments should protect rights, but the king took their rights away.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the Declaration said governments should protect people's rights, while King George III kept taking the colonists' rights away instead of protecting them.

choices.text: The Declaration stated governments should collect taxes, but the king stopped all their taxes.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the king did not stop all taxes — he actually taxed the colonists more, without their agreement.

choices.text: The Declaration stated governments should have kings, but the king wanted a new president.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the Declaration never said governments should have kings, and the king never asked for a president.

choices.text: The Declaration stated governments should start battles, but the king wanted a peaceful trade.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the article never says the Declaration wanted governments to start battles, and the king blocked trade rather than wanting peaceful trade.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: To find information about what caused the colonists to rebel and the effects of their actions on the world, which two headings should a reader use?

choices.text: "A Young Writer in a Rented Room" and "Writing the Declaration"
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because 'A Young Writer in a Rented Room' focuses on Jefferson sitting down to write, not on what caused the colonists to rebel.

choices.text: "Colonies, Kings, and Complaints" and "Words That Kept Working"
choices.feedback: B is correct because 'Colonies, Kings, and Complaints' explains the causes of the rebellion, and 'Words That Kept Working' shows how the Declaration affected people around the world.

choices.text: "Courage on Paper" and "A Young Writer in a Rented Room"
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because 'Courage on Paper' is about the bravery of signing the Declaration, not about causes or world effects.

choices.text: "Writing the Declaration" and "Colonies, Kings, and Complaints"
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because 'Writing the Declaration' explains what was written and who helped, but it doesn't cover the effects the Declaration had on the rest of the world.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: Which sequence of events best recounts the key details of the article?

choices.text: The king refused to listen, a committee wrote the Declaration, Congress adopted it, and people still use its words today.
choices.feedback: A is correct because it matches the order in the article: the king refused to listen, a committee wrote the Declaration, Congress adopted it, and its words are still used today.

choices.text: A committee wrote the Declaration, the king refused to listen, Congress adopted it, and people still use its words today.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because it says the committee wrote the Declaration first, but the king's refusal to listen actually came before the Declaration was written.

choices.text: The king refused to listen, Jefferson wrote the Declaration alone, the war started, and the king finally changed his mind.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because Jefferson did not write the Declaration alone — he worked with a committee — and the king never changed his mind.

choices.text: The colonists paid British taxes, soldiers lived in their homes, the king blocked their trade, and the colonists stayed peaceful.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because it only lists some of the colonists' complaints and skips the most important events in the article, like writing and adopting the Declaration.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: Based on the article, how were the soldiers at Lexington similar to the men who signed the Declaration?

choices.text: They both traveled to Britain to speak with the king.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the article never says the soldiers or the signers traveled to Britain to speak with King George III.

choices.text: They both risked their lives to break free from Britain.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the soldiers died in battle fighting Britain, and the signers risked being hanged for treason — both groups risked their lives to break free from British rule.

choices.text: They both helped Thomas Jefferson write the new document.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because only Thomas Jefferson and a small committee that included Benjamin Franklin and John Adams helped write the Declaration.

choices.text: They both worked as lawyers, merchants, and local doctors.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the article describes the signers as lawyers, merchants, farmers, and doctors — it says nothing like that about the soldiers at Lexington.

syllabus_metadata.title: We Hold These Truths: The Declaration of Independence

assessments.title: The Shot Heard Round the World: Lexington and Concord

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Shot Heard Round the World

A Midnight Warning

Two horses pounded through the darkness. It was not yet dawn on April 19, 1775. Paul Revere and William Dawes raced down the dirt roads of Massachusetts, banging on doors and shouting the same warning: British soldiers were coming.

You might think Paul Revere made this ride alone. Actually, he had help. Dawes took a different road so the warning would reach more towns. Revere was captured by a British patrol before he reached Concord, and a third rider named Samuel Prescott finished the job. Thanks to all three men, the colonists were ready when the British arrived.

The British planned to seize weapons and ammunition — supplies like gunpowder and bullets — that the colonists had been hiding in Concord. But why were colonists storing weapons in secret? For over ten years, Britain had been passing taxes and laws without asking the colonists what they thought. The colonists had no voice in the British government. They were British citizens fighting their own government — a frightening, dangerous choice.
stimulus.content_text:
The Shot Heard Round the WorldA Midnight Warning
Two horses pounded through the darkness. It was not yet dawn on April 19, 1775. Paul Revere and William Dawes raced down the dirt roads of Massachusetts, banging on doors and shouting the same warning: British soldiers were coming.
You might think Paul Revere made this ride alone. Actually, he had help. Dawes took a different road so the warning would reach more towns. Revere was captured by a British patrol before he reached Concord, and a third rider named Samuel Prescott finished the job. Thanks to all three men, the colonists were ready when the British arrived.
The British planned to seize weapons and ammunition — supplies like gunpowder and bullets — that the colonists had been hiding in Concord. But why were colonists storing weapons in secret? For over ten years, Britain had been passing taxes and laws without asking the colonists what they thought. The colonists had no voice in the British government. They were British citizens fighting their own government — a frightening, dangerous choice.

items.prompt: According to the text, what is true about Paul Revere's ride, even though people might think he rode alone?

choices.text: He had help from other riders.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage clearly states Revere had help from William Dawes and Samuel Prescott, who rode different roads to warn more towns.

choices.text: He reached Concord by himself.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the passage says Revere was captured by a British patrol before he reached Concord, so he did NOT get there by himself.

choices.text: He hid weapons from the soldiers.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because it was the colonists who hid weapons in Concord, not something Revere did during his ride.

choices.text: He captured the British patrol.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage says the British patrol captured Revere — it was the other way around!

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Lexington: The First Shots

As the sun rose, about 700 British soldiers marched into the small town of Lexington. Waiting on the town green stood just 77 militiamen — ordinary citizens who had trained to fight as soldiers, like volunteer firefighters who practice for emergencies. These men were called minutemen because they could be ready to fight at a minute's notice.

The British commander ordered them to drop their weapons and go home. The minutemen started to back away. Then a single shot rang out. Nobody knows who fired it. More shots followed. In minutes, eight colonists lay dead. Ten more were wounded. Only one British soldier was hurt. The British marched on toward Concord.

People later called that first shot "the shot heard round the world" because news of the fight spread far beyond Massachusetts. It told people everywhere that American colonists would use weapons to defend their rights.
stimulus.content_text:
Lexington: The First Shots
As the sun rose, about 700 British soldiers marched into the small town of Lexington. Waiting on the town green stood just 77 militiamen — ordinary citizens who had trained to fight as soldiers, like volunteer firefighters who practice for emergencies. These men were called minutemen because they could be ready to fight at a minute's notice.
The British commander ordered them to drop their weapons and go home. The minutemen started to back away. Then a single shot rang out. Nobody knows who fired it. More shots followed. In minutes, eight colonists lay dead. Ten more were wounded. Only one British soldier was hurt. The British marched on toward Concord.
People later called that first shot "the shot heard round the world" because news of the fight spread far beyond Massachusetts. It told people everywhere that American colonists would use weapons to defend their rights.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, why did people call the first shot 'the shot heard round the world'?

choices.text: because the gunshot was so loud that people far away could hear it
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage never says the gunshot was loud enough for faraway people to hear — 'heard round the world' is a way of saying the news traveled far, not the actual sound.

choices.text: because news of the fight spread and told people everywhere what happened
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly says the news spread far beyond Massachusetts and told people everywhere what the colonists were willing to do.

choices.text: because the British soldiers fired their weapons at the same time
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage describes one single mysterious shot, not many British soldiers firing their weapons together.

choices.text: because the shot was the last one fired in the battle at Lexington
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the famous shot was the first one fired in the battle, not the last.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Concord: The Colonists Fight Back

At Concord, the British searched the town but found almost nothing. The colonists had already moved most of the weapons. Meanwhile, hundreds of minutemen were pouring in from nearby towns. At the North Bridge in Concord, they fired on the British soldiers and forced them to retreat — to turn around and pull back, the way you might step away from a bee's nest.

The 18-mile march back to Boston turned into a nightmare for the British. Colonial fighters hid behind stone walls, barns, and trees. They fired at the red-coated soldiers as they passed. The British were easy to spot in their bright uniforms. By the time they stumbled into Boston, 273 British soldiers were dead or wounded. The colonists lost 95.
stimulus.content_text:
Concord: The Colonists Fight Back
At Concord, the British searched the town but found almost nothing. The colonists had already moved most of the weapons. Meanwhile, hundreds of minutemen were pouring in from nearby towns. At the North Bridge in Concord, they fired on the British soldiers and forced them to retreat — to turn around and pull back, the way you might step away from a bee's nest.
The 18-mile march back to Boston turned into a nightmare for the British. Colonial fighters hid behind stone walls, barns, and trees. They fired at the red-coated soldiers as they passed. The British were easy to spot in their bright uniforms. By the time they stumbled into Boston, 273 British soldiers were dead or wounded. The colonists lost 95.

items.prompt: According to the text, what did the colonists do before the British searched the town of Concord?

choices.text: They moved most of the weapons.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage clearly says 'The colonists had already moved most of the weapons' — the word 'already' tells us this happened before the British searched the town.

choices.text: They marched back toward Boston.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because it was the British soldiers who marched back to Boston, not the colonists.

choices.text: They fired at the North Bridge.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the firing at the North Bridge happened after the British searched the town, not before.

choices.text: They poured in from nearby towns.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the minutemen pouring in from nearby towns was happening during the search, not before it.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Farmers Who Changed Everything

The minutemen were not professional soldiers. They were farmers, shopkeepers, and craftsmen who grabbed their muskets — long guns that had to be loaded by hand after every shot — and ran toward danger. They proved that ordinary people could stand up to one of the most powerful armies on Earth.

News of the fighting raced through the thirteen colonies. Everywhere, people chose sides. There was no turning back. The American Revolution had begun.

A farmer wakes before sunrise, hears hoofbeats, grabs a heavy gun, and walks out to face trained soldiers. That kind of courage is the reason the United States exists at all.
stimulus.content_text:
Farmers Who Changed Everything
The minutemen were not professional soldiers. They were farmers, shopkeepers, and craftsmen who grabbed their muskets — long guns that had to be loaded by hand after every shot — and ran toward danger. They proved that ordinary people could stand up to one of the most powerful armies on Earth.
News of the fighting raced through the thirteen colonies. Everywhere, people chose sides. There was no turning back. The American Revolution had begun.
A farmer wakes before sunrise, hears hoofbeats, grabs a heavy gun, and walks out to face trained soldiers. That kind of courage is the reason the United States exists at all.

items.prompt: Based on the section "Farmers Who Changed Everything," how were the farmers, shopkeepers, and craftsmen similar?

choices.text: They all trained to be professional soldiers.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage clearly says the minutemen were NOT professional soldiers — they were regular people like farmers and shopkeepers.

choices.text: They all grabbed guns to face the danger.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage says they all 'grabbed their muskets and ran toward danger,' which means they all picked up guns to face the fight.

choices.text: They all rode horses to spread the news.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage says news raced through the colonies, but it doesn't say these farmers and shopkeepers rode horses to spread it.

choices.text: They all led the powerful British army.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the farmers and craftsmen stood up against the powerful army — they didn't lead it.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: Based on the text, how was the colonists' response to the British at Concord different from their response at Lexington?

choices.text: At Concord, they fought back and made the soldiers retreat.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage says the minutemen at Concord fired on the British and forced them to retreat, which is the opposite of what happened at Lexington.

choices.text: At Concord, they dropped their weapons and started to back away.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because backing away and dropping weapons is what the minutemen did at Lexington, not at Concord.

choices.text: At Concord, they rode fast horses to warn people in other towns.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because riding horses to warn people was done by Paul Revere, Dawes, and Prescott the night before the battles, not during the fight at Concord.

choices.text: At Concord, they hid their gunpowder and bullets from the army.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because hiding the weapons happened before the British arrived in Concord, not as a response to the soldiers during the battle.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: Why were the colonists able to fight back against the British soldiers at Concord and on the march to Boston?

choices.text: The colonists had trained as professional soldiers and wore uniforms for battle.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage clearly says the minutemen were ordinary citizens like farmers and shopkeepers, not professional soldiers, and there is no mention of them wearing uniforms.

choices.text: The midnight riders had warned the colonists and given them time to prepare.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the midnight riders warned the colonists ahead of time, giving them the chance to move their weapons and gather hundreds of fighters before the British arrived.

choices.text: The British soldiers had lost their weapons and needed to retreat quickly.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the British soldiers kept their weapons throughout the battle — it was the colonists' hidden supplies that had been moved away before the British could find them.

choices.text: The colonists had built forts along the roads and blocked the British path.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never mentions any forts; it says colonists hid behind stone walls, barns, and trees to fire at the British soldiers.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: To find out what the British soldiers did right after they left Lexington, which heading should you look under?

choices.text: The opening section, "A Midnight Warning"
choices.feedback: Section A is incorrect because 'A Midnight Warning' is about Paul Revere and the riders warning colonists the night before — that happened before the British even reached Lexington.

choices.text: The second section, "Lexington: The First Shots"
choices.feedback: Section B is incorrect because 'Lexington: The First Shots' tells us what happened IN Lexington, but we already know that part — we need to find out what came AFTER.

choices.text: The third section, "Concord: The Colonists Fight Back"
choices.feedback: Section C is correct because 'Concord: The Colonists Fight Back' is exactly where the passage describes what the British did next — they marched to Concord to search for hidden weapons.

choices.text: The conclusion section, "Farmers Who Changed Everything"
choices.feedback: Section D is incorrect because 'Farmers Who Changed Everything' wraps up the whole story and talks about what the battles meant, not the specific steps the British soldiers took after leaving Lexington.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: Compared to the British army, what main idea does the author show about the colonists across the whole article?

choices.text: The colonists had better weapons and training than the British soldiers.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the colonists were ordinary citizens with muskets, not professional soldiers, and the British had much more training and a bigger, better-equipped army.

choices.text: Ordinary people with courage could stand up to a powerful army.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the article repeatedly shows that everyday people like farmers and shopkeepers had the courage to stand up to one of the world's most powerful armies.

choices.text: The colonists won every battle because they outnumbered the British.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the colonists lost the fight at Lexington badly, with eight colonists killed and only one British soldier hurt.

choices.text: The British soldiers were too afraid to fight the colonists.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the British soldiers kept marching, searching Concord, and fighting — nothing in the article says they were too afraid to fight.

syllabus_metadata.title: The Shot Heard Round the World: Lexington and Concord

assessments.title: Don't Fire Until You See the Whites of Their Eyes: Bunker Hill

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Battle of Bunker Hill: The Fight That Shocked the British

Digging in the Dark

Shovels hit dirt in the darkness. It was nearly midnight on June 16, 1775, and about 1,200 colonists were digging as fast as they could on a hill above Boston. They dug trenches — long, narrow ditches in the ground, like channels carved into the earth. They piled the dirt into thick walls. Their commander, Colonel William Prescott, urged them to work faster. By sunrise, they needed to be ready.

These men were not trained soldiers. Most were farmers and shopkeepers. They had no uniforms. But they believed the British government had treated the colonies unfairly — taxing them without giving them any voice. Two months earlier, fighting had broken out at Lexington and Concord. Now these ordinary men were about to face one of the most powerful armies on Earth.
stimulus.content_text:
The Battle of Bunker Hill: The Fight That Shocked the BritishDigging in the Dark
Shovels hit dirt in the darkness. It was nearly midnight on June 16, 1775, and about 1,200 colonists were digging as fast as they could on a hill above Boston. They dug trenches — long, narrow ditches in the ground, like channels carved into the earth. They piled the dirt into thick walls. Their commander, Colonel William Prescott, urged them to work faster. By sunrise, they needed to be ready.
These men were not trained soldiers. Most were farmers and shopkeepers. They had no uniforms. But they believed the British government had treated the colonies unfairly — taxing them without giving them any voice. Two months earlier, fighting had broken out at Lexington and Concord. Now these ordinary men were about to face one of the most powerful armies on Earth.

items.prompt: According to the section "Digging in the Dark," how were the colonists different from the British soldiers?

choices.text: They were farmers and shopkeepers.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage directly says 'Most were farmers and shopkeepers' and that they were not trained soldiers, unlike the British army.

choices.text: They were men with bright uniforms.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the passage says the colonists had NO uniforms at all, so they definitely did not have bright uniforms.

choices.text: They were trained army leaders.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage clearly states these men were NOT trained soldiers — the opposite of trained army leaders.

choices.text: They were workers from Boston.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage never calls them workers from Boston; it describes them as farmers and shopkeepers.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
A Surprise on the Hill

When the sun rose on June 17, British soldiers in Boston stared up at the hill in shock. Overnight, the colonists had built fortifications — walls and barriers built to protect soldiers during a fight, like a dirt-and-wood fortress. The British expected farmers to run at the sight of red coats. Instead, those farmers had built a fort right above them.

You might think this battle happened on Bunker Hill, since that is its name. Actually, most of the fighting took place on a nearby rise called Breed's Hill. The name stuck anyway.

The British decided to attack right away. About 2,200 soldiers in red coats formed neat lines and marched straight up the steep hillside.
stimulus.content_text:
A Surprise on the Hill
When the sun rose on June 17, British soldiers in Boston stared up at the hill in shock. Overnight, the colonists had built fortifications — walls and barriers built to protect soldiers during a fight, like a dirt-and-wood fortress. The British expected farmers to run at the sight of red coats. Instead, those farmers had built a fort right above them.
You might think this battle happened on Bunker Hill, since that is its name. Actually, most of the fighting took place on a nearby rise called Breed's Hill. The name stuck anyway.
The British decided to attack right away. About 2,200 soldiers in red coats formed neat lines and marched straight up the steep hillside.

items.prompt: Based on the text, what caused the British soldiers to be shocked when the sun rose?

choices.text: The farmers ran away from the red coats.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage says the opposite — the British expected the farmers to run, but they did NOT run away.

choices.text: The colonists built a fort during the night.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage clearly states the colonists built walls and barriers overnight, which shocked the British soldiers when the sun rose.

choices.text: The farmers moved the fight to Bunker Hill.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage says the fighting happened on Breed's Hill, not that the farmers moved the fight anywhere.

choices.text: The colonists attacked the British soldiers right away.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because it was actually the British who decided to attack right away, not the colonists.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
"Don't Fire Until You See the Whites of Their Eyes"

Behind the dirt walls, the colonists watched the red coats climb closer. Their ammunition — the bullets and gunpowder needed to fire their weapons — was running low. Every single shot had to count. According to tradition, Colonel Prescott gave a famous order: "Don't fire until you see the whites of their eyes."

The British came closer. Closer. Then the colonists fired. The blast tore through the British lines. Rows of soldiers fell. The British stumbled back down the hill.

They formed their lines again and attacked a second time. The British retreated once more.

On the third attack, the colonists ran out of ammunition completely. Some grabbed bayonets — long, knife-like blades attached to the end of a musket. Others threw rocks or fought with bare hands. But without bullets, they could not hold the hill. They retreated. The British finally took the position.
stimulus.content_text:
"Don't Fire Until You See the Whites of Their Eyes"
Behind the dirt walls, the colonists watched the red coats climb closer. Their ammunition — the bullets and gunpowder needed to fire their weapons — was running low. Every single shot had to count. According to tradition, Colonel Prescott gave a famous order: "Don't fire until you see the whites of their eyes."
The British came closer. Closer. Then the colonists fired. The blast tore through the British lines. Rows of soldiers fell. The British stumbled back down the hill.
They formed their lines again and attacked a second time. The British retreated once more.
On the third attack, the colonists ran out of ammunition completely. Some grabbed bayonets — long, knife-like blades attached to the end of a musket. Others threw rocks or fought with bare hands. But without bullets, they could not hold the hill. They retreated. The British finally took the position.

items.prompt: According to the text, what happened right after the colonists fired at the British the first time?

choices.text: The colonists ran out of ammunition.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the colonists ran out of ammunition during the third attack, not right after the first time they fired.

choices.text: The British stumbled back down the hill.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage says right after the colonists fired, 'the British stumbled back down the hill.'

choices.text: The British attacked a second time.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the British attacked a second time only after they had already stumbled back down the hill.

choices.text: The colonists left the hill at once.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the colonists did not leave the hill after the first shot — they stayed and continued fighting through two more attacks.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
A Defeat That Felt Like a Victory

The British won the hill. But look at the numbers. They suffered over 1,000 casualties — soldiers killed or wounded in battle. That was nearly half of their entire force. The Americans lost about 400 men. The British paid a terrible price for one small hill.

But that loss gave the colonies something powerful — confidence. Before Bunker Hill, British leaders believed the colonists would scatter at the first real fight. Instead, untrained farmers and shopkeepers had caused devastating damage to professional soldiers. The news spread through every colony. If ordinary people could stand against the British army and nearly win, then maybe independence was something they could actually achieve.
stimulus.content_text:
A Defeat That Felt Like a Victory
The British won the hill. But look at the numbers. They suffered over 1,000 casualties — soldiers killed or wounded in battle. That was nearly half of their entire force. The Americans lost about 400 men. The British paid a terrible price for one small hill.
But that loss gave the colonies something powerful — confidence. Before Bunker Hill, British leaders believed the colonists would scatter at the first real fight. Instead, untrained farmers and shopkeepers had caused devastating damage to professional soldiers. The news spread through every colony. If ordinary people could stand against the British army and nearly win, then maybe independence was something they could actually achieve.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, how did the British losses compare to the American losses at Bunker Hill?

choices.text: The Americans lost many more soldiers than the British did.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage clearly shows the British lost over 1,000 soldiers, which is much more than the Americans' 400.

choices.text: The British lost far more soldiers than the Americans did.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage states the British suffered over 1,000 casualties compared to only about 400 for the Americans, making the British losses far greater.

choices.text: Both sides lost about the same number of soldiers.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because 1,000 and 400 are very different numbers, so the two sides definitely did not lose about the same amount.

choices.text: The British lost fewer soldiers because they were trained.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because even though the British were trained professional soldiers, the passage shows they actually lost more soldiers, not fewer.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
More Than a Battle

Think about what those 1,200 men did that night with their shovels. They had no training, no fancy weapons, and not enough ammunition. What they had was a belief that they deserved a say in their own government. Sometimes the people who change the world don't look like soldiers at all. They look like your neighbors.
stimulus.content_text:
More Than a Battle
Think about what those 1,200 men did that night with their shovels. They had no training, no fancy weapons, and not enough ammunition. What they had was a belief that they deserved a say in their own government. Sometimes the people who change the world don't look like soldiers at all. They look like your neighbors.

items.prompt: In the article "Don't Fire Until You See the Whites of Their Eyes: Bunker Hill," what does the author mean by "the people who change the world"?

choices.text: ordinary neighbors
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage directly says the people who change the world 'look like your neighbors,' meaning ordinary everyday people.

choices.text: trained soldiers
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the passage clearly says these men had 'no training,' which means they were not trained soldiers.

choices.text: government leaders
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the passage focuses on regular people, not government leaders.

choices.text: wealthy landowners
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the passage never mentions wealthy landowners — it describes ordinary men with shovels and a belief in fairness.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: Which statement best shows the contrast between what the British expected and what really happened at Bunker Hill?

choices.text: The British expected the colonists to run away, but the colonists stood and fought hard.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage directly says the British expected the farmers to run away, but instead the colonists built a fort and fought so fiercely that the British lost nearly half their soldiers.

choices.text: The British expected the colonists to attack Boston first, but the colonists waited on the hill.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the passage never mentions the British expecting the colonists to attack Boston — that idea is not in the story at all.

choices.text: The British expected the colonists to have plenty of bullets, but the colonists soon ran low.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because it was the colonists themselves who knew their ammunition was running low, not something the British expected or were surprised by.

choices.text: The British expected the battle to happen on Breed's Hill, but it was named Bunker Hill.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the mix-up between Bunker Hill and Breed's Hill is just about the battle's name, not about what the British expected to happen.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: What caused the colonists to feel more hopeful after the Battle of Bunker Hill?

choices.text: They proved ordinary people could fight a strong army.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage says that untrained farmers fighting so well gave the colonies confidence and made independence feel possible.

choices.text: They kept the British from taking over the dirt fort.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the colonists had to retreat and the British took over the dirt fort on the third attack.

choices.text: They received more ammunition from the other colonies.
