choices.feedback: C is wrong because the passage says the colonists ran completely out of ammunition and never received more.

choices.text: They forced the British soldiers to leave Boston safely.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the British won the battle and took the hill — they did not leave Boston.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: Which sequence of events best tells the main story of the colonists in this article?

choices.text: They built a dirt fort, fought off two attacks, and retreated when bullets ran out.
choices.feedback: A is correct because it lists the three main events in the right order: building the fort, fighting off two attacks, and retreating when bullets ran out.

choices.text: They ran out of bullets, built a dirt fort, and chased the British soldiers away.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the order is mixed up — the colonists built the fort first, and they ran out of bullets much later, near the end of the battle.

choices.text: They dug deep trenches at night, threw heavy rocks, and kept the hill for themselves.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because throwing rocks was only a small moment at the very end, and the colonists did not keep the hill — the British won it.

choices.text: They watched the British march, looked into their eyes, and spread news to the towns.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because these are small details from the story, not the main events that tell what the colonists did during the battle.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: Based on the article, how does the author compare the British army and the colonists during the battle?

choices.text: The British were highly trained and well-supplied, but the colonists lacked training and ran out of bullets.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the article clearly shows the British were trained professional soldiers with supplies, while the colonists were untrained farmers who ran out of ammunition during the third attack.

choices.text: The British built dirt walls for protection, but the colonists marched in straight lines up the steep hill.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because it has the details backwards — the colonists built the dirt walls for protection, and the British were the ones who marched in straight lines up the steep hill.

choices.text: The British fought with rocks and bare hands, but the colonists used long blades and heavy musket guns.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because it is also backwards — it was the colonists who ran out of bullets and had to fight with rocks and bare hands, not the British.

choices.text: The British wanted to help the local farmers, but the colonists wanted to rule over the powerful army.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because nothing in the article says the British wanted to help the farmers — the British were attacking the colonists because they had built a fort above Boston.

syllabus_metadata.title: Don't Fire Until You See the Whites of Their Eyes: Bunker Hill

assessments.title: General George Washington: The Leader America Needed

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The General Who Kept Losing — and Still Won

A Tall, Quiet Man Steps Forward

In June 1775, a tall man in a blue-and-buff military uniform stood before a room full of arguing leaders in Philadelphia. He did not shout. He did not brag. He simply said he would do whatever his country asked. His name was George Washington, and the Second Continental Congress — the group of leaders who spoke for the American colonies — had just given him the hardest job in America.

They named him commander-in-chief, the top leader of the brand-new Continental Army. The delegates, or representatives sent by each colony, picked Washington for smart reasons. He had fought in wars before. He looked and acted like a leader. And he came from Virginia, a large southern colony. Choosing a Virginian helped convince both northern and southern colonies to fight together.
stimulus.content_text:
The General Who Kept Losing — and Still WonA Tall, Quiet Man Steps Forward
In June 1775, a tall man in a blue-and-buff military uniform stood before a room full of arguing leaders in Philadelphia. He did not shout. He did not brag. He simply said he would do whatever his country asked. His name was George Washington, and the Second Continental Congress — the group of leaders who spoke for the American colonies — had just given him the hardest job in America.

They named him commander-in-chief, the top leader of the brand-new Continental Army. The delegates, or representatives sent by each colony, picked Washington for smart reasons. He had fought in wars before. He looked and acted like a leader. And he came from Virginia, a large southern colony. Choosing a Virginian helped convince both northern and southern colonies to fight together.

items.prompt: According to the text, which detail shows a contrast between how George Washington acted and how other leaders acted?

choices.text: He argued loudly with the leaders in Philadelphia.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because Washington was actually the calm one — the passage says the OTHER leaders were arguing, not him.

choices.text: He did not shout while the leaders were arguing.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage shows a contrast: the leaders were arguing loudly, but Washington did not shout, which means he acted very differently from them.

choices.text: He wore a blue-and-buff uniform like the delegates.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because the passage never says the delegates wore the same uniform as Washington, so there is no contrast or comparison there.

choices.text: He came from Virginia while the army was brand-new.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because the fact that Washington came from Virginia and the army was new are just background details — they don't show how his behavior was different from the other leaders.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Lessons from an Earlier War

Washington was a plantation owner — a person who ran a large farm worked by enslaved people — in Virginia. Years earlier, he had fought in the French and Indian War, a conflict between Britain and France over land in North America. During that war, he learned how to lead soldiers through thick forests and rough country. He also learned from failure. At just twenty-two years old, he lost a battle at a small fort called Fort Necessity. That stinging defeat taught him to be more careful and more patient.
stimulus.content_text:
Lessons from an Earlier War
Washington was a plantation owner — a person who ran a large farm worked by enslaved people — in Virginia. Years earlier, he had fought in the French and Indian War, a conflict between Britain and France over land in North America. During that war, he learned how to lead soldiers through thick forests and rough country. He also learned from failure. At just twenty-two years old, he lost a battle at a small fort called Fort Necessity. That stinging defeat taught him to be more careful and more patient.

items.prompt: The main idea of the text is that George Washington learned how to be a strong leader. How does the detail about Fort Necessity support this main idea?

choices.text: It shows that losing the battle caused him to be more careful.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage clearly says losing at Fort Necessity taught Washington to be more careful and more patient, which supports the idea that he became a stronger leader.

choices.text: It shows that being patient caused him to lose the battle.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because it gets the order backwards — Washington learned patience after losing the battle, not before it.

choices.text: It shows that the thick forests caused him to lose the battle.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage never says thick forests had anything to do with why Washington lost at Fort Necessity.

choices.text: It shows that losing the battle caused him to leave the forest.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage never mentions Washington leaving the forest as a result of losing the battle.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
An Army of Farmers Against the World's Best Soldiers

Washington's troops were farmers, shopkeepers, and craftsmen who had never trained for war. Many signed up for only a few months. When their time was done, they walked home.

Meanwhile, the British army was the most powerful fighting force on Earth. Its soldiers had fine uniforms, the best weapons, and years of practice. Behind them stood the whole British Empire and all its money. Washington's men often had no shoes, no warm coats, and not enough food. He wrote desperate letters to Congress begging for supplies. But the new nation had no treasury — no stored-up money — and no real way to collect taxes.
stimulus.content_text:
An Army of Farmers Against the World's Best Soldiers
Washington's troops were farmers, shopkeepers, and craftsmen who had never trained for war. Many signed up for only a few months. When their time was done, they walked home.

Meanwhile, the British army was the most powerful fighting force on Earth. Its soldiers had fine uniforms, the best weapons, and years of practice. Behind them stood the whole British Empire and all its money. Washington's men often had no shoes, no warm coats, and not enough food. He wrote desperate letters to Congress begging for supplies. But the new nation had no treasury — no stored-up money — and no real way to collect taxes.

items.prompt: According to the text, what is the correct order of events for many of Washington's soldiers?

choices.text: They signed up, served a few months, and walked home.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage says Washington's soldiers signed up, served a few months, and then walked home when their time was done — exactly that order.

choices.text: They practiced for years, wore fine uniforms, and stood ready.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because practicing for years and wearing fine uniforms describes the British army, not Washington's farmers and shopkeepers.

choices.text: They wrote to Congress, begged for supplies, and collected taxes.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because it was Washington who wrote to Congress begging for supplies — not his soldiers — and nobody in the passage collected taxes.

choices.text: They walked home, signed up, and served a few months.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the order is backwards — soldiers had to sign up and serve first before they could walk home.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Losing Battles, Winning the War

Here's the surprising part: Washington lost more battles than he won. He was not a flashy military genius who crushed the enemy in one big fight. Instead, he made strategic choices — careful plans about when and where to fight. He attacked when he had an advantage. He pulled back when he did not. He kept his army alive.

And he shared every hardship with his soldiers. He slept in the same freezing camps. He ate the same tiny rations — the small portions of food given to each soldier. His men saw this. They trusted him because of it. Even when things looked hopeless, Washington refused to quit, and his soldiers followed his example.

The war dragged on from 1775 all the way to 1783 — eight long, painful years. It's easy to think the Revolution ended quickly after the Declaration of Independence in 1776. Actually, most of the fighting and suffering happened after that famous document was signed.
stimulus.content_text:
Losing Battles, Winning the War
Here's the surprising part: Washington lost more battles than he won. He was not a flashy military genius who crushed the enemy in one big fight. Instead, he made strategic choices — careful plans about when and where to fight. He attacked when he had an advantage. He pulled back when he did not. He kept his army alive.

And he shared every hardship with his soldiers. He slept in the same freezing camps. He ate the same tiny rations — the small portions of food given to each soldier. His men saw this. They trusted him because of it. Even when things looked hopeless, Washington refused to quit, and his soldiers followed his example.

The war dragged on from 1775 all the way to 1783 — eight long, painful years. It's easy to think the Revolution ended quickly after the Declaration of Independence in 1776. Actually, most of the fighting and suffering happened after that famous document was signed.

items.prompt: Based on the text, how did Washington's life in the camps compare to his soldiers' lives?

choices.text: He shared the exact same hard conditions.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage directly says Washington slept in the same freezing camps and ate the same tiny rations as his soldiers, meaning they shared the exact same hard conditions.

choices.text: He slept in much warmer and safer tents.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the passage says Washington slept in the same freezing camps as his soldiers, not in warmer or safer tents.

choices.text: He ate much larger portions of daily food.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage says Washington ate the same tiny rations as his soldiers, not larger portions of food.

choices.text: He avoided the freezing and painful camps.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage clearly states Washington was right there in the freezing camps with his soldiers, not avoiding them.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
The Leader America Needed

It was holding a ragged, starving, outnumbered army together year after year until the British finally gave up. He was brave when others wanted to run.

Now imagine keeping at something hard — not for a day, not for a week, but for eight years, with people's lives depending on you. America's freedom was not won by a genius with a perfect plan. It was won by a man who simply refused to stop.
stimulus.content_text:
The Leader America Needed
It was holding a ragged, starving, outnumbered army together year after year until the British finally gave up. He was brave when others wanted to run.

Now imagine keeping at something hard — not for a day, not for a week, but for eight years, with people's lives depending on you. America's freedom was not won by a genius with a perfect plan. It was won by a man who simply refused to stop.

items.prompt: Read this sentence from the section: "Now imagine keeping at something hard — not for a day, not for a week, but for eight years..." What does the phrase "keeping at something hard" mean in this context?

choices.text: Making a perfect plan for the soldiers
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage says freedom was NOT won by a genius with a perfect plan, so making a perfect plan is the opposite of what the passage describes.

choices.text: Leading the army for eight long years
choices.feedback: B is correct because 'keeping at something hard' means not giving up on a tough job, and the passage shows Washington did exactly that by leading his army for eight whole years.

choices.text: Being brave for just one short day
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the sentence specifically says it was NOT just for one day — it lasted eight long years.

choices.text: Waiting until the British finally give up
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the British giving up was what happened after Washington kept going, not what the phrase 'keeping at something hard' means.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: According to the article, how was the British army different from George Washington's Continental Army?

choices.text: The British army had fine uniforms and practice, while Washington's army had no shoes and starved.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage directly says the British had fine uniforms, great weapons, and training, while Washington's men lacked shoes, coats, and food.

choices.text: The British army fought in thick, rough forests, while Washington's army fought in large, open fields.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because fighting in thick forests described Washington's experience in the earlier French and Indian War, not a comparison between the two armies.

choices.text: The British army signed up for a few months, while Washington's army served for eight long years.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect and backwards — it was Washington's own soldiers who signed up for only a few months and then went home, not the British.

choices.text: The British army was led by a flashy genius, while Washington's army was led by simple farmers.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the article says Washington was NOT a flashy genius, and the farmers and craftsmen were part of Washington's army, not the British army.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: Why does the author include the section "Lessons from an Earlier War"?

choices.text: To explain why Washington was careful and patient later on.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the section directly says Washington's loss at Fort Necessity taught him to be more careful and patient, which shaped how he led later.

choices.text: To explain why the delegates chose Washington as the leader.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the reasons the delegates picked Washington — like his experience and being from Virginia — are explained in the first section, not this one.

choices.text: To explain why Washington's men had no shoes or warm coats.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the soldiers' lack of shoes and warm coats is mentioned in the section about fighting the British army, not in this section.

choices.text: To explain why Washington won every single battle he fought.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the passage clearly says Washington lost more battles than he won, so he definitely did not win every single battle.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: The main idea of the article is that George Washington was a strong leader who never gave up. Which sequence of events best supports this main idea?

choices.text: He lost an early battle, learned to be careful, and led his army for eight years.
choices.feedback: A is correct because it lists the events in the right order and shows how each one helped Washington become the never-give-up leader the article is all about.

choices.text: He led his army for eight years, lost an early battle, and learned to be careful.
choices.feedback: B has the same events as A but in the wrong order — Washington lost the early battle first, not last, so this sequence doesn't make sense.

choices.text: He signed a famous document, received fine uniforms, and beat the enemy in one big fight.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because signing a famous document, having fine uniforms, and winning one big fight are either British details or things the article says Washington did NOT do.

choices.text: He stood in a quiet room, wore a blue uniform, and asked Congress for more supplies.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because standing in a room and wearing a uniform are tiny details from the beginning of the article and don't support the main idea of Washington never giving up.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: Based on the article, how were the soldiers' reasons for trusting Washington different from why Congress chose him?

choices.text: The soldiers cared that he shared their hard lives, but Congress cared about his past experience.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage says Congress picked Washington for his experience and where he was from, while soldiers trusted him because he shared their cold camps and small food rations.

choices.text: The soldiers cared that he was from Virginia, but Congress cared about his secret battle plans.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the soldiers never cared about him being from Virginia — that was Congress's reason — and the article never mentions any secret battle plans.

choices.text: The soldiers cared that he had a fine uniform, but Congress cared about his large plantation.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because neither the soldiers nor Congress cared about his uniform or his plantation when deciding to trust or choose him.

choices.text: The soldiers cared that he won every single battle, but Congress cared about his ability to train farmers.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the article actually tells us Washington lost more battles than he won, so his soldiers definitely did not trust him for winning every single battle.

syllabus_metadata.title: General George Washington: The Leader America Needed

assessments.title: Crossing the Delaware: Washington's Bold Surprise

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Bloody Footprints in the Snow

An Army Falling Apart

Bare feet on frozen ground. No shoes, no warm coats, barely any food. By December 1776, Washington's soldiers were in terrible shape. The British army had beaten them again and again. They had been chased out of New York and across New Jersey. Thousands of soldiers had given up and gone home. The men who stayed were cold, hungry, and exhausted. Many people believed the Revolution was already over.

Washington knew he needed a win — and fast. If he waited, his whole army might disappear. So he came up with a plan so risky it was almost crazy.
stimulus.content_text:
Bloody Footprints in the SnowAn Army Falling Apart
Bare feet on frozen ground. No shoes, no warm coats, barely any food. By December 1776, Washington's soldiers were in terrible shape. The British army had beaten them again and again. They had been chased out of New York and across New Jersey. Thousands of soldiers had given up and gone home. The men who stayed were cold, hungry, and exhausted. Many people believed the Revolution was already over.
Washington knew he needed a win — and fast. If he waited, his whole army might disappear. So he came up with a plan so risky it was almost crazy.

items.prompt: According to the passage, many people believed the Revolution was already over, but what did Washington believe he needed to do instead?

choices.text: wait for more soldiers to join the army
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage says Washington knew he couldn't wait, since waiting might cause his whole army to disappear.

choices.text: get a win before his army disappeared
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly says Washington 'knew he needed a win — and fast' before his army disappeared.

choices.text: march his army back to New York
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage says his army had already been chased out of New York, and going back there is never mentioned as his plan.

choices.text: ask the British army to stop fighting
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because Washington was planning a risky attack to win, not asking the British to stop fighting.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Crossing the Icy Delaware

On Christmas night, Washington led 2,400 soldiers to the banks of the Delaware River. Their target was the town of Trenton, New Jersey, on the other side. Hessian troops were camped there. Hessians were German soldiers hired by Britain to fight the Americans. They were well-trained, well-armed professionals.

The crossing was terrifying. Chunks of ice jammed the river. Cold rain turned to sleet — a stinging mix of rain and ice that hits your face like tiny needles. The boats were packed tight and hard to steer. Two other groups of soldiers were supposed to cross at different points along the river. They turned back because the ice was too dangerous. That meant Washington would have fewer troops than planned. He pushed forward anyway.

The crossing took far longer than expected. Washington's army did not reach the far shore until nearly four in the morning — hours behind schedule. They still had nine miles to march through the snow to reach Trenton. Many soldiers had no shoes. They wrapped rags around their feet and left bloody footprints in the white snow. But they kept going.
stimulus.content_text:
Crossing the Icy Delaware
On Christmas night, Washington led 2,400 soldiers to the banks of the Delaware River. Their target was the town of Trenton, New Jersey, on the other side. Hessian troops were camped there. Hessians were German soldiers hired by Britain to fight the Americans. They were well-trained, well-armed professionals.
The crossing was terrifying. Chunks of ice jammed the river. Cold rain turned to sleet — a stinging mix of rain and ice that hits your face like tiny needles. The boats were packed tight and hard to steer. Two other groups of soldiers were supposed to cross at different points along the river. They turned back because the ice was too dangerous. That meant Washington would have fewer troops than planned. He pushed forward anyway.
The crossing took far longer than expected. Washington's army did not reach the far shore until nearly four in the morning — hours behind schedule. They still had nine miles to march through the snow to reach Trenton. Many soldiers had no shoes. They wrapped rags around their feet and left bloody footprints in the white snow. But they kept going.

items.prompt: The main idea of the text 'Crossing the Delaware' is that Washington's journey was very difficult. How does the detail about the other groups turning back support this main idea?

choices.text: It explains why Washington had fewer troops than he planned.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage clearly links the other groups turning back to Washington having fewer troops than he planned.

choices.text: It explains why the Hessian soldiers were camped in Trenton.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the Hessians were camped in Trenton before the crossing even started — it had nothing to do with the other groups turning back.

choices.text: It explains why the army had to march nine miles in the snow.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the nine-mile march happened because Trenton was nine miles away, not because the other groups turned back.

choices.text: It explains why the soldiers wrapped rags around their feet.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the soldiers wrapped rags on their feet because they had no shoes, which is a separate detail from the other groups turning back.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Surprise at Trenton

You might think the Hessians lost because they were celebrating Christmas and not paying attention. Actually, they had been on high alert for days, patrolling constantly. But all that watchfulness had worn them out. And the terrible storm convinced them no army could possibly attack in such weather. They finally let their guard down — at exactly the wrong moment.

The Americans attacked around eight o'clock in the morning. Washington's men surrounded the town and blocked every escape route. The Hessian commander was mortally wounded — hurt so badly that he could not survive. His soldiers, confused and trapped, surrendered after about ninety minutes. The Americans captured around 900 prisoners, along with muskets, cannons, and supplies. Not a single American soldier died in the battle.
stimulus.content_text:
Surprise at Trenton
You might think the Hessians lost because they were celebrating Christmas and not paying attention. Actually, they had been on high alert for days, patrolling constantly. But all that watchfulness had worn them out. And the terrible storm convinced them no army could possibly attack in such weather. They finally let their guard down — at exactly the wrong moment.
The Americans attacked around eight o'clock in the morning. Washington's men surrounded the town and blocked every escape route. The Hessian commander was mortally wounded — hurt so badly that he could not survive. His soldiers, confused and trapped, surrendered after about ninety minutes. The Americans captured around 900 prisoners, along with muskets, cannons, and supplies. Not a single American soldier died in the battle.

items.prompt: According to the text, which list shows the correct order of events during the Battle of Trenton?

choices.text: The Hessians surrendered, then the Americans attacked, then the commander was wounded.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the Americans attacked first — the Hessians could not have surrendered before the battle even started.

choices.text: The Americans attacked, then the commander was wounded, then the Hessians surrendered.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage clearly shows the Americans attacked first, then the commander was wounded, and then the Hessians surrendered.

choices.text: The commander was wounded, then the Hessians surrendered, then the Americans attacked.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the commander was wounded during the battle, which means the Americans had already attacked before he was hurt.

choices.text: The Americans attacked, then the Hessians surrendered, then the commander was wounded.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage tells us the commander was wounded during the fighting, which happened before the Hessians surrendered, not after.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Hope Returns

The Battle of Trenton was small compared to other fights in the war. But it lifted American morale — the feeling of hope and confidence that keeps people going, like the energy a losing team feels after finally scoring a goal. Soldiers who had been ready to quit decided to stay. New volunteers signed up. People who had lost faith started to believe again.

A few days later, Washington struck again, beating British troops at Princeton, New Jersey. In just ten days, an army that seemed defeated was suddenly winning.

Years later, an artist painted a famous picture of Washington standing tall in his boat, crossing the Delaware in bright daylight. But the real crossing happened in total darkness, and Washington most likely sat down. The painting was made nearly 75 years after that night to inspire people, not show exactly what happened. The truth is even more impressive — because the real men who crossed that river were shivering, soaked, and scared. They crossed anyway.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, how are the real soldiers who crossed the Delaware and a losing team that finally scores a goal similar?

choices.text: Both were fighting in a war against a stronger enemy
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage only compares the feeling of hope, not the idea of fighting a stronger enemy — and a sports team isn't fighting in a war.

choices.text: Both got a boost of hope after a difficult time
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly compares the soldiers' boost of morale to the energy a losing team feels after finally scoring a goal — both got hope after a hard time.

choices.text: Both crossed a river on a cold winter night
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because a sports team scoring a goal has nothing to do with crossing a cold river at night.

choices.text: Both were painted in a famous picture later
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the famous painting was only about Washington and his soldiers, not about any sports team.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: How did the feelings of Washington's soldiers change from before the river crossing to after the Battle of Trenton?

choices.text: They went from feeling well-rested to feeling completely exhausted.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the soldiers were already exhausted and worn out before the crossing — they were not well-rested at any point in the story.

choices.text: They went from feeling ready to quit to feeling hopeful and confident.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage says soldiers were ready to quit before the battle, but after Trenton their morale lifted and they chose to stay and keep fighting.

choices.text: They went from feeling highly trained to feeling confused and trapped.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because 'confused and trapped' describes the Hessian enemy soldiers who surrendered, not Washington's American soldiers.

choices.text: They went from feeling warm and safe to feeling cold and terrified.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the soldiers were already cold and terrified during the river crossing, which happened before the Battle of Trenton, not after.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: The article defines words like 'Hessian,' 'sleet,' 'mortally wounded,' and 'morale' throughout the text. Why did the author most likely include these definitions?

choices.text: because those words appear in the most exciting parts of the story
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the bold words appear throughout the whole article, not only in the most exciting moments.

choices.text: because those words might be unfamiliar and are explained nearby
choices.feedback: B is correct because each bold word, like 'sleet' or 'morale,' is followed right away by an explanation of what it means.

choices.text: because those words name the most important people in the article
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because most of the bold words, like 'sleet' and 'muskets,' are things or ideas, not names of people.

choices.text: because those words show events that happened in the correct order
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the bold words are there to explain vocabulary, not to show the order in which events happened.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: Which sequence of events best supports the main idea that Washington's risky plan saved his army?

choices.text: The army was losing badly, Washington led a dangerous river crossing, and the Americans won a surprise victory.
choices.feedback: A is correct because it puts the three key events in the right order — the army struggling, the risky crossing, and the surprise win — which together show how Washington's plan saved his army.

choices.text: The freezing river was full of ice, the heavy boats were hard to steer, and the soldiers marched nine miles.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because it only lists small details about the river crossing and march, not the big events that show how the plan saved the army.

choices.text: The army was losing badly, the Hessians crossed the icy river, and the British won a surprise victory.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because it gets the facts wrong — the Americans crossed the river and won the battle, not the Hessians or the British.

choices.text: Washington led a dangerous river crossing, the Americans won a surprise victory, and the army was losing badly.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the events are in the wrong order — the army was losing badly at the start of the story, not at the end.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: How does the author compare the real crossing of the Delaware River to the famous painting of it?

choices.text: The real soldiers were well-armed professionals, but the painting shows them without weapons
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage never compares weapons between the real crossing and the painting.

choices.text: The real crossing happened in darkness with frightened soldiers, but the painting shows daylight
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage clearly states the real crossing was in total darkness with scared soldiers, while the painting shows bright daylight and Washington standing tall.

choices.text: The real soldiers crossed quickly in good weather, but the painting shows a dangerous storm
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because it has it backwards — the real crossing was the dangerous one, happening in a terrible storm.

choices.text: The real crossing used only a few small boats, but the painting shows many large ships
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage never mentions the number or size of the boats in either the real crossing or the painting.

syllabus_metadata.title: Crossing the Delaware: Washington's Bold Surprise

assessments.title: The Turning Point: Victory at Saratoga

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Battle That Brought a Friend: Saratoga, 1777

An Army Stuck in the Woods

Can you imagine being trapped in a foggy forest with nothing to eat and no way out? That is exactly what happened to six thousand British soldiers in New York. American fighters surrounded them on every side. It was October 1777, and the mighty British army was about to do something it had never done before — give up.
stimulus.content_text:
The Battle That Brought a Friend: Saratoga, 1777An Army Stuck in the Woods
Can you imagine being trapped in a foggy forest with nothing to eat and no way out? That is exactly what happened to six thousand British soldiers in New York. American fighters surrounded them on every side. It was October 1777, and the mighty British army was about to do something it had never done before — give up.

items.prompt: According to the passage, the British army was called 'mighty,' but what did it do for the first time at Saratoga?

choices.text: It marched into a foggy forest
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage never says marching into a foggy forest was something the British army had never done before.

choices.text: It gave up to the Americans
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage clearly states the British army was 'about to do something it had never done before — give up,' meaning surrendering was a first for them.

choices.text: It surrounded the fighters
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage says the American fighters surrounded the British soldiers, not the other way around.

choices.text: It sent six thousand soldiers
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because sending six thousand soldiers is just a fact about how many troops were there, and the passage never says this was a first.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Burgoyne's Big Plan

Here is where things get interesting. The British had a bold idea. They would send a huge army south from Canada through New York. This army would cut New England off from the other colonies, like slicing a sandwich in half. A British general named John Burgoyne led the march. He expected to win easily — but did he?

Clever American fighters chopped down trees and dragged them across the roads. Burgoyne's supply lines — the long chain of wagons and roads that carried food and bullets back to Canada — grew dangerously thin.
stimulus.content_text:
Burgoyne's Big Plan
Here is where things get interesting. The British had a bold idea. They would send a huge army south from Canada through New York. This army would cut New England off from the other colonies, like slicing a sandwich in half. A British general named John Burgoyne led the march. He expected to win easily — but did he?
Clever American fighters chopped down trees and dragged them across the roads. Burgoyne's supply lines — the long chain of wagons and roads that carried food and bullets back to Canada — grew dangerously thin.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, why did Burgoyne's army start running low on food and bullets?

choices.text: because the army was too large to feed itself
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage never says the army was too large to feed itself — it says the roads were blocked.

choices.text: because Americans blocked the roads with trees
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly says Americans chopped down trees and dragged them across the roads, which cut off the supply lines carrying food and bullets.

choices.text: because the British forgot to bring enough wagons
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage never mentions the British forgetting to bring wagons.

choices.text: because a snowstorm stopped the wagons from moving
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage does not mention a snowstorm at all.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Two Battles and a Surprising Hero

Now, get ready for a twist you will not see coming. American forces gathered to block Burgoyne. General Horatio Gates commanded them. Gates was a careful leader who liked to plan and wait for the right moment. The Battle of Saratoga was really two battles fought about three weeks apart. In the first, the Americans held their ground and stopped the British from pushing forward. In the second, they attacked hard and broke through British lines.

One of the bravest American officers at Saratoga was Benedict Arnold. Here is the surprising part: most people today only know Arnold as America's most famous traitor. But before he switched sides and helped the British, Arnold fought like a hero. At Saratoga, he led daring charges on horseback and suffered a serious leg wound. Without his courage, the Americans might not have won that day.

With no supplies, no reinforcements — fresh soldiers sent to help, like substitutes off the bench — and no escape route, Burgoyne surrendered his entire army of about 6,000 soldiers to General Gates. It was the first time a British army had surrendered in the whole war. Pretty amazing, right?
stimulus.content_text:
Two Battles and a Surprising Hero
Now, get ready for a twist you will not see coming. American forces gathered to block Burgoyne. General Horatio Gates commanded them. Gates was a careful leader who liked to plan and wait for the right moment. The Battle of Saratoga was really two battles fought about three weeks apart. In the first, the Americans held their ground and stopped the British from pushing forward. In the second, they attacked hard and broke through British lines.
One of the bravest American officers at Saratoga was Benedict Arnold. Here is the surprising part: most people today only know Arnold as America's most famous traitor. But before he switched sides and helped the British, Arnold fought like a hero. At Saratoga, he led daring charges on horseback and suffered a serious leg wound. Without his courage, the Americans might not have won that day.
With no supplies, no reinforcements — fresh soldiers sent to help, like substitutes off the bench — and no escape route, Burgoyne surrendered his entire army of about 6,000 soldiers to General Gates. It was the first time a British army had surrendered in the whole war. Pretty amazing, right?

items.prompt: According to the text, what happened right before Burgoyne surrendered his army?

choices.text: Benedict Arnold switched sides and helped the British fight
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because Arnold switching sides happened after the Battle of Saratoga, not right before Burgoyne surrendered.

choices.text: The Americans attacked hard and broke through British lines
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage says the Americans broke through British lines in the second battle, and then Burgoyne had no way out and surrendered.

choices.text: General Gates sent fresh reinforcements to help the soldiers
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says Burgoyne had NO reinforcements — fresh soldiers never came to help him.

choices.text: Burgoyne won the first battle and pushed the Americans back
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says the Americans stopped the British in the first battle and broke through in the second — Burgoyne did not win.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
A Victory That Crossed the Ocean

Here is something cool to think about. Saratoga mattered far beyond those New York woods. Across the Atlantic Ocean, France had been watching the war closely. The French wanted to see Britain weakened. But France would not help the Americans unless they proved they could actually win. Saratoga was that proof — and you can imagine how exciting that news must have been!

In 1778, France signed a treaty of alliance — an official agreement between two countries to fight on the same side. French money poured in. French soldiers sailed across the ocean to fight alongside the Americans. Most importantly, the powerful French naval forces — warships that could battle at sea — challenged the British fleet. The colonists were no longer fighting alone.
stimulus.content_text:
A Victory That Crossed the Ocean

The French wanted to see Britain weakened. But France would not help the Americans unless they proved they could actually win. Saratoga was that proof — and you can imagine how exciting that news must have been!
In 1778, France signed a treaty of alliance — an official agreement between two countries to fight on the same side. French money poured in. French soldiers sailed across the ocean to fight alongside the Americans. Most importantly, the powerful French naval forces — warships that could battle at sea — challenged the British fleet. The colonists were no longer fighting alone.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, how were the French soldiers and the American colonists alike after the treaty of alliance was signed?

choices.text: They both sailed across the ocean to battle.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because only the French soldiers sailed across the ocean — the American colonists were already in America.

choices.text: They both fought on the same side in the war.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage explains that a treaty of alliance means two countries agree to fight on the same side, which is exactly what France and the colonists did.

choices.text: They both wanted to prove they could win alone.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the treaty was about teaming up together, not proving they could each win on their own.

choices.text: They both signed an agreement to help Britain.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because France and the colonists signed the treaty to fight against Britain, not to help Britain.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Why Saratoga Still Matters

So what does all of this mean for you today? Before Saratoga, the American Revolution looked like it might fail. After Saratoga, the Americans had a powerful partner. The war lasted several more hard years. But the balance had shifted. Those hungry, determined American fighters in the New York forests did not just win a battle. They convinced an entire country across the ocean to join their side — and that made all the difference between a revolution that collapsed and a nation that was born. Next time you think one moment cannot change everything, remember Saratoga!
stimulus.content_text:
Why Saratoga Still Matters
So what does all of this mean for you today? Before Saratoga, the American Revolution looked like it might fail. After Saratoga, the Americans had a powerful partner. The war lasted several more hard years. But the balance had shifted. Those hungry, determined American fighters in the New York forests did not just win a battle. They convinced an entire country across the ocean to join their side — and that made all the difference between a revolution that collapsed and a nation that was born. Next time you think one moment cannot change everything, remember Saratoga!

items.prompt: The text states that after the battle of Saratoga, the Americans 'had a powerful partner.' Based on the section, what does this phrase mean?

choices.text: They convinced a whole country to join them.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage directly says the Americans 'convinced an entire country across the ocean to join their side,' which is exactly what having a powerful partner means.

choices.text: They found extra soldiers hiding in the forest.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the passage never mentions any soldiers hiding in the forest — that detail is made up.

choices.text: They decided to stop fighting the difficult war.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the passage says the war lasted 'several more hard years,' meaning the Americans kept on fighting, not stopping.

choices.text: They learned to fight better in the dark woods.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the passage does not say anything about the Americans learning new ways to fight in the woods.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: How does the article show that the Americans' situation was different before Saratoga compared to after Saratoga?

choices.text: Before Saratoga, the Americans had French ships, but after they fought alone.
choices.feedback: A is backwards — the article says France sent naval forces after Saratoga, not before it.

choices.text: Before Saratoga, the Americans fought alone, but after they gained France's help.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the article clearly shows the Americans fought alone before Saratoga, then gained France's soldiers, money, and ships afterward.

choices.text: Before Saratoga, the Americans were winning easily, but after they started losing.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the article says the Revolution looked like it might fail before Saratoga, not that the Americans were winning easily.

choices.text: Before Saratoga, the British had no plan, but after they made a bold one.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the British had a bold plan from the start — Burgoyne's march south from Canada — it just did not work out the way they hoped.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: Which pair of headings helps you find what caused the British to lose their supplies and what resulted from the American victory?

choices.text: "An Army Stuck in the Woods" and "Two Battles and a Surprising Hero"
choices.feedback: A is wrong because 'An Army Stuck in the Woods' describes the British being surrounded, but it doesn't explain what caused them to lose their supplies.

choices.text: "Burgoyne's Big Plan" and "A Victory That Crossed the Ocean"
choices.feedback: B is correct because 'Burgoyne's Big Plan' explains how the supply lines were cut, and 'A Victory That Crossed the Ocean' explains the big result — France joining the Americans.

choices.text: "Two Battles and a Surprising Hero" and "Why Saratoga Still Matters"
choices.feedback: C is wrong because 'Two Battles and a Surprising Hero' talks about the fighting itself, not what caused the British to lose their supplies.

choices.text: "An Army Stuck in the Woods" and "Why Saratoga Still Matters"
choices.feedback: D is wrong because 'An Army Stuck in the Woods' doesn't explain the supply problem, so it doesn't answer the first part of the question.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: Which list of events best tells the main story of the article in the order they happened?

choices.text: General Burgoyne marched south, Benedict Arnold rode a horse, and General Gates waited.
choices.feedback: A is wrong because it only lists small details like Arnold riding a horse and Gates waiting, which are not the main events that tell the big story of the battle.

choices.text: France signed a treaty, the Americans blocked the roads, and the British surrendered.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the order is mixed up — France signing the treaty happened after the Americans blocked the roads and the British surrendered, not before.

choices.text: The Americans blocked the roads, the British surrendered, and France agreed to help.
choices.feedback: C is correct because it lists the three most important events in the right order: Americans blocked the roads, the British surrendered, and then France agreed to help.

choices.text: The Americans chopped down trees, the French surrendered, and the British won easily.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because it has two big mistakes — the French never surrendered, and the British did not win easily; they actually lost and surrendered.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: Based on the article, how was the end of the battle different from what British General Burgoyne expected?

choices.text: He expected to win easily, but he had to surrender.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the article directly says Burgoyne expected to win easily, but he was surrounded with no food or escape route and had to give up.

choices.text: He expected to trap the Americans, but they ran away.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the Americans did not run away — they actually surrounded the British and blocked them from moving forward.

choices.text: He expected to wait patiently, but he attacked too soon.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because waiting patiently was General Gates's style, not Burgoyne's, and the article never says Burgoyne planned to wait.

choices.text: He expected to get fresh soldiers, but the French arrived.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the French did not arrive at the Battle of Saratoga — France only joined the war after hearing the news of the American victory there.

syllabus_metadata.title: The Turning Point: Victory at Saratoga

assessments.title: The Bitter Winter: Survival at Valley Forge

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Valley Forge: The Winter That Built an Army

Bloody Footprints in the Snow

Bloody footprints marked the road to Valley Forge. In December 1777, about 12,000 soldiers marched into a frozen stretch of Pennsylvania countryside. You could follow their path just by looking at the ground.

These men were part of the Continental Army — the fighting force created by the American colonies to win independence from Britain. You might picture them as trained soldiers in matching uniforms. Actually, most of them were farmers, shopkeepers, and tradespeople. They had left their homes and families to fight for freedom. Now, after months of hard battles, their clothes hung in shreds. And the worst part of the war was not a battle at all.
stimulus.content_text:
Valley Forge: The Winter That Built an ArmyBloody Footprints in the Snow
Bloody footprints marked the road to Valley Forge. In December 1777, about 12,000 soldiers marched into a frozen stretch of Pennsylvania countryside. You could follow their path just by looking at the ground.

These men were part of the Continental Army — the fighting force created by the American colonies to win independence from Britain. You might picture them as trained soldiers in matching uniforms. Actually, most of them were farmers, shopkeepers, and tradespeople. They had left their homes and families to fight for freedom. Now, after months of hard battles, their clothes hung in shreds. And the worst part of the war was not a battle at all.

items.prompt: The text says you might picture the Continental Army as trained soldiers. According to the text, who were most of these men actually?

choices.text: They were farmers, shopkeepers, and tradespeople.
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says 'most of them were farmers, shopkeepers, and tradespeople' — those are the exact words used to describe who these men really were.

choices.text: They were British fighters in matching uniforms.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because the passage says trained soldiers in matching uniforms is just what you might picture in your imagination, not who they actually were.

choices.text: They were men who stayed home with their families.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because the passage says these men 'left their homes and families' to fight, so they did the opposite of staying home.

choices.text: They were leaders who planned the hard battles.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because the passage never says anything about these men being leaders or planners of battles — that idea is not in the text at all.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Building a Camp in the Wilderness

Just twenty miles away, British soldiers rested in warm Philadelphia houses. The soldiers chopped trees and stacked logs into hundreds of small huts. The huts kept out the wind, but they were damp, cold, and full of smoke. Men slept on beds of straw, pressed together for warmth.

Food was desperately scarce. Some days, the only meal was firecake — a flat, tasteless lump made from flour and water cooked over flames. Think of a pancake with no eggs, no milk, no salt, and no flavor. Some soldiers chewed bark from trees. The army's system for delivering supplies had fallen apart, and Congress could not send enough provisions — the food, clothing, and goods an army needs to survive.

Building a Camp in the Wilderness
