Food was desperately scarce. Some days, the only meal was firecake — a flat, tasteless lump made from flour and water cooked over flames. Think of a pancake with no eggs, no milk, no salt, and no flavor. Some soldiers chewed bark from trees. The army's system for delivering supplies had fallen apart, and Congress could not send enough provisions — the food, clothing, and goods an army needs to survive.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, why did some soldiers chew bark from trees?

choices.text: because they wanted wood to build their huts
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage says soldiers chopped trees to stack into huts, which is a completely different reason than chewing bark for food.

choices.text: because there was not enough food to eat
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage clearly states food was 'desperately scarce,' and chewing bark shows just how hungry and desperate the soldiers were.

choices.text: because the firecake needed bark for flavor
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because firecake was only made from flour and water — the passage never mentions bark being used as an ingredient.

choices.text: because the supply wagons arrived too late
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because while the supply system had broken down, the passage does not say wagons arriving late was the specific reason soldiers chewed bark.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Disease and Death

Hunger was terrible, but sickness was worse. Diseases like typhus — a deadly fever spread by lice — and smallpox swept through the crowded huts. About 2,000 soldiers died at Valley Forge. Not one of them died in battle. They died from illness, cold, and malnutrition, which means their bodies broke down because they did not have enough food. Camp hospitals overflowed. Men died faster than graves could be dug in the frozen ground.
stimulus.content_text:
Disease and Death
Hunger was terrible, but sickness was worse. Diseases like typhus — a deadly fever spread by lice — and smallpox swept through the crowded huts. About 2,000 soldiers died at Valley Forge. Not one of them died in battle. They died from illness, cold, and malnutrition, which means their bodies broke down because they did not have enough food. Camp hospitals overflowed. Men died faster than graves could be dug in the frozen ground.

items.prompt: According to the passage, what happened after camp hospitals overflowed with sick soldiers?

choices.text: Soldiers began fighting in battles nearby
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage clearly states that not one soldier at Valley Forge died in battle.

choices.text: Men died faster than graves could be dug
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage says right after hospitals overflowed that 'men died faster than graves could be dug in the frozen ground.'

choices.text: New huts were built to hold more soldiers
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage never mentions building new huts to take care of sick soldiers.

choices.text: Doctors arrived to cure all the diseases
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage shows soldiers kept dying from disease, meaning no doctors arrived to cure everyone.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
A Leader Who Fought with a Pen

It's easy to think of him charging into battle with a sword. But as commanding general, his most powerful weapon that winter was a pen. His job was to lead, plan, and fight for supplies. He wrote letter after letter to Congress, describing soldiers who were "barefoot and otherwise naked." He warned that the army would not last without help. Congress tried, but the young nation had little money.
stimulus.content_text:
A Leader Who Fought with a Pen
It's easy to think of him charging into battle with a sword. But as commanding general, his most powerful weapon that winter was a pen. His job was to lead, plan, and fight for supplies. He wrote letter after letter to Congress, describing soldiers who were "barefoot and otherwise naked." He warned that the army would not last without help. Congress tried, but the young nation had little money.

items.prompt: The author compares a pen to a weapon. Based on the text, how did the general use his pen like a weapon?

choices.text: By writing letters to ask for help
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage says the general wrote letters to Congress describing his soldiers' needs and warning that the army needed help — his pen was his tool for fighting, just like a weapon.

choices.text: By charging into battle with a sword
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the passage says it would be easy to *imagine* him with a sword, but his real weapon that winter was actually his pen, not a sword.

choices.text: By earning money for the young nation
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the passage says the young nation had little money, and the general was asking for help, not earning money.

choices.text: By making warm clothes for the troops
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the passage never mentions the general or anyone else making clothes for the troops.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
A Stranger Who Changed Everything

Then, in February 1778, a loud, energetic stranger rode into camp. His name was Baron Friedrich von Steuben, a Prussian military officer — meaning he came from Prussia, a powerful kingdom in what is now Germany. Von Steuben looked at these shivering farmers and saw an army waiting to be trained.

He barely spoke English. He shouted orders in French and German while translators repeated his words. He was loud, funny, and passionate. Day after day, on frozen ground, he drilled the soldiers. He taught them to march in tight lines, load their weapons faster, and fight with bayonets — long blades attached to the ends of their guns, like knives on a stick. The men grew to admire him. Slowly, they stopped looking like a tired crowd and started moving like a real army.
stimulus.content_text:
A Stranger Who Changed Everything
Then, in February 1778, a loud, energetic stranger rode into camp. His name was Baron Friedrich von Steuben, a Prussian military officer — meaning he came from Prussia, a powerful kingdom in what is now Germany. Von Steuben looked at these shivering farmers and saw an army waiting to be trained.

He barely spoke English. He shouted orders in French and German while translators repeated his words. He was loud, funny, and passionate. Day after day, on frozen ground, he drilled the soldiers. He taught them to march in tight lines, load their weapons faster, and fight with bayonets — long blades attached to the ends of their guns, like knives on a stick. The men grew to admire him. Slowly, they stopped looking like a tired crowd and started moving like a real army.

items.prompt: According to the passage, what is the definition of the word bayonets?

choices.text: tight lines formed for marching
choices.feedback: Tight lines are how the soldiers marched, which is a different skill von Steuben taught — not what a bayonet is.

choices.text: long blades attached to guns
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says bayonets are 'long blades attached to the ends of their guns, like knives on a stick.'

choices.text: fast ways to load the weapons
choices.feedback: Loading weapons faster was another thing von Steuben trained the soldiers to do, but it has nothing to do with what a bayonet is.

choices.text: loud orders shouted in French
choices.feedback: Shouting orders in French is how von Steuben communicated with the soldiers, not the meaning of the word bayonets.

items.title: Section 6 - Guiding Question 5

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
Forged by Fire and Ice

When spring came, the soldiers who walked out of Valley Forge were not the same ones who had stumbled in. Yet Valley Forge was one of the most important moments of the American Revolution — proof that winning a war takes more than winning battles.
stimulus.content_text:
Forged by Fire and Ice
When spring came, the soldiers who walked out of Valley Forge were not the same ones who had stumbled in. Yet Valley Forge was one of the most important moments of the American Revolution — proof that winning a war takes more than winning battles.

items.prompt: Which detail gives evidence that the soldiers changed during their time at Valley Forge?

choices.text: They walked out instead of stumbling in.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage directly compares how the soldiers moved — they stumbled in but walked out — which is evidence they changed and grew stronger.

choices.text: They won many battles during the spring.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the passage never says anything about the soldiers winning battles during the spring.

choices.text: They ended the American Revolution early.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the passage says Valley Forge was an important moment in the Revolution, but it does not say the war ended early.

choices.text: They stayed in the camp for the whole war.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the passage says the soldiers walked out of Valley Forge in the spring, so they did not stay for the whole war.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: Which sentence tells a contrast between the British soldiers and the Continental soldiers at Valley Forge?

choices.text: The British soldiers stayed in warm houses, but the Continental soldiers built cold huts.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage directly tells us the British rested in warm houses while the Continental soldiers lived in cold, smoky huts — a clear contrast.

choices.text: The British soldiers marched through snow, but the Continental soldiers rested in Philadelphia.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because it has the two sides switched — the Continental soldiers marched through the snow, and the British soldiers were the ones resting comfortably in Philadelphia.

choices.text: The British soldiers taught new drills, but the Continental soldiers gave orders in German.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because it was Baron von Steuben, a Prussian visitor, who taught drills and shouted orders in German — not the British soldiers.

choices.text: The British soldiers had no food, but the Continental soldiers had plenty of supplies.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because it is the opposite of what the passage says — the Continental soldiers were the ones starving, while there is no mention of the British having food problems.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: Based on the article, what caused the soldiers at Valley Forge to change from a tired crowd into a real army?

choices.text: Congress finally sent enough money to buy new uniforms and food
choices.feedback: The article says Congress tried to help but the young nation had little money, so enough money and supplies never actually arrived.

choices.text: Baron von Steuben drilled and trained them day after day
choices.feedback: This is correct! The article says von Steuben drilled the soldiers day after day and they slowly went from looking like a tired crowd to moving like a real army.

choices.text: The soldiers built huts that kept them warm through winter
choices.feedback: The huts only kept out the wind — the article says they were still damp, cold, and full of smoke, so they didn't truly keep the soldiers warm.

choices.text: Spring weather arrived and made the soldiers feel stronger
choices.feedback: Spring arriving is mentioned at the very end, but the article never says the warmer weather is what turned the soldiers into a better army.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: Which sequence of events best tells the main idea of the article?

choices.text: Soldiers marched into the snowy country, built small wooden huts, and then ate tasteless firecake for meals.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because it only lists small details about where soldiers slept and what they ate, which are not the main idea of the whole article.

choices.text: Soldiers arrived in freezing weather, suffered from bad sickness, and then trained to become a real army.
choices.feedback: B is correct because it captures the three most important parts of the article in the right order: arriving in the cold, suffering from sickness, and being trained into a real army.

choices.text: Soldiers fought a hard winter battle, rested in warm city houses, and then defeated the British military.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the soldiers did not fight a winter battle, and it was the British soldiers — not the American soldiers — who rested in warm Philadelphia houses.

choices.text: Soldiers trained with a strict leader, marched into the frozen camp, and then got sick inside crowded huts.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because it has the order backwards — the soldiers arrived and got sick first, and von Steuben did not come to train them until later in the winter.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: How were the soldiers who arrived at Valley Forge and the soldiers who left Valley Forge alike in an important way?

choices.text: Both groups were well-trained and ready to fight in battles.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the soldiers who arrived were not well-trained at all — von Steuben had to spend weeks teaching them how to march, load weapons, and fight properly.

choices.text: Both groups were ordinary people fighting for freedom.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage tells us from the start that these were ordinary people fighting for freedom, and that important fact stayed true for both the soldiers who arrived and the soldiers who left.

choices.text: Both groups had warm uniforms and plenty of food.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage clearly describes soldiers with shredded clothes, no shoes, and barely any food — the opposite of warm uniforms and plenty to eat.

choices.text: Both groups were led by Baron von Steuben's training.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because von Steuben only arrived in February 1778, so the soldiers who stumbled into Valley Forge in December had never been trained by him yet.

syllabus_metadata.title: The Bitter Winter: Survival at Valley Forge

assessments.title: Victory at Yorktown: America Wins Its Independence

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Trapped at Yorktown: How the Revolution's Last Battle Was Won

A General with Nowhere to Go

On an October morning in 1781, British General Charles Cornwallis sat in a crumbling house in Yorktown, Virginia.

For six years, American colonists had been fighting Britain for independence. Thousands of soldiers on both sides had fought and died. The colonists believed the British government taxed them unfairly and gave them no voice in making laws. But beating the world's strongest army was almost impossible — unless you had help.
stimulus.content_text:
Trapped at Yorktown: How the Revolution's Last Battle Was WonA General with Nowhere to Go
On an October morning in 1781, British General Charles Cornwallis sat in a crumbling house in Yorktown, Virginia.

For six years, American colonists had been fighting Britain for independence. Thousands of soldiers on both sides had fought and died. The colonists believed the British government taxed them unfairly and gave them no voice in making laws. But beating the world's strongest army was almost impossible — unless you had help.

items.prompt: According to the text, the colonists wanted independence, but what made winning almost impossible?

choices.text: Beating the world's strongest army
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly says 'beating the world's strongest army was almost impossible,' which is exactly what made winning so hard.

choices.text: Fighting the war for six whole years
choices.feedback: Fighting for six years tells us how long the war lasted, but the passage does not say the length of the war is what made winning almost impossible.

choices.text: Hiding inside an old crumbling house
choices.feedback: The crumbling house is where the British general was sitting — it has nothing to do with why winning was almost impossible for the colonists.

choices.text: Paying taxes to the British government
choices.feedback: Paying unfair taxes is why the colonists wanted independence in the first place, not what made winning the war almost impossible.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
A Team Effort, Not a One-Man Show

But here is the surprising part: he could not have won at Yorktown without France. France sent thousands of soldiers, a powerful navy, and money to help the Americans. Washington and French General Rochambeau planned and marched together. Thousands of ordinary soldiers — American and French — did the dangerous, exhausting work of fighting. This victory belonged to a team, not one person.
stimulus.content_text:
A Team Effort, Not a One-Man Show
But here is the surprising part: he could not have won at Yorktown without France. France sent thousands of soldiers, a powerful navy, and money to help the Americans. Washington and French General Rochambeau planned and marched together. Thousands of ordinary soldiers — American and French — did the dangerous, exhausting work of fighting. This victory belonged to a team, not one person.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, why was the victory at Yorktown called a team effort?

choices.text: because Washington planned every part of the battle by himself
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage says Washington planned together with French General Rochambeau, so he did not do it alone.

choices.text: because France and America worked together to win the fight
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage clearly shows France and America worked together by sharing soldiers, a navy, money, and battle planning.

choices.text: because the French navy arrived after the battle had ended
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the French navy was part of the help France gave during the fight, not something that showed up after it was over.

choices.text: because one brave soldier did all of the dangerous fighting
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage says thousands of American and French soldiers did the dangerous, exhausting work of fighting together.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
The Trap Snaps Shut

Cornwallis had marched his tired army to Yorktown, a small port on the Chesapeake Bay. He expected British ships to bring him reinforcements — fresh soldiers sent to help, like substitutes running onto a soccer field. But those ships never came.

Washington learned that Cornwallis was vulnerable, meaning unprotected and easy to attack. He changed his plans fast. Instead of attacking New York, he marched his army and Rochambeau's French forces over 400 miles south to Virginia. The British did not see it coming.

Meanwhile, a French fleet commanded by Admiral de Grasse sailed into the Chesapeake Bay. French warships fought off the British navy and took control of the water. Now Cornwallis could not escape by sea either.
stimulus.content_text:
The Trap Snaps Shut
Cornwallis had marched his tired army to Yorktown, a small port on the Chesapeake Bay. He expected British ships to bring him reinforcements — fresh soldiers sent to help, like substitutes running onto a soccer field. But those ships never came.

Washington learned that Cornwallis was vulnerable, meaning unprotected and easy to attack. He changed his plans fast. Instead of attacking New York, he marched his army and Rochambeau's French forces over 400 miles south to Virginia. The British did not see it coming.

Meanwhile, a French fleet commanded by Admiral de Grasse sailed into the Chesapeake Bay. French warships fought off the British navy and took control of the water. Now Cornwallis could not escape by sea either.

items.prompt: According to the text, which sequence of events stopped Cornwallis from escaping by sea?

choices.text: French warships fought the British navy, and then they controlled the water.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage says French warships fought the British navy first, and then took control of the water, stopping Cornwallis from escaping by sea.

choices.text: Washington marched his army to Virginia, and then he attacked the ships.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because Washington marched his army to Virginia to surround Cornwallis on land — the passage never says he attacked any ships.

choices.text: Washington changed his battle plans fast, and then he attacked New York.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because Washington changed his plans so he would NOT attack New York — he went south to Virginia instead.

choices.text: Cornwallis waited for the British ships, and then he sailed away safely.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because Cornwallis never sailed away safely — he was trapped and could not escape by sea at all.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Digging Closer, Day by Day

The Battle of Yorktown — the last major battle of the American Revolution — began in late September as a siege. A siege is when an army surrounds an enemy and waits for them to run out of food and supplies. American and French soldiers dug trenches, long narrow ditches that protect soldiers the way a fort's walls do. Each night, they dug new trenches closer to the British lines.

Then came the bombardment — a nonstop attack with heavy cannons, like a thunderstorm made of iron. After three weeks, Cornwallis's soldiers had almost no food or ammunition left. Disease spread through his camp. He tried to escape across the river, but a storm drove his boats back.
stimulus.content_text:
Digging Closer, Day by Day
The Battle of Yorktown — the last major battle of the American Revolution — began in late September as a siege. A siege is when an army surrounds an enemy and waits for them to run out of food and supplies. American and French soldiers dug trenches, long narrow ditches that protect soldiers the way a fort's walls do. Each night, they dug new trenches closer to the British lines.

Then came the bombardment — a nonstop attack with heavy cannons, like a thunderstorm made of iron. After three weeks, Cornwallis's soldiers had almost no food or ammunition left. Disease spread through his camp. He tried to escape across the river, but a storm drove his boats back.

items.prompt: In the passage, the author compares trenches to a fort's walls. Based on this comparison, what does the word 'trenches' mean?

choices.text: paths that soldiers march along
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage says trenches protect soldiers, not that they are paths soldiers march along.

choices.text: ditches that keep soldiers safe
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage says trenches are long narrow ditches that protect soldiers, just like a fort's walls do.

choices.text: holes that store food and supplies
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage never mentions trenches being used to store food or supplies.

choices.text: tunnels that lead under the enemy
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage describes trenches as open ditches dug near enemy lines, not tunnels going underneath the enemy.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
The World Turned Upside Down

On October 19, 1781, Cornwallis surrendered. According to tradition, a British band played a song called "The World Turned Upside Down" as British soldiers marched out and laid down their weapons. American and French troops stood watching in silence.

But the war did not end that day. You might think a surrender finishes everything, but it took two more years of talking. Diplomats — people whose job is to make agreements between countries — from America, Britain, and France negotiated the peace. That means they discussed and argued until both sides agreed on terms. In 1783, Britain signed the Treaty of Paris. This document officially recognized the thirteen colonies as free and independent states, stretching from the Atlantic Ocean to the Mississippi River.
stimulus.content_text:
The World Turned Upside Down
On October 19, 1781, Cornwallis surrendered. According to tradition, a British band played a song called "The World Turned Upside Down" as British soldiers marched out and laid down their weapons. American and French troops stood watching in silence.

But the war did not end that day. You might think a surrender finishes everything, but it took two more years of talking. Diplomats — people whose job is to make agreements between countries — from America, Britain, and France negotiated the peace. That means they discussed and argued until both sides agreed on terms. In 1783, Britain signed the Treaty of Paris. This document officially recognized the thirteen colonies as free and independent states, stretching from the Atlantic Ocean to the Mississippi River.

items.prompt: According to the text, what are diplomats?

choices.text: soldiers who fight in wars for their country
choices.feedback: Soldiers fight in wars, but the passage says diplomats make agreements by talking — that's a very different job!

choices.text: people whose job is to make agreements between countries
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly defines diplomats as 'people whose job is to make agreements between countries.'

choices.text: leaders who sign treaties at the end of a battle
choices.feedback: The passage says diplomats negotiated and discussed until both sides agreed, not that they signed treaties at the end of a battle.

choices.text: people who write songs about important events
choices.feedback: The passage never mentions diplomats writing songs — that detail about a song is about the British band, not diplomats.

items.title: Section 6 - Guiding Question 5

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
What Freedom Actually Meant

Before the treaty, colonists followed laws made by a king across the ocean. After it, Americans could choose their own leaders and write their own laws. A group of colonies with no real navy, no professional army, and very little money had won their freedom — but only because they worked together. Even the biggest victories are never won alone.
stimulus.content_text:
What Freedom Actually Meant
Before the treaty, colonists followed laws made by a king across the ocean. After it, Americans could choose their own leaders and write their own laws. A group of colonies with no real navy, no professional army, and very little money had won their freedom — but only because they worked together. Even the biggest victories are never won alone.

items.prompt: In the section "What Freedom Actually Meant," which detail shows that the colonies were not fully prepared for a war?

choices.text: They followed the laws of a king.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because following the king's laws tells us who made the rules, not whether the colonies were ready for war.

choices.text: They had no professional army.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly states the colonies had 'no professional army,' meaning they lacked trained soldiers needed for fighting a war.

choices.text: They chose their own new leaders.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because choosing their own leaders was something that happened after they won freedom, not a sign of being unprepared.

choices.text: They worked together to win freedom.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because working together was actually what helped them win, so it shows their strength, not their lack of preparation.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: Based on the article, what is the main difference between how the Battle of Yorktown was won and how the war finally ended?

choices.text: The battle was won by fighting, but the war was ended by talking.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the article clearly shows soldiers fought at Yorktown using cannons and trenches, while diplomats later talked and negotiated the Treaty of Paris to officially end the war.

choices.text: The battle was won by the navy, but the war was ended by the army.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because while the French navy helped trap Cornwallis, the army also played a big role, and it was diplomats — not the army — who ended the war.

choices.text: The battle was won by a king, but the war was ended by a general.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because no king won the battle; it was American and French soldiers and generals, like Washington and Rochambeau, who worked together to win.

choices.text: The battle was won by talking, but the war was ended by fighting.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because it has the order backwards — the fighting happened first at the Battle of Yorktown, and the talking and negotiating came afterward to end the war.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: Based on the article, what caused General Cornwallis to be trapped and defeated at Yorktown?

choices.text: The British ships got lost at sea, and a bad storm ruined his camp.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the British ships were blocked by the French navy, not lost at sea, and while a storm did push back Cornwallis's escape boats, that wasn't the main reason he was trapped.

choices.text: The French navy blocked the water, and the armies surrounded his camp.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the French navy blocked Cornwallis from escaping by water, and the American and French armies surrounded his camp on land, leaving him with nowhere to go.

choices.text: The king changed the unfair laws, and the soldiers stopped their fighting.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the article never says the king changed any laws, and the soldiers kept fighting all the way until Cornwallis surrendered.

choices.text: The Americans built a strong fort, and the British ran out of cannons.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the soldiers dug trenches, not a fort, and it was Cornwallis's own army that ran out of supplies, not the Americans who ran out of cannons.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: Under which heading should you look to find what the soldiers did next after the French navy took control of the water?

choices.text: The heading 'A General with Nowhere to Go'
choices.feedback: Section A, 'A General with Nowhere to Go,' is at the very beginning and talks about why the colonists were fighting Britain — it happens way before the French navy shows up.

choices.text: The heading 'The Trap Snaps Shut'
choices.feedback: Section B, 'The Trap Snaps Shut,' is actually where the French navy takes control of the water, so it tells us about that event, not what happened after it.

choices.text: The heading 'Digging Closer, Day by Day'
choices.feedback: Section C, 'Digging Closer, Day by Day,' is correct because it starts right after the trap was set and tells us the soldiers began digging trenches and attacking with cannons — that's what came next!

choices.text: The heading 'The World Turned Upside Down'
choices.feedback: Section D, 'The World Turned Upside Down,' skips ahead to the surrender and the peace treaty, which happened much later, not right after the navy took control.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: The main idea of the passage is that America won its freedom through teamwork. How does the author compare the American and French soldiers to support this idea?

choices.text: By describing how both groups sailed powerful warships into the Chesapeake Bay.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because only the French fleet sailed into the Chesapeake Bay — the passage does not say American soldiers did this too.

choices.text: By pointing out that both groups had professional armies and plenty of money.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the passage says the colonies had no professional army and very little money, which is the opposite of what this answer claims.

choices.text: By explaining that both groups shared the hard work of fighting and digging.
choices.feedback: C is correct because the passage clearly says both American and French soldiers shared the hard work of fighting and digging trenches together each night.

choices.text: By showing that both groups negotiated the peace treaty with the British king.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the peace treaty was negotiated by diplomats, not by the soldiers who fought at Yorktown.

syllabus_metadata.title: Victory at Yorktown: America Wins Its Independence

assessments.title: Finding Your Way: Directions and the Compass Rose

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Finding Your Way: Directions, Compasses, and Maps

The Oak Tree Problem

You are standing in the middle of a wide, grassy field. Your friend calls and says, "Meet me at the big oak tree!" You spin around. Trees are everywhere. Which way do you walk?

Now imagine your friend says, "The oak tree is north of you." Suddenly, you have something to work with. That single word — north — cuts the whole field in half. Directions give you a language for describing where things are.
stimulus.content_text:
Finding Your Way: Directions, Compasses, and MapsThe Oak Tree Problem
You are standing in the middle of a wide, grassy field. Your friend calls and says, "Meet me at the big oak tree!" You spin around. Trees are everywhere. Which way do you walk?
Now imagine your friend says, "The oak tree is north of you." Suddenly, you have something to work with. That single word — north — cuts the whole field in half. Directions give you a language for describing where things are.

items.prompt: According to the text, what is the difference between looking for the oak tree at first and looking for it after the friend says 'north'?

choices.text: At first trees are everywhere, but later the field is cut in half.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage says 'trees are everywhere' at first, and then says the word 'north' 'cuts the whole field in half,' which matches this answer perfectly.

choices.text: At first the field is empty, but later trees are everywhere around you.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the passage never says the field is empty — it says trees are everywhere right from the start.

choices.text: At first you are spinning around, but later you are walking away fast.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because even though spinning and walking are mentioned in the passage, they are not the main difference the passage is trying to explain.

choices.text: At first the friend is lost, but later the friend is found quickly.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the friend is never lost in the story — the friend is actually the one helping by giving the direction 'north.'

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
The Four Cardinal Directions

The four main directions are called cardinal directions — the most basic set of directions, like the four legs of a table that hold everything up. They are north, south, east, and west.

Stand outside in the morning and face the rising sun. North is to your left. South is to your right. But turn around, and everything flips! Your body is what moved.

On most maps, north is at the top of the page. South is at the bottom. East is on the right, and west is on the left. You might think that means north is "up" — like up in the sky. But north is not up. It is a direction along the ground. Map makers just agreed to put it at the top. That agreement is called a convention — a rule people follow so everyone does things the same way.
stimulus.content_text:
The Four Cardinal Directions
The four main directions are called cardinal directions — the most basic set of directions, like the four legs of a table that hold everything up. They are north, south, east, and west.
Stand outside in the morning and face the rising sun. North is to your left. South is to your right. But turn around, and everything flips! Your body is what moved.
On most maps, north is at the top of the page. South is at the bottom. East is on the right, and west is on the left. You might think that means north is "up" — like up in the sky. But north is not up. It is a direction along the ground. Map makers just agreed to put it at the top. That agreement is called a convention — a rule people follow so everyone does things the same way.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, why does north appear at the top of most maps?

choices.text: because north is the direction that points up into the sky
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage clearly says 'north is not up' — it is a direction along the ground, not toward the sky.

choices.text: because map makers agreed to follow the same rule
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage says map makers agreed to put north at the top, and calls this a convention — a shared rule so everyone does it the same way.

choices.text: because the sun always rises in the north each morning
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage says you face the rising sun in the morning to find directions, and the sun rises in the east, not the north.

choices.text: because north is the most important cardinal direction
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage never says north is the most important direction — it just says all four cardinal directions are the most basic set of directions.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Directions In Between

What if the oak tree is not directly north or directly east?

That is where intermediate directions come in. Intermediate means "in between," like a rest stop halfway through a long drive. The four intermediate directions are northeast, northwest, southeast, and southwest. Northeast is between north and east. Southwest is between south and west.

Picture your school on a map. If the library is above and to the right, the library is northeast. If the park is below and to the left, it is southwest. With all eight directions, you can describe locations much more exactly.

That is where intermediate directions come in. Intermediate means "in between," like a rest stop halfway through a long drive. The four intermediate directions are northeast, northwest, southeast, and southwest. Northeast is between north and east. Southwest is between south and west.
Picture your school on a map. If the library is above and to the right, the library is northeast. If the park is below and to the left, it is southwest. With all eight directions, you can describe locations much more exactly.

items.prompt: According to the text, what does the author explain BEFORE introducing the four intermediate directions?

choices.text: how to find a library on a map
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the library is used as an example after the intermediate directions have already been introduced.

choices.text: what the word intermediate means
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage defines 'intermediate' as 'in between' right before listing the four intermediate directions.

choices.text: how many cardinal directions there are
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage never talks about how many cardinal directions there are.

choices.text: where the park is located on a map
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the park example appears after the intermediate directions are introduced, not before.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Reading the Compass Rose

Look at almost any map, and you will spot a symbol shaped like a star or a flower with pointed tips. This is a compass rose — a symbol printed on a map that shows which way is north, south, east, and west. Many compass roses also mark the four intermediate directions.

Sailors drew the first compass roses on sea charts — special maps made for ocean travel — hundreds of years ago. Those early designs were bright and beautiful, filled with color and swirling lines. Today, compass roses are simpler. But they still do the same job: they show you which direction is which so you can read the map correctly.

choices.text: They are both bright and filled with color.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage says only the early compass roses were bright and filled with color — today's compass roses are described as simpler.

choices.text: They both show directions on a map.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage says both old and new compass roses do 'the same job' of showing directions on a map.

choices.text: They are both drawn on sea charts.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because only the early compass roses were drawn on sea charts — today's compass roses appear on many different kinds of maps.

choices.text: They both have swirling lines on them.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
The Real-World Compass

A map has a compass rose. But what if you are outside, standing in that field, with no map at all? That is when a magnetic compass helps. A magnetic compass is a small tool with a needle that swings freely and points toward Earth's magnetic north — a spot near the top of the planet where Earth's magnetic pull is strongest. Before GPS existed, the compass was the most important direction-finding tool in the world. Sailors, explorers, and hikers all depended on it.
stimulus.content_text:
The Real-World Compass
A map has a compass rose. But what if you are outside, standing in that field, with no map at all? That is when a magnetic compass helps. A magnetic compass is a small tool with a needle that swings freely and points toward Earth's magnetic north — a spot near the top of the planet where Earth's magnetic pull is strongest. Before GPS existed, the compass was the most important direction-finding tool in the world. Sailors, explorers, and hikers all depended on it.

items.prompt: According to the text, what is the meaning of "magnetic north"?

choices.text: A small tool with a needle that swings freely
choices.feedback: This describes a magnetic compass — the tool — not magnetic north, which is a location on Earth.

choices.text: A spot where Earth's magnetic pull is strongest
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says magnetic north is 'a spot near the top of the planet where Earth's magnetic pull is strongest.'

choices.text: A drawing on a map that shows different directions
choices.feedback: This describes a compass rose, which is a drawing on a map — not magnetic north.

choices.text: A modern computer system that helps sailors navigate
choices.feedback: This describes GPS, which the passage mentions as something that came after the compass was already being used.

items.title: Section 6 - Guiding Question 5

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
Eight Words That Change Everything

Your friend says, "The oak tree is northwest of you." You face the morning sun, find east, figure out where north and west are, and then aim between them. You walk. And there it is — the big oak tree. Eight directions. That is all it took to turn a confusing field into a place you could understand.

choices.text: The person walks and finds the oak tree

assessments.title: How Far Is It? Understanding Map Scales

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Reading the Secret Language of Maps

A Whole State on a Sheet of Paper

Spread a road map of Texas across your kitchen table. The real Texas stretches 773 miles from east to west. But on that paper, the same distance fits under your arm. Someone shrank an entire state down to the size of a placemat. How do you figure out real distances from something so small?

A map scale shows the connection between a short distance on paper and a much longer distance on Earth. That is the job of a map scale — a tool printed on a map that tells you how much the real world has been shrunk.
stimulus.content_text:
Reading the Secret Language of MapsA Whole State on a Sheet of Paper
Spread a road map of Texas across your kitchen table. The real Texas stretches 773 miles from east to west. But on that paper, the same distance fits under your arm. Someone shrank an entire state down to the size of a placemat. How do you figure out real distances from something so small?
A map scale shows the connection between a short distance on paper and a much longer distance on Earth. That is the job of a map scale — a tool printed on a map that tells you how much the real world has been shrunk.

items.prompt: According to the text, what is the difference between the real state of Texas and the map of Texas?

choices.text: The real state stretches 773 miles, but the map fits under your arm.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage says the real Texas is 773 miles wide, but the map is small enough to fit under your arm.

choices.text: The real state fits under your arm, but the map stretches 773 miles.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because it has it backwards — the real state is the huge one, and the map is the small one that fits under your arm.

choices.text: The real state is a short distance, but the map is a longer distance.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because it also has it backwards — the real state is the long distance, and the map shows a short distance on paper.

choices.text: The real state is on a kitchen table, but the map is on planet Earth.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage says you spread the map on a kitchen table, and the real state is on Earth, not the other way around.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
How a Bar Scale Works

The most common type of map scale is a bar scale. It looks like a small, thick line printed near the edge of a map, with numbers marking off distances. One end might say "0" and the other end might say "100 miles."

Grab a ruler or the edge of a piece of paper. Hold it up to the bar scale and mark the distance it shows. Then move that same measurement between two places on the map. If the bar scale shows that one inch equals 100 miles, and two cities sit two inches apart, those cities are about 200 miles apart in real life.

Notice that word — about. Because Earth is round and a map is flat, flattening a curved surface always stretches or squishes things a little. Your measurement gives you a very good guess, not a perfect one.
stimulus.content_text:
How a Bar Scale Works
The most common type of map scale is a bar scale. It looks like a small, thick line printed near the edge of a map, with numbers marking off distances. One end might say "0" and the other end might say "100 miles."
Grab a ruler or the edge of a piece of paper. Hold it up to the bar scale and mark the distance it shows. Then move that same measurement between two places on the map. If the bar scale shows that one inch equals 100 miles, and two cities sit two inches apart, those cities are about 200 miles apart in real life.
Notice that word — about. Because Earth is round and a map is flat, flattening a curved surface always stretches or squishes things a little. Your measurement gives you a very good guess, not a perfect one.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, what causes a map measurement to be a guess instead of a perfect answer?

choices.text: Because a ruler is too short to measure long distances.
choices.feedback: The passage never mentions a ruler being too short — that idea doesn't come from the passage at all.

choices.text: Because the round Earth is flattened to make the map.
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says flattening Earth's curved surface stretches or squishes things, which is why your measurement is a guess, not a perfect answer.

choices.text: Because the bar scale only shows a few printed numbers.
choices.feedback: The passage doesn't say the number of printed numbers on the bar scale causes any measuring problems.

choices.text: Because the cities are too far apart to measure perfectly.
choices.feedback: The passage doesn't say that cities being far apart causes the measurement to be a guess — the real reason is Earth's round shape being flattened.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Big Detail, Small Area — and the Other Way Around

Not every map shows the same amount of space. A map of your school neighborhood shows every street, every building, every park. You can spot the basketball court and the library. A map like this, one that shows a small area with lots of detail, is called a large-scale map. "Large-scale" means the things on the map are drawn at a large size. Your school might be as big as your thumbnail. That is why you see so much detail.

Now picture a map of an entire continent — a giant landmass like Africa or North America. On that map, your whole city is just a tiny dot. Rivers look like thin threads. This is a small-scale map. Everything is shrunk way down to fit a huge area onto one page, so small details disappear.

A quick way to remember: large scale, large detail, small area.

Your school might be as big as your thumbnail. That is why you see so much detail.
Now picture a map of an entire continent — a giant landmass like Africa or North America. On that map, your whole city is just a tiny dot. Rivers look like thin threads. This is a small-scale map. Everything is shrunk way down to fit a huge area onto one page, so small details disappear.
A quick way to remember: large scale, large detail, small area.

items.prompt: In the section "Big Detail, Small Area," what does the text ask you to picture next, after it describes a map of your school neighborhood?

choices.text: A map of a small basketball court
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the basketball court is mentioned as a small detail you can spot on the neighborhood map, not something the text asks you to picture next.

choices.text: A map of an entire continent
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage uses the words 'Now picture a map of an entire continent' right after describing the school neighborhood map.

choices.text: A map of a very thin river thread
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because thin river threads are just a detail that describes what rivers look like on the continent map, not the main thing you are asked to picture.

choices.text: A map of a tiny dot for a city
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because a tiny dot for a city is a detail explaining how cities appear on the continent map, not the main thing the text asks you to imagine next.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Which Way Is North?

Most maps place north at the top of the page. Because north sits at the top of a map, you might think north means "up" — like up toward the sky. But north is really a direction along the ground, toward the North Pole. Many maps also include a compass rose, a small symbol with arrows pointing north, south, east, and west. It helps you figure out directions between places.
stimulus.content_text:
Which Way Is North?
Most maps place north at the top of the page. Because north sits at the top of a map, you might think north means "up" — like up toward the sky. But north is really a direction along the ground, toward the North Pole. Many maps also include a compass rose, a small symbol with arrows pointing north, south, east, and west. It helps you figure out directions between places.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, how are the top of a map and the sky similar?

choices.text: They both make you think of the word "up".
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage directly says that north being at the top of a map might make you think north means 'up — like up toward the sky,' connecting both ideas to the word 'up.'

choices.text: They both show the way to the North Pole.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the passage says north on a map points toward the North Pole, but the sky does not show the way to the North Pole.

choices.text: They both are directions along the ground.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the passage says north is a direction along the ground, and the sky is above the ground, not along it.

choices.text: They both use arrows to point out places.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because arrows are used in a compass rose on maps to point out directions, not something the sky does.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Picking the Right Map

Choosing a map is like choosing a tool from a toolbox. Need to find a street in your town? Use a large-scale map with plenty of detail. Want to see where your country sits compared to others? Use a small-scale map that shows a wide area. With just those two small tools — the map scale and the compass rose — a flat piece of paper can tell you how far it is from your home to the ocean, and which direction to go to get there.
stimulus.content_text:
Picking the Right Map

Use a small-scale map that shows a wide area. With just those two small tools — the map scale and the compass rose — a flat piece of paper can tell you how far it is from your home to the ocean, and which direction to go to get there.

choices.text: A map that shows a wide area
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly says to use a small-scale map when you want to see 'a wide area,' like where your country sits compared to others.

choices.text: A map that shows only one country
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage says a small-scale map shows many countries together, not just one country.

choices.text: A map that tells you which direction to go
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because telling you which direction to go is the job of the compass rose, not a small-scale map.

assessments.title: Lines Around the Globe: Latitude, Longitude, and Hemispheres

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Invisible Grid That Covers the Earth

Lines No One Can See

A helicopter pilot spots a boat sinking in the middle of the Pacific Ocean. The pilot radios for the boat's location and hears two numbers: 30 degrees north, 140 degrees west.

Those two numbers come from a system of imaginary lines called latitude and longitude. Geographers, people who study Earth's surface, invented this system to describe any place on the planet. You would never see these lines painted on the ground or floating on the ocean. They exist only on maps and globes. But they work like an invisible address for every spot on Earth.
stimulus.content_text:
The Invisible Grid That Covers the EarthLines No One Can See
A helicopter pilot spots a boat sinking in the middle of the Pacific Ocean. The pilot radios for the boat's location and hears two numbers: 30 degrees north, 140 degrees west.

Those two numbers come from a system of imaginary lines called latitude and longitude. Geographers, people who study Earth's surface, invented this system to describe any place on the planet. You would never see these lines painted on the ground or floating on the ocean. They exist only on maps and globes. But they work like an invisible address for every spot on Earth.

items.prompt: According to the text, how are latitude and longitude lines different from real lines you could find on the ground or ocean?

choices.text: They can only be seen from high up in a helicopter.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage never says anything about needing to be in a helicopter to see these lines — they can't be seen from anywhere in real life!

choices.text: They exist only on maps and globes, not in real life.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage clearly states that latitude and longitude lines 'exist only on maps and globes' and cannot be found on the ground or ocean.

choices.text: They were recently painted across the ocean surface.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage says these lines are imaginary and have never been painted on any surface.

choices.text: They can only be found in the Pacific Ocean area.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage says these lines give an address for 'every spot on Earth,' not just places in the Pacific Ocean.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Latitude: The Sideways Lines

Pick up a globe and look at the lines that run sideways, from east to west. These are lines of latitude. They measure how far north or south a place is. You might think latitude lines go up and down because "latitude" sounds like "ladder," and ladders stand tall. But picture a ladder laid flat on its side. The rungs go left to right. That is how latitude lines wrap around the globe.

These lines are sometimes called parallels, meaning they stay the same distance apart and never cross each other, like stripes on a shirt.

The most important latitude line is the equator. It sits at 0 degrees and splits Earth into two halves. Each half is called a hemisphere, which means "half of a ball." The top half is the Northern Hemisphere. The bottom half is the Southern Hemisphere. The United States, Europe, and most of Asia sit in the Northern Hemisphere. Most of South America, southern Africa, and Australia sit in the Southern Hemisphere. Latitude numbers count up from 0 at the equator to 90 at each pole.
stimulus.content_text:
Latitude: The Sideways Lines
Pick up a globe and look at the lines that run sideways, from east to west. These are lines of latitude. They measure how far north or south a place is. You might think latitude lines go up and down because "latitude" sounds like "ladder," and ladders stand tall. But picture a ladder laid flat on its side. The rungs go left to right. That is how latitude lines wrap around the globe.

These lines are sometimes called parallels, meaning they stay the same distance apart and never cross each other, like stripes on a shirt.

The most important latitude line is the equator. It sits at 0 degrees and splits Earth into two halves. Each half is called a hemisphere, which means "half of a ball." The top half is the Northern Hemisphere. The bottom half is the Southern Hemisphere. The United States, Europe, and most of Asia sit in the Northern Hemisphere. Most of South America, southern Africa, and Australia sit in the Southern Hemisphere. Latitude numbers count up from 0 at the equator to 90 at each pole.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, why might someone first think latitude lines go up and down?

choices.text: because the word sounds like ladder
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage directly says the word 'latitude' sounds like 'ladder,' and since ladders stand tall, someone might picture the lines going up and down.

choices.text: because the lines measure north and south
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because measuring north and south tells us what latitude does, not why the word itself makes people picture lines going up and down.

choices.text: because the equator splits Earth in half
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the equator splitting Earth in half is a fact about where the equator is, and has nothing to do with which direction latitude lines point.

choices.text: because the poles are at 90 degrees
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the number 90 degrees at the poles is just a measurement, and the passage never connects that number to why someone might think the lines go up and down.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Longitude: The Up-and-Down Lines

Now find the lines that run up and down, from the North Pole to the South Pole. These are lines of longitude. They measure how far east or west a place is. Unlike latitude lines, longitude lines are not parallel. They spread apart at the equator and meet at both poles, like the sections of a peeled orange.

The starting line for longitude is the prime meridian. "Prime" means first, and "meridian" means a line from pole to pole. It sits at 0 degrees and passes through Greenwich, a town near London, England. It splits Earth into the Eastern Hemisphere and the Western Hemisphere. Longitude numbers count from 0 up to 180 degrees in each direction.

The United States is in the Northern Hemisphere and the Western Hemisphere. Australia is in the Southern Hemisphere and the Eastern Hemisphere.
stimulus.content_text:
Longitude: The Up-and-Down Lines
Now find the lines that run up and down, from the North Pole to the South Pole. These are lines of longitude. They measure how far east or west a place is. Unlike latitude lines, longitude lines are not parallel. They spread apart at the equator and meet at both poles, like the sections of a peeled orange.

The starting line for longitude is the prime meridian. "Prime" means first, and "meridian" means a line from pole to pole. It sits at 0 degrees and passes through Greenwich, a town near London, England. It splits Earth into the Eastern Hemisphere and the Western Hemisphere. Longitude numbers count from 0 up to 180 degrees in each direction.

The United States is in the Northern Hemisphere and the Western Hemisphere. Australia is in the Southern Hemisphere and the Eastern Hemisphere.

items.prompt: According to the text, what do longitude lines do after they spread apart at the equator?

choices.text: They meet together at both poles.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage directly says longitude lines 'spread apart at the equator and meet at both poles,' like sections of a peeled orange.

choices.text: They become parallel to each other.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the passage clearly states that longitude lines are *not* parallel — they spread and meet instead of staying the same distance apart.

choices.text: They stop at the prime meridian.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the prime meridian is the starting line at 0 degrees, and the passage never says other longitude lines stop there.

choices.text: They split Earth into hemispheres.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because splitting Earth into the Eastern and Western Hemispheres is what the prime meridian does, not what longitude lines do after spreading at the equator.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Finding Any Place with Two Numbers

Together, latitude and longitude form a grid over the whole planet. Any location can be described with just two numbers. Washington, D.C., sits at about 38 degrees north latitude and 77 degrees west longitude. To find it on a globe, slide your finger to the 38th line above the equator. Then slide it left to the 77th line west of the prime meridian. Where your fingers meet, that is the capital.

Pilots use these numbers to fly planes across continents. Ship captains use them to cross oceans. Rescue teams use them to reach people in danger, just like that helicopter pilot over the Pacific. Even your phone's GPS, short for Global Positioning System, uses latitude and longitude to show the blue dot on your map.

And suddenly, a giant, spinning planet has an address for every single point on its surface.

Finding Any Place with Two Numbers
