Washington, D.C., sits at about 38 degrees north latitude and 77 degrees west longitude. To find it on a globe, slide your finger to the 38th line above the equator. Then slide it left to the 77th line west of the prime meridian. Where your fingers meet, that is the capital.

Pilots use these numbers to fly planes across continents. Ship captains use them to cross oceans. Rescue teams use them to reach people in danger, just like that helicopter pilot over the Pacific. Even your phone's GPS, short for Global Positioning System, uses latitude and longitude to show the blue dot on your map.

And suddenly, a giant, spinning planet has an address for every single point on its surface.

items.prompt: The text compares a phone's GPS to how pilots and captains use latitude and longitude. Based on this comparison, what does 'GPS' mean in the passage?

choices.text: A tool that finds a specific place
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage says GPS uses latitude and longitude to show your location as a blue dot on a map, which means it finds a specific place.

choices.text: A tool that flies a large airplane
choices.feedback: B is wrong because flying airplanes is the job of pilots, not what GPS does.

choices.text: A tool that rescues people in need
choices.feedback: C is wrong because rescuing people is what rescue teams do — GPS is just the tool that helps find locations.

choices.text: A tool that spins the whole planet
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the passage never says GPS spins the planet; that detail is not connected to GPS at all.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: According to the passage, what is one major difference between latitude lines and longitude lines?

choices.text: Latitude lines go straight up and down, while longitude lines go sideways.
choices.feedback: This is backwards — the passage says latitude lines run sideways (east to west) and longitude lines run up and down (pole to pole).

choices.text: Latitude lines never cross each other, while longitude lines meet at the poles.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage says latitude lines are 'parallels' that never cross, while longitude lines meet at both poles.

choices.text: Latitude lines divide the Earth into halves, while longitude lines do not do this.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because longitude lines do divide Earth into halves too — the prime meridian splits Earth into the Eastern and Western Hemispheres.

choices.text: Latitude lines are drawn on paper maps, while longitude lines are painted on land.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage clearly states that neither latitude nor longitude lines are painted anywhere — they only exist on maps and globes.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: What is the main effect of the author using the headings 'Latitude: The Sideways Lines' and 'Longitude: The Up-and-Down Lines'?

choices.text: They help readers quickly locate which way the lines travel.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the words 'Sideways Lines' and 'Up-and-Down Lines' in the headings quickly show readers which direction latitude and longitude lines travel.

choices.text: They help readers easily find where the two main lines cross.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the headings describe direction of the lines, not where latitude and longitude cross each other.

choices.text: They help readers quickly learn how to use a phone's GPS.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because GPS is talked about later in the article and has nothing to do with these two headings.

choices.text: They help readers easily count the exact number of lines.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the headings say nothing about counting lines — they only describe which way the lines go.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: The main idea of the passage is that imaginary lines help people find any spot on Earth. Which order of details best explains this main idea?

choices.text: First, geographers invent imaginary lines. Next, the lines form a grid. Finally, people use the grid to find places.
choices.feedback: A is correct because it matches the passage's order: geographers invent the lines, the lines form a grid, and people use the grid to find places.

choices.text: First, people use a grid to find places. Next, geographers invent imaginary lines. Finally, the lines form a grid.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the steps are out of order — people can't use the grid before geographers invent it.

choices.text: First, latitude lines travel sideways. Next, longitude lines travel up and down. Finally, the lines meet at the poles.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because it only describes how latitude and longitude lines are shaped, not how they help people find locations.

choices.text: First, the equator splits the Earth. Next, the prime meridian splits the Earth. Finally, the lines create four hemispheres.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because it only covers how the lines divide Earth into hemispheres, which is just one detail and not the full main idea.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: Which statement best compares how the author helps readers picture latitude lines and longitude lines?

choices.text: By comparing both kinds of lines to stripes on a shirt
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because only latitude lines are compared to stripes on a shirt (as 'parallels'), not longitude lines.

choices.text: By comparing latitude to a flat ladder and longitude to orange sections
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage really does compare latitude to a flat ladder and longitude to sections of a peeled orange.

choices.text: By comparing both kinds of lines to roads on a city map
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage never compares either type of line to roads on a city map.

choices.text: By comparing latitude to a peeled orange and longitude to a flat ladder
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because it has the comparisons backwards — it's latitude that's like a flat ladder and longitude that's like a peeled orange, not the other way around.

syllabus_metadata.title: Lines Around the Globe: Latitude, Longitude, and Hemispheres

assessments.title: Our Neighbor to the North: Exploring Canada's Land

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Canada: The Giant Next Door

A Country That Crosses Six Clocks

Picture a girl in Halifax eating dinner at six o'clock. At that exact moment, a boy in Vancouver is still at his school desk. They live in the same country, but the sun reaches them at different times. That country is Canada, and it is so wide that it crosses six time zones — six strips of Earth where clocks are set to different hours.

Canada stretches from the Atlantic Ocean in the east to the Pacific Ocean in the west. It reaches north all the way to the Arctic Ocean. Actually, Canada has only about 40 million people. The United States, its neighbor to the south, has over 330 million. That means Canada holds a lot of open space.
stimulus.content_text:
Canada: The Giant Next DoorA Country That Crosses Six Clocks
Picture a girl in Halifax eating dinner at six o'clock. At that exact moment, a boy in Vancouver is still at his school desk. They live in the same country, but the sun reaches them at different times. That country is Canada, and it is so wide that it crosses six time zones — six strips of Earth where clocks are set to different hours.

Canada stretches from the Atlantic Ocean in the east to the Pacific Ocean in the west. It reaches north all the way to the Arctic Ocean. Actually, Canada has only about 40 million people. The United States, its neighbor to the south, has over 330 million. That means Canada holds a lot of open space.

items.prompt: According to the passage, how is Canada's population different from the population of the United States?

choices.text: Canada has more people than the United States
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage says the United States has over 330 million people, which is much more than Canada's 40 million, so Canada actually has fewer people, not more.

choices.text: Canada has far fewer people than the United States
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage shows Canada has about 40 million people and the United States has over 330 million, which means Canada has far, far fewer people.

choices.text: Canada has about the same people as the United States
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because 40 million and 330 million are very different numbers, so the two countries do not have about the same number of people.

choices.text: Canada has slightly fewer people than the United States
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because 'slightly fewer' means just a little bit less, but Canada's 40 million is not just a little less than 330 million — it is a whole lot less!

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Its Own Country, Its Own Flag

Canada and the United States share the longest border between any two countries on Earth — nearly 5,525 miles, including the border with Alaska. The two nations have lived in peace along that border for more than two hundred years. But Canada is not part of the United States. It is a fully independent country with its own government, its own red-and-white maple leaf flag, and its own leaders. Canadians speak two official languages: English and French. Many Indigenous peoples — the first people who lived on this land long before European settlers arrived — still call Canada home today.
stimulus.content_text:
Its Own Country, Its Own Flag
Canada and the United States share the longest border between any two countries on Earth — nearly 5,525 miles, including the border with Alaska. The two nations have lived in peace along that border for more than two hundred years. But Canada is not part of the United States. It is a fully independent country with its own government, its own red-and-white maple leaf flag, and its own leaders. Canadians speak two official languages: English and French. Many Indigenous peoples — the first people who lived on this land long before European settlers arrived — still call Canada home today.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, why are some groups in Canada known as Indigenous peoples?

choices.text: They lived on the land before European settlers arrived.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage directly says Indigenous peoples are 'the first people who lived on this land long before European settlers arrived,' which is the definition the passage gives us.

choices.text: They speak both English and French as official languages.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because speaking English and French is about Canada's two official languages, and the passage never connects that to Indigenous peoples.

choices.text: They live along the longest border between two countries.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the long border between Canada and the United States has nothing to do with why people are called Indigenous.

choices.text: They run the independent government and choose the flag.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the passage talks about Canada having its own government and flag, but it never says Indigenous peoples are the ones who run the government or choose the flag.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Mountains, Shields, and Flat Golden Fields

Canada's land changes wildly from region to region. Along the western edge, the Rocky Mountains rise with snow-capped peaks and deep valleys. In the middle of the country, flat prairies — wide, grassy plains like a golden sea of wheat — spread out for hundreds of miles. Farmers grow grain here, which is why people call this area Canada's breadbasket, the region that feeds much of the nation.

Wrapped around a huge body of water called Hudson Bay sits the Canadian Shield. This is a massive area of rock that formed billions of years ago — some of the oldest rock on Earth. It holds valuable minerals and metals deep inside it, hidden under thin soil and thousands of lakes.

In the east, thick forests cover the hills, and small fishing villages line the coast. Canada also shares the Great Lakes with the United States. These five lakes hold about one-fifth of all the fresh surface water on the planet.
stimulus.content_text:
Mountains, Shields, and Flat Golden Fields
Canada's land changes wildly from region to region. Along the western edge, the Rocky Mountains rise with snow-capped peaks and deep valleys. In the middle of the country, flat prairies — wide, grassy plains like a golden sea of wheat — spread out for hundreds of miles. Farmers grow grain here, which is why people call this area Canada's breadbasket, the region that feeds much of the nation.

Wrapped around a huge body of water called Hudson Bay sits the Canadian Shield. This is a massive area of rock that formed billions of years ago — some of the oldest rock on Earth. It holds valuable minerals and metals deep inside it, hidden under thin soil and thousands of lakes.

In the east, thick forests cover the hills, and small fishing villages line the coast. Canada also shares the Great Lakes with the United States. These five lakes hold about one-fifth of all the fresh surface water on the planet.

items.prompt: According to the text, which region is described first?

choices.text: the eastern forests and coast
choices.feedback: The eastern forests and coast are mentioned last, in the third paragraph, so they are not described first.

choices.text: the Rocky Mountains in the west
choices.feedback: This is correct! The Rocky Mountains are the very first region described, right at the start of the first paragraph.

choices.text: the Great Lakes by the border
choices.feedback: The Great Lakes are mentioned near the end of the passage, so they come after the Rocky Mountains.

choices.text: the Canadian Shield by Hudson Bay
choices.feedback: The Canadian Shield is described in the second paragraph, which comes after the Rocky Mountains are already introduced.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
The Frozen North and the Warm South

The far north of Canada is called the tundra — a treeless, frozen stretch of land where no tall plants can grow, like a giant icy desert. Temperatures there drop to minus 40 degrees or colder. Polar bears, caribou, and Arctic foxes roam this wild place. Very few people live so far north.

Here's the surprising part: southern Canada has warm summers, sometimes as hot as many parts of the United States. That is exactly why most Canadians live within 100 miles of the U.S. Border. Major cities like Toronto, Montreal, and Vancouver sit in the south, where the weather is milder and the soil is better for farming and building.
stimulus.content_text:
The Frozen North and the Warm South
The far north of Canada is called the tundra — a treeless, frozen stretch of land where no tall plants can grow, like a giant icy desert. Temperatures there drop to minus 40 degrees or colder. Polar bears, caribou, and Arctic foxes roam this wild place. Very few people live so far north.

Here's the surprising part: southern Canada has warm summers, sometimes as hot as many parts of the United States. That is exactly why most Canadians live within 100 miles of the U.S. Border. Major cities like Toronto, Montreal, and Vancouver sit in the south, where the weather is milder and the soil is better for farming and building.

items.prompt: When the author compares the tundra to 'a giant icy desert,' what does this comparison help the reader understand about the word tundra?

choices.text: a place that is hot and sandy with no water
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the tundra is freezing cold, not hot, and the passage uses 'desert' only to show it is empty and bare, not to say it has sand or heat.

choices.text: a flat, empty land where very little grows
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage describes the tundra as treeless and frozen with no tall plants — a flat, empty land where very little grows, just like a desert.

choices.text: a frozen ocean covered in thick sheets of ice
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the tundra is a stretch of land, not an ocean, and the passage never mentions ice sheets covering water.

choices.text: a snowy forest filled with many tall trees
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage clearly states the tundra is 'treeless,' meaning there are no tall trees there at all.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
A Land That Changes with the Seasons

In autumn, Canada's maple trees blaze red and orange. Every season paints this country in new colors.

So the next time you look at a map and see that enormous shape above the United States, remember: it is not just empty space. It is a country of ancient rock, golden fields, frozen tundra, and bustling cities — all held together under one maple leaf flag.
stimulus.content_text:
A Land That Changes with the Seasons
In autumn, Canada's maple trees blaze red and orange. Every season paints this country in new colors.

So the next time you look at a map and see that enormous shape above the United States, remember: it is not just empty space. It is a country of ancient rock, golden fields, frozen tundra, and bustling cities — all held together under one maple leaf flag.

items.prompt: According to the passage, what does the author mean by saying 'every season paints this country in new colors'?

choices.text: people paint buildings different colors each year
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage never mentions anything about people painting buildings different colors.

choices.text: Canada's land looks different as the seasons change
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage shows us that Canada's land changes its appearance with each season, like when maple trees turn red and orange in autumn.

choices.text: artists travel to Canada to paint colorful pictures
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage does not mention any artists traveling to Canada to paint pictures.

choices.text: the maple leaf flag changes colors every season
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage says the maple leaf flag holds the country together, and never says it changes colors.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: Based on the article, how are the prairies different from the tundra?

choices.text: The prairies are covered in thick green forests, but the tundra has small fishing villages.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because thick forests and fishing villages are found in eastern Canada, not in the prairies or the tundra.

choices.text: The prairies are good for growing crops, but the tundra is too frozen for tall plants.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the article says prairies are flat fields used for growing grain, while the tundra is too frozen for tall plants to grow.

choices.text: The prairies are filled with deep valleys, but the tundra is made of solid ancient rock.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because deep valleys belong to the Rocky Mountains, and ancient rock describes the Canadian Shield — not the tundra.

choices.text: The prairies are home to wild polar bears, but the tundra is where most people live.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the article says polar bears live in the tundra, and most Canadians live in the warm south, not in the tundra.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: Based on the information in the article, what causes Canada to be described as 'The Giant Next Door'?

choices.text: It covers a massive amount of land and shares a very long border with the United States.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage shows Canada is enormous — it crosses six time zones and shares a nearly 5,525-mile border with the United States, making it a 'giant' right next door.

choices.text: It has a much larger population than the United States and grows most of the world's grain.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the article says Canada has about 40 million people while the United States has over 330 million, so Canada actually has far fewer people.

choices.text: It holds the oldest rocks on Earth and has thick forests that hide its many small villages.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because even though Canada has ancient rocks and forests, those facts are not what the title 'The Giant Next Door' is referring to.

choices.text: It is filled with tall mountains and has major cities that are built completely out of ice.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the article never says cities are built out of ice — Canada's major cities like Toronto and Vancouver are normal cities in the warmer southern part of the country.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: If you want to read about Canada's leaders first, and then read about its wild animals, which two headings should you choose in order?

choices.text: "A Country That Crosses Six Clocks" then "A Land That Changes with the Seasons"
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because 'A Country That Crosses Six Clocks' is about time zones, and 'A Land That Changes with the Seasons' is about seasons — neither one is mainly about leaders or wild animals.

choices.text: "Its Own Country, Its Own Flag" then "The Frozen North and the Warm South"
choices.feedback: B is correct because 'Its Own Country, Its Own Flag' mentions Canada's own leaders, and 'The Frozen North and the Warm South' talks about wild animals like polar bears and caribou — in exactly the right order.

choices.text: "The Frozen North and the Warm South" then "Its Own Country, Its Own Flag"
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because even though it has the right two sections, the order is flipped — it puts wild animals before leaders, but the question asks for leaders first.

choices.text: "Mountains, Shields, and Flat Golden Fields" then "A Country That Crosses Six Clocks"
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because 'Mountains, Shields, and Flat Golden Fields' is about land features like prairies and rocks, and 'A Country That Crosses Six Clocks' is about time zones — not leaders or wild animals.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: The main idea of the article is that Canada is a large, unique country. How does the author compare Canada to the United States to help explain this idea?

choices.text: By showing that both countries share large lakes and warm summer weather.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the article uses the shared Great Lakes and similar summer warmth to compare Canada and the United States, helping show what makes Canada unique and large.

choices.text: By showing that both countries have the same number of people living there.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the article says Canada has about 40 million people and the U.S. has over 330 million, so they have very different numbers of people.

choices.text: By showing that both countries fly the exact same red-and-white maple leaf flag.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the red-and-white maple leaf flag belongs only to Canada — the article uses it as an example of what makes Canada its own independent country.

choices.text: By showing that both countries have a frozen, treeless desert in the far north.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the frozen tundra is only described as part of Canada's far north, and the article never says the United States has a frozen, treeless desert too.

syllabus_metadata.title: Our Neighbor to the North: Exploring Canada's Land

assessments.title: Provinces and Territories: How Canada Is Organized

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Canada's Provinces and Territories

The World's Longest Skating Rink

Every winter in Ottawa, Canada's capital city, a canal freezes into a skating rink nearly five miles long. Thousands of people glide past snow-covered government buildings, sipping hot chocolate in the cold air. This is the Rideau Canal, and the city around it is where Canada's leaders meet to make laws. Ottawa sits along the Ottawa River in the province of Ontario, near the border with Quebec. Canada's Parliament — the group of people who write and vote on the country's laws, like Congress in the United States — meets here.
stimulus.content_text:
Canada's Provinces and TerritoriesThe World's Longest Skating Rink
Every winter in Ottawa, Canada's capital city, a canal freezes into a skating rink nearly five miles long. Thousands of people glide past snow-covered government buildings, sipping hot chocolate in the cold air. This is the Rideau Canal, and the city around it is where Canada's leaders meet to make laws. Ottawa sits along the Ottawa River in the province of Ontario, near the border with Quebec. Canada's Parliament — the group of people who write and vote on the country's laws, like Congress in the United States — meets here.

items.prompt: According to the text, what is the difference between the law-making groups in Canada and the United States?

choices.text: Canada's group is called Parliament, while the United States group is called Congress.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage directly says Canada's group is called Parliament and compares it to the United States group called Congress.

choices.text: Canada's group meets in the cold winter, while the United States group meets in summer.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the passage never mentions what season the United States group meets.

choices.text: Canada's group only writes the new laws, while the United States group votes on them.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the passage says Parliament writes AND votes on laws, so both groups do both jobs.

choices.text: Canada's group skates on a frozen canal, while the United States group sits in offices.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the passage never describes where the United States group meets or works.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Two Languages, One Country

Ontario and Quebec are Canada's two biggest provinces by population. Ontario is home to Toronto, Canada's largest city, and to thundering Niagara Falls, which drops water about 167 feet on the Canadian side. Quebec is the largest province by area, and here's the surprising part — walk down a street in Quebec City, and almost everyone around you is speaking French.

Today, Quebec's signs, schools, restaurants, and government all run in French. But most Canadians outside Quebec speak mainly English. French lives strongest in Quebec, and it gives Canada a culture unlike anywhere else in North America. Britain took control of Canada in 1763, but the French language held on in Quebec. Canada is officially bilingual, meaning it has two official languages: English and French, a status it has held since the Official Languages Act was passed in 1969.
stimulus.content_text:
Two Languages, One Country
Ontario and Quebec are Canada's two biggest provinces by population. Ontario is home to Toronto, Canada's largest city, and to thundering Niagara Falls, which drops water about 167 feet on the Canadian side. Quebec is the largest province by area, and here's the surprising part — walk down a street in Quebec City, and almost everyone around you is speaking French.
Today, Quebec's signs, schools, restaurants, and government all run in French. But most Canadians outside Quebec speak mainly English. French lives strongest in Quebec, and it gives Canada a culture unlike anywhere else in North America. Britain took control of Canada in 1763, but the French language held on in Quebec. Canada is officially bilingual, meaning it has two official languages: English and French, a status it has held since the Official Languages Act was passed in 1969.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, why does Canada have two official languages today?

choices.text: Because Britain made everyone speak both English and French in 1763
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because Britain took control in 1763 but did NOT make everyone speak both languages — most of Canada outside Quebec speaks mainly English.

choices.text: Because Quebec kept speaking French even after Britain took control
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage says 'the French language held on in Quebec' even after Britain took over, which is why Canada later became officially bilingual.

choices.text: Because Ontario and Quebec decided to trade their languages equally
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never mentions Ontario and Quebec making any kind of agreement to trade or share languages equally.

choices.text: Because French settlers moved to Ontario and spread their language
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says French stayed strong in Quebec specifically, not that French settlers moved to Ontario and spread the language there.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
West, East, and the Wide Middle

It's easy to picture Canada as one big frozen place. In the west, British Columbia has ancient forests, rocky coastlines, and the city of Vancouver — where winters are mild and rainy, not snowy. Further inland, Saskatchewan and Manitoba spread out in flat golden wheat fields. Together, these prairie provinces produce millions of tons of wheat each year, making Canada one of the world's top wheat exporters.

Along the eastern coast sit the Atlantic provinces: New Brunswick, Nova Scotia, Prince Edward Island, and Newfoundland and Labrador. These places have a long history of fishing and seafaring — traveling and working on the ocean. Their small towns line dramatic, rocky shores. Prince Edward Island, the smallest province with an area of about 2,185 square miles, is famous as the setting of the beloved novel Anne of Green Gables, published in 1908.
stimulus.content_text:

Further inland, Saskatchewan and Manitoba spread out in flat golden wheat fields. Together, these prairie provinces produce millions of tons of wheat each year, making Canada one of the world's top wheat exporters.

Prince Edward Island, the smallest province with an area of about 2,185 square miles, is famous as the setting of the beloved novel Anne of Green Gables, published in 1908.

items.prompt: According to the text, what is located further inland from British Columbia?

choices.text: Ancient and rainy forests
choices.feedback: Ancient forests and mild, rainy weather describe British Columbia itself, not the land further inland.

choices.text: Flat golden wheat fields
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says 'Further inland, Saskatchewan and Manitoba spread out in flat golden wheat fields,' which is exactly what is found inland from British Columbia.

choices.text: Dramatic and rocky shores
choices.feedback: Dramatic, rocky shores describe the Atlantic provinces on the eastern coast of Canada, not the area inland from British Columbia.

choices.text: Small ocean fishing towns
choices.feedback: Small ocean fishing towns are part of the Atlantic provinces in eastern Canada, not the inland area near British Columbia.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
The Vast North

Above all of these provinces lie three huge territories: Yukon, Northwest Territories, and Nunavut. Together they cover more than half of Canada's total land area, but fewer than 120,000 people live there. These territories are home to many Indigenous peoples — the First Nations, Inuit, and Métis communities whose ancestors lived on this land for thousands of years, long before European settlers arrived. Indigenous cultures, languages, and traditions are not just part of Canada's past.

Nunavut is the largest and newest territory. It was created on April 1, 1999, to give the Inuit people more control over their traditional homeland. The word "Nunavut" means "our land" in Inuktitut, the Inuit language.
stimulus.content_text:
The Vast North

Together they cover more than half of Canada's total land area, but fewer than 120,000 people live there. These territories are home to many Indigenous peoples — the First Nations, Inuit, and Métis communities whose ancestors lived on this land for thousands of years, long before European settlers arrived. Indigenous cultures, languages, and traditions are not just part of Canada's past.
Nunavut is the largest and newest territory. It was created on April 1, 1999, to give the Inuit people more control over their traditional homeland. The word "Nunavut" means "our land" in Inuktitut, the Inuit language.

items.prompt: According to the passage, how does the history of Indigenous peoples in the territories compare to the history of European settlers?

choices.text: Indigenous ancestors lived there long before the European settlers.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage says Indigenous ancestors lived there 'for thousands of years, long before European settlers arrived,' which means they were there first.

choices.text: Indigenous ancestors lived there long after the European settlers.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because it says Indigenous ancestors came after Europeans, but the passage says the exact opposite — they were there thousands of years before Europeans.

choices.text: Indigenous ancestors arrived there alongside the European settlers.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because 'alongside' means at the same time, but the passage says Indigenous peoples were there long before Europeans showed up.

choices.text: Indigenous ancestors moved away because of the European settlers.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the passage never says anything about Indigenous ancestors moving away — that idea is not in the passage at all.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
A Country of Many Stories

Canada is not one single place with one single story. Indigenous nations have called this land home for at least 15,000 years, long before any map was drawn. Its population of about 40 million people includes French grandmothers in Quebec, Inuit hunters in Nunavut, surfers off the coast of British Columbia, and wheat farmers watching golden fields bend in the prairie wind. Ten provinces and three territories, two official languages, and thousands of years of history make Canada one of the most diverse countries on Earth.
stimulus.content_text:
A Country of Many Stories
Canada is not one single place with one single story. Indigenous nations have called this land home for at least 15,000 years, long before any map was drawn. Its population of about 40 million people includes French grandmothers in Quebec, Inuit hunters in Nunavut, surfers off the coast of British Columbia, and wheat farmers watching golden fields bend in the prairie wind. Ten provinces and three territories, two official languages, and thousands of years of history make Canada one of the most diverse countries on Earth.

items.prompt: According to the text, how is Canada described because of its history, languages, and provinces?

choices.text: A single place with one story
choices.feedback: This is wrong because the passage says the opposite — it starts by telling us Canada is NOT one single place with one single story.

choices.text: A land with only one language
choices.feedback: This is wrong because the passage clearly says Canada has two official languages, not only one.

choices.text: One of the most diverse countries
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage says Canada's many provinces, territories, languages, and long history make it 'one of the most diverse countries on Earth.'

choices.text: A newly mapped place without history
choices.feedback: This is wrong because the passage tells us Indigenous nations have lived on this land for at least 15,000 years, which means Canada has a very long and rich history.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: Based on the article, how does the winter weather in Ottawa contrast with the winter weather in Vancouver?

choices.text: Ottawa has very mild and rainy winters, but Vancouver has freezing and snowy winters.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because it has the cities switched — Ottawa is the cold, snowy one, and Vancouver is the mild, rainy one, not the other way around.

choices.text: Ottawa has freezing and snowy winters, but Vancouver has mild and rainy winters.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the article shows Ottawa gets freezing, snowy winters (the canal turns into a skating rink!) while Vancouver has mild and rainy winters.

choices.text: Ottawa has very windy and dry winters, but Vancouver has freezing and snowy winters.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the article never says Ottawa's winters are windy and dry — it shows Ottawa is cold and snowy.

choices.text: Ottawa has freezing and snowy winters, but Vancouver has very cold and windy winters.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the article says Vancouver's winters are mild and rainy, not cold and windy.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: Which heading would help you most if you wanted to find out why Canada has both English and French today?

choices.text: The World's Longest Skating Rink
choices.feedback: This section is about the Rideau Canal skating rink in Ottawa, so it won't help you learn about Canada's two languages.

choices.text: Two Languages, One Country
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because this section explains how French survived in Quebec even after Britain took over, and how Canada became officially bilingual in 1969.

choices.text: West, East, and the Wide Middle
choices.feedback: This section describes different regions of Canada like the prairies and Atlantic coast, but doesn't explain why Canada has two languages.

choices.text: A Country of Many Stories
choices.feedback: This section gives a broad overview of Canada's diversity, but it doesn't go into detail about why both English and French are spoken today.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: The author shares a sequence of events, from Indigenous peoples living on the land first, to Britain taking control in 1763, to the creation of Nunavut in 1999. Which main idea do these events support?

choices.text: Canada's land and laws have changed over time to include many different groups.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage shows Canada's land and laws changed over thousands of years to include Indigenous peoples, European settlers, and groups like the Inuit who gained their own territory in 1999.

choices.text: Canada's provinces and territories were all created at the exact same time historically.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the passage shows territories were created at very different times — for example, Nunavut wasn't created until 1999.

choices.text: Canada's government has always been run by European settlers since the very beginning.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because Indigenous peoples lived on the land for at least 15,000 years before Europeans arrived, and Canada later created Nunavut to give the Inuit more control over their homeland.

choices.text: Canada's huge northern territories have always been home to the most people overall.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage says fewer than 120,000 people live in the vast northern territories, making them the least populated part of Canada.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: Based on the article, how do the three northern territories compare to large provinces like Ontario and Quebec?

choices.text: They have a much larger amount of land but a much smaller number of people.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage says the territories cover more than half of Canada's land but have fewer than 120,000 people — lots of land, very few people.

choices.text: They have a much smaller amount of land but a much larger number of people.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because it says the territories have less land, but the passage tells us they actually cover more than half of Canada's total land area.

choices.text: They have a similar amount of land and a similar number of people overall.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the territories are very different from big provinces — they have way more land but way fewer people, not similar amounts of either.

choices.text: They have a much larger amount of land and a much larger number of people.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because while the territories do have a huge amount of land, the passage says very few people live there, not a large number.

syllabus_metadata.title: Provinces and Territories: How Canada Is Organized

assessments.title: Two Countries, One Continent: Canada and the United States

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Canada: The Giant Next Door

Bigger Than You Think

You drive for hours. Then days. You cross into Canada and keep going — through thick forests, past frozen lakes, over wide prairies, and along rocky coastlines. You still have not reached the other side. Canada is the second-largest country on Earth, actually bigger than the United States in land area. But far fewer people live there. About 40 million Canadians share all that space, while over 330 million people live in the United States.
stimulus.content_text:
Canada: The Giant Next DoorBigger Than You Think
You drive for hours. Then days. You cross into Canada and keep going — through thick forests, past frozen lakes, over wide prairies, and along rocky coastlines. You still have not reached the other side. Canada is the second-largest country on Earth, actually bigger than the United States in land area. But far fewer people live there. About 40 million Canadians share all that space, while over 330 million people live in the United States.

items.prompt: According to the text, what is different about Canada and the United States in terms of how many people live there?

choices.text: Canada has more people because it is bigger.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because even though Canada is bigger in size, the passage says far fewer people live there — size and population are different things!

choices.text: Canada has far fewer people than the United States.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage clearly states that only about 40 million people live in Canada, while over 330 million people live in the United States, making Canada's population much smaller.

choices.text: Both countries have about the same number of people.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because 40 million and 330 million are very different numbers, so the two countries do not have about the same number of people.

choices.text: The United States has fewer people than Canada.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because it has it backwards — the United States has more people than Canada, not fewer.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Two Languages on Every Cereal Box

Canada is bilingual, which means it has two official languages — English and French. You might think everyone in Canada speaks French. Actually, most Canadians speak English as their main language. French is spoken mostly in a province called Quebec and in small parts of a few other provinces. Still, the two-language rule shows up everywhere. Highway signs, cereal boxes, and government papers all appear in both English and French. This tradition goes back hundreds of years, when both English and French settlers built communities across the land. In the United States, many languages are spoken at home, but English is used for most government work.
stimulus.content_text:
Two Languages on Every Cereal Box
Canada is bilingual, which means it has two official languages — English and French. You might think everyone in Canada speaks French. Actually, most Canadians speak English as their main language. French is spoken mostly in a province called Quebec and in small parts of a few other provinces. Still, the two-language rule shows up everywhere. Highway signs, cereal boxes, and government papers all appear in both English and French. This tradition goes back hundreds of years, when both English and French settlers built communities across the land. In the United States, many languages are spoken at home, but English is used for most government work.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, which key detail best supports the main idea that Canada is a bilingual country because of its history?

choices.text: Most Canadians speak English as their main language today
choices.feedback: This tells us what most Canadians speak today, but it doesn't explain the historical reason Canada became bilingual.

choices.text: English and French settlers both built communities long ago
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says English and French settlers both built communities long ago, and that history is exactly why Canada has two official languages.

choices.text: The United States uses English for most government work
choices.feedback: This is about the United States, so it doesn't support the main idea about Canada's bilingual history.

choices.text: French is spoken mostly in the province called Quebec
choices.feedback: This tells us where French is spoken now, but it doesn't explain the historical reason behind Canada being bilingual.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
A Prime Minister, Not a President

Canada runs its government differently than the United States does. Canada uses a parliamentary system — a way of governing where the leader comes from the group of lawmakers, like a team captain chosen by the players. The leader is called the prime minister. Canadians vote for members of Parliament, which is the group that makes laws. The political party that wins the most seats picks the prime minister. In the United States, voters choose the president in a separate election from the lawmakers in Congress.

Canada is also a constitutional monarchy. That means it officially recognizes the King of Britain as the head of state. You might think the King actually runs Canada and makes its laws. He does not. His role is ceremonial — meaning it is for tradition and special occasions only, like cutting a ribbon at a grand opening. The prime minister and Parliament make all the real decisions.
stimulus.content_text:
A Prime Minister, Not a President
Canada runs its government differently than the United States does. Canada uses a parliamentary system — a way of governing where the leader comes from the group of lawmakers, like a team captain chosen by the players. The leader is called the prime minister. Canadians vote for members of Parliament, which is the group that makes laws. The political party that wins the most seats picks the prime minister. In the United States, voters choose the president in a separate election from the lawmakers in Congress.
Canada is also a constitutional monarchy. That means it officially recognizes the King of Britain as the head of state. You might think the King actually runs Canada and makes its laws. He does not. His role is ceremonial — meaning it is for tradition and special occasions only, like cutting a ribbon at a grand opening. The prime minister and Parliament make all the real decisions.

items.prompt: According to the text, what happens first before Canada has a prime minister?

choices.text: The King of Britain picks the leader.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage clearly says the King's role is only ceremonial, like cutting a ribbon, and he does not make real decisions or pick Canada's leader.

choices.text: Canadians vote for members of Parliament.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage explains that Canadians vote for Parliament members first, and then the winning party chooses the prime minister — voting comes before a prime minister is picked.

choices.text: The prime minister chooses the lawmakers.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect and actually has it backwards — the passage says Parliament members are voted in first, and they help choose the prime minister, not the other way around.

choices.text: The president holds a separate election.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because separate elections for a president happen in the United States, not in Canada, which uses a different system called a parliamentary system.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Trading Partners and Shared Lakes

Every single day, trucks loaded with cars, oil, lumber, food, and factory-made goods roll across the border between Canada and the United States. These two countries are each other's biggest trading partners. They exchange billions of dollars in goods each year. When one country's economy slows down, the other feels it too.

They also share the Great Lakes and work together to keep that water clean. Their militaries watch the skies together through an organization called NORAD — a joint team that tracks anything flying over North America. The border between them stretches for thousands of miles, and most of it has no soldiers guarding it. In many spots, only a line of small markers in a forest or a field shows where one country ends and the other begins.
stimulus.content_text:
Trading Partners and Shared Lakes
Every single day, trucks loaded with cars, oil, lumber, food, and factory-made goods roll across the border between Canada and the United States. These two countries are each other's biggest trading partners. They exchange billions of dollars in goods each year. When one country's economy slows down, the other feels it too.
They also share the Great Lakes and work together to keep that water clean. Their militaries watch the skies together through an organization called NORAD — a joint team that tracks anything flying over North America. The border between them stretches for thousands of miles, and most of it has no soldiers guarding it. In many spots, only a line of small markers in a forest or a field shows where one country ends and the other begins.

items.prompt: The author calls NORAD a "joint team." Which phrase from the passage compares to this idea to help explain what "joint" means?

choices.text: watch the skies together
choices.feedback: A is correct because 'watch the skies together' uses the word 'together,' which shows two countries sharing one job — just like 'joint' means.

choices.text: exchange billions of dollars
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because exchanging billions of dollars is about trading, not about two groups working as one team.

choices.text: roll across the border
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because trucks rolling across the border describes movement of goods, not teamwork between two countries.

choices.text: keep that water clean
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because keeping water clean is another example of cooperation, but it is not the phrase right next to 'joint team' that helps explain its meaning.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Red Maple Leaves and October Thanksgiving

Hockey is not just a sport there — it is a national passion. Canadians celebrate Thanksgiving in October, not November. And the Canadian flag, bright red with a single maple leaf in the center, is known around the world.

Canada and the United States are not the same country. They govern differently, speak different combinations of languages, and celebrate on different dates. But their friendship has lasted for centuries, built on a shared belief in freedom and democracy. Being good neighbors does not mean being identical. It means showing up, working together, and respecting what makes the other side different.
stimulus.content_text:
Red Maple Leaves and October Thanksgiving
Hockey is not just a sport there — it is a national passion. Canadians celebrate Thanksgiving in October, not November. And the Canadian flag, bright red with a single maple leaf in the center, is known around the world.
Canada and the United States are not the same country. They govern differently, speak different combinations of languages, and celebrate on different dates. But their friendship has lasted for centuries, built on a shared belief in freedom and democracy. Being good neighbors does not mean being identical. It means showing up, working together, and respecting what makes the other side different.

items.prompt: According to the section 'Red Maple Leaves and October Thanksgiving,' what does it mean to be good neighbors?

choices.text: Showing up, working together, and respecting differences.
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage uses these exact words: 'It means showing up, working together, and respecting what makes the other side different.'

choices.text: Having the same government and speaking the same languages.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because the passage says Canada and the United States govern differently and speak different combinations of languages — and they are still good neighbors!

choices.text: Celebrating the same holidays on the exact same dates.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because the passage points out that Canada celebrates Thanksgiving in October and the United States celebrates it in November, yet they are still great neighbors.

choices.text: Playing hockey together and sharing a national passion.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because hockey is mentioned as something Canadians love, but the passage never says playing hockey together is what makes two countries good neighbors.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: According to the article, which statement describes how Canada is different from the United States?

choices.text: It prints cereal boxes in two languages and recognizes a king for special occasions.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the article specifically says cereal boxes appear in both English and French in Canada, and that the King's role is ceremonial — just for tradition and special occasions — which are both things that make Canada different from the United States.

choices.text: It uses mostly English for government work and chooses a leader in separate elections.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because using mostly English for government work and choosing a leader in a separate election are things the article says about the United States, not Canada.

choices.text: It tracks flying objects with a joint team and shares water in the Great Lakes.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because tracking flying objects with NORAD and sharing the Great Lakes are things Canada and the United States do together, so they don't make Canada different.

choices.text: It speaks many languages in the home and uses a team captain for making laws.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the article says speaking many languages at home describes the United States, and while Canada does have a team-like system for picking its leader, this answer mixes up details from both countries.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: Why does the article say Canada and the United States need to work closely together?

choices.text: Because they share water and trade many goods
choices.feedback: This is correct! The article says the two countries trade billions of dollars in goods every day and share the Great Lakes, so they need to cooperate to keep things running well.

choices.text: Because they speak the same two official languages
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because Canada has two official languages (English and French), but the United States does not — so they do NOT share the same two official languages.

choices.text: Because they choose their leaders in the same way
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the article explains that Canada and the United States actually choose their leaders in very different ways — Canada has a prime minister chosen by Parliament, while the United States elects a president separately.

choices.text: Because they celebrate Thanksgiving on the same day
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the article actually points out that Canada celebrates Thanksgiving in October while the United States celebrates it in November — they do NOT share the same Thanksgiving date.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: Which sequence of details best supports the main idea that Canada's history shaped its unique culture and government?

choices.text: Long ago, settlers built towns, and today Canada uses two languages and keeps a king for ceremonies.
choices.feedback: A is correct because it correctly connects old history — settlers building towns — to two things Canada has today: two languages and a king who is only there for tradition.

choices.text: Long ago, voters chose a prime minister, and today English and French settlers build new communities.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because it gets things backwards — voters don't choose a prime minister directly, and English and French settlers built communities long ago, not today.

choices.text: Long ago, the King of Britain made laws, and today the lawmakers in Parliament vote for a president.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage says the King does NOT make laws, and Canada has a prime minister, not a president.

choices.text: Long ago, the two nations shared lakes, and today they use a joint team to watch the skies together.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because sharing lakes and watching the skies together is about Canada and the U.S. being good neighbors today, not about how Canada's history shaped its own culture and government.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: A student wants to compare how Canada and the United States run their governments and how they protect their shared resources. Based on the headings, which two sections should the student read?

choices.text: "Bigger Than You Think" and "Two Languages on Every Cereal Box"
choices.feedback: These sections talk about how big Canada is and its two languages — not about how the government works or how the two countries protect their shared land.

choices.text: "A Prime Minister, Not a President" and "Trading Partners and Shared Lakes"
choices.feedback: This is correct because 'A Prime Minister, Not a President' explains how each country's government works, and 'Trading Partners and Shared Lakes' explains how they protect their shared water and skies together.

choices.text: "A Prime Minister, Not a President" and "Red Maple Leaves and October Thanksgiving"
choices.feedback: The 'Red Maple Leaves and October Thanksgiving' section is about Canadian holidays and culture, not about protecting shared land.

choices.text: "Trading Partners and Shared Lakes" and "Bigger Than You Think"
choices.feedback: 'Bigger Than You Think' only tells us about Canada's size and how many people live there, which doesn't help compare governments or shared land protection.

syllabus_metadata.title: Two Countries, One Continent: Canada and the United States

assessments.title: Rivers of Life: The Nile, Ganges, and Huang He

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Important Rivers of the World

A Gift of Dark Mud

Five thousand years ago, a farmer in Egypt knelt beside the Nile River and scooped up a handful of dark, wet mud. That mud was a gift. Every year, the Nile flooded and left behind a thick layer of rich soil. Without it, the farmer's land would be nothing but dry, golden desert. With it, he could grow wheat, barley, and flax. That simple gift of mud helped build one of the greatest civilizations — large groups of people who share laws, cities, and a way of life — the world has ever known.

The Nile River stretches over 4,100 miles through northeastern Africa, making it the longest river on Earth. It starts near Lake Victoria and flows northward through Sudan and Egypt before pouring into the Mediterranean Sea. You might think all rivers flow south, since "down" looks like south on a map. But rivers follow gravity, not compass directions. Water flows from high ground to low ground, like a ball rolling downhill.
stimulus.content_text:
Important Rivers of the WorldA Gift of Dark Mud
Five thousand years ago, a farmer in Egypt knelt beside the Nile River and scooped up a handful of dark, wet mud. That mud was a gift. Every year, the Nile flooded and left behind a thick layer of rich soil. Without it, the farmer's land would be nothing but dry, golden desert. With it, he could grow wheat, barley, and flax. That simple gift of mud helped build one of the greatest civilizations — large groups of people who share laws, cities, and a way of life — the world has ever known.
The Nile River stretches over 4,100 miles through northeastern Africa, making it the longest river on Earth. It starts near Lake Victoria and flows northward through Sudan and Egypt before pouring into the Mediterranean Sea. You might think all rivers flow south, since "down" looks like south on a map. But rivers follow gravity, not compass directions. Water flows from high ground to low ground, like a ball rolling downhill.

items.prompt: According to the section "A Gift of Dark Mud," which contrast does the author state about the farmer's land?

choices.text: With the rich soil, the land could grow crops, but without it, the land was dry desert.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage directly states the land would be 'dry, golden desert' without the mud, but with it the farmer could grow crops like wheat and barley.

choices.text: With the rich soil, the land had laws and cities, but without it, the land had no people.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the passage connects laws and cities to civilization, not to whether the farmer's land had rich soil or not.

choices.text: With the rich soil, the land was near Lake Victoria, but without it, the land was by the sea.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because Lake Victoria and the Mediterranean Sea describe where the Nile River starts and ends, not anything about the farmer's land.

choices.text: With the rich soil, the land flooded every year, but without it, the land never had water.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the flooding actually caused the rich soil to appear — the passage never says the land flooded because of the soil.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
A River People Call a Goddess

Thousands of miles to the east, another river means even more than food and water to the people who live near it. The Ganges River in India is considered sacred — deeply holy, the way a church or temple is holy — by Hindus. To them, the Ganges is not just a river. It is a goddess.

The Ganges begins high in the Himalayan mountains, where snow melts into icy streams. It flows more than 1,500 miles across northern India before reaching the Bay of Bengal. Millions of people bathe in its waters as a religious act. They believe the river can wash away their sins. It provides drinking water, supports industry, and waters farmland for hundreds of millions of people.
stimulus.content_text:
A River People Call a Goddess
Thousands of miles to the east, another river means even more than food and water to the people who live near it. The Ganges River in India is considered sacred — deeply holy, the way a church or temple is holy — by Hindus. To them, the Ganges is not just a river. It is a goddess.
The Ganges begins high in the Himalayan mountains, where snow melts into icy streams. It flows more than 1,500 miles across northern India before reaching the Bay of Bengal. Millions of people bathe in its waters as a religious act. They believe the river can wash away their sins. It provides drinking water, supports industry, and waters farmland for hundreds of millions of people.

items.prompt: Based on the text, why do millions of people bathe in the Ganges River?

choices.text: They believe it washes away their sins.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage directly says 'They believe the river can wash away their sins,' which is a religious reason for bathing.

choices.text: They need to gather safe drinking water.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the passage mentions drinking water as a separate use of the river, not as the reason people bathe in it.

choices.text: They want to cool off from the hot sun.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage never mentions hot weather or cooling off as a reason for bathing.

choices.text: They hope to bring water to their farms.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because watering farmland is a different use of the river mentioned in the passage, not the reason people bathe in it.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
China's Sorrow

In China, the Huang He — also called the Yellow River — earned its name from the yellowish silt it carries. Silt is made of tiny bits of sand, clay, and dirt. The Huang He carries so much silt that its water looks like flowing gold.

Some of the earliest Chinese settlements grew along this river's banks. That is why people call the Huang He the "cradle of Chinese civilization." Here is the surprising part about floods: they are not always disasters. Along the Nile, yearly floods helped farmers by spreading fertile soil — soil so rich and healthy that plants grow easily in it, like the dark dirt in a garden bed. But the Huang He's floods were different. Over many years, silt piled up and raised the riverbed higher and higher. People built levees — tall, thick walls of earth — to hold the water in. When those walls broke, water rushed across the flat land for miles, killing millions of people over the centuries. That is why the Huang He earned a second, sadder name: "China's Sorrow."
stimulus.content_text:
China's Sorrow

Some of the earliest Chinese settlements grew along this river's banks. That is why people call the Huang He the "cradle of Chinese civilization." Here is the surprising part about floods: they are not always disasters. Along the Nile, yearly floods helped farmers by spreading fertile soil — soil so rich and healthy that plants grow easily in it, like the dark dirt in a garden bed. But the Huang He's floods were different. Over many years, silt piled up and raised the riverbed higher and higher. People built levees — tall, thick walls of earth — to hold the water in. When those walls broke, water rushed across the flat land for miles, killing millions of people over the centuries. That is why the Huang He earned a second, sadder name: "China's Sorrow."

items.prompt: According to the passage, what happened after silt piled up and raised the riverbed of the Huang He?

choices.text: Farmers used the silt to grow crops easily
choices.feedback: This is wrong because the passage says farmers along the Nile — not the Huang He — used fertile silt to grow crops easily.

choices.text: People built levees to hold the water in
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly says that after silt raised the riverbed, people built levees to hold the water in.

choices.text: The river changed its name to the Yellow River
choices.feedback: This is wrong because the river got the name 'Yellow River' from its yellowish silt, not from the riverbed rising.

choices.text: Settlements first began along the river's banks
choices.feedback: This is wrong because settlements along the river's banks happened much earlier in the story, before the silt problem is even mentioned.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Why Rivers Still Matter

The Nile, the Ganges, and the Huang He share a pattern. People settled beside them because the rivers gave them what they needed most: water to drink, soil to farm, and a way to move goods by boat. Cities became great civilizations that lasted thousands of years.

Today, these three rivers still support hundreds of millions of people. But they face serious dangers from pollution and overuse. Protecting them matters — not just for the people living beside them right now, but for everyone who comes next.

Almost every time, you will spot a river running right through it. It is a pattern as old as human life itself.

Today, these three rivers still support hundreds of millions of people. But they face serious dangers from pollution and overuse. Protecting them matters — not just for the people living beside them right now, but for everyone who comes next.

items.prompt: In the sentence "The Nile, the Ganges, and the Huang He share a pattern," what does the word "share" show about the three rivers?

choices.text: They have the same important features.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage explains that all three rivers share the same important features — giving people water, farmable soil, and a way to travel by boat.

choices.text: They flow through the same country.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the Nile, Ganges, and Huang He are in different countries, and the passage never says they are in the same place.

choices.text: They started at the same time.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage does not mention anything about when these rivers started or formed.

choices.text: They carry the same amount of water.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage never talks about how much water each river carries or compares their water amounts.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: How does the author contrast the floods of the Nile River with the floods of the Huang He?

choices.text: By stating that the Nile brought yellow silt, but the Huang He brought dark mud.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because it swaps the details — the Nile had dark mud and the Huang He had yellow silt, not the other way around.

choices.text: By stating that the Nile helped farmers, but the Huang He caused deadly disasters.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage says the Nile's floods helped farmers with fertile soil, while the Huang He's floods were deadly disasters that killed millions of people.

choices.text: By stating that the Nile washed away sins, but the Huang He provided drinking water.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because washing away sins is something people believed about the Ganges River in India, not the Nile.

choices.text: By stating that the Nile destroyed big cities, but the Huang He ruined small farms.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage says the Nile helped build a great civilization — it never says the Nile destroyed cities.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: According to the passage, what caused great civilizations to grow along rivers like the Nile and the Huang He?

choices.text: The rivers provided the water and rich soil needed to grow crops.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage directly says people settled by rivers for water to drink, rich soil to farm, and a way to move goods, which helped build great civilizations.

choices.text: The rivers flowed quickly from high mountain peaks down into the ocean.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the passage mentions rivers flowing from high ground to low ground to explain gravity, not to explain why civilizations grew.

choices.text: The people wanted to build tall walls of earth to stop the floods.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because levees were built to hold back dangerous floods, not as a reason why people first came to live near rivers.

choices.text: The people believed the river water would wash away all of their sins.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the belief that river water washes away sins is only about the Ganges River and its religious meaning to Hindus, not the reason civilizations grew along rivers.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: Which sequence of events best supports the main idea about the history of these three rivers?

choices.text: People protected the rivers, then they built cities, and today the rivers are flooded.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage never says the rivers are flooded today — it says they face pollution and overuse.

choices.text: People settled by the water, then cities grew large, and today the rivers face danger.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage clearly shows people settled by the rivers first, cities and civilizations grew next, and today all three rivers face serious dangers.

choices.text: People polluted the water, then they started to farm, and today the rivers are sacred.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because people farmed before pollution became a problem, and only the Ganges is called sacred, not all three rivers.

choices.text: People built thick levees, then the water turned gold, and today the rivers are empty.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because levees and golden-colored water are details about only the Huang He, not a sequence that applies to all three rivers.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: To find information comparing two rivers that are similar because they both move special types of dirt, which two headings should you read?

choices.text: 'A Gift of Dark Mud' and 'China's Sorrow'
choices.feedback: This is correct because 'A Gift of Dark Mud' describes the Nile's rich dark mud and 'China's Sorrow' describes the Huang He's yellowish silt — both are about rivers that carry special types of dirt.

choices.text: 'A Gift of Dark Mud' and 'Why Rivers Still Matter'
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because 'Why Rivers Still Matter' talks about all three rivers in general and does not focus on special types of dirt.

choices.text: 'A River People Call a Goddess' and 'China's Sorrow'
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because 'A River People Call a Goddess' is about the Ganges being sacred to Hindus, not about the river carrying special dirt.

choices.text: 'A River People Call a Goddess' and 'Why Rivers Still Matter'
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because neither the Ganges section nor the final section focuses on rivers moving special types of dirt.

syllabus_metadata.title: Rivers of Life: The Nile, Ganges, and Huang He

assessments.title: Mighty Waters: The Amazon and the Congo

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Two Mighty Rivers: The Amazon and the Congo

A River Too Wide to See Across

Stand on the bank of the Amazon River in Brazil, and you cannot see the other side. This giant river carries more water than any other river on Earth — more than the next seven largest rivers put together.

You might think that the river with the most water must also be the longest. Actually, it isn't. The Nile River in Africa is generally considered the longest. The Amazon is the biggest by volume — the amount of water flowing through it. A river can be wide and deep without being the longest.

The Amazon begins as tiny streams high in the Andes Mountains of Peru. From there, it flows eastward across South America for about 4,000 miles. You might picture rivers flowing only north to south, straight downhill. But rivers twist and curve, following the lowest ground they can find. The Amazon winds east, collecting water from over 1,000 tributaries — smaller rivers that feed into a bigger one, the way small branches connect to a tree trunk.

Two Mighty Rivers: The Amazon and the CongoA River Too Wide to See Across
