You might think that the river with the most water must also be the longest. Actually, it isn't. The Nile River in Africa is generally considered the longest. The Amazon is the biggest by volume — the amount of water flowing through it. A river can be wide and deep without being the longest.

The Amazon begins as tiny streams high in the Andes Mountains of Peru. From there, it flows eastward across South America for about 4,000 miles. You might picture rivers flowing only north to south, straight downhill. But rivers twist and curve, following the lowest ground they can find. The Amazon winds east, collecting water from over 1,000 tributaries — smaller rivers that feed into a bigger one, the way small branches connect to a tree trunk.

items.prompt: According to the text, what is the difference between the Amazon River and the Nile River?

choices.text: The Amazon has the most water, but the Nile is the longest river.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage clearly states the Amazon has more water than any other river, while the Nile is generally considered the longest.

choices.text: The Amazon flows straight downhill, but the Nile twists and curves a lot.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the passage says the Amazon twists and curves to follow the lowest ground — it does not flow straight downhill.

choices.text: The Amazon is the longest river, but the Nile has the most water.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because it mixes up the two rivers — it's actually the Nile that is the longest, and the Amazon that has the most water.

choices.text: The Amazon is a tiny stream, but the Nile is a giant river.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the Amazon is described as a giant river — tiny streams are only where the Amazon starts, high up in the mountains.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Life in the Amazon Rainforest

The Amazon rainforest wraps around the river like a green blanket. It is the largest tropical rainforest on Earth. The Amazon basin — all the land that drains water into the Amazon, like a giant bowl tipped toward the river — holds an astonishing number of living things. Scientists have counted more than 40,000 plant species, 1,300 bird species, and 3,000 types of fish there. Pink dolphins swim through the muddy water. Jaguars hunt along the banks. Bright macaws flash red, blue, and yellow above the treetops. Every year, scientists still find species no one has ever seen before.
stimulus.content_text:
Life in the Amazon Rainforest
The Amazon rainforest wraps around the river like a green blanket. It is the largest tropical rainforest on Earth. The Amazon basin — all the land that drains water into the Amazon, like a giant bowl tipped toward the river — holds an astonishing number of living things. Scientists have counted more than 40,000 plant species, 1,300 bird species, and 3,000 types of fish there. Pink dolphins swim through the muddy water. Jaguars hunt along the banks. Bright macaws flash red, blue, and yellow above the treetops. Every year, scientists still find species no one has ever seen before.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, why does water drain into the Amazon River?

choices.text: Because the land is like a tipped bowl.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage directly compares the land to a 'giant bowl tipped toward the river,' which explains how the shape of the land causes water to drain into the Amazon.

choices.text: Because the forest is like a green blanket.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the 'green blanket' describes what the rainforest looks like wrapped around the river, not why water drains into it.

choices.text: Because the river is full of muddy water.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because muddy water describes what the river looks like, but it doesn't explain why water drains into the river.

choices.text: Because the basin is full of living things.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because living things tell us about the amazing wildlife in the basin, but they have nothing to do with how water drains into the river.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
The Congo: The World's Deepest River

Across the Atlantic Ocean, in the heart of Africa, the Congo River holds a different record. In some spots, the Congo plunges more than 700 feet down — deeper than a 60-story building is tall. Most rivers are shallow enough to measure in feet. The Congo drops to depths you would expect in the ocean.

The Congo does not flow in a straight line. It curves in a great arc — a wide, sweeping bend, like the curve of a rainbow — through central Africa for about 2,920 miles before reaching the Atlantic Ocean.

The Congo rainforest, the second largest in the world, grows thick along its banks. Gorillas, forest elephants, chimpanzees, and okapi — shy, striped animals related to giraffes — live here. Many of these creatures are found nowhere else on the planet.

The Congo: The World's Deepest River
Across the Atlantic Ocean, in the heart of Africa, the Congo River holds a different record. In some spots, the Congo plunges more than 700 feet down — deeper than a 60-story building is tall. Most rivers are shallow enough to measure in feet. The Congo drops to depths you would expect in the ocean.

The Congo does not flow in a straight line. It curves in a great arc — a wide, sweeping bend, like the curve of a rainbow — through central Africa for about 2,920 miles before reaching the Atlantic Ocean.

The Congo rainforest, the second largest in the world, grows thick along its banks. Gorillas, forest elephants, chimpanzees, and okapi — shy, striped animals related to giraffes — live here. Many of these creatures are found nowhere else on the planet.

items.prompt: According to the passage, which of the following describes the correct order of what the Congo River does as it moves through Africa?

choices.text: It reaches the Atlantic Ocean, then curves in a great arc through central Africa.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because it has the order backwards — the passage says the river curves through Africa first, and then reaches the Atlantic Ocean.

choices.text: It curves in a great arc through central Africa, then reaches the Atlantic Ocean.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage says the Congo curves through central Africa 'before reaching the Atlantic Ocean,' so the curving comes first.

choices.text: It flows in a straight line first, then curves into a great arc near the ocean.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage clearly says the Congo does NOT flow in a straight line.

choices.text: It starts at the Atlantic Ocean, then curves in a great arc through Africa.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the river begins in central Africa and flows toward the Atlantic Ocean — it does not start at the ocean.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Why These Rivers Matter to You

Even if you live far from South America or Africa, these rivers affect your life. Their rainforests pull a gas called carbon dioxide out of the air — the same gas you breathe out — and release oxygen back. Think of the trees as giant air filters, cleaning the atmosphere for the whole planet. The forests also store huge amounts of water and release it slowly, helping keep weather patterns steady around the world.

Both rivers face real danger. Deforestation — the cutting and burning of trees — is shrinking the rainforests year by year. Pollution from mining and farming poisons the water. As more people need land and resources, the pressure grows.

Every breath you take contains oxygen that forests like these helped produce. The Amazon and the Congo are not just faraway rivers on a map. They are part of the system that keeps the air you are breathing right now clean enough to fill your lungs.

Think of the trees as giant air filters, cleaning the atmosphere for the whole planet. The forests also store huge amounts of water and release it slowly, helping keep weather patterns steady around the world.

items.prompt: In the section, the author compares trees to 'giant air filters.' What do trees and air filters have in common, based on the passage?

choices.text: They both store water and release it slowly over time
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because storing water is something trees do, but air filters don't store water — so that's not what they have in common.

choices.text: They both remove unwanted things from the air
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage says trees pull carbon dioxide out of the air and release clean oxygen, just like a filter removes unwanted things to clean the air.

choices.text: They both grow larger when they get more sunlight
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage never mentions sunlight, and air filters don't grow at all.

choices.text: They both need carbon dioxide to keep working
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage doesn't say trees or air filters need carbon dioxide to keep working — trees actually remove carbon dioxide from the air.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: Based on the article, how is the Amazon River different from the Congo River?

choices.text: The Amazon holds the most water, but the Congo is the deepest.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage clearly states the Amazon holds the most water by volume, while the Congo is the world's deepest river at over 700 feet deep.

choices.text: The Amazon is the longest river, but the Congo is the widest.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the article says the Nile River, not the Amazon, is generally considered the longest river on Earth.

choices.text: The Amazon flows straight downhill, but the Congo curves in an arc.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the article says the Amazon also winds and curves — and it is actually the Congo that is described as curving in a great arc.

choices.text: The Amazon is in central Africa, but the Congo is in South America.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the locations are completely flipped — the Amazon is in South America and the Congo is in Africa, not the other way around.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: Based on the article, why would cutting down trees in the Amazon and Congo rainforests cause problems for people all over the world?

choices.text: because the rivers would stop flowing to the ocean
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the article never says that cutting down trees would stop the rivers from flowing to the ocean.

choices.text: because fewer trees would mean less clean air for everyone
choices.feedback: B is correct because the article explains that rainforest trees clean the air for the whole planet by removing carbon dioxide and releasing oxygen, so fewer trees means less clean air for everyone.

choices.text: because animals would move to other countries to find food
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the article does not mention animals moving to other countries if trees are cut down.

choices.text: because the rivers would become too deep for boats to travel
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the article never connects deforestation to rivers becoming too deep for boats to travel.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: Which order of key details does the author use to share the main idea about these two mighty rivers?

choices.text: First, describing their huge size; next, listing the animals; last, explaining how they clean the air.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage really does go in this order: big size first, then animals, then how the rivers clean our air.

choices.text: First, explaining how they clean the air; next, listing the animals; last, describing their huge size.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the author talks about cleaning the air last, not first — the order is flipped.

choices.text: First, naming the longest river in the world; next, talking about dolphins; last, explaining the dangers of mining.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because those are small details from the passage, not the main ideas the author uses to build the whole article.

choices.text: First, stating they flow in straight lines; next, saying they are shallow; last, explaining they only help locals.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the passage says the rivers curve and twist, are extremely deep, and help people all around the world — the opposite of what D says.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: Based on the article, how are the paths of the Amazon and the Congo rivers similar?

choices.text: They both travel in a straight line from north to south.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the article specifically says you might picture rivers flowing north to south in a straight line, but that is NOT how rivers actually move.

choices.text: They both twist and curve as they move across the land.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the article describes both the Amazon as twisting and curving and the Congo as curving in a great arc like a rainbow.

choices.text: They both plunge down deeper than a sixty-story building is tall.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because only the Congo is described as plunging more than 700 feet deep — the article never says the Amazon is that deep.

choices.text: They both shrink year by year because of heavy water pollution.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the article says the rainforests are shrinking because of deforestation (cutting down trees), not because the rivers themselves are shrinking from pollution.

syllabus_metadata.title: Mighty Waters: The Amazon and the Congo

assessments.title: Rivers That Built Nations: The Mississippi, Danube, Rhine, and Volga

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Important Rivers of the World

A Tiny Stream Becomes a Giant

A stream so small you could step across it trickles out of a lake in Minnesota. That stream flows south, and other streams join it. Then small rivers pour into it. Mile after mile, it grows wider and stronger. By the time it reaches the Gulf of Mexico, 2,340 miles later, it has become the mighty Mississippi River.

You might think rivers start out big. Actually, almost every river begins as a tiny stream. It grows because other streams and rivers flow into it along the way. The area of land that sends all its water into one river is called a basin — like a giant bowl that tips all its rain toward one drain. The Mississippi River basin collects water from 31 U.S. States, stretching from the Rocky Mountains to the Appalachian Mountains.
stimulus.content_text:
Important Rivers of the WorldA Tiny Stream Becomes a Giant
A stream so small you could step across it trickles out of a lake in Minnesota. That stream flows south, and other streams join it. Then small rivers pour into it. Mile after mile, it grows wider and stronger. By the time it reaches the Gulf of Mexico, 2,340 miles later, it has become the mighty Mississippi River.

You might think rivers start out big. Actually, almost every river begins as a tiny stream. It grows because other streams and rivers flow into it along the way. The area of land that sends all its water into one river is called a basin — like a giant bowl that tips all its rain toward one drain. The Mississippi River basin collects water from 31 U.S. States, stretching from the Rocky Mountains to the Appalachian Mountains.

items.prompt: According to the text, how is the Mississippi River at its start different from the Mississippi River at its end?

choices.text: It starts in the Gulf of Mexico and ends in Minnesota
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the river starts in Minnesota and ends at the Gulf of Mexico, not the other way around.

choices.text: It starts as a tiny stream and ends as a mighty river
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage clearly says the river begins as a tiny stream you could step across and grows into the mighty Mississippi by the end.

choices.text: It starts with water from 31 states and ends smaller
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the water from 31 states is collected along the way as the river grows, not something that happens at the start.

choices.text: It starts as a big river and ends as a small stream
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage says almost every river starts as a tiny stream and grows bigger, not starts big and gets smaller.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
The Mississippi: America's Highway of Water

The Mississippi passes through or borders ten states. For hundreds of years, people have used it like a road made of water. Native American peoples traveled it long before Europeans arrived. French explorers paddled canoes down its length. In the 1800s, steamboats — boats powered by steam engines that turned big paddle wheels — carried people and goods up and down the river. Today, barges haul millions of tons of grain, coal, and other products along its waters. A barge is a large, flat boat built to carry heavy loads, like a floating truck. The cities of Minneapolis, St. Louis, Memphis, and New Orleans all grew along the Mississippi because of the trade it carried.
stimulus.content_text:
The Mississippi: America's Highway of Water
The Mississippi passes through or borders ten states. For hundreds of years, people have used it like a road made of water. Native American peoples traveled it long before Europeans arrived. French explorers paddled canoes down its length. In the 1800s, steamboats — boats powered by steam engines that turned big paddle wheels — carried people and goods up and down the river. Today, barges haul millions of tons of grain, coal, and other products along its waters. A barge is a large, flat boat built to carry heavy loads, like a floating truck. The cities of Minneapolis, St. Louis, Memphis, and New Orleans all grew along the Mississippi because of the trade it carried.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, why did cities like St. Louis and New Orleans grow along the Mississippi River?

choices.text: because the river marked the borders of ten states
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage mentions the river borders ten states, but it never says that's the reason cities grew there.

choices.text: because the river carried trade and goods
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly says those cities grew along the Mississippi 'because of the trade it carried.'

choices.text: because French explorers built those cities first
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage only says French explorers paddled canoes on the river — it never says they built those cities.

choices.text: because steamboats needed places to turn around
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage never mentions steamboats needing places to turn around — that detail is not in the text at all.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Europe's Rivers Flow in Surprising Directions

Across the Atlantic Ocean, the Danube River holds a special record. It passes through ten European countries — more than any other river on Earth. It flows from the Black Forest in Germany through Austria, Hungary, Serbia, Romania, and other nations before emptying into the Black Sea. Vienna, Budapest, and Belgrade are great cities built along its banks. For thousands of years, the Danube has been a meeting place for different cultures and peoples.

Another European river, the Rhine, is much shorter than the Mississippi or the Danube. Here's the surprising part: the Rhine has been just as important. For over two thousand years, since the time of the Roman Empire, it has been a major trade route. It flows from the Swiss Alps through Germany and the Netherlands before reaching the North Sea. Stone castles dot the hills along its banks. Barges loaded with goods travel its waters every single day.

Rivers do not always flow south. They flow downhill, and "downhill" can point any direction on a map. The Rhine flows mostly northward.
stimulus.content_text:

Across the Atlantic Ocean, the Danube River holds a special record. It passes through ten European countries — more than any other river on Earth. It flows from the Black Forest in Germany through Austria, Hungary, Serbia, Romania, and other nations before emptying into the Black Sea. Vienna, Budapest, and Belgrade are great cities built along its banks. For thousands of years, the Danube has been a meeting place for different cultures and peoples.

Another European river, the Rhine, is much shorter than the Mississippi or the Danube. Here's the surprising part: the Rhine has been just as important. For over two thousand years, since the time of the Roman Empire, it has been a major trade route. It flows from the Swiss Alps through Germany and the Netherlands before reaching the North Sea. Stone castles dot the hills along its banks. Barges loaded with goods travel its waters every single day.

Rivers do not always flow south. They flow downhill, and "downhill" can point any direction on a map. The Rhine flows mostly northward.

items.prompt: According to the text, in what order does the Danube river flow through different places?

choices.text: Starts at the Black Forest, goes through Austria, and ends at the Black Sea.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage clearly says the Danube starts at the Black Forest, flows through Austria (and other countries), and ends at the Black Sea.

choices.text: Starts at the Black Sea, goes through Austria, and finally ends at the Black Forest.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because it has the Danube going the wrong direction — the river starts at the Black Forest and ends at the Black Sea, not the other way around.

choices.text: Starts at the Swiss Alps, goes through Germany, and ends at the North Sea.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because starting at the Swiss Alps and ending at the North Sea describes the Rhine River, not the Danube.

choices.text: Starts at the North Sea, goes through Germany, and ends at the Swiss Alps.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because it describes the Rhine River in reverse — the Rhine starts in the Swiss Alps and flows to the North Sea, not the other way around.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Mother Volga

Far to the east, the longest river in Europe winds through the heart of Russia. The Volga River stretches about 2,300 miles to the Caspian Sea. Russians call it "Mother Volga" because so much of their life depends on it. About one-third of Russia's people live near the Volga. They use its water for farming, drinking, and factories. Hydroelectric dams along the Volga turn the push of flowing water into electricity — the same way a spinning wheel can power a machine. These dams light up Russian cities.
stimulus.content_text:
Mother Volga
Far to the east, the longest river in Europe winds through the heart of Russia. The Volga River stretches about 2,300 miles to the Caspian Sea. Russians call it "Mother Volga" because so much of their life depends on it. About one-third of Russia's people live near the Volga. They use its water for farming, drinking, and factories. Hydroelectric dams along the Volga turn the push of flowing water into electricity — the same way a spinning wheel can power a machine. These dams light up Russian cities.

items.prompt: According to the text, how is a hydroelectric dam similar to a spinning wheel?

choices.text: They both use movement to create power.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage directly compares both a hydroelectric dam and a spinning wheel as things that use movement — flowing water or spinning — to create power.

choices.text: They both provide water for large farms.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the passage never connects spinning wheels to providing water for farms.

choices.text: They both stretch across the whole country.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because neither a spinning wheel nor a dam stretches across a country — that description is about the Volga River.

choices.text: They both light up the cities in Russia.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because lighting up cities is the result of the dam's power, not the reason the dam and a spinning wheel are similar.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
More Than Water

The Mississippi connected America's heartland to the sea. The Danube linked the peoples of ten nations. The Rhine powered trade across Western Europe. The Volga fed and powered Russia.

Follow it on a map. Every river is going somewhere, and somewhere along its banks, someone's life depends on it.
stimulus.content_text:
More Than Water
The Mississippi connected America's heartland to the sea. The Danube linked the peoples of ten nations. The Rhine powered trade across Western Europe. The Volga fed and powered Russia.

Follow it on a map. Every river is going somewhere, and somewhere along its banks, someone's life depends on it.

items.prompt: According to the text, what does the author mean when saying the Danube 'linked the peoples of ten nations'?

choices.text: It kept ten nations apart from each other
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because 'linked' means connected or joined together, which is the opposite of keeping people apart.

choices.text: It connected the people of ten countries
choices.feedback: B is correct because 'linked' means connected, and the passage tells us the Danube connected the people of ten nations (countries).

choices.text: It powered factories in ten countries
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage says the Rhine powered trade, not the Danube — and there is no mention of factories.

choices.text: It carried food to ten different nations
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because carrying food is what the passage says the Volga did for Russia, not the Danube.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: Based on the article, how is the Rhine River different from the Mississippi River?

choices.text: The Rhine flows mostly north, but the Mississippi flows mostly south.
choices.feedback: This is correct! The article says the Rhine 'flows mostly northward,' while the Mississippi flows south from Minnesota all the way to the Gulf of Mexico.

choices.text: The Rhine starts as a big river, but the Mississippi starts as a stream.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because the article says almost every river starts as a tiny stream, and that includes the Rhine — not just the Mississippi.

choices.text: The Rhine powers large cities, but the Mississippi powers large dams.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because it's the Volga River in Russia that has hydroelectric dams powering cities, not the Mississippi.

choices.text: The Rhine links many nations, but the Mississippi links many oceans.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because the Danube is the river that passes through many nations (ten countries!), and rivers flow into seas or oceans — they don't link oceans together.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: Which pair of headings helps you find out the reasons why rivers grow larger and flow in certain directions?

choices.text: "The Mississippi: America's Highway of Water" and "Europe's Rivers Flow in Surprising Directions"
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because 'The Mississippi: America's Highway of Water' is mostly about how people used the river for travel and trade, not about why rivers grow or flow in certain directions.

choices.text: "A Tiny Stream Becomes a Giant" and "Europe's Rivers Flow in Surprising Directions"
choices.feedback: This is correct because 'A Tiny Stream Becomes a Giant' explains how rivers grow larger, and 'Europe's Rivers Flow in Surprising Directions' explains that rivers flow downhill, which can be any direction.

choices.text: "A Tiny Stream Becomes a Giant" and "The Mississippi: America's Highway of Water"
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because 'The Mississippi: America's Highway of Water' talks about steamboats, barges, and cities — not about why rivers grow or flow in certain directions.

choices.text: "The Mississippi: America's Highway of Water" and "More Than Water"
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because both 'The Mississippi: America's Highway of Water' and 'More Than Water' are about how people used rivers, not about how rivers grow or which way they flow.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: Which sequence of details from the text best supports the main idea that rivers grow to become important to people?

choices.text: A tiny stream begins, other waters join to make it larger, and then people use it for trade.
choices.feedback: A is correct because it follows the right order from the passage — small stream, grows bigger, then people use it — which perfectly supports the idea that rivers grow to become important.

choices.text: Large cities are built, a tiny stream appears near the towns, and then boats travel on the water.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because cities don't appear before the river — the passage tells us cities like New Orleans grew *because* of the river, not the other way around.

choices.text: Rain falls into a basin, the water turns into electricity, and then it empties into the Black Sea.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because it mixes up details from different rivers (the Volga makes electricity, the Danube empties into the Black Sea), so it doesn't tell a connected story about how rivers grow and become important.

choices.text: A river leaves a lake, the water travels down to the sea, and then it flows up hills.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because rivers don't flow up hills — the passage clearly says rivers flow downhill, so 'flows up hills' is the opposite of what the text tells us.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: How are the Mississippi River and the Rhine River similar in the way people use them today?

choices.text: People build large dams on them to make electricity.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because the passage says hydroelectric dams are on the Volga River in Russia, not on the Mississippi or the Rhine.

choices.text: People use flat barges on them to carry goods.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage says barges carry goods on the Mississippi today, and barges loaded with goods also travel the Rhine every single day.

choices.text: People travel on their waters through ten different countries.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because the Danube is the river that flows through ten countries, not the Mississippi or the Rhine.

choices.text: People use their flowing waters to drink and farm.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because the passage connects drinking water and farming to the Volga River, not to the Mississippi or the Rhine.

units.title: The Human Body: The Muscular System

assessments.title: Your Amazing Muscles: Three Types That Keep You Moving

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Your 600 Muscles Are Working Right Now

Muscles You Cannot See

Bend your arm. Now straighten it. Two different muscles just took turns pulling on the same bone. Your body has more than 600 muscles, and they are busy right now — holding your head up, moving your eyes across this page, and pumping blood through your heart. Muscles are not just in your arms and legs. They wrap around your stomach, line your blood vessels, and even stretch across your face every time you smile.

All muscles work the same basic way. They contract, which means they get shorter and tighter, like a rubber band snapping back. When a muscle contracts, it pulls. When it relaxes, it gets longer and softer again. That pull-and-relax action is behind every movement in your body. There are three types of muscles, and each type has its own job.
stimulus.content_text:
Your 600 Muscles Are Working Right NowMuscles You Cannot See
Bend your arm. Now straighten it. Two different muscles just took turns pulling on the same bone. Your body has more than 600 muscles, and they are busy right now — holding your head up, moving your eyes across this page, and pumping blood through your heart. Muscles are not just in your arms and legs. They wrap around your stomach, line your blood vessels, and even stretch across your face every time you smile.

All muscles work the same basic way. They contract, which means they get shorter and tighter, like a rubber band snapping back. When a muscle contracts, it pulls. When it relaxes, it gets longer and softer again. That pull-and-relax action is behind every movement in your body. There are three types of muscles, and each type has its own job.

items.prompt: According to the text, what is different about a muscle when it pulls and when it rests?

choices.text: It gets tighter when it pulls and tighter when it rests.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage says a muscle gets softer when it relaxes, not tighter.

choices.text: It gets shorter when it pulls and longer when it rests.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage says a muscle gets shorter and tighter when it contracts (pulls) and longer and softer when it relaxes (rests).

choices.text: It gets longer when it pulls and shorter when it rests.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because it has it backwards — the passage says a muscle gets shorter when it pulls, not longer.

choices.text: It gets softer when it pulls and softer when it rests.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage says a muscle gets tighter when it pulls, not softer.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Skeletal Muscles: The Ones You Boss Around

Skeletal muscles attach to your bones. When you run, jump, wave, or kick a soccer ball, your brain sends a signal through your nerves — thin cables that carry messages the way a phone wire carries your voice. That signal tells the right muscle to contract, and the bone moves.

Here is the surprising part: muscles can only pull. They cannot push. So skeletal muscles work in pairs. Bend your arm, and a muscle on the front called the biceps contracts and pulls your forearm up. Now straighten your arm. A different muscle on the back called the triceps contracts and pulls it back down. Without a partner, neither muscle could finish the job.

Skeletal muscles are voluntary muscles. Voluntary means you choose to use them, the way you choose to pick up a pencil or raise your hand in class.
stimulus.content_text:
Skeletal Muscles: The Ones You Boss Around
Skeletal muscles attach to your bones. When you run, jump, wave, or kick a soccer ball, your brain sends a signal through your nerves — thin cables that carry messages the way a phone wire carries your voice. That signal tells the right muscle to contract, and the bone moves.

Here is the surprising part: muscles can only pull. They cannot push. So skeletal muscles work in pairs. Bend your arm, and a muscle on the front called the biceps contracts and pulls your forearm up. Now straighten your arm. A different muscle on the back called the triceps contracts and pulls it back down. Without a partner, neither muscle could finish the job.

Skeletal muscles are voluntary muscles. Voluntary means you choose to use them, the way you choose to pick up a pencil or raise your hand in class.

items.prompt: The main idea of this section is that skeletal muscles move your bones when you want them to. How does the detail about the brain sending a signal support this main idea?

choices.text: It explains what causes the muscles to move.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage shows that the brain's signal through the nerves is what causes the muscle to contract and move the bone — it explains the cause of the movement.

choices.text: It explains why muscles must work in pairs.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the brain signal detail is about starting movement, not about why muscles need a partner to work.

choices.text: It explains how muscles attach to the bones.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the passage describes nerves as message carriers, not as the way muscles attach to bones.

choices.text: It explains when muscles are able to push.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the passage clearly says muscles can only pull, never push, so no detail explains when muscles push.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Smooth Muscles: The Quiet Workers

Smooth muscles are found inside your organs — body parts like your stomach and intestines that each do a special job to keep you alive. After you eat lunch, smooth muscles squeeze and push food through your digestive system, the path food travels as your body breaks it down. You never have to think about this. Smooth muscles just keep working on their own.

Smooth muscles are involuntary. Involuntary means they work without any decision from you. You decide to chew your food. But once you swallow, smooth muscles take over.
stimulus.content_text:
Smooth Muscles: The Quiet Workers
Smooth muscles are found inside your organs — body parts like your stomach and intestines that each do a special job to keep you alive. After you eat lunch, smooth muscles squeeze and push food through your digestive system, the path food travels as your body breaks it down. You never have to think about this. Smooth muscles just keep working on their own.

Smooth muscles are involuntary. Involuntary means they work without any decision from you. You decide to chew your food. But once you swallow, smooth muscles take over.

items.prompt: According to the text, which list shows the correct order of what happens when you eat food?

choices.text: Smooth muscles squeeze the food, then you chew, then you swallow.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because smooth muscles don't squeeze food first — you have to chew and swallow before smooth muscles start working.

choices.text: You chew your food, then you swallow, then smooth muscles take over.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage says you chew first, then swallow, and then smooth muscles take over — that's the right order!

choices.text: You swallow your food, then you chew, then smooth muscles take over.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because you can't swallow your food before you chew it — chewing always comes before swallowing.

choices.text: Smooth muscles push the food, then you swallow, then you chew.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because smooth muscles don't push food first — they only take over after you have already chewed and swallowed.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Cardiac Muscle: The One That Never Quits

Cardiac muscle is found in only one place — your heart. The word cardiac means "having to do with the heart." This muscle contracts and relaxes about 100,000 times every single day, pushing blood to every corner of your body. It started beating before you were born. It has not stopped once.

You might think you can control your heart because you feel it speed up when you run. But your heart speeds up on its own. Cardiac muscle is involuntary, just like smooth muscle.
stimulus.content_text:
Cardiac Muscle: The One That Never Quits
Cardiac muscle is found in only one place — your heart. The word cardiac means "having to do with the heart." This muscle contracts and relaxes about 100,000 times every single day, pushing blood to every corner of your body. It started beating before you were born. It has not stopped once.

You might think you can control your heart because you feel it speed up when you run. But your heart speeds up on its own. Cardiac muscle is involuntary, just like smooth muscle.

items.prompt: Based on the meaning of the word "involuntary" in the text, how is cardiac muscle similar to smooth muscle?

choices.text: Both muscles work without you trying to control them.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage says cardiac muscle is 'involuntary, just like smooth muscle,' meaning both muscles work on their own without you controlling them.

choices.text: Both muscles push blood to every corner of your body.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the passage only says cardiac muscle pushes blood through your body — it never says smooth muscle does that too.

choices.text: Both muscles speed up only when you decide to run fast.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because 'involuntary' actually means these muscles work without you deciding anything, so you never control when they speed up.

choices.text: Both muscles stay in exactly one place inside your body.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the passage says cardiac muscle stays in one place (your heart), but it never says smooth muscle is stuck in just one place.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
A Team That Never Rests

Think about what happened in the last five minutes. Skeletal muscles moved your eyes and held this page. Smooth muscles quietly pushed food or liquid through your body. Cardiac muscle pumped blood to your brain so you could understand these words.

Tonight, when you fall asleep and your skeletal muscles finally rest, your smooth muscles and cardiac muscle will keep going. They do not sleep. They do not take breaks. You are never truly still — because inside you, muscles are always pulling, squeezing, and beating, keeping you alive without ever being asked.
stimulus.content_text:
A Team That Never Rests
Think about what happened in the last five minutes. Skeletal muscles moved your eyes and held this page. Smooth muscles quietly pushed food or liquid through your body. Cardiac muscle pumped blood to your brain so you could understand these words.

Tonight, when you fall asleep and your skeletal muscles finally rest, your smooth muscles and cardiac muscle will keep going. They do not sleep. They do not take breaks. You are never truly still — because inside you, muscles are always pulling, squeezing, and beating, keeping you alive without ever being asked.

items.prompt: According to the text, what does the author mean when she says 'your smooth muscles and cardiac muscle will keep going'?

choices.text: They will move your arms and legs while you dream.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because moving your arms and legs is done by skeletal muscles, not smooth or cardiac muscles.

choices.text: They will continue working even while you sleep.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage says smooth muscles and cardiac muscle 'do not sleep' and 'do not take breaks,' meaning they keep working while you sleep.

choices.text: They will rest for short breaks during the night.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage directly says these muscles do NOT take breaks.

choices.text: They will stop and start again each morning.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage says these muscles never stop — they work continuously, not in a stop-and-start pattern.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: Which statement best shows a contrast between skeletal muscles and the other two types of muscles?

choices.text: Skeletal muscles work only in your heart, but the other two work in your arms and legs.
choices.feedback: A is wrong because skeletal muscles work in your arms, legs, and body — not your heart; cardiac muscle is the one found only in the heart.

choices.text: Skeletal muscles move when you choose, but the other two keep working on their own.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage explains that skeletal muscles are voluntary (you control them), while smooth and cardiac muscles are involuntary (they work on their own without you deciding).

choices.text: Skeletal muscles push bones apart, but the other two pull bones back together.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the passage clearly states that muscles can only pull, never push — that goes for all three types of muscles.

choices.text: Skeletal muscles rest at night, but the other two stop only when you are awake.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because it has it backwards — the passage says smooth muscles and cardiac muscle keep working even while you sleep, not just when you're awake.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: According to the article, why do skeletal muscles need to work in pairs to move a bone?

choices.text: because each muscle can only pull in one direction
choices.feedback: This is correct because the article clearly says 'muscles can only pull' in one direction, so a second muscle is needed to pull the bone back the other way.

choices.text: because the brain can only send one signal at a time
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the article never says the brain can only send one signal at a time — that idea isn't mentioned anywhere in the passage.

choices.text: because muscles get tired and need a partner to rest
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the article never says muscles work in pairs so one can rest while the other works — that's not the reason given.

choices.text: because bones are too heavy for one muscle to move
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the article never says bones are too heavy for one muscle — the real reason is about pulling direction, not weight.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: Based on the article, which two headings show the muscles that rest first at night, followed by a type of muscle that keeps working after you fall asleep?

choices.text: "Skeletal Muscles: The Ones You Boss Around" then "Smooth Muscles: The Quiet Workers"
choices.feedback: A is correct because the article tells us skeletal muscles rest when you sleep, and smooth muscles keep working after that — exactly the order shown here.

choices.text: "Smooth Muscles: The Quiet Workers" then "Skeletal Muscles: The Ones You Boss Around"
choices.feedback: B is wrong because it puts smooth muscles first and skeletal muscles second, but the article says skeletal muscles rest first, not smooth muscles.

choices.text: "Cardiac Muscle: The One That Never Quits" then "Skeletal Muscles: The Ones You Boss Around"
choices.feedback: C is wrong because cardiac muscle never rests — the article says it beats about 100,000 times every day and has never stopped once.

choices.text: "Smooth Muscles: The Quiet Workers" then "Cardiac Muscle: The One That Never Quits"
choices.feedback: D is wrong because it leaves out skeletal muscles, which are the muscles that actually rest first when you fall asleep.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: The article describes three types of muscles that are different from each other. What main idea do all three types of muscles share that the author wants you to understand?

choices.text: All three types of muscles are controlled by your brain sending signals through nerves.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because only skeletal muscles use brain signals through nerves — smooth and cardiac muscles work on their own without nerve signals telling them what to do.

choices.text: All three types of muscles work by contracting and pulling to do their jobs.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the article clearly states that all muscles work the same basic way by contracting (getting shorter and pulling), and this is true for all three types — skeletal, smooth, and cardiac.

choices.text: All three types of muscles are found inside your organs to keep you alive.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because smooth muscles are found inside organs, but skeletal muscles attach to bones and cardiac muscle is only in your heart, so this doesn't describe all three types.

choices.text: All three types of muscles work in pairs so they can push and pull bones.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because working in pairs to push and pull bones is only how skeletal muscles work — smooth and cardiac muscles don't work in pairs or move bones.

syllabus_metadata.title: Your Amazing Muscles: Three Types That Keep You Moving

assessments.title: Push and Pull: How Muscles Work in Pairs

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Muscles That Move You

A Secret Inside Your Arm

Flex your arm right now. Bend it at the elbow like you are showing off your strength. Feel that bump on the front of your upper arm? Something just got shorter and tighter under your skin. Now straighten your arm out. The bump disappeared. A different part of your arm did the work this time. Two muscles just traded jobs — and they do it every single time you move.
stimulus.content_text:
The Muscles That Move YouA Secret Inside Your Arm
Flex your arm right now. Bend it at the elbow like you are showing off your strength. Feel that bump on the front of your upper arm? Something just got shorter and tighter under your skin. Now straighten your arm out. The bump disappeared. A different part of your arm did the work this time. Two muscles just traded jobs — and they do it every single time you move.

items.prompt: According to the text, how is straightening your arm different from bending your arm at the elbow?

choices.text: The bump gets bigger when you straighten your arm
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage says the bump *disappeared* when you straighten your arm — it does not get bigger.

choices.text: The bump disappears when you straighten your arm
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly states 'The bump disappeared' when you straighten your arm out.

choices.text: The same muscle does the work both times
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage says 'a different part of your arm did the work this time,' meaning two different muscles take turns.

choices.text: Both movements make your arm feel tighter
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because only bending your arm causes something to get shorter and tighter — the passage does not say straightening your arm feels tight too.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Muscles Can Only Pull

Here is the surprising part: muscles cannot push. They can only pull. You might think a muscle does both, the way your hand can push a door shut and pull it open. But a single muscle only pulls in one direction. So how does your arm bend and straighten? The answer is partners.

Muscles work in pairs. When one muscle pulls, the other relaxes. Then they switch. This back-and-forth system lets your body move in many directions, even though each muscle can only tug one way. Think of two friends on opposite sides of a heavy door. One pulls it open. The other pulls it closed. Neither one pushes. They just take turns pulling.
stimulus.content_text:
Muscles Can Only Pull
Here is the surprising part: muscles cannot push. They can only pull. You might think a muscle does both, the way your hand can push a door shut and pull it open. But a single muscle only pulls in one direction. So how does your arm bend and straighten? The answer is partners.

Muscles work in pairs. When one muscle pulls, the other relaxes. Then they switch. This back-and-forth system lets your body move in many directions, even though each muscle can only tug one way. Think of two friends on opposite sides of a heavy door. One pulls it open. The other pulls it closed. Neither one pushes. They just take turns pulling.

items.prompt: The main idea of the section is that muscles work in pairs. How does the detail that a muscle can only pull support this main idea?

choices.text: It shows why muscles must take turns.
choices.feedback: A is correct because since a muscle can only pull one way, it needs a partner muscle to pull the other way, so they must take turns to move your body in both directions.

choices.text: It shows why hands can push on doors.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the passage mentions hands and doors only as an example to explain muscles — it's not trying to explain how hands push.

choices.text: It shows why friends pull heavy doors.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the friends pulling the door is just a helpful comparison to explain how muscle pairs work, not a detail that supports the main idea about muscles.

choices.text: It shows why bodies move just one way.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the passage says the back-and-forth pulling system lets your body move in MANY directions, which is the opposite of just one way.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Meet the Biceps and Triceps

The most famous muscle pair lives in your upper arm. Your biceps sits on the front. Your triceps sits on the back. When you bend your arm, your biceps contracts — it gets shorter and tighter, pulling your forearm up toward your shoulder. You might think the muscle puffs up like a balloon when you flex. Actually, it shortens. That shortening is what makes the bump look bigger.

While your biceps pulls, your triceps relaxes and stretches to allow the movement. Now straighten your arm. The roles flip. Your triceps contracts and pulls your forearm back down. Your biceps relaxes and stretches out. One pulls. The other rests. Then they trade places.

But how does a muscle pull on a bone? Tough, rope-like cords called tendons connect each muscle to the bones around it — like a leash connecting a dog to your hand. When a muscle contracts, it tugs on the tendon, and the tendon tugs on the bone. That is what makes the bone move.

When you bend your arm, your biceps contracts — it gets shorter and tighter, pulling your forearm up toward your shoulder. You might think the muscle puffs up like a balloon when you flex. Actually, it shortens. That shortening is what makes the bump look bigger.

While your biceps pulls, your triceps relaxes and stretches to allow the movement. Now straighten your arm. The roles flip. Your triceps contracts and pulls your forearm back down. Your biceps relaxes and stretches out. One pulls. The other rests. Then they trade places.

But how does a muscle pull on a bone? Tough, rope-like cords called tendons connect each muscle to the bones around it — like a leash connecting a dog to your hand. When a muscle contracts, it tugs on the tendon, and the tendon tugs on the bone. That is what makes the bone move.

items.prompt: According to the text, what happens to the triceps right after the biceps contracts and pulls the forearm up?

choices.text: The triceps contracts and pulls the forearm down.
choices.feedback: This is actually what the triceps does later, when you straighten your arm — not right after the biceps pulls the forearm up.

choices.text: The triceps relaxes and stretches to allow movement.
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says 'your triceps relaxes and stretches to allow the movement' while the biceps is pulling.

choices.text: The triceps tugs on a tendon to move the bone.
choices.feedback: Tugging on a tendon is how muscles move bones in general, but the passage does not say this is what the triceps does right after the biceps contracts.

choices.text: The triceps gets shorter and makes a bigger bump.
choices.feedback: Getting shorter and making a bigger bump describes the biceps contracting, not what the triceps does at that moment.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Pairs Everywhere

These opposing pairs — muscles that work against each other, like two teams in a tug-of-war — are found all over your body. The front and back of your thigh have a pair that bends and straightens your knee. Muscles on either side of your stomach help you twist left and right. Even your forearm holds muscles that pull on long tendons running into your fingers, helping you grip a pencil or pick up a coin.

Without pairs, you could bend your arm but never straighten it. You could lift your leg but never set it back down.

items.prompt: The author compares muscles to "two teams in a tug-of-war." Based on this comparison, what does the word opposing mean?

choices.text: Pulling on a long rope
choices.feedback: Pulling on a rope is part of tug-of-war, but it doesn't explain what the word 'opposing' means.

choices.text: Working against each other
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says opposing muscles 'work against each other, like two teams in a tug-of-war,' so opposing means working against each other.

choices.text: Bending and straightening legs
choices.feedback: Bending and straightening legs is something muscles do, but it is not the meaning of the word 'opposing.'

choices.text: Moving around the human body
choices.feedback: Muscles do move around the body, but that has nothing to do with what the word 'opposing' means.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
More Than One Kind of Muscle

The muscles you flex and control on purpose are called skeletal muscles because they attach to your skeleton. But your body also has cardiac muscle in your heart and smooth muscle in your stomach and intestines. These muscles work without you telling them to — even while you sleep.
stimulus.content_text:
More Than One Kind of Muscle
The muscles you flex and control on purpose are called skeletal muscles because they attach to your skeleton. But your body also has cardiac muscle in your heart and smooth muscle in your stomach and intestines. These muscles work without you telling them to — even while you sleep.

items.prompt: According to the text, what are skeletal muscles?

choices.text: muscles in your heart
choices.feedback: Muscles in your heart are called cardiac muscles, not skeletal muscles — the passage tells us these are two different types.

choices.text: muscles attached to your skeleton
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says skeletal muscles 'attach to your skeleton,' which is exactly why they have that name.

choices.text: muscles in your stomach
choices.feedback: Muscles in your stomach are called smooth muscles, according to the passage — not skeletal muscles.

choices.text: muscles you use while sleeping
choices.feedback: The passage says skeletal muscles are ones you control on purpose, but muscles that work while you sleep are the ones you do NOT control.

items.title: Section 6 - Guiding Question 5

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
Your Body's Hidden Teamwork

Right now, hundreds of muscle pairs are firing and resting, pulling and releasing. Tiny ones hold your eyes steady on these words. Larger ones keep your back upright. Your heart muscle has not taken a single break since before you were born. Muscles are not just the parts you flex to look strong. They are the reason you can blink, breathe, run, and even smile at a friend across the room.

They are the reason you can blink, breathe, run, and even smile at a friend across the room.

choices.text: Large muscles support your back.
choices.feedback: Back muscles help you stay upright, but the passage does not say they help you read.

choices.text: Flexed muscles show your strength.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: According to the article, how is the job of a single muscle different from the job of a muscle pair?

choices.text: A single muscle pulls a bone one way, but a pair moves a bone back and forth.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the article says a single muscle can only pull one way, but a muscle pair takes turns pulling so the bone can move in both directions.

choices.text: A single muscle pushes a bone away, but a pair pulls a bone close to the body.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the article clearly states that muscles cannot push at all — neither a single muscle nor a pair ever pushes.

choices.text: A single muscle works without thinking, but a pair needs you to control it.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the article says skeletal muscles are the ones you control on purpose, and they work in pairs just like the biceps and triceps.

choices.text: A single muscle stays the same length, but a pair gets shorter and tighter.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the article explains that muscles do get shorter and tighter when they contract — staying the same length is the opposite of what happens.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: Which two headings help you find out why a single muscle cannot bend and straighten your arm?

choices.text: 'A Secret Inside Your Arm' and 'Pairs Everywhere'
choices.feedback: Option A is wrong because 'A Secret Inside Your Arm' only introduces the feeling of muscles moving — it doesn't explain why a single muscle can't do both jobs.

choices.text: 'Muscles Can Only Pull' and 'Meet the Biceps and Triceps'
choices.feedback: Option B is correct because 'Muscles Can Only Pull' explains that one muscle can only pull one way, and 'Meet the Biceps and Triceps' shows how two muscles must take turns to bend and straighten your arm.

choices.text: 'Meet the Biceps and Triceps' and 'More Than One Kind of Muscle'
choices.feedback: Option C is wrong because 'More Than One Kind of Muscle' is about heart and stomach muscles, not about why your arm needs two muscles to move.

choices.text: 'Pairs Everywhere' and 'Your Body's Hidden Teamwork'
choices.feedback: Option D is wrong because both 'Pairs Everywhere' and 'Your Body's Hidden Teamwork' talk about muscles in general around your body, not specifically about why one muscle can't bend and straighten your arm.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: Which choice shows the order of events when you bend your arm?

choices.text: The tendon pulls first, then the biceps contracts, and last the forearm moves up.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the tendon doesn't pull first — the biceps has to contract first, and only then does it tug on the tendon.

choices.text: The biceps contracts first, then the tendon tugs, and last the forearm moves up.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage says the biceps contracts first, which tugs the tendon, and that tendon pull is what moves the forearm up last.

choices.text: The triceps contracts first, then the biceps relaxes, and last the forearm moves up.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because when you bend your arm, the triceps relaxes — it's the biceps that contracts to pull your forearm up.

choices.text: The forearm moves first, then the tendon tugs, and last the biceps contracts.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the forearm moving is the last thing that happens, not the first — the muscle and tendon have to do their work before the bone moves.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: Based on the article, how are the author's examples of "friends at a door" and "teams in a tug-of-war" similar?

choices.text: They both explain how muscles connect to your bones.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because neither the door friends nor the tug-of-war teams have anything to do with tendons or muscles connecting to bones.

choices.text: They both show that muscles can push and pull things.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the article clearly states muscles can only pull, never push, and both examples only show pulling.

choices.text: They both explain how muscles pull in opposite ways.
choices.feedback: C is correct because both examples show two sides pulling in opposite directions, just like muscle pairs pull against each other to move your body.

choices.text: They both show that muscles work without any resting.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because both examples show the two sides taking turns, which means one always rests while the other is pulling.

syllabus_metadata.title: Push and Pull: How Muscles Work in Pairs

assessments.title: Connected by Tendons: How Muscles Attach to Bones

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Tendons: The Tough Ropes Inside You

What Tendons Are and How They Work With Muscles

Spread your fingers wide. Now wiggle them. See those thin lines sliding under the skin on the back of your hand? Those are tendons — strong, flexible bands of tissue (the material your body is built from, like fabric is the material a shirt is built from). Tendons connect your muscles to your bones. Without them, your muscles would squeeze and shorten but have nothing to pull on. Your body would not move at all.

When a muscle works, it gets shorter and tighter. That shortening tugs on the tendon, and the tendon yanks the bone. Think of it like a rope tied between a horse and a wagon. The horse pulls the rope. The rope pulls the wagon. The tendon is that rope.

But if muscles only pull, how does your arm bend and straighten?
stimulus.content_text:
Tendons: The Tough Ropes Inside YouWhat Tendons Are and How They Work With Muscles
Spread your fingers wide. Now wiggle them. See those thin lines sliding under the skin on the back of your hand? Those are tendons — strong, flexible bands of tissue (the material your body is built from, like fabric is the material a shirt is built from). Tendons connect your muscles to your bones. Without them, your muscles would squeeze and shorten but have nothing to pull on. Your body would not move at all.
When a muscle works, it gets shorter and tighter. That shortening tugs on the tendon, and the tendon yanks the bone. Think of it like a rope tied between a horse and a wagon. The horse pulls the rope. The rope pulls the wagon. The tendon is that rope.
But if muscles only pull, how does your arm bend and straighten?

items.prompt: According to the text, which choice shows the difference between what muscles do and what tendons do?

choices.text: Muscles slide under the skin, while tendons spread the fingers wide.
choices.feedback: A is wrong because the passage says the thin lines sliding under the skin are tendons, not muscles.

choices.text: Muscles get shorter and tighter, while tendons pull on the bone.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage clearly states muscles get shorter and tighter, while tendons yank the bone.

choices.text: Muscles connect to bones, while tendons squeeze and shorten.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because it has the jobs switched — tendons connect to bones, and muscles are the ones that squeeze and shorten.

choices.text: Muscles yank the wagon, while tendons pull the horse.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the horse-and-wagon story is a comparison to help explain how a tendon works, not a description of what muscles and tendons each do.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
What Tendons Are Made Of and Why They Are So Strong

Tendons are made of a tough material called collagen fibers — tiny threads of protein, the same stuff that helps make your skin strong. These fibers are twisted together the way strands of a rope are twisted together. That design makes tendons extremely strong. Some tendons can handle the force of hundreds of pounds without tearing.

They are not like rubber bands that bounce back. That stiffness is what makes them so good at their job. Every bit of pulling power from your muscle goes right to the bone, with almost nothing wasted.
stimulus.content_text:
What Tendons Are Made Of and Why They Are So Strong
Tendons are made of a tough material called collagen fibers — tiny threads of protein, the same stuff that helps make your skin strong. These fibers are twisted together the way strands of a rope are twisted together. That design makes tendons extremely strong. Some tendons can handle the force of hundreds of pounds without tearing.
They are not like rubber bands that bounce back. That stiffness is what makes them so good at their job. Every bit of pulling power from your muscle goes right to the bone, with almost nothing wasted.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, why does twisting collagen fibers together help tendons do their job well?

choices.text: It makes tendons soft, so they can stretch like rubber bands.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage says tendons are stiff and NOT like rubber bands, so they do not stretch.

choices.text: It makes tendons strong, so muscle pulling power reaches the bone.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage explains that twisting fibers together makes tendons very strong, which lets all the muscle's pulling power reach the bone.

choices.text: It makes tendons light, so bones do not have to work hard.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage never mentions tendons being light or says anything about bones not having to work hard.

choices.text: It makes tendons bouncy, so muscles can rest between pulls.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage describes tendons as stiff, not bouncy, and never mentions muscles resting between pulls.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
The Biggest Tendon and How Tendons Differ From Ligaments

Reach down and touch the back of your ankle, just above your heel. That thick cord you feel is the Achilles tendon, the largest tendon in your body. It connects your calf muscle to your heel bone. You use it every time you walk, run, jump, or stand on your tiptoes.

It is named after Achilles, a hero from ancient Greek stories. According to legend, Achilles was strong everywhere except the back of his heel. That one weak spot gave this mighty tendon its name.

Your body has another type of connector called a ligament. It is easy to mix them up. Here is the difference: tendons connect muscles to bones, but ligaments connect bones to other bones. They have different jobs. Tendons help you move. Ligaments hold your joints together so your bones do not slide apart.

Tendons themselves come in different shapes and sizes depending on where they are in your body. The ones in your fingers are long and thin, perfect for gripping a pencil or tying your shoes. The ones in your knees are thicker and help you kick, squat, and climb stairs.

It is named after Achilles, a hero from ancient Greek stories. According to legend, Achilles was strong everywhere except the back of his heel. That one weak spot gave this mighty tendon its name.
Your body has another type of connector called a ligament. It is easy to mix them up. Here is the difference: tendons connect muscles to bones, but ligaments connect bones to other bones. They have different jobs. Tendons help you move. Ligaments hold your joints together so your bones do not slide apart.
Tendons themselves come in different shapes and sizes depending on where they are in your body. The ones in your fingers are long and thin, perfect for gripping a pencil or tying your shoes. The ones in your knees are thicker and help you kick, squat, and climb stairs.

items.prompt: According to the text, what does the author tell you to do before explaining what the Achilles tendon is?

choices.text: learn about a hero from ancient Greek stories
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the story about the Greek hero Achilles comes after the author has already explained what the Achilles tendon is.

choices.text: reach down and touch the back of your ankle
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage begins with 'Reach down and touch the back of your ankle,' which happens right before the author explains what the Achilles tendon is.

choices.text: kick a ball and then climb up the stairs
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because kicking a ball is never mentioned in the passage, and climbing stairs is brought up much later when talking about knee tendons.

choices.text: think about how ligaments hold joints together
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because ligaments holding joints together is explained near the end of the passage, not before the Achilles tendon is introduced.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
How to Keep Your Tendons Healthy and Why They Matter

Tendons are tough, but they can still get hurt. A tendon injury is called a strain — a small tear that happens when you move too fast or repeat the same motion too many times without rest. Athletes sometimes strain their tendons during sports.

The good news is that a few simple habits can help protect your tendons. When you stretch before exercise, blood flows to your tendons and muscles. Warm tendons handle force better than cold ones. Resting when your body feels sore helps, too.

A tendon pulls. A bone moves. It happens in less than a blink. Tendons never get the fame that muscles and bones do, but every single movement you make depends on these quiet, rope-strong connectors doing their job perfectly, right now, inside you.

A tendon pulls. A bone moves. It happens in less than a blink. Tendons never get the fame that muscles and bones do, but every single movement you make depends on these quiet, rope-strong connectors doing their job perfectly, right now, inside you.

items.prompt: When the author calls tendons 'rope-strong connectors,' how does this comparison help the reader understand what the word 'tendons' means?

choices.text: It shows that tendons are made from actual rope material
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the author is using rope as a comparison to describe how strong tendons are, not to say tendons are actually made of rope.

choices.text: It shows that tendons are tough and hold things together
choices.feedback: B is correct because 'rope-strong' tells us tendons are tough, and 'connectors' tells us they hold things together, which matches what the passage says tendons do.

choices.text: It shows that tendons are famous like muscles and bones
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage actually says tendons do NOT get fame like muscles and bones do.

choices.text: It shows that tendons are easy to tear and break apart
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because calling something 'rope-strong' means it is hard to break, not easy to tear apart.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: Based on the article, what is the difference between a rubber band and a tendon?

choices.text: A rubber band is made of collagen, while a tendon is made of skin.
choices.feedback: A is wrong because the article says tendons are made of collagen fibers, and rubber bands are not made of collagen or skin.

choices.text: A rubber band stretches and bounces, while a tendon stays stiff to pull.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the article says tendons are NOT like rubber bands that bounce back, and their stiffness helps them send all the pulling power straight to the bone.

choices.text: A rubber band holds joints together, while a tendon connects bones to bones.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because it describes what ligaments do — ligaments connect bones to bones, not rubber bands.

choices.text: A rubber band gets shorter and tighter, while a tendon grows longer and thicker.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because muscles are what get shorter and tighter, and tendons do not grow longer and thicker when you move.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: Why can a strained Achilles tendon make it hard for a person to run or jump?

choices.text: Because the tendon connects bones to bones and cannot hold the ankle joint together
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because connecting bones to bones is the job of a ligament, not a tendon like the Achilles.

choices.text: Because the tendon connects the calf muscle to the heel bone and helps pull the foot
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage says the Achilles tendon connects the calf muscle to the heel bone and is used every time you run or jump, so a strain would make those movements hurt or stop working right.

choices.text: Because the tendon is the biggest tendon in the body and needs more blood than others
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage never says the Achilles tendon needs more blood than other tendons just because it is the biggest.

choices.text: Because the tendon is named after a hero and is weak at the back of the heel
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the story about the hero Achilles only explains where the tendon got its name, not why a strain makes it hard to move.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: The article uses section headings to organize information in a certain order. Which choice best describes the sequence the author follows from the first section to the last section?

choices.text: what tendons look like, how tendons get injured, how tendons compare to ligaments
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the article doesn't have a section focused only on what tendons look like, and the order of topics doesn't match the section headings.

choices.text: how tendons work, what makes them strong, how to keep them healthy
choices.feedback: B is correct because the section headings follow this exact order: how tendons work, what makes them strong (collagen fibers), and how to keep them healthy.

choices.text: how bones move, how muscles get stronger, how ligaments hold joints together
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the article is about tendons, not about how bones move on their own or how muscles get stronger.

choices.text: how to stretch before exercise, what collagen fibers are, what the Achilles tendon does
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because stretching, collagen fibers, and the Achilles tendon are small details found inside the sections, not the main topic of each section in order.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: The main idea of the article is that tendons are strong parts of your body that help you move. Which comparison from the text best supports this main idea?

choices.text: Tendons are like rubber bands because they easily stretch and bounce back.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because the article says tendons are NOT like rubber bands — they are stiff, not stretchy and bouncy.

choices.text: Tendons are like strong ropes because they pull bones without wasting power.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the article compares tendons to ropes and says they send all the muscle's pulling power straight to the bone without wasting any, showing they are strong and help you move.

choices.text: Tendons are like ligaments because they hold your bone joints safely together.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because tendons and ligaments have different jobs — tendons help you move, while ligaments hold bones together at joints.

choices.text: Tendons are like human skin because they are made of the exact same material.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because the article only says tendons and skin both contain collagen protein, not that they are made of the exact same material.
