assessments.title: Voluntary and Involuntary: Muscles You Control and Muscles That Work on Their Own

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Muscular System: Muscles You Boss Around and Muscles That Boss You

Muscles That Follow Orders

Bend your arm right now. Feel that tightness on top, near your shoulder? That is a muscle pulling on a bone. Your bones cannot move by themselves. Muscles are attached to them, and muscles do all the pulling.

Voluntary muscles are muscles you control on purpose. "Voluntary" means you choose to use them. When you decide to pick up a pencil, your brain sends a signal down to your hand. The muscles in your fingers tighten and grip. When you smile at a friend, your brain tells tiny muscles in your face to move.

Here is something surprising about how your arm works. Muscles can only pull. They cannot push. So they work in pairs. When you bend your arm, the muscle on top pulls tight. The muscle on the bottom relaxes and stretches out. When you straighten your arm, they switch jobs.

The muscles in your arms, legs, hands, feet, and face are all voluntary. They are also called skeletal muscles because they attach to your skeleton — your bones. When a rubber band shortens, it pulls two sticks closer together. That is exactly how skeletal muscles move your body.
stimulus.content_text:
The Muscular System: Muscles You Boss Around and Muscles That Boss YouMuscles That Follow Orders
Bend your arm right now. Feel that tightness on top, near your shoulder? That is a muscle pulling on a bone. Your bones cannot move by themselves. Muscles are attached to them, and muscles do all the pulling.

Voluntary muscles are muscles you control on purpose. "Voluntary" means you choose to use them. When you decide to pick up a pencil, your brain sends a signal down to your hand. The muscles in your fingers tighten and grip. When you smile at a friend, your brain tells tiny muscles in your face to move.

Here is something surprising about how your arm works. Muscles can only pull. They cannot push. So they work in pairs. When you bend your arm, the muscle on top pulls tight. The muscle on the bottom relaxes and stretches out. When you straighten your arm, they switch jobs.

The muscles in your arms, legs, hands, feet, and face are all voluntary. They are also called skeletal muscles because they attach to your skeleton — your bones. When a rubber band shortens, it pulls two sticks closer together. That is exactly how skeletal muscles move your body.

items.prompt: According to the text, how do the two muscles in your arm work differently from each other when you bend your arm?

choices.text: One muscle pushes while the other pulls
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage clearly states that muscles can only pull — they are never able to push.

choices.text: One muscle pulls tight while the other relaxes
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage says when you bend your arm, the top muscle pulls tight while the bottom muscle relaxes and stretches out.

choices.text: Both muscles pull tight at the same time
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage explains the two muscles take turns, not that they both tighten at the same time.

choices.text: Both muscles relax and stretch together
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because both muscles do not relax together — one always pulls tight while the other one relaxes.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Muscles That Work Without You

Now try to stop your stomach from digesting your lunch. Some muscles ignore your brain's commands completely.

Involuntary muscles work automatically, without any thought from you. The smooth muscles inside your stomach and intestines squeeze food along like toothpaste through a tube. They are called "smooth" because under a microscope, they look flat and even — no stripes or ridges. Smooth muscles also wrap around your blood vessels, the tiny tubes that carry blood through your body. These muscles tighten or relax to make blood vessels narrower or wider, which helps control how blood flows to different parts of your body. Your heart is the muscle that actually pumps blood through those vessels.
stimulus.content_text:
Muscles That Work Without You
Now try to stop your stomach from digesting your lunch. Some muscles ignore your brain's commands completely.

Involuntary muscles work automatically, without any thought from you. The smooth muscles inside your stomach and intestines squeeze food along like toothpaste through a tube. They are called "smooth" because under a microscope, they look flat and even — no stripes or ridges. Smooth muscles also wrap around your blood vessels, the tiny tubes that carry blood through your body. These muscles tighten or relax to make blood vessels narrower or wider, which helps control how blood flows to different parts of your body. Your heart is the muscle that actually pumps blood through those vessels.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, why do smooth muscles around blood vessels tighten or relax?

choices.text: to digest food in the stomach
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because digesting food is done by smooth muscles inside the stomach and intestines, not the ones wrapped around blood vessels.

choices.text: to control how blood flows
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly states that these muscles tighten or relax to make blood vessels narrower or wider, which controls how blood flows through the body.

choices.text: to make blood vessels smooth
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because 'smooth' describes how the muscles look under a microscope — flat and even — not something the muscles do to blood vessels.

choices.text: to pump blood through tubes
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage clearly says the heart is the muscle that pumps blood, not the smooth muscles around blood vessels.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
The Muscle That Never Rests

Press two fingers against the side of your neck. Feel that steady thump? That is your pulse — the beat of blood being pushed through your body by your heart. Your heart is made of a special muscle called cardiac muscle, found only in your heart. "Cardiac" means "having to do with the heart." Your cardiac muscle pumps blood about 100,000 times every single day. That is roughly 70 beats per minute, a little faster than a clock ticking off seconds. It beats while you run, while you sleep, and while you read this sentence.
stimulus.content_text:
The Muscle That Never Rests
Press two fingers against the side of your neck. Feel that steady thump? That is your pulse — the beat of blood being pushed through your body by your heart. Your heart is made of a special muscle called cardiac muscle, found only in your heart. "Cardiac" means "having to do with the heart." Your cardiac muscle pumps blood about 100,000 times every single day. That is roughly 70 beats per minute, a little faster than a clock ticking off seconds. It beats while you run, while you sleep, and while you read this sentence.

items.prompt: According to the text, what is the first step to feel your pulse?

choices.text: Press two fingers on your neck.
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage starts by telling you to press two fingers against your neck as the first step to feel your pulse.

choices.text: Count seventy beats every minute.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because counting 70 beats per minute is a fact the passage shares about your heart rate, not a step for feeling your pulse.

choices.text: Push blood all through your body.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because pushing blood through your body is what your heart does automatically — it is not something you do to feel your pulse.

choices.text: Listen to a clock ticking seconds.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the clock is only used in the passage to help you picture how fast your heart beats, not as a step to find your pulse.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
One Muscle That Works Both Ways

Your diaphragm breaks the rules. This large, dome-shaped muscle sits right below your lungs, like an upside-down bowl. Most of the time, it works involuntarily. It pulls air into your lungs and pushes it back out while you sleep, read, or play — and you never notice.

But you can also take charge of your diaphragm on purpose. Hold your breath. Blow out birthday candles. Take a giant gulp of air before jumping underwater. Each time, you are choosing to control this muscle voluntarily. The diaphragm lives in both worlds.
stimulus.content_text:
One Muscle That Works Both Ways
Your diaphragm breaks the rules. This large, dome-shaped muscle sits right below your lungs, like an upside-down bowl. Most of the time, it works involuntarily. It pulls air into your lungs and pushes it back out while you sleep, read, or play — and you never notice.

But you can also take charge of your diaphragm on purpose. Hold your breath. Blow out birthday candles. Take a giant gulp of air before jumping underwater. Each time, you are choosing to control this muscle voluntarily. The diaphragm lives in both worlds.

items.prompt: According to the text, how are holding your breath and blowing out birthday candles similar ways of using your diaphragm?

choices.text: Both are actions that happen while you are asleep.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage says sleeping is when your diaphragm works involuntarily — without you choosing it.

choices.text: Both are actions that you choose to do on purpose.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage says holding your breath and blowing out candles are both times when 'you are choosing to control this muscle voluntarily,' meaning on purpose.

choices.text: Both are actions that pull air into your own lungs.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because blowing out birthday candles pushes air out of your lungs, not in.

choices.text: Both are actions that work without you noticing it.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because actions you don't notice are involuntary, but both holding your breath and blowing out candles are things you choose to do on purpose.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
A Body That Thinks for Itself

Right now, your voluntary muscles are waiting for your next command. Meanwhile, your involuntary muscles are already at work, digesting food, pumping blood, and pulling air into your lungs. Your body handles hundreds of jobs at once so your brain can focus on the things that matter to you — like recess, or what to have for dinner.
stimulus.content_text:
A Body That Thinks for Itself
Right now, your voluntary muscles are waiting for your next command. Meanwhile, your involuntary muscles are already at work, digesting food, pumping blood, and pulling air into your lungs. Your body handles hundreds of jobs at once so your brain can focus on the things that matter to you — like recess, or what to have for dinner.

items.prompt: According to the text, what are involuntary muscles?

choices.text: muscles that you choose to move yourself
choices.feedback: This is actually the definition of voluntary muscles — the ones you control yourself — not involuntary muscles.

choices.text: muscles that work on their own
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage shows involuntary muscles work on their own by digesting food and pumping blood without you having to think about it.

choices.text: muscles that help you run at recess
choices.feedback: Running at recess uses muscles you choose to move, which are voluntary muscles, not involuntary ones.

choices.text: muscles that rest when you sleep
choices.feedback: The passage says involuntary muscles are always at work, so they don't rest — your heart and lungs keep going even when you're asleep!

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: Based on the article, how is the diaphragm different from the heart?

choices.text: You can control the diaphragm on purpose, but the heart works completely on its own.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the article says the diaphragm can be controlled on purpose (like when you hold your breath), while the heart beats completely on its own all day and night without you controlling it.

choices.text: The diaphragm pumps blood through the body, but the heart pulls air into the lungs.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because it has the jobs backwards — the heart pumps blood and the diaphragm pulls air into the lungs, not the other way around.

choices.text: The diaphragm rests when you are sleeping, but the heart beats all day and all night.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the article says the diaphragm keeps working while you sleep, so it does not rest at night.

choices.text: You use the diaphragm to move your bones, but the heart helps to digest your lunch.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the diaphragm helps you breathe (not move bones), and the heart pumps blood (not digest food).

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: Which pair of headings helps you locate what causes your arm to bend and what causes your lungs to fill with air?

choices.text: "Muscles That Follow Orders" and "One Muscle That Works Both Ways"
choices.feedback: A is correct because 'Muscles That Follow Orders' explains how your arm bends using voluntary muscles, and 'One Muscle That Works Both Ways' explains how the diaphragm fills your lungs with air.

choices.text: "Muscles That Follow Orders" and "The Muscle That Never Rests"
choices.feedback: B is wrong because 'The Muscle That Never Rests' is about your heart pumping blood, not about your lungs filling with air.

choices.text: "Muscles That Work Without You" and "One Muscle That Works Both Ways"
choices.feedback: C is wrong because 'Muscles That Work Without You' talks about your stomach and blood vessels, not about how your arm bends.

choices.text: "Muscles That Work Without You" and "The Muscle That Never Rests"
choices.feedback: D is wrong because neither 'Muscles That Work Without You' nor 'The Muscle That Never Rests' explains arm bending or lung filling.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: Which sequence best describes the main ideas the article teaches, from beginning to end?

choices.text: First muscles that work alone, then muscles you control, then the heart, then the diaphragm
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because it says muscles that work alone are taught first, but the article actually starts with muscles YOU control (voluntary muscles).

choices.text: First muscles you control, then muscles that work alone, then the heart, then the diaphragm
choices.feedback: B is correct because it matches the order of the article's sections: voluntary muscles first, then involuntary muscles, then the heart, then the diaphragm.

choices.text: First the heart that pumps blood, then the diaphragm, then muscles you control, then muscles that work alone
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the heart is discussed in the third section of the article, not the first.

choices.text: First the diaphragm that moves air, then the heart, then muscles that work alone, then muscles you control
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the diaphragm is the last big topic in the article, not the first.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: Based on the article, how are the muscles in your arm and your diaphragm similar?

choices.text: Both of them can be controlled by you on purpose.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the article tells us arm muscles are voluntary (you control them on purpose), and the diaphragm can also be controlled on purpose when you hold your breath or blow out candles.

choices.text: Both of them are smooth muscles that digest food.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because arm muscles are skeletal muscles that move your bones, not smooth muscles that digest food — and your diaphragm helps you breathe, not digest food.

choices.text: Both of them are used to pump blood to your body.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because pumping blood is the job of your heart's cardiac muscle, not your arm muscles or your diaphragm.

choices.text: Both of them work completely without your brain.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because arm muscles do NOT work without your brain — they are voluntary muscles that only move when you decide to move them.

syllabus_metadata.title: Voluntary and Involuntary: Muscles You Control and Muscles That Work on Their Own

units.title: The Human Body: The Skeletal System

assessments.title: The Framework Inside You: Your 206 Bones

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Skeletal System: 206 Bones Holding You Together

A Living Framework Inside You

Right now, press your finger against your kneecap. Feel that hard bump under your skin? That is one of 206 bones working inside your body this very second. Together, all those bones form your skeletal system — the framework that holds you up and gives your body its shape. Think of it like the wooden frame inside the walls of a house. Without that frame, the house would crumble. Without your skeleton, you would collapse into a soft heap on the floor, like a puppet with its strings cut.

You might picture bones as dry, white sticks — like plastic Halloween skeletons. But here is the surprising part: your bones are alive. Real bones are warm and slightly wet. Tiny blood vessels run through them, carrying food and oxygen. That is why a broken bone can heal itself. Living things grow and repair, and your bones do both every single day.
stimulus.content_text:
The Skeletal System: 206 Bones Holding You TogetherA Living Framework Inside You
Right now, press your finger against your kneecap. Feel that hard bump under your skin? That is one of 206 bones working inside your body this very second. Together, all those bones form your skeletal system — the framework that holds you up and gives your body its shape. Think of it like the wooden frame inside the walls of a house. Without that frame, the house would crumble. Without your skeleton, you would collapse into a soft heap on the floor, like a puppet with its strings cut.
You might picture bones as dry, white sticks — like plastic Halloween skeletons. But here is the surprising part: your bones are alive. Real bones are warm and slightly wet. Tiny blood vessels run through them, carrying food and oxygen. That is why a broken bone can heal itself. Living things grow and repair, and your bones do both every single day.

items.prompt: According to the text, how are real bones different from plastic Halloween skeletons?

choices.text: Real bones are dry and light inside the body
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage says real bones are 'slightly wet,' not dry.

choices.text: Real bones are alive and warm inside the body
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly says real bones are warm, alive, and have blood vessels running through them.

choices.text: Real bones are white and hard inside the body
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because being white and hard describes the plastic Halloween skeleton, not what makes real bones different.

choices.text: Real bones are stiff and cold inside the body
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage says real bones are warm, which is the opposite of cold.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
More Bones Than You Think

When you were born, your body held about 270 bones. That is more than an adult has! As you grow, some of those small bones slowly fuse — meaning they join together and become one bigger, stronger bone, the way two soap bubbles can merge into one. By the time you are fully grown, you will have exactly 206 bones. So right now, as a kid, you actually have more bones than your parents do.
stimulus.content_text:
More Bones Than You Think
When you were born, your body held about 270 bones. That is more than an adult has! As you grow, some of those small bones slowly fuse — meaning they join together and become one bigger, stronger bone, the way two soap bubbles can merge into one. By the time you are fully grown, you will have exactly 206 bones. So right now, as a kid, you actually have more bones than your parents do.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, why do adults have fewer bones than babies?

choices.text: Because adults lose some bones as they get older
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never says adults lose bones — instead, small bones join together to make bigger ones.

choices.text: Because some small bones fuse together during growth
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage clearly explains that small bones fuse, or join together, as you grow, which reduces the total number of bones.

choices.text: Because babies grow extra bones before they are born
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says babies are born with about 270 bones, not that they grow extra bones before birth.

choices.text: Because adults break bones and they disappear over time
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never mentions broken bones, and in real life broken bones heal — they do not disappear.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Three Big Jobs

Your skeleton does three important jobs. The first is support. It holds your body upright so you can stand, sit, and lift your head. Without bones, you would be as floppy as a jellyfish.

The second job is protection. Your skull wraps around your brain like a built-in helmet made of bone. Your rib cage — a set of curved bones in your chest — shields your heart and lungs the way a chest guard protects a baseball catcher. Your spine, a column of small bones stacked one on top of another, guards your spinal cord. The spinal cord is the bundle of nerves that carries messages between your brain and the rest of your body. Without bony protection, these soft, important organs would be in danger.

The third job is movement. Your bones work with muscles to help you walk, run, jump, and dance. Muscles attach to bones and pull on them. When a muscle contracts — which means it squeezes and gets shorter, like a rubber band snapping back — it tugs on a bone, and your body moves.
stimulus.content_text:
Three Big Jobs
Your skeleton does three important jobs. The first is support. It holds your body upright so you can stand, sit, and lift your head. Without bones, you would be as floppy as a jellyfish.
The second job is protection. Your skull wraps around your brain like a built-in helmet made of bone. Your rib cage — a set of curved bones in your chest — shields your heart and lungs the way a chest guard protects a baseball catcher. Your spine, a column of small bones stacked one on top of another, guards your spinal cord. The spinal cord is the bundle of nerves that carries messages between your brain and the rest of your body. Without bony protection, these soft, important organs would be in danger.
The third job is movement. Your bones work with muscles to help you walk, run, jump, and dance. Muscles attach to bones and pull on them. When a muscle contracts — which means it squeezes and gets shorter, like a rubber band snapping back — it tugs on a bone, and your body moves.

items.prompt: According to the passage, what is the correct order of steps when a muscle moves a bone?

choices.text: A muscle contracts, it tugs on a bone, and the body moves.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage says a muscle contracts first, then tugs on a bone, and then the body moves — exactly that order!

choices.text: A bone moves, a muscle contracts, and the body tugs.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because a bone doesn't move first; the muscle has to contract and tug before any bone moves.

choices.text: The body moves, a muscle tugs, and a bone contracts.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the body moving is the last step, not the first, and muscles tug — they don't contract last.

choices.text: A bone tugs on a muscle, it contracts, and the body moves.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage says muscles attach to bones and pull on them, so it's the muscle tugging the bone, not the bone tugging the muscle.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Bending Without Breaking

You might think your skeleton is stiff all the way through, like a statue. Actually, your body bends easily because of joints — the places where two bones meet. Your knees, elbows, shoulders, and knuckles are all joints. Joints let you twist, bend, and turn. Some bones flex a little, too. Press gently on your ribs and feel them give. That tiny bend helps your chest expand when you breathe.
stimulus.content_text:
Bending Without Breaking
You might think your skeleton is stiff all the way through, like a statue. Actually, your body bends easily because of joints — the places where two bones meet. Your knees, elbows, shoulders, and knuckles are all joints. Joints let you twist, bend, and turn. Some bones flex a little, too. Press gently on your ribs and feel them give. That tiny bend helps your chest expand when you breathe.

items.prompt: In the section, the author compares a skeleton to a statue by saying it might seem 'stiff all the way through, like a statue.' Based on this comparison, what does the word 'stiff' most likely mean?

choices.text: hard to bend or move
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage contrasts 'stiff' with bending easily, showing that stiff means hard to bend or move.

choices.text: strong and not easy to break
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the passage is comparing how things bend, not how easily they break.

choices.text: cold and hard to touch
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage never talks about temperature or how something feels when you touch it.

choices.text: tall and hard to reach
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because height and reach have nothing to do with the comparison the author is making about bending.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Big Bones, Tiny Bones

Bones come in many shapes and sizes. The longest bone in your body is the femur, or thigh bone. It stretches from your hip to your knee, and it is incredibly strong. The smallest bone is the stapes, deep inside your ear, which helps you hear. It is only about the size of a grain of rice.
stimulus.content_text:
Big Bones, Tiny Bones
Bones come in many shapes and sizes. The longest bone in your body is the femur, or thigh bone. It stretches from your hip to your knee, and it is incredibly strong. The smallest bone is the stapes, deep inside your ear, which helps you hear. It is only about the size of a grain of rice.

items.prompt: According to the passage, what is the femur?

choices.text: the smallest bone inside your ear
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage says the stapes is the small bone inside your ear, not the femur.

choices.text: the thigh bone in your body
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly says the femur is 'the thigh bone' in your body.

choices.text: the strongest muscle in your leg
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the femur is a bone, not a muscle — the passage never mentions muscles at all.

choices.text: the joint that connects your hip
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage describes the femur as a bone that goes from your hip to your knee, not as a joint.

items.title: Section 6 - Guiding Question 5

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
A System That Grows With You

It is alive, it is growing, and it is healing — right now, inside you. Your bones are as busy as you are.
stimulus.content_text:
A System That Grows With You
It is alive, it is growing, and it is healing — right now, inside you. Your bones are as busy as you are.

items.prompt: Which detail from the section "A System That Grows With You" best supports the idea that bones are active inside your body?

choices.text: Bones stay still inside your body.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage says bones are 'as busy as you are,' which means they are active, not still.

choices.text: Bones are growing and healing right now.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly says bones are 'growing and healing right now, inside you,' showing they are always active.

choices.text: Bones are only important when you run.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage never mentions running or says bones are only important at certain times.

choices.text: Bones are the same size your whole life.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage says bones are growing, which means they do change size over time.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: Based on the article, how is the femur different from the spine?

choices.text: The femur is a single long bone, but the spine is many small bones.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage clearly describes the femur as one long thigh bone and the spine as many small bones stacked together, showing they are built in completely different ways.

choices.text: The femur protects the soft organs, but the spine helps the body move.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the article says the spine protects the spinal cord, not the femur — the femur's job is to help with movement, not protection of soft organs.

choices.text: The femur is found in the chest, but the spine is found in the legs.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the article places the femur in your thigh (between your hip and knee), not your chest, and the spine runs down your back, not your legs.

choices.text: The femur bends easily at the joints, but the spine is completely stiff.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the article does not say the femur bends at joints or that the spine is completely stiff — in fact, the spine is made of many small bones that allow some movement.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: Which two section headings contain the information about what causes a broken bone to heal and what causes your body to bend?

choices.text: 'Three Big Jobs' and 'A Living Framework Inside You'
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because 'Three Big Jobs' covers support, protection, and movement — it does not explain why broken bones heal or why your body bends.

choices.text: 'A Living Framework Inside You' and 'Bending Without Breaking'
choices.feedback: B is correct because 'A Living Framework Inside You' explains that living blood vessels help broken bones heal, and 'Bending Without Breaking' explains that joints are what let your body bend.

choices.text: 'More Bones Than You Think' and 'A System That Grows With You'
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because 'More Bones Than You Think' is about bones fusing together as you grow, and 'A System That Grows With You' is just a short closing section — neither one answers both questions.

choices.text: 'Bending Without Breaking' and 'Big Bones, Tiny Bones'
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because 'Big Bones, Tiny Bones' only describes the sizes and names of different bones, not what causes bending or healing.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: Which sequence of events best supports the main idea that your skeleton is a living, changing system?

choices.text: First, you start with 270 bones, then some bones join together, and finally they keep growing and healing.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage says you're born with 270 bones, some fuse together as you grow, and your bones keep healing and growing every day — all proof that your skeleton is alive and always changing.

choices.text: First, you start with 206 bones, then they break into smaller pieces, and finally you have 270 bones.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because it has the facts backwards — you start with 270 bones (not 206), and bones join together as you grow instead of breaking into smaller pieces.

choices.text: First, your bones form a wooden frame, then they turn into dry sticks, and finally they stay completely stiff.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the passage says the opposite — bones are NOT dry sticks, they are warm and alive, and they can bend a little instead of staying completely stiff.

choices.text: First, your muscles attach to your bones, then the muscles squeeze tightly, and finally your body moves around.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because it describes how muscles pull on bones to make your body move, which is about the job of movement, not about the skeleton being a living, changing system.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: How is the skull's job similar to the rib cage's job, according to the article?

choices.text: Both are made of many small bones stacked together.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the article describes the spine — not the skull or rib cage — as small bones stacked on top of each other.

choices.text: Both use hard bone to shield soft organs inside.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the article says both the skull and rib cage use hard bone to protect soft, important organs — the brain, heart, and lungs.

choices.text: Both help muscles pull so the body can move.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because helping muscles pull for movement is a job described for bones in general, and is not how the skull and rib cage are similar to each other.

choices.text: Both fuse from smaller bones as a child grows up.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because while the article does mention bones fusing as you grow, it does not say this is something the skull and rib cage share as a special similarity.

syllabus_metadata.title: The Framework Inside You: Your 206 Bones

assessments.title: Bending and Twisting: How Joints Let You Move

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Skeletal System: Why Your Bones Don't Move Without Joints

A Body Made of Stone — and Why Joints Change Everything

Hold your arm out straight. Now try to pick up a cup without bending your elbow. Your fingers reach, but the cup barely moves. Bend your elbow, and suddenly the cup lifts right up. That bend happens at a joint — a place where two or more bones meet and allow movement. Without joints, your skeleton would be locked in one position, like a statue carved from rock.

You might think joints only exist in your arms and legs. Actually, they are all over your body. Your spine is a tower of 33 small bones, and joints connect each one. That is how you can twist to look behind you or bend to tie your shoes. Your jaw has a joint that works every time you chew or talk. Even your fingers have joints — three in each finger! Altogether, your body has hundreds of joints, and different types allow different kinds of movement.
stimulus.content_text:
The Skeletal System: Why Your Bones Don't Move Without JointsA Body Made of Stone — and Why Joints Change Everything
Hold your arm out straight. Now try to pick up a cup without bending your elbow. Your fingers reach, but the cup barely moves. Bend your elbow, and suddenly the cup lifts right up. That bend happens at a joint — a place where two or more bones meet and allow movement. Without joints, your skeleton would be locked in one position, like a statue carved from rock.
You might think joints only exist in your arms and legs. Actually, they are all over your body. Your spine is a tower of 33 small bones, and joints connect each one. That is how you can twist to look behind you or bend to tie your shoes. Your jaw has a joint that works every time you chew or talk. Even your fingers have joints — three in each finger! Altogether, your body has hundreds of joints, and different types allow different kinds of movement.

items.prompt: According to the text, how is a skeleton without joints different from a skeleton with joints?

choices.text: It would be locked in one position.
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says a skeleton without joints would be 'locked in one position, like a statue carved from rock,' which means it couldn't move.

choices.text: It would be made of many small bones.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because having many small bones describes the spine, not what happens when joints are missing.

choices.text: It would be able to bend very easily.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because it's the opposite of what the passage says — without joints, you would NOT be able to bend easily at all.

choices.text: It would be located only in your fingers.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage actually says joints are found all over your body, not just in your fingers.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Hinge Joints and Ball-and-Socket Joints: Two Ways to Move

A hinge joint works exactly like the hinge on a door. A door swings open and closed in one direction. It does not swing sideways. Your elbows and knees have hinge joints. For example, when you kick a soccer ball, your knee swings your lower leg forward. When you curl a heavy book toward your chest, your elbow bends your arm inward. One direction — open and closed.

A ball-and-socket joint gives you much more freedom. Picture a ball sitting inside a smooth, round cup. The ball can roll forward, backward, sideways, and even spin in circles. Your shoulders and hips have ball-and-socket joints. That is why you can swing your arm all the way around like a windmill or stretch your leg out to the side during a cartwheel. Your hip joint lets you kick your leg forward, backward, and out to the side.

Hinge Joints and Ball-and-Socket Joints: Two Ways to Move

For example, when you kick a soccer ball, your knee swings your lower leg forward. When you curl a heavy book toward your chest, your elbow bends your arm inward. One direction — open and closed.
A ball-and-socket joint gives you much more freedom. Picture a ball sitting inside a smooth, round cup. The ball can roll forward, backward, sideways, and even spin in circles. Your shoulders and hips have ball-and-socket joints. That is why you can swing your arm all the way around like a windmill or stretch your leg out to the side during a cartwheel. items.prompt: Based on the passage, why can you swing your arm all the way around like a windmill?

choices.text: because your arm has a hinge joint that opens and closes
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because hinge joints, like the ones in your elbows and knees, only open and close in one direction — they cannot swing all the way around.

choices.text: because your shoulder has a ball-and-socket joint
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly says your shoulder has a ball-and-socket joint, and that is why you can swing your arm all the way around like a windmill.

choices.text: because your elbow lets your arm move in circles
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because your elbow has a hinge joint, which only bends in one direction, not in circles.

choices.text: because your muscles push your bones in one direction
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage never mentions muscles pushing bones in one direction as the reason for the windmill movement.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Fixed Joints: Locked Tight

Some joints do not move at all. These are called fixed joints, and the bones of your skull are connected by them. When you were a baby, small gaps separated those skull bones so your brain had room to grow. As you got older, your skull bones grew together and locked in place, forming a strong shell around your brain.
stimulus.content_text:
Fixed Joints: Locked Tight
Some joints do not move at all. These are called fixed joints, and the bones of your skull are connected by them. When you were a baby, small gaps separated those skull bones so your brain had room to grow. As you got older, your skull bones grew together and locked in place, forming a strong shell around your brain.

items.prompt: According to the text, what happens to your skull bones after you are a baby?

choices.text: They grow together and lock in place.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage says skull bones 'grew together and locked in place' after you are a baby.

choices.text: They form small gaps for the brain.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because small gaps exist when you are a baby, not after you grow older.

choices.text: They turn into completely new bones.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never mentions the bones turning into completely new bones.

choices.text: They start to move around very freely.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because skull bones do the opposite — they lock in place and stop moving.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
What Keeps Joints Running Smoothly

Bones at a movable joint do not actually touch each other. A smooth, rubbery layer called cartilage covers the ends of each bone, like a soft cap. Cartilage acts as a cushion so the bones do not bump and grind together. Many movable joints are also filled with a slippery liquid called synovial fluid (say it: sih-NO-vee-ul). Think of it as oil inside a machine — it keeps the parts sliding easily. Together, cartilage and synovial fluid keep your joints working properly.
stimulus.content_text:
What Keeps Joints Running Smoothly
Bones at a movable joint do not actually touch each other. A smooth, rubbery layer called cartilage covers the ends of each bone, like a soft cap. Cartilage acts as a cushion so the bones do not bump and grind together. Many movable joints are also filled with a slippery liquid called synovial fluid (say it: sih-NO-vee-ul). Think of it as oil inside a machine — it keeps the parts sliding easily. Together, cartilage and synovial fluid keep your joints working properly.

items.prompt: Based on the text, how are cartilage and synovial fluid similar?

choices.text: They both act like oil inside a machine.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage only compares synovial fluid to oil inside a machine, not cartilage.

choices.text: They both help joints work smoothly.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage says both cartilage and synovial fluid work together to keep joints working properly.

choices.text: They both cover the ends of bones.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because only cartilage covers the ends of bones — synovial fluid fills the joint space as a liquid.

choices.text: They both are made of hard bone.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because cartilage is described as smooth and rubbery, and synovial fluid is a slippery liquid, so neither one is hard bone.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
A Living, Moving Frame

Here is a surprising fact: bones are not dead like rocks. They are alive. They grow and change just like the rest of your body. Because bones are living tissue, they can heal when they break and get stronger when you exercise.

Right now, joints are at work in your body. Your fingers bend around a book or a tablet. Your neck tilts your head. Your jaw opens slightly with each breath. Every wave, every jump, every nod uses a different set of joints doing exactly the job they were shaped to do. Your skeleton is not a statue. It is a living, moving frame — and joints are the reason it bends.

items.title: Quiz Question 1

items.prompt: Which sequence of events is a key detail that supports the main idea that bones are living things that grow and change?

choices.text: First, babies have gaps in their skulls, and then the bones grow together.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage describes how baby skull bones have gaps and then grow together over time, which is a clear example of living bones growing and changing.

assessments.title: Not Just Hard Stuff: Cartilage and What It Does

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Human Body: The Skeletal System

Two Kinds of Tough

Reach up and gently squeeze the tip of your nose. It bends. It wiggles. Now tap the top of your head. Hard as a helmet. Both your nose and your skull belong to your skeletal system, but they feel completely different. Your skull is made of bone, a material that is stiff and strong. That hardness has a job — your skull wraps around your brain like armor, protecting it every time you bump your head. Your nose, though, is made of something softer called cartilage — a firm but bendable tissue, like a thick rubber eraser that bounces back into shape.
stimulus.content_text:
The Human Body: The Skeletal SystemTwo Kinds of Tough
Reach up and gently squeeze the tip of your nose. It bends. It wiggles. Now tap the top of your head. Hard as a helmet. Both your nose and your skull belong to your skeletal system, but they feel completely different. Your skull is made of bone, a material that is stiff and strong. That hardness has a job — your skull wraps around your brain like armor, protecting it every time you bump your head. Your nose, though, is made of something softer called cartilage — a firm but bendable tissue, like a thick rubber eraser that bounces back into shape.

items.prompt: According to the text, what is one way the nose is different from the skull?

choices.text: The nose is hard, but the skull is bendable.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because it has it backwards — the passage says the nose is soft and bendable, while the skull is the hard one.

choices.text: The nose is softer, but the skull is stiff.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage clearly states the nose is made of softer cartilage and the skull is made of stiff, strong bone.

choices.text: The nose protects the brain, but the skull helps it wiggle.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage says the skull protects the brain like armor, not the nose.

choices.text: The nose is made of bone, but the skull is cartilage.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because it has the materials switched — the passage says the nose is cartilage and the skull is bone, not the other way around.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Where Cartilage Hides

You carry cartilage in more places than you might guess. Pinch the outside of your ear. That flexible curve? Cartilage. Wiggle your ear and you can feel it bend. Cartilage also lines the ends of many bones inside your joints — the places where two bones meet, like your knees, elbows, and hips. There, it works like a smooth, slippery pad. Without that pad, your bones would scrape together every time you took a step or bent your arm. Imagine two rocks grinding against each other. Ouch. Cartilage keeps that from happening.

You also have cartilage between the bones in your spine. These small pads are called discs — thin cushions stacked between each bone like soft coins in a tower. They absorb the shock when you run and jump. They also let your spine twist and bend instead of staying stiff as a pole.

Where Cartilage Hides

Without that pad, your bones would scrape together every time you took a step or bent your arm. Imagine two rocks grinding against each other. Ouch. Cartilage keeps that from happening.
You also have cartilage between the bones in your spine. These small pads are called discs — thin cushions stacked between each bone like soft coins in a tower. They absorb the shock when you run and jump. They also let your spine twist and bend instead of staying stiff as a pole.

items.prompt: Which detail from the article best supports the idea that cartilage in joints keeps people from getting hurt?

choices.text: It bends when you wiggle your ear back and forth.
choices.feedback: This is about how cartilage in your ear bends and wiggles, which has nothing to do with protecting joints from pain.

choices.text: It stops bones from scraping together when you move.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly says cartilage acts like a pad in joints so bones don't scrape together, which would hurt.

choices.text: It stacks between spine bones like soft coins in a tower.
choices.feedback: This talks about cartilage discs in your spine, not about cartilage in joints keeping you from getting hurt.

choices.text: It makes your spine twist and bend instead of staying stiff.
choices.feedback: This is also about spine cartilage helping you twist and bend, not about joint cartilage protecting you from injury.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
A Skeleton That Started Soft

Here is something astonishing. When you were a baby, most of your skeleton was not bone at all. It was cartilage. As you grew, that soft cartilage slowly hardened into real bone. Scientists call this process ossification — a word that means "turning into bone." Ossification is still happening inside you right now, and it will keep going until you are a young adult.

You might think bones are dead and solid, like rocks. Actually, bones are alive. They have living cells inside them. They grow longer and stronger as you get older. That is why a broken bone can heal — living tissue repairs itself. Some cartilage in your body will become bone through ossification. Other cartilage, like the kind in your nose and ears, stays soft your whole life.

A Skeleton That Started Soft

Scientists call this process ossification — a word that means "turning into bone." Ossification is still happening inside you right now, and it will keep going until you are a young adult.
You might think bones are dead and solid, like rocks. Actually, bones are alive. They have living cells inside them. They grow longer and stronger as you get older. That is why a broken bone can heal — living tissue repairs itself. Some cartilage in your body will become bone through ossification. Other cartilage, like the kind in your nose and ears, stays soft your whole life.

items.prompt: According to the text, what happened first in the change from a baby's skeleton to an adult skeleton?

choices.text: Most of the skeleton was cartilage.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage clearly states that babies start with mostly cartilage, which is the very first step before it turns into bone.

choices.text: Most of the skeleton was real bone.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because real bone comes later in the process — the skeleton starts as cartilage first, then slowly becomes bone.

choices.text: The nose and ears turned into bone.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage says the cartilage in your nose and ears stays soft your whole life and never turns into bone.

choices.text: The bones stopped growing longer.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because bones stopping their growth is something that happens at the end, when you become a young adult, not at the beginning.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Slow to Heal

Cartilage is different from bone in one important way. Bones have blood vessels running through them. Blood carries nutrients — the food and energy cells need to stay healthy and repair themselves. Cartilage has no blood vessels of its own. Instead, it soaks up nutrients from the fluid around it, the way a sponge soaks up water from a puddle. This slower delivery system means cartilage heals much more slowly than bone when it gets hurt.
stimulus.content_text:
Slow to Heal
Cartilage is different from bone in one important way. Bones have blood vessels running through them. Blood carries nutrients — the food and energy cells need to stay healthy and repair themselves. Cartilage has no blood vessels of its own. Instead, it soaks up nutrients from the fluid around it, the way a sponge soaks up water from a puddle. This slower delivery system means cartilage heals much more slowly than bone when it gets hurt.

items.prompt: The text states that both bones and cartilage need 'nutrients.' Based on the meaning of this word, how are bones and cartilage similar?

choices.text: They both use blood vessels to carry their food.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage says cartilage has NO blood vessels — only bones do.

choices.text: They both need food and energy to fix themselves.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage defines nutrients as the food and energy cells need to repair themselves, and both bones and cartilage need nutrients to heal.

choices.text: They both heal at the exact same slow speed.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage clearly says cartilage heals much MORE slowly than bone, so they do not heal at the same speed.

choices.text: They both soak up liquid from a small puddle.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the puddle is just a comparison (like a sponge in a puddle) to help explain how cartilage soaks up nutrients — neither bones nor cartilage actually sit in puddles.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
More Than a Frame

Your skeletal system does far more than hold you upright. Your skull shields your brain. Your ribs curve around your heart and lungs like a cage. Cartilage cushions every jump and twist. And deep inside your bones, your body makes new blood cells every single day.

So the next time you wiggle your nose or bend your ear, think about what is happening under your skin. Soft cartilage and hard bone are working together — one bending, one protecting, both alive and both changing as you grow. Your skeleton is not a statue. It is growing right along with you.

More Than a Frame

So the next time you wiggle your nose or bend your ear, think about what is happening under your skin. Soft cartilage and hard bone are working together — one bending, one protecting, both alive and both changing as you grow. Your skeleton is not a statue. It is growing right along with you.

choices.text: Your bones stay the same forever
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because it is the opposite of what the text says — the author is telling us bones do change as you grow.

choices.text: Your skeleton is very hard to move

choices.text: Bone makes new blood cells, but cartilage protects your brain.

items.prompt: According to the article, why are bones able to heal faster than cartilage?

choices.text: They use blood vessels to quickly bring food to living cells.

items.prompt: Use the headings in the article to help you. Which sequence shows the correct order of topics the author uses to explain cartilage and bone?

choices.text: First explaining what they are, next how they grow, and last how they heal.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the article's headings show it first describes what bone and cartilage are, then how they grow through ossification, and last how they heal slowly.

choices.text: First explaining how they heal, next what they are, and last how they grow.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the article does not start with healing — healing is the last big topic, covered in 'Slow to Heal.'

choices.feedback: C is wrong because the article does not start with growing — it starts by explaining what bone and cartilage are.

choices.text: First explaining what they are, next how they heal, and last how they grow.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because it puts healing before growing, but in the article, 'A Skeleton That Started Soft' (growing) comes before 'Slow to Heal' (healing).

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: The article describes both bone and cartilage. What is one important similarity between bone and cartilage that the author shares across the article?

choices.text: They are both hard materials that protect the brain.

choices.text: They both heal quickly using blood vessels.

choices.text: They are both alive and both change as you grow.
choices.feedback: C is correct because the article says both bone and cartilage are alive and keep changing as you grow, and the author even uses those exact words at the end of the article.

assessments.title: Inside a Bone: Marrow, Growth, and Strength

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Human Body: The Skeletal System

A Skeleton That's Alive

Most people picture a skeleton as something dry and white — like a Halloween decoration. But your real bones are nothing like that. They are warm, slightly wet, and very much alive.
stimulus.content_text:
The Human Body: The Skeletal SystemA Skeleton That's Alive
Most people picture a skeleton as something dry and white — like a Halloween decoration. But your real bones are nothing like that. They are warm, slightly wet, and very much alive.

items.prompt: According to the text, how are real bones different from a Halloween skeleton decoration?

choices.text: They are dry, white, and not alive
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because dry, white, and not alive describes the Halloween decoration — the passage says real bones are the opposite of that.

choices.text: They are warm, slightly wet, and alive
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage uses those exact words — 'warm, slightly wet, and very much alive' — to describe real bones.

choices.text: They are cold, slightly wet, and not alive
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage says real bones are warm (not cold) and that they ARE alive.

choices.text: They are warm, very dry, and alive
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage says bones are 'slightly wet,' so calling them 'very dry' is the opposite of what the text says.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
What's Inside a Bone

The outside of a bone is covered in a hard, smooth layer. This layer is dense, meaning the material is packed tightly together, like a brick wall with no gaps. That toughness lets your bones hold up your weight and protect your organs. Your skull shields your brain. Your ribs wrap around your heart and lungs like a cage.

Just beneath that hard outer layer is a spongy layer. It looks like a honeycomb, full of tiny holes and spaces. This spongy layer keeps bones strong without making them heavy. If your bones were solid like rocks, they would weigh so much you could barely walk.

Even deeper inside many bones sits a soft, jelly-like material called bone marrow. Red bone marrow is your body's blood cell factory. It builds red blood cells, which carry oxygen everywhere your body needs it. It makes white blood cells, which fight germs and keep you healthy. And it creates tiny cell pieces called platelets — little patches that help your blood clot and stop the bleeding when you get a cut.

What's Inside a Bone

Just beneath that hard outer layer is a spongy layer. It looks like a honeycomb, full of tiny holes and spaces. This spongy layer keeps bones strong without making them heavy. If your bones were solid like rocks, they would weigh so much you could barely walk.
Even deeper inside many bones sits a soft, jelly-like material called bone marrow. Red bone marrow is your body's blood cell factory. It builds red blood cells, which carry oxygen everywhere your body needs it. It makes white blood cells, which fight germs and keep you healthy. And it creates tiny cell pieces called platelets — little patches that help your blood clot and stop the bleeding when you get a cut.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, why does the spongy layer have tiny holes and spaces?

choices.text: to make bones softer and easier to bend
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage never says the spongy layer makes bones softer or easier to bend — that's not its job.

choices.text: to keep bones strong but not too heavy
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly says the spongy layer 'keeps bones strong without making them heavy,' and the holes and spaces are what make that possible.

choices.text: to help bones build blood cells faster
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because building blood cells is done by bone marrow, which is a different, deeper part of the bone.

choices.text: to make the hard outer layer grow thicker
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage never connects the spongy layer to making the hard outer layer grow thicker.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Bones That Build and Bones That Break

Because bones are living tissue, they grow and change. That's why you get taller every year. Two special types of cells do this work. Osteoblasts build new bone, like construction workers adding bricks. Osteoclasts break down old bone, like a cleanup crew removing worn-out material. Together, they rebuild your skeleton bit by bit throughout your whole life.

Living bones can also heal. If you fracture — or break — a bone, your body starts repairing it right away. New bone cells slowly grow across the break and fill the gap. A doctor may put a cast on your arm or leg, but the cast does not fix the bone. It just holds the pieces still so your body can do the real work. Over several weeks, the bone becomes whole again — all on its own.
stimulus.content_text:
Bones That Build and Bones That Break
Because bones are living tissue, they grow and change. That's why you get taller every year. Two special types of cells do this work. Osteoblasts build new bone, like construction workers adding bricks. Osteoclasts break down old bone, like a cleanup crew removing worn-out material. Together, they rebuild your skeleton bit by bit throughout your whole life.
Living bones can also heal. If you fracture — or break — a bone, your body starts repairing it right away. New bone cells slowly grow across the break and fill the gap. A doctor may put a cast on your arm or leg, but the cast does not fix the bone. It just holds the pieces still so your body can do the real work. Over several weeks, the bone becomes whole again — all on its own.

items.prompt: According to the text, what happens first after a bone breaks?

choices.text: A doctor puts on a cast
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the cast comes after the body has already started repairing — the passage says the body begins fixing the bone right away, and the cast just helps hold it still.

choices.text: The body starts repairing it
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage clearly says 'your body starts repairing it right away' — that's the very first thing that happens after a break.

choices.text: The bone becomes whole again
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the bone becoming whole again is the last step, happening 'over several weeks' after everything else.

choices.text: Old bone is broken down
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because breaking down old bone is something osteoclasts do all the time as part of normal bone rebuilding, not specifically the first step after a fracture.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
A System That Moves Together

You have 206 bones in your body, and they work together as a system. Bones meet at places called joints — the spots where two bones connect. Your elbow is a hinge joint. It swings open and closed like a door. Your shoulder is a ball-and-socket joint. The round end of one bone fits into a cup-shaped space in another, so your arm can swing in a circle. Without joints, your skeleton would be stiff as a statue. Muscles pull on bones at these joints to make you run, jump, and wave.
stimulus.content_text:
A System That Moves Together
You have 206 bones in your body, and they work together as a system. Bones meet at places called joints — the spots where two bones connect. Your elbow is a hinge joint. It swings open and closed like a door. Your shoulder is a ball-and-socket joint. The round end of one bone fits into a cup-shaped space in another, so your arm can swing in a circle. Without joints, your skeleton would be stiff as a statue. Muscles pull on bones at these joints to make you run, jump, and wave.

items.prompt: Which comparison best helps the reader understand the meaning of hinge joint?

choices.text: a joint that spins like a wheel
choices.feedback: Spinning like a wheel is not in the passage — that does not describe how a hinge joint moves.

choices.text: a joint that opens like a door
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says a hinge joint 'swings open and closed like a door,' which is exactly what answer B says.

choices.text: a joint that bends like a rope
choices.feedback: A rope bends and flops in all directions, but a hinge joint only swings back and forth in one direction, like your elbow.

choices.text: a joint that rolls like a ball
choices.feedback: Rolling like a ball describes the ball-and-socket joint (like your shoulder), not the hinge joint.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Keeping Your Bones Strong

You can help your bones stay healthy. Foods rich in calcium — like milk, cheese, and yogurt — give bones the building material they need. Calcium is a mineral that makes bone hard, the way cement makes concrete strong. Vitamin D, which you get from sunlight and certain foods, helps your body absorb — or soak up — that calcium so it actually reaches your bones. And exercise matters too. When you run or jump, your body senses the push on your bones and builds them thicker and stronger.

So the next time you wiggle your fingers or bend your knee, remember: that movement is 206 living bones, dozens of joints, and billions of brand-new blood cells — all working together inside you right now.

items.prompt: In the section "Keeping Your Bones Strong," what does the word "absorb" mean?

choices.text: To soak up
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage uses a dash to tell you that 'absorb' means 'soak up,' just like a sponge soaks up water.

choices.text: To push on
choices.feedback: This is incorrect — 'to push on' is what happens when you run or jump and your body pushes on your bones, which is a different idea from absorbing.

choices.text: To make hard
choices.feedback: This is incorrect — 'to make hard' describes what calcium does to bones, not what the word 'absorb' means.

choices.text: To work with
choices.feedback: This is incorrect — 'to work with' comes from the last sentence about bones working together, but that has nothing to do with the meaning of 'absorb.'

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: Based on the article, how is the job of a cast different from the job of a joint?

choices.text: A cast holds bones still, but a joint lets bones move.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage clearly says a cast 'holds the pieces still,' while joints let bones move so you can run, jump, and wave.

choices.text: A cast makes bones heavy, but a joint makes bones light.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the article never connects casts to making bones heavy or joints to making bones light — that's not what either one does.

choices.text: A cast builds new bone cells, but a joint breaks them down.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because osteoblasts build new bone and osteoclasts break down old bone — casts and joints have nothing to do with that job.

choices.text: A cast protects your organs, but a joint makes blood cells.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the article says your skull and ribs protect your organs, and bone marrow makes blood cells — not casts or joints.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: Based on the article, why does exercise help make bones thicker and stronger?

choices.text: Exercise sends calcium into the bone marrow to build new blood cells.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because calcium is used to make bones hard, and the article never says exercise sends calcium into bone marrow to make blood cells.

choices.text: Exercise creates a push on bones that tells the body to build them up.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the article directly says the body senses the push from running and jumping and responds by building bones thicker and stronger.

choices.text: Exercise breaks down old bone so osteoclasts can remove worn-out material.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because osteoclasts remove old bone as part of normal bone rebuilding, and the article does not connect this to exercise.

choices.text: Exercise warms up bones so they can absorb more vitamin D from sunlight.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because vitamin D helps your body absorb calcium, but the article never links exercise to warming bones or absorbing vitamin D from sunlight.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: Based on the order of the headings, which topic is explained right before 'A System That Moves Together'?

choices.text: How bones grow and heal
choices.feedback: This is correct because the heading 'Bones That Build and Bones That Break' — which is about how bones grow and heal — comes right before 'A System That Moves Together' in the article.

choices.text: What the spongy layer does
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the spongy layer is talked about in the 'What's Inside a Bone' section, which is two sections before 'A System That Moves Together.'

choices.text: Why calcium is important
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because calcium is explained in the last section, 'Keeping Your Bones Strong,' which actually comes after 'A System That Moves Together.'

choices.text: How a real skeleton looks
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because how a real skeleton looks is described at the very beginning of the article, in the first section called 'A Skeleton That's Alive.'

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: The author compares bone cells to construction workers and compares calcium to cement. What main idea do both of these details show?

choices.text: Bones use special workers and materials to stay strong.
choices.feedback: A is correct because both the construction worker comparison and the cement comparison point to the same big idea: bones need special builders and materials to stay strong.

choices.text: Bones hold a soft jelly that makes new blood cells.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because bone marrow making blood cells is a different fact from the passage and has nothing to do with either comparison.

choices.text: Bones fix themselves slowly after they get a bad break.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because bone healing is a separate topic in the passage and is not connected to the construction worker or cement comparisons.

choices.text: Bones weigh a lot because they are solid like rocks.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the passage says the opposite — bones are NOT solid like rocks, and the spongy layer actually keeps them from being too heavy.

units.title: The Human Body: The Nervous System

assessments.title: Your Body's Command Center: The Brain

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Human Body: The Nervous System

The Nervous System: Your Body's Speedy Control Network

That signal moves at over 250 miles per hour — faster than most cars on the road. Your brain is reading these words, understanding them, and storing them away, all in a fraction of a second.

Your brain is the main city at the center. Your spinal cord, the thick bundle of nerves inside your backbone, is the major highway leading out. And smaller nerves branch off like roads, reaching every finger, every toe, every inch of your skin. That speed comes from your nervous system — the network of your brain, spinal cord, and nerves that controls everything your body does.

It is the most complex thing scientists have ever studied. You might have heard that people only use 10% of their brain. However, that is not true — every part of your brain has a job, and the whole brain stays active.

Your brain has three main parts, and each one does something different.
stimulus.content_text:
The Human Body: The Nervous SystemThe Nervous System: Your Body's Speedy Control Network
That signal moves at over 250 miles per hour — faster than most cars on the road. Your brain is reading these words, understanding them, and storing them away, all in a fraction of a second.
Your brain is the main city at the center. Your spinal cord, the thick bundle of nerves inside your backbone, is the major highway leading out. And smaller nerves branch off like roads, reaching every finger, every toe, every inch of your skin. That speed comes from your nervous system — the network of your brain, spinal cord, and nerves that controls everything your body does.
It is the most complex thing scientists have ever studied. You might have heard that people only use 10% of their brain. However, that is not true — every part of your brain has a job, and the whole brain stays active.
Your brain has three main parts, and each one does something different.

items.prompt: According to the text, how is the brain different from the spinal cord?

choices.text: The brain is the main city, while the spinal cord is the major highway.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage uses these exact comparisons — the brain is called 'the main city' and the spinal cord is called 'the major highway.'

choices.text: The brain is a thick bundle, while the spinal cord is the main center.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because it switches the descriptions around — the passage says the spinal cord is the 'thick bundle,' not the brain.

choices.text: The brain is a smaller road, while the spinal cord is the main city.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage says smaller roads represent the nerves that reach your fingers and toes, not the brain.

choices.text: The brain is the major highway, while the spinal cord is a smaller road.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because it has the brain and spinal cord swapped — the passage gives the highway role to the spinal cord, not the brain.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
The Cerebrum and Cerebellum: Thinking, Moving, and Balancing

The biggest part is the cerebrum, which fills most of the top of your head. The cerebrum handles thinking, memory, language, and problem-solving. In addition, it controls voluntary movements — movements you choose to make, like raising your hand or kicking a soccer ball.

The cerebrum is split into two halves called hemispheres, like two matching halves of a walnut. Here is something strange: the left hemisphere mostly controls the right side of your body. Similarly, the right hemisphere mostly controls the left side. Your brain works in a crisscross!

Tucked behind and below the cerebrum sits a smaller, bumpy part called the cerebellum. It coordinates — or organizes — your balance and smooth movement, like a coach making sure every player does the right thing at the right time. When you write neatly, catch a ball, or ride a bike without wobbling, your cerebellum is hard at work. It takes movement commands from the cerebrum and then fine-tunes them.
stimulus.content_text:
The Cerebrum and Cerebellum: Thinking, Moving, and Balancing
The biggest part is the cerebrum, which fills most of the top of your head. The cerebrum handles thinking, memory, language, and problem-solving. In addition, it controls voluntary movements — movements you choose to make, like raising your hand or kicking a soccer ball.
The cerebrum is split into two halves called hemispheres, like two matching halves of a walnut. Here is something strange: the left hemisphere mostly controls the right side of your body. Similarly, the right hemisphere mostly controls the left side. Your brain works in a crisscross!
Tucked behind and below the cerebrum sits a smaller, bumpy part called the cerebellum. It coordinates — or organizes — your balance and smooth movement, like a coach making sure every player does the right thing at the right time. When you write neatly, catch a ball, or ride a bike without wobbling, your cerebellum is hard at work. It takes movement commands from the cerebrum and then fine-tunes them.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, what causes a person to be able to ride a bike without wobbling?

choices.text: The cerebrum solving a hard problem.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the cerebrum handles thinking and problem-solving, not the balance and smooth movement needed to ride a bike.

choices.text: The left side controlling the right side.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the left-right crisscross pattern describes which hemisphere controls which side of your body, not how you keep your balance.

choices.text: The cerebellum fine-tuning the commands.
choices.feedback: C is correct because the passage directly says the cerebellum coordinates balance and smooth movement, and uses riding a bike without wobbling as its example.

choices.text: The brain working in a crisscross pattern.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the crisscross pattern is about the hemispheres controlling opposite sides of the body, which has nothing to do with staying balanced on a bike.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
The Brain Stem and Nerves: Automatic Actions and Body-Wide Signals

At the very base of your brain, where it connects to the spinal cord, sits the brain stem. You might think your heart beats and your lungs breathe all on their own, without any help from your brain. Actually, the brain stem runs all of those automatic actions. It keeps your heart pumping, your lungs filling with air, and your food digesting. It controls sneezing, swallowing, and blinking too. The brain stem works around the clock, even while you sleep.

None of this would work if your brain could not talk to the rest of your body. That is where nerves come in. Nerves are thin fibers that carry tiny electrical signals back and forth. For example, when you touch something hot, nerves in your hand send a danger signal up through your spinal cord to your brain. Your brain sends a signal back: Pull away! All of this happens before you even feel the pain.

Every sight, sound, taste, smell, and touch travels along nerves.

Even when you drift off at night, your brain keeps working. It organizes your memories and builds your dreams. It sends signals to keep your heart beating and your lungs breathing. It is not just the organ that lets you think — it is the organ that keeps you alive, every single second. Therefore, the three pounds inside your skull are working harder right now than you might ever imagine.

Actually, the brain stem runs all of those automatic actions. It keeps your heart pumping, your lungs filling with air, and your food digesting. It controls sneezing, swallowing, and blinking too. The brain stem works around the clock, even while you sleep.

Every sight, sound, taste, smell, and touch travels along nerves.
Even when you drift off at night, your brain keeps working. It organizes your memories and builds your dreams. It sends signals to keep your heart beating and your lungs breathing. It is not just the organ that lets you think — it is the organ that keeps you alive, every single second.
