items.prompt: According to the text, what happens right after nerves send a danger signal to your brain?

choices.text: Your brain sends a signal back.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage says 'Your brain sends a signal back: Pull away!' — that happens right after the danger signal reaches the brain.

choices.text: Your hand feels the sharp pain.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the passage says you pull your hand away *before* you even feel the pain, so feeling pain is not what happens right after the signal arrives.

choices.text: Your brain stem digests your food.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because digesting food is an automatic job the brain stem does all the time, and it is not part of the hot-touch example in the passage.

choices.text: Your spinal cord pulls your hand.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the spinal cord carries the signal up to the brain, but it is the brain — not the spinal cord — that sends the 'pull away' command back.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: According to the text, how is the cerebrum different from the brain stem?

choices.text: The cerebrum controls actions you choose to make, but the brain stem controls actions that happen automatically.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage clearly states the cerebrum controls voluntary movements you choose to make, while the brain stem controls automatic actions like heartbeat and breathing.

choices.text: The cerebrum works only when you are awake, but the brain stem works only while you sleep.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the passage says the brain stem works 'around the clock, even while you sleep,' meaning it is always on, not just during sleep.

choices.text: The cerebrum sends messages to the spinal cord, but the brain stem sends messages to the nerves.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage never describes the cerebrum and brain stem as sending messages to different destinations like the spinal cord versus the nerves.

choices.text: The cerebrum organizes your balance and smooth movement, but the brain stem organizes your thoughts and memories.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because it swaps the jobs — the cerebellum handles balance and smooth movement, and the cerebrum handles thinking and memory, not the other way around.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: Based on the headings, a student reads the first and third sections to find out what causes the body to react so quickly to a hot stove. Why are both sections needed to explain this?

choices.text: The first section explains what causes signals to travel fast, and the third section explains what causes the hand to pull away.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the first section explains the speed of signals, and the third section explains the hand pulling away from the hot stove.

choices.text: The first section explains what causes the hand to pull away, and the third section explains what causes signals to travel fast.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because it has the sections switched — the first section talks about signal speed, not pulling away, and the third section explains the hand reaction, not signal speed.

choices.text: The first section explains what causes the brain to have parts, and the third section explains what causes the body to fall asleep.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because neither section is about why the brain has parts or about falling asleep.

choices.text: The first section explains what causes the body to balance well, and the third section explains what causes the heart to keep beating.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the first section is about signal speed, not balance, and the third section mentions the heart but that has nothing to do with reacting to a hot stove.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: The main idea of the article is that the nervous system is a fast network that controls your body. Which sequence of events best shows how this network works?

choices.text: Nerves send a warning signal, the spinal cord passes it, and the brain reacts.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage says nerves send a danger signal up through the spinal cord to the brain, and the brain sends back a reaction signal — all before you feel the pain.

choices.text: The body feels the pain, nerves send a warning signal, and the brain reacts.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the passage tells us the body reacts before feeling the pain, so feeling the pain does not come first.

choices.text: The heart pumps your blood, the lungs fill with air, and the stomach digests.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because heartbeating, breathing, and digesting are automatic actions controlled by the brain stem, not a sequence showing how the nervous system sends signals.

choices.text: The cerebrum thinks of moving, the cerebellum goes to sleep, and the brain wakes.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the cerebellum helps coordinate balance and smooth movement — it does not go to sleep while the brain wakes up.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: Based on the article, how is the brain's work while you are awake similar to its work while you are asleep?

choices.text: It rests so the spinal cord can take over.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the article never says the brain rests at night or that the spinal cord takes over its job.

choices.text: It keeps sending signals to run your body.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the article says the brain keeps sending signals to run your heart and lungs both while you are awake and while you sleep.

choices.text: It only uses ten percent of its total power.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the article clearly states that the idea of only using 10% of your brain is not true.

choices.text: It stops controlling your automatic actions.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the brain stem keeps controlling automatic actions like heartbeating and breathing even while you are asleep.

syllabus_metadata.title: Your Body's Command Center: The Brain

assessments.title: The Information Highway: Spinal Cord and Nerves

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Nervous System: Your Body's Lightning-Fast Messenger

A Signal Faster Than a Race Car

Before you even think about it, your foot jerks away. That whole reaction took less than a second. But here is the surprising part — a tiny electrical signal had to race all the way from your foot up to your spine, then back down to the muscles in your leg. It felt instant, but that signal actually traveled a real distance through your body at up to 250 miles per hour. That is about as fast as some of the quickest race cars in the world.

How did that message move so quickly? You might think your brain connects directly to every part of your body, but it does not. Instead, your brain depends on a network of special pathways to send and receive messages. That network is called the nervous system.
stimulus.content_text:
The Nervous System: Your Body's Lightning-Fast MessengerA Signal Faster Than a Race Car
Before you even think about it, your foot jerks away. That whole reaction took less than a second. But here is the surprising part — a tiny electrical signal had to race all the way from your foot up to your spine, then back down to the muscles in your leg. It felt instant, but that signal actually traveled a real distance through your body at up to 250 miles per hour. That is about as fast as some of the quickest race cars in the world.
How did that message move so quickly? You might think your brain connects directly to every part of your body, but it does not. Instead, your brain depends on a network of special pathways to send and receive messages. That network is called the nervous system.

items.prompt: According to the text, you might think your brain connects directly to your body, but what does it use instead?

choices.text: A network of special pathways
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says your brain uses 'a network of special pathways' called the nervous system to send and receive messages.

choices.text: A group of very fast race cars
choices.feedback: This is incorrect — race cars are used in the passage to describe the speed of the signal, not to explain how the brain sends messages.

choices.text: A single long muscle in the leg
choices.feedback: This is incorrect — the passage never mentions a single long muscle being used to send messages from the brain.

choices.text: A tiny electrical machine part
choices.feedback: This is incorrect — the passage does not mention any electrical machine part; the electrical signal described is a natural signal inside your body.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
The Spinal Cord: A Protected Highway

Running down the center of your back is your spinal cord — a thick bundle of nerves about as wide as your thumb. It is not a bone. The spinal cord is soft and made of nerve tissue. It is the main highway between your brain and the rest of your body. Almost every message your brain sends or receives passes through it.

So what protects something that important? Your spine does. Your spine is a column of small bones called vertebrae — think of them as stacked rings of armor. Each vertebra wraps around the spinal cord the way a helmet wraps around your head. The bones are hard. The cord inside is not. That armor matters.
stimulus.content_text:
The Spinal Cord: A Protected Highway
Running down the center of your back is your spinal cord — a thick bundle of nerves about as wide as your thumb. It is not a bone. The spinal cord is soft and made of nerve tissue. It is the main highway between your brain and the rest of your body. Almost every message your brain sends or receives passes through it.
So what protects something that important? Your spine does. Your spine is a column of small bones called vertebrae — think of them as stacked rings of armor. Each vertebra wraps around the spinal cord the way a helmet wraps around your head. The bones are hard. The cord inside is not. That armor matters.

items.prompt: According to the passage, why does the spinal cord need protection from the spine?

choices.text: Because the spinal cord is made of hard bone
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage clearly says the spinal cord is soft and made of nerve tissue, not hard bone.

choices.text: Because the spinal cord is soft nerve tissue
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage says the spinal cord is soft nerve tissue, which means it needs the spine's hard bones to protect it.

choices.text: Because the spinal cord protects the vertebrae
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says the spine protects the spinal cord, not the other way around.

choices.text: Because the spinal cord runs through the head
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says the spinal cord runs down the center of your back, not through your head.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Nerves: Branches That Reach Everywhere

Nerves are thin fibers that branch out from the spinal cord into every corner of your body. Picture a tree. The trunk is your spinal cord. The branches split into smaller and smaller twigs, reaching your fingers, your toes, your skin, and your organs — the body parts inside you that do special jobs, like your heart and stomach. No part of you is left without a connection.

The signals that travel along your nerves are called impulses. An impulse is a tiny burst of electricity, like a spark zipping along a wire. These sparks carry every message your body needs — what you feel, what you see, and what your muscles should do next.
stimulus.content_text:
Nerves: Branches That Reach Everywhere
Nerves are thin fibers that branch out from the spinal cord into every corner of your body. Picture a tree. The trunk is your spinal cord. The branches split into smaller and smaller twigs, reaching your fingers, your toes, your skin, and your organs — the body parts inside you that do special jobs, like your heart and stomach. No part of you is left without a connection.
The signals that travel along your nerves are called impulses. An impulse is a tiny burst of electricity, like a spark zipping along a wire. These sparks carry every message your body needs — what you feel, what you see, and what your muscles should do next.

items.prompt: According to the text, what happens to the nerve branches after they leave the spinal cord?

choices.text: They split into smaller twigs.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage uses a tree comparison to explain that nerve branches 'split into smaller and smaller twigs' after leaving the spinal cord.

choices.text: They turn into tiny sparks.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because tiny sparks describe impulses — the signals that travel along nerves — not the nerve branches themselves.

choices.text: They become special body organs.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because organs are special body parts like your heart and stomach, and the passage never says nerves turn into them.

choices.text: They zip along a thin wire.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because zipping along a wire is how the passage describes an impulse traveling, not what happens to the nerve branches.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Two Types of Nerves, Two Jobs

Your body has two main types of nerves, and each one has a different job. Sensory nerves carry information from your body to your brain. Touch a hot pan, and sensory nerves in your fingers fire off a message: "This is hot!" Motor nerves do the opposite. They carry commands from your brain to your muscles. Your brain decides to yank your hand away, and motor nerves deliver that order to the muscles in your arm.

Sensory nerves are the cars heading toward the brain, bringing news. Motor nerves are the cars heading away, carrying instructions. Without both types working together, your brain would be like a general with no way to talk to the troops.
stimulus.content_text:
Two Types of Nerves, Two Jobs

Touch a hot pan, and sensory nerves in your fingers fire off a message: "This is hot!" Motor nerves do the opposite. They carry commands from your brain to your muscles. Your brain decides to yank your hand away, and motor nerves deliver that order to the muscles in your arm.
Sensory nerves are the cars heading toward the brain, bringing news. Motor nerves are the cars heading away, carrying instructions. Without both types working together, your brain would be like a general with no way to talk to the troops.

items.prompt: The author states that motor nerves 'do the opposite' of sensory nerves. How does the text describe motor nerves to show this difference?

choices.text: They act like a general who is trying to send a message to the troops.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the 'general' in the passage is used to describe the brain, not motor nerves.

choices.text: They act like cars heading away from the brain to carry instructions.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly says motor nerves are like cars heading away from the brain, carrying instructions to the muscles.

choices.text: They act like cars heading toward the brain to bring news.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because cars heading toward the brain describes sensory nerves, which is the opposite of motor nerves.

choices.text: They act like a hot pan sending a message to the fingers.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the hot pan example is about sensory nerves sending a message to the brain, not motor nerves.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
A Body That Never Stops Talking

Remember that sharp pebble from the start? Some reactions happen so fast that your spinal cord handles them on its own, without waiting for your brain. These quick reactions are called reflexes, and they keep you safe when every fraction of a second counts.

Right now, as you read these words, impulses are firing through your nerves. Every sensation, every movement, every thought depends on those tiny sparks racing through you at astonishing speed.

items.prompt: According to the text, what are reflexes?

choices.text: signals that your brain sends to your muscles
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage says reflexes happen without waiting for your brain, so the brain is not the one sending the signals.

choices.text: quick reactions handled by the spinal cord
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly says reflexes are 'quick reactions' that 'your spinal cord handles on its own, without waiting for your brain.'

choices.text: tiny sparks that help you read and think
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because 'tiny sparks' is how the passage describes nerve impulses that help you move and think, not reflexes.

choices.text: slow movements that protect you from harm
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage says reflexes happen when 'every fraction of a second counts,' meaning they are fast, not slow.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: By looking at the key words in the headings, what is the main difference between the spinal cord and the nerves?

choices.text: The spinal cord is a fast race car, but the nerves are slow walkers.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage never says nerves are slow — signals zip through nerves at up to 250 miles per hour, which is super fast!

choices.text: The spinal cord is one main road, but the nerves are many small paths.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage calls the spinal cord a 'highway' (one main road) and describes nerves as 'branches' that split into smaller and smaller paths all over your body.

choices.text: The spinal cord is a hard bone, but the nerves are soft tissues.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage actually tells us the spinal cord is NOT a bone — it's soft nerve tissue, just like the nerves themselves.

choices.text: The spinal cord is a message sender, but the nerves are message receivers.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because both the spinal cord and nerves carry messages in both directions — the spinal cord passes along messages going to and from the brain.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: According to the article, what causes your brain to know that you touched a hot pan?

choices.text: Motor nerves send a quick reflex command to your arm.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because motor nerves carry orders from your brain to your muscles, not messages going up to your brain.

choices.text: The hard bones of the spine send a warning signal.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the spine bones are hard protective armor around the spinal cord, not signal senders.

choices.text: Sensory nerves send an electrical spark up the spinal cord.
choices.feedback: C is correct because sensory nerves carry electrical impulses from your fingers up through the spinal cord to tell your brain 'This is hot!'

choices.text: The brain connects directly to the skin on your fingers.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the article clearly says your brain does NOT connect directly to every part of your body — it uses the nervous system instead.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: Which sequence of events best supports the main idea that the nervous system is a fast messenger?

choices.text: The sensory nerves message the brain, and then the brain sends orders to the muscles.
choices.feedback: A is correct because it shows the right order — sensory nerves bring news to the brain fast, and then motor nerves quickly carry the brain's orders to the muscles, which is exactly how the nervous system acts as a speedy messenger.

choices.text: The brain makes an electrical spark, and then the spine turns into a hard bone.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the spine is already made of hard bones that protect the spinal cord — it doesn't change or 'turn into' a hard bone.

choices.text: The muscles pull away from danger, and then the sensory nerves talk to the brain.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because it has the steps in the wrong order — sensory nerves send the message to the brain first, and the muscles only pull away after the brain sends back a command.

choices.text: The spinal cord grows long branches, and then the branches reach inside the human body.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because it describes how nerves are shaped and spread through the body, but it doesn't show how the nervous system sends messages quickly.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: Based on the article, how are the brain and the spinal cord similar?

choices.text: Both are protected by small bones called vertebrae.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because vertebrae protect the spinal cord, but the brain is protected by your skull, not vertebrae.

choices.text: Both can send instructions to your muscles to move.
choices.feedback: This is correct because both the brain and spinal cord work together to send movement commands through motor nerves to your muscles.

choices.text: Both are made of thin branches that reach everywhere.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the article says nerves are the thin branches that reach everywhere in your body, not the brain or spinal cord.

choices.text: Both travel through the body at very high speeds.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because it is the electrical impulses (signals) that travel at high speeds, not the brain or spinal cord themselves.

assessments.title: See, Hear, Taste, Smell, Touch: Your Five Senses

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Human Body: The Nervous System

Your Body's Command Center

You pull your hand off a hot stove before you even think about it. In that split second, a message raced from your fingertips up through your arm, into your spine, and straight to your brain — then a command shot back down telling your muscles to move. All of that happened faster than a blink.

That lightning-fast message traveled through your nervous system — the network of your brain, spinal cord, and nerves that controls everything your body does. Think of it like a giant web of telephone wires running through your whole body. Your brain sits inside your skull, protected by bone. It is the boss. Your spinal cord, a thick bundle of nerves inside your backbone, acts like a superhighway connecting your brain to the rest of you. And billions of nerves branch out from the spinal cord to every finger, toe, and organ.

These nerves carry tiny electrical messages called signals. Your senses collect information, nerves carry it as signals to your brain, and your brain decides what to do. This all happens in less than a second.

That lightning-fast message traveled through your nervous system — the network of your brain, spinal cord, and nerves that controls everything your body does. Think of it like a giant web of telephone wires running through your whole body. Your brain sits inside your skull, protected by bone. It is the boss. Your spinal cord, a thick bundle of nerves inside your backbone, acts like a superhighway connecting your brain to the rest of you. And billions of nerves branch out from the spinal cord to every finger, toe, and organ.
These nerves carry tiny electrical messages called signals. Your senses collect information, nerves carry it as signals to your brain, and your brain decides what to do. This all happens in less than a second.

items.prompt: According to the text, what is the difference between what your senses do and what your brain does?

choices.text: Your senses carry signals through your spine, but your brain sends signals to your fingers.
choices.feedback: A is wrong because the passage says nerves carry signals through your spine, not your senses.

choices.text: Your senses collect information from around you, but your brain decides what to do.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage clearly states that senses collect information and the brain decides what to do.

choices.text: Your senses decide what your body should do, but your brain collects the information.
choices.feedback: C has it completely backwards — the passage says the brain decides what to do, not the senses.

choices.text: Your senses store electrical messages inside your body, but your brain sends them back out.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the passage never says senses store electrical messages — that idea isn't in the text at all.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Five Senses — and Maybe More

You know the big five: sight, hearing, taste, smell, and touch. But here is a surprise — scientists believe you actually have more than five senses, like balance from your inner ear and knowing where your arms are even with your eyes closed.

Still, the famous five do immense work. Your eyes detect light and send signals through the optic nerve — a cable of nerve fibers connecting each eye to the brain — so your brain can build the images you see. You might think your eyes "see" all by themselves. Actually, your eyes just collect light. Your brain does the real seeing, turning those signals into colors, shapes, and motion.

Your ears pick up vibrations, which are tiny back-and-forth movements in the air, like the shaking of a drumhead. Those vibrations travel deep into your ear and become signals your brain reads as sound — music, voices, a dog barking down the street.

Your eyes detect light and send signals through the optic nerve — a cable of nerve fibers connecting each eye to the brain — so your brain can build the images you see. You might think your eyes "see" all by themselves. Actually, your eyes just collect light. Your brain does the real seeing, turning those signals into colors, shapes, and motion.
Your ears pick up vibrations, which are tiny back-and-forth movements in the air, like the shaking of a drumhead. Those vibrations travel deep into your ear and become signals your brain reads as sound — music, voices, a dog barking down the street.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, what causes your brain to build the images you see?

choices.text: The eyes sending signals through a nerve.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage says eyes send signals through the optic nerve to the brain, and the brain uses those signals to build the images you see.

choices.text: The brain shining light into the eyes.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the brain never shines light — the eyes are the ones that detect light from the outside world.

choices.text: The ears catching vibrations in the air.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because vibrations caught by the ears help you hear sounds, not see images.

choices.text: The eyes seeing pictures by themselves.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the passage clearly says your eyes just collect light and your brain does the real seeing, so the eyes cannot see pictures all by themselves.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Taste, Smell, and Touch

Your tongue is covered with about 10,000 tiny bumps called taste buds. They detect five basic tastes: sweet, sour, salty, bitter, and umami — a savory flavor found in cheese and tomatoes. You might have seen diagrams showing that sweet is only on the tip of your tongue and bitter is only in the back. That is not true! Taste buds for all five flavors are spread all over your tongue.

Special cells inside your nose detect tiny particles floating in the air and send signals to your brain. That is why food tastes bland when your nose is stuffed up. Your sense of smell also warns you about danger, like smoke from a fire.

Touch covers your entire body. Your skin holds millions of nerve endings — the tiny tips of nerves that sit just below the surface, like sensors buried under a blanket. They detect pressure, temperature, pain, and texture. Your fingertips and lips are packed with nerve endings, making them extra sensitive. Your back has fewer, so it notices less.

Special cells inside your nose detect tiny particles floating in the air and send signals to your brain. That is why food tastes bland when your nose is stuffed up. Your sense of smell also warns you about danger, like smoke from a fire.

They detect pressure, temperature, pain, and texture. Your fingertips and lips are packed with nerve endings, making them extra sensitive. Your back has fewer, so it notices less.

items.prompt: According to the text, what is the correct sequence of steps for how your sense of smell works?

choices.text: Particles float in the air, cells detect them, and signals go to the brain.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage says particles float in the air, special cells detect them, and then signals go to the brain — exactly that order!

choices.text: Signals go to the brain, particles float in the air, and cells detect them.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the brain doesn't act first; it receives the signal last, after the cells in your nose detect the particles.

choices.text: A nose gets stuffed up, special cells detect it, and food tastes very bland.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because a stuffed-up nose is just an example of smell not working well, not a description of the steps of how smelling works.

choices.text: The brain senses danger, smoke floats in the air, and cells warn your body.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because it reverses the process — your nose cells detect danger like smoke first, and then the signal goes to your brain, not the other way around.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Everything Working Together

Five senses fire off signals at the same time, and your nervous system rushes every message to your brain. Your brain pieces it all together into one experience: apple.

Right now, your nervous system is reading these words, keeping your heart beating, and balancing you in your chair — all at once, all without you asking it to.
stimulus.content_text:
Everything Working Together
Five senses fire off signals at the same time, and your nervous system rushes every message to your brain. Your brain pieces it all together into one experience: apple.
Right now, your nervous system is reading these words, keeping your heart beating, and balancing you in your chair — all at once, all without you asking it to.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, how is the way your brain handles the apple experience similar to the way your nervous system works while you read?

choices.text: Both involve only one sense at a time
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage clearly says all five senses fire signals at the same time, not one at a time.

choices.text: Both involve doing many jobs at once
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage shows both the brain (handling five senses at once) and the nervous system (reading, heartbeating, and balancing at once) do many jobs at the same time.

choices.text: Both require you to ask them to work
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage says your nervous system works 'without you asking it to,' so you don't have to ask either one to work.

choices.text: Both send signals only to your eyes
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because signals come from all five senses and go to the brain, not just to your eyes.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: How is your sense of touch on your fingertips different from your sense of touch on your back?

choices.text: Your fingertips feel only pressure, but your back feels only pain.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says nerve endings detect pressure, temperature, pain, AND texture — both your fingertips and back can feel all of these things.

choices.text: Your fingertips have more sensors, so they notice more.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage says your fingertips are packed with nerve endings making them extra sensitive, while your back has fewer nerve endings and notices less.

choices.text: Your back sends signals faster than your fingertips do.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never mentions anything about your back sending signals faster than your fingertips.

choices.text: Your back collects light, but your fingertips do not.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because collecting light is the job of your eyes, not your back or fingertips.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: Based on the article, what causes your brain to feel a hot temperature on your fingertips?

choices.text: The brain sends a fast command down the spinal cord to move the muscles.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because sending a command down the spinal cord is what the brain does after it already received the heat signal — it doesn't explain how the brain felt the heat.

choices.text: Nerve endings detect the heat and send signals up the spinal cord.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the article says nerve endings in your skin detect temperature and send signals to your brain, which is exactly how your brain knows your fingertips are touching something hot.

choices.text: The optic nerve collects light from the hot object and builds an image.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the optic nerve connects your eyes to your brain to help you see light and images, not to feel heat on your skin.

choices.text: The tiny bumps on the skin vibrate back and forth to carry the message.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because vibrations are how your ears detect sound, not how your skin senses temperature.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: If you wanted to find the steps that happen in order when you touch a hot stove, which heading would help you locate that information?

choices.text: Five Senses — and Maybe More
choices.feedback: This section is about your five senses like sight and hearing, not about the steps that happen when you touch a hot stove.

choices.text: Your Body's Command Center
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because this section describes each step in order — the message traveling from your fingers, through your spine, to your brain, and back to your muscles.

choices.text: Taste, Smell, and Touch
choices.feedback: This section explains taste buds and your sense of smell, so it does not have the hot stove steps.

choices.text: Everything Working Together
choices.feedback: This section talks about all your senses working together at the same time, not the specific steps of what happens when you touch something hot.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: The main idea of the text is that your senses collect information for your nervous system. How do the details about your eyes and your nose compare to support this main idea?

choices.text: They both show how the body gathers light to build images.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because only the eyes detect light — the nose detects tiny particles floating in the air, not light.

choices.text: They both show how these parts send signals to your brain.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage explains that both the eyes and the nose send signals to the brain, which supports the main idea that senses collect information for the nervous system.

choices.text: They both show how the spinal cord makes the muscles move.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the details about eyes and nose have nothing to do with the spinal cord making muscles move.

choices.text: They both show how the skin warns you about a hot surface.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because warning about a hot surface is about the sense of touch and skin nerve endings, not the eyes or nose.

assessments.title: Don't Think, Just React: How Reflexes Work

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Reflexes: Your Body's Lightning-Fast Safety System

A Hand Pulls Away

Your finger brushes a hot pan on the stove. Before you even know what happened, your hand flies back. You didn't decide to move it. You didn't think about moving it. Your body did it all on its own — in less than a blink.

That lightning-fast movement is called a reflex — an automatic action your body takes without waiting for your brain to give the order. Reflexes are part of your nervous system, the network of signals that lets every part of your body talk to every other part.
stimulus.content_text:
Reflexes: Your Body's Lightning-Fast Safety SystemA Hand Pulls Away
Your finger brushes a hot pan on the stove. Before you even know what happened, your hand flies back. You didn't decide to move it. You didn't think about moving it. Your body did it all on its own — in less than a blink.
That lightning-fast movement is called a reflex — an automatic action your body takes without waiting for your brain to give the order. Reflexes are part of your nervous system, the network of signals that lets every part of your body talk to every other part.

items.prompt: According to the text, how is a reflex different from a regular decision to move?

choices.text: A reflex happens without your brain giving the order
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage directly says a reflex is an automatic action that happens 'without waiting for your brain to give the order.'

choices.text: A reflex happens after your brain gives the order
choices.feedback: B is wrong because it says the brain gives the order first, but the passage says a reflex happens WITHOUT the brain's order.

choices.text: A reflex happens once your brain sends a slow signal
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the passage describes reflexes as lightning-fast, not slow signals.

choices.text: A reflex happens when your brain carefully thinks it over
choices.feedback: D is wrong because reflexes happen automatically and instantly — there is no careful thinking involved at all.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
The Normal Route vs. the Shortcut

Most of the time, your body sends messages along a familiar path. Your senses notice something. They send a signal through special cells called neurons — tiny message carriers that work like links in a chain, passing signals from one to the next. The signal travels up to your brain. Your brain decides what to do. Then it sends a command back down to your muscles.

That process works well for everyday actions. But it takes time. When danger is close, even a fraction — a tiny piece — of a second matters. Your body needs a faster plan.

Here's the surprising part: during a reflex, the signal never reaches your brain at all. Instead, it takes a shortcut through your spinal cord, the long bundle of nerves that runs down the inside of your backbone. Picture a thick cable hidden inside the bones of your spine. That cable can make decisions on its own.

This shortcut has a name: a reflex arc. The signal races from your hand to your spinal cord. The spinal cord instantly sends a command back to your arm muscles: pull away! Your hand moves before your brain even gets the news. A moment later, you feel the pain. That's your brain finally receiving the message — after your body already protected you.

The Normal Route vs. the Shortcut

They send a signal through special cells called neurons — tiny message carriers that work like links in a chain, passing signals from one to the next. The signal travels up to your brain. Your brain decides what to do. Then it sends a command back down to your muscles.
That process works well for everyday actions. But it takes time. When danger is close, even a fraction — a tiny piece — of a second matters. Your body needs a faster plan.
Here's the surprising part: during a reflex, the signal never reaches your brain at all. Instead, it takes a shortcut through your spinal cord, the long bundle of nerves that runs down the inside of your backbone. Picture a thick cable hidden inside the bones of your spine. That cable can make decisions on its own.
This shortcut has a name: a reflex arc. The signal races from your hand to your spinal cord. The spinal cord instantly sends a command back to your arm muscles: pull away! Your hand moves before your brain even gets the news. A moment later, you feel the pain. That's your brain finally receiving the message — after your body already protected you.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, why does a reflex signal take a shortcut through the spinal cord?

choices.text: Because the body needs a faster plan to escape danger.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage directly says the body needs a faster plan when danger is close, and the spinal cord shortcut is exactly that faster plan.

choices.text: Because the brain tells the spinal cord to move the arm.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the passage says the signal never reaches the brain during a reflex — the spinal cord makes the decision all by itself.

choices.text: Because the spinal cord is hidden safely inside the bones.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the spinal cord being hidden inside the bones just describes its location, not the reason the shortcut is used.

choices.text: Because the body uses this fast path for everyday actions.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage says the normal route through the brain is used for everyday actions, while the shortcut is only for quick danger responses.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Reflexes You Already Know

You use reflexes more often than you realize. When something flies toward your face, you blink. When you step on something sharp, your foot lifts before you can think. When a bright light shines in your eyes, your pupils — the dark circles in the center of your eyes — get smaller right away to block the extra light.

A true reflex happens without any choice at all. That's different from something like catching a ball, which is something you choose to do — your brain decides to reach out and grab it. But with a reflex, there is no deciding. Your body simply acts.

Reflexes You Already Know

When a bright light shines in your eyes, your pupils — the dark circles in the center of your eyes — get smaller right away to block the extra light.
A true reflex happens without any choice at all. That's different from something like catching a ball, which is something you choose to do — your brain decides to reach out and grab it. But with a reflex, there is no deciding. Your body simply acts.

items.prompt: According to the text, what is the relationship between thinking and lifting your foot when you step on something sharp?

choices.text: Your foot lifts up before you are able to think.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage says your foot lifts 'before you can think,' meaning the movement happens faster than thinking can.

choices.text: You think about the sharp item before you lift it.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the passage tells us your foot lifts before you think, not after you think about the sharp item.

choices.text: Your brain decides to move before your foot lifts.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage says reflexes happen without any deciding, so your brain does not choose to move your foot.

choices.text: Your foot waits for a choice before it lifts up.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because reflexes never wait for a choice — your body just acts on its own right away.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Tap, Kick, Healthy

Doctors use reflexes to check if your nervous system is working well. Maybe a doctor has tapped just below your kneecap with a small rubber hammer. When they do, your lower leg kicks forward on its own. This is called the knee-jerk reflex. If your leg kicks, the nerves in that part of your body are healthy. If it doesn't kick, the doctor knows to look more closely.

That one small tap tells a big story about the signals traveling through your body.

Tap, Kick, Healthy

When they do, your lower leg kicks forward on its own. This is called the knee-jerk reflex. If your leg kicks, the nerves in that part of your body are healthy. If it doesn't kick, the doctor knows to look more closely.
That one small tap tells a big story about the signals traveling through your body.

items.prompt: How does the text compare a leg that kicks to a leg that stays still?

choices.text: A kicking leg shows healthy nerves, while a still leg needs a closer check.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage directly says a kicking leg means healthy nerves, and a still leg means the doctor needs to look more closely.

choices.text: A kicking leg needs a small hammer, while a still leg needs a big hammer.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the passage only mentions one small rubber hammer used for both situations — it never talks about a big hammer.

choices.text: A kicking leg sends fast signals, while a still leg sends very slow signals.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the passage never compares fast and slow signals — it only talks about whether the leg kicks or stays still.

choices.text: A kicking leg needs a closer check, while a still leg shows healthy nerves.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because it has the meaning flipped — the passage says a kicking leg shows healthy nerves, not a still leg.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Faster Than Thought

Right now, your nervous system is at work — carrying signals, watching for danger, ready to protect you in an instant. Your brain handles most of the job. But your spinal cord stands guard too, ready to act before your brain even knows there's trouble.

No thought. No planning. Just your body keeping you safe at a speed your brain can't match.
stimulus.content_text:
Faster Than Thought
Right now, your nervous system is at work — carrying signals, watching for danger, ready to protect you in an instant. Your brain handles most of the job. But your spinal cord stands guard too, ready to act before your brain even knows there's trouble.
No thought. No planning. Just your body keeping you safe at a speed your brain can't match.

items.prompt: According to the text, what does the author mean by saying the spinal cord 'stands guard'?

choices.text: It sends signals only when the brain tells it to
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage says the spinal cord acts before the brain even knows there's trouble, so it doesn't wait for the brain's signal.

choices.text: It stays ready to protect you from danger
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage says the spinal cord is 'ready to act' before the brain knows there's danger, meaning it stays ready to protect you.

choices.text: It controls all the thinking your body does
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage says the brain handles most of the thinking, not the spinal cord.

choices.text: It replaces the brain during sleep at night
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage never mentions sleep or the spinal cord taking over at night.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: Based on the article, how is the "normal route" for messages different from a reflex like blinking?

choices.text: The normal route lets your brain make a choice, but blinking happens without deciding.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the article says the normal route lets your brain decide what to do, while a reflex like blinking happens automatically with no choice involved.

choices.text: The normal route happens without any thought, but blinking requires your brain to plan.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because it has things backwards — reflexes are the ones that happen without thought, and the normal route is the one that involves your brain planning and deciding.

choices.text: The normal route uses the spinal cord shortcut, but blinking waits for the brain to act.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because it's also backwards — the article clearly says reflexes use the spinal cord shortcut, while the normal route is the one that travels up to the brain.

choices.text: The normal route protects you from hot objects, but blinking blocks out the bright light.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because it only talks about examples of each, not the real difference, which is whether or not your brain makes a decision.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: Based on the article, why does the "knee-jerk reflex" happen without you deciding to move your leg?

choices.text: The brain sends a lightning-fast command to the muscles.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the article tells us the brain does NOT send the command during a reflex — the spinal cord handles it instead.

choices.text: The signal takes a shortcut through the spinal cord.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the article explains that reflex signals take a shortcut through the spinal cord, which makes decisions on its own without waiting for the brain.

choices.text: The leg reacts to the sharp pain from the hammer.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the knee-jerk reflex is triggered by a tap, not sharp pain, and it's the spinal cord — not pain — that causes the kick.

choices.text: The muscles have practiced the movement many times.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because reflexes are automatic actions your body is born with, not movements your muscles learn through practice.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: Which sentence tells the main idea of the article in the right order?

choices.text: Danger happens, the brain feels pain first, and then the body moves.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage tells us the body moves BEFORE the brain feels pain, not after — so the order is backwards.

choices.text: Danger happens, the body reacts fast, and then the brain gets the message.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage clearly shows that danger triggers a fast body reaction through the spinal cord, and the brain only gets the message afterward.

choices.text: Danger happens, the muscles wait, and then the spinal cord asks the brain.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the muscles do NOT wait — the spinal cord sends the command right away without ever asking the brain.

choices.text: Danger happens, the doctor checks the nerves, and then the leg kicks.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the knee-jerk reflex is just one example from one section, not the main idea the whole article is about.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: How are pulling your hand from a hot pan and blinking when something flies at your face alike?

choices.text: Both actions are choices your brain makes quickly.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because reflexes are not choices — your brain doesn't decide anything during a reflex; your body just acts on its own.

choices.text: Both actions happen without you deciding to do them.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage says reflexes happen without any choice at all, and both pulling your hand away and blinking are listed as examples of reflexes.

choices.text: Both actions send signals only to your brain.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage explains that reflex signals never reach the brain — they take a shortcut through the spinal cord instead.

choices.text: Both actions are things a doctor tests with a hammer.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the hammer test is only used for the knee-jerk reflex, not for pulling your hand away from a hot pan or blinking.
