assessments.title: Taking Care of Your Brain: Sleep, Safety, and Health

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Taking Care of Your Nervous System

A Brain That Never Stops

Right now, as your eyes move across these words, tiny electrical signals are racing from your eyes to your brain at over 250 miles per hour. It does this every second of every day, even when you are sound asleep.

Your brain is the command center of your nervous system — the network of your brain, spinal cord, and nerves that controls everything your body does. You might think the brain works alone, but it does not. Nerves spread through your whole body like wires in a house. Touch a hot pan, and nerves in your fingers fire a message to your brain and back in less than a second. Taking care of your brain means taking care of this entire system.
stimulus.content_text:
Taking Care of Your Nervous SystemA Brain That Never Stops
Right now, as your eyes move across these words, tiny electrical signals are racing from your eyes to your brain at over 250 miles per hour. It does this every second of every day, even when you are sound asleep.
Your brain is the command center of your nervous system — the network of your brain, spinal cord, and nerves that controls everything your body does. You might think the brain works alone, but it does not. Nerves spread through your whole body like wires in a house. Touch a hot pan, and nerves in your fingers fire a message to your brain and back in less than a second. Taking care of your brain means taking care of this entire system.

items.prompt: According to the text, which statement shows a difference between what people might think about the brain and what it actually does?

choices.text: People might think the brain works alone, but it actually works with the nervous system.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage directly says people might think the brain works alone, but it actually works with the nervous system, nerves, and spinal cord.

choices.text: People might think the brain works all day, but it actually turns off when you fall asleep.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the passage tells us the brain keeps working every second, even when you are asleep — it never turns off.

choices.text: People might think the brain sends fast signals, but it actually takes a long time to respond.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the passage says signals race at over 250 miles per hour, which is very fast, not slow.

choices.text: People might think the brain controls the body, but it actually only works with your fingers.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the passage says the brain controls everything your body does, not just your fingers.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Sleep: Your Brain's Cleanup Crew

While your body lies still, your brain gets to work. It sorts through everything you learned that day. Children need about 9 to 12 hours of sleep each night to give the brain enough time for all this work.

It becomes harder to pay attention, remember facts, or make good choices. Think of sleep as a cleaning crew that comes in after a big party. Skip the cleanup, and the mess just piles up.
stimulus.content_text:
Sleep: Your Brain's Cleanup Crew
While your body lies still, your brain gets to work. It sorts through everything you learned that day. Children need about 9 to 12 hours of sleep each night to give the brain enough time for all this work.
It becomes harder to pay attention, remember facts, or make good choices. Think of sleep as a cleaning crew that comes in after a big party. Skip the cleanup, and the mess just piles up.

items.prompt: Based on the text, why do children need 9 to 12 hours of sleep each night?

choices.text: To let the body rest and lie completely still.
choices.feedback: The body lying still is mentioned in the passage, but it's not the reason children need 9 to 12 hours — that's about giving the brain enough time to do its work.

choices.text: To sort through what was learned that day.
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage directly says children need 9 to 12 hours so the brain has enough time to sort through everything learned that day.

choices.text: To clean up the house after a really big party.
choices.feedback: The 'cleaning crew after a party' is a comparison the author uses to explain sleep, not a real reason children need those hours of sleep.

choices.text: To forget the facts that were taught earlier.
choices.feedback: This is the opposite of what the passage says — sleep helps your brain remember facts, not forget them.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Protecting the Soft Brain Inside Hard Bone

Your skull is a helmet made of bone. It does a good job guarding your brain. But here is the surprising part — your brain is soft. It floats inside a thin layer of fluid. A hard hit to the head can slam your brain against the inside of your skull. That can cause a concussion, a brain injury that happens when the brain bumps or twists inside the skull.

That is why wearing a helmet matters so much. When you ride a bike, skateboard, or play contact sports, a helmet absorbs the force of a hit — called an impact, like a pillow catching a falling egg. Your brain does not have to take the blow. Always buckle your helmet before you ride.
stimulus.content_text:
Protecting the Soft Brain Inside Hard Bone
Your skull is a helmet made of bone. It does a good job guarding your brain. But here is the surprising part — your brain is soft. It floats inside a thin layer of fluid. A hard hit to the head can slam your brain against the inside of your skull. That can cause a concussion, a brain injury that happens when the brain bumps or twists inside the skull.
That is why wearing a helmet matters so much. When you ride a bike, skateboard, or play contact sports, a helmet absorbs the force of a hit — called an impact, like a pillow catching a falling egg. Your brain does not have to take the blow. Always buckle your helmet before you ride.

items.prompt: According to the text, what happens first when a person gets a hard hit to the head without a helmet?

choices.text: The brain gets a concussion injury right away
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because a concussion is what happens after the brain slams against the skull, not the very first step.

choices.text: The brain slams against the inside of the skull
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage clearly says a hard hit slams the brain against the inside of the skull first, and that causes the concussion.

choices.text: The brain sends a signal to the rest of the body
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage never mentions the brain sending signals to the body at all.

choices.text: The brain absorbs the force like a soft pillow
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage says the helmet absorbs force like a pillow — the brain does not do this.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Moving Your Body Feeds Your Brain

When you run, jump, swim, or play tag, your heart pumps faster. It pushes extra blood up to your brain. That blood carries oxygen and nutrients — tiny building blocks in food that keep your body strong, like fuel in a gas tank. Your brain cells use that oxygen and those nutrients to send signals faster and think more clearly. Studies show that kids who exercise regularly do better in school and feel happier. Playing outside is not just fun. It is a meal for your brain.
stimulus.content_text:
Moving Your Body Feeds Your Brain
When you run, jump, swim, or play tag, your heart pumps faster. It pushes extra blood up to your brain. That blood carries oxygen and nutrients — tiny building blocks in food that keep your body strong, like fuel in a gas tank. Your brain cells use that oxygen and those nutrients to send signals faster and think more clearly. Studies show that kids who exercise regularly do better in school and feel happier. Playing outside is not just fun. It is a meal for your brain.

items.prompt: In the passage, the author compares nutrients to 'fuel in a gas tank.' What does this comparison help the reader understand about nutrients?

choices.text: They are liquids that flow through your body
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the 'fuel in a gas tank' comparison is about giving energy, not about nutrients being a liquid that flows.

choices.text: They give your body energy to keep working
choices.feedback: B is correct because fuel gives a car energy to keep running, and the passage uses that same idea to show nutrients give your body energy to stay strong and work well.

choices.text: They are found only inside gas tanks nearby
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because nutrients come from the food you eat, not from real gas tanks — the gas tank is just used as a comparison to explain what nutrients do.

choices.text: They make your heart pump faster than normal
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage says your heart pumps faster when you exercise, not because of nutrients specifically.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
The Right Fuel for Brain Cells

What you eat changes how well your brain works. Fish and nuts contain omega-3 fatty acids, special healthy fats that help build and protect brain cells — like oil that keeps a machine running smoothly. Fruits, vegetables, and whole grains give your brain steady energy throughout the day. Water matters too. Even mild dehydration — when your body does not have enough water — can make it harder to concentrate and remember things. A few extra sips of water can sharpen your thinking.
stimulus.content_text:
The Right Fuel for Brain Cells
What you eat changes how well your brain works. Fish and nuts contain omega-3 fatty acids, special healthy fats that help build and protect brain cells — like oil that keeps a machine running smoothly. Fruits, vegetables, and whole grains give your brain steady energy throughout the day. Water matters too. Even mild dehydration — when your body does not have enough water — can make it harder to concentrate and remember things. A few extra sips of water can sharpen your thinking.

items.prompt: According to the text, what does the word 'dehydration' mean?

choices.text: when your body has too much water
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because dehydration is the opposite — it means your body does NOT have enough water, not too much.

choices.text: when your body does not have enough water
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly defines dehydration as 'when your body does not have enough water,' right after the word itself.

choices.text: when your body needs more food to eat
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because dehydration is about not having enough water, not about needing more food.

choices.text: when your body is ready to fall asleep
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage never says dehydration has anything to do with feeling sleepy or ready to fall asleep.

items.title: Section 6 - Guiding Question 5

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
The System That Makes You You

Your nervous system lets you feel a kitten's fur, laugh at a joke, catch a ball, and dream wild dreams. Every thought and every move travels along that network of nerves, spinal cord, and brain. When you sleep well, wear your helmet, exercise, and eat good food, you are powering the entire system that makes you who you are.
stimulus.content_text:
The System That Makes You You
Your nervous system lets you feel a kitten's fur, laugh at a joke, catch a ball, and dream wild dreams. Every thought and every move travels along that network of nerves, spinal cord, and brain. When you sleep well, wear your helmet, exercise, and eat good food, you are powering the entire system that makes you who you are.

items.prompt: The author states that the nervous system controls your senses and movements. Which detail from the section gives evidence for this idea?

choices.text: Feeling a kitten's fur and catching a ball
choices.feedback: This is correct because 'feeling a kitten's fur' is an example of using your senses, and 'catching a ball' is an example of movement — both are directly controlled by the nervous system.

choices.text: Traveling along the nerves and the spinal cord
choices.feedback: This tells us the path signals travel inside your body, but it doesn't give an example of a sense or a movement.

choices.text: Wearing a safety helmet and sleeping very well
choices.feedback: Wearing a helmet and sleeping well are ways to protect your nervous system, not examples of it controlling your senses or movements.

choices.text: Powering the system that makes you who you are
choices.feedback: This is a big-picture idea about the nervous system, but it doesn't point to a specific sense or movement as evidence.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: Which two headings help you find the difference between what happens to the brain when the body is moving and when it is still?

choices.text: "A Brain That Never Stops" and "Sleep: Your Brain's Cleanup Crew"
choices.feedback: A is wrong because 'A Brain That Never Stops' talks about how your brain works all the time, not about what happens when your body is moving versus still.

choices.text: "Sleep: Your Brain's Cleanup Crew" and "Moving Your Body Feeds Your Brain"
choices.feedback: B is correct because 'Sleep: Your Brain's Cleanup Crew' explains what the brain does when your body is still, and 'Moving Your Body Feeds Your Brain' explains what happens when your body is active — showing the difference between the two.

choices.text: "Moving Your Body Feeds Your Brain" and "The Right Fuel for Brain Cells"
choices.feedback: C is wrong because 'The Right Fuel for Brain Cells' is about the food you eat, not about whether your body is moving or resting.

choices.text: "Protecting the Soft Brain Inside Hard Bone" and "The System That Makes You You"
choices.feedback: D is wrong because 'Protecting the Soft Brain Inside Hard Bone' is about helmets and injuries, and 'The System That Makes You You' is a summary — neither one compares moving and resting.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: Which choice best explains why the author tells kids to exercise and drink water?

choices.text: Because both help protect the brain from hard hits
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage says helmets protect your brain from hard hits — not exercise or drinking water.

choices.text: Because both help the brain think and work better
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage says exercise helps your brain 'think more clearly' and water helps you 'concentrate and remember things,' so both help your brain work better.

choices.text: Because both help the body get more hours of sleep
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage never connects exercise or drinking water to getting more hours of sleep.

choices.text: Because both help the skull grow stronger bones
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage does not mention exercise or water making your skull bones stronger.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: The main idea of the article is that taking care of your body powers your nervous system. Which sequence of key details best explains how eating right and exercising work together to support this main idea?

choices.text: First, you eat food with nutrients; next, exercise makes your heart pump them to your brain; last, your brain uses them to send signals.
choices.feedback: A is correct because it matches the passage's order: eat nutrients, exercise pumps them to your brain, then your brain uses them to think and send signals.

choices.text: First, your brain uses nutrients to send signals; next, exercise makes your heart pump them to your brain; last, you eat food with nutrients.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the steps are backwards — you need to eat the nutrients first before your brain can use them.

choices.text: First, you eat food with oxygen; next, your nerves make your spinal cord pump it to your brain; last, your skull uses it to build bone.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because food gives your brain nutrients, not oxygen, and your skull guards your brain but does not pump anything anywhere.

choices.text: First, you eat food like a cleanup crew; next, exercise makes your body rest so the brain works; last, your brain stores the food.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because it mixes up ideas — the 'cleanup crew' is about sleep, not eating, and exercise helps your brain think, not rest.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: According to the article, how is skipping sleep similar to having mild dehydration?

choices.text: They both make it harder to pay attention and remember.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the article says both skipping sleep AND not drinking enough water make it harder to pay attention and remember things.

choices.text: They both cause the soft brain to hit the hard skull.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the brain bumping against the skull is caused by a hard hit to the head, not by missing sleep or being dehydrated.

choices.text: They both stop the heart from pumping blood and oxygen.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the article never says that skipping sleep or being dehydrated stops your heart from pumping blood and oxygen.

choices.text: They both leave a messy pile of trash behind them.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the 'messy pile' is only a metaphor for what happens when you skip sleep — the article never connects that image to dehydration.

syllabus_metadata.title: Taking Care of Your Brain: Sleep, Safety, and Health

assessments.title: Faster Than Anything: How Light Travels

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Fastest Traveler in the Universe

A Race Nothing Can Win

Flip a light switch in a dark room. Before your finger even lifts off the switch, light has already filled every corner, bounced off every wall, and reached your eyes. It happened so fast you never saw it move. Light travels at about 186,000 miles per second. At that speed, a beam of light could circle the entire Earth more than seven times in one single second. No cheetah, no rocket ship, nothing moves that fast.
stimulus.content_text:
The Fastest Traveler in the UniverseA Race Nothing Can Win
Flip a light switch in a dark room. Before your finger even lifts off the switch, light has already filled every corner, bounced off every wall, and reached your eyes. It happened so fast you never saw it move. Light travels at about 186,000 miles per second. At that speed, a beam of light could circle the entire Earth more than seven times in one single second. No cheetah, no rocket ship, nothing moves that fast.

items.prompt: According to the section 'A Race Nothing Can Win,' what does the author contrast light with to show that nothing moves as fast?

choices.text: airplanes and race cars
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage never mentions airplanes or race cars.

choices.text: cheetahs and rocket ships
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage specifically says 'No cheetah, no rocket ship, nothing moves that fast' when describing light's speed.

choices.text: trains and submarines
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because trains and submarines are not mentioned anywhere in the passage.

choices.text: comets and shooting stars
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage does not talk about comets or shooting stars.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Straight Lines and Shadows

Light travels in straight lines called rays — think of them like invisible arrows shooting out from a lamp or the sun. You can test this yourself. Hold your hand in front of a flashlight. The light cannot bend around your hand, so a dark shape appears on the wall behind it. That dark shape is a shadow. Shadows match the outline of the object blocking the light. A bigger object blocks more rays, so it makes a bigger shadow.
stimulus.content_text:
Straight Lines and Shadows
Light travels in straight lines called rays — think of them like invisible arrows shooting out from a lamp or the sun. You can test this yourself. Hold your hand in front of a flashlight. The light cannot bend around your hand, so a dark shape appears on the wall behind it. That dark shape is a shadow. Shadows match the outline of the object blocking the light. A bigger object blocks more rays, so it makes a bigger shadow.

items.prompt: Which detail from "Straight Lines and Shadows" best supports the main idea that light travels in straight lines?

choices.text: A dark shape appears because light bends around your hand.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage clearly says light cannot bend around your hand — that is the whole reason a shadow forms.

choices.text: A shadow appears because your hand blocks the light.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage explains that light travels in straight lines and cannot bend around your hand, so your hand blocks the rays and creates a shadow.

choices.text: A shadow appears because the wall sends light back.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the wall does not send light back — the shadow forms because light rays are blocked before they even reach that spot on the wall.

choices.text: A dark shape appears because your hand makes new light.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because your hand does not create new light; it simply gets in the way of the light rays coming from the flashlight.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Where Light Comes From

Objects that make their own light are called luminous objects — they glow on their own, like a candle flame or a firefly on a summer night. The sun is the biggest luminous object in our lives. Even though it sits about 93 million miles away, its light takes only about 8 minutes and 20 seconds to reach Earth. Light bulbs, campfires, and even some deep-sea fish are luminous too.

Most things around you are not luminous at all. Your desk, your shoes, and this page do not glow. So how do you see them? Here is the surprising part: your eyes do not send out beams to find objects. Instead, light from a luminous source bounces off those objects and travels into your eyes. Your eyes catch the light — they do not shoot it out.
stimulus.content_text:
Where Light Comes From
Objects that make their own light are called luminous objects — they glow on their own, like a candle flame or a firefly on a summer night. The sun is the biggest luminous object in our lives. Even though it sits about 93 million miles away, its light takes only about 8 minutes and 20 seconds to reach Earth. Light bulbs, campfires, and even some deep-sea fish are luminous too.
Most things around you are not luminous at all. Your desk, your shoes, and this page do not glow. So how do you see them? Here is the surprising part: your eyes do not send out beams to find objects. Instead, light from a luminous source bounces off those objects and travels into your eyes. Your eyes catch the light — they do not shoot it out.

items.prompt: According to the passage, what happens right after light from a luminous source bounces off an object?

choices.text: The object begins to glow on its own
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage says objects like your desk and shoes do not glow — they are not luminous.

choices.text: The light travels into your eyes
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage says light 'bounces off those objects and travels into your eyes' — that is exactly what happens next!

choices.text: Your eyes send out beams to find it
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage clearly states 'your eyes do not send out beams to find objects' — it is the opposite of what really happens.

choices.text: The object becomes a luminous source
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage never says bouncing light turns an object into a luminous source — only objects that make their own light are luminous.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
How Light Travels

Light is a form of energy. It moves in waves, like ripples spreading across a pond after you toss in a stone. But light waves are special. Pond ripples need water. Sound waves need air. Light waves need nothing at all. They can travel through the emptiness of space, where there is no air. That empty space is called a vacuum — a place completely empty of matter, like the huge stretches between the sun and Earth.

Light moves fastest through a vacuum. It slows down a little in air. It slows down even more in water or glass. You can see this happen. Put a straw in a glass of water and look from the side. The straw looks bent or broken where it enters the water. It is not really broken. The light slowed down as it passed from air into water, and that change in speed bent the rays heading to your eyes.
stimulus.content_text:
How Light Travels
Light is a form of energy. It moves in waves, like ripples spreading across a pond after you toss in a stone. But light waves are special. Pond ripples need water. Sound waves need air. Light waves need nothing at all. They can travel through the emptiness of space, where there is no air. That empty space is called a vacuum — a place completely empty of matter, like the huge stretches between the sun and Earth.
Light moves fastest through a vacuum. It slows down a little in air. It slows down even more in water or glass. You can see this happen. Put a straw in a glass of water and look from the side. The straw looks bent or broken where it enters the water. It is not really broken. The light slowed down as it passed from air into water, and that change in speed bent the rays heading to your eyes.

items.prompt: Read this sentence from the text: 'That empty space is called a vacuum — a place completely empty of matter, like the huge stretches between the sun and Earth.' To help explain the word 'vacuum,' how does the author show that a vacuum and the space between the sun and Earth are alike?

choices.text: They both are completely empty of any matter.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage directly says a vacuum is 'completely empty of matter,' and the space between the sun and Earth is given as an example of that same emptiness.

choices.text: They both slow down light that passes through.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the passage says light moves fastest through a vacuum — it does not slow down there.

choices.text: They both need air to help energy move around.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage explains that light does NOT need air, which is exactly what makes a vacuum and space special.

choices.text: They both bend light rays heading to your eyes.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the bending of light happens when light travels from air into water, not in a vacuum or in space.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
A World Built on Light

Without light, there would be no colors, no shadows, and no rainbows. Plants could not grow because they use light to make their own food. Earth would be a frozen, dark world. Light gives us warmth and lets our eyes take in everything around us.

So the next time you see a shadow stretch across the sidewalk or a straw "break" inside a glass of water, you are watching light follow its rules — traveling in straight lines, bouncing off surfaces, and bending when it slows down. Light is not just fast. It is the reason you can see anything at all.

items.prompt: According to the text, how does the author define the 'rules' of light?

choices.text: Making colors, shadows, and rainbows
choices.feedback: Making colors, shadows, and rainbows are effects of light, not the 'rules' the author describes.

choices.text: Giving warmth and helping plants grow
choices.feedback: Giving warmth and helping plants grow are benefits of light, not the rules the author is talking about.

choices.text: Traveling straight, bouncing, and bending
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says light follows rules by 'traveling in straight lines, bouncing off surfaces, and bending when it slows down.'

choices.text: Stretching across sidewalks and breaking straws
choices.feedback: Stretching shadows and a straw looking broken are just real-life examples that show light's rules at work, not the rules themselves.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: To find the difference between light moving straight and light bending, which two headings should you read?

choices.text: "A Race Nothing Can Win" and "Where Light Comes From"
choices.feedback: These two sections talk about how fast light travels and how our eyes see objects — neither one explains light bending.

choices.text: "Straight Lines and Shadows" and "How Light Travels"
choices.feedback: This is correct! 'Straight Lines and Shadows' explains light moving in straight rays, and 'How Light Travels' explains how light bends when it slows down in water.

choices.text: "Where Light Comes From" and "A World Built on Light"
choices.feedback: These sections talk about luminous objects and why light is important to life, not about straight lines or bending.

choices.text: "A Race Nothing Can Win" and "Straight Lines and Shadows"
choices.feedback: 'A Race Nothing Can Win' only tells us how fast light is — it does not explain anything about light bending.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: Light is the fastest thing in the universe. Based on the article, what causes light from the sun to still take over eight minutes to reach Earth?

choices.text: The sun is millions of miles away from our planet.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the article says the sun is about 93 million miles away, and that giant distance is why it takes over 8 minutes for light to arrive, even at 186,000 miles per second.

choices.text: The light must pass through a completely empty space.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the article explains that light actually travels fastest through a vacuum (empty space), so passing through empty space would not slow it down or make the trip take longer.

choices.text: The light slows down when it hits the air on Earth.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because while the article does say light slows down a little in air, that tiny slowdown is not the reason the trip takes over eight minutes — the enormous distance of 93 million miles is the real cause.

choices.text: The sun's rays bounce off other objects in the sky.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the article never says sunlight bounces off other objects in the sky on its way to Earth — that idea is not supported anywhere in the passage.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: A main idea of the text is that light travels from a source and changes speed depending on what it passes through. Which sequence of details best supports this idea?

choices.text: Light leaves the sun, travels through a vacuum, and slows down in water.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage tells us light travels from the sun through the vacuum of space and slows down when it enters water — exactly the sequence described here.

choices.text: Light shoots from your eyes, hits a dark shadow, and travels to the sun.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the passage specifically says your eyes do NOT send out beams; instead, they catch light that bounces off objects.

choices.text: Light bends inside a glass, turns into a color, and makes a dark shadow.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because bending light doesn't turn into a color, and shadows are caused by blocked light rays, not by light bending in a glass.

choices.text: Light fills up an empty room, stops completely, and creates a dark space.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because light never stops completely — it only slows down — and shadows form when an object blocks light, not when light fills a space.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: Which statement best compares the sun and a desk in the article?

choices.text: Both make their own light and help your eyes see.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because a desk is not luminous — it does not make its own light at all.

choices.text: Both send out rays, but only the desk glows on its own.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because desks do not send out rays, and it is the sun, not the desk, that glows on its own.

choices.text: Both can be seen, but only the sun makes its own light.
choices.feedback: C is correct because the article says the sun is luminous and makes its own light, while a desk does not glow but can still be seen when light bounces off it.

choices.text: Both block light rays, but only the sun makes shadows.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the sun is a light source, not something that blocks light rays to make shadows.

syllabus_metadata.title: Faster Than Anything: How Light Travels

assessments.title: Bouncing Light: How Reflection Works

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Bounce That Lets You See

Every Single Night, the Moon Is Lying to You

Every single night, the Moon glows in the sky like a giant lamp. However, it does not make any light of its own. That bright glow you see is sunlight bouncing off the Moon's rocky surface and traveling all the way to your eyes. The Moon is really just a giant mirror hanging in space.

This bouncing of light is called reflection — what happens when light hits a surface and bounces back, the way a tennis ball bounces off a sidewalk.
stimulus.content_text:
The Bounce That Lets You SeeEvery Single Night, the Moon Is Lying to You
Every single night, the Moon glows in the sky like a giant lamp. However, it does not make any light of its own. That bright glow you see is sunlight bouncing off the Moon's rocky surface and traveling all the way to your eyes. The Moon is really just a giant mirror hanging in space.
This bouncing of light is called reflection — what happens when light hits a surface and bounces back, the way a tennis ball bounces off a sidewalk.

items.prompt: The author says the Moon glows like a giant lamp, but what does the text say it really is?

choices.text: A giant mirror hanging in space
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage uses those exact words — 'a giant mirror hanging in space' — to explain what the Moon really is.

choices.text: A giant tennis ball bouncing back
choices.feedback: This is incorrect — a tennis ball is used in the passage to explain what reflection means, not to describe what the Moon is.

choices.text: A giant rocky sidewalk in the sky
choices.feedback: This is incorrect — the passage says the Moon has a rocky surface, but it never calls the Moon a sidewalk; the sidewalk is part of the tennis ball example.

choices.text: A giant star making its own light
choices.feedback: This is incorrect — the passage actually tells us the opposite, that the Moon does NOT make its own light, which is what a star does.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
You See Because Light Bounces

You cannot actually see most objects by themselves. You see them because light bounces off them and flies into your eyes. For example, when you look at a red apple on a table, light is hitting that apple and reflecting toward you. Your brain reads that reflected light and figures out the apple's color, shape, and size.

Actually, every single object you can see is reflecting light. If something did not reflect light at all, it would be completely invisible. You would not be able to see it!
stimulus.content_text:
You See Because Light Bounces
You cannot actually see most objects by themselves. You see them because light bounces off them and flies into your eyes. For example, when you look at a red apple on a table, light is hitting that apple and reflecting toward you. Your brain reads that reflected light and figures out the apple's color, shape, and size.
Actually, every single object you can see is reflecting light. If something did not reflect light at all, it would be completely invisible. You would not be able to see it!

items.prompt: Based on the passage, why would an object that does not reflect light be invisible?

choices.text: because the object would be too small for your eyes to notice
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage never says anything about an object's size making it invisible — it's all about whether light bounces off it.

choices.text: because no light would bounce off it and reach your eyes
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage explains that you see objects when light bounces off them and reaches your eyes, so an object with no reflected light would be invisible.

choices.text: because your brain would stop reading all light around you
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because your brain doesn't stop reading all light — it simply wouldn't get any reflected light from that specific object.

choices.text: because the object would absorb your eyes' light beams
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage explains that outside light bounces off objects into your eyes — your eyes don't shoot out light beams.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
The Rule Light Always Follows

Light follows a pattern when it bounces. Scientists call this pattern the law of reflection — a rule that says light bounces off a surface at the same angle it arrived. An angle is the tilt or slant of that path — how far it leans from going perfectly straight. Think about tossing a ball straight at a wall. It bounces straight back. Now toss it at a slant. It then bounces away at the same slant, but on the opposite side. Light behaves the same way every time.
stimulus.content_text:
The Rule Light Always Follows
Light follows a pattern when it bounces. Scientists call this pattern the law of reflection — a rule that says light bounces off a surface at the same angle it arrived. An angle is the tilt or slant of that path — how far it leans from going perfectly straight. Think about tossing a ball straight at a wall. It bounces straight back. Now toss it at a slant. It then bounces away at the same slant, but on the opposite side. Light behaves the same way every time.

items.prompt: According to the text, which sequence shows how light moves when it hits a surface?

choices.text: First it arrives at a surface, then it bounces off at the same angle.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage clearly states that light arrives at a surface first and then bounces off at the same angle it came in — that's the law of reflection!

choices.text: First it bounces off a surface, then it arrives at the very same angle.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because it has the steps in the wrong order — light must arrive at the surface before it can bounce off, not the other way around.

choices.text: First it travels perfectly straight, then it leans away to form a new angle.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage says light bounces off at the same angle it arrived, not a brand new angle.

choices.text: First it follows a new pattern, then it bounces straight back to the start.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage says light always follows the same pattern (the law of reflection), not a new one, and it only bounces straight back if it arrived perfectly straight.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Smooth Surfaces vs. Bumpy Surfaces

A smooth, shiny surface reflects light evenly. That is why mirrors show such clear pictures. A mirror is made of smooth glass with a thin coating of metal behind it. When rays of light — thin beams that travel in straight lines, like the beam of a flashlight — hit the mirror, they all bounce off in the same orderly direction. The image stays sharp. That is why you can see every detail of your face. Similarly, flat water reflects the sky and trees above it almost perfectly.

But what about a brick wall or a crumpled piece of paper? These surfaces are rough. Tiny bumps and dips cover them. Light still bounces off, but the bumps send each ray flying in a different direction. This scattered bounce is called diffuse reflection — "diffuse" means spread out, like dandelion seeds blowing apart in the wind. Because the light scatters, you do not see a clear image. You can see the wall just fine, but you will never spot your reflection in it.

That is why you can see every detail of your face. Similarly, flat water reflects the sky and trees above it almost perfectly.
But what about a brick wall or a crumpled piece of paper? These surfaces are rough. Tiny bumps and dips cover them. Light still bounces off, but the bumps send each ray flying in a different direction. This scattered bounce is called diffuse reflection — "diffuse" means spread out, like dandelion seeds blowing apart in the wind. Because the light scatters, you do not see a clear image. You can see the wall just fine, but you will never spot your reflection in it.

items.prompt: The author compares "diffuse reflection" to dandelion seeds blowing in the wind to show that the light rays...

choices.text: travel together in straight lines.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because light traveling together in straight lines describes how rays behave on smooth surfaces like mirrors, not on rough bumpy surfaces.

choices.text: scatter apart in many directions.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage says 'diffuse' means spread out, just like dandelion seeds blowing apart — showing that light rays scatter in many different directions.

choices.text: bounce off in an orderly way.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because bouncing off in an orderly way is what happens on smooth surfaces like mirrors, which is the opposite of diffuse reflection.

choices.text: form a perfectly clear picture.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because when light scatters in many directions, it does not form a clear picture — that is exactly why you cannot see your reflection in a brick wall.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Reflection Keeps You Safe

People use reflection in smart ways every day. Mirrors help you check your face when you get dressed. In addition, reflectors on bicycles and road signs catch car headlights and bounce the light straight back toward drivers. That bright flash warns drivers that something is ahead, keeping everyone safer at night. Large curved mirrors in telescopes use the same idea to gather and focus reflected light, and that focused light helps scientists see stars and galaxies trillions of miles away and farther.
stimulus.content_text:
Reflection Keeps You Safe
People use reflection in smart ways every day. Mirrors help you check your face when you get dressed. In addition, reflectors on bicycles and road signs catch car headlights and bounce the light straight back toward drivers. That bright flash warns drivers that something is ahead, keeping everyone safer at night. Large curved mirrors in telescopes use the same idea to gather and focus reflected light, and that focused light helps scientists see stars and galaxies trillions of miles away and farther.

items.prompt: According to the section 'Reflection Keeps You Safe', what are reflectors?

choices.text: Items that catch and bounce light back
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage says reflectors catch headlights and bounce the light straight back, which is exactly what 'items that catch and bounce light back' means.

choices.text: Mirrors that help you check your face
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the passage says mirrors help you check your face, but that describes a regular mirror, not a reflector.

choices.text: Tools that help scientists see the stars
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the passage says telescopes help scientists see stars, not reflectors.

choices.text: Lamps that shine brightly on the street
choices.feedback: D is wrong because reflectors don't create their own light — they only bounce light from somewhere else, like car headlights.

items.title: Section 6 - Guiding Question 5

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
A World Made Visible by Bouncing Light

Everything else — every tree, every building, every face — is only visible because light bounced off it and reached your eyes. Therefore, you do not just look at the world. The world reflects itself to you, one bounce at a time.
stimulus.content_text:
A World Made Visible by Bouncing Light
Everything else — every tree, every building, every face — is only visible because light bounced off it and reached your eyes. Therefore, you do not just look at the world. The world reflects itself to you, one bounce at a time.

choices.text: Light bounced off them and reached our eyes
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage says everything is visible because 'light bounced off it and reached your eyes,' which is exactly what this answer says.

choices.text: Our eyes send light out to each object
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the passage never says our eyes send light out to objects — it says light bounces off objects and comes to our eyes.

choices.text: Objects make their own light for us to see
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage says objects reflect light that already exists, not that they create their own light.

choices.text: We look directly at objects without any light
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage explains that we need light bouncing off objects to see them — we cannot see things without that bouncing light.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: According to the article, how is a bicycle reflector different from a brick wall?

choices.text: A reflector makes its own bright light, but a wall only reflects the light.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because reflectors do not make their own light — they only bounce light from car headlights back toward drivers.

choices.text: A reflector bounces light straight back, but a wall scatters light apart.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the article says reflectors bounce light straight back toward drivers, while rough surfaces like brick walls scatter light in many different directions.

choices.text: A reflector creates a sharp clear image, but a wall stays totally unseen.

choices.text: A reflector has a rough and bumpy shape, but a wall has a smooth coating.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: Which two headings help you locate the causes for why we can see a red apple and why a mirror shows a sharp picture?

choices.text: "You See Because Light Bounces" and "Smooth Surfaces vs. Bumpy Surfaces"
choices.feedback: This is correct because 'You See Because Light Bounces' explains the apple example and 'Smooth Surfaces vs. Bumpy Surfaces' explains why mirrors show clear images.

choices.text: "The Rule Light Always Follows" and "A World Made Visible by Bouncing Light"
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because 'The Rule Light Always Follows' talks about the angle light bounces at, and 'A World Made Visible by Bouncing Light' is just a wrap-up — neither one explains the apple or the mirror in detail.

choices.text: "Reflection Keeps You Safe" and "Every Single Night, the Moon Is Lying to You"
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because 'Reflection Keeps You Safe' is about bike reflectors and telescopes, and 'Every Single Night, the Moon Is Lying to You' is about the Moon — neither explains the apple or the mirror.

choices.text: "Smooth Surfaces vs. Bumpy Surfaces" and "The Rule Light Always Follows"
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because 'The Rule Light Always Follows' explains the angle pattern of bouncing light, not why we see the apple, so it doesn't match both causes we're looking for.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: Which sequence of events best explains the main idea of how we are able to see things?

choices.text: First light leaves our eyes, next it hits an object, and finally it bounces.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because light does not start from our eyes — it comes from a source like the sun, hits an object, and then bounces to our eyes.

choices.text: First light hits an object, next it bounces, and finally it enters our eyes.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage explains that light hits an object, bounces off it, and then flies into your eyes so your brain can figure out what you're seeing.

choices.text: First light hits an object, next it scatters, and finally it stays inside.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because light does not stay inside an object — it bounces away and travels to your eyes so you can see.

choices.text: First light reaches our eyes, next it bounces, and finally it hits an object.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because light must hit the object and bounce first before it can reach your eyes, so the order is backwards.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: Based on the article, how are the Moon and a red apple similar?

choices.text: Both of them make their own bright light that travels straight to your eyes.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the article clearly says the Moon does NOT make its own light, and apples don't make light either — they both only reflect light that hits them.

choices.text: Both of them are smooth surfaces that reflect light perfectly evenly like glass.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the article describes the Moon as having a rocky surface, not a smooth one like glass, and it never says apples reflect light perfectly evenly.

choices.text: Both of them are only visible because light bounces off them toward you.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the article explains that the Moon is visible because sunlight bounces off it toward your eyes, and you see a red apple because light reflects off it toward you — both work the same way!

choices.text: Both of them gather and focus light to help scientists see far away.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the article says it is large curved mirrors in telescopes that gather and focus light to help scientists see far away, not the Moon or an apple.

syllabus_metadata.title: Bouncing Light: How Reflection Works

assessments.title: Bending Light: Refraction and Rainbows

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Trick That Fools Your Eyes

A Straw That Seems to Snap

Right where the straw meets the water, it appears bent — almost broken. Pull it out, and it is perfectly straight again.

What you just saw has a name: refraction. Refraction is the bending of light as it moves from one material into another — the way a shopping cart pulls to one side when you roll it from a smooth floor onto grass. Light travels incredibly fast through air. But when it enters water or glass, it slows down. That change in speed forces the light to shift direction, just a little. Your eyes follow the light in a straight line back to where they think the straw is. But the light bent on its way to you, so the straw looks out of place. The straw never moved. The light did.
stimulus.content_text:
The Trick That Fools Your EyesA Straw That Seems to Snap
Right where the straw meets the water, it appears bent — almost broken. Pull it out, and it is perfectly straight again.

What you just saw has a name: refraction. Refraction is the bending of light as it moves from one material into another — the way a shopping cart pulls to one side when you roll it from a smooth floor onto grass. Light travels incredibly fast through air. But when it enters water or glass, it slows down. That change in speed forces the light to shift direction, just a little. Your eyes follow the light in a straight line back to where they think the straw is. But the light bent on its way to you, so the straw looks out of place. The straw never moved. The light did.

items.prompt: According to the text, what happens to the straw when you pull it out of the water, in contrast to how it looked in the water?

choices.text: It stays bent from being in the water
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage says the straw is 'perfectly straight' when pulled out, so it does not stay bent.

choices.text: It is perfectly straight once again
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly states 'Pull it out, and it is perfectly straight again,' showing the straw was never really bent.

choices.text: It breaks apart into smaller pieces
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the straw never breaks — the passage explains it only *appears* bent due to light bending, not because anything physical happened to the straw.

choices.text: It changes shape because of the light
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because light does not change the shape of the straw — it only changes how the straw *looks* while it is in the water.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Hidden Colors Inside White Light

Sunlight looks white. But white light is actually a mix of many colors traveling together. You can prove it with a prism — a solid piece of glass shaped like a triangle. When white light passes through a prism, each color inside it bends at a slightly different angle. Red bends the least. Violet bends the most. The colors fan out and separate into a band called a spectrum — a lineup of colors arranged in order, like crayons sorted from warm to cool. That spectrum holds seven colors: red, orange, yellow, green, blue, indigo, and violet.

Actually, those colors were hiding inside the white light all along. The prism just pulls them apart so you can see each one.
stimulus.content_text:
Hidden Colors Inside White Light
Sunlight looks white. But white light is actually a mix of many colors traveling together. You can prove it with a prism — a solid piece of glass shaped like a triangle. When white light passes through a prism, each color inside it bends at a slightly different angle. Red bends the least. Violet bends the most. The colors fan out and separate into a band called a spectrum — a lineup of colors arranged in order, like crayons sorted from warm to cool. That spectrum holds seven colors: red, orange, yellow, green, blue, indigo, and violet.

Actually, those colors were hiding inside the white light all along. The prism just pulls them apart so you can see each one.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, why do the colors fan out and separate when white light passes through a prism?

choices.text: because the prism adds new colors to the white light
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage says the colors were already hiding inside the white light — the prism just pulls them apart, it doesn't add anything new.

choices.text: because each color bends at a slightly different angle
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly states that each color bends at a slightly different angle, which is exactly what causes them to fan out and separate.

choices.text: because the prism changes white light into violet light
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the prism separates white light into all seven colors of the spectrum, not just violet light.

choices.text: because red light and violet light travel at the same angle
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage tells us red bends the least and violet bends the most, meaning they travel at different angles, not the same one.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
How Rainbows Really Work

After a rainstorm, millions of tiny water droplets hang in the air. Each droplet works like a tiny prism. Sunlight enters a droplet and slows down. That bending is refraction. Inside the droplet, the light bounces off the back wall — that bounce is called reflection, which means light hitting a surface and springing back, like a ball thrown against a wall. Then the light bends again as it exits the droplet. Each color exits at a different angle. When millions of droplets do this at once, you see a glowing arc of color across the sky.

To spot a rainbow, the sun must be behind you and the rain must be in front of you. The sunlight enters the drops, separates into colors, and bounces back toward your eyes.

You could never walk to one and touch it. A rainbow is light arriving at your eyes from a certain angle. If you take three steps to the left, the rainbow seems to move, too. It exists only in the space between the sun, the raindrops, and you.
stimulus.content_text:
How Rainbows Really Work
After a rainstorm, millions of tiny water droplets hang in the air. Each droplet works like a tiny prism. Sunlight enters a droplet and slows down. That bending is refraction. Inside the droplet, the light bounces off the back wall — that bounce is called reflection, which means light hitting a surface and springing back, like a ball thrown against a wall. Then the light bends again as it exits the droplet. Each color exits at a different angle. When millions of droplets do this at once, you see a glowing arc of color across the sky.

To spot a rainbow, the sun must be behind you and the rain must be in front of you. The sunlight enters the drops, separates into colors, and bounces back toward your eyes.

You could never walk to one and touch it. A rainbow is light arriving at your eyes from a certain angle. If you take three steps to the left, the rainbow seems to move, too. It exists only in the space between the sun, the raindrops, and you.

items.prompt: According to the passage, what are the steps that happen to sunlight inside a water droplet to create a rainbow?

choices.text: Light bounces off the front of the droplet, bends once, and then exits as white light.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage says light bends twice (entering and exiting), and it comes out as separate colors, not white light.

choices.text: Light enters and bends, bounces off the back wall, and bends again as it exits.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage clearly describes these three steps in order: light enters and bends, bounces off the back wall, and bends again as it exits.

choices.text: Light exits the droplet first, then bounces inside, and finally enters the droplet.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because it has the steps in the wrong order — light enters the droplet first, it doesn't exit first.

choices.text: Light splits into colors outside the droplet, then enters and bounces back out.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage says the light splits into colors inside the droplet, not outside of it.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Bending Light on Purpose

People use refraction every day. Eyeglasses have curved lenses that bend light before it reaches your eyes. Some eyes bend light too much or too little on their own, so images look blurry. The glasses bend the light just the right amount to sharpen the picture. Camera lenses do something similar — they bend light so it lands in exactly the right spot to make a clear photo.

That lens bends incoming light and aims it at the back of your eye, where a thin layer detects the image.
stimulus.content_text:
Bending Light on Purpose
People use refraction every day. Eyeglasses have curved lenses that bend light before it reaches your eyes. Some eyes bend light too much or too little on their own, so images look blurry. The glasses bend the light just the right amount to sharpen the picture. Camera lenses do something similar — they bend light so it lands in exactly the right spot to make a clear photo.

That lens bends incoming light and aims it at the back of your eye, where a thin layer detects the image.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, how are eyeglasses and camera lenses similar?

choices.text: They both make light travel faster than before.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage never mentions light speeding up — it only talks about light being bent.

choices.text: They both bend light to make images clearer.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage says both eyeglasses and camera lenses bend light to make images sharp and clear.

choices.text: They both fix eyes that see things too far away.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage describes eyes that bend light too much or too little, which is not the same as only fixing eyes that see things far away.

choices.text: They both detect images on a thin layer inside.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the thin layer that detects images is inside your eye, not part of the glasses or camera lens.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Light Tells the Truth — Sideways

That one simple rule explains the broken-looking straw, the rainbow after a storm, and the glasses on your teacher's face. It is light following the rules of speed. Once you know those rules, you start seeing refraction everywhere you look.
stimulus.content_text:
Light Tells the Truth — Sideways
That one simple rule explains the broken-looking straw, the rainbow after a storm, and the glasses on your teacher's face. It is light following the rules of speed. Once you know those rules, you start seeing refraction everywhere you look.

items.prompt: According to the text, what does refraction mean?

choices.text: light making colors after a storm
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because a rainbow after a storm is just one example of refraction happening, not the definition of what refraction means.

choices.text: light following the rules of speed
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly says 'It is light following the rules of speed,' and 'it' refers to refraction.

choices.text: light bending only in a glass of water
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage says you can see refraction 'everywhere you look,' not only in a glass of water.

choices.text: light looking broken inside a glass of water
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage never says refraction means showing objects from the side — that idea is not in the text.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: Based on the article, how is the way a prism uses refraction different from the way eyeglasses use refraction?

choices.text: A prism separates light into different colors, but eyeglasses bend light to make pictures clear.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the article says a prism separates white light into colors, while eyeglasses bend light to make blurry images clear — two different uses of refraction.

choices.text: A prism makes straight objects look broken, but eyeglasses make broken objects look perfectly straight.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the broken-looking straw happens when light bends through water, and that has nothing to do with prisms or eyeglasses.

choices.text: A prism bounces light off its back wall, but eyeglasses bounce light back toward your eyes.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because bouncing light off a back wall describes reflection inside a raindrop, not anything a prism or eyeglasses do.

choices.text: A prism works only when it is raining, but eyeglasses work only when it is sunny.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the article never says prisms only work in rain or that eyeglasses only work in sunshine — both work whenever light passes through them.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: What causes both a straw in water to look bent and a rainbow to appear in the sky?

choices.text: Light moves in a straight line through every material
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage says light changes direction when it moves from air into water — it does NOT travel straight through every material.

choices.text: Light changes direction when it moves into water
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage explains that refraction, which is light changing direction when entering water, causes both the bent-looking straw and the rainbow.

choices.text: Water reflects all the light back to your eyes
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because while reflection does happen inside raindrops, it is the bending (refraction) of light — not just reflection — that explains both the straw and the rainbow.

choices.text: Sunlight turns into colors only after a rainstorm
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage tells us that colors are always hidden inside white sunlight and can be separated by a prism anytime, not only after a rainstorm.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: You want to learn how light separates into colors in glass, and then how it separates in water. Which steps show the correct order to find this information?

choices.text: First read the section about hidden colors, then read the section about rainbows.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the 'Hidden Colors' section covers light separating in glass (a prism), and the 'Rainbows' section covers light separating in water droplets — which is exactly the order the question asks for.

choices.text: First read the section about rainbows, then read the section about hidden colors.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because it has you read about water (rainbows) before glass (the prism), but the question asks about glass first, then water.

choices.text: First read the section about a bent straw, then read the section about bending light.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the straw section explains why a straw looks bent, not how light separates into colors.

choices.text: First read the section about bending light, then read the section about a bent straw.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the 'Bending Light on Purpose' section is about eyeglasses and cameras, not about light separating into different colors.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: How are the examples of the straw, the rainbow, and the eyeglasses alike in supporting the main idea of the passage?

choices.text: They all explain how light speeds up when it enters water.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage actually says light slows down when it enters water, not speeds up.

choices.text: They all explain how bending light changes the way things look.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the straw, the rainbow, and the eyeglasses are all examples of bending light changing the way things look, which is the main idea of the whole passage.

choices.text: They all explain how white light hides many colors inside it.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because hidden colors in white light is only talked about in the prism section, not in the straw or eyeglasses examples.

choices.text: They all explain how curved glass makes blurry pictures look clear.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because curved glass making pictures clear is only about eyeglasses and cameras, not about the straw or the rainbow.
