assessments.title: Making Things Bigger and Smaller: Lenses at Work

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Glass That Bends Light

A Tiny World Made Big

A ladybug sits on a leaf. It is smaller than your fingernail. But hold a magnifying glass above it, and suddenly you can count the black spots on its back. You can see its tiny legs gripping the leaf. The ladybug did not grow. It stayed exactly the same size. What changed was the light traveling from the bug to your eyes.

That magnifying glass is a lens — a curved piece of clear glass or plastic that bends light as it passes through. A lens does not make its own light. It has no batteries and no bulbs. It only changes the direction of light that is already there. Think of a lens like a ramp for a skateboard. The ramp does not push the skateboard. It just sends it in a new direction.
stimulus.content_text:
The Glass That Bends LightA Tiny World Made Big
A ladybug sits on a leaf. It is smaller than your fingernail. But hold a magnifying glass above it, and suddenly you can count the black spots on its back. You can see its tiny legs gripping the leaf. The ladybug did not grow. It stayed exactly the same size. What changed was the light traveling from the bug to your eyes.

That magnifying glass is a lens — a curved piece of clear glass or plastic that bends light as it passes through. A lens does not make its own light. It has no batteries and no bulbs. It only changes the direction of light that is already there. Think of a lens like a ramp for a skateboard. The ramp does not push the skateboard. It just sends it in a new direction.

items.prompt: According to the text, what is different between a lens and a bulb?

choices.text: A lens makes light, but a bulb bends light.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because it has it backwards — the passage says a lens bends light, and a bulb is something that makes light, not the other way around.

choices.text: A lens bends light, but a bulb makes light.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage clearly states a lens bends light and has no bulbs, meaning a bulb makes light while a lens only changes light's direction.

choices.text: A lens grows bigger, but a bulb stays the same.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because nothing in the passage says a lens grows bigger — that idea comes from the ladybug staying the same size, which is a different thing.

choices.text: A lens uses batteries, but a bulb uses glass.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage says a lens has NO batteries, so it is wrong to say a lens uses batteries.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Why Light Bends in Curved Glass

You can see this when sunlight streams through a window and makes a bright stripe across the floor. But when light enters curved glass, the glass slows the light down a little. Because the glass is curved, one side of the light beam slows before the other. This forces the beam to change direction. That bending is what gives lenses their power.
stimulus.content_text:
Why Light Bends in Curved Glass
You can see this when sunlight streams through a window and makes a bright stripe across the floor. But when light enters curved glass, the glass slows the light down a little. Because the glass is curved, one side of the light beam slows before the other. This forces the beam to change direction. That bending is what gives lenses their power.

items.prompt: The main idea of this section is that curved glass bends light. Which detail supports this main idea by explaining what causes the bending?

choices.text: The curved glass makes the light beam move much faster.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage says curved glass slows light down a little — it does not make light move faster.

choices.text: One side of the light beam slows before the other.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly states that one side of the light beam slows before the other, and that uneven slowing is what forces the beam to bend.

choices.text: The glass slows down the entire light beam all at once.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage explains that the two sides of the beam slow down at different times, not all at once.

choices.text: The light beam changes its direction before entering glass.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage says the light changes direction when it enters the curved glass, not before it gets there.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Two Shapes, Two Jobs

There are two main types of lenses, and they bend light in opposite ways.

A convex lens is thicker in the middle and thinner at the edges — shaped like a lentil bean. Light rays bend inward when they pass through it. The rays meet at a point called the focus. A convex lens makes objects appear bigger. Your magnifying glass is a convex lens. The ladybug stays small, but the picture your eye receives looks large.

A concave lens is the opposite shape. It is thinner in the middle and thicker at the edges — like a bowl carved into glass. Light rays spread outward when they pass through it. This makes images appear smaller. If you have peeked through a peephole in a door, you have seen a concave lens at work.
stimulus.content_text:
Two Shapes, Two Jobs
There are two main types of lenses, and they bend light in opposite ways.

A convex lens is thicker in the middle and thinner at the edges — shaped like a lentil bean. Light rays bend inward when they pass through it. The rays meet at a point called the focus. A convex lens makes objects appear bigger. Your magnifying glass is a convex lens. The ladybug stays small, but the picture your eye receives looks large.

A concave lens is the opposite shape. It is thinner in the middle and thicker at the edges — like a bowl carved into glass. Light rays spread outward when they pass through it. This makes images appear smaller. If you have peeked through a peephole in a door, you have seen a concave lens at work.

items.prompt: According to the text, what do light rays do after they bend inward through a convex lens?

choices.text: They meet at a point called the focus.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage directly states that after light rays bend inward through a convex lens, 'the rays meet at a point called the focus.'

choices.text: They spread outward to make the images smaller.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because spreading outward to make images smaller is what light does through a concave lens, which is the opposite of a convex lens.

choices.text: They carve a bowl shape into the glass.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because a bowl shape describes the appearance of a concave lens, not something light rays do.

choices.text: They change the actual size of the ladybug.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage clearly says the ladybug stays small — the convex lens only changes how big the image looks to your eye, not the actual size of the object.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Lenses You Use Every Day

Millions of people wear eyeglasses, and every pair holds lenses doing careful work. Some people are nearsighted, meaning they see nearby things clearly but faraway objects look blurry. Their glasses use concave lenses to spread the light just enough to sharpen distant views. Other people are farsighted, meaning they see far away but close-up things look blurry. Their glasses use convex lenses to pull the light together and bring near objects into focus.

Cameras use lenses too. The lens bends light to focus a sharp image onto a sensor — a tiny chip inside the camera that captures the picture, the way film used to. By moving the lens forward or back, a photographer can snap clear photos of things both near and far.
stimulus.content_text:
Lenses You Use Every Day
Millions of people wear eyeglasses, and every pair holds lenses doing careful work. Some people are nearsighted, meaning they see nearby things clearly but faraway objects look blurry. Their glasses use concave lenses to spread the light just enough to sharpen distant views. Other people are farsighted, meaning they see far away but close-up things look blurry. Their glasses use convex lenses to pull the light together and bring near objects into focus.

Cameras use lenses too. The lens bends light to focus a sharp image onto a sensor — a tiny chip inside the camera that captures the picture, the way film used to. By moving the lens forward or back, a photographer can snap clear photos of things both near and far.

items.prompt: Based on the text, how are the words "sensor" and "film" similar in meaning?

choices.text: They both mean a tiny chip inside a digital camera.
choices.feedback: A is wrong because only the sensor is described as a tiny chip — film is the older way of capturing pictures and is not a chip.

choices.text: They both mean a camera part that captures a picture.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage says the sensor captures the picture 'the way film used to,' showing both words mean a camera part that captures a picture.

choices.text: They both mean a curved glass that bends the light.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because curved glass that bends light describes a lens, not a sensor or film.

choices.text: They both mean a camera piece moving forward and back.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the passage says the lens moves forward or back to focus photos — not the sensor or film.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Seeing What Eyes Cannot

Scientists pair lenses together to see things no human eye could reach alone. Telescopes stack lenses to magnify objects in space — planets, stars, and distant galaxies. Microscopes work the other direction. They magnify things that are too small to see, like bacteria — living things so tiny that thousands could fit on the period at the end of this sentence.
stimulus.content_text:
Seeing What Eyes Cannot
Scientists pair lenses together to see things no human eye could reach alone. Telescopes stack lenses to magnify objects in space — planets, stars, and distant galaxies. Microscopes work the other direction. They magnify things that are too small to see, like bacteria — living things so tiny that thousands could fit on the period at the end of this sentence.

items.prompt: In the section titled 'Seeing What Eyes Cannot,' how can a reader find the definition of bacteria in this section?

choices.text: by reading the first sentence of the section
choices.feedback: The first sentence talks about scientists using lenses together, not about bacteria or its definition.

choices.text: by looking for the dash and definition right after the word
choices.feedback: The word 'bacteria' is printed in bold, and the definition appears right next to it, making it the fastest way to find the meaning.

choices.text: by studying the title of the section
choices.feedback: The title 'Seeing What Eyes Cannot' tells you what the section is about, but it doesn't give you the definition of bacteria.

choices.text: by counting the paragraphs in the section
choices.feedback: Counting paragraphs doesn't point you to any specific word or definition in the passage.

items.title: Section 6 - Guiding Question 5

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
A Curve with Great Power

Light is racing in a straight line, hitting a curve of glass, and bending — just enough to show you something your eyes could never find on their own. A simple curve in a piece of glass is doing all the work.
stimulus.content_text:
A Curve with Great Power
Light is racing in a straight line, hitting a curve of glass, and bending — just enough to show you something your eyes could never find on their own. A simple curve in a piece of glass is doing all the work.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, which detail best supports the idea that a curved piece of glass helps people see things they normally cannot?

choices.text: Light always travels in a curved path through the air
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage says light travels in a straight line through the air, not a curved path.

choices.text: Light bends when it hits the curve of the glass
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly says light bends when it hits the curve of the glass, and that bending helps you see things your eyes normally cannot.

choices.text: Light stops moving when it reaches the glass
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage says light keeps moving and bends — it never says light stops at the glass.

choices.text: Light passes through glass without changing at all
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage clearly says light does change when it hits the glass — it bends, which is the whole point!

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: Based on the text, what is the difference between the lenses used in glasses for farsighted people and nearsighted people?

choices.text: Lenses for farsighted people are thicker in the middle, but lenses for nearsighted people are thinner.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage says farsighted people use convex lenses (thicker in the middle) and nearsighted people use concave lenses (thinner in the middle).

choices.text: Lenses for farsighted people are thinner in the middle, but lenses for nearsighted people are thicker.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because it gets the shapes backwards — convex lenses for farsighted people are thicker in the middle, not thinner.

choices.text: Lenses for farsighted people are shaped like a ramp, but lenses for nearsighted people are shaped like a bowl.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage uses the ramp and bowl descriptions to explain how lenses work in general, not to describe eyeglass lenses specifically.

choices.text: Lenses for farsighted people are made of flat glass, but lenses for nearsighted people are made of curved glass.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage never says any eyeglass lenses are made of flat glass — all lenses described in the passage are curved.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: A reader wants to know what causes the lenses in nearsighted glasses to spread light outward. Which heading should the reader look under to find the answer?

choices.text: "A Tiny World Made Big"
choices.feedback: Section A, 'A Tiny World Made Big,' introduces what a lens is using a ladybug example, but it doesn't explain the shapes of lenses or why they spread light.

choices.text: "Why Light Bends in Curved Glass"
choices.feedback: Section B, 'Why Light Bends in Curved Glass,' explains how curved glass slows and bends light, but it doesn't describe the two lens shapes or how concave lenses work.

choices.text: "Two Shapes, Two Jobs"
choices.feedback: Section C, 'Two Shapes, Two Jobs,' is correct because it explains that concave lenses are thinner in the middle and spread light rays outward — which is exactly what nearsighted glasses use.

choices.text: "Seeing What Eyes Cannot"
choices.feedback: Section D, 'Seeing What Eyes Cannot,' talks about telescopes and microscopes, not about the shapes of lenses used in eyeglasses.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: Which choice best shows the order of ideas that supports the main idea of the article about how lenses work?

choices.text: Light is made by a lens, then glass changes its speed, then objects look clearer.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the article clearly tells us a lens has no batteries or bulbs and does not make its own light — it only bends light that is already there.

choices.text: Light enters curved glass, then the light bends, then the image looks bigger or smaller.
choices.feedback: B is correct because it follows the article's explanation step by step: light enters curved glass, the glass bends the light, and the result is an image that looks bigger or smaller.

choices.text: A camera takes a picture, then a sensor bends light, then the lens moves backward.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the order is mixed up and it wrongly says the sensor bends light — the article says the lens bends light and the sensor just captures the picture.

choices.text: A ladybug grows larger, then the focus moves, then a microscope finds bacteria.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the article specifically says the ladybug stayed exactly the same size and never grew bigger.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: How are telescopes and microscopes similar in the way they help people see, according to the article?

choices.text: They both spread light outward to make images smaller and sharper.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because spreading light outward makes images smaller (that's what concave lenses do), but telescopes and microscopes are used to magnify things, not shrink them.

choices.text: They both pair lenses together to show things eyes cannot see alone.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the article directly says scientists pair lenses together in both telescopes and microscopes to help people see things their eyes cannot see on their own.

choices.text: They both move lenses forward and back to focus on nearby objects.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because moving lenses forward and back is described in the article as something cameras do, not telescopes or microscopes.

choices.text: They both use a single curved lens to bend light in one direction.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the article says scientists 'pair lenses together,' which means both telescopes and microscopes use more than one lens, not just a single curved lens.

syllabus_metadata.title: Making Things Bigger and Smaller: Lenses at Work

assessments.title: How Your Eyes See: The Science of Vision

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Upside-Down Picture Inside Your Eye

Light Bounces In

Right now, light is bouncing off this page and flying into your eyes. Close your eyes for three seconds. Everything goes dark. Open them, and the world rushes back. That happened because your eyes do not shoot out beams or rays to find things. Instead, they catch light that bounces off objects around you. This bouncing is called reflection — light hitting a surface and springing back, the way a tennis ball bounces off a wall. Without reflected light entering your eyes, you would see nothing at all.
stimulus.content_text:
The Upside-Down Picture Inside Your EyeLight Bounces In
Right now, light is bouncing off this page and flying into your eyes. Close your eyes for three seconds. Everything goes dark. Open them, and the world rushes back. That happened because your eyes do not shoot out beams or rays to find things. Instead, they catch light that bounces off objects around you. This bouncing is called reflection — light hitting a surface and springing back, the way a tennis ball bounces off a wall. Without reflected light entering your eyes, you would see nothing at all.

items.prompt: In the section "Light Bounces In," what is different about how eyes work and how beams or rays work?

choices.text: Eyes send out light to find things, but beams or rays catch light from objects.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because it has it backwards — the passage says eyes CATCH light, not send it out.

choices.text: Eyes catch light from objects, but beams or rays shoot out to find things.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage clearly states eyes catch reflected light from objects, while beams or rays are described as shooting out to find things.

choices.text: Eyes make everything dark, but beams or rays bring the world back.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because closing your eyes making things dark is about blocking light, and it has nothing to do with how beams or rays work.

choices.text: Eyes reflect off surfaces, but beams or rays bounce like tennis balls.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage says light reflects off surfaces like a tennis ball — that describes reflection, not how eyes work.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
The Front Door of Your Eye

Reflected light first hits the cornea, a clear, curved cover on the front of your eye. Think of it as a tiny window that also bends light inward, like the curved glass of a magnifying lens. Behind the cornea, you'll notice a dark circle. That's your pupil. You might think the pupil is a black dot painted on your eye. Actually, it is a hole — an opening that lets light pass through, like a doorway into a dark room.

Wrapped around the pupil is the iris, the colored part of your eye. Your iris might be brown, blue, green, or hazel. But it does more than look nice. It works like a curtain that opens and closes. In bright sunlight, your iris squeezes the pupil smaller so less light floods in. In a dim room, your iris pulls the pupil wider to grab every bit of light it can. Try this: look in a mirror, cover one eye for ten seconds, then uncover it. Watch that pupil shrink as light hits it.
stimulus.content_text:
The Front Door of Your Eye
Reflected light first hits the cornea, a clear, curved cover on the front of your eye. Think of it as a tiny window that also bends light inward, like the curved glass of a magnifying lens. Behind the cornea, you'll notice a dark circle. That's your pupil. You might think the pupil is a black dot painted on your eye. Actually, it is a hole — an opening that lets light pass through, like a doorway into a dark room.
Wrapped around the pupil is the iris, the colored part of your eye. Your iris might be brown, blue, green, or hazel. But it does more than look nice. It works like a curtain that opens and closes. In bright sunlight, your iris squeezes the pupil smaller so less light floods in. In a dim room, your iris pulls the pupil wider to grab every bit of light it can. Try this: look in a mirror, cover one eye for ten seconds, then uncover it. Watch that pupil shrink as light hits it.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, why does your iris squeeze the pupil smaller when you are in bright sunlight?

choices.text: so the colored part of your eye looks nicer
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage says the iris does more than 'look nice' — its job in sunlight is to control how much light gets in, not to look prettier.

choices.text: so less light floods into your eye
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly says the iris squeezes the pupil smaller in bright sunlight 'so less light floods in,' protecting your eye.

choices.text: so more light can enter your eye
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because squeezing the pupil smaller actually lets in LESS light, which is the opposite of what this answer says.

choices.text: so the cornea can bend light inward
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because bending light inward is the cornea's job, as described at the beginning of the passage — not something the iris does.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Bending Light into a Picture

After slipping through the pupil, light reaches the lens. The lens is a clear, flexible part that bends light even more precisely than the cornea. It can stretch thin to focus on faraway trees or bulge thick to focus on a book in your hands. The lens aims all that light at the back wall of your eye.

That back wall is the retina, a thin layer packed with millions of tiny light-sensing cells. These cells come in two types. Rod cells work best in dim light. They pick up shapes and movement, which is why you can still see your bedroom furniture at night — but only in shades of gray. Cone cells need brighter light. They let you see colors and sharp details. So the next time colors seem to disappear in a dark room, you'll know why. Your cone cells have gone quiet, and your rod cells have taken over.

Here is the wildest part: the image that lands on your retina is upside down. The lens flips it as it bends the light. A tree looks like it's growing downward. Your friend's face is turned on its head. So why doesn't the world look flipped?
stimulus.content_text:
Bending Light into a Picture
After slipping through the pupil, light reaches the lens. The lens is a clear, flexible part that bends light even more precisely than the cornea. It can stretch thin to focus on faraway trees or bulge thick to focus on a book in your hands. The lens aims all that light at the back wall of your eye.
That back wall is the retina, a thin layer packed with millions of tiny light-sensing cells. These cells come in two types. Rod cells work best in dim light. They pick up shapes and movement, which is why you can still see your bedroom furniture at night — but only in shades of gray. Cone cells need brighter light. They let you see colors and sharp details. So the next time colors seem to disappear in a dark room, you'll know why. Your cone cells have gone quiet, and your rod cells have taken over.
Here is the wildest part: the image that lands on your retina is upside down. The lens flips it as it bends the light. A tree looks like it's growing downward. Your friend's face is turned on its head. So why doesn't the world look flipped?

items.prompt: According to the text, which happens first in the eye?

choices.text: Light reaches the lens.
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says light reaches the lens right after passing through the pupil, making it the first step described.

choices.text: Light lands on the retina.
choices.feedback: This happens later — light only lands on the retina after it has already passed through the lens first.

choices.text: The lens bends the light.
choices.feedback: The lens does bend the light, but it can only do that after the light has already reached it, so this is the second step, not the first.

choices.text: Rod cells sense the light.
choices.feedback: Rod cells sense the light at the very end of the journey, after the light has already traveled through the lens and landed on the retina.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Your Brain Finishes the Job

Your eyes alone cannot actually "see." They collect light and turn it into electrical signals — tiny bursts of energy, like messages zipping through a wire. Those signals race along the optic nerve, a cable made of over one million thin fibers that connects each eye to your brain. Your brain receives the signals, flips the upside-down image right-side up, adds color, and builds the picture you experience.

It happens right now as you read this word. And this one.

You didn't just see something. Your whole body worked together to build it for you.

Those signals race along the optic nerve, a cable made of over one million thin fibers that connects each eye to your brain. Your brain receives the signals, flips the upside-down image right-side up, adds color, and builds the picture you experience.
It happens right now as you read this word. And this one.
You didn't just see something. Your whole body worked together to build it for you.

items.prompt: The author compares electrical signals to "messages zipping through a wire." Based on this comparison, what is the meaning of "electrical signals" in the text?

choices.text: Fast bursts of energy that carry information
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage calls electrical signals 'tiny bursts of energy' that zip like messages, meaning they are fast and carry information.

choices.text: Thick cables of fibers that connect the eyes
choices.feedback: B is wrong because thick cables of fibers describe the optic nerve, not the electrical signals.

choices.text: Bright flashes of light that flip the picture
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the passage never says signals are bright flashes of light, and it's the brain — not the signals — that flips the image.

choices.text: Slow moving colors that build the final image
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the signals are described as fast (zipping), not slow, and it is the brain that adds color and builds the final picture.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: Based on the article, what is one difference between the cornea and the lens?

choices.text: The cornea is a front cover, but the lens is a flexible part.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage clearly says the cornea is a 'curved cover on the front of your eye' and the lens is a 'flexible part' — showing they are two different things with different descriptions.

choices.text: The cornea is a dark opening, but the lens is a colored curtain.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because a dark opening describes the pupil, and a colored curtain describes the iris — neither of those is the cornea or the lens.

choices.text: The cornea senses the dim light, but the lens sees the sharp details.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because sensing dim light is what rod cells do, and seeing sharp details is what cone cells do — those are cells in the retina, not the cornea or lens.

choices.text: The cornea flips the image over, but the lens turns it right-side up.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage says the lens flips the image upside down, and the brain is what turns the image right-side up — not the cornea or lens doing those opposite jobs.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: Which idea from the article best explains why you cannot see colors well in a dark room?

choices.text: The pupil closes tightly, so no light can enter the eye.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the article says your iris makes the pupil wider in a dim room to let in more light, not smaller or closed tightly.

choices.text: The cone cells need bright light, so rod cells do most of the work.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the article clearly states that cone cells need brighter light to show colors, and when it's dark, rod cells take over and only show gray shapes.

choices.text: The lens flips the picture, so colors turn upside down in the retina.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the lens flipping the image upside down is about the picture being inverted on the retina, not about colors disappearing in the dark.

choices.text: The optic nerve slows down, so color messages cannot reach the brain.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the article never mentions the optic nerve slowing down — it describes the optic nerve as a fast cable that carries signals to the brain.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: Which list of key details shows the correct order of what happens when you see?

choices.text: Light enters the pupil, the lens flips the picture upside down, and the brain turns it right-side up.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage clearly shows this order: light enters the pupil, the lens flips the image upside down, and then the brain turns it right-side up.

choices.text: The brain turns the picture right-side up, light enters the pupil, and the lens flips it upside down.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because it puts the brain's job first, but the brain can only flip the image after light has already traveled through the pupil and lens.

choices.text: Light enters the pupil, the brain flips the picture upside down, and the lens turns it right-side up.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because it says the brain flips the picture upside down, but the passage tells us it's the lens that flips it — the brain actually fixes it.

choices.text: Light enters the pupil, the cornea flips the picture upside down, and the retina turns it right-side up.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the passage says the lens flips the image upside down and the brain turns it right-side up, not the cornea and retina.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: Based on the article, how are the iris and the rod cells alike?

choices.text: Both are special parts that help you see things in dim light.
choices.feedback: This is correct! Both the iris (by opening the pupil wider) and rod cells help your eyes work better when the light is low or dim.

choices.text: Both are clear parts that bend light toward the back of the eye.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the clear parts that bend light are the cornea and lens — not the iris or rod cells.

choices.text: Both are colored parts that open and close like a window curtain.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because only the iris opens and closes like a curtain; rod cells are tiny sensing cells in the retina, not colored or curtain-like.

choices.text: Both are tiny cables that carry electrical signals straight to the brain.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the optic nerve is the cable that carries electrical signals to the brain — not the iris or rod cells.

syllabus_metadata.title: How Your Eyes See: The Science of Vision

assessments.title: Good Vibrations: What Makes Sound

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Every Sound Starts with a Shake

Vibrations You Can Feel

Press your fingers gently against your throat and hum. Feel that buzzing? That tiny shaking under your skin is called a vibration — a quick back-and-forth movement, like a ruler twanging on the edge of a desk. Every sound in the world begins exactly this way. A clap, a whisper, a thunderclap, a bird's song — each one starts when something vibrates.

When an object vibrates, it pushes the air around it. The air is made of tiny bits called particles — pieces so small you could never see even one. Think of particles like a crowd standing shoulder to shoulder. If someone bumps a person on one side, that person bumps the next, and the push travels through the whole crowd. Air particles work the same way, passing the vibration along in ripples called sound waves.
stimulus.content_text:
Every Sound Starts with a ShakeVibrations You Can Feel
Press your fingers gently against your throat and hum. Feel that buzzing? That tiny shaking under your skin is called a vibration — a quick back-and-forth movement, like a ruler twanging on the edge of a desk. Every sound in the world begins exactly this way. A clap, a whisper, a thunderclap, a bird's song — each one starts when something vibrates.
When an object vibrates, it pushes the air around it. The air is made of tiny bits called particles — pieces so small you could never see even one. Think of particles like a crowd standing shoulder to shoulder. If someone bumps a person on one side, that person bumps the next, and the push travels through the whole crowd. Air particles work the same way, passing the vibration along in ripples called sound waves.

items.prompt: Based on the text, how is a vibration different from a sound wave?

choices.text: A vibration is the movement passed along, while a sound wave is the ripple carrying it.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage says a vibration is a back-and-forth movement, and sound waves are the ripples that carry that movement through air particles.

choices.text: A vibration is a single tiny particle, while a sound wave is a large human crowd.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because a vibration is a type of movement, not a tiny particle — particles are the little bits of air that pass the vibration along.

choices.text: A vibration is a quiet whispering sound, while a sound wave is a loud thunder clap.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because whispering and thunder are examples of sounds that start with vibrations, not definitions of what vibrations or sound waves are.

choices.text: A vibration is a solid wooden object, while a sound wave is an empty air space.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage never says a vibration is a wooden object — it uses a ruler as an example of something that can vibrate, and sound waves travel through air, not empty space.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Riding the Wave

Picture dropping a pebble into a still pond. Ripples spread out in circles across the water. Sound waves spread the same way, except they move outward in every direction — up, down, left, right, and all around. When those invisible waves reach your ears, the thin skin inside called the eardrum vibrates too. Your brain reads those vibrations and turns them into the sounds you hear.

You can watch vibrations happen. Pluck a guitar string and look closely. The string blurs because it is shaking back and forth so fast. The moment the string stops moving, the sound stops too.
stimulus.content_text:
Riding the Wave
Picture dropping a pebble into a still pond. Ripples spread out in circles across the water. Sound waves spread the same way, except they move outward in every direction — up, down, left, right, and all around. When those invisible waves reach your ears, the thin skin inside called the eardrum vibrates too. Your brain reads those vibrations and turns them into the sounds you hear.
You can watch vibrations happen. Pluck a guitar string and look closely. The string blurs because it is shaking back and forth so fast. The moment the string stops moving, the sound stops too.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, why does the sound stop when a guitar string stops moving?

choices.text: because the waves stop making circles
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because circles describe how ripples move in a pond — the passage never says guitar strings make circles.

choices.text: because the string stops vibrating
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly says 'The moment the string stops moving, the sound stops too,' and moving back and forth is what vibrating means.

choices.text: because the eardrum stops the string
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the eardrum is inside your ear and only receives sound waves — it has nothing to do with stopping the guitar string.

choices.text: because the brain stops reading
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the brain stops hearing the sound after the string stops, but the string stopping its vibration is the actual cause of the sound ending.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
What Makes Sounds Different

Not all vibrations are the same. Some are fast and some are slow. When something vibrates quickly, it makes a high pitch — pitch means how high or low a sound is. A whistle has a high pitch. When something vibrates slowly, it makes a low pitch. A big drum has a low pitch.

Sounds also differ in volume, which means how loud or quiet a sound is. A strong vibration makes a loud sound. A gentle vibration makes a soft one. Imagine tapping a drum lightly, then hitting it hard. The hard hit shakes the drum more, so the sound is louder. Volume changes how loud a sound is, not how fast it moves.
stimulus.content_text:
What Makes Sounds Different
Not all vibrations are the same. Some are fast and some are slow. When something vibrates quickly, it makes a high pitch — pitch means how high or low a sound is. A whistle has a high pitch. When something vibrates slowly, it makes a low pitch. A big drum has a low pitch.
Sounds also differ in volume, which means how loud or quiet a sound is. A strong vibration makes a loud sound. A gentle vibration makes a soft one. Imagine tapping a drum lightly, then hitting it hard. The hard hit shakes the drum more, so the sound is louder. Volume changes how loud a sound is, not how fast it moves.

items.prompt: According to the passage, when you imagine the drum example, what do you do before you hit the drum hard?

choices.text: You listen to a whistle sound
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the whistle is only mentioned when talking about high pitch — it has nothing to do with the drum example.

choices.text: You tap the drum lightly
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage clearly says you imagine 'tapping a drum lightly' before you hit it hard.

choices.text: You shake the drum more
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because shaking the drum more is the effect of hitting it hard, not something you do beforehand.

choices.text: You make the pitch higher
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the drum example is about volume (how loud a sound is), not about making the pitch higher or lower.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Sound on the Move

Sound waves need something to travel through. They move through solids like wood, liquids like water, and gases like air. But they cannot travel through a vacuum — a completely empty space with no particles at all. Outer space is a vacuum, which means it is perfectly silent. No particles means no bumping, and no bumping means no sound.

Here is a surprising part: sound moves fastest through solids, slower through liquids, and slowest through air. Solid particles are packed tightly together, so vibrations jump quickly from one to the next. Try this: press your ear flat against a wooden table and ask a friend to tap the far end. You will hear the tap through the wood before you hear it through the air.
stimulus.content_text:

But they cannot travel through a vacuum — a completely empty space with no particles at all. Outer space is a vacuum, which means it is perfectly silent. No particles means no bumping, and no bumping means no sound.
Here is a surprising part: sound moves fastest through solids, slower through liquids, and slowest through air. Solid particles are packed tightly together, so vibrations jump quickly from one to the next. Try this: press your ear flat against a wooden table and ask a friend to tap the far end. You will hear the tap through the wood before you hear it through the air.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, how does the speed of sound through solids compare to the speed of sound through air?

choices.text: Sound moves slower through solids than through air.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage says the opposite — sound moves fastest through solids, not slower than air.

choices.text: Sound moves faster through solids than through air.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage clearly states that sound moves fastest through solids and slowest through air, and the table experiment shows this in action.

choices.text: Sound moves at the same speed through solids and air.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage explains that solids and air are different — sound moves at very different speeds through each one.

choices.text: Sound cannot move through solids or through air.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage tells us that sound can travel through both solids and air — it just moves at different speeds through each.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
A World Full of Shaking

Right now, vibrations are reaching your ears from all around — maybe a fan humming, a voice in another room, or a car passing outside. Each of those sounds follows the same path you just learned about: something vibrates, particles bump into each other, sound waves travel outward, and your eardrum picks them up.

Here is one more fact to remember: the human ear can hear about 400,000 different sounds. From the low rumble of thunder to the high chirp of a cricket, your ears and brain work together to sort them all out. Every single one of those sounds started with a shake.

A World Full of Shaking
Right now, vibrations are reaching your ears from all around — maybe a fan humming, a voice in another room, or a car passing outside. Each of those sounds follows the same path you just learned about: something vibrates, particles bump into each other, sound waves travel outward, and your eardrum picks them up.
Here is one more fact to remember: the human ear can hear about 400,000 different sounds. From the low rumble of thunder to the high chirp of a cricket, your ears and brain work together to sort them all out. Every single one of those sounds started with a shake.

items.prompt: In the section 'A World Full of Shaking,' what does the author mean by the word 'vibrations'?

choices.text: loud noises that hurt your ears
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because vibrations are not just loud noises that hurt your ears — even soft, gentle sounds like a fan humming start with vibrations.

choices.text: waves that move through water
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the passage describes sound waves traveling through the air around us, not waves moving through water.

choices.text: shaking that makes sound waves
choices.feedback: C is correct because the passage explains that something vibrates (shakes), which causes particles to bump together and sound waves to travel outward — and it says every sound 'started with a shake.'

choices.text: bumps that happen underground
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because underground bumps describe something like an earthquake, not the everyday vibrations that create the sounds we hear.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: Based on the article, how is a vacuum in outer space different from the air around us?

choices.text: Air has particles that carry sound, but a vacuum has no particles.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the article says air has particles that pass vibrations along, but a vacuum has no particles at all, so sound cannot travel through it.

choices.text: Air makes sounds travel very fast, but a vacuum makes them slow.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because a vacuum doesn't just slow sound down — without any particles, sound cannot travel through a vacuum at all.

choices.text: Air creates high pitches of sound, but a vacuum creates low pitches.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because pitch has nothing to do with air or vacuums; the article explains that pitch depends on how fast or slow something vibrates.

choices.text: Air stops vibrations from moving far, but a vacuum lets them spread.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because it has things backwards — air actually allows sound to spread outward, while a vacuum prevents sound from traveling because there are no particles.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: According to the article, what causes a loud sound to reach your brain?

choices.text: A strong vibration moves through particles to your eardrum.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the article tells us a strong vibration makes a loud sound, and vibrations travel through air particles to your eardrum so your brain can hear it.

choices.text: A fast vibration travels through a vacuum to your eardrum.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the article says sound cannot travel through a vacuum at all since there are no particles to bump into each other.

choices.text: A gentle vibration carries a low pitch toward your eardrum.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the article says a gentle vibration makes a soft, quiet sound — not a loud one.

choices.text: A slow vibration stops moving before reaching your eardrum.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because a slow vibration makes a low-pitched sound and still travels to your eardrum — it doesn't stop on the way.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: Based on the article, what happens right after an object vibrates but before your eardrum shakes?

choices.text: Air particles bump into each other.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the article says vibrating objects push air particles, which bump into each other like a crowd, sending sound waves to your eardrum — this happens right after the vibration and before your eardrum shakes.

choices.text: The brain turns waves into sounds.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the brain turning waves into sounds happens after your eardrum shakes, not before it.

choices.text: The object makes a very high pitch.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because high pitch describes how fast something vibrates, not a step in how sound travels from an object to your ear.

choices.text: A vacuum stops the sound completely.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because a vacuum stops sound from traveling at all, so it has nothing to do with the normal path sound takes to reach your eardrum.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: Based on the article, how are a guitar string and a drum alike in showing the main idea?

choices.text: They both must shake to make any sound.
choices.feedback: A is correct because both the guitar string and the drum must vibrate (shake) to make sound, which matches the article's main idea that every sound starts with a shake.

choices.text: They both make a very high and fast pitch.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the article says a big drum makes a low pitch, not a high one, so they do not both make a high pitch.

choices.text: They both move sound waves through a vacuum.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the article clearly states that sound cannot travel through a vacuum at all — no particles means no sound.

choices.text: They both stop shaking when a sound starts.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because it has it backwards — the article says the sound stops when the guitar string stops shaking, meaning shaking must continue for sound to keep going.

syllabus_metadata.title: Good Vibrations: What Makes Sound

assessments.title: High and Low: What Determines Pitch

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
High and Low: The Science of Pitch

What Pitch Is and How Vibrations Create It

Stretch a rubber band between your two thumbs. Pluck it. Now stretch it tighter and pluck again. That second sound is higher. You just changed the pitch — how high or low a sound is. The tighter band vibrated faster, and faster vibrations make higher sounds. You did not pluck harder. You changed the tightness. That tiny difference matters more than you might think.

Every sound starts with something vibrating — moving back and forth very quickly. When an object vibrates fast, it makes a high-pitched sound, like a whistle or a tiny flute called a piccolo. When an object vibrates slowly, it makes a low-pitched sound, like a big bass drum or a rumble of thunder.

A high-pitched bird chirp might vibrate thousands of times each second. A low lion's roar vibrates only a few hundred times per second. They call it frequency — the number of times an object vibrates in one second. Scientists measure frequency in units called hertz, written as Hz. A rope spinning ten times in one second has a higher frequency than one spinning twice.

High and Low: The Science of PitchWhat Pitch Is and How Vibrations Create It
Stretch a rubber band between your two thumbs. Pluck it. Now stretch it tighter and pluck again. That second sound is higher. You just changed the pitch — how high or low a sound is. The tighter band vibrated faster, and faster vibrations make higher sounds. You did not pluck harder. You changed the tightness. That tiny difference matters more than you might think.
Every sound starts with something vibrating — moving back and forth very quickly. When an object vibrates fast, it makes a high-pitched sound, like a whistle or a tiny flute called a piccolo. When an object vibrates slowly, it makes a low-pitched sound, like a big bass drum or a rumble of thunder.
A high-pitched bird chirp might vibrate thousands of times each second. A low lion's roar vibrates only a few hundred times per second. They call it frequency — the number of times an object vibrates in one second. Scientists measure frequency in units called hertz, written as Hz. A rope spinning ten times in one second has a higher frequency than one spinning twice.

items.prompt: According to the passage, how does a high-pitched sound differ from a low-pitched sound?

choices.text: A high-pitched sound is made by plucking harder than a low-pitched sound
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage clearly says plucking harder does NOT change the pitch — changing the tightness of the rubber band is what made the sound higher.

choices.text: A high-pitched sound is made by faster vibrations than a low-pitched sound
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage explains that faster vibrations create higher-pitched sounds, like a bird chirp vibrating thousands of times per second.

choices.text: A high-pitched sound is made by bigger objects than a low-pitched sound
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage never connects size to high pitch — in fact, it says a big bass drum makes a low-pitched sound.

choices.text: A high-pitched sound is made by fewer vibrations than a low-pitched sound
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because fewer vibrations per second means a LOWER pitch, not a higher one, as shown by the lion's roar example in the passage.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Pitch Is Not the Same as Loudness

You might think that a louder sound is automatically a higher sound. Actually, pitch and loudness are two separate things. Loudness — also called volume — is how soft or loud a sound is. You can whisper a high note. You can shout a low note. Hitting a drum harder makes it louder, but it does not change the pitch. To change the pitch, you need to change the size, tightness, or length of the thing that vibrates.
stimulus.content_text:
Pitch Is Not the Same as Loudness
You might think that a louder sound is automatically a higher sound. Actually, pitch and loudness are two separate things. Loudness — also called volume — is how soft or loud a sound is. You can whisper a high note. You can shout a low note. Hitting a drum harder makes it louder, but it does not change the pitch. To change the pitch, you need to change the size, tightness, or length of the thing that vibrates.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, why does hitting a drum harder NOT change its pitch?

choices.text: because hitting it harder only changes the size of the drum
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because hitting a drum harder does not change the size of the drum — the drum stays the same size no matter how hard you hit it.

choices.text: because hitting it harder only changes how loud the sound is
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage says hitting a drum harder only makes it louder (changes the volume), and loudness and pitch are two separate things.

choices.text: because hitting it harder makes the drum vibrate faster
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage never mentions that hitting harder makes the drum vibrate faster — that idea is not in the passage at all.

choices.text: because hitting it harder changes the tightness of the drum
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because hitting a drum harder does not change the tightness of the drum, and the passage says tightness is one of the things that would need to change to affect pitch.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
How Instruments and the Human Voice Change Pitch

Look at the strings on a guitar. The thin ones vibrate faster and play higher notes. The thick ones vibrate slower and play lower notes. Now watch a guitar player press a string down against the neck. That shortens the vibrating part of the string. A shorter string vibrates faster, so the note goes up. She changed the length.

Instruments are not the only things with pitch. Your voice has pitch, too. Inside your throat sit two small flaps called vocal cords — picture two tiny rubber bands side by side. When you talk or sing, air from your lungs rushes past them and makes them vibrate. Sing a high note, and your vocal cords pull tight and vibrate fast. Sing a low note, and they loosen and vibrate slowly. Press your fingers gently against your throat and hum from a low note to a high one. You can feel the change. The same idea works in animals: a small bird's vocal parts vibrate quickly, so it chirps high notes, while a lion's vocal parts are large and vibrate slowly, so it roars with a deep, low sound.
stimulus.content_text:
How Instruments and the Human Voice Change Pitch
Look at the strings on a guitar. The thin ones vibrate faster and play higher notes. The thick ones vibrate slower and play lower notes. Now watch a guitar player press a string down against the neck. That shortens the vibrating part of the string. A shorter string vibrates faster, so the note goes up. She changed the length.
Instruments are not the only things with pitch. Your voice has pitch, too. Inside your throat sit two small flaps called vocal cords — picture two tiny rubber bands side by side. When you talk or sing, air from your lungs rushes past them and makes them vibrate. Sing a high note, and your vocal cords pull tight and vibrate fast. Sing a low note, and they loosen and vibrate slowly. Press your fingers gently against your throat and hum from a low note to a high one. You can feel the change. The same idea works in animals: a small bird's vocal parts vibrate quickly, so it chirps high notes, while a lion's vocal parts are large and vibrate slowly, so it roars with a deep, low sound.

items.prompt: According to the text, what happens after a guitar player presses a string down against the neck?

choices.text: The string becomes thicker and the note goes lower.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because pressing the string doesn't make it thicker, and the passage says the note goes up, not lower.

choices.text: The vibrating part gets shorter and the note goes up.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly states that pressing the string shortens the vibrating part, and a shorter string vibrates faster so the note goes up.

choices.text: The string vibrates slower and the sound gets softer.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage says a shorter string vibrates faster, not slower, and nothing is mentioned about the sound getting softer.

choices.text: The guitar neck gets longer and the note stays the same.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the neck doesn't get longer, and the note does change — it goes higher, not the same.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
What Your Ears Can Hear and Why Pitch Matters

Human ears pick up sounds between about 20 Hz and 20,000 Hz. Sounds below 20 Hz are too low for us. Sounds above 20,000 Hz are too high. That is why a dog whistle seems silent to you, yet your dog comes running.

Pitch turns a flat, boring hum into a melody you can remember, a voice you can recognize, and a world full of sounds worth hearing. When notes move up and down, the distance between them is what gives a song its shape. Your rubber band already proved it — one small change in vibration, and everything sounds different.

What Your Ears Can Hear and Why Pitch Matters
Human ears pick up sounds between about 20 Hz and 20,000 Hz. Sounds below 20 Hz are too low for us. Sounds above 20,000 Hz are too high. That is why a dog whistle seems silent to you, yet your dog comes running.
Pitch turns a flat, boring hum into a melody you can remember, a voice you can recognize, and a world full of sounds worth hearing. When notes move up and down, the distance between them is what gives a song its shape. Your rubber band already proved it — one small change in vibration, and everything sounds different.

choices.text: They are both sounds that dogs and people hear the same way.

choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the article talks about rubber bands and vocal cords too, not just instruments played by musicians.

assessments.title: Loud and Soft: Understanding Volume and Amplitude

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Big Vibrations, Loud Sounds

A Drum Hit That Shakes the Air

A boy slams his hand down on a drum. Across the room, his little sister covers her ears. Now he taps the drum with one finger. The drumhead barely moves. His sister doesn't even look up.

Same drum. Same room. Same boy. The only difference is how hard he hit it. That difference has a name: volume — how loud or soft a sound is. And volume depends on something called amplitude.
stimulus.content_text:
Big Vibrations, Loud SoundsA Drum Hit That Shakes the Air
A boy slams his hand down on a drum. Across the room, his little sister covers her ears. Now he taps the drum with one finger. The drumhead barely moves. His sister doesn't even look up.
Same drum. Same room. Same boy. The only difference is how hard he hit it. That difference has a name: volume — how loud or soft a sound is. And volume depends on something called amplitude.

items.prompt: According to the passage, when the boy plays the drum, how is his sister's reaction to a hard hit different from her reaction to a soft tap?

choices.text: She covers her ears for the hard hit, but she does not look up for the soft tap.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage clearly says she covers her ears for the hard hit and doesn't look up for the soft tap — in that order!

choices.text: She does not look up for the hard hit, but she covers her ears for the soft tap.
choices.feedback: This is backwards — the passage says she covers her ears for the HARD hit, not the soft tap.

choices.text: She watches the drumhead for the hard hit, but she touches the drum for the soft tap.
choices.feedback: This is made up — the passage never says the sister watches the drumhead or touches the drum at all.

choices.text: She leaves the room for the hard hit, but she stays in the room for the soft tap.
choices.feedback: This is also made up — the passage never says the sister leaves the room for any reason.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
What Amplitude Looks Like

When you hit a drum hard, the drumhead vibrates — it moves back and forth quickly, like a trampoline after a big jump. Those big movements push hard on the air molecules nearby. The molecules bump into their neighbors, and those neighbors bump into the next ones. This chain of bumps travels outward as a sound wave.

A hard hit makes large vibrations and a tall sound wave. A gentle tap makes small vibrations and a short sound wave. Think of pushing a friend on a swing. A strong push sends the swing soaring high. More energy means a louder sound.

You might think that hitting a drum harder also changes the note it plays. Actually, it doesn't. The note — whether a sound is high like a whistle or low like a tuba — is called pitch. Pitch and volume are two different things. Smacking a drum harder makes it louder, but it still plays the same note.

What Amplitude Looks Like

The molecules bump into their neighbors, and those neighbors bump into the next ones. This chain of bumps travels outward as a sound wave.
A hard hit makes large vibrations and a tall sound wave. A gentle tap makes small vibrations and a short sound wave. Think of pushing a friend on a swing. A strong push sends the swing soaring high. More energy means a louder sound.
You might think that hitting a drum harder also changes the note it plays. Actually, it doesn't. The note — whether a sound is high like a whistle or low like a tuba — is called pitch. Pitch and volume are two different things. Smacking a drum harder makes it louder, but it still plays the same note.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, why does hitting a drum harder make a louder sound?

choices.text: because the hard hit changes the note the drum plays
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage clearly says that hitting a drum harder makes it louder but it still plays the same note — the note does not change.

choices.text: because the hard hit puts more energy into larger vibrations
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage explains that a hard hit creates large vibrations and more energy, and more energy means a louder sound.

choices.text: because the hard hit sends the sound wave in a new direction
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage never mentions the direction of the sound wave changing when you hit the drum harder.

choices.text: because the hard hit makes the air molecules heavier than before
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage says air molecules bump into each other to carry the sound, but nothing in the passage says the molecules get heavier.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Measuring Volume in Decibels

Scientists measure volume in units called decibels, written as dB. A whisper is about 20 dB. A normal conversation is about 60 dB. A lawn mower roars at about 90 dB. A rock concert can hit 120 dB. A jet engine blasts at about 140 dB — so loud it hurts your ears without protection.

Sounds above 85 dB can damage your hearing over time. That is about as loud as heavy traffic. Louder sounds can hurt your ears even faster. That is why workers near loud machines wear special earplugs or headphones. It is also why you should keep your headphone volume low when you listen to music. Your ears do not heal the way a cut on your skin does. Once the tiny parts inside your ear are damaged, they stay damaged.

Sounds above 85 dB can damage your hearing over time. That is about as loud as heavy traffic. Louder sounds can hurt your ears even faster. That is why workers near loud machines wear special earplugs or headphones. It is also why you should keep your headphone volume low when you listen to music. Your ears do not heal the way a cut on your skin does. Once the tiny parts inside your ear are damaged, they stay damaged.

items.prompt: According to the text, which list shows sounds in order from softest to loudest?

choices.text: whisper, lawn mower, conversation, jet engine
choices.feedback: Answer A is wrong because it puts the lawn mower (90 dB) before the conversation (60 dB), but the conversation is actually softer than the lawn mower.

choices.text: whisper, conversation, lawn mower, jet engine
choices.feedback: Answer B is correct because it lists the sounds in the exact order of their decibel numbers from smallest to largest: 20 dB, 60 dB, 90 dB, and 140 dB.

choices.text: conversation, whisper, lawn mower, jet engine
choices.feedback: Answer C is wrong because it puts the conversation (60 dB) before the whisper (20 dB), but a whisper is softer and should come first.

choices.text: lawn mower, conversation, whisper, jet engine
choices.feedback: Answer D is wrong because it starts with the lawn mower (90 dB), which is much louder than both a conversation and a whisper, so it definitely should not be first.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Why Sounds Fade with Distance

Picture an ambulance racing down your street, siren screaming. It sounds very loud when it passes your house. But as it drives away, the siren gets softer and softer. The sound is not disappearing. The sound wave spreads out in every direction, like a ripple in a pond. The farther the ripple travels, the more it stretches and thins. The energy spreads over a bigger area, so less of it reaches your ears.

A whisper and a shout both travel through air at the same speed. A loud sound does not move faster than a quiet one. The difference is only in how much energy the wave carries, not how quickly it moves.

The energy spreads over a bigger area, so less of it reaches your ears.
A whisper and a shout both travel through air at the same speed. A loud sound does not move faster than a quiet one. The difference is only in how much energy the wave carries, not how quickly it moves.

items.prompt: The author compares a "sound wave" to a ripple in a pond. Based on this comparison, what does the phrase "sound wave" mean?

choices.text: A form of sound energy that spreads in all directions.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage compares a sound wave to a pond ripple that spreads outward in every direction, carrying energy with it.

choices.text: A form of sound energy that travels faster than whispers.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the passage says a loud sound does not move faster than a quiet one — they travel at the same speed.

choices.text: A form of sound energy that completely disappears over time.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the passage directly says 'the sound is not disappearing' — it just spreads over a bigger area.

choices.text: A form of sound energy that stays in one exact location.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the ripple comparison shows that sound moves outward in all directions, not that it stays in one spot.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Your Built-In Volume Knob

You change your own volume all day long. You whisper in the library. You cheer at a ball game. You speak softly to a baby and call across the street to a friend. Each time, your body pushes air over your vocal cords — two small flaps inside your throat that vibrate to make your voice. Push air harder, and the vibrations grow. The amplitude rises. Your voice gets loud.

You are not just hearing noise — you are feeling energy that traveled through the air to reach you.

Your voice gets loud.
You are not just hearing noise — you are feeling energy that traveled through the air to reach you.

choices.text: the sound you make when you whisper
choices.feedback: Whispering is one way you use your voice, but it is not the definition of vocal cords.

choices.text: because cheering makes your vocal cords vibrate at a higher pitch
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because pitch means how high or low a sound is, like a whistle versus a tuba, and has nothing to do with how loud your voice gets.

choices.text: because cheering pushes more air and makes bigger vibrations
choices.feedback: B is correct because the article explains that pushing more air over your vocal cords makes bigger vibrations and higher amplitude, which is what makes your voice louder.

choices.text: because cheering makes the sound travel faster through the air

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: Based on the article, why does hitting a drum harder make a louder sound?

choices.text: A harder hit makes bigger vibrations that push more energy through the air.

choices.text: Both need larger vibrations to add energy.

assessments.title: Catching Sound Waves: How Your Ear Works

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
How Your Ears Catch Every Sound

A Clap That Reaches Your Brain

In less time than it takes to blink, that sharp sound traveled through the air, entered your ear, bounced through tiny bones, rippled through liquid, and arrived in your brain as a message: clap. All of that happened before your palms even stopped stinging.

The clap already existed as waves moving through the air, like ripples spreading across a pond. Your ear's job is to catch those waves and turn them into something your brain can understand. It does this in three steps, using three parts.
stimulus.content_text:
How Your Ears Catch Every SoundA Clap That Reaches Your Brain
In less time than it takes to blink, that sharp sound traveled through the air, entered your ear, bounced through tiny bones, rippled through liquid, and arrived in your brain as a message: clap. All of that happened before your palms even stopped stinging.
The clap already existed as waves moving through the air, like ripples spreading across a pond. Your ear's job is to catch those waves and turn them into something your brain can understand. It does this in three steps, using three parts.

items.prompt: According to the text, how is the clap different in the air compared to when it reaches the brain?

choices.text: It is a loud noise in the air but a quiet noise in the brain.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage never says anything about the clap being loud in the air and quiet in the brain — that idea isn't in the text at all.

choices.text: It is waves in the air but a message in the brain.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage clearly says the clap travels as waves through the air and then arrives in the brain as a message.

choices.text: It is a ripple in the air but a wave in the brain.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage says the clap is waves in the air (not ripples), and it becomes a message in the brain (not a wave) — this answer has both parts mixed up.

choices.text: It is a message in the air but waves in the brain.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because it has it completely backwards — the passage says waves come first (in the air) and the message comes second (in the brain), not the other way around.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
The Outer Ear: A Sound Catcher

The outer ear is the part you can see on the side of your head. It curves like a funnel, and that shape matters. It scoops up sound waves from the air and steers them into a narrow tube called the ear canal. Think of cupping your hand behind your ear to hear better. Your outer ear does that all the time.

At the end of the ear canal sits a thin, stretched piece of skin called the eardrum. When sound waves hit it, the eardrum shakes back and forth — it vibrates, just like the skin of a drum vibrates when you tap it with a stick.
stimulus.content_text:
The Outer Ear: A Sound Catcher
The outer ear is the part you can see on the side of your head. It curves like a funnel, and that shape matters. It scoops up sound waves from the air and steers them into a narrow tube called the ear canal. Think of cupping your hand behind your ear to hear better. Your outer ear does that all the time.
At the end of the ear canal sits a thin, stretched piece of skin called the eardrum. When sound waves hit it, the eardrum shakes back and forth — it vibrates, just like the skin of a drum vibrates when you tap it with a stick.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, why does the eardrum vibrate when you hear a sound?

choices.text: because the ear canal pushes air out of the ear
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage never says the ear canal pushes air out — it says the ear canal carries sound waves toward the eardrum.

choices.text: because sound waves travel in and hit the eardrum
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly says sound waves hit the eardrum and make it vibrate, just like tapping a drum with a stick.

choices.text: because the outer ear shakes back and forth quickly
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage says the eardrum vibrates, not the outer ear — the outer ear's job is to scoop up and steer sound waves inward.

choices.text: because the eardrum sends sound waves into the air
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the eardrum vibrates when sound waves come in and hit it, not by sending sound waves back out into the air.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
The Middle Ear: Three Tiny Bones

Behind the eardrum hides a small space holding three of the tiniest bones in your whole body. They are called the hammer, the anvil, and the stirrup because of their shapes. Lined up in a row, all three could fit on a penny.

When the eardrum vibrates, it shakes the hammer. The hammer passes that vibration to the anvil, and the anvil passes it to the stirrup. Each bone amplifies the vibration — meaning it makes the vibration stronger, like a friend pushing you higher on a swing. By the time the vibration reaches the stirrup, it is much more powerful than when it started.

Actually, a whisper and a shout travel through air at the same speed. The middle ear bones make the vibration bigger, not quicker, and bigger vibrations are what your brain reads as louder sounds.
stimulus.content_text:
The Middle Ear: Three Tiny Bones
Behind the eardrum hides a small space holding three of the tiniest bones in your whole body. They are called the hammer, the anvil, and the stirrup because of their shapes. Lined up in a row, all three could fit on a penny.
When the eardrum vibrates, it shakes the hammer. The hammer passes that vibration to the anvil, and the anvil passes it to the stirrup. Each bone amplifies the vibration — meaning it makes the vibration stronger, like a friend pushing you higher on a swing. By the time the vibration reaches the stirrup, it is much more powerful than when it started.
Actually, a whisper and a shout travel through air at the same speed. The middle ear bones make the vibration bigger, not quicker, and bigger vibrations are what your brain reads as louder sounds.

items.prompt: According to the passage, what happens right after the hammer shakes?

choices.text: The eardrum starts to vibrate
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the eardrum vibrating is what starts the whole chain — it happens before the hammer shakes, not after.

choices.text: The anvil receives the vibration
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage says the hammer passes its vibration to the anvil, which is the very next step after the hammer shakes.

choices.text: The stirrup receives the vibration
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the stirrup comes after the anvil — the vibration must pass through the anvil first before it ever reaches the stirrup.

choices.text: The brain reads a louder sound
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the brain reading a louder sound is the last thing that happens, not what comes right after the hammer shakes.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
The Inner Ear: Where Vibrations Become Messages

The stirrup presses against a small, snail-shaped organ called the cochlea. The cochlea is filled with liquid and lined with thousands of tiny hair-like cells. When the stirrup pushes, it sends waves rippling through that liquid — sound does not only travel through air, but through liquid, metal, and wood, too.

Those waves bend the tiny hair cells. Different hair cells respond to different pitches — the highness or lowness of a sound. Some hair cells pick up a bird's high tweet. Others pick up a low rumble of thunder. When the hair cells bend, they create electrical signals, tiny bursts of energy like messages sent along a wire.

Those signals race along the auditory nerve, a bundle of fibers that connects your ear to your brain. Your brain reads the signals and figures out what you are hearing. Music? A voice? A car horn? Your brain can tell the difference between thousands of sounds in a split second.

The Inner Ear: Where Vibrations Become Messages
