When the stirrup pushes, it sends waves rippling through that liquid — sound does not only travel through air, but through liquid, metal, and wood, too.
Those waves bend the tiny hair cells. Different hair cells respond to different pitches — the highness or lowness of a sound. Some hair cells pick up a bird's high tweet. Others pick up a low rumble of thunder. When the hair cells bend, they create electrical signals, tiny bursts of energy like messages sent along a wire.
Those signals race along the auditory nerve, a bundle of fibers that connects your ear to your brain. Your brain reads the signals and figures out what you are hearing. Music? A voice? A car horn? Your brain can tell the difference between thousands of sounds in a split second.

items.prompt: The article compares electrical signals to 'messages sent along a wire.' Based on this comparison, what does the phrase 'electrical signals' most likely mean?

choices.text: tiny bursts of energy that carry information
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage describes electrical signals as 'tiny bursts of energy' that act like messages, meaning they carry information — just like answer A says.

choices.text: small waves that ripple through liquid
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because waves rippling through liquid happen in the cochlea, which is a different step that comes before the electrical signals are made.

choices.text: loud sounds that travel through the air
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because electrical signals are not sounds traveling through air — they are energy bursts inside your ear and brain.

choices.text: thin fibers that connect parts of the body
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because thin fibers that connect parts of the body describes the auditory nerve, not the electrical signals that travel through it.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Protecting the Sound Catchers

Those hair cells inside the cochlea are powerful but fragile. Very loud sounds can flatten them, and once they are damaged, they never grow back. That is why wearing ear protection at loud events matters. Keeping your headphone volume at a safe level helps, too.

They are detectors — and they are working right now, catching sounds you have not even noticed yet.
stimulus.content_text:
Protecting the Sound Catchers
Those hair cells inside the cochlea are powerful but fragile. Very loud sounds can flatten them, and once they are damaged, they never grow back. That is why wearing ear protection at loud events matters. Keeping your headphone volume at a safe level helps, too.
They are detectors — and they are working right now, catching sounds you have not even noticed yet.

items.prompt: The passage describes the hair cells as 'detectors.' Based on the text, what is the meaning of a detector?

choices.text: Something that catches sounds
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage says the hair cells are 'catching sounds you have not even noticed yet,' which tells us a detector is something that catches sounds.

choices.text: Something that flattens the cells
choices.feedback: B is wrong because flattening the hair cells is what loud sounds do to them — that's the damage, not the meaning of detector.

choices.text: Something that fixes the damage
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the passage says damaged hair cells never grow back, so nothing in the passage fixes them.

choices.text: Something that creates loud noises
choices.feedback: D is wrong because hair cells pick up sounds that are already there — they don't make loud noises themselves.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: Based on the article, how is the way sound waves travel in the outer ear different from how they travel in the inner ear?

choices.text: They travel through air in the outer ear, but they travel through liquid in the inner ear.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the article says sound waves travel through air in the outer ear and through liquid inside the cochlea in the inner ear.

choices.text: They travel through bones in the outer ear, but they travel through air in the inner ear.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the tiny bones (hammer, anvil, stirrup) are in the middle ear, not the outer ear.

choices.text: They travel through liquid in the outer ear, but they travel through bones in the inner ear.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because it has the outer and inner ear switched — the outer ear has air, and the inner ear has liquid.

choices.text: They travel through nerves in the outer ear, but they travel through skin in the inner ear.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the auditory nerve carries electrical signals to the brain, and skin (the eardrum) is part of the outer ear, so neither one describes how sound travels through both the outer and inner ear.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: According to the article, what chain of events causes the hair cells to create electrical signals?

choices.text: The outer ear scoops air, the eardrum stops, and the brain reads the pitches.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the eardrum doesn't stop — it vibrates — and the brain reads electrical signals, not pitches directly from the outer ear.

choices.text: The eardrum vibrates, the tiny bones shake, and the cochlea's liquid ripples.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage clearly shows this order: the eardrum vibrates, the tiny bones pass and strengthen that vibration, and the cochlea's liquid ripples to bend the hair cells and create electrical signals.

choices.text: The auditory nerve bends, the cochlea shakes, and the eardrum makes the sound.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because it has the steps backwards — the auditory nerve carries signals to the brain at the end of the process, not at the beginning.

choices.text: The brain sends signals, the tiny bones push, and the ear canal catches sound.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the brain receives signals rather than sending them to start hearing, so the whole chain is described in the wrong direction.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: How do the headings 'The Outer Ear,' 'The Middle Ear,' and 'The Inner Ear' help you understand the sequence of hearing?

choices.text: They show the path a sound wave takes as it travels toward the brain.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the headings follow the exact route a sound wave travels — from outside your head, through the middle ear bones, and into the inner ear on its way to your brain.

choices.text: They list the ear parts from the most important to the least important.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the passage never says any one ear part is more important than the others — all three parts work together as a team.

choices.text: They explain the steps you should take to protect your hearing from noise.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the headings describe how each part of the ear works, not steps you should take to protect your hearing.

choices.text: They describe the different speeds at which sounds move through the air.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage actually tells us that a whisper and a shout travel through air at the same speed, and the headings say nothing about how fast sounds move.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: The main idea of the article is that the ear turns sound waves into details the brain can understand. Based on the text, how do the jobs of the middle ear and the inner ear compare to support this idea?

choices.text: The middle ear changes how loud a sound is, while the inner ear picks up the pitch of a sound.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage says the middle ear bones make vibrations bigger (louder), while the inner ear's hair cells detect different pitches like high tweets or low thunder rumbles.

choices.text: The middle ear protects the ear from loud noises, while the inner ear makes the sounds travel much faster.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the middle ear amplifies vibrations to make them stronger — it does not protect the ear from loud noises.

choices.text: The middle ear turns the sound into liquid waves, while the inner ear turns the sound into air waves.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because it's the stirrup in the middle ear pressing on the cochlea that creates liquid waves, and that happens at the start of the inner ear's job, not the middle ear's.

choices.text: The middle ear sends messages directly to the brain, while the inner ear catches the waves from the air.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the middle ear passes vibrations through tiny bones to the inner ear — it does not send messages directly to the brain.

syllabus_metadata.title: Catching Sound Waves: How Your Ear Works

assessments.title: Living Together: What Is an Ecosystem?

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Hidden World Inside a Rotting Log

A Tiny World Under Your Feet

A fallen log lies on the forest floor, covered in green moss. Lift the bark, and life explodes. Beetles scurry. Pale mushrooms push through the damp wood. Tiny worms twist through soft, crumbling pieces. This rotting log is an ecosystem — all the living and non-living things in one area, working together like teammates on the same field.

You might think an ecosystem has to be something huge, like a rain forest or an ocean. Actually, ecosystems come in every size. A desert that stretches for hundreds of miles is an ecosystem. So is a puddle in your backyard, where tiny organisms — living things too small to see without help — swim and feed. Even your schoolyard counts. The grass, the ants, the soil, and the rain all connect there.
stimulus.content_text:
The Hidden World Inside a Rotting LogA Tiny World Under Your Feet
A fallen log lies on the forest floor, covered in green moss. Lift the bark, and life explodes. Beetles scurry. Pale mushrooms push through the damp wood. Tiny worms twist through soft, crumbling pieces. This rotting log is an ecosystem — all the living and non-living things in one area, working together like teammates on the same field.

You might think an ecosystem has to be something huge, like a rain forest or an ocean. Actually, ecosystems come in every size. A desert that stretches for hundreds of miles is an ecosystem. So is a puddle in your backyard, where tiny organisms — living things too small to see without help — swim and feed. Even your schoolyard counts. The grass, the ants, the soil, and the rain all connect there.

items.prompt: According to the passage, how is a backyard puddle different from a desert as an ecosystem?

choices.text: A puddle has organisms but a desert does not
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says the desert is an ecosystem, which means it has organisms (living things) too.

choices.text: A puddle is tiny but a desert stretches for miles
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage says a desert 'stretches for hundreds of miles' while a puddle is small enough to be in your backyard, showing they are very different in size.

choices.text: A puddle is an ecosystem but a desert is not
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage clearly states that a desert IS an ecosystem, just like a puddle.

choices.text: A puddle has non-living things but a desert does not
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because all ecosystems include non-living things, and the passage never says the desert is missing them.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Living and Non-Living Partners

Every ecosystem has two kinds of parts. The living parts are called biotic factors. Think of "bio" as meaning "life." Plants, animals, insects, and even fungi — living things like mushrooms and mold that break down dead material — are all biotic. So are bacteria, creatures so tiny that millions of them fit on the tip of a pencil. You cannot see bacteria, but they live in soil, water, and air all around you.

The non-living parts are called abiotic factors. Water, sunlight, air, soil, and temperature are abiotic. Here is the surprising part: these non-living things matter just as much as the living ones. Without sunlight, plants could not turn light into food inside their leaves. Without water, seeds could not sprout. Without soil, roots would have nowhere to grip. Living things depend on non-living things every single day.
stimulus.content_text:
Living and Non-Living Partners
Every ecosystem has two kinds of parts. The living parts are called biotic factors. Think of "bio" as meaning "life." Plants, animals, insects, and even fungi — living things like mushrooms and mold that break down dead material — are all biotic. So are bacteria, creatures so tiny that millions of them fit on the tip of a pencil. You cannot see bacteria, but they live in soil, water, and air all around you.

The non-living parts are called abiotic factors. Water, sunlight, air, soil, and temperature are abiotic. Here is the surprising part: these non-living things matter just as much as the living ones. Without sunlight, plants could not turn light into food inside their leaves. Without water, seeds could not sprout. Without soil, roots would have nowhere to grip. Living things depend on non-living things every single day.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, why does the author say non-living things matter just as much as living ones?

choices.text: because non-living things like soil and water created all living things long ago
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage never says non-living things like soil and water created living things — it only says living things depend on non-living things today.

choices.text: because living things need non-living things to grow and survive
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage gives clear examples showing that plants, seeds, and roots all need non-living things like sunlight, water, and soil to grow and survive.

choices.text: because non-living things can turn into living things over time
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage never says non-living things can turn into living things over time.

choices.text: because living things give energy back to non-living things each day
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage never mentions living things sending energy back to non-living things — it only goes the other way around.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Every Living Thing Has a Job

Each organism in an ecosystem has its own niche — a special role, like a position on a soccer team. A goalkeeper and a striker both play soccer, but they do very different jobs. In a forest, bees pollinate flowers by carrying dusty pollen from bloom to bloom so plants can make seeds. Worms chew through dead leaves and mix nutrients — the vitamins and minerals that help plants grow — back into the soil. Trees give shade and shelter to birds and squirrels. Every organism's niche is different, and every one matters.
stimulus.content_text:
Every Living Thing Has a Job
Each organism in an ecosystem has its own niche — a special role, like a position on a soccer team. A goalkeeper and a striker both play soccer, but they do very different jobs. In a forest, bees pollinate flowers by carrying dusty pollen from bloom to bloom so plants can make seeds. Worms chew through dead leaves and mix nutrients — the vitamins and minerals that help plants grow — back into the soil. Trees give shade and shelter to birds and squirrels. Every organism's niche is different, and every one matters.

items.prompt: According to the text, what do worms do before they mix nutrients back into the soil?

choices.text: They carry dusty pollen to flowers.
choices.feedback: This is what bees do — the passage says bees carry dusty pollen from flower to flower, not worms.

choices.text: They chew through dead leaves.
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says worms 'chew through dead leaves' before they mix nutrients back into the soil.

choices.text: They make new seeds for plants.
choices.feedback: Making seeds is part of what happens when bees pollinate flowers, not something worms do at all.

choices.text: They take vitamins from the dirt.
choices.feedback: This gets it backwards — worms mix nutrients back INTO the soil, they don't take vitamins out of it.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
When One Piece Changes, Everything Feels It

Knock one piece over, and the others fall too.

In Yellowstone National Park, wolves were removed in the 1920s. Without wolves hunting deer, the deer population grew and grew. The deer ate so many young trees and bushes that riverbanks lost their plants. Without roots holding the soil, the riverbanks began to wash away. Birds that nested in those bushes lost their homes. One missing animal changed the water, the soil, the plants, and dozens of other species.

A drought — a long stretch of time with no rain — can cause the same kind of chain reaction. If a pond dries up, fish die. Birds that ate those fish lose their food. Insects that the birds used to eat multiply fast. One change ripples outward like a stone dropped in water.
stimulus.content_text:
When One Piece Changes, Everything Feels It
Knock one piece over, and the others fall too.

In Yellowstone National Park, wolves were removed in the 1920s. Without wolves hunting deer, the deer population grew and grew. The deer ate so many young trees and bushes that riverbanks lost their plants. Without roots holding the soil, the riverbanks began to wash away. Birds that nested in those bushes lost their homes. One missing animal changed the water, the soil, the plants, and dozens of other species.

A drought — a long stretch of time with no rain — can cause the same kind of chain reaction. If a pond dries up, fish die. Birds that ate those fish lose their food. Insects that the birds used to eat multiply fast. One change ripples outward like a stone dropped in water.

items.prompt: How is removing wolves from Yellowstone similar to a drought drying up a pond?

choices.text: Both changes helped plants and animals grow stronger over time
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because both the wolf removal and the drought hurt plants and animals — they did not help them grow stronger.

choices.text: Both changes caused a chain reaction that hurt many living things
choices.feedback: B is correct because both events started a chain reaction where one change led to many other living things being hurt, just like a stone dropping in water and making ripples.

choices.text: Both changes only affected one type of animal in the ecosystem
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because both changes affected many different species — plants, birds, fish, insects, and more — not just one type of animal.

choices.text: Both changes were caused by people making bad choices on purpose
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because a drought is a natural event with no rain, not a choice that people made on purpose.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
You Are Standing in an Ecosystem Right Now

Scientists study these connections carefully so they can protect them. They track which organisms depend on which, and what happens when abiotic factors like temperature or rainfall shift.

The birds, the breeze, the rain puddle on the sidewalk — none of them exist alone. You are standing inside a web of connections, and every thread holds the others in place.
stimulus.content_text:
You Are Standing in an Ecosystem Right Now
Scientists study these connections carefully so they can protect them. They track which organisms depend on which, and what happens when abiotic factors like temperature or rainfall shift.

The birds, the breeze, the rain puddle on the sidewalk — none of them exist alone. You are standing inside a web of connections, and every thread holds the others in place.

items.prompt: According to the text, what are "abiotic factors"?

choices.text: Things like temperature or rainfall
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly says 'abiotic factors like temperature or rainfall,' giving us the definition right in the sentence.

choices.text: Living things like birds or bugs
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because birds and bugs are living things, and the passage describes abiotic factors as things like temperature and rainfall, not living creatures.

choices.text: People like scientists or teachers
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because scientists and teachers are people, and the passage never calls people abiotic factors.

choices.text: Places like sidewalks or streets
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because sidewalks and streets are places, not the temperature or rainfall examples the passage uses to explain abiotic factors.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: Based on the article, how is the job of a mushroom different from the job of a bee?

choices.text: A mushroom breaks down dead material, while a bee helps plants make seeds.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the article clearly says fungi like mushrooms break down dead material, and bees carry pollen so plants can make seeds.

choices.text: A mushroom gives shade to small animals, while a bee mixes the soil.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the article says trees give shade and worms mix nutrients into the soil — not mushrooms and bees.

choices.text: A mushroom hunts for other insects, while a bee makes food from sunlight.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because mushrooms don't hunt insects, and making food from sunlight is something plants do, not bees.

choices.text: A mushroom drinks water from puddles, while a bee lives inside rotting logs.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the article never mentions mushrooms drinking from puddles or bees living inside rotting logs.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: Which two headings show where to find information about how non-living things cause changes for living things?

choices.text: "A Tiny World Under Your Feet" and "Every Living Thing Has a Job"
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because 'A Tiny World Under Your Feet' introduces ecosystems and 'Every Living Thing Has a Job' is about the special roles organisms play, not about non-living things causing changes.

choices.text: "Living and Non-Living Partners" and "When One Piece Changes, Everything Feels It"
choices.feedback: B is correct because 'Living and Non-Living Partners' explains how non-living things like water and sunlight affect living things, and 'When One Piece Changes, Everything Feels It' shows how a drought (a non-living change) caused a chain reaction for fish, birds, and insects.

choices.text: "Every Living Thing Has a Job" and "You Are Standing in an Ecosystem Right Now"
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because 'Every Living Thing Has a Job' is about niches like pollinating flowers, and 'You Are Standing in an Ecosystem Right Now' is about scientists studying connections, not about non-living things causing changes.

choices.text: "A Tiny World Under Your Feet" and "You Are Standing in an Ecosystem Right Now"
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because 'A Tiny World Under Your Feet' only introduces what an ecosystem is and gives examples of different sizes, not how non-living things cause changes for living things.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: How does the author order the details to support the main idea that everything in an ecosystem is connected?

choices.text: First, by explaining how a pond dries up, and then by describing the special jobs of different insects.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage doesn't begin with the pond drying up — that example appears near the end, not at the start.

choices.text: First, by listing the parts of an ecosystem, and then by showing how losing one part hurts the rest.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the author first describes the living and non-living parts of an ecosystem, and then uses examples like the Yellowstone wolves to show how losing one part harms everything else.

choices.text: First, by describing a huge rain forest, and then by explaining how to build a new backyard puddle.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the author never explains how to build a backyard puddle, and the rain forest is only mentioned briefly as one example of a big ecosystem.

choices.text: First, by showing how animals hunt for food, and then by listing the different types of forest weather.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage is not organized around animals hunting or types of forest weather — those are not the main topics covered.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: Based on the article, how is an ecosystem similar to a soccer team?

choices.text: Each part has a special job to help the whole group.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the article directly compares a niche to a position on a soccer team, showing that each part has a special job that helps the whole ecosystem work.

choices.text: Each part needs sunlight and water to stay alive.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because sunlight and water are abiotic (non-living) factors, and the comparison to a soccer team is about roles and jobs, not what living things need to survive.

choices.text: Each part moves from place to place to find food.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the article never mentions ecosystem parts moving from place to place to find food as something they all have in common.

choices.text: Each part grows much larger when the weather changes.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the article does not say that parts of an ecosystem grow larger when the weather changes — that idea is never part of the soccer team comparison.

syllabus_metadata.title: Living Together: What Is an Ecosystem?

assessments.title: Home Sweet Habitat: Where Living Things Thrive

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Home Sweet Habitat

A Frog in the Wrong Place

Picture a tiny tree frog sitting on a sand dune in a scorching desert. No pond. No shade. No insects buzzing near the water. That frog would not last a single day. Now picture it back at a cool, muddy pond ringed with tall grasses. It catches flies with its sticky tongue. It hides under wet leaves. It lays eggs in the shallow water. That pond is the frog's habitat — the natural place where a living thing finds everything it needs to survive.
stimulus.content_text:
Home Sweet HabitatA Frog in the Wrong Place
Picture a tiny tree frog sitting on a sand dune in a scorching desert. No pond. No shade. No insects buzzing near the water. That frog would not last a single day. Now picture it back at a cool, muddy pond ringed with tall grasses. It catches flies with its sticky tongue. It hides under wet leaves. It lays eggs in the shallow water. That pond is the frog's habitat — the natural place where a living thing finds everything it needs to survive.

items.prompt: According to the text, which detail shows how the frog's pond habitat is different from the desert?

choices.text: It has shallow water for eggs.
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says the frog lays eggs in the shallow water at the pond, and the desert has no pond or water at all.

choices.text: It has hot sand for resting.
choices.feedback: This is wrong — hot sand is in the desert, not the pond, and it's one of the reasons the desert is a bad place for the frog.

choices.text: It has no insects to catch.
choices.feedback: This is wrong — the passage says the desert has 'no insects buzzing near the water,' so no insects is a problem with the desert, not the pond.

choices.text: It has no shade to hide in.
choices.feedback: This is wrong — 'no shade' describes the desert, not the pond; the passage says the frog hides under wet leaves at the pond.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
More Than Just a Place

You might think a habitat is just one thing, like "water" for a fish or "trees" for an owl. Actually, a habitat is a whole package of conditions working together. Every organism — a word scientists use for any living thing, from a daisy to a dolphin — needs four things from its habitat: food, water, shelter, and space. Take away even one, and that organism is in trouble.

A cactus lives in a hot, dry desert where rain is rare. But the desert also gives it bright sunlight and room to spread its roots. A clownfish lives on a warm coral reef that provides tiny food particles, hiding places among the tentacles of sea anemones, and salty ocean water at just the right temperature.
stimulus.content_text:
More Than Just a Place
You might think a habitat is just one thing, like "water" for a fish or "trees" for an owl. Actually, a habitat is a whole package of conditions working together. Every organism — a word scientists use for any living thing, from a daisy to a dolphin — needs four things from its habitat: food, water, shelter, and space. Take away even one, and that organism is in trouble.
A cactus lives in a hot, dry desert where rain is rare. But the desert also gives it bright sunlight and room to spread its roots. A clownfish lives on a warm coral reef that provides tiny food particles, hiding places among the tentacles of sea anemones, and salty ocean water at just the right temperature.

items.prompt: According to the passage, what causes an organism to be in trouble?

choices.text: Moving into a hot and dry desert
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage uses the desert as an example of a good habitat for a cactus, not a dangerous place.

choices.text: Missing one of its habitat needs
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly says that if an organism is missing even one of its four habitat needs, it is in trouble.

choices.text: Hiding inside a warm coral reef
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage describes the coral reef as a helpful home for the clownfish, giving it food, hiding places, and the right water.

choices.text: Getting too much bright sunlight
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage mentions bright sunlight as something the cactus benefits from in its desert habitat, not something that causes harm.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Habitats Around the World

Earth has many kinds of habitats. Forests are thick with trees and home to deer, owls, and countless insects. Grasslands are wide, open areas where zebras, bison, and prairie dogs roam. Deserts get little rain, so only tough plants like cacti and hardy animals like lizards survive there. The tundra is a cold, treeless land found near the North Pole and on high mountaintops. Caribou and Arctic foxes make their home in its frozen ground. Freshwater habitats include rivers, lakes, and ponds. The ocean is the largest habitat on Earth. It holds everything from plankton — living things so tiny you need a microscope to see them — to enormous blue whales.
stimulus.content_text:
Habitats Around the World
Earth has many kinds of habitats. Forests are thick with trees and home to deer, owls, and countless insects. Grasslands are wide, open areas where zebras, bison, and prairie dogs roam. Deserts get little rain, so only tough plants like cacti and hardy animals like lizards survive there. The tundra is a cold, treeless land found near the North Pole and on high mountaintops. Caribou and Arctic foxes make their home in its frozen ground. Freshwater habitats include rivers, lakes, and ponds. The ocean is the largest habitat on Earth. It holds everything from plankton — living things so tiny you need a microscope to see them — to enormous blue whales.

items.prompt: According to the passage, which habitat does the author describe right after grasslands?

choices.text: forests with trees and deer
choices.feedback: Forests are described at the very beginning of the passage, before grasslands, not after them.

choices.text: deserts with little rain
choices.feedback: This is correct! The sentence about deserts comes right after the sentence about grasslands in the passage.

choices.text: the ocean with blue whales
choices.feedback: The ocean is mentioned near the end of the passage, several habitats after grasslands.

choices.text: the tundra with frozen ground
choices.feedback: The tundra is described after deserts, not right after grasslands.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Built to Survive

A polar bear does not pick the Arctic the way you pick a seat in the lunchroom. It survives there because its body fits that place perfectly. Its thick, white fur keeps it warm and helps it blend into the snow. That blending is called camouflage — like wearing a white coat in a white room so no one spots you. A camel has wide, flat feet for walking on sand and can go days without drinking water. A fish has gills for breathing underwater and fins for swimming. These special body features are called adaptations — built-in tools that help a living thing survive in its habitat. Adaptations develop slowly over many generations, or rounds of families over time, passed from parents to young.
stimulus.content_text:
Built to Survive
A polar bear does not pick the Arctic the way you pick a seat in the lunchroom. It survives there because its body fits that place perfectly. Its thick, white fur keeps it warm and helps it blend into the snow. That blending is called camouflage — like wearing a white coat in a white room so no one spots you. A camel has wide, flat feet for walking on sand and can go days without drinking water. A fish has gills for breathing underwater and fins for swimming. These special body features are called adaptations — built-in tools that help a living thing survive in its habitat. Adaptations develop slowly over many generations, or rounds of families over time, passed from parents to young.

items.prompt: Based on the text, how are a polar bear's fur and a camel's feet alike?

choices.text: They are both special ways the animals hide from other creatures.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because camouflage (hiding) describes only the polar bear's fur, not the camel's feet, so they are not both used for hiding.

choices.text: They are both built-in tools that help the animals to survive.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage calls both features 'adaptations,' which it defines as 'built-in tools that help a living thing survive in its habitat.'

choices.text: They are both special features that change in a few days.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage says adaptations develop slowly over many generations, not in a few days.

choices.text: They are both smart choices the animals make to stay safe.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because adaptations are not smart choices animals make — they are special body features animals are born with.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
When Habitats Disappear

An animal's adaptations fit its old home, not a strange new one. Some organisms cannot move or change fast enough.

That is why people work hard to protect habitats. National parks and wildlife reserves guard wild places. Conservation programs — organized efforts to care for nature, like a neighborhood cleanup but for whole forests and oceans — help keep habitats healthy for the organisms that depend on them.
stimulus.content_text:
When Habitats Disappear
An animal's adaptations fit its old home, not a strange new one. Some organisms cannot move or change fast enough.
That is why people work hard to protect habitats. National parks and wildlife reserves guard wild places. Conservation programs — organized efforts to care for nature, like a neighborhood cleanup but for whole forests and oceans — help keep habitats healthy for the organisms that depend on them.

items.prompt: According to the text, what are conservation programs?

choices.text: places where wild animals move to new homes
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage never says conservation programs move animals to new homes — that's not what they do.

choices.text: organized efforts to care for nature
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly defines conservation programs as 'organized efforts to care for nature,' like a big cleanup for forests and oceans.

choices.text: special changes that help animals survive
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because special changes that help animals survive describes adaptations, a different idea from the passage.

choices.text: strange new homes for many organisms
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because 'strange new homes' is used in the passage to describe the problem animals face when their habitats are lost, not what conservation programs are.

items.title: Section 6 - Guiding Question 5

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
Your Habitat, Too

You have food, water, shelter, and space in your own home — you live in a habitat, too. But a frog cannot build a new pond, and a polar bear cannot order a thicker coat. When you protect a pond, a forest, or a reef, you are protecting the only home some creatures will ever have.
stimulus.content_text:
Your Habitat, Too
You have food, water, shelter, and space in your own home — you live in a habitat, too. But a frog cannot build a new pond, and a polar bear cannot order a thicker coat. When you protect a pond, a forest, or a reef, you are protecting the only home some creatures will ever have.

items.prompt: Which detail from the section "Your Habitat, Too" gives evidence that animals are unable to change their own habitats?

choices.text: A frog cannot build a new pond.
choices.feedback: A is correct because 'a frog cannot build a new pond' is a specific detail that shows animals cannot change or create their own habitats.

choices.text: You have food and water at home.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because it talks about what people have at home, not about whether animals can change their habitats.

choices.text: You live in a habitat of your own.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because it tells us that people live in habitats too, but says nothing about animals being unable to change their homes.

choices.text: A forest is a home for creatures.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because saying a forest is a home for creatures does not give any evidence about whether animals can or cannot change their habitats.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: Based on the article, how is a pond habitat different from a tundra habitat?

choices.text: A pond has tall grasses and shallow water, but a tundra has frozen ground and no trees.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the article specifically describes ponds as having shallow water and tall grasses, and the tundra as having frozen ground and no trees.

choices.text: A pond is found in wide and open spaces, but a tundra is found in hot and sandy deserts.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because wide open spaces describe grasslands, not ponds, and the tundra is cold and near the North Pole — not hot and sandy like a desert.

choices.text: A pond provides food and shelter for animals, but a tundra only provides space for animals.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the tundra does provide food and shelter for animals like caribou and Arctic foxes, not just space.

choices.text: A pond is a home where living things thrive, but a tundra is a home where no animals survive.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because animals like caribou and Arctic foxes do survive and live in the tundra, so it is definitely a real home for living things.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: Which two headings show where to read about what causes an animal's body to fit its home perfectly and why losing that home is dangerous?

choices.text: "Built to Survive" and "When Habitats Disappear"
choices.feedback: This is correct because 'Built to Survive' explains adaptations that make an animal's body fit its home, and 'When Habitats Disappear' explains why losing that home is dangerous.

choices.text: "More Than Just a Place" and "Your Habitat, Too"
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because 'More Than Just a Place' explains what a habitat includes and 'Your Habitat, Too' connects habitats to your own home — neither one focuses on body adaptations or the danger of losing a habitat.

choices.text: "A Frog in the Wrong Place" and "Built to Survive"
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because 'A Frog in the Wrong Place' introduces what a habitat is using a frog as an example, but it does not explain how an animal's body is built to fit its home.

choices.text: "Habitats Around the World" and "Your Habitat, Too"
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because 'Habitats Around the World' describes different types of habitats like forests and deserts, and 'Your Habitat, Too' talks about your own home — neither section is about body adaptations or the danger of habitat loss.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: Which choice best shows the order of ideas the article uses to teach the main idea about habitats?

choices.text: First it lists world habitats, next it explains habitat needs, then it tells about the frog.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the article starts with the frog story, not a list of world habitats.

choices.text: First it shows a frog's home, next it explains habitat needs, then it gives more habitat examples.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the article begins with the frog at the pond, then explains the four habitat needs, then gives more habitat examples like the cactus and clownfish.

choices.text: First it explains conservation work, next it tells about animal adaptations, then it names habitats.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because conservation is discussed near the end of the article, not at the beginning.

choices.text: First it tells about people at home, next it explains camouflage, then it describes oceans.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the article opens with a frog in the desert and pond, not with people at home.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: How are a cactus in the desert and a clownfish on a coral reef similar?

choices.text: They both live in habitats that are very cold.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage says the desert is hot and dry, and the coral reef is warm — neither habitat is cold.

choices.text: They both get what they need from their habitats.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage explains that both the cactus and the clownfish get food, water, shelter, and space from their own habitats, even though those habitats are very different.

choices.text: They both need the same kind of food to survive.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because a cactus is a plant that makes its own food from sunlight, while a clownfish eats tiny food particles in the ocean — they do not eat the same things.

choices.text: They both live in habitats that have a lot of water.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the desert has very little water or rain, which is the opposite of having a lot of water.

syllabus_metadata.title: Home Sweet Habitat: Where Living Things Thrive

assessments.title: Who Eats Whom: Food Chains Explained

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Chain That Feeds the World

A Meadow Full of Clues

A red fox crouches low in a green meadow. Thirty feet away, a rabbit chews on grass. The rabbit does not see the fox — not yet. But something connects these two animals, even before the chase begins. Energy. The same energy that started as sunlight now sits inside that blade of grass, and it is about to move again. This path of energy, flowing from one living thing to the next, is called a food chain.
stimulus.content_text:
The Chain That Feeds the WorldA Meadow Full of Clues
A red fox crouches low in a green meadow. Thirty feet away, a rabbit chews on grass. The rabbit does not see the fox — not yet. But something connects these two animals, even before the chase begins. Energy. The same energy that started as sunlight now sits inside that blade of grass, and it is about to move again. This path of energy, flowing from one living thing to the next, is called a food chain.

items.prompt: According to the text, what is the difference between where the energy started and where it is now?

choices.text: It started as sunlight, but now it sits inside the grass.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage says the energy 'started as sunlight' and 'now sits inside that blade of grass,' which matches this answer perfectly.

choices.text: It started in the fox, but now it sits inside the rabbit.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the passage never says energy started inside the fox — it says energy started as sunlight.

choices.text: It started as a chase, but now it sits inside the meadow.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because a chase is something that happens, not a place where energy begins, and the passage never says this.

choices.text: It started in the dirt, but now it sits inside the sunlight.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the passage clearly says the energy started as sunlight, not in the dirt.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Producers, Consumers, and How Energy Moves

Every food chain begins with a producer — a living thing that makes its own food from sunlight. Think of producers as tiny factories powered by sunshine. Plants, trees, and algae (small plant-like living things found in water) are all producers. They pull off an impressive trick called photosynthesis. That long word means "building with light." During photosynthesis, a plant mixes sunlight, water, and carbon dioxide — a gas you breathe out — to create sugar. That sugar is the plant's food. This is the moment the sun's energy enters the food chain.

Not every living thing can make its own food. A rabbit cannot photosynthesize. Neither can you. Living things that must eat other organisms to get energy are called consumers. Consumers are like customers at a restaurant. They cannot cook, so they depend on someone else's work.

Some consumers eat only plants. A rabbit munching grass is a plant-eater, called a herbivore. Other consumers eat animals. A fox that catches the rabbit is a meat-eater, called a carnivore. Some consumers eat both plants and animals. A raccoon that eats berries and frogs is an omnivore — an everything-eater.

Here is a simple food chain: sunlight feeds the grass, the rabbit eats the grass, and the fox eats the rabbit. Energy flows from one link to the next. But here is the surprising part — not all the energy gets passed along. The rabbit uses most of the energy from grass just to hop, breathe, and stay warm. Only a small amount is left in its body for the fox. It is like pouring water from one cup to another with a hole in the bottom. That is why a field has many more rabbits than foxes.

Producers, Consumers, and How Energy Moves
Every food chain begins with a producer — a living thing that makes its own food from sunlight. Think of producers as tiny factories powered by sunshine. Plants, trees, and algae (small plant-like living things found in water) are all producers. They pull off an impressive trick called photosynthesis. That long word means "building with light." During photosynthesis, a plant mixes sunlight, water, and carbon dioxide — a gas you breathe out — to create sugar. That sugar is the plant's food. This is the moment the sun's energy enters the food chain.
Not every living thing can make its own food. A rabbit cannot photosynthesize. Neither can you. Living things that must eat other organisms to get energy are called consumers. Consumers are like customers at a restaurant. They cannot cook, so they depend on someone else's work.
Some consumers eat only plants. A rabbit munching grass is a plant-eater, called a herbivore. Other consumers eat animals. A fox that catches the rabbit is a meat-eater, called a carnivore. Some consumers eat both plants and animals. A raccoon that eats berries and frogs is an omnivore — an everything-eater.
Here is a simple food chain: sunlight feeds the grass, the rabbit eats the grass, and the fox eats the rabbit. Energy flows from one link to the next. But here is the surprising part — not all the energy gets passed along. The rabbit uses most of the energy from grass just to hop, breathe, and stay warm. Only a small amount is left in its body for the fox. It is like pouring water from one cup to another with a hole in the bottom. That is why a field has many more rabbits than foxes.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, why is only a small amount of energy left in a rabbit for a fox to get?

choices.text: Because the grass does not pass enough energy to the rabbit.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage shows that grass does pass energy to the rabbit — the problem is what happens to that energy after the rabbit gets it.

choices.text: Because the rabbit uses most of the energy to live and move.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly says the rabbit uses most of its energy to hop, breathe, and stay warm, leaving only a small amount for the fox.

choices.text: Because the rabbit cannot make its own food from the sunlight.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the rabbit not being able to photosynthesize explains why it eats grass, but it does not explain why the fox gets less energy.

choices.text: Because the fox uses most of the energy to hunt in the field.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage never says the fox uses energy before eating the rabbit — the energy loss happens inside the rabbit, not the fox.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Food Webs, Decomposers, and Why Every Link Matters

You might think a food chain is a single straight line, but real life is messier. Rabbits nibble clover and dandelions, not just grass. Foxes also catch mice and eat berries. These overlapping chains tangle together into a food web. An ecosystem — a community of living things sharing one place, like a pond or a forest — is held together by its food web.

You might also think the food chain ends with the fox or the hawk. But what happens when those animals die? Decomposers take over. Mushrooms, bacteria, and tiny insects break down dead plants and animals, like nature's cleanup crew. They return nutrients to the soil, and those nutrients help new plants grow. The chain does not just end — it circles back to the beginning.

In the ocean, algae feed small fish, small fish feed big fish, and big fish feed sharks. In a forest, leaves feed caterpillars, caterpillars feed robins, and robins feed hawks. Remove one link, and the whole chain wobbles.

And somewhere inside you right now, energy that once traveled 93 million miles from the sun is keeping your heart beating.

And somewhere inside you right now, energy that once traveled 93 million miles from the sun is keeping your heart beating.

items.prompt: According to the text, what happens after decomposers break down dead plants and animals?

choices.text: Animals at the top of the chain eat them
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because animals at the top of the chain eat living prey, not decomposers breaking down dead things.

choices.text: Nutrients go back into the soil
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly says decomposers 'return nutrients to the soil,' which then helps new plants grow.

choices.text: Plants and animals die right away
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because decomposers actually help the cycle continue by putting nutrients back into the soil, not causing things to die right away.

choices.text: The food web turns into one straight line
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage explains that food chains overlap into a food web, and decomposers make the chain circle back — not turn into one straight line.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: Based on the article, how are producers different from decomposers?

choices.text: Producers break down dead animals, but decomposers eat living plants.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because it has the jobs completely switched around — producers make food from sunlight, and decomposers break down dead things, not the other way around.

choices.text: Producers make food from sunlight, but decomposers clean up dead things.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the article clearly says producers make food from sunlight using photosynthesis, and decomposers like mushrooms and bacteria break down dead plants and animals to return nutrients to the soil.

choices.text: Producers live only in ocean water, but decomposers live only in forests.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the article shows producers and decomposers living in many places, like forests and oceans, not just one specific place each.

choices.text: Producers hunt and eat other animals, but decomposers make their own sugar.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because hunting animals are called consumers, not producers, and it's producers that make sugar — decomposers break things down instead.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: Based on the article, why do meat-eaters like foxes need the sun to survive?

choices.text: Because the sun gives them energy to make their own food.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because foxes are consumers, not producers — they cannot use sunlight to make their own food like plants can.

choices.text: Because the sun feeds the plants that other animals eat.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the sun gives energy to plants first, and that energy travels up the food chain to plant-eaters and then to meat-eaters like foxes.

choices.text: Because the sun helps decomposers clean up dead animals.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because decomposers breaking down dead animals is a separate part of the food chain, not the reason foxes depend on the sun.

choices.text: Because the sun stops the food web from getting tangled.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the article never says the sun prevents the food web from tangling — in fact, it says food webs are naturally messy.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: Which list shows the order the article gives for how energy moves through a forest?

choices.text: leaves, robins, caterpillars, hawks
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because it puts robins before caterpillars, but the article says caterpillars feed robins, so caterpillars must come first.

choices.text: leaves, caterpillars, robins, hawks
choices.feedback: B is correct because it matches exactly what the article says: leaves feed caterpillars, caterpillars feed robins, and robins feed hawks.

choices.text: caterpillars, leaves, hawks, robins
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because it starts with caterpillars, but leaves come first since they are the producers that start the food chain.

choices.text: hawks, robins, caterpillars, leaves
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because it lists the animals in reverse order, starting with hawks at the top instead of leaves at the beginning.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt:
Read these sentences from the article:

"In the ocean, algae feed small fish, small fish feed big fish, and big fish feed sharks. In a forest, leaves feed caterpillars, caterpillars feed robins, and robins feed hawks."

What main idea do these examples show?

choices.text: Energy flows from one living thing to the next.
choices.feedback: A is correct because both examples show energy passing from one living thing to the next, which matches what the article says about how food chains work.

choices.text: Animals that eat meat get the most energy.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the article explains that energy is lost at each step, like water leaking from a cup, so meat-eaters actually get less energy, not more.

choices.text: Every food chain has exactly three living things.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because both examples in those sentences have four living things in the chain, not exactly three.

choices.text: Animals in the ocean get more energy than animals on land.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the article never says anything about ocean animals getting more energy than land animals — it just uses both as examples of food chains.

syllabus_metadata.title: Who Eats Whom: Food Chains Explained

assessments.title: The Web of Life: From Food Chains to Food Webs

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Tangled Web of Who Eats What

One Robin, Many Meals

A robin hops across a wet lawn at sunrise. It yanks a worm from the soil and swallows it whole. Later, it pecks a beetle off a leaf. By noon, it plucks a caterpillar from a branch. That single robin eats three completely different foods in one day.

A food chain shows energy passing from one living thing to the next in a straight line. But a robin does not follow a straight line. Neither does a fox, which eats rabbits, mice, birds, and fruit. When you connect all of these overlapping food chains together, you get something bigger and messier — a food web. A food web is a network of many food chains linked together inside an ecosystem, which is a community of living things and the place where they live. Think of a single food chain as one thread. A food web is a whole net woven from dozens of threads.
stimulus.content_text:
The Tangled Web of Who Eats WhatOne Robin, Many Meals
A robin hops across a wet lawn at sunrise. It yanks a worm from the soil and swallows it whole. Later, it pecks a beetle off a leaf. By noon, it plucks a caterpillar from a branch. That single robin eats three completely different foods in one day.

A food chain shows energy passing from one living thing to the next in a straight line. But a robin does not follow a straight line. Neither does a fox, which eats rabbits, mice, birds, and fruit. When you connect all of these overlapping food chains together, you get something bigger and messier — a food web. A food web is a network of many food chains linked together inside an ecosystem, which is a community of living things and the place where they live. Think of a single food chain as one thread. A food web is a whole net woven from dozens of threads.

items.prompt: According to the text, how is a food web different from a food chain?

choices.text: A food web is one straight line of energy.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage says a food chain is the straight line, not a food web.

choices.text: A food web is many linked food chains.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly says a food web is 'a network of many food chains linked together.'

choices.text: A food web is one animal eating all day.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because one animal eating different foods all day describes the robin in the story, not the definition of a food web.

choices.text: A food web is the place animals live.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage calls the place where living things live an ecosystem, not a food web.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
A Meadow Full of Connections

Picture a sunny meadow. Grass and wildflowers grow in the soil. These plants are producers — living things that make their own food using energy from sunlight. They are the starting point of every food chain in the meadow.

Mice nibble seeds from the wildflowers. Rabbits eat both the grass and the wildflowers. Frogs snap up grasshoppers. Snakes swallow mice and frogs. Hawks dive down and catch snakes, mice, and rabbits. Every one of these feeding paths crosses another, like roads meeting at intersections.

When scientists draw a food web, they use arrows. You might think the arrow points from the animal doing the eating toward its food. Actually, it works the other way. The arrow follows the energy. It points from the organism being eaten to the organism eating it — because that is the direction the energy travels.
stimulus.content_text:
A Meadow Full of Connections
Picture a sunny meadow. Grass and wildflowers grow in the soil. These plants are producers — living things that make their own food using energy from sunlight. They are the starting point of every food chain in the meadow.

Mice nibble seeds from the wildflowers. Rabbits eat both the grass and the wildflowers. Frogs snap up grasshoppers. Snakes swallow mice and frogs. Hawks dive down and catch snakes, mice, and rabbits. Every one of these feeding paths crosses another, like roads meeting at intersections.

When scientists draw a food web, they use arrows. You might think the arrow points from the animal doing the eating toward its food. Actually, it works the other way. The arrow follows the energy. It points from the organism being eaten to the organism eating it — because that is the direction the energy travels.

items.prompt: The main idea of the text is that a meadow is a connected food web. Based on the passage, what causes the feeding paths to cross and form this web?

choices.text: Because plants make their own food using sunlight.
choices.feedback: This tells us how plants get energy, which is the starting point of food chains, but it doesn't explain why the feeding paths cross each other.

choices.text: Because animals eat several different kinds of food.
choices.feedback: This is correct! Because each animal eats several different things, their feeding paths overlap and cross, creating a connected web.

choices.text: Because scientists draw arrows to show energy flow.
choices.feedback: Scientists drawing arrows is just a tool to show the food web on paper — it doesn't cause the feeding connections to exist in the first place.

choices.text: Because roads meet at intersections inside the meadow.
choices.feedback: The passage uses roads and intersections as a comparison (a simile) to help describe the web, but there are no actual roads in the meadow causing anything.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
What Happens When a Species Disappears

Every species in a food web matters. Imagine all the frogs in the meadow suddenly vanished. Grasshoppers would have fewer predators, so their population — the total number of one species living in an area — could explode. Swarms of grasshoppers might devour too much grass, leaving less food for rabbits. Meanwhile, snakes would lose one of their meals. Would the snakes just starve? Not exactly. They would switch and eat more mice instead. One missing species sends changes rippling across the whole web.

Top predators control the populations of animals below them. Without hawks hunting mice and rabbits, those animals could multiply fast and eat too many plants.
stimulus.content_text:
What Happens When a Species Disappears
Every species in a food web matters. Imagine all the frogs in the meadow suddenly vanished. Grasshoppers would have fewer predators, so their population — the total number of one species living in an area — could explode. Swarms of grasshoppers might devour too much grass, leaving less food for rabbits. Meanwhile, snakes would lose one of their meals. Would the snakes just starve? Not exactly. They would switch and eat more mice instead. One missing species sends changes rippling across the whole web.

Top predators control the populations of animals below them. Without hawks hunting mice and rabbits, those animals could multiply fast and eat too many plants.

items.prompt: According to the text, what is the first thing that happens after the frogs vanish?

choices.text: Grasshoppers have fewer predators.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage says the first thing that happens after frogs vanish is that grasshoppers have fewer predators hunting them.

choices.text: Grasshoppers devour too much grass.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because grasshoppers devouring too much grass happens after their population grows, making it the second step, not the first.

choices.text: Snakes eat more mice instead.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because snakes eating more mice is a later effect that happens after the frogs are already gone and snakes need a new food source.

choices.text: Hawks hunt mice and rabbits.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because hawks hunting mice and rabbits is mentioned in a separate paragraph about top predators and is not part of the frog disappearing chain of events.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Decomposers Close the Loop

There is one more group you cannot see from across the meadow. Decomposers are living things like fungi, bacteria, and certain insects that break down dead plants and animals. They turn dead matter back into nutrients in the soil. Those nutrients feed the producers — the grass and wildflowers — so new growth can begin. Decomposers connect the end of the food web back to the start.
stimulus.content_text:
Decomposers Close the Loop
There is one more group you cannot see from across the meadow. Decomposers are living things like fungi, bacteria, and certain insects that break down dead plants and animals. They turn dead matter back into nutrients in the soil. Those nutrients feed the producers — the grass and wildflowers — so new growth can begin. Decomposers connect the end of the food web back to the start.

items.prompt: According to the paragraph, how are the words 'fungi' and 'bacteria' similar in meaning?

choices.text: Both words name living things that break down dead plants.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage says decomposers like fungi and bacteria are living things that break down dead plants and animals.

choices.text: Both words name helpful nutrients that feed the new grass.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because nutrients are what get made from dead matter to feed the grass — fungi and bacteria are not nutrients.

choices.text: Both words name dead matter that turns into the soil.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because fungi and bacteria break down dead matter; they are not the dead matter turning into soil.

choices.text: Both words name new producers that grow in the meadow.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because producers are plants like grass and wildflowers, not decomposers like fungi and bacteria.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Why More Species Means a Stronger Web

A food web with many different species has biodiversity — a big word that simply means "lots of different kinds of life," the way a box of sixty-four crayons has more variety than a box of eight. High biodiversity makes a food web stronger. If one species disappears, other species still have different foods to rely on. But if too many species vanish, the web starts to fall apart — like a net with too many holes cut through it.

You are watching one tiny tug on a web that holds an entire meadow together.

High biodiversity makes a food web stronger. If one species disappears, other species still have different foods to rely on. But if too many species vanish, the web starts to fall apart — like a net with too many holes cut through it.

You are watching one tiny tug on a web that holds an entire meadow together.

items.prompt: According to the text, what does biodiversity mean?

choices.text: a web with only a few species
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because biodiversity means having MANY different kinds of life, not just a few species.

choices.text: lots of different kinds of life
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly defines biodiversity as 'lots of different kinds of life.'

choices.text: animals fighting over the same food
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage never mentions animals fighting over food — that idea isn't in the text at all.

choices.text: holes cut through a net
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the net with holes is a comparison for what happens when a food web breaks down, not a definition of biodiversity.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: Based on the article, how is the job of a producer different from the job of a decomposer?

choices.text: Producers eat living plants and animals, but decomposers eat only the dead plants.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because producers don't eat living plants and animals — they make their own food using sunlight, so they don't eat anything.

choices.text: Producers start the web by making food, but decomposers end it by breaking down dead matter.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the article says producers are the starting point of food chains by making food from sunlight, while decomposers break down dead matter and return nutrients to the soil, closing the loop.

choices.text: Producers hunt for meals in the meadow, but decomposers hide in the dirt to catch their prey.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because neither producers nor decomposers hunt or catch prey — producers grow and make food from sunlight, and decomposers simply break down things that are already dead.

choices.text: Producers give energy back to the sun, but decomposers take energy away from the rich soil.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the article never says producers give energy back to the sun — producers actually capture energy from the sun, and decomposers return nutrients to the soil, not take energy away from it.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: Which cause-and-effect relationship does the heading 'Decomposers Close the Loop' help you locate?

choices.text: How dead matter causes food chains to start over
choices.feedback: This is correct because the decomposers section explains that dead matter causes nutrients to return to the soil, which feeds producers and starts the food chain over again.

choices.text: How bright sunlight causes the meadow animals to hunt
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never says sunlight causes animals to hunt — sunlight helps plants make food, and that idea is in the producers section, not the decomposers section.

choices.text: How missing frogs cause the whole food web to break
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because missing frogs causing changes to the food web is talked about in the 'What Happens When a Species Disappears' section, not the decomposers section.

choices.text: How one robin causes the different chains to overlap
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the robin eating different foods and overlapping food chains is explained in the very first section of the article, not in the decomposers section.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: Which key detail supports the main idea that the end of the food web connects back to the start?

choices.text: Decomposers turn dead matter into nutrients so new plants can grow.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage says decomposers turn dead matter into soil nutrients that feed producers, which is exactly how the end of the food web connects back to the start.

choices.text: Producers use energy from sunlight so they can make their own food.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because producers using sunlight to make food describes where the food web starts, not how it loops back around to the beginning.

choices.text: Top predators hunt mice and rabbits so those animals do not multiply fast.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because top predators controlling animal populations is about keeping numbers balanced in the web, not about connecting the end of the web back to the start.

choices.text: Animals eat many different kinds of foods so their food chains overlap.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because animals eating many different foods explains why food webs are more complex than food chains, not how the web's end connects back to its beginning.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: Based on the article, how is having top predators similar to having high biodiversity?

choices.text: Both allow animal populations to grow out of control.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because top predators and biodiversity actually PREVENT animal populations from growing out of control — that's the opposite of what the article says.

choices.text: Both help keep the food web strong and balanced.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the article explains that top predators keep animal populations balanced, and high biodiversity keeps the food web strong so it doesn't fall apart.

choices.text: Both change dead plants and animals into new nutrients.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because turning dead plants and animals into nutrients is the job of decomposers, not top predators or biodiversity.

choices.text: Both create their own food using energy from sunlight.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because making food from sunlight is what producers like grass and wildflowers do, not top predators or biodiversity.

syllabus_metadata.title: The Web of Life: From Food Chains to Food Webs

assessments.title: Plant Eaters, Meat Eaters, and Everything in Between

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Built to Eat: How Animals' Bodies Match Their Food

A Mouth Full of Clues

You will feel sharp, pointy teeth in the front and flat, bumpy ones in the back. The flat ones grind. Your mouth is a clue — it tells a story about what kind of eater you are.

Scientists sort animals into groups based on what they eat. And here is the unexpected part: an animal's body is shaped by its food. Teeth, beaks, claws, and even stomachs look different depending on what an animal needs to survive.
stimulus.content_text:
Built to Eat: How Animals' Bodies Match Their FoodA Mouth Full of Clues
You will feel sharp, pointy teeth in the front and flat, bumpy ones in the back. The flat ones grind. Your mouth is a clue — it tells a story about what kind of eater you are.
Scientists sort animals into groups based on what they eat. And here is the unexpected part: an animal's body is shaped by its food. Teeth, beaks, claws, and even stomachs look different depending on what an animal needs to survive.

items.prompt: According to the text, what is the difference between the teeth in the front of your mouth and the teeth in the back?

choices.text: The front teeth are for grinding, but the back teeth are for biting.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because it has it backwards — the passage says the back teeth grind, not the front teeth.

choices.text: The front teeth are sharp and pointy, but the back teeth are flat and bumpy.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly states that front teeth are sharp and pointy, while back teeth are flat and bumpy.

choices.text: The front teeth are flat and bumpy, but the back teeth are sharp and pointy.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because it switches the descriptions — the passage says the FRONT teeth are sharp and pointy, not the back ones.

choices.text: The front teeth and the back teeth are both flat and bumpy.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage clearly describes two different kinds of teeth, not two that look the same.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Plant Eaters: The Herbivores

A cow stands in a field, chewing the same mouthful of grass over and over. Its jaw slides side to side, grinding tough stems into mush. That cow is a herbivore — an animal that eats only plants. Rabbits, deer, caterpillars, and horses are herbivores too.

Herbivores spend a lot of time eating because plants do not pack as much energy as meat. To get enough energy, herbivores must eat large amounts of food. Many of them have flat, wide teeth called molars — the broad back teeth built for crushing and grinding, like a millstone smashing grain.

A cow even has a stomach with four separate chambers — four pocket-like sections — to break down all that tough grass.
stimulus.content_text:
Plant Eaters: The Herbivores
A cow stands in a field, chewing the same mouthful of grass over and over. Its jaw slides side to side, grinding tough stems into mush. That cow is a herbivore — an animal that eats only plants. Rabbits, deer, caterpillars, and horses are herbivores too.
Herbivores spend a lot of time eating because plants do not pack as much energy as meat. To get enough energy, herbivores must eat large amounts of food. Many of them have flat, wide teeth called molars — the broad back teeth built for crushing and grinding, like a millstone smashing grain.
A cow even has a stomach with four separate chambers — four pocket-like sections — to break down all that tough grass.

items.prompt: The text shows that herbivores spend a lot of time eating. Which key detail explains why this happens?

choices.text: Because their jaws slide side to side.
choices.feedback: Sliding jaws describe the way herbivores chew their food, not the reason they have to eat so much.

choices.text: Because plants have less energy than meat.
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says plants have less energy than meat, so herbivores must eat large amounts to get enough energy.

choices.text: Because they have flat teeth called molars.
choices.feedback: Flat molars describe a tool herbivores use for chewing, but they don't explain why herbivores spend so much time eating.

choices.text: Because their stomachs have four chambers.
choices.feedback: Four stomach chambers help break down tough grass, but the passage doesn't say this is why herbivores eat so much.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Meat Eaters: The Carnivores

A hawk drops from the sky, slams its talons — sharp, curved claws — into a mouse, and carries it away. That hawk is a carnivore, an animal that eats other animals. Lions, spiders, and sharks are carnivores too.

You might think carnivores are mean or scary. Actually, they are just doing what their bodies were built to do. A lion has long, pointed canine teeth — the fang-like teeth near the front of the mouth — that grip and tear meat. A hawk has a hooked beak for catching prey, the animals that a hunter catches for food.

Because meat is packed with energy, carnivores do not need to eat as often as herbivores. A lion may eat one big meal and then rest for days.
stimulus.content_text:

A hawk has a hooked beak for catching prey, the animals that a hunter catches for food.
