items.prompt: According to the passage, what does a hawk do in order, from first to last, when it catches a mouse?

choices.text: It carries the mouse, slams its talons, and drops from the sky.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because it scrambles the order — the hawk drops from the sky first, not last.

choices.text: It drops from the sky, slams its talons, and carries it away.
choices.feedback: B is correct because it matches the exact order from the passage: drops from the sky, slams its talons, then carries the mouse away.

choices.text: It slams its talons, drops from the sky, and eats the mouse.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the steps are in the wrong order, and the passage does not say the hawk eats the mouse right after catching it.

choices.text: It uses its hooked beak, drops from the sky, and grabs the mouse.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage says the hawk uses its talons to catch the mouse, not its hooked beak.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Plant and Meat Eaters: The Omnivores

A black bear flips over a rock, licks up a line of ants, then wanders to a bush and gobbles a pawful of berries. That bear is an omnivore — an animal that eats both plants and animals. Raccoons, pigs, and humans are omnivores too.

Omnivores have the most flexible diets. A raccoon might munch on fruit and then catch a frog. But omnivores do not always eat equal amounts of plants and meat. A bear might eat mostly berries and only sometimes catch fish. Omnivores often have a mix of tooth types — some sharp, some flat — which lets them handle many different meals.
stimulus.content_text:
Plant and Meat Eaters: The Omnivores
A black bear flips over a rock, licks up a line of ants, then wanders to a bush and gobbles a pawful of berries. That bear is an omnivore — an animal that eats both plants and animals. Raccoons, pigs, and humans are omnivores too.
Omnivores have the most flexible diets. A raccoon might munch on fruit and then catch a frog. But omnivores do not always eat equal amounts of plants and meat. A bear might eat mostly berries and only sometimes catch fish. Omnivores often have a mix of tooth types — some sharp, some flat — which lets them handle many different meals.

items.prompt: Based on the section "Plant and Meat Eaters: The Omnivores," how are a black bear and a raccoon alike?

choices.text: They both eat the same amount of plants and meat.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage clearly says omnivores do not always eat equal amounts of plants and meat.

choices.text: They both can eat plants and animals.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage shows both the bear and raccoon eating plants and animals, which is what omnivores do.

choices.text: They both use only sharp teeth for food.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says omnivores have a mix of sharp and flat teeth, not only sharp teeth.

choices.text: They both always catch frogs before fruit.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never says raccoons always catch frogs before eating fruit — it just gives one example of what a raccoon might do.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Why Every Eater Matters

All these eaters live together in an ecosystem — a community of living things and the environment they share, like a neighborhood where every member has a job. Herbivores control plant growth so forests and fields do not get too thick. Carnivores keep herbivore numbers in check — without wolves, deer herds could eat every leaf and twig bare. Omnivores are adapted — shaped over time to fit their surroundings — so they can fill many roles when food changes with the seasons.

Now look at your mirror smile one more time. Sharp teeth and flat teeth, side by side. You eat apples, carrots, and chicken. You are an omnivore — one living link in a chain that connects every creature on Earth, from the cow chewing grass to the hawk diving from the sky.
stimulus.content_text:
Why Every Eater Matters
All these eaters live together in an ecosystem — a community of living things and the environment they share, like a neighborhood where every member has a job. Herbivores control plant growth so forests and fields do not get too thick. Carnivores keep herbivore numbers in check — without wolves, deer herds could eat every leaf and twig bare. Omnivores are adapted — shaped over time to fit their surroundings — so they can fill many roles when food changes with the seasons.
Now look at your mirror smile one more time. Sharp teeth and flat teeth, side by side. You eat apples, carrots, and chicken. You are an omnivore — one living link in a chain that connects every creature on Earth, from the cow chewing grass to the hawk diving from the sky.

items.prompt: According to the text, what is an ecosystem?

choices.text: a group of plants that grow in thick forests
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because an ecosystem is not just a group of plants — it includes all living things and the environment they share together.

choices.text: a community of living things and their shared environment
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly defines an ecosystem as 'a community of living things and the environment they share,' which is exactly what this answer says.

choices.text: a chain that connects every creature on Earth together
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the phrase 'a chain that connects every creature on Earth' describes a food chain idea from the passage, not the definition of an ecosystem.

choices.text: a type of animal that eats both plants and meat
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because an animal that eats both plants and meat is called an omnivore, not an ecosystem — those are two different words with two different meanings.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: Based on the text, how are the eating habits of herbivores different from the eating habits of carnivores?

choices.text: Herbivores eat all the time, while carnivores eat less often.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage says herbivores spend a lot of time eating large amounts of food, while carnivores like lions can eat one big meal and rest for days.

choices.text: Herbivores use sharp teeth, while carnivores use flat teeth.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect and backwards — the passage says herbivores have flat, wide teeth for grinding plants, while carnivores have sharp, pointed teeth for tearing meat.

choices.text: Herbivores hunt for animals, while carnivores look for plants.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect and backwards — the passage clearly says herbivores eat plants and carnivores eat other animals, not the other way around.

choices.text: Herbivores eat in the winter, while carnivores eat in the summer.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage never says anything about herbivores eating in winter or carnivores eating in summer — that idea is not in the text at all.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: Based on the article, why do omnivores have both sharp teeth and flat teeth?

choices.text: because they need to eat more often than carnivores do
choices.feedback: This is incorrect — the article says carnivores don't need to eat as often as herbivores, but it never says omnivores eat more often than carnivores.

choices.text: because they eat both plants and animals
choices.feedback: This is correct — the article directly says omnivores have a mix of sharp and flat teeth because they eat both plants and animals, which requires different tools for different foods.

choices.text: because their stomachs have four chambers
choices.feedback: This is incorrect — the four-chamber stomach belongs to the cow, a herbivore, not to omnivores.

choices.text: because they live in the same ecosystems as herbivores
choices.feedback: This is incorrect — living near herbivores in an ecosystem doesn't explain why omnivores have different types of teeth.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: The author shares key details to explain the main idea that animals' bodies match their food. Which list recounts these details in the order they appear in the article?

choices.text: Facts about plant eaters, facts about meat eaters, and facts about animals that eat both.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the article covers plant eaters, then meat eaters, then animals that eat both — exactly in that order, just like the section headings show.

choices.text: Facts about meat eaters, facts about plant eaters, and facts about animals that eat both.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because it says meat eaters come first, but the article actually starts with plant eaters before talking about meat eaters.

choices.text: Facts about plant eaters, facts about animals that eat both, and facts about ecosystem jobs.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because it leaves out the whole section about meat eaters, which is an important part of the article.

choices.text: Facts about plant eaters, facts about meat eaters, and facts about how teeth grind food.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the third topic in the article is omnivores (animals that eat both), not facts about how teeth grind food.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: Based on the article, how does the author show that a cow and a hawk are alike?

choices.text: By explaining that both animals have bodies shaped by what they must eat.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the article uses both the cow and the hawk as examples of animals whose bodies — teeth, beaks, claws, and stomachs — are shaped by what they need to eat.

choices.text: By showing that both animals spend most of their time hunting for extra energy.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because hunting for extra energy describes herbivores like cows, not carnivores like hawks, which actually rest for days after one big meal.

choices.text: By describing how both animals help control the amount of plants in the forest.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the article says herbivores help control plant growth, but hawks are carnivores that keep animal numbers in check, not plant growth.

choices.text: By pointing out that both animals use sharp, curved claws to catch their meals.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because only the hawk has sharp, curved talons — cows do not have claws and do not catch meals at all.

syllabus_metadata.title: Plant Eaters, Meat Eaters, and Everything in Between

assessments.title: Nature's Recyclers: Decomposers and Human Impact

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Tiny Heroes That Keep the World Alive

A Leaf Hits the Ground

A brown leaf drops from an oak tree and lands on the forest floor. Tiny living things, smaller than a grain of sand, get right to work breaking that leaf apart. In a few months, the leaf will be completely gone.

These tiny living things are called decomposers. Decomposers are living things that break down dead plants and animals into tiny pieces. But here is the surprising part: they don't destroy anything. They turn dead things into nutrients — the good stuff that soil needs to help new plants grow. Think of nutrients like ingredients in a recipe. Plants soak them up through their roots to build new leaves, stems, and flowers.
stimulus.content_text:
The Tiny Heroes That Keep the World AliveA Leaf Hits the Ground
A brown leaf drops from an oak tree and lands on the forest floor. Tiny living things, smaller than a grain of sand, get right to work breaking that leaf apart. In a few months, the leaf will be completely gone.

These tiny living things are called decomposers. Decomposers are living things that break down dead plants and animals into tiny pieces. But here is the surprising part: they don't destroy anything. They turn dead things into nutrients — the good stuff that soil needs to help new plants grow. Think of nutrients like ingredients in a recipe. Plants soak them up through their roots to build new leaves, stems, and flowers.

items.prompt: According to the text, what do decomposers do instead of destroying dead things?

choices.text: They bury dead things deep in the soil.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage never says decomposers bury dead things deep in the soil.

choices.text: They turn dead things into nutrients.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly states that decomposers 'turn dead things into nutrients' instead of destroying them.

choices.text: They carry dead things to other places.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage never says decomposers carry dead things to other places.

choices.text: They keep dead things from changing.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because decomposers actually do change dead things — they break them down into nutrients, so dead things definitely don't stay the same.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
The Workers You Cannot See

When you think of a decomposer, you might picture a worm. Earthworms are decomposers! They chew through rotting leaves and mix the pieces into the soil. But most decomposers are way too small to see.

Bacteria are tiny living things so small that millions of them can fit on the tip of your finger. Fungi are another group of decomposers. Fungi include mushrooms and mold — that fuzzy green stuff you sometimes see on old bread. The mushroom you see above the ground is only a small part of the fungus. Under the ground, fungi spread out in thin threads, like a hidden web, breaking down dead wood and leaves.

Without decomposers, dead leaves and fallen trees would pile up forever. Decomposers are not gross — they are heroes!
stimulus.content_text:
The Workers You Cannot See
When you think of a decomposer, you might picture a worm. Earthworms are decomposers! They chew through rotting leaves and mix the pieces into the soil. But most decomposers are way too small to see.

Bacteria are tiny living things so small that millions of them can fit on the tip of your finger. Fungi are another group of decomposers. Fungi include mushrooms and mold — that fuzzy green stuff you sometimes see on old bread. The mushroom you see above the ground is only a small part of the fungus. Under the ground, fungi spread out in thin threads, like a hidden web, breaking down dead wood and leaves.

Without decomposers, dead leaves and fallen trees would pile up forever. Decomposers are not gross — they are heroes!

items.prompt: Based on the passage, what would happen if there were no decomposers?

choices.text: Soil would become richer and healthier over time
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because decomposers are what help make soil healthy, so without them the soil would not get richer — it would actually be worse off.

choices.text: Dead leaves and fallen trees would keep piling up
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the passage directly states, 'Without decomposers, dead leaves and fallen trees would pile up forever.'

choices.text: New plants would break down the dead wood instead
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never mentions plants taking over the job of breaking down dead wood — only decomposers do that.

choices.text: Fallen trees would slowly sink deeper into the ground
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage does not say anything about fallen trees sinking into the ground — that idea is not in the passage at all.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
The Nutrient Cycle

Here is how the cycle works. A plant grows by soaking up nutrients from the soil. An animal eats the plant. When the plant or animal dies, decomposers break it down. The nutrients go back into the soil. Then new plants soak up those same nutrients and grow. The cycle starts all over again!

Decomposers are the last link in the food chain — the path that energy and nutrients follow from one living thing to the next. They connect death back to new life.
stimulus.content_text:
The Nutrient Cycle
Here is how the cycle works. A plant grows by soaking up nutrients from the soil. An animal eats the plant. When the plant or animal dies, decomposers break it down. The nutrients go back into the soil. Then new plants soak up those same nutrients and grow. The cycle starts all over again!

Decomposers are the last link in the food chain — the path that energy and nutrients follow from one living thing to the next. They connect death back to new life.

items.prompt: According to the text, what happens after decomposers break down a dead plant or animal?

choices.text: The animal eats the plant
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the animal eating the plant happens earlier in the cycle, before the plant or animal dies and before decomposers do their job.

choices.text: The nutrients go back into the soil
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage clearly states 'The nutrients go back into the soil' right after decomposers break down the dead plant or animal.

choices.text: New plants soak up nutrients first
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because new plants soak up nutrients only after the nutrients have already gone back into the soil — that step comes later.

choices.text: The food chain comes to an end
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage says decomposers connect death back to new life, meaning the cycle starts all over again instead of ending.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
When Humans Upset the Balance

Deforestation means cutting down large areas of forest. This takes away the trees that drop leaves for decomposers to break down. Without tree roots holding the soil in place, rain washes the nutrient-rich soil away. When the soil disappears, decomposers lose their home, and the nutrient cycle slows down.

Pollution hurts decomposers too. Smoke from cars and trucks dirties the air and can poison the soil where bacteria and fungi live. Plastic trash and other waste can end up on the ground or in rivers, making it harder for decomposers to do their important work. Even overfishing upsets the balance, because when too many fish are caught, fewer nutrients return to the water for tiny ocean decomposers.
stimulus.content_text:
When Humans Upset the Balance
Deforestation means cutting down large areas of forest. This takes away the trees that drop leaves for decomposers to break down. Without tree roots holding the soil in place, rain washes the nutrient-rich soil away. When the soil disappears, decomposers lose their home, and the nutrient cycle slows down.

Pollution hurts decomposers too. Smoke from cars and trucks dirties the air and can poison the soil where bacteria and fungi live. Plastic trash and other waste can end up on the ground or in rivers, making it harder for decomposers to do their important work. Even overfishing upsets the balance, because when too many fish are caught, fewer nutrients return to the water for tiny ocean decomposers.

items.prompt: How are deforestation and pollution similar in the way they affect decomposers?

choices.text: They both wash nutrient-rich soil into rivers.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because only deforestation washes nutrient-rich soil away — the passage never says pollution does this.

choices.text: They both make it harder for decomposers to do their work.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage shows that deforestation removes decomposers' home and pollution poisons their soil and adds trash, so both make it harder for decomposers to do their work.

choices.text: They both cause too many fish to be caught in the ocean.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because catching too many fish is about overfishing, which is a different problem — not something deforestation or pollution causes.

choices.text: They both add more leaves for decomposers to break down.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because cutting down forests actually means fewer trees and fewer leaves, so decomposers get less to break down, not more.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Fighting Back

People are working hard to protect nature. Conservation means caring for and protecting the natural world. Scientists study endangered animals and plants — ones that are in danger of disappearing forever — and work to save them. Communities come together to clean up trash from beaches and rivers.

There are simple things you can do too! Plant a tree to give decomposers a place to keep their cycle going. Recycle and use less energy to cut down on pollution. Pick up trash so it doesn't end up in the soil where decomposers work.

Right now, under your feet, billions of bacteria and fungi are quietly recycling dead things into food for living things. The least we can do is protect the world they help keep running.
stimulus.content_text:
Fighting Back
People are working hard to protect nature. Conservation means caring for and protecting the natural world. Scientists study endangered animals and plants — ones that are in danger of disappearing forever — and work to save them. Communities come together to clean up trash from beaches and rivers.

There are simple things you can do too! Plant a tree to give decomposers a place to keep their cycle going. Recycle and use less energy to cut down on pollution. Pick up trash so it doesn't end up in the soil where decomposers work.

Right now, under your feet, billions of bacteria and fungi are quietly recycling dead things into food for living things. The least we can do is protect the world they help keep running.

items.prompt: In the section 'Fighting Back,' what does the word 'conservation' mean?

choices.text: studying animals in a science lab
choices.feedback: Studying animals in a lab is something scientists might do, but the passage never says that's what conservation means.

choices.text: caring for and protecting nature
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says right after the bold word, 'Conservation means caring for and protecting the natural world.'

choices.text: cleaning up trash from beaches
choices.feedback: Cleaning up beaches is one example of people helping nature, but it's only a small part of what conservation means.

choices.text: recycling dead things into food
choices.feedback: Recycling dead things into food is what decomposers like bacteria and fungi do underground, not what conservation means.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: According to the article, how is the way decomposers change the soil different from the way deforestation changes the soil?

choices.text: Decomposers take nutrients out of the soil, but deforestation puts nutrients into the soil.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because it has it backwards — the article says decomposers add nutrients to the soil, not take them out, and deforestation washes soil away, not puts nutrients in.

choices.text: Decomposers add healthy nutrients to the soil, but deforestation causes the soil to wash away.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the article says decomposers turn dead things into nutrients that help the soil, while deforestation removes trees so rain washes the nutrient-rich soil away.

choices.text: Decomposers build hidden webs in the soil, but deforestation pulls the webs out of the soil.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the hidden web threads are just how fungi spread underground to break down dead things, and the article never says deforestation pulls those webs out.

choices.text: Decomposers mix dead leaves into the soil, but deforestation poisons the soil with dirty smoke.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because it confuses two different problems — the article says dirty smoke from cars and trucks poisons the soil, but that is pollution, not deforestation.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: A reader wants to know what causes the problems that people are fixing in the section 'Fighting Back.' Why is the heading 'When Humans Upset the Balance' the best place to look?

choices.text: It lists the different types of tiny workers that people are trying to save.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because 'When Humans Upset the Balance' does not list types of tiny workers — that information is in 'The Workers You Cannot See' section.

choices.text: It explains how pollution and cutting down trees hurt the decomposers.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the section clearly explains how pollution and deforestation harm decomposers, which are the exact problems that people are working to fix in 'Fighting Back.'

choices.text: It shows the exact steps of how nutrients move through the forest soil.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the steps of how nutrients move through the soil are explained in 'The Nutrient Cycle' section, not in 'When Humans Upset the Balance.'

choices.text: It explains how planting new trees gives decomposers a safe place to live.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because planting trees is a solution mentioned in 'Fighting Back,' not a topic covered in 'When Humans Upset the Balance.'

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: Which list recounts the main points of the article in the order they are written?

choices.text: Decomposers create healthy soil, humans harm the natural cycle, and people work to fix it.
choices.feedback: A is correct because it lists the three main ideas of the article — decomposers helping soil, humans causing harm, and people fixing it — in the exact order they appear in the article.

choices.text: Plants grow from the rich dirt, animals eat the green plants, and fungi break them apart.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because it only describes one small part of the article (the nutrient cycle) and leaves out the big ideas about humans and conservation.

choices.text: Earthworms chew the dead leaves, mushrooms spread underground, and bacteria live in soil.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because it only mentions a few details about earthworms, mushrooms, and bacteria, which are small details, not the main points of the whole article.

choices.text: People work to fix the problems, decomposers create healthy soil, and humans harm nature.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because it puts the ideas in the wrong order — it says people fix problems first, but in the article that idea comes last.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: Based on the article, how is the work of decomposers similar to the actions of people who pick up trash?

choices.text: They both turn dead leaves into food for the animals.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because decomposers turn dead leaves into nutrients for plants, not food for animals.

choices.text: They both stop waste from piling up on the ground.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the article says dead things would pile up without decomposers, and picking up trash also stops waste from building up on the ground.

choices.text: They both grow new green plants from the healthy dirt.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because neither decomposers nor people who pick up trash are described as directly growing new plants.

choices.text: They both clean up plastic trash from the deep rivers.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the article talks about picking up trash from beaches and rivers, not deep rivers, and decomposers break down natural things, not plastic trash.

syllabus_metadata.title: Nature's Recyclers: Decomposers and Human Impact

assessments.title: Our Neighborhood in Space: The Solar System

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Our Solar System: A Neighborhood in Space

The Star at the Center

Right now, a ball of blazing gas one million times bigger than Earth is holding you in place. That ball is the sun, and without it, our planet would drift into cold, dark space.

It makes energy deep inside itself in a special way that never runs out. The sun sits at the center of our solar system — the collection of planets, moons, and other objects that circle around it.

The sun holds everything together with its gravity — an invisible pull that draws objects toward each other, like a magnet tugging on a paper clip. The sun has so much mass, or stuff packed inside it, that its gravity reaches across enormous distances. More than 99 percent of all the mass in our solar system belongs to the sun alone.
stimulus.content_text:
Our Solar System: A Neighborhood in SpaceThe Star at the Center
Right now, a ball of blazing gas one million times bigger than Earth is holding you in place. That ball is the sun, and without it, our planet would drift into cold, dark space.
It makes energy deep inside itself in a special way that never runs out. The sun sits at the center of our solar system — the collection of planets, moons, and other objects that circle around it.
The sun holds everything together with its gravity — an invisible pull that draws objects toward each other, like a magnet tugging on a paper clip. The sun has so much mass, or stuff packed inside it, that its gravity reaches across enormous distances. More than 99 percent of all the mass in our solar system belongs to the sun alone.

items.prompt: According to the text, what would happen to Earth WITHOUT the sun, in contrast to what happens now?

choices.text: It would spin faster and get very hot
choices.feedback: The passage never says Earth would spin faster or get hotter without the sun — actually, it says space would be cold and dark, which is the opposite of hot.

choices.text: It would drift into cold, dark space
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage directly states that without the sun, 'our planet would drift into cold, dark space.'

choices.text: It would move closer to the other planets
choices.feedback: The passage doesn't say anything about Earth moving closer to other planets — it says Earth would drift into cold, dark space, not toward the other planets.

choices.text: It would stay in place but lose its moon
choices.feedback: The passage never mentions Earth staying in place or losing its moon if the sun were gone — the sun is what keeps Earth in place, so without it, Earth would drift away.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Eight Planets, Two Groups

Eight planets orbit the sun. To orbit means to travel around something in a curved path, the way a runner circles a track. Starting closest to the sun and moving outward, the planets are Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars, Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, and Neptune. Here is a trick to remember the order: My Very Excited Mother Just Served Us Nachos. Each first letter matches a planet.

The four inner planets — Mercury, Venus, Earth, and Mars — are called the rocky planets. Earth is the only rocky planet with liquid water on its surface and a thick blanket of air that living things can breathe. That is what makes our home so special. But you would not want to stand on Venus — it is hot enough there to melt lead!

The four outer planets — Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, and Neptune — are giants. Jupiter and Saturn are called gas giants because they are made mostly of thick, swirling gas. If you tried to land a spaceship on Jupiter, it would just sink deeper and deeper into clouds.
stimulus.content_text:
Eight Planets, Two Groups
Eight planets orbit the sun. To orbit means to travel around something in a curved path, the way a runner circles a track. Starting closest to the sun and moving outward, the planets are Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars, Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, and Neptune. Here is a trick to remember the order: My Very Excited Mother Just Served Us Nachos. Each first letter matches a planet.
The four inner planets — Mercury, Venus, Earth, and Mars — are called the rocky planets. Earth is the only rocky planet with liquid water on its surface and a thick blanket of air that living things can breathe. That is what makes our home so special. But you would not want to stand on Venus — it is hot enough there to melt lead!
The four outer planets — Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, and Neptune — are giants. Jupiter and Saturn are called gas giants because they are made mostly of thick, swirling gas. If you tried to land a spaceship on Jupiter, it would just sink deeper and deeper into clouds.

items.prompt: Based on the text, why would a spaceship sink if it tried to land on Jupiter?

choices.text: It is made of thick, swirling gas.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage says Jupiter is made mostly of thick, swirling gas, which means there is no solid surface — a spaceship would just sink through the clouds.

choices.text: It is hot enough to melt solid lead.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the passage says Venus is hot enough to melt lead, not Jupiter.

choices.text: It is covered in deep liquid water.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage says Earth is the rocky planet with liquid water on its surface, not Jupiter.

choices.text: It travels fast in a curved path.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because traveling in a curved path describes orbiting the sun, which all planets do — it has nothing to do with why a spaceship would sink on Jupiter.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Dwarf Planets, Asteroids, and Comets

You may have heard that Pluto is a planet. It used to be called the ninth planet! But in 2006, scientists looked more carefully. Pluto is very small and shares its part of space with many other icy objects. So scientists created a new category called dwarf planets — objects that orbit the sun and are round, but are too small to count as full planets. Pluto fits that group perfectly.

Between Mars and Jupiter, millions of rocky chunks called asteroids orbit the sun in a wide ring called the asteroid belt. Actually, they are spread so far apart that a spaceship can fly right through without hitting one.

Comets are balls of ice and dust that travel toward the sun from far away. When a comet gets close to the sun, the heat creates a bright, glowing tail that stretches across the sky.
stimulus.content_text:
Dwarf Planets, Asteroids, and Comets
You may have heard that Pluto is a planet. It used to be called the ninth planet! But in 2006, scientists looked more carefully. Pluto is very small and shares its part of space with many other icy objects. So scientists created a new category called dwarf planets — objects that orbit the sun and are round, but are too small to count as full planets. Pluto fits that group perfectly.
Between Mars and Jupiter, millions of rocky chunks called asteroids orbit the sun in a wide ring called the asteroid belt. Actually, they are spread so far apart that a spaceship can fly right through without hitting one.
Comets are balls of ice and dust that travel toward the sun from far away. When a comet gets close to the sun, the heat creates a bright, glowing tail that stretches across the sky.

items.prompt: According to the text, what happened after scientists looked more carefully at Pluto in 2006?

choices.text: Scientists created a new category called dwarf planets.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage directly states that after looking more carefully, scientists created a new category called dwarf planets and placed Pluto in it.

choices.text: Scientists found Pluto in the asteroid belt.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the asteroid belt is between Mars and Jupiter, and the passage never puts Pluto there.

choices.text: Scientists saw Pluto grow a bright, glowing tail.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because bright, glowing tails are described for comets, not for Pluto.

choices.text: Scientists said Pluto was still the ninth planet.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because scientists did the opposite — they decided Pluto was no longer the ninth planet and moved it to the dwarf planet category.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Moons and Enormous Distances

Many planets have moons orbiting around them. Some moons are bigger than the planet Mercury.

The distances in our solar system are hard to imagine. Light is the fastest thing there is, and even light takes over four hours to travel from the sun to Neptune. If you shrank the sun to the size of a basketball, Earth would be a tiny grain of sand sitting 26 meters away.

Yet all of this — the sun, eight planets, dwarf planets, moons, asteroids, and comets — is just one small neighborhood inside a galaxy filled with billions of other stars. Every time you look up at the night sky, you are staring at the addresses of places we have not visited yet.
stimulus.content_text:
Moons and Enormous Distances
Many planets have moons orbiting around them. Some moons are bigger than the planet Mercury.
The distances in our solar system are hard to imagine. Light is the fastest thing there is, and even light takes over four hours to travel from the sun to Neptune. If you shrank the sun to the size of a basketball, Earth would be a tiny grain of sand sitting 26 meters away.
Yet all of this — the sun, eight planets, dwarf planets, moons, asteroids, and comets — is just one small neighborhood inside a galaxy filled with billions of other stars. Every time you look up at the night sky, you are staring at the addresses of places we have not visited yet.

items.prompt: How does the author help the reader understand the sizes of the sun and Earth?

choices.text: By comparing them to a basketball and a grain of sand.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage directly says to imagine the sun as a basketball and Earth as a grain of sand to show how different their sizes are.

choices.text: By comparing them to a large planet and a small moon.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the passage never uses a large planet and a small moon to explain the sizes of the sun and Earth.

choices.text: By comparing them to a neighborhood and a huge galaxy.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the neighborhood and galaxy comparison is used to show how big space is, not to explain the sizes of the sun and Earth.

choices.text: By comparing them to a bright star and a dark comet.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the passage never compares the sun to a bright star or Earth to a dark comet when explaining their sizes.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: Based on the article, how is the sun's energy different from a comet's glowing tail?

choices.text: The sun makes energy inside itself, while a comet glows from the sun's heat.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage clearly says the sun makes energy deep inside itself, while a comet's glowing tail is caused by the sun's heat — not the comet making its own light.

choices.text: The sun gets energy from its gravity, while a comet makes light deep inside.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the passage says gravity is an invisible pull that holds things together, not something that makes the sun's energy, and comets definitely don't make light deep inside themselves.

choices.text: The sun burns thick swirling gases, while a comet burns solid rocky chunks.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage connects swirling gases to Jupiter and Saturn, not the sun, and comets are described as balls of ice and dust — not rocky chunks.

choices.text: The sun loses its energy quickly, while a comet keeps its tail forever.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage says the sun's energy never runs out, and a comet only has its glowing tail when it is near the sun, not all the time.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: According to the text, what causes objects like the rocky planets and the icy comets to travel around the sun?

choices.text: The sun contains a huge amount of mass that creates a strong invisible pull.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage clearly states that the sun's huge mass creates gravity, an invisible pull that reaches across enormous distances and keeps everything orbiting around it.

choices.text: The objects are made of swirling gas that pushes them in a wide ring.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because swirling gas is how Jupiter and Saturn are described, and the passage never says gas pushes objects into orbit.

choices.text: The objects contain a special type of energy that drives them in a circle.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage never mentions a special energy inside planets or comets that makes them circle the sun.

choices.text: The sun has a bright, glowing tail that wraps around the whole solar system.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage says comets grow a glowing tail when they get near the sun — the sun itself does not have a tail that wraps around the solar system.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: By reading the information under the headings "Eight Planets, Two Groups" and "Dwarf Planets, Asteroids, and Comets," what order of objects will you find moving outward from the sun?

choices.text: The sun appears first, and then the many moons orbit closely around it.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because moons orbit around their planets, not closely around the sun itself.

choices.text: The rocky planets appear first, and then an asteroid belt orbits before the giants.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage says the rocky planets are closest to the sun, the asteroid belt sits between Mars and Jupiter, and the giant planets are farther out.

choices.text: The icy comets appear first, and then they travel toward the distant dwarf planets.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because comets travel toward the sun from far away — they don't appear first near the sun or travel toward dwarf planets.

choices.text: The gas giants appear first, and then the smaller rocky planets follow behind them.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage says the rocky planets are the inner planets closest to the sun, and the gas giants are the outer planets farther away — it's the opposite of what D says.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: Compared to the other objects in the solar system, what main idea does the article share about the sun?

choices.text: It is the only object that orbits other objects
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the sun does not orbit other objects — it is the planets, moons, and other objects that orbit the sun.

choices.text: It is bigger and holds more mass than all else
choices.feedback: B is correct because the article says the sun holds more than 99 percent of all the mass in the solar system and is one million times bigger than Earth.

choices.text: It is made of ice and dust like the comets are
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because comets are made of ice and dust, but the sun is a blazing ball of gas, not ice and dust.

choices.text: It is a rocky object like the inner four planets
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because rocky planets like Mercury, Venus, Earth, and Mars are rocky, but the sun is a huge ball of hot gas, not a rocky object.

syllabus_metadata.title: Our Neighborhood in Space: The Solar System

assessments.title: A Tour of the Planets: Worlds of Fire, Ice, and Gas

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
A Tour of Eight Worlds

More Than 1,300 Earths Could Fit Inside Jupiter

Picture this: a storm so huge it could swallow our entire planet. That storm is real. It swirls on Jupiter right now, and it has been spinning for hundreds of years. Scientists call it the Great Red Spot. And Jupiter is just one of eight planets circling our sun. Each one is a strange, surprising world.
stimulus.content_text:
A Tour of Eight WorldsMore Than 1,300 Earths Could Fit Inside Jupiter
Picture this: a storm so huge it could swallow our entire planet. That storm is real. It swirls on Jupiter right now, and it has been spinning for hundreds of years. Scientists call it the Great Red Spot. And Jupiter is just one of eight planets circling our sun. Each one is a strange, surprising world.

items.prompt: According to the text, how is the Great Red Spot different from storms on Earth?

choices.text: It is smaller than storms on Earth
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage says the storm is so huge it could swallow Earth, which means it is much bigger than storms on Earth, not smaller.

choices.text: It is big enough to swallow our planet
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage says the storm is 'so huge it could swallow our entire planet,' which means it is big enough to swallow Earth.

choices.text: It only lasted for a few short days
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage says the storm has been spinning for hundreds of years, so it lasted much longer than a few short days.

choices.text: It moves around from planet to planet
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage only says the storm is on Jupiter — it never says the storm moves around from planet to planet.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
The Rocky Planets Close to the Sun

Mercury is the smallest planet and the closest to the sun. It is covered in craters, so it looks a lot like our moon. Mercury has almost no atmosphere — the blanket of gases that wraps around a planet the way a coat wraps around your body. Without that blanket, Mercury cannot hold onto heat. During the day, its surface reaches 800 degrees Fahrenheit. At night, it drops to nearly 300 degrees below zero.

Here is something surprising. Venus is farther from the sun than Mercury, but Venus is the hottest planet. How? Venus has a thick atmosphere full of carbon dioxide, a heavy gas that traps heat the way a car's closed windows trap warmth on a sunny day. That trapped heat pushes temperatures to about 900 degrees Fahrenheit — hot enough to melt metal. The clouds on Venus are made of sulfuric acid, a dangerous liquid that can burn through almost anything.

Earth is our home. It is the only planet known to have liquid water on its surface and living things. Earth's atmosphere blocks harmful radiation — invisible energy from the sun that could hurt us, like an extreme sunburn that never stops. From space, Earth looks like a blue marble streaked with white clouds.

Mars is called the Red Planet. Its color comes from iron oxide, which is the same thing as rust. Imagine a whole planet coated in rust! Mars has the tallest volcano in the solar system. Olympus Mons rises nearly three times higher than Mount Everest. Mars also has a canyon called Valles Marineris so wide it could stretch across the entire United States.

Mercury has almost no atmosphere — the blanket of gases that wraps around a planet the way a coat wraps around your body. Without that blanket, Mercury cannot hold onto heat. During the day, its surface reaches 800 degrees Fahrenheit. At night, it drops to nearly 300 degrees below zero.

Here is something surprising. Venus is farther from the sun than Mercury, but Venus is the hottest planet. How? Venus has a thick atmosphere full of carbon dioxide, a heavy gas that traps heat the way a car's closed windows trap warmth on a sunny day. That trapped heat pushes temperatures to about 900 degrees Fahrenheit — hot enough to melt metal. The clouds on Venus are made of sulfuric acid, a dangerous liquid that can burn through almost anything.

Earth is our home. It is the only planet known to have liquid water on its surface and living things. Earth's atmosphere blocks harmful radiation — invisible energy from the sun that could hurt us, like an extreme sunburn that never stops. From space, Earth looks like a blue marble streaked with white clouds.

Mars is called the Red Planet. Its color comes from iron oxide, which is the same thing as rust. Imagine a whole planet coated in rust! Mars has the tallest volcano in the solar system. Olympus Mons rises nearly three times higher than Mount Everest. Mars also has a canyon called Valles Marineris so wide it could stretch across the entire United States.

items.prompt: Which detail best supports the main idea that a planet's air can cause it to be much hotter or colder?

choices.text: Mercury has almost no air, so it cannot keep heat.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage directly says Mercury has almost no atmosphere, which means it cannot hold heat — showing how a planet's air controls how hot or cold it gets.

choices.text: Venus is farther from the sun, so it has more clouds.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because it talks about Venus being far from the sun and having clouds, but it does not explain how the air itself traps heat.

choices.text: Earth has liquid water, so it looks blue from space.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because Earth's liquid water and blue color from space have nothing to do with air making a planet hotter or colder.

choices.text: Mars has red rust, so it has the tallest volcano.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because Mars's rust color and tall volcano are not caused by its air, so they do not support the main idea about air and temperature.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
The Giant Planets Far from the Sun

Jupiter is the largest planet. More than 1,300 Earths could fit inside it. Jupiter has over 90 moons. Four of the biggest were spotted by an astronomer named Galileo over 400 years ago.

Saturn is famous for its bright, beautiful rings. These rings are made of billions of pieces of ice and rock. Some pieces are as tiny as grains of sand. Others are as big as houses. Saturn is also very light for its size. If you could find a bathtub big enough, Saturn would float. That is because its density — how tightly its material is packed together — is less than water's.

Uranus is tilted on its side. It rolls around the sun like a bowling ball instead of spinning upright. Scientists think something enormous crashed into it long ago and knocked it over. Methane gas fills its atmosphere and gives Uranus a blue-green color.

Neptune is the farthest planet from the sun. It is a cold, dark world with the strongest winds in the solar system. Those winds blast at over 1,200 miles per hour — more than twice as fast as a passenger jet.
stimulus.content_text:
The Giant Planets Far from the Sun
Jupiter is the largest planet. More than 1,300 Earths could fit inside it. Jupiter has over 90 moons. Four of the biggest were spotted by an astronomer named Galileo over 400 years ago.

Saturn is famous for its bright, beautiful rings. These rings are made of billions of pieces of ice and rock. Some pieces are as tiny as grains of sand. Others are as big as houses. Saturn is also very light for its size. If you could find a bathtub big enough, Saturn would float. That is because its density — how tightly its material is packed together — is less than water's.

Uranus is tilted on its side. It rolls around the sun like a bowling ball instead of spinning upright. Scientists think something enormous crashed into it long ago and knocked it over. Methane gas fills its atmosphere and gives Uranus a blue-green color.

Neptune is the farthest planet from the sun. It is a cold, dark world with the strongest winds in the solar system. Those winds blast at over 1,200 miles per hour — more than twice as fast as a passenger jet.

items.prompt: According to the text, what happened to Uranus before it started rolling around the sun like a bowling ball?

choices.text: Something enormous crashed into it.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage directly says something enormous crashed into Uranus and knocked it over, which is why it rolls around the sun like a bowling ball.

choices.text: Strong winds knocked the planet over.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the passage talks about strong winds on Neptune, not Uranus, and winds are never mentioned as the reason Uranus tilted.

choices.text: The planet started spinning upright.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because spinning upright is actually the opposite of what Uranus does — it was knocked away from spinning upright.

choices.text: Methane gas filled its atmosphere.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because methane gas explains Uranus's blue-green color, not why it is tilted on its side.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Eight Worlds, One Star

Every one of these planets orbits the same star — our sun. Yet no two of them look, feel, or behave alike. Rusty deserts, acid clouds, floating ice rings, sideways spins. The solar system is not just Earth's neighborhood. It is eight completely different answers to the question: what can a planet be?
stimulus.content_text:
Eight Worlds, One Star
Every one of these planets orbits the same star — our sun. Yet no two of them look, feel, or behave alike. Rusty deserts, acid clouds, floating ice rings, sideways spins. The solar system is not just Earth's neighborhood. It is eight completely different answers to the question: what can a planet be?

items.prompt: Based on the passage, how are the eight planets compared to each other?

choices.text: They are very similar because they orbit the same star.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage clearly says 'no two of them look, feel, or behave alike,' so sharing the same star does not make them similar.

choices.text: They are all different even though they share one sun.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage says all eight planets orbit the same sun but 'no two of them look, feel, or behave alike,' showing they are all different.

choices.text: They are exactly the same size but have different names.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage never mentions anything about the planets being the same size.

choices.text: They are different because each one has its own star.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage tells us all eight planets share the same star — our sun — not separate stars.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: According to the article, what is one major difference between the planets close to the sun and the planets far from the sun?

choices.text: The planets close to the sun are rocky, while the planets far from the sun are giant.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the article's two section headings clearly label the inner planets as 'Rocky' and the outer planets as 'Giant.'

choices.text: The planets close to the sun have rings, while the planets far from the sun have none.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because Saturn, one of the far planets, is the one famous for its rings — not the close planets.

choices.text: The planets close to the sun are freezing, while the planets far from the sun are hot.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the planets near the sun, like Venus and Mercury, are described as extremely hot, not freezing.

choices.text: The planets close to the sun are gas, while the planets far from the sun are metal.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the article never describes any planets as being made of metal — that detail does not appear in the passage.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: Which statement best explains why the author says the planets are "eight completely different answers" to what a planet can be?

choices.text: Because all the planets circle the same sun, they end up looking and acting the same way.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because the passage tells us the planets do NOT look or act the same — each one is completely different.

choices.text: Because different planets have different features, they look and behave in very different ways.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage gives many examples of different features — rust, acid clouds, icy rings, sideways spinning — showing every planet is unique.

choices.text: Because the planets far from the sun are bigger, they are the only planets with unusual traits.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because planets close to the sun, like Mercury and Venus, also have unusual traits like extreme temperatures and acid clouds.

choices.text: Because Earth has water and life, the other planets cannot have clouds, storms, or rings.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because the passage describes clouds on Venus, a giant storm on Jupiter, and rings on Saturn, so other planets definitely have interesting features too.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: Which heading should you read first and then next to find out the order of planets from the ones nearest the sun to the ones farthest away?

choices.text: First "The Giant Planets Far from the Sun," then "The Rocky Planets Close to the Sun"
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because it has the order backwards — reading about the far planets first and the close planets second would go from farthest to nearest, not nearest to farthest.

choices.text: First "The Rocky Planets Close to the Sun," then "The Giant Planets Far from the Sun"
choices.feedback: B is correct because reading 'The Rocky Planets Close to the Sun' first and then 'The Giant Planets Far from the Sun' takes you through all eight planets in order from the ones nearest the sun to the ones farthest away.

choices.text: First "More Than 1,300 Earths Could Fit Inside Jupiter," then "Eight Worlds, One Star"
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because 'More Than 1,300 Earths Could Fit Inside Jupiter' is just an introduction about Jupiter, and 'Eight Worlds, One Star' is the conclusion — neither section lists planets in order from the sun.

choices.text: First "Eight Worlds, One Star," then "More Than 1,300 Earths Could Fit Inside Jupiter"
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because those two headings are the introduction and conclusion of the article, and they don't give you a list of planets ordered by their distance from the sun.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: What is the main idea that the author supports by comparing details about all eight planets across the article?

choices.text: Each planet has a thick atmosphere that controls its temperature.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage tells us Mercury has almost no atmosphere, so not every planet has a thick one.

choices.text: The planets closest to the sun are always hotter than the far ones.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because Venus is farther from the sun than Mercury but is still the hottest planet, which proves closer doesn't always mean hotter.

choices.text: No two planets are alike even though they all orbit the same sun.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the author describes something unique and different about every single planet, and the ending directly says no two planets look, feel, or behave alike.

choices.text: The giant planets are more interesting than the rocky planets.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the author gives interesting facts about all eight planets equally and never says one group is better or more interesting than the other.

syllabus_metadata.title: A Tour of the Planets: Worlds of Fire, Ice, and Gas

assessments.title: Spin and Circle: Earth's Rotation and Revolution

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Earth's Spin, Earth's Trip, and the Secret of Seasons

The Trick the Sun Plays on You

Right now, you are moving at over 1,000 miles per hour. You cannot feel it, but Earth is spinning beneath your feet, carrying you along for the ride.

That spinning has a name: rotation. Rotation is Earth turning around and around, the way a basketball spins on a player's fingertip. Earth spins on an invisible line called its axis. Think of a stick pushed through the center of an apple, from top to bottom. Earth's axis runs from the North Pole to the South Pole, just like that.

As Earth rotates, the side facing the sun gets sunlight — that side has daytime. The side facing away sits in shadow — that side has nighttime. When your part of the world turns toward the sun, you see a sunrise. When it turns away, you see a sunset.

You might think the sun is sliding across the sky like a slow airplane. Actually, the sun stays put. It only looks like it travels overhead because you are the one spinning.
stimulus.content_text:
Earth's Spin, Earth's Trip, and the Secret of SeasonsThe Trick the Sun Plays on You
Right now, you are moving at over 1,000 miles per hour. You cannot feel it, but Earth is spinning beneath your feet, carrying you along for the ride.

That spinning has a name: rotation. Rotation is Earth turning around and around, the way a basketball spins on a player's fingertip. Earth spins on an invisible line called its axis. Think of a stick pushed through the center of an apple, from top to bottom. Earth's axis runs from the North Pole to the South Pole, just like that.

As Earth rotates, the side facing the sun gets sunlight — that side has daytime. The side facing away sits in shadow — that side has nighttime. When your part of the world turns toward the sun, you see a sunrise. When it turns away, you see a sunset.

You might think the sun is sliding across the sky like a slow airplane. Actually, the sun stays put. It only looks like it travels overhead because you are the one spinning.

items.prompt: According to the text, what is the difference between what the sun seems to do and what is really happening?

choices.text: The sun moves slowly, but Earth moves fast
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage never says the sun moves at all — slowly or quickly — it says the sun stays put.

choices.text: The sun stays put, but Earth is spinning
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly says 'the sun stays put' and that it only looks like it moves because Earth is spinning.

choices.text: The sun rises in the east, but sets in the west
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage never mentions which direction the sun rises or sets — east or west.

choices.text: The sun gives light, but the moon gives shadow
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage explains that Earth's own rotation causes shadow and night, and the moon is never mentioned.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
A Year-Long Road Trip Through Space

Earth does not just spin in place. It also travels around the sun along a big oval-shaped path called an orbit. This movement is called revolution — Earth's long, looping trip around the sun that takes a whole year to finish.

One full trip takes about 365 and one-quarter days. We call that one year. But what about that leftover quarter day? It adds up. After four years, those quarter days equal one full extra day. That is why every four years, we add a leap day — February 29th — to keep our calendar on track.
stimulus.content_text:
A Year-Long Road Trip Through Space
Earth does not just spin in place. It also travels around the sun along a big oval-shaped path called an orbit. This movement is called revolution — Earth's long, looping trip around the sun that takes a whole year to finish.

One full trip takes about 365 and one-quarter days. We call that one year. But what about that leftover quarter day? It adds up. After four years, those quarter days equal one full extra day. That is why every four years, we add a leap day — February 29th — to keep our calendar on track.

items.prompt: Based on the text, why do we add a leap day to the calendar every four years?

choices.text: Because Earth travels in a big oval path.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because Earth's oval-shaped path explains what an orbit is, but the passage never says that shape is the reason we add a leap day.

choices.text: Because the leftover quarter days add up.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage clearly states that the leftover quarter days from each year add up to one full extra day after four years, which is exactly why we add February 29th.

choices.text: Because the sun takes a year to finish its trip.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage says Earth travels around the sun — not the other way around — and that fact alone doesn't explain why we need a leap day.

choices.text: Because Earth spins in place on its own.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because Earth spinning in place is called rotation, and the passage never connects spinning to the reason we add a leap day.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
The Tilt That Changes Everything

For that, you need to know about Earth's tilt.

Earth's axis does not point straight up and down. It leans to one side, about 23.5 degrees. The real Earth tilts the same way, and that lean never changes direction as Earth orbits the sun.

Here is how the tilt creates seasons. When the Northern Hemisphere — the top half of Earth — leans toward the sun, sunlight hits that half more directly. Imagine holding a flashlight straight down over your hand. You get a bright, small circle of light. Now tilt the flashlight at an angle — the light spreads out and feels weaker. Direct sunlight works the same way. When sunlight hits your part of Earth straight on, it warms the ground more. The days stretch longer. That is summer.

At that same moment, the Southern Hemisphere — the bottom half of Earth — tilts away from the sun. It gets weaker, spread-out sunlight and shorter days. So when kids in New York splash in swimming pools, kids in Australia bundle up in coats.

Six months later, Earth has traveled to the other side of the sun. Now the Southern Hemisphere tilts toward the sun and gets summer, while the Northern Hemisphere tilts away and gets winter. Spring and autumn happen in between, as each half slowly shifts from more sunlight to less, or less to more.

Here is the surprising part: Earth is actually a little closer to the sun during January, when the Northern Hemisphere has winter.
