Earth's axis does not point straight up and down. It leans to one side, about 23.5 degrees. The real Earth tilts the same way, and that lean never changes direction as Earth orbits the sun.

Here is how the tilt creates seasons. When the Northern Hemisphere — the top half of Earth — leans toward the sun, sunlight hits that half more directly. Imagine holding a flashlight straight down over your hand. You get a bright, small circle of light. Now tilt the flashlight at an angle — the light spreads out and feels weaker. Direct sunlight works the same way. When sunlight hits your part of Earth straight on, it warms the ground more. The days stretch longer. That is summer.

At that same moment, the Southern Hemisphere — the bottom half of Earth — tilts away from the sun. It gets weaker, spread-out sunlight and shorter days. So when kids in New York splash in swimming pools, kids in Australia bundle up in coats.

Six months later, Earth has traveled to the other side of the sun. Now the Southern Hemisphere tilts toward the sun and gets summer, while the Northern Hemisphere tilts away and gets winter. Spring and autumn happen in between, as each half slowly shifts from more sunlight to less, or less to more.

Here is the surprising part: Earth is actually a little closer to the sun during January, when the Northern Hemisphere has winter.

items.prompt: According to the text, what happens in the Southern Hemisphere six months after the Northern Hemisphere leans toward the sun?

choices.text: It tilts away from the sun and gets winter.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because tilting away from the sun and getting winter is what the Southern Hemisphere does at the same time the Northern Hemisphere has summer, not six months later.

choices.text: It tilts toward the sun and gets summer.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly states that six months later, the Southern Hemisphere tilts toward the sun and gets summer.

choices.text: It stays tilted the same and keeps the same season.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage explains that Earth's position changes as it orbits the sun, so which hemisphere tilts toward the sun does change over time.

choices.text: It moves closer to the sun and gets summer.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage says it is the tilt toward the sun — not moving closer to the sun — that causes summer.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
You Are an Astronaut Who Never Left the Ground

Every sunrise you watch, every season you feel — these happen because you live on a tilted, spinning planet racing around a star. You do not need a spaceship to travel through space. You have been doing it your entire life.
stimulus.content_text:
You Are an Astronaut Who Never Left the Ground
Every sunrise you watch, every season you feel — these happen because you live on a tilted, spinning planet racing around a star. You do not need a spaceship to travel through space. You have been doing it your entire life.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, how is living on Earth similar to riding in a spaceship?

choices.text: Both are traveling through space.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage says you don't need a spaceship to travel through space — Earth is already racing through space, just like a spaceship would.

choices.text: Both have tilted, spinning floors.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because only Earth is described as tilted and spinning — the passage never says spaceships have tilted, spinning floors.

choices.text: Both create sunrises and seasons.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because sunrises and seasons are caused by Earth's tilt and spin, and spaceships don't create those things.

choices.text: Both stay on the ground forever.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the passage says the opposite — we are traveling through space our whole lives, not staying on the ground forever.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: Using the bold words to locate information in the text, what is the difference between **rotation** and **revolution**?

choices.text: Rotation means Earth spins in place, but revolution means Earth travels a path.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage says rotation is Earth spinning in place on its axis, while revolution is Earth traveling its oval-shaped orbit around the sun.

choices.text: Rotation means Earth takes one year, but revolution means Earth takes one day.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because it has the times backwards — rotation (spinning) takes one day, and revolution (the trip around the sun) takes one year.

choices.text: Rotation means Earth changes the seasons, but revolution means Earth changes the weather.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage explains that seasons are caused by Earth's tilt, not by rotation or revolution alone.

choices.text: Rotation means Earth moves the sun, but revolution means Earth stays perfectly still.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage clearly states that the sun stays put and Earth is the one that moves, not the other way around.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: Based on the whole article, why does the author compare a flashlight tilted at an angle to the way sunlight hits Earth?

choices.text: To show that Earth's spinning makes sunlight weaker at night
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the flashlight example is about Earth's tilt, not Earth's spinning, and it has nothing to do with nighttime.

choices.text: To help explain why Earth's tilt causes some places to get less warmth
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage uses the tilted flashlight to show that angled sunlight spreads out and feels weaker, just like what happens to places on Earth that tilt away from the sun.

choices.text: To prove that Earth is closer to the sun during summer months
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the article actually says Earth is closer to the sun in January during Northern Hemisphere winter, which is the opposite of what this answer says, and the flashlight has nothing to do with distance.

choices.text: To show that the sun moves across the sky like a flashlight beam
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the article clearly explains that the sun does NOT move across the sky — it only looks that way because Earth is spinning.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: How does the author order the key details to support the main idea that Earth is always moving?

choices.text: First by explaining how Earth spins to make day and night, and then by explaining how its trip around the sun makes seasons.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage first explains how Earth's spinning creates day and night, and then explains how Earth's orbit and tilt create the seasons — exactly in that order.

choices.text: First by explaining how Earth is closest to the sun in January, and then by explaining how spinning makes a leap day happen.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the fact about January being closest to the sun is just a small surprising detail, and it is spinning (rotation) that creates day and night, not leap days.

choices.text: First by explaining how the Southern Hemisphere gets summer, and then by explaining how the sun moves across the sky daily.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage explains the sun's movement across the sky first, and talks about the Southern Hemisphere's seasons later — the opposite of what this answer says.

choices.text: First by explaining how a flashlight makes direct sunlight, and then by explaining how an apple shows that Earth stays still.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the flashlight example explains how direct sunlight causes seasons, and the apple example explains Earth's axis — neither one is used to show that Earth stays still.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: Based on the article, how is Earth's actual axis different from a stick pushed straight up and down through an apple?

choices.text: Earth's axis leans to one side, while the stick points straight up and down.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage clearly states Earth's axis leans about 23.5 degrees to one side, unlike a stick pushed straight up and down through an apple.

choices.text: Earth's axis is a real solid object, while the stick is an invisible line.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the passage calls Earth's axis an 'invisible line,' meaning it is NOT a real solid object — the stick is the solid object in this comparison.

choices.text: Earth's axis changes its direction, while the stick keeps the same direction.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage says Earth's tilt 'never changes direction' as Earth orbits the sun.

choices.text: Earth's axis travels in a big oval, while the stick stays in one single spot.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because traveling in a big oval describes Earth's orbit around the sun, not anything about the axis itself.

syllabus_metadata.title: Spin and Circle: Earth's Rotation and Revolution

assessments.title: The Changing Moon: Phases from New to Full

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Moon Is Lying to You

A Shape-Shifter in the Sky

Every single night, the Moon is lying to you. The Moon looks like it is changing shape — growing, shrinking, melting away. But here is the truth: the Moon never changes shape at all. What changes is how much of its bright side you can see from where you stand on Earth.
stimulus.content_text:
The Moon Is Lying to YouA Shape-Shifter in the Sky
Every single night, the Moon is lying to you. The Moon looks like it is changing shape — growing, shrinking, melting away. But here is the truth: the Moon never changes shape at all. What changes is how much of its bright side you can see from where you stand on Earth.

items.prompt: According to the text, the Moon looks like it is changing shape, but what is the truth?

choices.text: It grows larger every single night.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage says the Moon never changes shape — it does not actually grow larger every night.

choices.text: It melts away into the dark sky.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because 'melting away' is just how the Moon looks to us, and the passage tells us that is not the truth.

choices.text: It never changes its shape at all.
choices.feedback: C is correct because the passage directly states, 'the Moon never changes shape at all' — that is the truth the author wants us to know.

choices.text: It turns to hide its bright side.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage never mentions the Moon turning around to hide its bright side.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Borrowed Light

The Moon has a secret. It does not make its own light. The Sun is like a giant flashlight, and the Moon is like a ball sitting in that flashlight's beam. The sunlight bounces off the Moon's rocky surface and travels to your eyes. That bounce is called reflecting. At any moment, the Sun lights up exactly one half of the Moon. The other half sits in darkness.

You might think Earth's shadow covers part of the Moon and causes those changing shapes. Smart guess, but that only happens during a rare event called a lunar eclipse. The real reason is simpler: as the Moon moves around Earth, you see its sunlit half from different angles.
stimulus.content_text:
Borrowed Light
The Moon has a secret. It does not make its own light. The Sun is like a giant flashlight, and the Moon is like a ball sitting in that flashlight's beam. The sunlight bounces off the Moon's rocky surface and travels to your eyes. That bounce is called reflecting. At any moment, the Sun lights up exactly one half of the Moon. The other half sits in darkness.

You might think Earth's shadow covers part of the Moon and causes those changing shapes. Smart guess, but that only happens during a rare event called a lunar eclipse. The real reason is simpler: as the Moon moves around Earth, you see its sunlit half from different angles.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, what causes the Moon to seem to change shape?

choices.text: Earth's shadow covers the Moon
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage says Earth's shadow only blocks the Moon during a rare event called a lunar eclipse, not regularly.

choices.text: The Moon moves around Earth
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly states that as the Moon moves around Earth, we see its sunlit half from different angles, causing the changing shapes.

choices.text: The Sun turns its light away
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage never mentions the Sun turning its light away — the Sun keeps shining on the Moon.

choices.text: The Moon makes less light
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the Moon doesn't make any light at all; it only reflects light that comes from the Sun.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
A Month of Moon Faces

The Moon orbits Earth — it travels in a big loop around our planet, the way a runner circles a track. One full loop takes about 29.5 days. During that loop, the Moon shows you different amounts of its bright side. These different views are called the phases of the Moon.

The cycle begins with the new moon. During this phase, the Moon sits between Earth and the Sun. Its sunlit side faces away from you. Its dark side faces toward you. The Moon is up there, but you cannot see it at all.

Over the next few days, a thin, curved slice of light appears on the Moon's right side. This is a crescent moon — shaped like a fingernail clipping. The bright slice grows wider each night. When exactly half of the Moon's face glows bright, you have reached the first quarter. That name sounds strange because the Moon looks half lit. But "quarter" means the Moon has finished one quarter of its trip around Earth.

The bright area keeps growing. When most of the Moon's face shines but a small dark edge remains, it is called a gibbous moon — a word that means "humped," like a balloon almost fully blown up. Finally, about two weeks after the new moon, you see the entire sunlit half. This is the full moon, round and blazing. Earth is now between the Sun and the Moon, so the whole bright side faces you.
stimulus.content_text:
A Month of Moon Faces
The Moon orbits Earth — it travels in a big loop around our planet, the way a runner circles a track. One full loop takes about 29.5 days. During that loop, the Moon shows you different amounts of its bright side. These different views are called the phases of the Moon.

The cycle begins with the new moon. During this phase, the Moon sits between Earth and the Sun. Its sunlit side faces away from you. Its dark side faces toward you. The Moon is up there, but you cannot see it at all.

Over the next few days, a thin, curved slice of light appears on the Moon's right side. This is a crescent moon — shaped like a fingernail clipping. The bright slice grows wider each night. When exactly half of the Moon's face glows bright, you have reached the first quarter. That name sounds strange because the Moon looks half lit. But "quarter" means the Moon has finished one quarter of its trip around Earth.

The bright area keeps growing. When most of the Moon's face shines but a small dark edge remains, it is called a gibbous moon — a word that means "humped," like a balloon almost fully blown up. Finally, about two weeks after the new moon, you see the entire sunlit half. This is the full moon, round and blazing. Earth is now between the Sun and the Moon, so the whole bright side faces you.

items.prompt: According to the passage, what happens to the bright area of the Moon between the first quarter phase and the full moon phase?

choices.text: It shrinks until the Moon looks like a fingernail clipping.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the crescent that looks like a fingernail clipping happens near the start of the cycle, long before the first quarter phase.

choices.text: It keeps growing until the entire sunlit half is visible.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly says 'The bright area keeps growing' after the first quarter, all the way until the full moon shows the entire sunlit half.

choices.text: It stays the same size for about two weeks.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage says the bright area keeps growing during this time, not staying the same size.

choices.text: It disappears because the dark side faces Earth.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the dark side facing Earth describes the new moon phase at the beginning of the cycle, not what happens after the first quarter.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Growing and Shrinking

After the full moon, everything runs backward. The bright area shrinks night after night. You see a gibbous moon again, then a third quarter moon (another half circle, but lit on the opposite side), then a thin crescent. Finally the Moon slips back to new, and the whole cycle starts over.

When the Moon's bright area is growing larger each night, astronomers say it is waxing — think of a candle gaining wax. When the bright area is shrinking, they say it is waning — like that candle melting down.
stimulus.content_text:
Growing and Shrinking
After the full moon, everything runs backward. The bright area shrinks night after night. You see a gibbous moon again, then a third quarter moon (another half circle, but lit on the opposite side), then a thin crescent. Finally the Moon slips back to new, and the whole cycle starts over.

When the Moon's bright area is growing larger each night, astronomers say it is waxing — think of a candle gaining wax. When the bright area is shrinking, they say it is waning — like that candle melting down.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, how does the meaning of 'waxing' compare to the meaning of 'waning'?

choices.text: Waxing means the Moon is bright, and waning means it is dark
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because waxing and waning describe whether the bright area is growing or shrinking, not simply whether the Moon is bright or dark.

choices.text: Waxing means getting bigger, and waning means getting smaller
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly says waxing means the bright area is 'growing larger' and waning means it is 'shrinking.'

choices.text: Waxing means the Moon is full, and waning means it is new
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because waxing and waning describe a change happening over time, not the specific phases of full moon or new moon.

choices.text: Waxing means melting down, and waning means gaining wax
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because it has the meanings switched — the passage says waxing is like a candle gaining wax (bigger) and waning is like melting down (smaller).

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Your Calendar in the Sky

The word "month" actually comes from the word "moon." Thousands of years ago, before anyone had a printed calendar, people counted time by watching these phases. One full cycle of the Moon equaled roughly one month.

You are reading the same clock that humans have read for thousands of years — light reflected off a giant ball of rock hanging in the sky.
stimulus.content_text:
Your Calendar in the Sky
The word "month" actually comes from the word "moon." Thousands of years ago, before anyone had a printed calendar, people counted time by watching these phases. One full cycle of the Moon equaled roughly one month.

You are reading the same clock that humans have read for thousands of years — light reflected off a giant ball of rock hanging in the sky.

items.prompt: According to the text, what is a month?

choices.text: one full turn of the Earth
choices.feedback: The passage never says a month is about the Earth turning — that's actually what makes a day, not a month.

choices.text: one full cycle of the Moon
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage directly says 'one full cycle of the Moon equaled roughly one month.'

choices.text: one full trip around the Sun
choices.feedback: A trip around the Sun takes a whole year, and the passage doesn't mention the Sun when explaining what a month is.

choices.text: one full change in the stars
choices.feedback: The passage never talks about stars changing to measure a month — only the Moon's cycle is mentioned.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: How is a new moon different from a full moon?

choices.text: A new moon has its dark side facing Earth, but a full moon has its bright side facing Earth.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage says the new moon's dark side faces Earth so you can't see it, while the full moon's entire bright side faces Earth so you see it fully lit.

choices.text: A new moon happens when Earth blocks the Sun, but a full moon happens when nothing is blocked.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because Earth blocking the Sun only happens during a lunar eclipse, which the passage says is a rare event — not what causes the regular phases.

choices.text: A new moon reflects no sunlight at all, but a full moon reflects sunlight from both sides.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage explains that the Sun always lights up exactly one half of the Moon, so the Moon is always reflecting sunlight on one side.

choices.text: A new moon is only lit on one half, but a full moon is lit on every part of its surface.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage clearly states that the Sun lights up exactly one half of the Moon at any moment — never the whole surface.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: To find out what causes the Moon to shine and what causes its bright area to shrink, which two headings should you look under?

choices.text: The headings "A Shape-Shifter in the Sky" and "A Month of Moon Faces"
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because 'A Shape-Shifter in the Sky' only gives a quick intro about the Moon looking like it changes, and 'A Month of Moon Faces' talks about the phases but not what makes the Moon shine or why its bright area shrinks.

choices.text: The headings "Borrowed Light" and "Growing and Shrinking"
choices.feedback: B is correct because 'Borrowed Light' explains that the Moon shines by reflecting sunlight, and 'Growing and Shrinking' explains that the Moon's bright area shrinks during the waning part of its cycle.

choices.text: The headings "A Month of Moon Faces" and "Your Calendar in the Sky"
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because 'A Month of Moon Faces' focuses on the different phases and 'Your Calendar in the Sky' talks about how people used the Moon to track months — neither one fully explains what makes the Moon shine.

choices.text: The headings "A Shape-Shifter in the Sky" and "Borrowed Light"
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because 'A Shape-Shifter in the Sky' is just a short introduction and doesn't explain why the bright area shrinks, even though 'Borrowed Light' does correctly explain why the Moon shines.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: The main idea of the article is that the Moon's bright shape seems to change in a repeating pattern. Which sequence of details best supports this main idea?

choices.text: The Moon changes from new, to first quarter, to full, to third quarter, and back to new.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage clearly describes the Moon's phases in exactly this order: new moon, crescent, first quarter, gibbous, full moon, then back through gibbous, third quarter, crescent, and new moon again.

choices.text: The Moon changes from new, to third quarter, to full, to first quarter, and back to new.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because it lists third quarter before full moon, but the article tells us full moon comes first, and third quarter happens afterward as the Moon's bright side shrinks.

choices.text: The Moon changes from new, to a lunar eclipse, to full, to a lunar eclipse, and back to new.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the article explains that a lunar eclipse is a rare event, not a regular phase that happens every month in the Moon's cycle.

choices.text: The Moon changes from new, to Earth's shadow, to full, to Earth's shadow, and back to new.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the article actually tells us that Earth's shadow is NOT what causes the Moon's changing shapes — that's a common mix-up the passage corrects.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: Based on the article, how are the first quarter moon and the third quarter moon alike?

choices.text: They both show exactly half of the Moon's face shining.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the article describes both the first quarter and third quarter as half-circle shapes, meaning exactly half of the Moon's face is shining in both phases.

choices.text: They both mean the Moon has finished a quarter trip.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because only the first quarter moon means the Moon has finished one quarter of its journey — the third quarter moon means it has completed three quarters of the trip.

choices.text: They both appear when the Moon's bright area is growing.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because after the full moon the bright area starts shrinking, so the third quarter moon appears while the Moon is waning, not waxing.

choices.text: They both look like a thin fingernail clipping in space.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the thin fingernail clipping shape describes the crescent moon, which is a different phase that comes before the first quarter.

syllabus_metadata.title: The Changing Moon: Phases from New to Full

assessments.title: When Shadows Cross: Solar and Lunar Eclipses

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
When the Sky Goes Dark: Solar and Lunar Eclipses

Day Turns to Night

On August 21, 2017, chickens across America walked into their coops to sleep. It was 2:30 in the afternoon.

A solar eclipse happens when the Moon slides directly between Earth and the Sun. The Moon blocks the Sun's light and casts a shadow on part of Earth's surface. The Moon is simply in the way, like a hand held in front of a flashlight.

Here's the surprising part: the Sun is about 400 times wider than the Moon. So how can the tiny Moon cover the giant Sun? The Moon is also about 400 times closer to us. That lucky match means both objects look almost exactly the same size in our sky. The Moon fits over the Sun like a perfect lid on a pot.
stimulus.content_text:
When the Sky Goes Dark: Solar and Lunar EclipsesDay Turns to Night
On August 21, 2017, chickens across America walked into their coops to sleep. It was 2:30 in the afternoon.

A solar eclipse happens when the Moon slides directly between Earth and the Sun. The Moon blocks the Sun's light and casts a shadow on part of Earth's surface. The Moon is simply in the way, like a hand held in front of a flashlight.

Here's the surprising part: the Sun is about 400 times wider than the Moon. So how can the tiny Moon cover the giant Sun? The Moon is also about 400 times closer to us. That lucky match means both objects look almost exactly the same size in our sky. The Moon fits over the Sun like a perfect lid on a pot.

items.prompt: According to the text, which contrast explains how the Moon can cover the Sun?

choices.text: The Moon is brighter, but the Sun is dimmer.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage never says anything about the Sun being dimmer or the Moon being brighter.

choices.text: The Sun is wider, but the Moon is closer.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage clearly states the Sun is 400 times wider but the Moon is 400 times closer, and that lucky match makes them look the same size in our sky.

choices.text: The Sun is cooler, but the Moon is hotter.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage does not mention the temperature of the Sun or the Moon at all.

choices.text: The Moon is larger, but the Sun is farther.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because it has the facts backwards — the passage says the Sun is the larger one and the Moon is the closer one, not the other way around.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
A Narrow Strip of Shadow

The Moon's shadow on Earth is small. It covers a narrow strip only about 100 miles wide. If you stand inside that strip, you see a total eclipse. For a few minutes, everything feels like nighttime.

People outside that strip see a partial eclipse, where the Moon covers only part of the Sun. They still get daylight. They miss the full show. At any one spot on Earth, a total solar eclipse happens only about once every 375 years. That is why people travel hundreds of miles to stand inside the shadow.
stimulus.content_text:
A Narrow Strip of Shadow
The Moon's shadow on Earth is small. It covers a narrow strip only about 100 miles wide. If you stand inside that strip, you see a total eclipse. For a few minutes, everything feels like nighttime.

People outside that strip see a partial eclipse, where the Moon covers only part of the Sun. They still get daylight. They miss the full show. At any one spot on Earth, a total solar eclipse happens only about once every 375 years. That is why people travel hundreds of miles to stand inside the shadow.

items.prompt: Based on the text, why do people travel hundreds of miles to see a total solar eclipse?

choices.text: Because it happens very rarely in one single place.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage says a total solar eclipse only happens at one spot every 375 years, which is so rare that people travel far to see it.

choices.text: Because the Moon's shadow covers the entire planet.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the text clearly says the Moon's shadow only covers a narrow strip about 100 miles wide, not the entire planet.

choices.text: Because they want to watch a partial eclipse instead.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because people travel to see the total eclipse, and the passage says those outside the strip only get a partial eclipse and 'miss the full show.'

choices.text: Because the sky stays completely dark for many hours.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the passage says the darkness lasts only 'a few minutes,' not many hours.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
The Moon Turns Red

The Moon does not make its own light. So what happens when Earth blocks that sunlight?

A lunar eclipse happens when Earth passes between the Sun and the Moon. Earth's shadow falls across the Moon, and the Moon slowly darkens. Unlike a solar eclipse, anyone on the nighttime side of Earth can watch it. That makes lunar eclipses much easier to see.

During a total lunar eclipse, the Moon does not go completely black. Instead, it often glows a deep, coppery red. This happens because some sunlight bends around Earth's atmosphere — the thick blanket of air that wraps around our planet. The atmosphere filters out blue light and lets red light pass through. That red light reaches the Moon and paints it with a warm glow. People sometimes call this a "blood moon."
stimulus.content_text:
The Moon Turns Red
The Moon does not make its own light. So what happens when Earth blocks that sunlight?

A lunar eclipse happens when Earth passes between the Sun and the Moon. Earth's shadow falls across the Moon, and the Moon slowly darkens. Unlike a solar eclipse, anyone on the nighttime side of Earth can watch it. That makes lunar eclipses much easier to see.

During a total lunar eclipse, the Moon does not go completely black. Instead, it often glows a deep, coppery red. This happens because some sunlight bends around Earth's atmosphere — the thick blanket of air that wraps around our planet. The atmosphere filters out blue light and lets red light pass through. That red light reaches the Moon and paints it with a warm glow. People sometimes call this a "blood moon."

items.prompt: According to the passage, what happens right after Earth's shadow falls across the Moon?

choices.text: The Moon starts to make its own light
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage clearly states that the Moon does not make its own light.

choices.text: The Moon slowly begins to darken
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says that after Earth's shadow falls across the Moon, 'the Moon slowly darkens.'

choices.text: The Sun moves behind the Moon
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because during a lunar eclipse, Earth passes between the Sun and the Moon — the Sun does not move behind the Moon.

choices.text: The atmosphere filters out red light
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says the atmosphere filters out blue light and lets red light pass through, not the other way around.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Why Not Every Month?

The Moon orbits Earth — travels around our planet — about once every month. So why don't we get eclipses every month? The Moon's path is slightly tilted. Picture tossing a ball at a target on a wall. Most throws go a little too high or too low. Only once in a while does the ball hit the bullseye. The Moon usually passes just above or below the perfect lineup. Only when everything lines up exactly does an eclipse happen.
stimulus.content_text:
Why Not Every Month?
The Moon orbits Earth — travels around our planet — about once every month. So why don't we get eclipses every month? The Moon's path is slightly tilted. Picture tossing a ball at a target on a wall. Most throws go a little too high or too low. Only once in a while does the ball hit the bullseye. The Moon usually passes just above or below the perfect lineup. Only when everything lines up exactly does an eclipse happen.

items.prompt: The author compares tossing a ball to the Moon's path. Based on this comparison, what does the word "bullseye" mean in this text?

choices.text: A lineup that is exactly perfect
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage tells us an eclipse only happens when everything lines up exactly, and the bullseye in the comparison represents that perfect, exact lineup.

choices.text: A path that is tilted slightly
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the tilted path is the reason the Moon usually misses the perfect lineup, not what the bullseye means.

choices.text: A throw that goes much too high
choices.feedback: C is wrong because a throw going too high is an example of missing the bullseye, which is the opposite of what a bullseye means.

choices.text: An orbit that travels around the Earth
choices.feedback: D is wrong because orbiting Earth is just the Moon's regular monthly path, not the special perfect lineup that the bullseye represents.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Watching the Same Sky

Thousands of years ago, people in ancient China banged drums and pots during solar eclipses. They believed a dragon was eating the Sun and wanted to scare it away. In parts of ancient India, people thought a demon had swallowed the Moon during a lunar eclipse. Every culture had its own story for these strange, sudden changes in the sky.

Today, scientists use eclipses to study the Sun's outer layers and test how light bends through Earth's atmosphere. But you don't need to be a scientist to feel the power of an eclipse. The next time the Moon's shadow crosses your part of the world, step outside. You will be watching the same event that made ancient drummers pound their drums — and made chickens go to bed in the middle of the afternoon.
stimulus.content_text:
Watching the Same Sky
Thousands of years ago, people in ancient China banged drums and pots during solar eclipses. They believed a dragon was eating the Sun and wanted to scare it away. In parts of ancient India, people thought a demon had swallowed the Moon during a lunar eclipse. Every culture had its own story for these strange, sudden changes in the sky.

Today, scientists use eclipses to study the Sun's outer layers and test how light bends through Earth's atmosphere. But you don't need to be a scientist to feel the power of an eclipse. The next time the Moon's shadow crosses your part of the world, step outside. You will be watching the same event that made ancient drummers pound their drums — and made chickens go to bed in the middle of the afternoon.

items.prompt: According to the text, what does the author say scientists use eclipses to do?

choices.text: to scare away dragons from the Sun
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because scaring away dragons is what ancient Chinese people believed, not something scientists do.

choices.text: to study the Sun's outer layers
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly states that scientists use eclipses to study the Sun's outer layers.

choices.text: to watch chickens go to bed early
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because chickens going to bed early is a fun detail in the passage, but it has nothing to do with what scientists use eclipses for.

choices.text: to bang drums and pots loudly
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because banging drums and pots is what ancient people in China did during eclipses, not what scientists do.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: How is watching a solar eclipse different from watching a lunar eclipse?

choices.text: A solar eclipse makes the Moon turn red, but a lunar eclipse makes the sky go dark
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage says the Moon turns red during a lunar eclipse, not a solar eclipse, and it's a solar eclipse that makes the sky go dark like nighttime.

choices.text: A solar eclipse can be seen only from a narrow strip, but a lunar eclipse can be seen widely
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage says a solar eclipse is only visible from a narrow 100-mile-wide strip, while a lunar eclipse can be seen by anyone on the nighttime side of Earth.

choices.text: A solar eclipse happens every month, but a lunar eclipse happens only once every 375 years
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage says a total solar eclipse happens at one spot only about once every 375 years — not every month.

choices.text: A solar eclipse is caused by Earth's shadow, but a lunar eclipse is caused by the Moon's shadow
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because it's backwards — a solar eclipse is caused by the Moon's shadow falling on Earth, and a lunar eclipse is caused by Earth's shadow falling on the Moon.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: Based on the section headings and the information in the article, which heading helps a reader find the cause of the Moon turning a coppery red color during a lunar eclipse?

choices.text: "Day Turns to Night" because it explains how light is blocked
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because 'Day Turns to Night' is about solar eclipses and how the Moon blocks sunlight from reaching Earth, not about why the Moon turns red.

choices.text: "The Moon Turns Red" because it explains how Earth's atmosphere bends light
choices.feedback: B is correct because the section 'The Moon Turns Red' directly explains that Earth's atmosphere bends red light onto the Moon, causing its coppery red glow during a lunar eclipse.

choices.text: "Why Not Every Month?" because it explains how the Moon's path is tilted
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because 'Why Not Every Month?' explains why eclipses don't happen every month due to the Moon's tilted path, which has nothing to do with the Moon's red color.

choices.text: "A Narrow Strip of Shadow" because it explains how shadows move across Earth
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because 'A Narrow Strip of Shadow' describes how the Moon's small shadow falls on Earth during a solar eclipse, not what causes the Moon to turn red.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: The main idea of the article is that eclipses happen when space objects block sunlight and create shadows. Which sequence of details best supports this main idea?

choices.text: First, the text explains how the Moon casts a shadow, and later how Earth casts a shadow.
choices.feedback: A is correct because both details — the Moon's shadow and Earth's shadow — directly support the main idea that eclipses happen when space objects block sunlight and create shadows.

choices.text: First, the text explains how the chickens go to sleep, and later how ancient people bang loud drums.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because chickens going to sleep and people banging drums are interesting details, but they don't explain how eclipses happen or support the main idea about shadows.

choices.text: First, the text explains how the Moon orbits our Earth, and later how it misses the perfect lineup.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the Moon's orbit and missing the lineup explain why eclipses don't happen every month, not how shadows cause eclipses.

choices.text: First, the text explains how some people stand in a strip, and later how scientists test bending light.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because people standing in a strip and scientists testing light are small supporting details, not the key details that explain the main idea about shadows blocking sunlight.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: Which comparison best shows how the article explains that solar and lunar eclipses are different to watch from Earth?

choices.text: A solar eclipse can be seen by everyone on Earth's daytime side, but a lunar eclipse can be seen only in a 100-mile strip.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because it has the two eclipses switched — the solar eclipse is seen in a narrow 100-mile strip, and the lunar eclipse can be seen by many more people, not the other way around.

choices.text: A solar eclipse darkens a small part of Earth, but a lunar eclipse can be watched by anyone on Earth's nighttime side.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the article says the solar eclipse shadow covers only a small 100-mile strip on Earth, while a lunar eclipse can be watched by anyone on the nighttime side of Earth.

choices.text: A solar eclipse happens every month, but a lunar eclipse happens only once every 375 years in one place.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the article says a total solar eclipse happens only once every 375 years in one spot, not a lunar eclipse, and neither one happens every month.

choices.text: A solar eclipse makes the Moon glow red, but a lunar eclipse makes the Sun look like a perfect lid.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because it mixes up the details — the Moon glows red during a lunar eclipse, and the Moon fits over the Sun like a lid during a solar eclipse, not the other way around.

syllabus_metadata.title: When Shadows Cross: Solar and Lunar Eclipses

assessments.title: Beyond Our Solar System: Constellations and Galaxies

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Pictures in the Stars

Stars Hide in Daylight

You cannot see them because the Sun is too bright. Its light floods the sky and drowns out every other star. When the Sun sets and the sky turns black, they finally appear — like turning off a bright lamp so you can see tiny candles on the ceiling.

Your Sun is a star, too — the same kind of burning ball of gas as the stars you see at night. It only looks bigger and brighter because it is so much closer to Earth.
stimulus.content_text:
Pictures in the StarsStars Hide in Daylight
You cannot see them because the Sun is too bright. Its light floods the sky and drowns out every other star. When the Sun sets and the sky turns black, they finally appear — like turning off a bright lamp so you can see tiny candles on the ceiling.

Your Sun is a star, too — the same kind of burning ball of gas as the stars you see at night. It only looks bigger and brighter because it is so much closer to Earth.

items.prompt: According to the text, what is different about the Sun and the other stars?

choices.text: The Sun is hidden in daylight, but the other stars are easy to see.
choices.feedback: This is backwards — the passage says the other stars are hidden during the day because the Sun is too bright, not the other way around.

choices.text: The Sun is closer to Earth, but the other stars are farther away.
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says the Sun only looks bigger and brighter because it is much closer to Earth, meaning the other stars are farther away.

choices.text: The Sun is smaller than Earth, but the other stars are larger.
choices.feedback: The passage never compares the size of the Sun to Earth, so there is no evidence for this answer.

choices.text: The Sun is not a star, but the other stars are stars.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because the passage directly says 'Your Sun is a star, too — the same kind of burning ball of gas as the stars you see at night.'

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Connecting the Dots

For thousands of years, people have looked at groups of stars and imagined pictures. A line of bright dots became a bear. A cluster of lights became a hunter. These star patterns are called constellations — groups of stars that seem to form shapes when you look at them from Earth. Think of them like connect-the-dot drawings spread across the night sky.

Some stars in a single constellation are millions of miles apart. They only look close together because of where we stand on Earth.

One of the easiest patterns to find is the Big Dipper. It looks like a giant cup with a long handle. The two stars at the front of the cup point straight toward Polaris, the North Star. For hundreds of years, travelers used Polaris to figure out which direction was north. If you were lost at sea with no map, that one star could guide you home.

Another famous constellation is Orion the Hunter. Look for three bright stars in a straight row — that is his belt. Ancient Greek storytellers said Orion was a mighty hunter placed among the stars by the gods.
stimulus.content_text:
Connecting the Dots
For thousands of years, people have looked at groups of stars and imagined pictures. A line of bright dots became a bear. A cluster of lights became a hunter. These star patterns are called constellations — groups of stars that seem to form shapes when you look at them from Earth. Think of them like connect-the-dot drawings spread across the night sky.

Some stars in a single constellation are millions of miles apart. They only look close together because of where we stand on Earth.

One of the easiest patterns to find is the Big Dipper. It looks like a giant cup with a long handle. The two stars at the front of the cup point straight toward Polaris, the North Star. For hundreds of years, travelers used Polaris to figure out which direction was north. If you were lost at sea with no map, that one star could guide you home.

Another famous constellation is Orion the Hunter. Look for three bright stars in a straight row — that is his belt. Ancient Greek storytellers said Orion was a mighty hunter placed among the stars by the gods.

items.prompt: Based on the text, why do the stars in a constellation seem to be close together?

choices.text: Because we view them from our specific place on Earth.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage directly says stars only look close together 'because of where we stand on Earth,' meaning our viewing spot makes them appear near each other.

choices.text: Because they are joined by bright, glowing lines in space.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the passage never mentions any glowing lines in space — connect-the-dot lines are just a way we imagine the shapes, not something real in the sky.

choices.text: Because they are pulled tightly toward the bright North Star.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because Polaris helps people find north, but the passage never says it pulls other stars toward it.

choices.text: Because they were placed in tight groups by ancient gods.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the story about gods placing Orion in the sky is an ancient myth, and the passage does not say gods arranged stars into tight groups.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Same Stars, Different Stories

Different cultures looked at the same sky and saw different things. The pattern that Greeks called a great bear, some Native American groups saw as a different animal entirely. What one group called a hunter, another group called a farmer or a warrior. The stars did not change. The stories did. Every culture painted its own pictures onto the same sky.
stimulus.content_text:
Same Stars, Different Stories
Different cultures looked at the same sky and saw different things. The pattern that Greeks called a great bear, some Native American groups saw as a different animal entirely. What one group called a hunter, another group called a farmer or a warrior. The stars did not change. The stories did. Every culture painted its own pictures onto the same sky.

items.prompt: According to the text, what happened after different cultures looked at the same stars?

choices.text: They changed the stars in the sky.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage clearly says 'The stars did not change' — no one could change the stars!

choices.text: They told different stories about them.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage says 'The stories did' change, and gives examples of different groups telling different stories about the same stars.

choices.text: They all named the stars the same way.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage shows that different groups gave the stars different names, like 'great bear' versus 'a different animal entirely.'

choices.text: They looked at different skies at night.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage says every culture looked at the same sky — that's the whole point of the story!

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Our Galaxy and Beyond

Every star you can see at night belongs to our galaxy — a huge collection of stars, gas, dust, and planets held together by gravity, the invisible force that pulls objects toward each other. Gravity is what keeps your feet on the ground. It also keeps billions of stars spinning together in one enormous group.

Our galaxy is called the Milky Way. It holds between 100 and 400 billion stars. Our Sun is just one of them. On a very dark night, far from city lights, you can sometimes see the Milky Way as a pale, glowing stripe across the sky. That glow comes from billions of distant stars blending their light together.

As huge as the Milky Way is, it is only one galaxy among billions. Some galaxies are spiral-shaped, swirling like pinwheels. Others are round like eggs. Still others have no clear shape at all. The distances between galaxies are so vast that scientists measure them in millions of light-years — a light-year is the distance light travels in one full year, nearly six trillion miles.
stimulus.content_text:
Our Galaxy and Beyond
Every star you can see at night belongs to our galaxy — a huge collection of stars, gas, dust, and planets held together by gravity, the invisible force that pulls objects toward each other. Gravity is what keeps your feet on the ground. It also keeps billions of stars spinning together in one enormous group.

Our galaxy is called the Milky Way. It holds between 100 and 400 billion stars. Our Sun is just one of them. On a very dark night, far from city lights, you can sometimes see the Milky Way as a pale, glowing stripe across the sky. That glow comes from billions of distant stars blending their light together.

As huge as the Milky Way is, it is only one galaxy among billions. Some galaxies are spiral-shaped, swirling like pinwheels. Others are round like eggs. Still others have no clear shape at all. The distances between galaxies are so vast that scientists measure them in millions of light-years — a light-year is the distance light travels in one full year, nearly six trillion miles.

items.prompt: Based on the text, how are spiral-shaped galaxies similar to pinwheels?

choices.text: They both are made of bright gas.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage never mentions that pinwheels or spiral galaxies are made of bright gas.

choices.text: They both move in a swirling way.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage says spiral galaxies swirl 'like pinwheels,' and pinwheels are known for their swirling, spinning shape.

choices.text: They both travel for a full year.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because traveling for a full year is part of the definition of a light-year, which has nothing to do with pinwheels or spiral galaxies.

choices.text: They both look like glowing eggs.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage describes egg-shaped galaxies as a separate kind, not the spiral ones that look like pinwheels.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
A Tiny Speck with Big Eyes

Earth is one small planet circling one star in a galaxy of hundreds of billions of stars, in a universe of billions of galaxies. But we are a tiny speck that looks up. They are compasses, calendars, and storybooks written in light — and you already know how to start reading them.
stimulus.content_text:
A Tiny Speck with Big Eyes
Earth is one small planet circling one star in a galaxy of hundreds of billions of stars, in a universe of billions of galaxies. But we are a tiny speck that looks up. They are compasses, calendars, and storybooks written in light — and you already know how to start reading them.

items.prompt: In the section 'A Tiny Speck with Big Eyes,' which phrase does the author use to define Earth and the people on it?

choices.text: A tiny speck that looks up
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly says 'we are a tiny speck that looks up' to describe Earth and the people living on it.

choices.text: A storybook written in light
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because 'a storybook written in light' describes the stars, not Earth or the people on it.

choices.text: A galaxy of many bright stars
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because 'a galaxy of many bright stars' describes outer space, not Earth or its people.

choices.text: A compass used for reading
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because a compass is used to describe what the stars are like for us, not what Earth or people are.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: Under which two headings would you look to find the difference between a star pattern and an enormous group of billions of stars?

choices.text: "Stars Hide in Daylight" and "Connecting the Dots"
choices.feedback: Wrong because 'Stars Hide in Daylight' is about why the Sun blocks our view of stars during the day — it doesn't explain constellations or galaxies.

choices.text: "Connecting the Dots" and "Our Galaxy and Beyond"
choices.feedback: Correct! 'Connecting the Dots' explains star patterns called constellations, and 'Our Galaxy and Beyond' explains that a galaxy is a huge group of billions of stars.

choices.text: "Same Stars, Different Stories" and "A Tiny Speck with Big Eyes"
choices.feedback: Wrong because 'Same Stars, Different Stories' is about how different cultures saw different pictures in the same stars, and neither section explains what a galaxy is.

choices.text: "Stars Hide in Daylight" and "Our Galaxy and Beyond"
choices.feedback: Wrong because 'Stars Hide in Daylight' only talks about the Sun being too bright to see other stars — it doesn't describe star patterns like constellations.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: According to the article, why were travelers able to use Polaris to find their way north?

choices.text: because Polaris is the brightest star in the Milky Way galaxy
choices.feedback: The article never says Polaris is the brightest star in the Milky Way — it only says the Big Dipper points to it.

choices.text: because the Big Dipper's front stars point toward Polaris
choices.feedback: This is correct! The article says the two front stars of the Big Dipper point straight toward Polaris, which is how travelers could find north.

choices.text: because gravity pulls all stars toward the north direction
choices.feedback: The article explains that gravity pulls objects toward each other, not toward any direction like north.

choices.text: because Polaris is closer to Earth than any other star is
choices.feedback: The article never mentions Polaris being the closest star to Earth — that idea is not in the passage at all.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: Which choice best tells the order of the main ideas in the article from first to last?

choices.text: from stories about star shapes, to the Sun in daytime, to Earth as a tiny speck
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the article starts with why stars hide in daytime, not with stories about star shapes — those come in the second section.

choices.text: from why stars appear at night, to star pictures people made, to our galaxy and beyond
choices.feedback: B is correct because it follows the real order of the article: stars appearing at night, then constellation stories, then the galaxy and universe.

choices.text: from how galaxies pull on Earth, to how travelers used maps, to why stars hide in daylight
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the article never says galaxies pull on Earth, and it says travelers used stars — not maps — to find north.

choices.text: from the Milky Way and other galaxies, to culture stories, to the North Star's handle
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the Milky Way and galaxies are talked about near the end of the article, not at the beginning.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: Based on the article, how are the Sun and the stars in the night sky similar?

choices.text: They are both burning balls of gas inside the Milky Way.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage says the Sun is 'the same kind of burning ball of gas as the stars you see at night,' and both belong to the Milky Way galaxy.

choices.text: They are both bright groups of stars inside a large constellation.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because individual stars are not 'groups of stars,' and the Sun is not described as part of a constellation.

choices.text: They are both giant rocky planets inside the same solar system.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the passage clearly says the Sun and stars are burning balls of gas, not giant rocky planets.

choices.text: They are both swirling pinwheel shapes inside a very dark sky.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because pinwheel or spiral shapes describe some galaxies, not the Sun or the stars we see at night.

syllabus_metadata.title: Beyond Our Solar System: Constellations and Galaxies

assessments.title: Backbone or No Backbone: Vertebrates and Invertebrates

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Backbone or No Backbone? How Scientists Sort the Animal Kingdom

One Question That Sorts Millions of Animals

A tiny hummingbird weighs less than a nickel. A blue whale stretches longer than two school buses. A frog starts life underwater, breathing like a fish. These animals look nothing alike. But they all share one hidden feature: a backbone running down the middle of their body. That single detail is the first thing scientists check when they sort animals into groups.

Animals with backbones are called vertebrates — the word comes from "vertebra," the name for each small bone stacked in your spine. Press your fingers along the center of your back. Those bumps you feel? Those are vertebrae. They protect the nerves inside your spine and hold your body upright. Dogs, eagles, goldfish, and lizards all have this same bony column. So do you.
stimulus.content_text:
Backbone or No Backbone? How Scientists Sort the Animal KingdomOne Question That Sorts Millions of Animals
A tiny hummingbird weighs less than a nickel. A blue whale stretches longer than two school buses. A frog starts life underwater, breathing like a fish. These animals look nothing alike. But they all share one hidden feature: a backbone running down the middle of their body. That single detail is the first thing scientists check when they sort animals into groups.

Animals with backbones are called vertebrates — the word comes from "vertebra," the name for each small bone stacked in your spine. Press your fingers along the center of your back. Those bumps you feel? Those are vertebrae. They protect the nerves inside your spine and hold your body upright. Dogs, eagles, goldfish, and lizards all have this same bony column. So do you.

items.prompt: According to the text, which contrast is true about the hummingbird, blue whale, and frog?

choices.text: They live in different places, but they all breathe underwater.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says the frog starts life underwater, but it never says all three animals breathe underwater.

choices.text: They look very different, but they all have a backbone.
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says the animals 'look nothing alike' but they all share a backbone, which matches exactly what option B says.

choices.text: They are different sizes, but they all start life in water.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because only the frog is described as starting life in water — the hummingbird and blue whale are not.

choices.text: They move in different ways, but they all have wings.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never mentions wings, and animals like frogs and whales definitely do not have wings.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Five Groups of Vertebrates

Mammals are warm-blooded, meaning their bodies stay about the same temperature no matter what the weather is doing outside. Mammals have hair or fur and feed their babies milk. Cats, bats, and whales are all mammals.

Birds are warm-blooded too. Feathers cover their bodies, and they lay hard-shelled eggs. Every bird has feathers — even penguins, which cannot fly.

Reptiles like snakes, turtles, and lizards have dry, scaly skin. They are cold-blooded. You might think that means their blood feels icy, but cold-blooded means an animal's body temperature changes with its surroundings. A lizard on a hot rock has warm blood. Move it to a shady spot, and its body cools down.

Amphibians like frogs and salamanders go through metamorphosis — a big transformation of the whole body. A frog begins life as a tadpole, swimming with a tail and breathing through gills, body parts that pull oxygen from water. Over weeks, the tadpole grows legs, loses its tail, and develops lungs so it can breathe on land.

Fish spend their entire lives in water. They are cold-blooded and breathe through gills. From tiny seahorses to massive whale sharks, all fish are vertebrates. A backbone lets vertebrates like elephants and giraffes grow large and move in powerful ways.

Five Groups of Vertebrates

Birds are warm-blooded too. Feathers cover their bodies, and they lay hard-shelled eggs. Every bird has feathers — even penguins, which cannot fly.

Reptiles like snakes, turtles, and lizards have dry, scaly skin. They are cold-blooded. You might think that means their blood feels icy, but cold-blooded means an animal's body temperature changes with its surroundings. A lizard on a hot rock has warm blood. Move it to a shady spot, and its body cools down.

Amphibians like frogs and salamanders go through metamorphosis — a big transformation of the whole body. A frog begins life as a tadpole, swimming with a tail and breathing through gills, body parts that pull oxygen from water. Over weeks, the tadpole grows legs, loses its tail, and develops lungs so it can breathe on land.

Fish spend their entire lives in water. They are cold-blooded and breathe through gills. From tiny seahorses to massive whale sharks, all fish are vertebrates. A backbone lets vertebrates like elephants and giraffes grow large and move in powerful ways.

items.prompt: Based on the text, what causes a growing frog to be able to breathe on land?

choices.text: Developing new lungs
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage directly says the tadpole 'develops lungs so it can breathe on land.'

choices.text: Losing its long tail
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because losing its tail is part of the frog's transformation, but the passage never connects the tail to breathing.

choices.text: Having a warm body
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because body temperature has nothing to do with how a frog breathes — frogs are actually cold-blooded!

choices.text: Breathing with gills
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because gills help the tadpole breathe in water, not on land — the frog has to grow lungs to breathe air.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
The Other 95 Percent

Vertebrates make up only about 5 percent of all animal species — all the different kinds of animals on Earth. The other 95 percent are invertebrates, animals with no backbone at all.

Ants, spiders, worms, jellyfish, snails, and crabs are all invertebrates. Many invertebrates wear their support on the outside. An ant has a hard outer shell called an exoskeleton — think of it as a suit of armor built into the body. A worm has no hard parts at all. Their smaller size lets many invertebrates live in tiny spaces and survive on little food.

Still, not all invertebrates are tiny. A giant squid can stretch over 40 feet — bigger than many vertebrates. Size alone does not tell you which group an animal belongs to.

An ant has a hard outer shell called an exoskeleton — think of it as a suit of armor built into the body. A worm has no hard parts at all. Still, not all invertebrates are tiny. A giant squid can stretch over 40 feet — bigger than many vertebrates. Size alone does not tell you which group an animal belongs to.

items.prompt: According to the text, what does the author describe about invertebrates BEFORE explaining that not all invertebrates are tiny?

choices.text: A giant squid can stretch over 40 feet long
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the giant squid fact is what comes AFTER the part about small size, not before it.

choices.text: Many invertebrates are small and live in tiny spaces
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage describes invertebrates being small and living in tiny spaces right before introducing the giant squid as a surprise example.

choices.text: Vertebrates make up all the animals on Earth
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage clearly states vertebrates make up only about 5 percent of animals, not all of them.

choices.text: Size tells you which group an animal belongs to
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage actually tells us that size alone does NOT tell you which group an animal belongs to.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Why Sorting Animals Matters

Insects alone outnumber every other animal group combined. The oceans hold millions more invertebrates, from shrimp to sea stars. Every creature plays an important role in the balance of life on Earth.

Scientists sort animals into groups for the same reason you sort socks into a drawer — it helps you find patterns. Once you know a new animal is a reptile, you already know something about its skin and body temperature. One label carries a lot of information. You have already started thinking like a scientist.
stimulus.content_text:

Once you know a new animal is a reptile, you already know something about its skin and body temperature. One label carries a lot of information. You have already started thinking like a scientist.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, how is sorting animals into groups similar to sorting socks into a drawer?

choices.text: They both help a person find patterns.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage directly says sorting animals is like sorting socks because 'it helps you find patterns.'

choices.text: They both keep a bedroom looking clean.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the passage never talks about keeping a bedroom clean — that idea is not in the text at all.

choices.text: They both take a very long time to finish.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the passage does not say anything about how much time either kind of sorting takes.

choices.text: They both require the exact same labels.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because animal labels like 'reptile' and sock labels are completely different — the passage never says they are the same.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: According to the article, what is one difference between how a backbone helps a vertebrate and how a small size helps an invertebrate?

choices.text: A backbone helps an animal stay warm in the cold, while a small size helps an animal stay cool in the sun.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the article never connects a backbone to staying warm or small size to staying cool in the sun.

choices.text: A backbone helps an animal grow to a large size, while a small size helps an animal survive on little food.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the article directly states that a backbone helps vertebrates grow large, and that small size helps invertebrates survive on little food.

choices.text: A backbone helps an animal breathe air on the land, while a small size helps an animal breathe underwater with gills.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because breathing with gills is a feature of fish and tadpoles, not something caused by having a backbone or being small.

choices.text: A backbone helps an animal change its whole body shape, while a small size helps an animal build a hard outer shell.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because changing body shape refers to metamorphosis in amphibians, and a hard outer shell is an exoskeleton — neither is linked to backbone or small size the way the question asks.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: Why does the author explain both that a backbone helps vertebrates move in powerful ways and that many invertebrates can live in tiny spaces?

choices.text: to show that the place an animal lives decides whether it has a backbone
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage never says that where an animal lives determines whether it has a backbone.

choices.text: to show that body structure can affect how different animals survive and live
choices.feedback: B is correct because both details — backbones helping vertebrates move powerfully and invertebrates' small size helping them live in tiny spaces — show how body structure affects how animals survive and live.

choices.text: to show that animals with backbones are always bigger than animals without them
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage specifically says a giant squid has no backbone but can still be bigger than many vertebrates, so size doesn't follow the backbone rule.

choices.text: to show that scientists sort animals mainly by how large they grow
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage explains that scientists sort animals by body features like backbones, skin, and temperature — not by how big they are.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: Which choice best tells the order of ideas the article uses to build its main idea about sorting animals?

choices.text: First it tells how frogs change as they grow, next it explains why birds have feathers, and last it shows that fish are the biggest group.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the article is not mainly about frogs changing, why birds have feathers, or fish being the biggest group — those are small details, not the big ideas that build the whole article.

choices.text: First it introduces backbones, next it describes vertebrate groups and invertebrates, and last it explains why sorting animals is useful.
choices.feedback: B is correct because it matches the real order of the article: first backbones are introduced, then vertebrate and invertebrate groups are described, and finally the article explains why sorting animals is useful.

choices.text: First it explains that most animals are invertebrates, next it names mammal body parts, and last it shows that all animals live in water first.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the article does not start with invertebrates, does not focus on naming mammal body parts, and never says all animals live in water first.

choices.text: First it compares socks and animals, next it lists ocean creatures, and last it defines vertebrae and exoskeletons.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the sock comparison and ocean creatures come near the end of the article, not the beginning, so the order is completely backwards.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: How do the headings "Five Groups of Vertebrates" and "The Other 95 Percent" help a reader compare the two main types of animals?

choices.text: They show that animals with backbones are sorted into groups, while animals without backbones make up most of the animal kingdom.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the headings show that vertebrates are sorted into five named groups, while invertebrates are described as making up 95 percent of all animal species.

choices.text: They show that animals with backbones grow to a very large size, while animals without backbones are too small to be sorted into groups.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the passage actually tells us that some invertebrates, like the giant squid, can grow bigger than many vertebrates.

choices.text: They show that animals with backbones live mostly on the land, while animals without backbones live mostly in the deep ocean waters.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage never says where each group mostly lives — it does not compare land versus deep ocean homes.

choices.text: They show that animals with backbones are always warm-blooded, while animals without backbones are always cold-blooded in the wild.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage clearly states that reptiles and fish are vertebrates that are cold-blooded, so not all animals with backbones are warm-blooded.

assessments.title: Warm Blood and Milk: What Makes a Mammal

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Classification of Animals: What Makes a Mammal?

A Whale Breathes Air

A humpback whale bursts through the ocean surface, sprays water from a hole on top of its head, and gulps a huge breath of air. It has fins. It swims. But here is the surprising part — a whale is actually a mammal, just like your pet dog, just like a bat, and just like you.

Scientists sort animals into groups based on features they share. This sorting is called classification — organizing living things into groups, the way you might sort buttons by color or shape. One of the biggest groups is mammals. Others include fish, birds, reptiles, amphibians, and insects. Each group has its own set of rules. So what are the rules for mammals?
stimulus.content_text:
Classification of Animals: What Makes a Mammal?A Whale Breathes Air
A humpback whale bursts through the ocean surface, sprays water from a hole on top of its head, and gulps a huge breath of air. It has fins. It swims. But here is the surprising part — a whale is actually a mammal, just like your pet dog, just like a bat, and just like you.
Scientists sort animals into groups based on features they share. This sorting is called classification — organizing living things into groups, the way you might sort buttons by color or shape. One of the biggest groups is mammals. Others include fish, birds, reptiles, amphibians, and insects. Each group has its own set of rules. So what are the rules for mammals?

items.prompt: According to the text, what is surprising about a whale even though it has fins and swims?

choices.text: It is actually a type of fish
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage says the surprising thing is that a whale is NOT a fish — even though it swims and has fins like one.

choices.text: It is actually a mammal
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly says 'a whale is actually a mammal, just like your pet dog, just like a bat, and just like you.'

choices.text: It is actually a type of bird
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage never mentions anything about whales being birds, and birds are listed as a completely different group of animals.

choices.text: It is actually a type of reptile
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage never says whales are reptiles — reptiles are listed as a separate group with their own set of rules.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Warm Blood and Hair: Two Key Mammal Traits

All mammals are warm-blooded. That means their bodies keep a steady temperature inside, no matter what is happening outside. Think of it like a house with a heater and an air conditioner that never turns off. A polar bear standing in a blizzard stays warm inside. A camel crossing a scorching desert stays cool enough inside. Their bodies do the work automatically.

Fish are cold-blooded — their body temperature changes with the water around them, like a glass of water left on a table matching the room's temperature. Cold-blooded animals slow down in cold weather. Mammals do not. That steady inner heat lets mammals stay active in freezing snow or burning sand.

Dogs and bears wear thick coats that trap warm air close to their skin. Elephants have only a thin layer of hair, so thin you can barely see it. Even whales grow a few tiny hairs, especially when they are young.
stimulus.content_text:
Warm Blood and Hair: Two Key Mammal Traits
