Think of it like a house with a heater and an air conditioner that never turns off. A polar bear standing in a blizzard stays warm inside. A camel crossing a scorching desert stays cool enough inside. Their bodies do the work automatically.
Fish are cold-blooded — their body temperature changes with the water around them, like a glass of water left on a table matching the room's temperature. Cold-blooded animals slow down in cold weather. Mammals do not. That steady inner heat lets mammals stay active in freezing snow or burning sand.
Dogs and bears wear thick coats that trap warm air close to their skin. Elephants have only a thin layer of hair, so thin you can barely see it. Even whales grow a few tiny hairs, especially when they are young.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, why can mammals stay active in freezing snow or burning sand?

choices.text: because they have thick coats of hair that trap warm air
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says some mammals like elephants and whales have very little hair, yet they are still mammals that stay active — so thick hair is not the real reason.

choices.text: because their bodies keep a steady temperature inside
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly says that mammals' steady inner temperature is what lets them stay active in freezing snow or burning sand.

choices.text: because their body temperature changes with the weather
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because changing body temperature with the weather describes cold-blooded animals like fish, not mammals.

choices.text: because they slow down to save energy in cold weather
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says cold-blooded animals slow down in cold weather — mammals do the opposite and stay active.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Milk: The One Rule That Matters Most

Every mammal mother feeds her babies milk. Milk is a liquid her body makes that gives young mammals all the nutrition — the food energy and building blocks — they need to grow. This trait is so important that the word "mammal" comes from "mammary," which means the parts of the body that produce milk.

Most mammals give birth to live young. A kitten is born alive, not hatched from an egg. A baby dolphin is born in the ocean and starts swimming right away. The platypus, which lives in Australia, is a mammal that lays eggs! After the eggs hatch, the mother still feeds her babies milk. She makes milk. She is warm-blooded. She has hair. So she is a mammal. The way a baby is born does not decide the group. Milk does.

This trait is so important that the word "mammal" comes from "mammary," which means the parts of the body that produce milk.
Most mammals give birth to live young. A kitten is born alive, not hatched from an egg. A baby dolphin is born in the ocean and starts swimming right away. The platypus, which lives in Australia, is a mammal that lays eggs! After the eggs hatch, the mother still feeds her babies milk. She makes milk. She is warm-blooded. She has hair. So she is a mammal. The way a baby is born does not decide the group. Milk does.

items.prompt: According to the passage, what is the correct order of steps for how a platypus mother raises her babies?

choices.text: She makes milk, then lays eggs, then the eggs hatch.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the mother lays her eggs first — she doesn't make milk before laying the eggs.

choices.text: She lays eggs, the eggs hatch, then she feeds her babies milk.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage clearly states the platypus lays eggs, the eggs hatch, and then the mother feeds her babies milk — in that exact order.

choices.text: She feeds her babies milk, then the eggs hatch, then she swims.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the babies haven't hatched yet when milk-feeding is listed first, and swimming is mentioned for dolphins, not platypuses.

choices.text: She gives birth to live young, then makes milk, then grows hair.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage tells us platypuses lay eggs — they do not give birth to live young.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Mammals Everywhere: Adaptable, Smart, and Social

Mammals live in almost every place on Earth. Whales and dolphins swim through oceans. Bats fly through the night sky. Moles tunnel underground. Monkeys swing through tropical forests. Camels trek across deserts. Polar bears roam Arctic ice. This variety shows how adaptable mammals are — able to fit into many different places.

Many mammals live in groups and take care of their young for a long time. Elephants mourn their dead. Dolphins play games. Chimpanzees pick up sticks and use them as tools. Mammals are some of the smartest animals alive.
stimulus.content_text:
Mammals Everywhere: Adaptable, Smart, and Social
Mammals live in almost every place on Earth. Whales and dolphins swim through oceans. Bats fly through the night sky. Moles tunnel underground. Monkeys swing through tropical forests. Camels trek across deserts. Polar bears roam Arctic ice. This variety shows how adaptable mammals are — able to fit into many different places.
Many mammals live in groups and take care of their young for a long time. Elephants mourn their dead. Dolphins play games. Chimpanzees pick up sticks and use them as tools. Mammals are some of the smartest animals alive.

items.prompt: Read these sentences from the text: "Whales and dolphins swim through oceans. Bats fly through the night sky. Moles tunnel underground... This variety shows how adaptable mammals are." Based on the meaning of "adaptable," how are these animals similar?

choices.text: They show that mammals can fit into many different places.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage lists animals in oceans, the sky, and underground, then says this shows mammals are 'adaptable' — able to fit into many different places.

choices.text: They show that mammals can use sticks as helpful tools.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because using sticks as tools is something chimpanzees do, and it is mentioned in a different part of the passage, not in the sentences about adaptable mammals.

choices.text: They show that mammals can live in large animal groups.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because living in groups is mentioned later in the passage, but the sentences about whales, bats, and moles are about where animals live, not how many live together.

choices.text: They show that mammals can play fun games with others.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because playing games is something dolphins do, and it is mentioned in a different part of the passage, not in the sentences being asked about.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
You Belong to This Group

When you were a baby, you drank milk. You are warm-blooded. You are a mammal. The same rules that connect you to a tiny mouse also connect you to a giant blue whale half a world away. Classification is not just a list scientists made up. It is a map that shows how all living things are related — and you are on it.
stimulus.content_text:
You Belong to This Group
When you were a baby, you drank milk. You are warm-blooded. You are a mammal. The same rules that connect you to a tiny mouse also connect you to a giant blue whale half a world away. Classification is not just a list scientists made up. It is a map that shows how all living things are related — and you are on it.

items.prompt: In the section 'You Belong to This Group,' what does the author say classification is?

choices.text: a list that scientists made up long ago
choices.feedback: This is actually the opposite of what the author says — the passage tells us classification is NOT just a list scientists made up.

choices.text: a map that shows how living things are related
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage uses those exact words: 'It is a map that shows how all living things are related.'

choices.text: a rule that tells which animals drink milk
choices.feedback: The passage mentions drinking milk as something mammals do, but it never says classification is a rule about drinking milk.

choices.text: a group that only includes warm-blooded animals
choices.feedback: Classification covers all living things, not just warm-blooded animals, so this answer is too narrow.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: You want to read about why a whale stays active in freezing water while a fish slows down. Which text feature helps you find this information?

choices.text: The heading about whales breathing air
choices.feedback: This heading is about how whales breathe air at the surface, not about body temperature or why fish slow down in cold water.

choices.text: The heading about warm blood and hair
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because the warm blood and hair section explains exactly how warm-blooded mammals stay active in cold weather while cold-blooded fish slow down.

choices.text: The heading about milk being the rule
choices.feedback: This heading is about milk and how mammal mothers feed their babies, so it won't help you find information about body temperature.

choices.text: The heading about belonging to a group
choices.feedback: This heading is about humans being mammals, not about the difference between warm-blooded and cold-blooded animals.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: Based on the article, what causes scientists to put very different animals into the mammal group?

choices.text: Because they live in the same places like oceans or deserts.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because mammals live in all kinds of different places — oceans, deserts, forests, and even underground — so where they live is not what puts them in the same group.

choices.text: Because they share special traits like warm blood and milk.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the article explains that all mammals share key traits: they are warm-blooded, they have at least some hair, and their mothers feed them milk.

choices.text: Because they are very smart animals that play games together.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because while some mammals like dolphins and chimpanzees are smart and playful, the article does not list being smart as a rule for being a mammal.

choices.text: Because they are born alive instead of hatching from eggs.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the platypus is a mammal that hatches from an egg, so being born alive is not the rule — the article says milk is the most important rule.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: How does the author order the ideas in the article to explain what a mammal is?

choices.text: First by describing a whale, next by explaining the rules, and last by connecting them to you.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the article truly starts with a whale, then explains the mammal rules, and finishes by connecting everything to you the reader.

choices.text: First by showing mammal habitats, next by explaining the rules, and last by describing a large whale.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because mammal habitats are talked about near the end of the article, not at the beginning.

choices.text: First by describing a platypus, next by explaining the rules, and last by describing a cold fish.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the platypus is just a small example used in the middle of the article, not the opening idea.

choices.text: First by connecting them to you, next by explaining the rules, and last by describing a whale.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the part connecting the rules to you comes at the very end of the article, not the beginning.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: According to the article, which trait is the same for a platypus, a humpback whale, and you?

choices.text: All three are born alive instead of hatching.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the platypus lays eggs and hatches, so not all three are born alive.

choices.text: All three drink milk as babies to help grow.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the article says every mammal feeds its babies milk, and that includes the platypus, the humpback whale, and you.

choices.text: All three grow thick coats to trap warm air.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because whales only have a few tiny hairs and platypuses don't have thick coats either.

choices.text: All three change body heat to match the room.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because changing body heat to match the surroundings describes cold-blooded animals, but all three of these are warm-blooded mammals.

assessments.title: Feathers and Flight: Birds, Reptiles, and Amphibians

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Birds, Reptiles, and Amphibians: Three Lives, Three Styles

A Lizard on a Hot Rock

A desert lizard sits perfectly still on a flat rock at sunrise. Without the sun's heat soaking into its body, this lizard cannot run, hunt, or even digest its food. It needs warmth from the outside world because it is cold-blooded — its body cannot make its own heat the way yours can. You might think a cold-blooded animal always feels cold to the touch. Actually, if you picked up that lizard after an hour in the sun, it would feel warm. "Cold-blooded" just means the animal depends on its surroundings to control its temperature.

Birds, reptiles, and amphibians are all vertebrates — animals with backbones, the chain of small bones running down the middle of their backs. These three groups share that bony spine, but almost everything else about them is different. But reptiles are only one of the three groups we are going to explore.

Birds, Reptiles, and Amphibians: Three Lives, Three StylesA Lizard on a Hot Rock

Actually, if you picked up that lizard after an hour in the sun, it would feel warm. "Cold-blooded" just means the animal depends on its surroundings to control its temperature.

These three groups share that bony spine, but almost everything else about them is different. But reptiles are only one of the three groups we are going to explore.

items.prompt: According to the text, how is a lizard's body different from your body?

choices.text: Its body cannot make its own heat.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage says a lizard is cold-blooded, meaning its body cannot make its own heat the way a human body can.

choices.text: Its body cannot feel warm to touch.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the passage tells us the lizard would actually feel warm to the touch after sitting in the sun for an hour.

choices.text: Its body does not have a backbone.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage says reptiles like lizards are vertebrates, which means they do have backbones.

choices.text: Its body does not need the hot sun.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage explains that the lizard depends on the sun's heat to do basic things like run, hunt, and digest food.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Birds: Feathers and Warm Blood

Birds are warm-blooded, meaning their bodies make their own heat from the inside. A robin can hop around on a snowy morning because its body stays warm no matter what the weather does. All birds have wings, though not every bird uses them to fly — some found other ways to move. All birds lay hard-shelled eggs, and many build nests to keep those eggs safe. Some feathers are bright colors that help birds attract mates.
stimulus.content_text:
Birds: Feathers and Warm Blood
Birds are warm-blooded, meaning their bodies make their own heat from the inside. A robin can hop around on a snowy morning because its body stays warm no matter what the weather does. All birds have wings, though not every bird uses them to fly — some found other ways to move. All birds lay hard-shelled eggs, and many build nests to keep those eggs safe. Some feathers are bright colors that help birds attract mates.

items.prompt: The main idea of the first part of the text is that birds are warm-blooded. How does the detail about the robin hopping in the snow support this main idea?

choices.text: It gives an example of how having strong wings causes birds to fly through storms.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the robin detail is about making body heat to stay warm, not about having strong wings or flying through storms.

choices.text: It gives an example of how making body heat causes birds to stay active in winter.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage explains that warm-blooded means making body heat from the inside, and the robin hopping in snow shows how that heat keeps birds active even in cold winter weather.

choices.text: It gives an example of how building safe nests causes birds to hide from the cold.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the robin is hopping around outside in the snow, not hiding in a nest to escape the cold.

choices.text: It gives an example of how laying hard eggs causes birds to hop on snowy mornings.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because laying hard-shelled eggs is a separate bird fact and has nothing to do with why a robin can stay active on a snowy morning.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Reptiles: Scales and Sun

Reptiles include snakes, lizards, turtles, and crocodilians — the group that holds alligators and crocodiles. Unlike birds, reptiles are cold-blooded. That is why you see them basking in sunlight. They soak up energy from the sun so they can move and hunt. Most reptiles lay eggs, though a few kinds give birth to live young. Because cold-blooded animals do not burn energy to stay warm, they need far less food. A snake might eat only once a week — or even once a month.

Scientists believe that birds actually evolved — slowly changed over millions of years — from ancient reptiles. So a sparrow and a crocodile are distant cousins.
stimulus.content_text:
Reptiles: Scales and Sun
Reptiles include snakes, lizards, turtles, and crocodilians — the group that holds alligators and crocodiles. Unlike birds, reptiles are cold-blooded. That is why you see them basking in sunlight. They soak up energy from the sun so they can move and hunt. Most reptiles lay eggs, though a few kinds give birth to live young. Because cold-blooded animals do not burn energy to stay warm, they need far less food. A snake might eat only once a week — or even once a month.
Scientists believe that birds actually evolved — slowly changed over millions of years — from ancient reptiles. So a sparrow and a crocodile are distant cousins.

items.prompt: According to the text, what must reptiles do first before they can move and hunt?

choices.text: Soak up energy from the sun
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage directly says reptiles 'soak up energy from the sun so they can move and hunt.'

choices.text: Burn energy inside their bodies
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the passage explains that reptiles do NOT burn energy inside their bodies to stay warm — that is what warm-blooded animals do.

choices.text: Eat a large meal to get strong
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage never says reptiles need a big meal before moving; it actually says they need very little food.

choices.text: Give birth to their live young
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because giving birth to live young is something only a few reptiles do, and it has nothing to do with getting energy to move and hunt.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Amphibians: The Double Life

The word amphibian comes from a Greek word meaning "double life." That name fits perfectly. Most amphibians start life in water and later move to land. Think about a frog. It begins as a tadpole, swimming in a pond and breathing through gills like a fish. Then it goes through metamorphosis — a big body change, like a costume swap that happens from the inside out. The tadpole grows legs, develops lungs, loses its tail, and hops onto shore as a frog. Salamanders and toads go through similar changes.

Amphibians are sometimes mixed up with reptiles, but they belong to separate groups for good reasons. Reptiles have dry, scaly skin, while amphibians have smooth, moist skin. Amphibians can actually absorb — soak up, the way a paper towel soaks up a spill — water and even some air right through their skin. Their skin must stay damp to work. That is why you find frogs and salamanders near ponds, streams, and wet forests.

Amphibians: The Double Life
The word amphibian comes from a Greek word meaning "double life." That name fits perfectly. Most amphibians start life in water and later move to land. Think about a frog. It begins as a tadpole, swimming in a pond and breathing through gills like a fish. Then it goes through metamorphosis — a big body change, like a costume swap that happens from the inside out. The tadpole grows legs, develops lungs, loses its tail, and hops onto shore as a frog. Salamanders and toads go through similar changes.
Amphibians are sometimes mixed up with reptiles, but they belong to separate groups for good reasons. Reptiles have dry, scaly skin, while amphibians have smooth, moist skin. Amphibians can actually absorb — soak up, the way a paper towel soaks up a spill — water and even some air right through their skin. Their skin must stay damp to work. That is why you find frogs and salamanders near ponds, streams, and wet forests.

items.prompt: In the section, the author compares amphibian skin to a paper towel. How are amphibian skin and a paper towel similar, based on the meaning of the word absorb?

choices.text: They both feel smooth and dry to the touch.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage says amphibian skin is smooth and moist, not dry, and a paper towel isn't being compared by how it feels.

choices.text: They both soak up water that touches them.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage explains that 'absorb' means to soak up water, just like a paper towel soaks up a spill, and amphibian skin does the same thing.

choices.text: They both need to stay near ponds to survive.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because staying near ponds is about where amphibians live, not about what amphibian skin and a paper towel have in common.

choices.text: They both have scales that hold in moisture.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because amphibians have smooth skin, not scales — the passage says scales belong to reptiles, not amphibians.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Same Backbone, Different Playbook

Each one carries a backbone inside its body, just like you. But feathers, scales, and moist skin tell three completely different stories about how to survive on this planet. One animal made its own heat. One borrowed heat from the sun. One split its life between water and land. The backbone was just the starting point. What each group did with it — that is where the real story begins.

One animal made its own heat. One borrowed heat from the sun. One split its life between water and land. The backbone was just the starting point. What each group did with it — that is where the real story begins.

items.prompt:
Read this paragraph from the text:

"Each one carries a backbone inside its body, just like you. But feathers, scales, and moist skin tell three completely different stories about how to survive on this planet. One animal made its own heat. One borrowed heat from the sun. One split its life between water and land. The backbone was just the starting point. What each group did with it — that is where the real story begins."

According to the paragraph, what is "the real story" about?

choices.text: What each group did with its own backbone
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage directly says 'What each group did with it — that is where the real story begins,' and 'it' refers to the backbone.

choices.text: How each group borrowed heat from the sun
choices.feedback: B is wrong because borrowing heat from the sun is just one detail about one group, not what the whole 'real story' is about.

choices.text: Why each group grew feathers on its skin
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the passage never says any group grew feathers on its skin — feathers are just mentioned as a clue about one group.

choices.text: Where each group spent time in the water
choices.feedback: D is wrong because spending time in water is only a detail about one group, not the main idea the passage calls 'the real story.'

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: Based on the text, how is a bird different from a reptile when it comes to needing food?

choices.text: A bird needs more food because it must make its own body heat.
choices.feedback: A is correct because birds are warm-blooded and must burn energy to make their own body heat, which means they need more food than cold-blooded reptiles.

choices.text: A bird needs less food because it takes in energy from the sun.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because it's reptiles — not birds — that take in energy from the sun, and that's actually why reptiles need LESS food, not more.

choices.text: A bird needs more food because it soaks up water through its skin.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because soaking up water through skin is something amphibians like frogs do, not birds.

choices.text: A bird needs less food because it stays perfectly still on hot rocks.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because sitting still on hot rocks is what cold-blooded reptiles do to warm up, not something birds do.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: Based on the text, why would an amphibian have a harder time surviving in a dry desert than a reptile would?

choices.text: because an amphibian needs moist skin to absorb water and air
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage says amphibians absorb water and air through their skin, and their skin must stay damp — a dry desert would dry out their skin and make survival very hard.

choices.text: because an amphibian is cold-blooded and cannot make body heat
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because both amphibians and reptiles are cold-blooded, so this would not give reptiles an advantage over amphibians in the desert.

choices.text: because an amphibian has scales that dry out in hot weather
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because amphibians have smooth, moist skin — it is reptiles that have dry, scaly skin.

choices.text: because an amphibian must eat more food than a reptile does
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage does not say anything about amphibians needing more food than reptiles.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: Under which two headings should you look to find the steps of a frog growing up and the steps of how birds slowly changed from ancient animals?

choices.text: "Birds: Feathers and Warm Blood" and "Amphibians: The Double Life"
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the 'Birds: Feathers and Warm Blood' section does not explain how birds slowly changed from ancient animals — that information is found in the 'Reptiles' section.

choices.text: "Reptiles: Scales and Sun" and "Amphibians: The Double Life"
choices.feedback: B is correct because 'Reptiles: Scales and Sun' explains how birds evolved from ancient reptiles, and 'Amphibians: The Double Life' describes the steps of a frog growing from a tadpole into a frog.

choices.text: "A Lizard on a Hot Rock" and "Same Backbone, Different Playbook"
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because neither 'A Lizard on a Hot Rock' nor 'Same Backbone, Different Playbook' contains details about frog development or how birds evolved over time.

choices.text: "Reptiles: Scales and Sun" and "Birds: Feathers and Warm Blood"
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the 'Birds: Feathers and Warm Blood' section does not describe how birds slowly changed from ancient animals — you need to look in the 'Reptiles' section for that.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: Which sentence best explains the main idea of the whole article?

choices.text: Birds, reptiles, and amphibians share a backbone but survive in different ways.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the article is all about how birds, reptiles, and amphibians share a backbone but each survives in its own unique way.

choices.text: Birds, reptiles, and amphibians all need the sun's heat to keep their bodies warm.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because birds are warm-blooded and make their own heat — they do not need the sun to stay warm.

choices.text: Birds, reptiles, and amphibians start their lives in the water and move to the land.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because only amphibians live in water first and then move to land, not birds or reptiles.

choices.text: Birds, reptiles, and amphibians are distant cousins that slowly changed over time.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the article only briefly mentions that birds evolved from ancient reptiles — that is one small detail, not the main idea.

assessments.title: Fins and Gills: The World of Fish

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Classification of Animals: Fish

A World Beneath the Water

A whale shark the size of a school bus glides through warm ocean water. A tiny stout infantfish, smaller than your fingernail, hides near a coral reef. Both are fish. Scientists have found over 30,000 species — or kinds — of fish on Earth. That is more than all the mammals, birds, reptiles, and amphibians added together.
stimulus.content_text:
Classification of Animals: FishA World Beneath the Water
A whale shark the size of a school bus glides through warm ocean water. A tiny stout infantfish, smaller than your fingernail, hides near a coral reef. Both are fish. Scientists have found over 30,000 species — or kinds — of fish on Earth. That is more than all the mammals, birds, reptiles, and amphibians added together.

items.prompt: According to the text, how are the whale shark and the stout infantfish different from each other?

choices.text: The whale shark lives in warm water while the infantfish lives in cold water
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because while the passage does say the whale shark glides through warm water, it never says anything about the infantfish living in cold water.

choices.text: The whale shark is very large while the infantfish is very small
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly compares their sizes — the whale shark is as big as a school bus, while the infantfish is smaller than your fingernail.

choices.text: The whale shark swims fast while the infantfish moves slowly
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never mentions how fast or slow either the whale shark or the infantfish moves.

choices.text: The whale shark eats coral while the infantfish hides near coral
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says the infantfish hides near coral, but it never says the whale shark eats coral.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Built for Water

Over millions of years, fish have become well adapted to life in water. They are cold-blooded, which means their body temperature changes to match the water around them. If the water is cool, a fish's body is cool. If the water is warm, the fish warms up too.

Fish breathe through gills, organs on the sides of a fish's head that pull oxygen right out of the water. Gills work a bit like a strainer in your kitchen — water flows through, and the gills catch the oxygen a fish needs. Then the gills release carbon dioxide, a waste gas the fish's body does not need anymore.

Not every ocean animal breathes this way. Dolphins are not fish — they breathe air with lungs, are warm-blooded, and must swim to the surface to take a breath.
stimulus.content_text:
Built for Water
Over millions of years, fish have become well adapted to life in water. They are cold-blooded, which means their body temperature changes to match the water around them. If the water is cool, a fish's body is cool. If the water is warm, the fish warms up too.

Fish breathe through gills, organs on the sides of a fish's head that pull oxygen right out of the water. Gills work a bit like a strainer in your kitchen — water flows through, and the gills catch the oxygen a fish needs. Then the gills release carbon dioxide, a waste gas the fish's body does not need anymore.

Not every ocean animal breathes this way. Dolphins are not fish — they breathe air with lungs, are warm-blooded, and must swim to the surface to take a breath.

items.prompt: According to the section 'Built for Water,' what causes a fish's body temperature to change?

choices.text: the fish breathing in oxygen through its gills
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because gills are used for breathing oxygen, not for changing the fish's body temperature.

choices.text: the temperature of the water around the fish
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly states that a fish's body temperature changes to match the temperature of the water around it.

choices.text: the fish swimming faster or slower in the ocean
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage never mentions swimming speed as something that affects a fish's body temperature.

choices.text: the carbon dioxide leaving the fish's body
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because carbon dioxide is a waste gas the fish releases, and the passage does not say it has anything to do with body temperature.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Scales, Fins, and a Hidden Balloon

Most fish have bodies covered in scales — thin, overlapping plates that act like tiny suits of armor. A layer of slippery mucus covers the scales, helping the fish slide through water and keeping germs away.

Fish also use fins to move and steer. The tail fin pushes a fish forward with strong side-to-side sweeps. Other fins on the sides, top, and belly help the fish steer, balance, and stop. Tuna and marlins are shaped for speed. Seahorses, on the other hand, swim upright, using a small fin on their back to push themselves forward and tiny fins near their head to steer.

Many fish also have a swim bladder, a small sac filled with gas inside their body. By adding or letting out gas, a fish can float at any depth without swimming hard. This saves a lot of energy.

Scales, Fins, and a Hidden Balloon

Fish also use fins to move and steer. The tail fin pushes a fish forward with strong side-to-side sweeps. Other fins on the sides, top, and belly help the fish steer, balance, and stop. Tuna and marlins are shaped for speed. Seahorses, on the other hand, swim upright, using a small fin on their back to push themselves forward and tiny fins near their head to steer.

Many fish also have a swim bladder, a small sac filled with gas inside their body. By adding or letting out gas, a fish can float at any depth without swimming hard. This saves a lot of energy.

items.prompt: According to the passage, what does a fish do with its swim bladder before it can float at any depth?

choices.text: It moves its tail fin side to side
choices.feedback: Moving the tail fin side to side pushes a fish forward through the water, but it has nothing to do with the swim bladder or floating at different depths.

choices.text: It adds or lets out gas from it
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says a fish adds or lets out gas from its swim bladder to float at any depth without swimming hard.

choices.text: It covers its scales with mucus
choices.feedback: Mucus covers the scales to help the fish glide through water and fight germs — it is not connected to the swim bladder at all.

choices.text: It uses its back fin to steer
choices.feedback: Using a back fin to steer is something seahorses do, and it has nothing to do with the swim bladder or floating at different depths.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Not All Fish Are the Same

Here is a surprising part: not all fish skeletons are made of bone. For example, sharks and rays have skeletons made of cartilage, the same bendy material in your nose and ears. Scientists call them cartilaginous fish. Most other fish, like bass and clownfish, have hard bony skeletons. So even inside the fish group, scientists classify fish into smaller groups based on their differences.
stimulus.content_text:
Not All Fish Are the Same
Here is a surprising part: not all fish skeletons are made of bone. For example, sharks and rays have skeletons made of cartilage, the same bendy material in your nose and ears. Scientists call them cartilaginous fish. Most other fish, like bass and clownfish, have hard bony skeletons. So even inside the fish group, scientists classify fish into smaller groups based on their differences.

items.prompt: Based on the text, how does the author compare sharks and bass?

choices.text: The author explains that a shark has a skeleton made of hard bone, while a bass has a skeleton made of bendy cartilage.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because it has the details backwards — the passage says sharks have cartilage and bass have bone, not the other way around.

choices.text: The author explains that a shark has a skeleton made of bendy cartilage, while a bass has a skeleton made of hard bone.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage clearly states sharks have bendy cartilage skeletons and that bass, like most other fish, have hard bony skeletons.

choices.text: The author explains that a shark has a skeleton located in its nose, while a bass has a skeleton located in its ears.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage mentions noses and ears only to describe what cartilage feels like, not to say where the skeletons are located.

choices.text: The author explains that a shark has a skeleton made of bendy cartilage, while a bass has no skeleton inside its body.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage never says bass have no skeleton — it says bass have hard bony skeletons.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Where Fish Live

Freshwater fish live in rivers, lakes, ponds, and streams. Trout, bass, and catfish are common freshwater fish. Saltwater fish live in the oceans and seas. Clownfish, sharks, and tuna call salt water home. A few fish live in both. Salmon hatch in freshwater streams, swim out to the ocean to grow, and then return to fresh water to lay their eggs.
stimulus.content_text:
Where Fish Live
Freshwater fish live in rivers, lakes, ponds, and streams. Trout, bass, and catfish are common freshwater fish. Saltwater fish live in the oceans and seas. Clownfish, sharks, and tuna call salt water home. A few fish live in both. Salmon hatch in freshwater streams, swim out to the ocean to grow, and then return to fresh water to lay their eggs.

items.prompt: According to the passage, what does the word 'freshwater' mean when describing where fish live?

choices.text: water that is clean and safe to drink
choices.feedback: The passage never mentions anything about water being clean or safe to drink — that's not what 'freshwater' means here.

choices.text: water found in rivers, lakes, and streams
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says freshwater fish live in 'rivers, lakes, ponds, and streams,' which is exactly what answer B says.

choices.text: water that is new and recently formed
choices.feedback: The passage doesn't say anything about water being new or recently formed, so this meaning doesn't fit.

choices.text: water found only in very deep ponds
choices.feedback: The passage lists rivers, lakes, ponds, and streams as freshwater places — not just deep ponds, so this answer is too narrow.

items.title: Section 6 - Guiding Question 5

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
A Staggering Variety

Fish come in wild shapes, sizes, and colors. The whale shark can stretch 40 feet long. Others are flat like pancakes or puff up like balloons when scared.

Whatever shape or size, almost every fish breathes with gills instead of lungs, floats without effort, and belongs to the biggest group of vertebrates on the planet. Knowing how to classify a fish — cold-blooded, gills, scales, fins — means you are reading the animal the same way a scientist would.

items.prompt: Which detail from the article best supports the idea that fish come in many different shapes and sizes?

choices.text: Almost every fish breathes with gills instead of lungs.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because breathing with gills is something almost ALL fish do the same way, so it doesn't show variety in shapes and sizes.

choices.text: Others are flat like pancakes or puff up like balloons.
choices.feedback: B is correct because comparing fish to flat pancakes and puffy balloons gives two great examples of very different fish shapes, which supports the idea that fish come in many shapes and sizes.

choices.text: Fish belong to the biggest group of vertebrates on Earth.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because belonging to the biggest group of vertebrates is about how many fish exist, not about the different shapes and sizes they come in.

choices.text: A scientist classifies fish by their scales and fins.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because classifying fish by scales and fins is about how scientists study fish, not about showing that fish have many different shapes and sizes.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: Based on the article, how is a dolphin different from a shark?

choices.text: A dolphin breathes using its lungs, while a shark breathes using its gills.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the article clearly states that dolphins breathe air with lungs, while sharks are fish that breathe through gills.

choices.text: A dolphin lives in fresh water only, while a shark lives in salt water only.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the article never says dolphins live only in fresh water — dolphins actually live in the ocean.

choices.text: A dolphin is a cold-blooded animal, while a shark is a warm-blooded animal.
choices.feedback: C has it backwards — the article says fish (including sharks) are cold-blooded, while dolphins are warm-blooded.

choices.text: A dolphin floats with a swim bladder, while a shark floats with its tail fin.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the article never mentions dolphins using a swim bladder, and tail fins help fish move forward, not float in place.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: To find out what causes a fish to slide easily through the water and what causes scientists to put fish into smaller groups, which two headings should you read?

choices.text: "A World Beneath the Water" and "Scales, Fins, and a Hidden Balloon"
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because 'A World Beneath the Water' is about how many kinds of fish exist, not about what makes fish slide through water or how scientists group them.

choices.text: "A Staggering Variety" and "Not All Fish Are the Same"
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because 'A Staggering Variety' talks about fish shapes and sizes, not about what helps fish slide through water.

choices.text: "Scales, Fins, and a Hidden Balloon" and "Not All Fish Are the Same"
choices.feedback: C is correct because 'Scales, Fins, and a Hidden Balloon' explains that slippery mucus helps fish slide through water, and 'Not All Fish Are the Same' explains that scientists group fish into smaller groups based on whether they have bony or cartilage skeletons.

choices.text: "Where Fish Live" and "Scales, Fins, and a Hidden Balloon"
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because 'Where Fish Live' is about freshwater and saltwater habitats, not about what helps fish slide through water or how scientists classify them into smaller groups.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: At the end of the article, in what order does the author list key details to summarize the main idea about fish?

choices.text: First that they breathe with gills, next that they float easily, and last that they are a big animal group.

choices.text: First that they have hard skeletons, next that they swim really fast, and last that they live in ocean water.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the ending summary does not mention hard skeletons or swimming fast — those details appear in earlier sections, not the final summary.

choices.text: First that they are warm-blooded, next that they breathe with lungs, and last that they are a little group.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because it says the opposite of what the article teaches — fish are cold-blooded and breathe with gills, not warm-blooded with lungs.

choices.text: First that they live in fresh water, next that they have cartilage, and last that they lay their tiny eggs.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the final summary does not talk about fresh water, cartilage, or laying eggs — those details come from the middle of the article.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: Based on the article, which statement correctly compares a clownfish and a shark?

choices.text: Both live in salt water, but a clownfish has a bony skeleton and a shark has a cartilage skeleton.

choices.feedback: C is wrong because the article clearly says sharks have cartilage skeletons, not bony skeletons.

choices.text: Both have cartilage skeletons, but a clownfish lives in fresh water and a shark lives in salt water.

assessments.title: Exoskeletons and Soft Bodies: Arthropods and Mollusks

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Arthropods and Mollusks: Animals Without Backbones

A Crab on the Beach

A crab scuttles sideways across wet sand. Its hard shell gleams in the sun. Its legs bend and click at every joint. This small creature has no bones inside its body at all. It is an invertebrate — an animal without a backbone. Most animals on Earth are invertebrates. Two of the biggest groups are arthropods and mollusks. They have no bones, but they have astonishing ways to protect themselves and move through the world.
stimulus.content_text:
Arthropods and Mollusks: Animals Without BackbonesA Crab on the Beach
A crab scuttles sideways across wet sand. Its hard shell gleams in the sun. Its legs bend and click at every joint. This small creature has no bones inside its body at all. It is an invertebrate — an animal without a backbone. Most animals on Earth are invertebrates. Two of the biggest groups are arthropods and mollusks. They have no bones, but they have astonishing ways to protect themselves and move through the world.

items.prompt: According to the passage, what is true about arthropods and mollusks even though they have no bones?

choices.text: They have astonishing ways to protect themselves.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage says arthropods and mollusks 'have astonishing ways to protect themselves and move through the world,' even without bones.

choices.text: They have soft shells that bend very easily.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the passage describes the crab's shell as hard and gleaming, not soft and bendy.

choices.text: They have trouble moving across the wet sand.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the crab scuttles across the sand easily, so these animals do not have trouble moving.

choices.text: They have special bones inside of their legs.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the passage says these animals have no bones inside their bodies at all.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Arthropods: Armor on the Outside

Arthropods are the largest group of animals on Earth. Insects, spiders, crabs, lobsters, scorpions, and centipedes are all arthropods. Three features make them a group. They all have jointed legs that bend like hinges on a door. Their bodies are divided into segments — separate sections linked together, like the cars of a train. And they all wear an exoskeleton, a hard outer covering that works like a suit of armor over their soft bodies.

An exoskeleton is rigid — stiff and unable to stretch. So as an arthropod grows, it must molt, or shed its old exoskeleton completely. The arthropod wriggles out of its old covering, and underneath, a new, bigger exoskeleton hardens around its body.
stimulus.content_text:
Arthropods: Armor on the Outside
Arthropods are the largest group of animals on Earth. Insects, spiders, crabs, lobsters, scorpions, and centipedes are all arthropods. Three features make them a group. They all have jointed legs that bend like hinges on a door. Their bodies are divided into segments — separate sections linked together, like the cars of a train. And they all wear an exoskeleton, a hard outer covering that works like a suit of armor over their soft bodies.
An exoskeleton is rigid — stiff and unable to stretch. So as an arthropod grows, it must molt, or shed its old exoskeleton completely. The arthropod wriggles out of its old covering, and underneath, a new, bigger exoskeleton hardens around its body.

items.prompt: Based on the section, which detail best supports the main idea that arthropods need to molt because of their exoskeletons?

choices.text: Their bodies are divided into segments linked together.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because having body segments is just one feature of arthropods — it has nothing to do with why they need to molt.

choices.text: An exoskeleton is rigid and unable to stretch.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage says the exoskeleton is rigid and can't stretch, which is the exact reason arthropods must shed it when they grow.

choices.text: They all have jointed legs that bend like hinges.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because jointed legs are a feature of arthropods, but the passage never connects them to molting.

choices.text: A new exoskeleton is bigger than the old one.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because it describes what happens after molting, but it doesn't explain the reason molting is necessary.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Insects, Arachnids, and Crustaceans

Insects are the biggest group of arthropods by far. About 70 percent of all known animal species are insects. Scientists have identified over a million kinds, and they believe millions more are still waiting to be found. Every insect has six legs and three body segments: the head, the thorax (the middle section, where the legs attach), and the abdomen (the back section, like the tail end of the body). Most insects also have wings. Butterflies, beetles, ants, bees, mosquitoes, and grasshoppers are all insects.

People often call spiders "bugs" or "insects." But spiders belong to a different group called arachnids. Count a spider's legs and you will see the difference right away — spiders have eight legs, not six. They are arthropods, but they are not insects.

Crustaceans are arthropods that mostly live in water. Crabs, lobsters, and shrimp are crustaceans. They share the same arthropod features — jointed legs, segments, and an exoskeleton — but their bodies are built for ocean life.

Most insects also have wings. Butterflies, beetles, ants, bees, mosquitoes, and grasshoppers are all insects.
People often call spiders "bugs" or "insects." But spiders belong to a different group called arachnids. Count a spider's legs and you will see the difference right away — spiders have eight legs, not six. They are arthropods, but they are not insects.
Crustaceans are arthropods that mostly live in water. Crabs, lobsters, and shrimp are crustaceans. They share the same arthropod features — jointed legs, segments, and an exoskeleton — but their bodies are built for ocean life.

items.prompt: According to the text, what are the three body segments of an insect in order from front to back?

choices.text: the thorax, the head, and the abdomen
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because it puts the thorax first, but the passage says the head comes first since it is the front part of the body.

choices.text: the head, the thorax, and the abdomen
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage lists the three body segments in this exact order: the head (front), the thorax (middle), and the abdomen (back).

choices.text: the abdomen, the thorax, and the head
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because it lists the segments backwards, starting with the abdomen, which the passage says is actually the back section.

choices.text: the head, the abdomen, and the thorax
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because it puts the abdomen in the middle, but the passage says the thorax is the middle section and the abdomen is at the back.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Mollusks: Soft Bodies, Surprising Tricks

Mollusks are a different kind of invertebrate. They have soft bodies. Many protect themselves with hard shells. Snails, clams, oysters, and mussels all carry shells that guard them from predators — animals that hunt and eat other animals.

But not all mollusks have shells. Octopuses and squid are mollusks too. Without shells, they survive using other tricks. Octopuses can change color in an instant. They squirt ink to confuse predators and squeeze through tiny spaces. They are some of the smartest invertebrates alive. Squid use jet propulsion — they shoot water out of their bodies to launch themselves through the ocean at high speed.

But not all mollusks have shells. Octopuses and squid are mollusks too. Without shells, they survive using other tricks. Octopuses can change color in an instant. They squirt ink to confuse predators and squeeze through tiny spaces. They are some of the smartest invertebrates alive. Squid use jet propulsion — they shoot water out of their bodies to launch themselves through the ocean at high speed.

items.prompt: Based on the text, how are octopuses and squid different from snails and clams?

choices.text: They use hard shells to hide from predators.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because using hard shells to hide is exactly what snails and clams do — not octopuses and squid.

choices.text: They stay safe without having hard shells.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage says octopuses and squid have no shells but use special tricks like squirting ink and jet propulsion to stay safe.

choices.text: They are the only mollusks with soft bodies.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage says ALL mollusks have soft bodies, not just octopuses and squid.

choices.text: They hunt and eat other kinds of animals.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage never says octopuses or squid hunt other animals — it only talks about how they escape from predators.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Small Animals, Big Jobs

These invertebrates keep ecosystems — communities of living things and their surroundings — running. Bees pollinate flowers by carrying pollen from bloom to bloom, which helps plants make fruits and seeds. Clams and oysters filter ocean water, cleaning it as they feed. Without these small, boneless creatures, plants would struggle to grow and oceans would turn murky.

So the next time a ladybug lands on your finger, remember: you are holding one of Earth's most successful animals. No backbone needed.

choices.feedback: A is incorrect because 'small, boneless creatures that clean ocean water' describes invertebrates like clams and oysters, not what an ecosystem means.

choices.text: Fruits and seeds that grow on many different plants
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because fruits and seeds are what plants make when they are pollinated, which has nothing to do with the meaning of ecosystem.

choices.text: Communities of living things and their surroundings
choices.feedback: C is correct because the passage gives us the definition right in the sentence — 'communities of living things and their surroundings' — placed between two dashes right after the word ecosystems.

choices.text: Earth's most successful animals that lack backbones
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because 'Earth's most successful animals that lack backbones' describes invertebrates like the ladybug mentioned at the end of the passage, not the meaning of ecosystem.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: Based on the article, what is one way arthropods are different from mollusks?

choices.text: Arthropods have bodies divided into segments, while mollusks have soft bodies often protected by hard shells.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the article clearly states arthropods have segmented bodies and exoskeletons, while mollusks have soft bodies often protected by hard shells.

choices.text: Arthropods use jet propulsion to escape from predators, while mollusks use jointed legs that bend like hinges.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because jet propulsion is used by squid, which are mollusks, and jointed legs belong to arthropods — the answer has them completely switched around.

choices.text: Arthropods are described as the smartest invertebrates alive, while mollusks are the largest group of animals on Earth.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the article says octopuses (mollusks) are the smartest invertebrates, and arthropods — not mollusks — are described as the largest group of animals on Earth.

choices.text: Arthropods must keep their outer coverings forever, while mollusks must shed their hard shells completely to grow.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because arthropods actually shed (molt) their exoskeletons to grow, and the article never says mollusks shed their shells.

items.title: Quiz Question 2

choices.feedback: B is correct because 'Arthropods: Armor on the Outside' explains that arthropods molt because their exoskeleton can't stretch as they grow, and 'Mollusks: Soft Bodies, Surprising Tricks' explains that octopuses squirt ink to confuse predators.

choices.text: "Arthropods: Armor on the Outside" and "Insects, Arachnids, and Crustaceans"

choices.text: "Mollusks: Soft Bodies, Surprising Tricks" and "Small Animals, Big Jobs"
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because 'Small Animals, Big Jobs' focuses on how invertebrates help the environment and does not explain molting or ink-squirting.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: Which sequence of key details does the author use to explain the main idea about invertebrates?

choices.text: First by explaining what they are, then by describing their bodies, and last by showing how they help nature.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage first tells us what invertebrates are, then describes their bodies, and finally explains how they help ecosystems — exactly that order.

choices.text: First by showing how they help nature, then by explaining what they are, and last by describing their bodies.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the passage starts by explaining what invertebrates are, not by showing how they help nature.

choices.text: First by listing the different types of insects, then by describing how they grow, and last by showing how they swim.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the passage covers many animals and topics beyond just insects, growing, and swimming.

choices.feedback: D is wrong because the passage never uses catching food, where they live, and hiding as its main sequence of ideas.

assessments.title: Alexander Graham Bell: The Man Who Made the World Talk

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Alexander Graham Bell and the Voice in the Wire

A Voice Travels Through a Wire

On March 10, 1876, a man in a small workshop leaned toward a strange metal device on a table and spoke: "Mr. Watson, come here. I want to see you." In another room, his assistant Thomas Watson pressed his ear to a matching device. He heard the words — scratchy, faint, but real. A human voice had just traveled through a wire for the first time. The man who spoke those words was Alexander Graham Bell.
stimulus.content_text:
Alexander Graham Bell and the Voice in the WireA Voice Travels Through a Wire
On March 10, 1876, a man in a small workshop leaned toward a strange metal device on a table and spoke: "Mr. Watson, come here. I want to see you." In another room, his assistant Thomas Watson pressed his ear to a matching device. He heard the words — scratchy, faint, but real. A human voice had just traveled through a wire for the first time. The man who spoke those words was Alexander Graham Bell.

items.prompt: According to the text, how was the voice Thomas Watson heard different from a normal speaking voice?

choices.text: It was loud and clear.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage says the voice was 'faint,' which means the opposite of loud and clear.

choices.text: It was scratchy and faint.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage uses the exact words 'scratchy' and 'faint' to describe what Thomas Watson heard.

choices.text: It was silent and still.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because Watson really did hear a voice — the whole point of the story is that a voice successfully traveled through the wire.

choices.text: It was musical and smooth.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage never describes the voice as musical or smooth — those words are not in the text at all.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
A Family Full of Sound

Bell was born in Edinburgh, Scotland, in 1847. Sound filled his family like music fills a concert hall. His father taught people how to speak clearly. Young Alexander grew up surrounded by the science of the human voice.

But here is the surprising part. Bell's mother was nearly deaf. She could barely hear the people around her. Alexander learned to talk to her by pressing close to her forehead and speaking in low, deep tones. She could feel the vibrations — the tiny back-and-forth shaking that sound makes, like the buzz you feel when you hum with your lips together. Later, Bell married a woman named Mabel, who was also deaf. These people he loved made him want to understand sound better than anyone.
stimulus.content_text:
A Family Full of Sound
Bell was born in Edinburgh, Scotland, in 1847. Sound filled his family like music fills a concert hall. His father taught people how to speak clearly. Young Alexander grew up surrounded by the science of the human voice.

But here is the surprising part. Bell's mother was nearly deaf. She could barely hear the people around her. Alexander learned to talk to her by pressing close to her forehead and speaking in low, deep tones. She could feel the vibrations — the tiny back-and-forth shaking that sound makes, like the buzz you feel when you hum with your lips together. Later, Bell married a woman named Mabel, who was also deaf. These people he loved made him want to understand sound better than anyone.

items.prompt: The main idea of the text is that Alexander Graham Bell's family caused his deep interest in sound. Which detail from the text best supports this main idea?

choices.text: He lived in a building filled with loud music.
choices.feedback: A is wrong because the passage never says Bell lived in a building filled with loud music — that detail does not exist in the text.

choices.text: He loved family members who were nearly deaf.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly says that the deaf family members Bell loved made him want to understand sound better than anyone.

choices.text: He learned to speak in very low, deep tones.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because speaking in low, deep tones was just how Bell talked to his mother, not the reason he became deeply interested in sound.

choices.text: He felt the tiny shaking of a person humming.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the humming example is used to explain what vibrations feel like, not to show why Bell's family made him interested in sound.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Teaching Deaf Students

Before he built his famous invention, Bell worked as a teacher of deaf students. He built small machines to show his students how sound moves through the air. This hands-on work gave Bell ideas that other inventors simply did not have. His deep knowledge of sound — learned through years of teaching — is what made the invention of the telephone possible.

Bell's work with deaf students was also a separate, lifelong effort that went beyond the telephone. The telephone was designed to carry speech across distances for hearing people, but Bell never stopped working to help deaf people communicate throughout his life.
stimulus.content_text:
Teaching Deaf Students
Before he built his famous invention, Bell worked as a teacher of deaf students. He built small machines to show his students how sound moves through the air. This hands-on work gave Bell ideas that other inventors simply did not have. His deep knowledge of sound — learned through years of teaching — is what made the invention of the telephone possible.

Bell's work with deaf students was also a separate, lifelong effort that went beyond the telephone. The telephone was designed to carry speech across distances for hearing people, but Bell never stopped working to help deaf people communicate throughout his life.

items.prompt: According to the text, what did Bell do before he built the telephone?

choices.text: He worked as a teacher of deaf students.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage clearly states, 'Before he built his famous invention, Bell worked as a teacher of deaf students.'

choices.text: He carried speech across very long distances.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because carrying speech across distances describes what the telephone does, not what Bell did before inventing it.

choices.text: He stopped helping deaf people to communicate.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the passage says Bell never stopped working to help deaf people — he kept helping them his whole life.

choices.text: He shared his new ideas with other inventors.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the passage says other inventors simply did not have Bell's ideas, meaning he did not share them with others.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
The First Telephone

Bell's telephone worked by turning sound vibrations into electrical signals — tiny pulses of energy that zip through a wire the way water rushes through a hose. At the other end, the signals turned back into sound. The first telephone could only send a voice a short distance. The sound was fuzzy and quiet. The device looked nothing like the slim phone in your pocket today. It was a clunky wooden box with metal parts.

Bell filed a patent for his telephone — an official paper from the government that says "this invention belongs to you." He was not the only person working on ways to send voice through a wire, but he was the one who earned that patent. Great inventions often have more than one person racing toward the same idea.

The device looked nothing like the slim phone in your pocket today. It was a clunky wooden box with metal parts.
