He was not the only person working on ways to send voice through a wire, but he was the one who earned that patent. Great inventions often have more than one person racing toward the same idea.

items.prompt: The passage says electrical signals 'zip through a wire the way water rushes through a hose.' How are electrical signals and water alike in this sentence?

choices.text: They both make a loud, fuzzy sound.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage never says electrical signals or water make a loud or fuzzy sound — that description is about how the telephone's voice sounded.

choices.text: They both move fast through a path.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage compares electrical signals zipping through a wire to water rushing through a hose, showing that both move quickly through a path.

choices.text: They both need a wooden box to work.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the wooden box describes what Bell's telephone looked like, and has nothing to do with how water or electrical signals work.

choices.text: They both turn into a voice at the end.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because while electrical signals do turn back into sound, water in a hose does not turn into a voice — that part is only about the signals.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Beyond the Telephone

Within a few years, telephone wires stretched across cities. Families separated by miles could hear each other's voices. Businesses could share news in seconds instead of days. During his lifetime, Bell received many awards and honors for this work.

But Bell never stopped being curious. He built giant kites and tested airplane designs, exploring early ideas about flight. He worked on hydrofoils — boats built to rise up and skim above the water at high speeds, like a stone skipping across a pond. He also helped start the National Geographic Society, a group dedicated to exploring and sharing knowledge about the Earth. You may have seen their famous yellow-bordered magazine.
stimulus.content_text:
Beyond the Telephone
Within a few years, telephone wires stretched across cities. Families separated by miles could hear each other's voices. Businesses could share news in seconds instead of days. During his lifetime, Bell received many awards and honors for this work.

But Bell never stopped being curious. He built giant kites and tested airplane designs, exploring early ideas about flight. He worked on hydrofoils — boats built to rise up and skim above the water at high speeds, like a stone skipping across a pond. He also helped start the National Geographic Society, a group dedicated to exploring and sharing knowledge about the Earth. You may have seen their famous yellow-bordered magazine.

items.prompt: According to the text, what are hydrofoils?

choices.text: boats that carry people across cities
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage never says hydrofoils carry people across cities — that detail isn't in the text at all.

choices.text: boats that rise above the water
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage clearly says hydrofoils are 'boats built to rise up and skim above the water at high speeds.'

choices.text: kites that help planes fly faster
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because hydrofoils are boats, not kites — Bell did work on kites, but those are two different things.

choices.text: groups that share knowledge of Earth
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because sharing knowledge about the Earth describes the National Geographic Society, not hydrofoils.

items.title: Section 6 - Guiding Question 5

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
A Voice That Still Carries

When Bell died, telephone companies paid tribute to him by silencing their lines for one minute.
stimulus.content_text:
A Voice That Still Carries
When Bell died, telephone companies paid tribute to him by silencing their lines for one minute.

items.prompt: Which detail from the text gives evidence that telephone companies wanted to honor Alexander Graham Bell?

choices.text: They silenced their lines for one minute.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage directly says telephone companies silenced their lines for one minute as a tribute, which means they were honoring Bell.

choices.text: They rang their bells for one full hour.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the passage never mentions ringing bells for an hour — that detail is not in the text at all.

choices.text: They talked on the phone for one minute.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the companies actually went silent, which is the opposite of talking on the phone.

choices.text: They turned off all lines for one year.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage says the silence lasted one minute, not one whole year.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: Based on the text, how was the first telephone different from the telephones that were used a few years later?

choices.text: The first telephone sent voices a short way, but later ones reached across miles.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage says the first telephone could only send a voice a short distance, but later telephones stretched across cities so people miles apart could talk.

choices.text: The first telephone was a small slim device, but later ones were big clunky boxes.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because it has it backwards — the passage says the first telephone was the clunky wooden box, not the later telephones.

choices.text: The first telephone was used by businesses, but later ones were used by families.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage says both families and businesses used telephones after they spread across cities, not just one group at a time.

choices.text: The first telephone used sound vibrations, but later ones used electrical signals.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage explains that both the first telephone and later telephones worked by turning sound into electrical signals — that did not change.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: What caused Bell to want to understand sound better than anyone?

choices.text: His love for his mother and wife, who were both deaf.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage directly says that his mother and wife, both of whom were deaf and whom he loved, made him want to understand sound better than anyone.

choices.text: His goal to invent a machine that could send a voice through a wire.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because inventing the telephone came later — it was something Bell worked toward after he already cared deeply about sound.

choices.text: His job as a teacher showing deaf students how sound moves.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because teaching deaf students helped Bell get ideas for his invention, but it wasn't the reason he first wanted to deeply understand sound.

choices.text: His father's work teaching people how to speak clearly.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because while Bell's father taught people to speak clearly, the passage never says this is what drove Bell to want to understand sound better than anyone.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: Which sentence best tells the main idea of the article?

choices.text: Bell built giant kites for flying, and later he started a famous magazine.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because building kites and starting a magazine are small details mentioned near the end, not the main idea of the whole article.

choices.text: Bell studied how sound moves, and later he invented the first telephone.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the entire article explains how Bell's lifelong study of sound led directly to his invention of the telephone.

choices.text: Bell invented the telephone, and later he learned to teach deaf students.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because it has the order backwards — Bell taught deaf students before he invented the telephone, not after.

choices.text: Bell made a clunky wooden box, and later he sent water through a long hose.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the wooden box and the hose comparison are just small details that describe the telephone, not the main idea of the article.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: How were Bell's work as a teacher of deaf students and his work as an inventor of the telephone similar?

choices.text: Both used vibrations felt through the forehead to send messages.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because pressing close to a forehead was only how Bell spoke to his mother — it was not part of his teaching or his telephone invention.

choices.text: Both grew from his deep understanding of how sound works.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage says Bell's deep knowledge of sound, built through teaching, is exactly what made it possible for him to invent the telephone.

choices.text: Both were designed mainly to help deaf people communicate.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because the passage clearly says the telephone was designed to carry speech for hearing people, not for deaf people.

choices.text: Both happened before he moved away from Scotland.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because Bell had already moved away from Scotland before he became a teacher of deaf students and before he invented the telephone.

syllabus_metadata.title: Alexander Graham Bell: The Man Who Made the World Talk

assessments.title: Copernicus: The Astronomer Who Moved the Earth

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Copernicus: The Man Who Moved the Earth

A Sky That Seemed to Spin

Step outside tonight and watch the stars. They slide across the darkness, east to west, as if the whole sky is spinning around you. The sun does the same thing every day — rising on one side, setting on the other. Your eyes tell you that everything up there is moving and you are standing still.

This idea is called the geocentric model — "geo" means Earth, so geocentric means "Earth-centered." The ancient Greek thinker Ptolemy taught this model, and the Catholic Church supported it.

Then a quiet astronomer from Poland looked more carefully.
stimulus.content_text:
Copernicus: The Man Who Moved the EarthA Sky That Seemed to Spin
Step outside tonight and watch the stars. They slide across the darkness, east to west, as if the whole sky is spinning around you. The sun does the same thing every day — rising on one side, setting on the other. Your eyes tell you that everything up there is moving and you are standing still.
This idea is called the geocentric model — "geo" means Earth, so geocentric means "Earth-centered." The ancient Greek thinker Ptolemy taught this model, and the Catholic Church supported it.
Then a quiet astronomer from Poland looked more carefully.

items.prompt: According to Section 1, which contrast does the author describe between the sky and a person on Earth?

choices.text: The sky seems to move, but a person seems to stand still.
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says the sky moves (stars slide, sun rises and sets) while 'you are standing still.'

choices.text: The sky seems to stand still, but a person seems to move.
choices.feedback: This is backwards — the passage says the sky seems to MOVE, not stand still, and the person seems to stand still, not move.

choices.text: The sky seems to rise, but a person seems to set.
choices.feedback: This doesn't make sense — people don't 'set' like the sun, and the passage never says anything like that.

choices.text: The sky seems to spin, but a person seems to spin.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because the passage says the person stands still, not that the person spins along with the sky.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
A Boy from Torun

Nicolaus Copernicus was born in 1473 in the town of Torun, Poland. He studied many subjects — mathematics, law, and medicine. But astronomy, the study of stars and planets, pulled at him the most. Night after night, year after year, he tracked the planets as they crawled across the sky.

Some planets seemed to stop, slide backward, then move forward again. If Earth truly sat still at the center, why would a planet reverse direction? The geocentric model could not explain this well.
stimulus.content_text:
A Boy from Torun
Nicolaus Copernicus was born in 1473 in the town of Torun, Poland. He studied many subjects — mathematics, law, and medicine. But astronomy, the study of stars and planets, pulled at him the most. Night after night, year after year, he tracked the planets as they crawled across the sky.
Some planets seemed to stop, slide backward, then move forward again. If Earth truly sat still at the center, why would a planet reverse direction? The geocentric model could not explain this well.

items.prompt: Based on the text, what caused Copernicus to question if Earth sat still at the center?

choices.text: The stars crawled slowly across the dark sky.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage mentions stars moving slowly, but that's not what made Copernicus question whether Earth was at the center.

choices.text: Some planets seemed to slide backward.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly says some planets seemed to slide backward, and Copernicus couldn't explain that if Earth was sitting still at the center.

choices.text: The planets stayed completely still in space.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage says the opposite — the planets moved in strange ways instead of staying still.

choices.text: The Earth reversed its direction in the sky.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because it was the other planets that appeared to reverse direction in the sky, not Earth itself.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
A New Idea About the Sun

Copernicus asked a daring question: what if Earth is not the center at all? What if Earth and the other planets all circle the sun instead?

He called this the heliocentric model. "Helios" is the Greek word for sun, so heliocentric means "sun-centered." In his model, the sun sat at the center of our solar system — the family of planets near our sun. Actually, he showed it was the center of our solar system — the universe is far, far bigger. Earth and the other planets orbited the sun, meaning they traveled around it in big loops, the way a dog on a long leash circles a post.

Earth also spun on its own axis, like a stick through a spinning top. That spin gives us day and night. The side facing the sun gets daylight. The side facing away gets darkness.

This new model also explained why some planets seemed to go backward. They were not really going backward. Earth was simply passing them on a closer, faster orbit — like a car you pass on the highway that seems to drift behind you even though it is still moving forward.
stimulus.content_text:
A New Idea About the Sun
Copernicus asked a daring question: what if Earth is not the center at all? What if Earth and the other planets all circle the sun instead?
He called this the heliocentric model. "Helios" is the Greek word for sun, so heliocentric means "sun-centered." In his model, the sun sat at the center of our solar system — the family of planets near our sun. Actually, he showed it was the center of our solar system — the universe is far, far bigger. Earth and the other planets orbited the sun, meaning they traveled around it in big loops, the way a dog on a long leash circles a post.
Earth also spun on its own axis, like a stick through a spinning top. That spin gives us day and night. The side facing the sun gets daylight. The side facing away gets darkness.
This new model also explained why some planets seemed to go backward. They were not really going backward. Earth was simply passing them on a closer, faster orbit — like a car you pass on the highway that seems to drift behind you even though it is still moving forward.

items.prompt: According to the text, what happens when Earth passes other planets on a closer, faster orbit?

choices.text: They seem to drift behind.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage directly says that when Earth passes other planets, they 'seem to drift behind' — just like a car you pass on the highway.

choices.text: They stop moving forward completely.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the passage clearly says the planets are 'still moving forward' — they never actually stop.

choices.text: They spin on their own axis.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because spinning on an axis is something Earth does to create day and night, and has nothing to do with passing other planets.

choices.text: They travel in big loops.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because traveling in big loops describes how planets orbit the sun, not what happens when Earth passes them.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
A Book and a Long Wait

Challenging a belief held by the Church and by powerful leaders was risky. So he worked quietly for decades, collecting evidence and sharpening his math. He wrote everything in a book called On the Revolutions of the Heavenly Spheres. According to legend, a copy was placed in his hands on the very day he died in 1543.

Copernicus did not live to see the world accept his work. Later scientists built on his ideas to prove he was right. The Italian astronomer Galileo used a telescope to observe moons circling Jupiter — not Earth — and the phases of Venus, which matched the heliocentric model. The German mathematician Johannes Kepler figured out the exact shapes of the planets' paths around the sun.
stimulus.content_text:
A Book and a Long Wait
Challenging a belief held by the Church and by powerful leaders was risky. So he worked quietly for decades, collecting evidence and sharpening his math. He wrote everything in a book called On the Revolutions of the Heavenly Spheres. According to legend, a copy was placed in his hands on the very day he died in 1543.
Copernicus did not live to see the world accept his work. Later scientists built on his ideas to prove he was right. The Italian astronomer Galileo used a telescope to observe moons circling Jupiter — not Earth — and the phases of Venus, which matched the heliocentric model. The German mathematician Johannes Kepler figured out the exact shapes of the planets' paths around the sun.

items.prompt: Based on the text, how were the scientists Galileo and Kepler alike?

choices.text: They both used a telescope to see Jupiter.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because only Galileo used a telescope to observe Jupiter — the passage does not say Kepler used a telescope at all.

choices.text: They both helped prove Copernicus was right.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage says later scientists built on Copernicus's ideas to prove he was right, and it shows both Galileo and Kepler each contributed evidence that supported his heliocentric model.

choices.text: They both wrote a book about the universe.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage only mentions Copernicus writing a book — it does not say Galileo or Kepler wrote one.

choices.text: They both kept their math a secret for years.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because it was Copernicus who worked quietly and kept his work private for decades, not Galileo or Kepler.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
One Planet Among Many

Tonight, when you watch the sun set, remember: it is not really sinking below the edge of the world. You are the one moving, riding a spinning planet that circles a star. Copernicus showed that your own senses can fool you — and that asking "what if we are wrong?" can lead to the biggest discoveries of all.
stimulus.content_text:
One Planet Among Many
Tonight, when you watch the sun set, remember: it is not really sinking below the edge of the world. You are the one moving, riding a spinning planet that circles a star. Copernicus showed that your own senses can fool you — and that asking "what if we are wrong?" can lead to the biggest discoveries of all.

items.prompt: According to the section "One Planet Among Many," what does a sun set really mean?

choices.text: the sun moving below the edge of the world
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage directly tells us the sun is NOT really moving below the edge of the world — that's just what it looks like.

choices.text: the Earth turning as you ride on it
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage says 'you are the one moving, riding a spinning planet,' which means the Earth turning is what causes a sunset.

choices.text: the star circling around your planet
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage says our planet circles the star, not that the star circles our planet.

choices.text: the world stopping at the end of the day
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage never says the world stops — it describes Earth as a spinning, moving planet.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: According to the article, what is the difference between what the planets seem to do and what they are actually doing?

choices.text: They seem to spin like a top, but they are actually sliding across space.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the article never says planets spin like a top or slide across space — that's not how the article describes their movement.

choices.text: They seem to travel in big loops, but they are actually sitting completely still.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the article clearly says planets travel in big loops around the sun, so they are moving, not sitting still.

choices.text: They seem to move backward, but they are actually just being passed by Earth.
choices.feedback: C is correct because the article explains that planets only seem to go backward, but they are actually still moving forward — Earth is just passing them on a closer, faster orbit.

choices.text: They seem to circle the sun, but they are actually traveling around the Earth.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because Copernicus's whole big idea was that planets circle the sun, not Earth — that's the heliocentric model!

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: To find out what causes the planets to look like they are moving backward, which two headings should a student read?

choices.text: "A Sky That Seemed to Spin" and "A Boy from Torun"
choices.feedback: A is wrong because 'A Sky That Seemed to Spin' is about how the whole sky looks like it moves around Earth, not about planets appearing to go backward.

choices.text: "A Boy from Torun" and "A New Idea About the Sun"
choices.feedback: B is correct because 'A Boy from Torun' introduces the mystery of planets looking like they go backward, and 'A New Idea About the Sun' explains exactly why that happens.

choices.text: "A New Idea About the Sun" and "A Book and a Long Wait"
choices.feedback: C is wrong because 'A Book and a Long Wait' is mostly about Copernicus writing his book and other scientists who came after him, not about why planets look like they go backward.

choices.text: "A Book and a Long Wait" and "One Planet Among Many"
choices.feedback: D is wrong because 'A Book and a Long Wait' and 'One Planet Among Many' are about Copernicus sharing his ideas and what we can learn from him, not about the backward-moving planet mystery.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: Which order of events best tells the main idea of the article?

choices.text: He studied the sky for years, he created a sun-centered model, and later scientists proved it.
choices.feedback: A is correct because it matches the article's main story in the right order: years of sky-watching, then the sun-centered model, then later scientists proving it.

choices.text: He created a sun-centered model, later scientists proved it, and he studied the sky for years.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because it puts the steps in the wrong order — Copernicus studied the sky first, before he ever created his model.

choices.text: He tracked the planets moving, he saw them slide backward, and he thought the Earth sat still.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because it only describes small details about planet movement, not the big main idea of the whole article.

choices.text: He learned from other thinkers, he created a sun-centered model, and he proved it all himself.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the article clearly says Copernicus did not prove his idea himself — Galileo and Kepler did that after he died.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: Based on the article, how does the author compare what our eyes tell us with what Copernicus showed?

choices.text: Our eyes tell us the sky is spinning, but Copernicus showed that Earth is the one spinning.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage says our eyes make the sky look like it is spinning around us, but Copernicus proved it is really Earth that spins on its own axis.

choices.text: Our eyes tell us the planets stop moving, but Copernicus showed that they are the ones spinning.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the article talks about planets seeming to go backward, not about our eyes thinking planets stop spinning.

choices.text: Our eyes tell us the sun sinks away, but Copernicus showed that it shrinks during the night.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the article never says the sun shrinks during the night — Copernicus showed Earth moves, not that the sun changes size.

choices.text: Our eyes tell us the Earth is traveling, but Copernicus showed that it stands perfectly still instead.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because it flips the truth — our eyes make us feel like Earth stands still, and Copernicus showed that Earth is actually the one moving.

syllabus_metadata.title: Copernicus: The Astronomer Who Moved the Earth

assessments.title: Mae Jemison: From Chicago to the Stars

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Mae Jemison: From Chicago to the Stars

A Girl with a Library Card and Big Plans

Mae Jemison was born in Decatur, Alabama, on October 17, 1956, and grew up in Chicago, Illinois. Young Mae spent hours at the public library. She read about astronomy, the study of stars and planets. She explored anthropology, the study of how people around the world live and celebrate. She dug into archaeology, the science of uncovering old objects to learn how people lived long ago.

She didn't think she had to pick just one thing to be good at. That habit of following many interests would shape her whole life — all the way to outer space.
stimulus.content_text:
Mae Jemison: From Chicago to the StarsA Girl with a Library Card and Big Plans
Mae Jemison was born in Decatur, Alabama, on October 17, 1956, and grew up in Chicago, Illinois. Young Mae spent hours at the public library. She read about astronomy, the study of stars and planets. She explored anthropology, the study of how people around the world live and celebrate. She dug into archaeology, the science of uncovering old objects to learn how people lived long ago.

She didn't think she had to pick just one thing to be good at. That habit of following many interests would shape her whole life — all the way to outer space.

items.prompt: According to the text, how is archaeology different from anthropology?

choices.text: It focuses on reading about stars and planets
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because studying stars and planets is the definition of astronomy, not archaeology.

choices.text: It focuses on uncovering old objects from the past
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly says archaeology is 'the science of uncovering old objects to learn how people lived long ago.'

choices.text: It focuses on how people celebrate around the world
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because learning how people celebrate around the world is the definition of anthropology, not archaeology.

choices.text: It focuses on following many different interests
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because following many different interests describes Mae's personal habit, not what archaeology means.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
A Student Who Surprised Everyone

Mae entered Stanford University at just 16 years old. She earned a degree in chemical engineering — the science of designing medicines, fuels, and new materials by understanding how tiny particles called molecules work together. She also studied African and African American history. Then she went to Cornell Medical School and became a doctor.

Mae's wide training made her exactly the kind of person NASA needed. Actually, NASA wants people with many different skills, and Mae had spent her whole life building them.
stimulus.content_text:
A Student Who Surprised Everyone
Mae entered Stanford University at just 16 years old. She earned a degree in chemical engineering — the science of designing medicines, fuels, and new materials by understanding how tiny particles called molecules work together. She also studied African and African American history. Then she went to Cornell Medical School and became a doctor.

Mae's wide training made her exactly the kind of person NASA needed. Actually, NASA wants people with many different skills, and Mae had spent her whole life building them.

items.prompt: In the section 'A Student Who Surprised Everyone,' what is the main reason Mae was exactly the kind of person NASA needed?

choices.text: Because she went to college at a young age.
choices.feedback: Going to college at 16 is impressive, but the passage never says NASA needed her because of her young age.

choices.text: Because she learned many different skills.
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says NASA wants people with many different skills, and Mae spent her whole life building lots of different skills.

choices.text: Because she wanted to design new medicines.
choices.feedback: Designing medicines was just one part of what she studied, and the passage doesn't say that's why NASA needed her.

choices.text: Because she surprised everyone at her school.
choices.feedback: The passage never says NASA was interested in Mae because she surprised people at school.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Healing People Far from Home

Before joining NASA, Mae joined the Peace Corps, a program where Americans volunteer to help communities in other countries. She worked as a medical officer in Sierra Leone and Liberia in West Africa. Sometimes she had very few supplies — no fancy hospital, no shelves full of medicine. She solved problems with whatever she had. That made her tough, creative, and calm under pressure.
stimulus.content_text:
Healing People Far from Home
Before joining NASA, Mae joined the Peace Corps, a program where Americans volunteer to help communities in other countries. She worked as a medical officer in Sierra Leone and Liberia in West Africa. Sometimes she had very few supplies — no fancy hospital, no shelves full of medicine. She solved problems with whatever she had. That made her tough, creative, and calm under pressure.

items.prompt: According to the text, what is the order of Mae's two jobs?

choices.text: She worked in the Peace Corps after joining NASA.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage says she joined the Peace Corps *before* NASA, not after.

choices.text: She worked in the Peace Corps before joining NASA.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage starts with 'Before joining NASA, Mae joined the Peace Corps,' which tells us the Peace Corps came first.

choices.text: She worked in the Peace Corps while working at NASA.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage does not say she did both jobs at the same time.

choices.text: She worked in the Peace Corps instead of joining NASA.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because Mae did join NASA — the Peace Corps was just something she did earlier in her life.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Science in Space — Not Just Flying

Back in the United States, Mae applied to NASA's astronaut program. NASA accepted her in 1987. After years of training, she launched aboard the Endeavour. On September 12, 1992, she strapped herself into the Space Shuttle Endeavour and roared off the launch pad, becoming the first African American woman to travel in space.

It's easy to imagine that astronauts just fly the spaceship. The surprising part: most of their time is spent doing science experiments. Mae studied motion sickness and bone cell growth — how the tiny building blocks inside your bones change and multiply. On Earth, gravity pulls on your body. In space, your body floats. Do bones grow the same way without gravity? These experiments could only happen in orbit, the looping path a spacecraft follows around Earth. Mae circled Earth 127 times over nearly eight days. Looking down at our blue planet, she later said she felt connected to all people everywhere.
stimulus.content_text:
Science in Space — Not Just Flying
Back in the United States, Mae applied to NASA's astronaut program. NASA accepted her in 1987. After years of training, she launched aboard the Endeavour. On September 12, 1992, she strapped herself into the Space Shuttle Endeavour and roared off the launch pad, becoming the first African American woman to travel in space.

It's easy to imagine that astronauts just fly the spaceship. The surprising part: most of their time is spent doing science experiments. Mae studied motion sickness and bone cell growth — how the tiny building blocks inside your bones change and multiply. On Earth, gravity pulls on your body. In space, your body floats. Do bones grow the same way without gravity? These experiments could only happen in orbit, the looping path a spacecraft follows around Earth. Mae circled Earth 127 times over nearly eight days. Looking down at our blue planet, she later said she felt connected to all people everywhere.

items.prompt: The author compares bone cells to 'building blocks.' Based on this comparison, what does the phrase 'building blocks' mean in the passage?

choices.text: Tiny pieces that help build a bone
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage says bone cells are 'tiny building blocks inside your bones,' meaning they are small pieces that help make up and build your bones.

choices.text: Heavy forces that pull on the body
choices.feedback: B is wrong because heavy forces that pull on the body describes gravity, not bone cells or building blocks.

choices.text: Looping paths that go around Earth
choices.feedback: C is wrong because a looping path around Earth describes orbit, which is a completely different word and idea in the passage.

choices.text: Sick feelings that happen in space
choices.feedback: D is wrong because sick feelings in space describes motion sickness, which Mae studied but is not related to what 'building blocks' means.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
The Work After the Famous Moment

Mae left NASA in 1993. But sometimes what a person does after a famous achievement matters just as much. Mae founded the Jemison Group, a company that develops new technologies and brings advanced science into everyday life. She also created a program called The Earth We Share, which helps young people use science to solve real problems. She has always believed science education should reach everyone — especially kids from communities that have been left out.

Her contributions were recognized with a place in the National Women's Hall of Fame and the International Space Hall of Fame. She even appeared on the TV show Star Trek — a show that had inspired her as a kid in Chicago.
stimulus.content_text:

Mae left NASA in 1993. But sometimes what a person does after a famous achievement matters just as much. Mae founded the Jemison Group, a company that develops new technologies and brings advanced science into everyday life. She also created a program called The Earth We Share, which helps young people use science to solve real problems. She has always believed science education should reach everyone — especially kids from communities that have been left out.

Her contributions were recognized with a place in the National Women's Hall of Fame and the International Space Hall of Fame. She even appeared on the TV show Star Trek — a show that had inspired her as a kid in Chicago.

items.prompt: According to the text, what is The Earth We Share?

choices.text: A company that makes new science tools.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because making new science tools describes the Jemison Group, not The Earth We Share.

choices.text: A program that helps kids solve problems.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly says The Earth We Share is a program that helps young people use science to solve real problems.

choices.text: A television show that inspires young children.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because Star Trek is the television show mentioned in the passage, not The Earth We Share.

choices.text: A special place that honors famous women.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the Halls of Fame are the special places that honored Mae, not a program she created.

items.title: Section 6 - Guiding Question 5

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
Why Mae Jemison's Story Matters to You

She was a reader, a dancer, an engineer, a doctor, a volunteer, an astronaut, and a teacher. She didn't wait for someone to invite her into science.
stimulus.content_text:
Why Mae Jemison's Story Matters to You
She was a reader, a dancer, an engineer, a doctor, a volunteer, an astronaut, and a teacher. She didn't wait for someone to invite her into science.

items.prompt: Which detail from the text best shows that Mae Jemison went after what she wanted?

choices.text: She was a reader and a dancer.
choices.feedback: This tells us two things Mae enjoyed, but it doesn't show us that she chased her dreams or went after what she wanted.

choices.text: She did not wait for an invitation.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly says she 'didn't wait for someone to invite her,' which shows she took action and went after her goals herself.

choices.text: She wanted her story to matter to you.
choices.feedback: The passage never says Mae wanted her story to matter to you — that's the title of the article, not something Mae did.

choices.text: She invited other people into science.
choices.feedback: This is the opposite of what the passage says — the text tells us Mae didn't wait for an invitation, not that she gave invitations to others.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: Based on the text, how was Mae's work in the Peace Corps different from her work at NASA?

choices.text: She used very few supplies in the Peace Corps, but she used a spaceship at NASA.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage clearly says Mae had very few supplies in the Peace Corps, while at NASA she used a Space Shuttle and ran science experiments in space.

choices.text: She studied how people live in the Peace Corps, but she studied old objects at NASA.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because studying how people live and studying old objects were things Mae read about as a child at the library, not things she did in the Peace Corps or at NASA.

choices.text: She stayed in her hometown for the Peace Corps, but she traveled to Africa for NASA.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the passage says Mae went to West Africa for the Peace Corps, not her hometown — it's the opposite of what the text says.

choices.text: She built large hospitals in the Peace Corps, but she built large schools at NASA.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the passage never mentions Mae building hospitals or schools in either the Peace Corps or NASA.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: Why was Mae able to do so many different things in her life, from helping people in Africa to doing science experiments in space?

choices.text: Because she became famous after her space flight
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because becoming famous was a result of her hard work, not the reason she was able to do so many things.

choices.text: Because she followed many interests as a child
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly says her habit of following many interests shaped her whole life, from helping people in Africa to doing science in space.

choices.text: Because NASA trained her to learn new subjects
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage says NASA wanted her because she already had many different skills — she built those skills long before NASA.

choices.text: Because she read only about astronomy as a child
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because Mae read about many subjects as a child, including astronomy, anthropology, and archaeology — not just astronomy.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: The main idea of the article is that Mae Jemison followed many interests that led to her success. How does the order of events in the text support this main idea?

choices.text: It describes how she read as a child, learned skills in college, and used them in space.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the article follows Mae's life in order — reading as a child, building skills in school, and using them all in space — which shows how her many interests led to her success.

choices.text: It describes how she traveled to space first, became a doctor next, and read books last.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the order is backwards — Mae read books and became a doctor first, and only traveled to space later in her life.

choices.text: It describes how she focused on only one subject in school before deciding to join NASA.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because Mae did the opposite of focusing on one subject — she studied engineering, history, and medicine, and that's what made her special.

choices.text: It describes how she helped people in Africa so she could start her own science company.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because even though Mae did help people in Africa and later started a company, the article doesn't say one caused the other.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: Based on the article, how was Mae's work at NASA similar to her work at the Jemison Group?

choices.text: Both used science to discover or make new things.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the article shows that at NASA Mae did science experiments to learn new things, and at the Jemison Group she developed new technologies — both used science to discover or create.

choices.text: Both required her to travel deep into outer space.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because only her NASA job involved traveling to space; the Jemison Group was work she did on Earth after leaving NASA.

choices.text: Both helped her work as a doctor in West Africa.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because her doctor work in West Africa happened before both NASA and the Jemison Group, so it wasn't part of either job.

choices.text: Both involved studying how human bones multiply.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because studying bone cell growth was something Mae did during her NASA mission, not something she did at the Jemison Group.

syllabus_metadata.title: Mae Jemison: From Chicago to the Stars

assessments.title: Lines and Shapes Everywhere: The Building Blocks of Art

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Elements of Art Review

Lines Are Everywhere

A bolt of lightning cracks across a dark sky. It's a line. So is the edge where your classroom wall meets the ceiling. So is the curve of a smile on your best friend's face. Lines are one of the basic building blocks artists use to make every drawing, painting, and sculpture.

A wavy line feels playful, like the surface of the ocean. For example, a curved line feels gentle and flowing, like a river bending through a valley.

Lines create outlines that show the edges of objects. Curved lines can follow the path of a dancer to show movement. Soft, curving lines might show peace. In addition, artists use lines to guide your eye across a picture, pulling you from one part to another.

Actually, lines show up in places you don't expect. The edge where a red shape meets a blue background creates a line. The fold in a piece of cloth creates a line. Once you start looking, you'll find lines made by edges, borders, and boundaries all through art — and all through the real world.

Elements of Art ReviewLines Are Everywhere

A wavy line feels playful, like the surface of the ocean. For example, a curved line feels gentle and flowing, like a river bending through a valley.

Lines create outlines that show the edges of objects. Curved lines can follow the path of a dancer to show movement. Soft, curving lines might show peace. In addition, artists use lines to guide your eye across a picture, pulling you from one part to another.

Actually, lines show up in places you don't expect. The edge where a red shape meets a blue background creates a line. The fold in a piece of cloth creates a line. Once you start looking, you'll find lines made by edges, borders, and boundaries all through art — and all through the real world.

items.prompt: According to the text, how does a wavy line feel different from a curved line?

choices.text: A wavy line feels gentle while a curved line feels strong
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage says a curved line feels gentle, not strong — the word 'strong' never appears in the text.

choices.text: A wavy line feels playful while a curved line feels gentle
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly states that a wavy line feels playful and a curved line feels gentle, matching this answer perfectly.

choices.text: A wavy line feels peaceful while a curved line feels playful
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because it has the feelings backwards — the passage says wavy lines feel playful, not peaceful.

choices.text: A wavy line feels flowing while a curved line feels sharp
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage describes curved lines as gentle and flowing, and never uses the word 'sharp' to describe any line.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Two Kinds of Shapes

When lines close together and surround an area, they create a shape. There are two main kinds.

Geometric shapes — shapes with precise, regular edges — are the ones you know from math class. Circles, squares, triangles, and rectangles are all geometric. You see them in buildings, road signs, and tile patterns. You might think geometric shapes belong only in math, not in art. However, the Dutch painter Piet Mondrian made famous paintings using only rectangles and squares filled with red, yellow, and blue. Artists across the world have used geometric shapes in beautiful tile designs for hundreds of years. Geometric shapes are powerful artistic choices.

Organic shapes — shapes that are irregular and free-flowing — look like things you find in nature. Think of the shape of a leaf, a cloud, or a puddle. No two are exactly alike. They feel natural and alive.
stimulus.content_text:
Two Kinds of Shapes

Geometric shapes — shapes with precise, regular edges — are the ones you know from math class. Circles, squares, triangles, and rectangles are all geometric. You see them in buildings, road signs, and tile patterns. You might think geometric shapes belong only in math, not in art. However, the Dutch painter Piet Mondrian made famous paintings using only rectangles and squares filled with red, yellow, and blue. Artists across the world have used geometric shapes in beautiful tile designs for hundreds of years. Geometric shapes are powerful artistic choices.

Organic shapes — shapes that are irregular and free-flowing — look like things you find in nature. Think of the shape of a leaf, a cloud, or a puddle. No two are exactly alike. They feel natural and alive.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, why do organic shapes feel natural and alive?

choices.text: because they are used in tile designs around the world
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage says tile designs are made using geometric shapes, not organic shapes.

choices.text: because they are irregular and no two are exactly alike
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly says organic shapes are irregular and no two are exactly alike, which is why they feel natural and alive.

choices.text: because they have precise and regular edges like circles
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because precise, regular edges are what describe geometric shapes like circles and squares, which is the opposite of organic shapes.

choices.text: because they are made when lines close together in art
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because lines closing together explains how all shapes are created in general, not what makes organic shapes feel natural and alive.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
From Flat Shapes to Solid Forms

Here's something that trips people up. A circle drawn on paper is flat. It has height and width, but you can't pick it up. It's a shape. Now imagine an artist adds shading to that circle — a dark side, a light side, a shadow underneath. Suddenly it looks like a ball you could hold. That three-dimensional version — something that appears to have depth, like a real solid object — is called a form.

A square becomes a cube. A triangle becomes a cone or a pyramid. Forms look like you could reach out and grab them, even on a flat piece of paper. Artists create this trick because they use shading, shadows, and careful placement of light and dark areas.
stimulus.content_text:
From Flat Shapes to Solid Forms
Here's something that trips people up. A circle drawn on paper is flat. It has height and width, but you can't pick it up. It's a shape. Now imagine an artist adds shading to that circle — a dark side, a light side, a shadow underneath. Suddenly it looks like a ball you could hold. That three-dimensional version — something that appears to have depth, like a real solid object — is called a form.

A square becomes a cube. A triangle becomes a cone or a pyramid. Forms look like you could reach out and grab them, even on a flat piece of paper. Artists create this trick because they use shading, shadows, and careful placement of light and dark areas.

items.prompt: According to the text, what steps does an artist follow to make a flat circle look like a ball?

choices.text: First the artist draws a circle, then adds bright colors, then places it next to other shapes.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage never says anything about using bright colors or placing shapes next to other shapes.

choices.text: First the artist draws a circle, then adds shading and shadows, then makes it look solid.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage clearly shows the artist draws a circle first, then adds shading and shadows, and that makes it look like a solid, three-dimensional ball.

choices.text: First the artist draws a square, then adds shading and shadows, then makes it look solid.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage says the artist starts with a circle to make a ball, not a square.

choices.text: First the artist adds shading and shadows, then draws a circle, then makes it look solid.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because according to the passage, the circle is drawn first and the shading is added after, so the order is flipped in this answer.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Putting It All Together

Artists combine lines, shapes, and forms in endless ways. A painting of rolling hills might use curved lines for the land, geometric shapes for houses, and organic shapes for trees and clouds. Similarly, a portrait uses lines to trace the curves of a face. Shading then turns those flat shapes into forms that seem to rise off the paper.

Right now, wherever you are sitting, lines and shapes surround you. The rectangles of windows. The organic shape of a tree branch outside. The straight lines of letters on this very page. Every artist who ever lived looked at the same world you see — and used these same building blocks to capture it. Therefore, you'll be speaking the language that artists have shared for thousands of years.

Right now, wherever you are sitting, lines and shapes surround you. The rectangles of windows. The organic shape of a tree branch outside. The straight lines of letters on this very page. Every artist who ever lived looked at the same world you see — and used these same building blocks to capture it.
