items.prompt: According to the section, how are a painting of rolling hills and a portrait similar?

choices.text: Both use only geometric shapes to show objects.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the hills painting uses organic shapes for trees and clouds, not only geometric shapes.

choices.text: Both use lines and shapes as building blocks.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage shows both the hills painting and the portrait using lines and shapes to build their pictures.

choices.text: Both show the same outdoor scene with trees.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because a portrait shows a person's face, not the same outdoor scene as the hills painting.

choices.text: Both need shading but do not need any lines.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage clearly says lines are used in both paintings, so lines are definitely needed.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: Based on the article, how is creating a shape different from creating a form?

choices.text: A shape is made by closing lines together, but a form is made by adding shadows.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the article says shapes form when lines close together, and forms are made by adding shadows and shading to make something look solid and 3D.

choices.text: A shape is made by adding dark shadows, but a form is made by closing lines together.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because it has the definitions switched — shapes use closed lines, not shadows, and forms use shadows, not closed lines.

choices.text: A shape is made by using math tools, but a form is made by looking at nature.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the article never says you need math tools to make a shape or that forms come from looking at nature.

choices.text: A shape is made by mixing bright colors, but a form is made by drawing flat edges.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the article never mentions mixing bright colors as a way to make shapes or forms.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: Which section heading would best help you find what causes flat art to look solid and what causes your eye to move across a picture?

choices.text: Lines Are Everywhere and From Flat Shapes to Solid Forms
choices.feedback: A is correct because 'Lines Are Everywhere' explains how lines move your eye across a picture, and 'From Flat Shapes to Solid Forms' explains how shading makes flat art look solid.

choices.text: Two Kinds of Shapes and Putting It All Together
choices.feedback: B is wrong because 'Two Kinds of Shapes' talks about geometric and organic shapes, and 'Putting It All Together' reviews everything — neither one focuses on making your eye move or making art look solid.

choices.text: Lines Are Everywhere and Two Kinds of Shapes
choices.feedback: C is wrong because 'Two Kinds of Shapes' is about different types of shapes, not about making flat art look solid.

choices.text: From Flat Shapes to Solid Forms and Putting It All Together
choices.feedback: D is wrong because 'Putting It All Together' gives a general review of everything and doesn't specifically explain what makes your eye move across a picture.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: How does the author organize the ideas in the article to explain the building blocks of art?

choices.text: The author explains lines first, next describes how lines make shapes, and finally shows how shapes become forms.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the article's sections go in this exact order: lines first, then how lines make shapes, then how shapes become forms.

choices.text: The author explains forms first, next describes how forms make shapes, and finally shows how shapes become lines.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the article begins with lines, not forms, and forms are actually explained last, not first.

choices.text: The author explains shading first, next describes how shading makes lines, and finally shows how lines become shapes.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because shading is only mentioned near the end of the article as a way to turn shapes into forms — it is not the starting topic.

choices.text: The author explains shapes first, next describes how shapes make forms, and finally shows how forms become lines.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because shapes are the second topic explained in the article, not the first — lines come before shapes.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: Based on the article, what do lines, shapes, and forms all have in common?

choices.text: They are all building blocks used to make art.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the article calls lines 'basic building blocks' of art and shows how lines, shapes, and forms all work together as tools artists use to create artwork.

choices.text: They are all flat drawings that cannot be held.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because forms are described as three-dimensional and looking like solid objects you could reach out and grab, so they are definitely not all flat.

choices.text: They are all found only in math and road signs.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the article says lines and shapes are found everywhere in art and the real world, not just in math and road signs.

choices.text: They are all made by adding shadows to pictures.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because adding shadows is only how artists turn flat shapes into forms — lines and shapes are made in other ways.

syllabus_metadata.title: Lines and Shapes Everywhere: The Building Blocks of Art

assessments.title: A World of Color: Primary, Secondary, Warm, and Cool

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Secret Lives of Colors

Three Colors That Start It All

A sunflower field blazes yellow against a bright blue sky. A red cardinal lands on a green branch. Color is everywhere, but here is something wild: almost every color you see in a painting starts with just three. Red, yellow, and blue are called the primary colors — the starter ingredients, like flour, sugar, and eggs in baking. No matter how hard you try, you cannot mix other paints together to make red. You cannot mix your way to yellow or blue, either. They are the colors that come first.

Arrange them in a circle, and you have built a color wheel, one of the most useful tools any artist can own.

You might wonder: what happens if you mix all three primary colors at once? Actually, it makes brown or muddy gray! That surprise catches many young artists off guard. Also, a few tricky colors — like bright pink or turquoise — are hard to mix perfectly from red, yellow, and blue alone. But these three will still get you most of the way to any color you dream up.
stimulus.content_text:
The Secret Lives of ColorsThree Colors That Start It All
A sunflower field blazes yellow against a bright blue sky. A red cardinal lands on a green branch. Color is everywhere, but here is something wild: almost every color you see in a painting starts with just three. Red, yellow, and blue are called the primary colors — the starter ingredients, like flour, sugar, and eggs in baking. No matter how hard you try, you cannot mix other paints together to make red. You cannot mix your way to yellow or blue, either. They are the colors that come first.
Arrange them in a circle, and you have built a color wheel, one of the most useful tools any artist can own.
You might wonder: what happens if you mix all three primary colors at once? Actually, it makes brown or muddy gray! That surprise catches many young artists off guard. Also, a few tricky colors — like bright pink or turquoise — are hard to mix perfectly from red, yellow, and blue alone. But these three will still get you most of the way to any color you dream up.

items.prompt: According to the text, even though primary colors can make almost any color, which colors are hard to mix perfectly?

choices.text: Pink or turquoise
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage specifically names 'bright pink or turquoise' as colors that are hard to mix perfectly from the three primary colors.

choices.text: Brown or muddy gray
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because brown or muddy gray is what happens when you mix all three primary colors together — it's an easy accident, not a hard color to make!

choices.text: Red, yellow, or blue
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because red, yellow, and blue are the primary colors — they are the starter colors you begin with, not colors you need to mix.

choices.text: Bright blue or green
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage never says bright blue or green are hard to mix — those colors are not mentioned as tricky ones.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Warm Colors and Cool Colors

Colors belong to two families based on the feelings they create. Red, orange, and yellow are warm colors. Think of campfires, sunsets, and hot sand. Warm colors feel energetic. In a painting, they seem to jump toward you, grabbing your eyes first.

Blue, green, and purple are cool colors. Think of ocean waves, shady forests, and ice cubes. Cool colors feel calm and quiet. In a painting, they seem to drift backward, making things look farther away. That is why distant mountains in paintings often look blue or purple — the artist uses cool colors to push them into the background.

Interestingly, a color's warmth or coolness can even shift depending on its neighbors. Place a yellow-green next to orange, and it looks cool. Place that same yellow-green next to deep blue, and suddenly it looks warm. Colors change their personality based on who stands beside them.
stimulus.content_text:
Warm Colors and Cool Colors
Colors belong to two families based on the feelings they create. Red, orange, and yellow are warm colors. Think of campfires, sunsets, and hot sand. Warm colors feel energetic. In a painting, they seem to jump toward you, grabbing your eyes first.
Blue, green, and purple are cool colors. Think of ocean waves, shady forests, and ice cubes. Cool colors feel calm and quiet. In a painting, they seem to drift backward, making things look farther away. That is why distant mountains in paintings often look blue or purple — the artist uses cool colors to push them into the background.
Interestingly, a color's warmth or coolness can even shift depending on its neighbors. Place a yellow-green next to orange, and it looks cool. Place that same yellow-green next to deep blue, and suddenly it looks warm. Colors change their personality based on who stands beside them.

items.prompt: Which key detail explains why artists paint distant mountains blue or purple?

choices.text: Cool colors push things into the background.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage directly says cool colors make things look farther away and push them into the background, which is exactly why artists use blue or purple for distant mountains.

choices.text: Cool colors make things jump toward your eyes.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because jumping toward your eyes is what warm colors do, not cool colors.

choices.text: Cool colors change based on their neighbors.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because colors changing based on their neighbors is a different idea from the passage and has nothing to do with painting distant mountains.

choices.text: Cool colors remind people of hot campfires.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because campfires are hot and remind us of warm colors like red, orange, and yellow — the opposite of cool colors.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Light, Dark, and Mood

Artists also change colors by adding white or black paint. Mix white into red, and you get soft pink. That lighter version is called a tint — like adding milk to darken tea in reverse. Mix black into red, and you get a deep maroon. That darker version is called a shade, like a color standing in a shadow. Tints and shades give artists dozens of choices from a single color.

All of these choices — warm or cool, tint or shade — help artists create mood: the feeling a painting gives you, the way a song can feel happy or sad. Bright reds and oranges can make a painting feel joyful or intense, meaning strong and powerful. Soft blues and greens can make a painting feel dreamy or peaceful. You often feel the mood of a painting before you even notice what it shows.
stimulus.content_text:
Light, Dark, and Mood
Artists also change colors by adding white or black paint. Mix white into red, and you get soft pink. That lighter version is called a tint — like adding milk to darken tea in reverse. Mix black into red, and you get a deep maroon. That darker version is called a shade, like a color standing in a shadow. Tints and shades give artists dozens of choices from a single color.
All of these choices — warm or cool, tint or shade — help artists create mood: the feeling a painting gives you, the way a song can feel happy or sad. Bright reds and oranges can make a painting feel joyful or intense, meaning strong and powerful. Soft blues and greens can make a painting feel dreamy or peaceful. You often feel the mood of a painting before you even notice what it shows.

items.prompt: According to the text, which happens first when an artist changes red into a tint?

choices.text: getting a deep maroon
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because deep maroon comes from mixing black into red, which makes a shade — not a tint.

choices.text: mixing white into red
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage tells us the first step to making a tint is mixing white into red.

choices.text: calling the color a shade
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because 'shade' describes a color made darker with black, not the lighter tint made with white.

choices.text: feeling the painting's mood
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because feeling a painting's mood is about how colors make you feel, not about how tints are made.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Color Beyond the Canvas

The painter Claude Monet once filled a canvas with a haystack at sunset. He used warm oranges and pinks to make the hay glow. Then he painted the same haystack in winter, using cool blues and purples. Same haystack, completely different feeling. Color made the difference.

You make color choices every day, too — the shirt you wear, the marker you reach for, the background you pick for a drawing. Every time you choose a color, you are picking a mood.
stimulus.content_text:
Color Beyond the Canvas
The painter Claude Monet once filled a canvas with a haystack at sunset. He used warm oranges and pinks to make the hay glow. Then he painted the same haystack in winter, using cool blues and purples. Same haystack, completely different feeling. Color made the difference.
You make color choices every day, too — the shirt you wear, the marker you reach for, the background you pick for a drawing. Every time you choose a color, you are picking a mood.

items.prompt: In the article, Monet used warm oranges and pinks for a sunset haystack and cool blues and purples for a winter haystack. Based on this, how are the words 'warm' and 'cool' alike in their meaning?

choices.text: They both describe the temperature outside
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because 'warm' and 'cool' in this passage describe colors and the feelings they create, not the actual temperature of the weather outside.

choices.text: They both describe a feeling that colors create
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage shows that both 'warm' oranges and pinks and 'cool' blues and purples are used to create different feelings or moods in the paintings.

choices.text: They both describe the shape of a haystack
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because neither 'warm' nor 'cool' says anything about the shape of the haystack — they only describe the colors used.

choices.text: They both describe the time of year shown
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because even though the paintings show different seasons, the words 'warm' and 'cool' are describing the feeling the colors create, not the time of year.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: Which choice best shows the contrast the article makes between using warm colors and using cool colors in a painting?

choices.text: Warm colors are harder to mix, but cool colors are easier to mix.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the article never compares how easy or hard warm and cool colors are to mix.

choices.text: Warm colors pull your eyes forward, but cool colors make things seem farther away.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the article directly says warm colors jump toward you while cool colors make things look farther away, which is the main contrast described.

choices.text: Warm colors are always primary colors, but cool colors are always secondary colors.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because warm and cool groups include both primary and secondary colors — for example, blue is primary and cool, but green is secondary and also cool.

choices.text: Warm colors need white added, but cool colors need black added.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because adding white or black creates tints and shades, which is a separate idea from whether colors are warm or cool.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: Which section would best help you find out why an artist might mix black into red paint?

choices.text: Three Colors That Start It All
choices.feedback: Section A is incorrect because it talks about primary colors like red, yellow, and blue, not about what happens when you add black to a color.

choices.text: Warm Colors and Cool Colors
choices.feedback: Section B is incorrect because it explains warm and cool colors and the feelings they create, not about mixing black or white into paint.

choices.text: Light, Dark, and Mood
choices.feedback: Section C is correct because it directly explains that mixing black into red makes a shade called maroon, which is exactly what the question asks about.

choices.text: Color Beyond the Canvas
choices.feedback: Section D is incorrect because it tells a story about how Monet used colors in his paintings, but doesn't explain why an artist would mix black into red paint.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: Which sequence of steps best supports the main idea that artists build colors to change how a painting feels?

choices.text: First they start with primary colors, next they add black or white, and finally they create a mood.
choices.feedback: A is correct because it follows the passage's main idea — artists use primary colors, then add black or white to make tints and shades, and all those choices together create a mood in a painting.

choices.text: First they paint a summer haystack, next they use warm colors, and finally they paint a winter haystack.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because it only describes Monet's haystack example, which is just one small detail in the passage, not the main idea about how artists build colors to create feelings.

choices.text: First they mix all three primary colors, next they make muddy gray, and finally they build a color wheel.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because mixing all three primary colors to get muddy gray is just a surprising fun fact in the passage, not a sequence that shows how artists use color to change a painting's mood.

choices.text: First they feel a strong mood, next they look at a painting, and finally they notice the hidden colors.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the steps are backwards — the passage says you feel the mood before you notice what the painting shows, so you don't start by feeling a strong mood first.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: Based on the article, how is placing a color next to a new neighbor similar to adding white or black to a color?

choices.text: Both choices change the feeling or mood the color gives.
choices.feedback: A is correct because both placing a color next to a new neighbor and adding white or black change the feeling or mood the color gives you, as the article explains in the sections about warm and cool colors and tints and shades.

choices.text: Both choices turn a warm color into a cool color instead.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because changing a color's neighbor doesn't always turn it from warm to cool — it can go either way depending on what's next to it.

choices.text: Both choices push the painted color into the background.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because pushing colors into the background is about using cool colors in a painting, not specifically about changing neighbors or mixing in white and black.

choices.text: Both choices mix the starter ingredients to make muddy gray.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because muddy gray comes from mixing all three primary colors together at once, which is a completely different idea from these two choices.

syllabus_metadata.title: A World of Color: Primary, Secondary, Warm, and Cool

assessments.title: Touch with Your Eyes: Texture, Form, and Space in Art

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Texture, Form, and Space: Three Tricks That Fool Your Eyes

A Surface You Can Feel — Or Can You?

Run your fingers across a tree's bark. Now press your palm against a smooth glass window. Your skin notices the difference right away. That difference has a name: texture, the way a surface feels when you touch it — or the way it looks like it would feel. Texture is one of the elements of art. You have already learned others, like line, color, shape, and value. Today, let's look closely at three more: texture, form, and space.

A sculpture carved from rough stone has actual texture. Actual texture is real texture you can touch with your fingers.

Implied texture is texture that only looks real — the word "implied" means suggested, like a hint. For example, a painter might dab thick, bumpy paint to make a stone wall look gritty. Another might add thin, wispy lines to make hair look silky. You might think the paper would feel rough or soft. However, if you touched it, you would feel only flat, smooth paper. The artist used brush strokes or pencil marks to trick your eyes.
stimulus.content_text:
Texture, Form, and Space: Three Tricks That Fool Your EyesA Surface You Can Feel — Or Can You?
Run your fingers across a tree's bark. Now press your palm against a smooth glass window. Your skin notices the difference right away. That difference has a name: texture, the way a surface feels when you touch it — or the way it looks like it would feel. Texture is one of the elements of art. You have already learned others, like line, color, shape, and value. Today, let's look closely at three more: texture, form, and space.

A sculpture carved from rough stone has actual texture. Actual texture is real texture you can touch with your fingers.

Implied texture is texture that only looks real — the word "implied" means suggested, like a hint. For example, a painter might dab thick, bumpy paint to make a stone wall look gritty. Another might add thin, wispy lines to make hair look silky. You might think the paper would feel rough or soft. However, if you touched it, you would feel only flat, smooth paper. The artist used brush strokes or pencil marks to trick your eyes.

items.prompt: According to the text, even though implied texture on paper might look rough, how does it actually feel?

choices.text: It feels like flat, smooth paper.
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says that if you touched implied texture, 'you would feel only flat, smooth paper' — that's the real feeling, no matter what it looks like.

choices.text: It feels like thick, bumpy paint.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because thick, bumpy paint describes how implied texture might look, not how the flat paper actually feels when you touch it.

choices.text: It feels like rough, carved stone.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because rough, carved stone describes actual texture — something you really can touch and feel — not the surface of a flat piece of paper.

choices.text: It feels like thin, silky hair.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because silky hair is just an example of what implied texture might look like in a drawing, not what the paper would actually feel like under your fingers.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Form: When Flat Shapes Pop Out

Form is an element that makes art look three-dimensional — meaning it seems to have height, width, and depth, like a real object you could hold. A shape is flat. A form is not. A circle is a shape. A sphere — like a basketball — is a form. A square is a shape. A cube — like a cereal box — is a form. Everything around you in real life has form: your water bottle, an apple, your shoe.

Sculptors build actual forms you can walk around and view from every side. But artists who draw or paint work on flat paper or canvas. So how do they make a flat circle look like a solid ball? The secret is shading. Shading means using light and dark values — areas of brightness and shadow — the way a dimmer switch moves a lamp from bright to dark. The artist makes one side of the circle bright, where light hits it. The opposite side then gets darker, where shadow falls. This simple trick fools your brain into seeing a round, solid ball sitting on the page.
stimulus.content_text:
Form: When Flat Shapes Pop Out
Form is an element that makes art look three-dimensional — meaning it seems to have height, width, and depth, like a real object you could hold. A shape is flat. A form is not. A circle is a shape. A sphere — like a basketball — is a form. A square is a shape. A cube — like a cereal box — is a form. Everything around you in real life has form: your water bottle, an apple, your shoe.

Sculptors build actual forms you can walk around and view from every side. But artists who draw or paint work on flat paper or canvas. So how do they make a flat circle look like a solid ball? The secret is shading. Shading means using light and dark values — areas of brightness and shadow — the way a dimmer switch moves a lamp from bright to dark. The artist makes one side of the circle bright, where light hits it. The opposite side then gets darker, where shadow falls. This simple trick fools your brain into seeing a round, solid ball sitting on the page.

items.prompt: Based on the section, what is the main reason artists use shading when they draw on flat paper?

choices.text: to make the artwork more colorful and bright
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage never says shading makes art more colorful — shading is about using light and dark, not adding color.

choices.text: to make flat shapes look like solid forms
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly says shading uses light and dark values to make a flat circle look like a solid, round ball.

choices.text: to show that shapes are different from forms
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because shading isn't used to show the difference between shapes and forms — it's used to turn a flat shape INTO a form.

choices.text: to copy the way sculptors build their art
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage actually says painters and drawers use shading instead of building real forms the way sculptors do, not to copy them.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Space: Near, Far, and In Between

Space is how artists show distance and depth on a flat surface. Picture a road stretching away from you toward the mountains. The road looks wide near your feet and narrow far away. Artists use that same idea on paper.

They draw nearby objects larger and place them lower. Actually, it is a regular-sized house drawn small to show that it is far from you. Your brain reads the size difference as distance.

Another trick is overlapping, which means one object partly covers another — like when a friend stands in front of you in line and blocks part of your body. Similarly, in a painting, a tree that covers part of a barn tells your eyes the tree is closer. The barn must be farther back. These tricks work together to create a feeling of deep space on a flat piece of paper.
stimulus.content_text:
Space: Near, Far, and In Between
Space is how artists show distance and depth on a flat surface. Picture a road stretching away from you toward the mountains. The road looks wide near your feet and narrow far away. Artists use that same idea on paper.

They draw nearby objects larger and place them lower. Actually, it is a regular-sized house drawn small to show that it is far from you. Your brain reads the size difference as distance.

Another trick is overlapping, which means one object partly covers another — like when a friend stands in front of you in line and blocks part of your body. Similarly, in a painting, a tree that covers part of a barn tells your eyes the tree is closer. The barn must be farther back. These tricks work together to create a feeling of deep space on a flat piece of paper.

items.prompt: According to the text, how does a road look as it goes from near your feet to far away?

choices.text: It changes from wide to narrow.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage directly says the road 'looks wide near your feet and narrow far away.'

choices.text: It changes from narrow to wide.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because it is the opposite — the road starts wide (near you) and gets narrow (far away), not the other way around.

choices.text: It changes from small to large.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage says faraway objects look smaller, not larger, so size goes from large to small, not small to large.

choices.text: It changes from dark to light.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage never mentions anything about a road changing from dark to light.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Why Artists Need All Three

Space pushes some things close and pulls others far away. In addition, texture makes surfaces look touchable, and form makes flat shapes appear solid. Together, they are powerful tools in every artist's set.

You are building a world — one that someone else's eyes might just believe is real.
stimulus.content_text:
Why Artists Need All Three
Space pushes some things close and pulls others far away. In addition, texture makes surfaces look touchable, and form makes flat shapes appear solid. Together, they are powerful tools in every artist's set.

You are building a world — one that someone else's eyes might just believe is real.

items.prompt: How are texture and form alike in this section?

choices.text: Both make objects look farther away.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage says space is what pushes things far away, not texture or form.

choices.text: Both help flat art look more real.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage says texture makes surfaces look touchable and form makes flat shapes look solid, which both help flat art look more real.

choices.text: Both change where things are placed.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because placing things closer or farther is the job of space, not texture or form.

choices.text: Both tell artists what colors to use.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage never talks about colors at all.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: According to the article, what is the main difference between a sculpture and a painting?

choices.text: A sculpture has real texture and form, while a painting uses tricks on flat paper.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the article says sculptures have real texture and form you can touch and walk around, while painters use tricks like shading and brush strokes on flat paper to make things only look real.

choices.text: A sculpture has light and dark shadows, while a painting uses overlapping lines.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the article says shading with light and dark values is a trick painters use, not something that describes sculptures.

choices.text: A sculpture shows objects that are far, while a painting shows objects that are near.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the article never says sculptures show far-away objects and paintings show nearby objects — that idea is not in the passage at all.

choices.text: A sculpture is made of flat, smooth shapes, while a painting is made of solid forms.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because it has it backwards — the article says sculptures are solid and three-dimensional, while paintings are the ones made on flat, smooth surfaces.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: According to the article, what is the effect of an artist using tricks like shading, overlapping, and drawing wispy lines?

choices.text: It makes flat paper look like a real, solid world.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the article says shading, overlapping, and texture tricks all work together to make flat paper look like a real, solid world you could almost touch.

choices.text: It turns flat paper into a rough, heavy sculpture.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because these tricks only make flat paper look three-dimensional — they cannot actually turn paper into a heavy sculpture you can pick up.

choices.text: It helps flat paper stay clean and perfectly smooth.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because none of the tricks described in the article have anything to do with keeping paper clean or smooth.

choices.text: It changes flat paper into a bright, colorful shape.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because shading, overlapping, and wispy lines are about creating depth and texture, not about making paper brighter or more colorful.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: Which order of section headings would help you quickly find information about these ideas: how a surface feels, how flat shapes look solid, and how art shows near and far?

choices.text: Texture, then form, then space
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage sections go in exactly this order: texture first, then form, then space — matching all three ideas in the right sequence.

choices.text: Texture, then space, then form
choices.feedback: B is wrong because it puts space before form, but in the passage, the form section comes before the space section.

choices.text: Form, then texture, then space
choices.feedback: C is wrong because it starts with form, but the passage talks about texture first, before form.

choices.text: Space, then form, then texture
choices.feedback: D is wrong because it starts with space and puts form in the middle, which is the opposite of the order used in the passage.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: Which sentence best states the main idea of the article?

choices.text: Texture, form, and space are different kinds of lines that artists draw on paper.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because texture, form, and space are not types of lines — they are separate art elements that do very different things.

choices.text: Texture, form, and space are different art tools that make flat art seem real.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the article explains how all three elements — texture, form, and space — are tools that trick your eyes into thinking flat art looks and feels real.

choices.text: Texture, form, and space are different steps that artists use in the same order.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the article never says artists must use texture, form, and space in a specific order or as steps.

choices.text: Texture, form, and space are different ways that artists make objects look much smaller.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because making objects look smaller is only one small part of how space works, and it does not describe texture or form at all.

syllabus_metadata.title: Touch with Your Eyes: Texture, Form, and Space in Art

assessments.title: Giotto: The Painter Who Made Art Come Alive

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Giotto: The Painter Who Made People Look Alive

A Boy Drawing Sheep on a Rock

A shepherd boy crouched beside a flat rock in the Italian countryside, scratching a picture of a sheep into the stone with a sharp point. According to an old legend, a famous painter named Cimabue walked by and stopped. The drawing was so good that Cimabue took the boy home to train him. That boy was Giotto di Bondone, born around 1267 near the city of Florence, Italy. Whether or not the legend is true, Giotto did study with Cimabue. He became Cimabue's apprentice — a young student who learns a skill by working alongside a master. Before long, Giotto's paintings surpassed his teacher's. That means he grew even better than the person who trained him.
stimulus.content_text:
Giotto: The Painter Who Made People Look AliveA Boy Drawing Sheep on a Rock
A shepherd boy crouched beside a flat rock in the Italian countryside, scratching a picture of a sheep into the stone with a sharp point. According to an old legend, a famous painter named Cimabue walked by and stopped. The drawing was so good that Cimabue took the boy home to train him. That boy was Giotto di Bondone, born around 1267 near the city of Florence, Italy. Whether or not the legend is true, Giotto did study with Cimabue. He became Cimabue's apprentice — a young student who learns a skill by working alongside a master. Before long, Giotto's paintings surpassed his teacher's. That means he grew even better than the person who trained him.

items.prompt: According to the text, how were Giotto's paintings different from Cimabue's paintings?

choices.text: Giotto's paintings were made earlier than Cimabue's paintings
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage never compares when their paintings were made — it only compares how good they were.

choices.text: Giotto's paintings became better than Cimabue's paintings
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage says Giotto's paintings 'surpassed' his teacher's, which the text explains means he became even better than Cimabue.

choices.text: Giotto's paintings looked the same as Cimabue's paintings
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because surpassing someone means doing better than them, not the same as them.

choices.text: Giotto's paintings were about sheep unlike Cimabue's paintings
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage never tells us what Cimabue painted, and it doesn't say Giotto only painted sheep.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Why Paintings Used to Look Flat

Medieval artists chose to paint figures in a flat, stiff style on purpose. Their goal was to show how important religious figures were, not to make them look like real people. They used bright gold backgrounds and serious, calm faces. The paintings were meant to feel holy, not lifelike.

Giotto's figures could make you cry. Their robes folded and draped the way real cloth does. Most importantly, their faces showed real feelings — grief, joy, surprise, and tenderness. He wanted you to look at a painting and feel like you were watching a real moment happen.
stimulus.content_text:
Why Paintings Used to Look Flat
Medieval artists chose to paint figures in a flat, stiff style on purpose. Their goal was to show how important religious figures were, not to make them look like real people. They used bright gold backgrounds and serious, calm faces. The paintings were meant to feel holy, not lifelike.
Giotto's figures could make you cry. Their robes folded and draped the way real cloth does. Most importantly, their faces showed real feelings — grief, joy, surprise, and tenderness. He wanted you to look at a painting and feel like you were watching a real moment happen.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, why did Giotto show real feelings on people's faces?

choices.text: To make the paintings feel holy
choices.feedback: Making paintings feel holy was the goal of the medieval artists who came before Giotto — Giotto actually wanted his paintings to feel real, not just holy.

choices.text: To make the scene seem real
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says Giotto wanted viewers to feel like they were watching a real moment, and showing real feelings on faces helped make that happen.

choices.text: To make the gold stand out
choices.feedback: The passage never says anything about gold standing out — in fact, Giotto moved away from the gold backgrounds that older artists used.

choices.text: To make the people look stiff
choices.feedback: Making people look stiff was the opposite of what Giotto wanted — the passage says medieval artists painted stiff figures, but Giotto wanted his figures to look and feel real.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Painting Straight into the Wall

Giotto's most famous work fills the walls of the Scrovegni Chapel in Padua, Italy. He painted these scenes using a technique called fresco. A fresco is a painting made directly on wet plaster. The artist mixes pigment with water and paints it onto the plaster while it is still damp. When the plaster dries, the color becomes locked into the wall itself.

Giotto covered the chapel with scenes from the life of Christ and the Virgin Mary. In one scene, people mourn over Christ's body. Angels above them cry out in pain. For people used to the still, calm paintings of that time, seeing these images must have felt like a shock.
stimulus.content_text:
Painting Straight into the Wall
Giotto's most famous work fills the walls of the Scrovegni Chapel in Padua, Italy. He painted these scenes using a technique called fresco. A fresco is a painting made directly on wet plaster. The artist mixes pigment with water and paints it onto the plaster while it is still damp. When the plaster dries, the color becomes locked into the wall itself.
Giotto covered the chapel with scenes from the life of Christ and the Virgin Mary. In one scene, people mourn over Christ's body. Angels above them cry out in pain. For people used to the still, calm paintings of that time, seeing these images must have felt like a shock.

items.prompt: According to the text, what happens after the artist paints on the wet plaster?

choices.text: The artist adds more water to the wall.
choices.feedback: The passage never mentions adding more water to the wall after painting — that step doesn't exist in the fresco process described.

choices.text: The plaster dries and locks the color in.
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage clearly states that when the plaster dries, the color becomes locked into the wall itself.

choices.text: The artist mixes the plaster with pigment.
choices.feedback: Mixing pigment with water happens before the artist paints, so this is a step that comes earlier, not after painting.

choices.text: The color washes off the wall over time.
choices.feedback: This is the opposite of what the passage says — the color gets permanently locked in, not washed off.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Making Space Feel Real

Giotto also changed how artists used space. He placed his figures inside rooms and outdoor settings that looked three-dimensional — meaning they seemed to have depth, height, and width, like a real place you could step into. He used light and shadow to make objects look solid and grouped people together naturally, as if you were peeking through a window at a real event.
stimulus.content_text:
Making Space Feel Real
Giotto also changed how artists used space. He placed his figures inside rooms and outdoor settings that looked three-dimensional — meaning they seemed to have depth, height, and width, like a real place you could step into. He used light and shadow to make objects look solid and grouped people together naturally, as if you were peeking through a window at a real event.

items.prompt: How were Giotto's paintings like looking through a window?

choices.text: They showed flat shapes on a plain wall.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because Giotto's paintings were the opposite of flat — he used depth, light, and shadow to make them look three-dimensional and real.

choices.text: They showed real-looking scenes with depth.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage says Giotto made scenes look like 'a real place you could step into,' just like looking through a window at something really happening.

choices.text: They showed tiny objects without any shadows.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage says Giotto used light and shadow to make objects look solid, so shadows were actually an important part of his paintings.

choices.text: They showed empty rooms without any people.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage says Giotto grouped people together naturally inside his scenes, so his paintings were full of people, not empty.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
An Early Spark for Something Huge

People often call Giotto the "Father of the Renaissance." The Renaissance was a great burst of new art, science, and learning that spread across Europe. It began in Italy and is usually said to have started in the 1300s or 1400s. But Giotto didn't cause the Renaissance all by himself. Many other artists, thinkers, and big changes in society helped it happen, too. Giotto was one early spark in a much larger fire. Later artists like Leonardo da Vinci and Michelangelo studied what Giotto had done and built on his ideas to create even more lifelike and powerful art.

Giotto died in 1337, but his work still covers those chapel walls in Padua. He proved that a painting is not just shapes and colors. It can be a window, and through it, you can feel what someone else feels. Next time you draw a face and try to show what that person is feeling, you are doing exactly what Giotto did seven hundred years ago.
stimulus.content_text:
An Early Spark for Something Huge
