It began in Italy and is usually said to have started in the 1300s or 1400s. But Giotto didn't cause the Renaissance all by himself. Many other artists, thinkers, and big changes in society helped it happen, too. Giotto was one early spark in a much larger fire. Later artists like Leonardo da Vinci and Michelangelo studied what Giotto had done and built on his ideas to create even more lifelike and powerful art.
Giotto died in 1337, but his work still covers those chapel walls in Padua. He proved that a painting is not just shapes and colors. It can be a window, and through it, you can feel what someone else feels. Next time you draw a face and try to show what that person is feeling, you are doing exactly what Giotto did seven hundred years ago.

items.prompt: According to the text, what does the word "Renaissance" mean?

choices.text: A great burst of new art, science, and learning.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly says 'The Renaissance was a great burst of new art, science, and learning' — those are the author's own words explaining the word!

choices.text: A painting made of only simple shapes and colors.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage uses 'shapes and colors' to talk about what a painting is NOT just made of, and it has nothing to do with the word Renaissance.

choices.text: A famous chapel wall located in the city of Padua.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the chapel walls in Padua are where Giotto's paintings are found, not a definition of the word Renaissance.

choices.text: A special window that shows what a person feels.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage uses the idea of a window showing feelings to describe what a great painting can do, not to explain what Renaissance means.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: How were Giotto's paintings different from the paintings made by medieval artists before him?

choices.text: Giotto used bright gold backgrounds to show importance
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage says medieval artists used bright gold backgrounds, not Giotto — that was the old style he moved away from.

choices.text: Giotto's figures showed real feelings and emotions
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly says Giotto's figures showed real feelings like grief, joy, and surprise, which was brand new compared to the flat, calm paintings made before him.

choices.text: Giotto painted only calm and serious-looking faces
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because calm and serious faces describe the medieval artists who came before Giotto, not Giotto's own paintings.

choices.text: Giotto copied the flat style his teacher Cimabue used
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because Giotto did not copy the flat style — he changed it completely and even surpassed his teacher Cimabue.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: Which two headings show where to find information about what caused Giotto's paintings to look like real life?

choices.text: "A Boy Drawing Sheep on a Rock" and "Painting Straight into the Wall"
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because 'A Boy Drawing Sheep on a Rock' is about Giotto's childhood and how he became an apprentice, not about what made his paintings look real.

choices.text: "Why Paintings Used to Look Flat" and "Making Space Feel Real"
choices.feedback: B is correct because 'Why Paintings Used to Look Flat' explains how Giotto added real feelings and details, and 'Making Space Feel Real' explains how he used depth and shadow to make scenes look three-dimensional.

choices.text: "Painting Straight into the Wall" and "An Early Spark for Something Huge"
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because 'An Early Spark for Something Huge' is about Giotto's place in history and the Renaissance, not about the techniques he used to make paintings look lifelike.

choices.text: "A Boy Drawing Sheep on a Rock" and "An Early Spark for Something Huge"
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because both 'A Boy Drawing Sheep on a Rock' and 'An Early Spark for Something Huge' are about Giotto's life story and his influence, not about what made his paintings look real.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: Which list shows the correct order of events from Giotto's life?

choices.text: He drew on a rock, learned from a master, painted lifelike walls, and inspired future artists.
choices.feedback: A is correct because it follows the exact order from the passage: rock drawing, learning from Cimabue, painting the chapel walls, and inspiring future artists.

choices.text: He learned from a master, drew on a rock, inspired many future artists, and painted lifelike walls.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because it flips the first two steps — Giotto drew on the rock first, and that drawing is what led Cimabue to find him and teach him.

choices.text: He drew on a rock, painted flat figures, painted lifelike walls, and started the Renaissance.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because Giotto never painted flat figures — flat painting was the old style he changed, and he didn't start the Renaissance all by himself.

choices.text: He painted lifelike walls, drew on a rock, learned from a master, and inspired future artists.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because painting the chapel walls happened when Giotto was already a grown-up artist, not at the start of his life.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: Which statement best compares Giotto's paintings with the paintings that came before them?

choices.text: They showed holy people with gold backgrounds and calm faces.
choices.feedback: This describes the old medieval paintings that came BEFORE Giotto — with gold backgrounds and calm faces — not what made Giotto's paintings special.

choices.text: They showed real feelings and spaces that looked more real.
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says Giotto's figures showed real feelings and that he made spaces look three-dimensional, which was very different from the flat, stiff paintings before him.

choices.text: They were painted by an apprentice who copied his teacher.
choices.feedback: This tells us how Giotto learned to paint by working with a teacher, but it doesn't compare his paintings to the ones that came before them.

choices.text: They were made on wet plaster inside a chapel wall.
choices.feedback: Painting on wet plaster (fresco) describes the technique Giotto used in the chapel, but it doesn't explain what was different about his paintings compared to older art.

syllabus_metadata.title: Giotto: The Painter Who Made Art Come Alive

assessments.title: The Great Wave: Hokusai and Japanese Prints

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Hokusai and The Great Wave

A Wave You Already Know

A huge, curling wave rises like a giant claw. White foam flies from its tips like fingers reaching into the sky. Tiny fishing boats slide through the rough water below. And far in the background, small and calm beneath all that fury, sits the snow-capped peak of Mount Fuji. You have probably seen this image before — on a poster, a T-shirt, or a phone case — even if you never knew who made it.

A Japanese artist named Katsushika Hokusai created it around 1831.
stimulus.content_text:
Hokusai and The Great WaveA Wave You Already Know
A huge, curling wave rises like a giant claw. White foam flies from its tips like fingers reaching into the sky. Tiny fishing boats slide through the rough water below. And far in the background, small and calm beneath all that fury, sits the snow-capped peak of Mount Fuji. You have probably seen this image before — on a poster, a T-shirt, or a phone case — even if you never knew who made it.
A Japanese artist named Katsushika Hokusai created it around 1831.

items.prompt: The text describes the wave as huge and full of fury. According to the text, how is Mount Fuji described in contrast?

choices.text: It is small and calm.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage uses the exact words 'small and calm' to describe Mount Fuji, showing it is the opposite of the huge, furious wave.

choices.text: It is huge and curling.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because 'huge and curling' describes the wave, not Mount Fuji.

choices.text: It is tiny and sliding.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because 'tiny and sliding' describes the fishing boats moving through the water.

choices.text: It is white and flying.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because 'white and flying' describes the foam coming off the tips of the wave.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
A Life Spent Drawing

Hokusai was born in 1760 in Edo, the city we now call Tokyo. He loved drawing from a young age. As a teenager, he became an apprentice — a young worker who learns a skill from an expert, the way a new cook might learn recipes by working beside a chef. His training took place in a woodblock print studio, and he spent the rest of his long life creating art. Hokusai lived to be 88 years old. During those years, he produced over 30,000 paintings, sketches, and prints.
stimulus.content_text:
A Life Spent Drawing
Hokusai was born in 1760 in Edo, the city we now call Tokyo. He loved drawing from a young age. As a teenager, he became an apprentice — a young worker who learns a skill from an expert, the way a new cook might learn recipes by working beside a chef. His training took place in a woodblock print studio, and he spent the rest of his long life creating art. Hokusai lived to be 88 years old. During those years, he produced over 30,000 paintings, sketches, and prints.

items.prompt: Based on the text, why was Hokusai able to create over 30,000 pieces of art?

choices.text: He lived a very long life.
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says Hokusai lived to be 88 years old and spent his whole long life making art, which gave him enough time to create over 30,000 pieces.

choices.text: He moved to a large city.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because even though Hokusai was born in a city called Edo, the passage never says that living in a big city helped him make more art.

choices.text: He trained to be a chef.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because Hokusai trained in a woodblock print studio, not as a chef — the chef was just used as an example to explain what an apprentice is.

choices.text: He owned a print art studio.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says he trained in a woodblock print studio, but it never says he owned it.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
How Woodblock Printing Works

Woodblock printing in Japan was a team effort. An artist drew the design, and a carver cut it into a wooden block, leaving the lines raised. A printer then spread ink on the raised parts of the block and pressed paper onto it to create the print.

Making a print with many colors took even more work. The team carved a separate block for each color. They printed one color, let it dry, then lined up the next block exactly right and printed the next color on top. This careful lining up is called alignment — like stacking clear sheets of sticker paper so every layer lands in the perfect spot. One tiny slip, and the whole image would look blurry.
stimulus.content_text:
How Woodblock Printing Works
Woodblock printing in Japan was a team effort. An artist drew the design, and a carver cut it into a wooden block, leaving the lines raised. A printer then spread ink on the raised parts of the block and pressed paper onto it to create the print.
Making a print with many colors took even more work. The team carved a separate block for each color. They printed one color, let it dry, then lined up the next block exactly right and printed the next color on top. This careful lining up is called alignment — like stacking clear sheets of sticker paper so every layer lands in the perfect spot. One tiny slip, and the whole image would look blurry.

items.prompt: According to the text, what did the team do right AFTER they printed one color on the paper?

choices.text: They spread ink on the block.
choices.feedback: Spreading ink on the block happens before you press it onto the paper, not after printing a color.

choices.text: They let the color dry.
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage clearly says they 'printed one color, let it dry' — so drying came right after printing.

choices.text: They drew a new design.
choices.feedback: The passage never says the artist drew a new design between printing colors — that's not part of this process.

choices.text: They carved a new block.
choices.feedback: The blocks were already carved before printing started, so carving a new block is not what happened right after printing a color.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Thirty-Six Views of One Mountain

The Great Wave belongs to a series Hokusai called Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji. In this series, he showed Japan's most famous mountain from many angles, in different weather and seasons. Sometimes Mount Fuji stands tall and dramatic. Other times, like in The Great Wave, it appears tiny and distant behind something else that grabs your eye first. Because the prints were made using woodblocks, many copies could be produced and sold. They were popular, affordable art — sold to ordinary people the way posters are sold today. Almost anyone in Edo could buy one and hang it on a wall.
stimulus.content_text:
Thirty-Six Views of One Mountain
The Great Wave belongs to a series Hokusai called Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji. In this series, he showed Japan's most famous mountain from many angles, in different weather and seasons. Sometimes Mount Fuji stands tall and dramatic. Other times, like in The Great Wave, it appears tiny and distant behind something else that grabs your eye first. Because the prints were made using woodblocks, many copies could be produced and sold. They were popular, affordable art — sold to ordinary people the way posters are sold today. Almost anyone in Edo could buy one and hang it on a wall.

items.prompt: When the author compares woodblock prints to posters sold today, what does this comparison help the reader understand about the phrase 'popular, affordable art'?

choices.text: that the prints were made by famous artists for museums
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage says the prints were sold to ordinary people, not made for museums.

choices.text: that the prints were common and cheap enough for regular people
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly says the prints were affordable and sold to ordinary people, just like posters are today.

choices.text: that the prints were sold only in special stores for collectors
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage says almost anyone could buy one, not just special collectors in special stores.

choices.text: that the prints were rare and hard for most people to find
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage says the prints were popular and affordable, which means they were easy to find, not rare or hard to get.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
A Splash Felt Across the Ocean

In the 1800s, Japanese woodblock prints began arriving in Europe. Western artists were amazed. The flat areas of bright color, the bold outlines, and the unusual viewpoints looked completely different from European paintings. Many famous European painters studied Hokusai's work closely. Claude Monet, an Impressionist — an artist who tried to capture quick moments of light and color — admired his prints. So did Vincent van Gogh, a Post-Impressionist who came a little later and built on the Impressionists' ideas. Van Gogh even collected Japanese prints and wrote letters about how much they inspired him.
stimulus.content_text:
A Splash Felt Across the Ocean
In the 1800s, Japanese woodblock prints began arriving in Europe. Western artists were amazed. The flat areas of bright color, the bold outlines, and the unusual viewpoints looked completely different from European paintings. Many famous European painters studied Hokusai's work closely. Claude Monet, an Impressionist — an artist who tried to capture quick moments of light and color — admired his prints. So did Vincent van Gogh, a Post-Impressionist who came a little later and built on the Impressionists' ideas. Van Gogh even collected Japanese prints and wrote letters about how much they inspired him.

items.prompt: According to the text, what is an Impressionist?

choices.text: An artist who paints with flat areas of bright color
choices.feedback: Flat areas of bright color describe Japanese woodblock prints, not Impressionist artists.

choices.text: An artist who tries to capture moments of light and color
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage defines an Impressionist as 'an artist who tried to capture quick moments of light and color.'

choices.text: An artist who builds on the ideas of earlier painters
choices.feedback: The passage says Van Gogh, a Post-Impressionist, built on the Impressionists' ideas — so this describes Post-Impressionists, not Impressionists.

choices.text: An artist who creates pictures by carving into woodblocks
choices.feedback: Carving into woodblocks describes how Japanese prints were made, not what an Impressionist artist does.

items.title: Section 6 - Guiding Question 5

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
Never Finished Learning

Even in old age, Hokusai kept pushing himself. He once said that nothing he created before age 70 was worth noticing. He believed his art would only get better the longer he lived. Think about that the next time you feel frustrated with a drawing. Hokusai had been making art for over fifty years and still thought his best work was ahead of him. The greatest artist of the wave never stopped riding it.
stimulus.content_text:
Never Finished Learning
Even in old age, Hokusai kept pushing himself. He once said that nothing he created before age 70 was worth noticing. He believed his art would only get better the longer he lived. Think about that the next time you feel frustrated with a drawing. Hokusai had been making art for over fifty years and still thought his best work was ahead of him. The greatest artist of the wave never stopped riding it.

items.prompt: Which detail from the text best shows that Hokusai wanted to keep improving his art?

choices.text: He felt frustrated when he worked on a drawing.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage says YOU might feel frustrated with a drawing — it never says Hokusai felt frustrated.

choices.text: He believed his art would get better as he lived.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly says Hokusai believed his art would only get better the longer he lived, which shows he always wanted to improve.

choices.text: He had been making art for over fifty long years.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because making art for fifty years just tells us how long he worked, not that he wanted to keep getting better.

choices.text: He created many artworks before he turned seventy.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because creating art before age 70 is just a fact about when he worked, not a sign that he wanted to improve.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: Based on the article, how was Hokusai's opinion of his early art different from the facts about his life?

choices.text: He thought his early art was not worth noticing, even though he spent many years making thousands of pictures.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the article shows Hokusai said his work before age 70 wasn't worth noticing, even though he had been making thousands of pieces of art since he was a teenager.

choices.text: He thought his early art was not worth noticing, even though he only started drawing when he was an old man.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the article clearly says Hokusai loved drawing from a young age and became an apprentice as a teenager — he did not start drawing when he was old.

choices.text: He thought his early art was his very best work, even though he spent many years making thousands of pictures.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because Hokusai did not think his early art was his best — he said nothing before age 70 was worth noticing, meaning he thought his best work came later.

choices.text: He thought his early art was his very best work, even though he only started drawing when he was an old man.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect for two reasons: Hokusai did not think his early art was his best, and he did not start drawing when he was old — he started as a young person.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: If you want to learn what caused Hokusai to become such a great artist, which two headings should you read?

choices.text: "A Wave You Already Know" and "Thirty-Six Views of One Mountain"
choices.feedback: These sections describe what the wave looks like and how Hokusai showed Mount Fuji in different ways, but they don't explain what made him such a great artist.

choices.text: "A Life Spent Drawing" and "Never Finished Learning"
choices.feedback: This is correct because 'A Life Spent Drawing' shows he worked hard from a young age, and 'Never Finished Learning' shows he kept pushing himself to improve his whole life — both explain why he became so great.

choices.text: "How Woodblock Printing Works" and "A Splash Felt Across the Ocean"
choices.feedback: These sections explain how woodblock printing is made and how his art influenced artists in Europe, but they don't focus on what caused Hokusai to become great.

choices.text: "Thirty-Six Views of One Mountain" and "A Splash Felt Across the Ocean"
choices.feedback: These sections are about his series of Mount Fuji prints and how his work traveled to Europe, not about the reasons behind his greatness as an artist.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: Which sequence of events best supports the main idea that Hokusai was dedicated to his art throughout his entire life?

choices.text: He trained as an apprentice, created thousands of artworks, and kept practicing into old age.
choices.feedback: A is correct because it follows Hokusai from his early training all the way to old age, showing he never stopped working hard at his art.

choices.text: He drew a paper design, carved a wooden block, and printed colors one by one.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because it describes the steps of making a woodblock print, not the events of Hokusai's life.

choices.text: He collected Japanese prints, wrote letters about them, and painted the quick moments of light.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because collecting prints and writing letters about them is what Van Gogh did, not Hokusai.

choices.text: He became a famous artist, trained as a young worker, and then drew Mount Fuji.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the events are in the wrong order — Hokusai trained as an apprentice first, long before he became famous.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: Based on the article, how were Hokusai as a teenager and the European painters in the 1800s similar?

choices.text: Both studied the work of other artists to learn new things.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage shows Hokusai learned from an expert as an apprentice, and the European painters studied Hokusai's prints to learn new ideas — both learned from other artists.

choices.text: Both crossed the wide ocean to share their beautiful paintings.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never says Hokusai or the European painters crossed the ocean; it was the woodblock prints that traveled to Europe, not the artists themselves.

choices.text: Both carved thick wooden blocks to make many colorful prints.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because carving wooden blocks was the job of carvers in Japan, not something Hokusai as a teenager or the European painters did.

choices.text: Both ignored their early drawings to focus on better ideas.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage actually says the opposite — Hokusai kept working hard his whole life, and the European painters admired and collected the Japanese prints rather than ignoring their early work.

syllabus_metadata.title: The Great Wave: Hokusai and Japanese Prints

assessments.title: Quilts That Tell Stories: Faith Ringgold's Art

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Faith Ringgold's Flying Quilts

A Girl on a Rooftop

Imagine this: a little girl lies on a blanket on a rooftop in New York City. She lifts off the blanket and flies — over buildings, over bridges, over the whole glittering night. Can you picture it? Here's the coolest part — this scene is not in a painting on a wall. It is sewn into a quilt! The artist who made it is Faith Ringgold, and she turned quilts into something the art world had never seen before.
stimulus.content_text:
Faith Ringgold's Flying QuiltsA Girl on a Rooftop
Imagine this: a little girl lies on a blanket on a rooftop in New York City. She lifts off the blanket and flies — over buildings, over bridges, over the whole glittering night. Can you picture it? Here's the coolest part — this scene is not in a painting on a wall. It is sewn into a quilt! The artist who made it is Faith Ringgold, and she turned quilts into something the art world had never seen before.

items.prompt: According to the text, how is Faith Ringgold's scene of the flying girl different from a painting on a wall?

choices.text: It is drawn with crayons on paper
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage never mentions crayons or paper — those aren't used to make quilts!

choices.text: It is sewn into a quilt
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage says directly that the scene 'is sewn into a quilt,' not painted on a wall.

choices.text: It is printed inside a book
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage never says the scene is printed inside a book.

choices.text: It is carved into a block of wood
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage never mentions carving or wood — quilts are made from fabric and thread.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Growing Up in Harlem

Faith Ringgold was born in 1930 in Harlem, a neighborhood in New York City. Harlem was alive with African American music, culture, and art. Faith's mother was a fashion designer and dressmaker. She taught Faith to cut, stitch, and work with fabric from an early age. Can you imagine learning to sew when you were just about your age? Creativity filled their home like sunlight fills a window. Faith's family loved stories, and that love stayed with her for life.
stimulus.content_text:
Growing Up in Harlem
Faith Ringgold was born in 1930 in Harlem, a neighborhood in New York City. Harlem was alive with African American music, culture, and art. Faith's mother was a fashion designer and dressmaker. She taught Faith to cut, stitch, and work with fabric from an early age. Can you imagine learning to sew when you were just about your age? Creativity filled their home like sunlight fills a window. Faith's family loved stories, and that love stayed with her for life.

items.prompt: Read these two sentences from the passage: 'Faith's mother was a fashion designer and dressmaker. She taught Faith to cut, stitch, and work with fabric from an early age.' How are these two sentences connected?

choices.text: The first sentence explains the cause of the events in the second sentence.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the mother's job as a dressmaker is the cause — it explains WHY she taught Faith to cut, stitch, and work with fabric.

choices.text: The first sentence explains the problem with the events in the second sentence.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because neither sentence describes a problem; both sentences share positive, connected facts about Faith's mother and her teaching.

choices.text: The first sentence explains the opposite of the events in the second sentence.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the second sentence doesn't say something opposite — it shows what happened *because of* the first sentence, not something different from it.

choices.text: The first sentence explains the final step of the events in the second sentence.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the first sentence is not a final step in a process; it is the reason or cause that leads to what happens in the second sentence.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Inventing the Story Quilt

Ringgold studied art in college and started out as a painter. But in the 1980s, she began making something brand new — and you are going to love this: story quilts! A story quilt is a large artwork with a painted canvas — a strong cloth that artists use for painting — right in the center. Quilted fabric borders frame the painting like a colorful picture frame. Then, around the edges, Ringgold writes words directly onto the cloth. She and her mother sewed the quilted borders together. The painting, the stitching, and the written story all work together to tell a tale. Pretty amazing, right?
stimulus.content_text:
Inventing the Story Quilt
Ringgold studied art in college and started out as a painter. But in the 1980s, she began making something brand new — and you are going to love this: story quilts! A story quilt is a large artwork with a painted canvas — a strong cloth that artists use for painting — right in the center. Quilted fabric borders frame the painting like a colorful picture frame. Then, around the edges, Ringgold writes words directly onto the cloth. She and her mother sewed the quilted borders together. The painting, the stitching, and the written story all work together to tell a tale. Pretty amazing, right?

items.prompt: According to the text, what is the correct order of steps Ringgold follows when making a story quilt?

choices.text: She writes words on cloth, then paints the canvas, then sews the borders.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because it says writing words comes first, but the passage tells us the painted canvas is the starting point in the center of the quilt.

choices.text: She paints a canvas, adds quilted borders, then writes words on the edges.
choices.feedback: B is correct because it matches exactly what the passage describes: painting the canvas first, then adding the quilted borders to frame it, then writing words around the edges.

choices.text: She sews the quilted borders, then writes words, then paints the canvas.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because it says the borders are sewn first, but the passage shows the painting in the center comes before the borders are added around it.

choices.text: She writes words on cloth, then sews the borders, then adds the painting.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because it starts with writing words on cloth, but according to the passage, the painted canvas is created first before anything else is added.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Tar Beach: Her Most Famous Work

Have you ever wished you could fly? Ringgold's best-known story quilt is called Tar Beach, and it is all about that dream. It shows a girl named Cassie on the rooftop of her apartment building on a warm summer night. In the story, Cassie imagines she can fly over the city. She soars above the George Washington Bridge. Her father helped build that bridge, but he was not allowed to join the workers' union — a group that workers form to protect their jobs and rights, like a team that speaks up for its members. Because of his race, the union kept him out. In her imagination, Cassie claims the bridge for her father. The quilt is about dreaming big, the freedom of imagination, and the real struggles African American families faced.
stimulus.content_text:
Tar Beach: Her Most Famous Work
Have you ever wished you could fly? Ringgold's best-known story quilt is called Tar Beach, and it is all about that dream. It shows a girl named Cassie on the rooftop of her apartment building on a warm summer night. In the story, Cassie imagines she can fly over the city. She soars above the George Washington Bridge. Her father helped build that bridge, but he was not allowed to join the workers' union — a group that workers form to protect their jobs and rights, like a team that speaks up for its members. Because of his race, the union kept him out. In her imagination, Cassie claims the bridge for her father. The quilt is about dreaming big, the freedom of imagination, and the real struggles African American families faced.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, how is a union similar to a team?

choices.text: Both are groups that build bridges together
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage never says that teams or unions build bridges — only Cassie's father helped build the bridge.

choices.text: Both are groups that speak up for members
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly says a union is 'like a team that speaks up for its members,' which means both groups speak up for the people in them.

choices.text: Both are groups that keep certain people out
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because keeping certain people out was an unfair thing the union did, not the reason it is similar to a team.

choices.text: Both are groups that fly over the city
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because flying over the city is part of Cassie's imagination, and has nothing to do with what a union or team does.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Quilts That Belong in Museums

Here is something worth thinking about: some people used to believe that quilts were just blankets — useful but not "real" art. What do you think? Ringgold proved them wrong. Her quilts deal with racial equality — the idea that people of every race deserve the same fair treatment. They explore women's rights and African American history. They tell stories about real people and real struggles, yet they overflow with beauty, joy, and hope.

In many communities, quilts were ways to preserve — to keep safe and protect from being lost — stories, memories, and family history.

items.prompt: According to the text, what is the meaning of "racial equality"?

choices.text: The idea that stories and memories must be kept safe.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect — keeping stories and memories safe is the meaning of 'preserve,' a different word explained later in the passage.

choices.text: The idea that people deserve the same fair treatment.
choices.feedback: This is correct — the passage directly defines 'racial equality' as 'the idea that people of every race deserve the same fair treatment.'

choices.text: The idea that quilts are just useful blankets for beds.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect — the passage actually says some people used to think quilts were just blankets, but that idea is challenged, not used to define 'racial equality.'

choices.text: The idea that beautiful art belongs inside a big museum.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect — the passage never says art belongs in a museum; that idea is not connected to the meaning of 'racial equality' at all.

items.title: Section 6 - Guiding Question 5

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
Thread, Paint, and Power

So next time you see a quilt, take a closer look — it might hold a story stitched into every seam! Faith Ringgold picked up a needle, thread, and paint and made art that hangs in one of the world's greatest museums. You can do big, creative things too. A quilt kept Cassie warm on that rooftop — and then it taught her to fly.
stimulus.content_text:
Thread, Paint, and Power
So next time you see a quilt, take a closer look — it might hold a story stitched into every seam! Faith Ringgold picked up a needle, thread, and paint and made art that hangs in one of the world's greatest museums. You can do big, creative things too. A quilt kept Cassie warm on that rooftop — and then it taught her to fly.

items.prompt: Which detail from the section best supports the idea that Faith Ringgold achieved something great with her art?

choices.text: She used a quilt to keep Cassie warm on a rooftop.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because keeping Cassie warm on a rooftop is something that happened in a story, not something that shows Faith Ringgold achieved greatness as an artist.

choices.text: Her art hangs in one of the world's greatest museums.
choices.feedback: B is correct because having your art displayed in one of the world's greatest museums is strong evidence that Faith Ringgold achieved something truly great.

choices.text: She picked up a needle and thread to sew quilts.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because picking up a needle and thread just describes how she started making art — it doesn't show that she achieved something great.

choices.text: She taught readers to take a closer look at quilts.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because teaching readers to look closely at quilts is a lesson from the passage, not a detail about Faith Ringgold's personal achievement as an artist.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: Based on the article, how is Faith Ringgold's quilt 'Tar Beach' different from a regular blanket?

choices.text: It is created as a piece of real art that tells a story about dreams and struggles.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the article says 'Tar Beach' is a story quilt displayed in a museum that tells a story about dreams, imagination, and the real struggles African American families faced.

choices.text: It is used mostly to keep people warm when they sleep outside on cold city rooftops.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the article never says the quilt is used to keep people warm — it was created as a work of art, not as a blanket for sleeping.

choices.text: It is painted directly onto the side of a tall building instead of on strong cloth.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because Ringgold paints on canvas cloth sewn into the center of the quilt, not on the side of a building.

choices.text: It is built by a large union of workers who construct bridges across New York City.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the union in the story refused to let Cassie's father join — it had nothing to do with creating the quilt.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: Which section heading would best help you find information about why Faith Ringgold had the skills she needed to make her quilts?

choices.text: Tar Beach: Her Most Famous Work
choices.feedback: This section is about the story and meaning of her quilt called Tar Beach, not about how she learned her skills.

choices.text: Growing Up in Harlem
choices.feedback: This is the correct answer because it tells us Faith's mother taught her to sew and work with fabric when she was little, which gave her the skills to make quilts later in life.

choices.text: Thread, Paint, and Power
choices.feedback: This section is the ending of the article that encourages you to look at quilts differently, but it doesn't explain how Faith learned her skills.

choices.text: A Girl on a Rooftop
choices.feedback: This section describes a scene from her quilt to grab your attention, but it doesn't tell you anything about how Faith learned to sew or make art.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: Which order of events best tells the main idea of Faith Ringgold's life as an artist?

choices.text: She learned to sew, worked as a painter, and then invented story quilts.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage clearly shows Faith learned to sew as a child, then became a painter, and then invented story quilts in the 1980s — in that exact order.

choices.text: She invented story quilts, worked as a painter, and then learned to sew.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because it flips the order — Faith actually learned to sew first and invented story quilts last, not the other way around.

choices.text: She flew over a bridge, rested on a rooftop, and then painted a canvas.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because flying over a bridge and resting on a rooftop are things that happen in the story inside the Tar Beach quilt, not real events in Faith Ringgold's life.

choices.text: She built a large bridge, joined a labor union, and then made a blanket.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because building a bridge and joining a union are things connected to Cassie's father in the quilt's story, not anything Faith Ringgold did in real life.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: How are Faith Ringgold's story quilts similar to the quilts made in many communities?

choices.text: Both are made using strong painted cloth.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because using a strong painted canvas in the center is a special feature of Faith Ringgold's story quilts, not something all community quilts do.

choices.text: Both are used to share and save stories.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage says Ringgold's quilts tell stories AND that community quilts were used to preserve stories and memories — so both kinds of quilts share and save stories.

choices.text: Both are shown inside large art museums.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage only mentions that Ringgold's quilts hang in a famous museum, not that all community quilts are shown in museums.

choices.text: Both are created by teams of bridge workers.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because bridge workers appear in the story inside the Tar Beach quilt — they have nothing to do with who makes quilts.

syllabus_metadata.title: Quilts That Tell Stories: Faith Ringgold's Art

assessments.title: Walls That Speak: Diego Rivera's Murals

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Diego Rivera: The Man Who Turned Walls into Stories

A Boy Who Drew on Walls

A toddler in Guanajuato, Mexico, dragged a crayon across his bedroom wall. That toddler was Diego Rivera, born in 1886, and he never stopped drawing on walls for the rest of his life.

He studied painting in Mexico City as a young man. Then he traveled to Europe, where he spent years in Paris and Italy learning from famous painters. He studied how they used light, color, and shape. But he carried something with him the whole time — a love for Mexico and its people.
stimulus.content_text:
Diego Rivera: The Man Who Turned Walls into StoriesA Boy Who Drew on Walls
A toddler in Guanajuato, Mexico, dragged a crayon across his bedroom wall. That toddler was Diego Rivera, born in 1886, and he never stopped drawing on walls for the rest of his life.

He studied painting in Mexico City as a young man. Then he traveled to Europe, where he spent years in Paris and Italy learning from famous painters. He studied how they used light, color, and shape. But he carried something with him the whole time — a love for Mexico and its people.

items.prompt: The text says Diego Rivera 'spent years in Paris and Italy learning from famous painters. But he carried something with him the whole time — a love for Mexico and its people.' What contrast does the word 'But' show here?

choices.text: He studied in Europe but kept his love for Mexico.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage directly says he studied in Europe AND carried a love for Mexico the whole time — that contrast is exactly what 'But' is showing.

choices.text: He loved Mexico but never went back home.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the passage never mentions whether Diego went back to Mexico or not.

choices.text: He learned to paint but forgot what he learned.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the passage says he was learning from famous painters — there is no mention of him forgetting anything.

choices.text: He lived in Mexico but wanted to stay in Europe.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the passage never says Diego wanted to stay in Europe — in fact, his love for Mexico suggests the opposite.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
A Country in Need of Its Own Art

When Rivera came home, Mexico looked very different. The country had just been through the Mexican Revolution — a long, violent struggle where ordinary people fought to take power away from a small group of wealthy rulers. People wanted a fresh start. Rivera did too.

He decided to make art that belonged to everyone, not just rich people in fancy galleries. So he painted murals — enormous paintings made directly on the walls of public buildings. Picture a painting the size of your entire classroom wall, or even bigger. Anyone walking by could see a mural for free — farmers, factory workers, children, grandparents. You didn't need a ticket. You just needed eyes.
stimulus.content_text:
A Country in Need of Its Own Art
When Rivera came home, Mexico looked very different. The country had just been through the Mexican Revolution — a long, violent struggle where ordinary people fought to take power away from a small group of wealthy rulers. People wanted a fresh start. Rivera did too.

He decided to make art that belonged to everyone, not just rich people in fancy galleries. So he painted murals — enormous paintings made directly on the walls of public buildings. Picture a painting the size of your entire classroom wall, or even bigger. Anyone walking by could see a mural for free — farmers, factory workers, children, grandparents. You didn't need a ticket. You just needed eyes.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, what is the main reason Rivera chose to paint murals on public buildings?

choices.text: He wanted to show off the skills he learned in Europe
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage never mentions Rivera wanting to show off anything he learned in Europe — his reason was about sharing art with everyone.

choices.text: He wanted everyone to be able to enjoy art for free
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage clearly says Rivera wanted art to belong to everyone, and that anyone could see his murals for free without a ticket.

choices.text: He wanted rich people to buy his paintings in galleries
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because Rivera actually moved away from galleries and rich buyers — he wanted ordinary people, not just wealthy ones, to enjoy his art.

choices.text: He wanted to make buildings look more colorful outside
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage never mentions making buildings look colorful — Rivera's goal was to make art free and available to all people.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Painting on Wet Plaster

You might think Rivera just grabbed a brush and painted on a dry wall, the way you would paint a poster. Actually, he often used a special technique called fresco. In fresco, the artist spreads wet plaster on part of a wall, then paints on it before the plaster dries. The paint soaks right into the wall and becomes part of it, like dye soaking into fabric. That is why Rivera's murals still look bright today, nearly a hundred years later.

His murals were so huge that he worked with teams of assistants. Rivera directed them all, like a coach running a team. But his hand guided the most important figures and details.
stimulus.content_text:
Painting on Wet Plaster
You might think Rivera just grabbed a brush and painted on a dry wall, the way you would paint a poster. Actually, he often used a special technique called fresco. In fresco, the artist spreads wet plaster on part of a wall, then paints on it before the plaster dries. The paint soaks right into the wall and becomes part of it, like dye soaking into fabric. That is why Rivera's murals still look bright today, nearly a hundred years later.

His murals were so huge that he worked with teams of assistants. Rivera directed them all, like a coach running a team. But his hand guided the most important figures and details.

items.prompt: According to the text, what does Rivera do right after he spreads wet plaster on part of a wall?

choices.text: He waits for the plaster to dry
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage says Rivera paints BEFORE the plaster dries, not after he waits for it to dry.

choices.text: He paints on it right away
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage clearly states he paints on the wet plaster before it dries.

choices.text: He adds dry plaster on top
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage never mentions adding dry plaster on top — that is not part of the fresco technique described.

choices.text: He asks assistants to wash it
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage does not say assistants wash the plaster; they help Rivera paint, with Rivera guiding the most important parts himself.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
What the Murals Show

Rivera's murals burst with people, action, and color. He painted farmers planting corn. Factory workers building machines. Teachers standing before classrooms. Families dancing at festivals. He depicted — or showed in pictures — the hard work and dignity of regular people. Dignity means the deep respect every person deserves, like the pride you feel when someone truly sees your effort.

Rivera's style blended many influences. He studied ancient Mexican art, especially the carvings and paintings of the Aztec and Maya civilizations — groups of people who built great cities and cultures long before Europeans arrived. He mixed those bold shapes with what he had learned in Europe. The result was completely his own: large, solid figures with strong outlines and rich colors you could see clearly from far away.
stimulus.content_text:
What the Murals Show
Rivera's murals burst with people, action, and color. He painted farmers planting corn. Factory workers building machines. Teachers standing before classrooms. Families dancing at festivals. He depicted — or showed in pictures — the hard work and dignity of regular people. Dignity means the deep respect every person deserves, like the pride you feel when someone truly sees your effort.

Rivera's style blended many influences. He studied ancient Mexican art, especially the carvings and paintings of the Aztec and Maya civilizations — groups of people who built great cities and cultures long before Europeans arrived. He mixed those bold shapes with what he had learned in Europe. The result was completely his own: large, solid figures with strong outlines and rich colors you could see clearly from far away.

items.prompt: Based on the text, how are the farmers, factory workers, and teachers in Rivera's murals similar?

choices.text: They are all regular people who work hard.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage directly says Rivera depicted 'the hard work and dignity of regular people,' and farmers, factory workers, and teachers are all examples of those regular, hard-working people.

choices.text: They are all ancient people who built cities.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because ancient people who built great cities refers to the Aztec and Maya civilizations, not the workers Rivera painted in his murals.

choices.text: They are all painters who studied in Europe.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because Rivera is the painter who studied in Europe — the farmers, factory workers, and teachers in the murals are not painters.

choices.text: They are all carvers who made bold shapes.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because bold carvings and shapes describe the ancient Aztec and Maya art style, not the people shown working in Rivera's murals.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Walls That Still Speak

In the National Palace in Mexico City, his paintings stretch across massive walls and tell the entire story of Mexico, from ancient times to the modern day. He also painted in the United States. At the Detroit Institute of Arts, he created a famous series showing American auto workers building cars.

Rivera believed art could teach, inspire, and bring people together. He wanted working people to feel proud of their history when they looked at his walls.

Most art lives behind locked doors, waiting for visitors. Rivera's art lives where people live — in hallways, stairwells, and courtyards. Someone decided that wall had a story to tell, and that you deserved to see it. That idea started with artists like Diego Rivera.
stimulus.content_text:
Walls That Still Speak
In the National Palace in Mexico City, his paintings stretch across massive walls and tell the entire story of Mexico, from ancient times to the modern day. He also painted in the United States. At the Detroit Institute of Arts, he created a famous series showing American auto workers building cars.

Rivera believed art could teach, inspire, and bring people together. He wanted working people to feel proud of their history when they looked at his walls.

Most art lives behind locked doors, waiting for visitors. Rivera's art lives where people live — in hallways, stairwells, and courtyards. Someone decided that wall had a story to tell, and that you deserved to see it. That idea started with artists like Diego Rivera.

items.prompt: According to the text, what is a 'series' of paintings?

choices.text: a single large painting on one wall
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because a series means a group of paintings, not just a single one.

choices.text: a group of paintings about one topic
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage shows Rivera's series was a group of paintings all about the same topic — auto workers building cars.

choices.text: a painting that tells about ancient times
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because ancient times is mentioned in a different part of the passage and has nothing to do with what the word 'series' means.

choices.text: a type of painting made with bright colors
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage never talks about bright colors when explaining what a series is.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: Which statement shows a contrast between where Diego Rivera learned about art and what kind of art he chose to make?

choices.text: He learned from painters in Europe, but he made public murals about Mexico's people.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage clearly shows Rivera studied art in Europe but came home and made public murals for everyday Mexican people — that's the contrast the question is asking about.

choices.text: He learned from workers in Mexico, but he painted only for leaders in Europe.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the passage says Rivera learned from famous painters in Europe, not from workers, and he painted for ordinary people, not for leaders.

choices.text: He learned fresco in galleries, but he painted small posters in classrooms.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because Rivera learned fresco by painting on walls, not in galleries, and his murals were enormous — not small posters.

choices.text: He learned from ancient rulers, but he made murals only about modern machines.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because Rivera studied ancient Mexican civilizations like the Aztec and Maya, not ancient rulers, and his murals showed farmers, families, and workers — not just modern machines.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: Based on the text, what caused Rivera's murals to feature large, solid figures with rich colors?

choices.text: He combined ancient Mexican art shapes with the painting skills he learned in Europe.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage directly says Rivera mixed bold shapes from ancient Mexican art with what he learned in Europe, and that combination created his large, solid figures with rich colors.

choices.text: He applied wet plaster to the walls to make his bright paint colors last longer.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because wet plaster (fresco) helped the paint last longer, but it did not cause his style of large, solid figures and rich colors.

choices.text: He designed his giant artwork to fit inside the fancy galleries of wealthy rulers.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because Rivera painted murals specifically to avoid fancy galleries — he wanted regular people, not wealthy rulers, to see his art.

choices.text: He hired large teams of factory workers to help him paint the massive public buildings.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because his assistants helped him cover the large walls, but they were not factory workers, and using helpers had nothing to do with why his figures looked the way they did.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: How does the author organize the information in the section "A Boy Who Drew on Walls"?

choices.text: By comparing Diego Rivera's paintings to the art of other famous painters
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the section never compares Rivera's paintings to other famous painters' art.

choices.text: By describing the events of Diego Rivera's early life in the order they happened
choices.feedback: B is correct because the section follows the order of Rivera's early life: toddler drawing on walls, then studying in Mexico City, then traveling to Europe.

choices.text: By explaining a problem Diego Rivera had with drawing on walls and how he solved it
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because Rivera didn't have a problem with drawing on walls — the section says he never stopped doing it!

choices.text: By giving reasons why Diego Rivera decided to travel to Europe
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the section only mentions that Rivera traveled to Europe but doesn't explain the reasons why he decided to go.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: How does the article compare Diego Rivera's murals with most other art?

choices.text: Rivera's murals use brighter colors than other art does
choices.feedback: The article talks about Rivera's rich colors, but it never compares his colors to other artists and says his are brighter.

choices.text: Rivera's murals are free and open while most art is locked away
choices.feedback: This is correct because the article directly says Rivera's murals were free for everyone to see, while 'most art lives behind locked doors, waiting for visitors.'

choices.text: Rivera's murals are older than most other art in museums
choices.feedback: The article never compares how old Rivera's murals are to other art sitting in museums.

choices.text: Rivera's murals show people while most other art shows nature
choices.feedback: The article never says that most other art shows nature — that comparison is never made anywhere in the passage.

syllabus_metadata.title: Walls That Speak: Diego Rivera's Murals

assessments.title: The Tune You Remember: Melody and Harmony

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Elements of Music

The Tune You Can't Forget

A five-year-old at a birthday party opens her mouth and sings: "Happy Birthday to you…" Every kid at the table joins in. Nobody reads the notes off a page. They just know the tune. That tune has a name. Musicians call it the melody — a series of notes arranged in a pattern that creates a recognizable song. A melody is like a path through the woods. You follow it one step at a time, one note after the next, moving forward.

The way these notes are arranged gives each melody its own personality. The melody of "Happy Birthday" bounces upward and sounds cheerful. The melody of a lullaby drifts gently downward and sounds calm. Each melody tells a small story through its pattern of notes.

You might think that melody and the beat of a song are the same thing, since you hum them together. But the steady beat underneath a song is called rhythm — a separate element of music, like the ticking of a clock behind the singer's voice. Melody rides on top of rhythm the way a surfer rides on top of a wave. They move together, but they are not the same thing.
stimulus.content_text:
Elements of MusicThe Tune You Can't Forget
A five-year-old at a birthday party opens her mouth and sings: "Happy Birthday to you…" Every kid at the table joins in. Nobody reads the notes off a page. They just know the tune. That tune has a name. Musicians call it the melody — a series of notes arranged in a pattern that creates a recognizable song. A melody is like a path through the woods. You follow it one step at a time, one note after the next, moving forward.

The way these notes are arranged gives each melody its own personality. The melody of "Happy Birthday" bounces upward and sounds cheerful. The melody of a lullaby drifts gently downward and sounds calm. Each melody tells a small story through its pattern of notes.

You might think that melody and the beat of a song are the same thing, since you hum them together. But the steady beat underneath a song is called rhythm — a separate element of music, like the ticking of a clock behind the singer's voice. Melody rides on top of rhythm the way a surfer rides on top of a wave. They move together, but they are not the same thing.

items.prompt: According to the text, how is the melody of a lullaby different from the melody of "Happy Birthday"?

choices.text: It drifts downward and sounds calm.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage says a lullaby 'drifts gently downward and sounds calm,' which is exactly what this answer says.

choices.text: It bounces upward and sounds cheerful.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because 'bounces upward and sounds cheerful' describes 'Happy Birthday,' not a lullaby — it's the opposite!

choices.text: It ticks steadily and stays separate.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because ticking steadily describes rhythm, not the melody of a lullaby.

choices.text: It moves forward and tells stories.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because moving forward and telling stories is how the passage describes melodies in general, not what makes a lullaby different from 'Happy Birthday.'

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Notes Stacked Like Building Blocks

Now picture something different. Instead of notes marching forward one at a time, imagine two or three notes ringing out at the exact same moment. That sound is called harmony — what happens when two or more different notes sound together at once. If melody is a single voice singing alone, harmony is a whole group singing different notes at the same time.

A chord is a specific group of three or more notes played together to create harmony. Think of a chord like a sandwich — bread, cheese, and tomato stacked together make one thing you can taste all at once. When a guitar player strums a chord, several notes blend into one full, rich sound.

Think of the cheerful strum at the start of "You Are My Sunshine." Other chords sound dark and moody, like the creepy music in a movie when a door slowly opens.

Here's a surprising part: harmony does not always sound smooth or pretty. Sometimes a songwriter puts notes together that clash on purpose. They create excitement or suspense, like the scary chords in a thunderstorm scene. Harmony is any combination of notes sounding at the same time — pleasant or not.
stimulus.content_text:
Notes Stacked Like Building Blocks
Now picture something different. Instead of notes marching forward one at a time, imagine two or three notes ringing out at the exact same moment. That sound is called harmony — what happens when two or more different notes sound together at once. If melody is a single voice singing alone, harmony is a whole group singing different notes at the same time.

A chord is a specific group of three or more notes played together to create harmony. Think of a chord like a sandwich — bread, cheese, and tomato stacked together make one thing you can taste all at once. When a guitar player strums a chord, several notes blend into one full, rich sound.

Think of the cheerful strum at the start of "You Are My Sunshine." Other chords sound dark and moody, like the creepy music in a movie when a door slowly opens.

Here's a surprising part: harmony does not always sound smooth or pretty. Sometimes a songwriter puts notes together that clash on purpose. They create excitement or suspense, like the scary chords in a thunderstorm scene. Harmony is any combination of notes sounding at the same time — pleasant or not.

items.prompt: Why does a songwriter sometimes put notes together that clash?

choices.text: To make the music sound smooth and pretty.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage says clashing notes are used when the songwriter does NOT want smooth or pretty music.

choices.text: To build a feeling of excitement or suspense.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly says songwriters use clashing notes on purpose 'to create excitement or suspense.'

choices.text: To help a single voice sing all alone.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because a single voice singing alone describes melody, not harmony, which is about multiple notes sounding together.

choices.text: To keep notes marching forward one by one.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because notes marching forward one at a time describes melody, not what happens with harmony or clashing notes.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
The Star and the Backup Group

The harmony fills in the background, adding color and emotion. Without harmony, a melody can sound thin and bare, like a singer alone in an empty gym. Without melody, harmony is just a wash of sound with nothing to grab onto.

Together, they create something bigger than either one alone. The melody gives you a tune to follow. The harmony wraps around it and makes you feel something — joy, sadness, mystery, excitement.
stimulus.content_text:
The Star and the Backup Group
The harmony fills in the background, adding color and emotion. Without harmony, a melody can sound thin and bare, like a singer alone in an empty gym. Without melody, harmony is just a wash of sound with nothing to grab onto.

Together, they create something bigger than either one alone. The melody gives you a tune to follow. The harmony wraps around it and makes you feel something — joy, sadness, mystery, excitement.

items.prompt: According to the text, what does the harmony do after the melody gives you a tune to follow?

choices.text: It fills up an empty gym.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the empty gym is used to describe how lonely a melody sounds without harmony, not something harmony does.

choices.text: It wraps around the tune.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly says 'The harmony wraps around it,' meaning it wraps around the melody's tune.

choices.text: It washes away the sound.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage says harmony alone is 'a wash of sound,' but it never says harmony washes away sound.

choices.text: It creates a bare melody.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because a bare melody is what happens when there is no harmony, which is the opposite of what harmony creates.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Listening Like a Musician

The next time a song plays, try splitting it apart with your ears. Follow the melody first — the tune you could hum by yourself. Then listen deeper for the harmony underneath, the chords and background notes that fill out the sound. Notice the rhythm holding it all together like a heartbeat.

Most people hear music as one big sound. But now you know it is built from separate pieces — melody, harmony, rhythm — layered on top of each other. That means every song you hear is a small invention, with someone choosing each piece on purpose. You are someone who understands how the invention works.

The next time a song plays, try splitting it apart with your ears. Follow the melody first — the tune you could hum by yourself. Then listen deeper for the harmony underneath, the chords and background notes that fill out the sound. Notice the rhythm holding it all together like a heartbeat.

Most people hear music as one big sound. But now you know it is built from separate pieces — melody, harmony, rhythm — layered on top of each other. That means every song you hear is a small invention, with someone choosing each piece on purpose. You are someone who understands how the invention works.

items.prompt: Read this sentence from the text: 'Notice the rhythm holding it all together like a heartbeat.' Based on this comparison, what does the phrase 'like a heartbeat' tell us about the word 'rhythm'?

choices.text: It is a steady beat that keeps the song going.
choices.feedback: A is correct because a heartbeat is steady and never stops, and the passage compares rhythm to a heartbeat to show it keeps a steady beat that holds the whole song together.

choices.text: It is a loud noise that plays over the music.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because a heartbeat is quiet and steady, not loud, and the passage never says rhythm plays over the music.

choices.text: It is a simple tune that is easy to hum.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because humming a tune describes the melody — the passage talks about melody and rhythm as two separate things.

choices.text: It is a background note that fills out the sound.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because background notes that fill out the sound describe harmony, not rhythm — the passage explains these as different pieces of music.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: Based on the article, how is a melody different from a harmony?

choices.text: A melody plays notes one at a time, but a harmony plays notes all at once.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage says melody moves 'one note after the next,' while harmony is 'two or more different notes sound together at once.'

choices.text: A melody acts as a steady beat, but a harmony acts as a singer's voice.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the article says the steady beat is called rhythm, not melody, and harmony fills the background, not the singer's main voice.

choices.text: A melody sounds like a ticking clock, but a harmony sounds like a thunderstorm.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the ticking clock is used in the article to describe rhythm, not melody.

choices.text: A melody is always cheerful and happy, but a harmony is always dark and creepy.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the article shows melodies can be cheerful or calm, and harmonies can sound happy, dark, or even scary — so neither is always just one feeling.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: According to the article, what happens when a musician adds harmony to a melody?

choices.text: It turns the song into a steady rhythm.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because rhythm is the steady beat that's already in the song, and adding harmony doesn't turn anything into a rhythm — they are separate parts of music.

choices.text: It makes the song sound thin and bare.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the article says a melody sounds thin and bare *without* harmony, meaning harmony actually does the opposite and makes the sound fuller.

choices.text: It creates emotion and a fuller sound.
choices.feedback: C is correct because the article says harmony adds 'color and emotion' and makes you feel joy, sadness, mystery, or excitement, creating a bigger and fuller sound together with the melody.

choices.text: It forces the notes to clash on purpose.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because while harmony can sometimes use clashing notes on purpose, the article explains that harmony is any combination of notes sounding together — it doesn't always clash.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: If you want to first learn how single notes make a tune, and then learn how multiple notes sound together, which headings should you read in order?

choices.text: "The Tune You Can't Forget" then "Notes Stacked Like Building Blocks"
choices.feedback: This is correct because 'The Tune You Can't Forget' explains how single notes make a melody, and 'Notes Stacked Like Building Blocks' explains how multiple notes sound together as harmony and chords — exactly the right order.

choices.text: "Notes Stacked Like Building Blocks" then "The Star and the Backup Group"
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because it starts with multiple notes sounding together before you've learned how single notes make a tune.

choices.text: "The Star and the Backup Group" then "Listening Like a Musician"
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because these sections are about how melody and harmony work as a team and how to listen to music, not about learning each idea from the beginning.

choices.text: "Listening Like a Musician" then "The Tune You Can't Forget"
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because it starts with the listening tips section, which comes after you've already learned about melody and harmony, so it's going in the wrong order.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: Compared to hearing music as 'one big sound,' what does the article's main idea say about how music is actually built?

choices.text: Music is built from rhythm alone, which holds everything together
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because rhythm is just one of three pieces the article describes — melody and harmony are equally important parts of how music is built.

choices.text: Music is built from separate pieces that each do a different job
choices.feedback: B is correct because the article directly says music is 'built from separate pieces — melody, harmony, rhythm' that each do a different job working together.

choices.text: Music is built by stacking chords that always sound smooth
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the article clearly explains that harmony does not always sound smooth — songwriters sometimes use clashing notes on purpose to create suspense or excitement.

choices.text: Music is built from melodies that all move in the same direction
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the article shows that melodies move in different directions, with 'Happy Birthday' bouncing upward and lullabies drifting gently downward.

syllabus_metadata.title: The Tune You Remember: Melody and Harmony

assessments.title: Keep the Beat: Rhythm, Tempo, and Dynamics

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Elements of Music

The Steady Pulse Underneath It All

A drummer in a marching band strikes her bass drum: BOOM, BOOM, BOOM, BOOM. Each hit lands at exactly the same time apart, like footsteps on a sidewalk. That steady thump is called the beat — the even pulse that keeps music moving forward. Every song has one. Without it, music would fall apart into a mess of random noise.

Now here is where things get interesting. Rhythm is not the same thing as beat, even though people mix them up all the time. Your feet hit the ground in a steady beat when you walk. But the words you speak while walking have their own pattern — some long, some short, some with pauses. That pattern of long and short sounds is rhythm. The beat stays steady underneath, like a clock ticking. The rhythm dances on top of it, changing and surprising you.

Try this: clap four steady beats. Now clap the pattern CLAP-clap-CLAP — pause — CLAP. Feel how that pause, that little pocket of silence, shapes the whole pattern? Those silent moments are called rests. Actually, rests are just as powerful as sounds. They give rhythm its shape, the same way spaces between words let you read a sentence.

Elements of MusicThe Steady Pulse Underneath It All
