That steady thump is called the beat — the even pulse that keeps music moving forward. Every song has one. Without it, music would fall apart into a mess of random noise.

Now here is where things get interesting. Rhythm is not the same thing as beat, even though people mix them up all the time. Your feet hit the ground in a steady beat when you walk. But the words you speak while walking have their own pattern — some long, some short, some with pauses. That pattern of long and short sounds is rhythm. The beat stays steady underneath, like a clock ticking. The rhythm dances on top of it, changing and surprising you.

Try this: clap four steady beats. Now clap the pattern CLAP-clap-CLAP — pause — CLAP. Feel how that pause, that little pocket of silence, shapes the whole pattern? Those silent moments are called rests. Actually, rests are just as powerful as sounds. They give rhythm its shape, the same way spaces between words let you read a sentence.

items.prompt: According to the text, how is rhythm different from beat?

choices.text: Rhythm is the even pulse that stays the same throughout a song
choices.feedback: This is actually describing the beat — the passage says the beat is the 'even pulse' that stays steady, not rhythm.

choices.text: Rhythm is a pattern of long and short sounds over the beat
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says rhythm is the pattern of long and short sounds that dances on top of the steady beat.

choices.text: Rhythm is the silent pause that comes between two sounds
choices.feedback: Those silent moments are called rests, which help shape rhythm, but they are not what rhythm itself means.

choices.text: Rhythm is the speed at which a drummer hits the bass drum
choices.feedback: The passage never connects rhythm to the speed of a drummer — that idea isn't in the text at all.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Fast, Slow, and Everything Between

The speed of the beat has a special name: tempo. Tempo tells musicians how fast or slow to play a piece. A fast tempo makes your heart race. A slow tempo helps you breathe and relax.

Composers — the people who write music — use Italian words to describe tempo. Why Italian? Hundreds of years ago, Italy was the center of European music. Italian musicians wrote the first directions on sheet music, and their words stuck. Today, musicians everywhere still use them.

Allegro means fast. Picture an exciting chase scene in a movie, with the music racing along. Andante means a walking pace — relaxed and steady, like a stroll through a park. Adagio means slow — gentle and calm, like watching a sunset fade. The tempo a composer picks helps set the mood before a single melody — the main tune you hum — even begins.
stimulus.content_text:
Fast, Slow, and Everything Between
The speed of the beat has a special name: tempo. Tempo tells musicians how fast or slow to play a piece. A fast tempo makes your heart race. A slow tempo helps you breathe and relax.

Composers — the people who write music — use Italian words to describe tempo. Why Italian? Hundreds of years ago, Italy was the center of European music. Italian musicians wrote the first directions on sheet music, and their words stuck. Today, musicians everywhere still use them.

Allegro means fast. Picture an exciting chase scene in a movie, with the music racing along. Andante means a walking pace — relaxed and steady, like a stroll through a park. Adagio means slow — gentle and calm, like watching a sunset fade. The tempo a composer picks helps set the mood before a single melody — the main tune you hum — even begins.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, why do musicians everywhere still use Italian words for tempo?

choices.text: Because Italian words make the music sound faster
choices.feedback: The passage never says Italian words change how fast the music sounds — they are just labels that describe the speed.

choices.text: Because early music directions were written in Italian
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says Italian musicians wrote the first directions on sheet music, and those words stuck around for everyone to use.

choices.text: Because composers today only write music in Italy
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says musicians *everywhere* still use Italian words, not that composers only write music in Italy today.

choices.text: Because slow songs were first played in Italian
choices.feedback: The passage never mentions slow songs starting in Italy — it only explains that Italy was the center of music and Italian words were used first for all kinds of directions.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
The Volume Knob of Music

Dynamics describe how loud or soft the music is. Composers use Italian words for dynamics, too. Forte means loud, like a marching band blasting down your street. Piano means soft, like a secret whispered in your ear.

It is easy to think dynamics just mean "loud" or "soft," like flipping a light switch on or off. But dynamics work more like a dimmer switch. Music can shift smoothly from soft to loud and back again. A crescendo is when the music gradually gets louder, like a thunderstorm building from a distant rumble to a roaring crash. A decrescendo is the opposite — the sound slowly fades, like that storm moving away.

But when the music swells during a powerful moment, then drops to a whisper during a tender one, you feel something.

It is easy to think dynamics just mean "loud" or "soft," like flipping a light switch on or off. But dynamics work more like a dimmer switch. Music can shift smoothly from soft to loud and back again. A crescendo is when the music gradually gets louder, like a thunderstorm building from a distant rumble to a roaring crash. A decrescendo is the opposite — the sound slowly fades, like that storm moving away.

But when the music swells during a powerful moment, then drops to a whisper during a tender one, you feel something.

items.prompt: According to the text, what is the sequence of sounds during a crescendo?

choices.text: It slowly fades from a roaring crash to a distant rumble.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because going from a roaring crash to a distant rumble means the sound is fading, which describes a decrescendo, not a crescendo.

choices.text: It builds from a distant rumble to a roaring crash.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage uses the thunderstorm example to show a crescendo builds gradually from a quiet rumble to a loud crash.

choices.text: It quickly flips from a soft whisper to a loud blast.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage says dynamics work like a dimmer switch — they change gradually, not quickly like flipping a light switch on or off.

choices.text: It suddenly drops from a powerful swell to a soft whisper.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because suddenly dropping from loud to soft is not what a crescendo means — that sounds more like a sudden change, not a gradual build.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
How the Pieces Fit Together

Beat, rhythm, tempo, and dynamics do not work alone. They team up with melody and harmony — the blend of different notes played at the same time, like voices singing together in a choir. All these elements combine to build the songs on your playlist, the symphonies — long pieces written for a full orchestra — in concert halls, and the soundtracks in your favorite movies.

You are reading the language that every musician on Earth speaks — no matter what country they come from or what instrument they play.
stimulus.content_text:
How the Pieces Fit Together

All these elements combine to build the songs on your playlist, the symphonies — long pieces written for a full orchestra — in concert halls, and the soundtracks in your favorite movies.

You are reading the language that every musician on Earth speaks — no matter what country they come from or what instrument they play.

items.prompt: To help explain the word harmony, the author compares it to a choir. Based on the text, how is harmony similar to a choir?

choices.text: Both blend different sounds together at the same time.
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says harmony is a blend of different notes at the same time, just like voices in a choir blend together — both mix different sounds at once.

choices.text: Both are long pieces written for a large orchestra.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage uses 'long pieces written for a full orchestra' to describe symphonies, not harmony or a choir.

choices.text: Both are soundtracks played during your favorite movies.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because soundtracks are mentioned as something all the music elements help create, not as a way to explain what harmony or a choir is.

choices.text: Both are languages spoken by musicians around the world.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage talks about music being a language all musicians speak, but that idea is separate from the explanation of harmony and choirs.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: Based on the text, what is the difference between tempo and dynamics?

choices.text: Tempo changes the pattern of the notes, while dynamics change the steady pulse.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because tempo controls speed, not note patterns, and dynamics control volume, not the steady pulse (that's the beat).

choices.text: Tempo changes the speed of a song, while dynamics change the volume level.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly states that tempo is the speed of the beat and dynamics describe how loud or soft the music is.

choices.text: Tempo uses Italian words for directions, while dynamics use English words.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage tells us that both tempo and dynamics use Italian words, not English.

choices.text: Tempo makes the music gradually louder, while dynamics make it fade away.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because gradually getting louder (crescendo) and fading away (decrescendo) are types of dynamics, not descriptions of what tempo does.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: To find out what causes a song to make your heart race and what causes it to sound like a roaring storm, which two headings should you read?

choices.text: "The Steady Pulse Underneath It All" and "How the Pieces Fit Together"
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because 'The Steady Pulse Underneath It All' is about beat and rhythm, and neither of those sections talks about heart-racing tempos or roaring storm sounds.

choices.text: "Fast, Slow, and Everything Between" and "The Volume Knob of Music"
choices.feedback: This is correct because 'Fast, Slow, and Everything Between' explains that a fast tempo makes your heart race, and 'The Volume Knob of Music' explains that a crescendo sounds like a roaring storm.

choices.text: "The Steady Pulse Underneath It All" and "Fast, Slow, and Everything Between"
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because 'The Steady Pulse Underneath It All' is about beat and rhythm, so it won't help you find the roaring storm clue.

choices.text: "The Volume Knob of Music" and "How the Pieces Fit Together"
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because 'How the Pieces Fit Together' only gives a summary of all the music elements working together, and it doesn't explain what makes your heart race.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: Which sequence best describes how the author organizes the details to support the main idea that music is made of many parts?

choices.text: First explaining the steady beat, then describing speed and volume, and finally showing how they all blend together.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the article really does go in this order — beat first, then speed and volume, then showing how everything blends together at the end.

choices.text: First showing how they all blend together, then explaining the steady beat, and finally describing speed and volume.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the author does NOT start with how everything blends together — that part comes at the very end of the article, not the beginning.

choices.text: First explaining the steady beat, then describing how to clap a pattern, and finally showing how to use Italian words.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because 'how to clap a pattern' and 'Italian words' are just small details from the article, not the main topics the author uses to organize the whole piece.

choices.text: First explaining how to play a drum, then describing how to walk in a park, and finally showing how to watch a sunset.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because playing a drum, walking in a park, and watching a sunset are just little examples the author uses to explain ideas — they are not the main way the article is organized.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: Based on the article, how are rhythm and dynamics similar to each other?

choices.text: They both change and shift throughout a piece of music.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the article shows that rhythm changes and surprises you, and dynamics shift smoothly from loud to soft like a dimmer switch — so both change throughout a piece of music.

choices.text: They both keep a steady pulse underneath a piece of music.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the article says the beat is what keeps a steady pulse underneath the music, not rhythm or dynamics.

choices.text: They both use Italian words to describe a piece of music.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because Italian words are used to describe tempo and dynamics, but the article never mentions Italian words for rhythm.

choices.text: They both control the volume level during a piece of music.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because only dynamics control how loud or soft the music is — rhythm is about patterns of long and short sounds, not volume.

syllabus_metadata.title: Keep the Beat: Rhythm, Tempo, and Dynamics

assessments.title: How Songs Are Built: Musical Form

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
How Songs Are Built: The Secret Shapes of Music

A Song You Already Know

Picture this: "Old MacDonald had a farm, E-I-E-I-O." You sing that line, and then you hear about a cow. Then the same line comes back. Then a pig. Then the same line again. That song is not just a jumble of animal sounds. Someone planned it. Someone decided which parts repeat and which parts change.

Every piece of music has a plan like this. That plan is called its form — the way a piece of music is organized into sections. Think of form like the floor plan of a house. A floor plan shows where each room goes. Musical form shows where each section of music goes and how those sections fit together.
stimulus.content_text:
How Songs Are Built: The Secret Shapes of MusicA Song You Already Know
Picture this: "Old MacDonald had a farm, E-I-E-I-O." You sing that line, and then you hear about a cow. Then the same line comes back. Then a pig. Then the same line again. That song is not just a jumble of animal sounds. Someone planned it. Someone decided which parts repeat and which parts change.
Every piece of music has a plan like this. That plan is called its form — the way a piece of music is organized into sections. Think of form like the floor plan of a house. A floor plan shows where each room goes. Musical form shows where each section of music goes and how those sections fit together.

items.prompt: According to the text, how is a song different from a jumble of sounds?

choices.text: a song uses only animal sounds
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage only mentions animal sounds as an example from one song, not as something all songs must have.

choices.text: a song has a plan for its parts
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly says a song 'is not just a jumble' because someone planned which parts repeat and which parts change.

choices.text: a song always repeats the same line
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage explains that songs have parts that repeat AND parts that change, not that everything repeats.

choices.text: a song must have a floor plan
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because a floor plan is just a comparison the author uses to explain musical form — it is not something a real song needs.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Verses and Choruses

Most pop songs on the radio use a pattern called verse-chorus form. A verse is a section that tells the story of the song. Each verse uses the same melody — the main tune you hum — but the words change every time. A chorus is the big, catchy section that repeats with the same words and the same melody.

Actually, the chorus is the part you remember, because the words never change. That is why it gets stuck in your head.

A pop song might follow this order: verse, chorus, verse, chorus, bridge, chorus. The bridge is a short section that sounds different from both the verse and the chorus — a surprise detour before the familiar chorus returns.
stimulus.content_text:
Verses and Choruses
Most pop songs on the radio use a pattern called verse-chorus form. A verse is a section that tells the story of the song. Each verse uses the same melody — the main tune you hum — but the words change every time. A chorus is the big, catchy section that repeats with the same words and the same melody.
Actually, the chorus is the part you remember, because the words never change. That is why it gets stuck in your head.
A pop song might follow this order: verse, chorus, verse, chorus, bridge, chorus. The bridge is a short section that sounds different from both the verse and the chorus — a surprise detour before the familiar chorus returns.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, how does the bridge section affect the way a listener feels when the chorus comes back?

choices.text: It makes the chorus feel boring because the same words repeat again.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage explains the chorus is the catchy part that gets stuck in your head — not something boring.

choices.text: It makes the returning chorus feel familiar after a surprising change.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage calls the bridge 'a surprise detour,' which makes the returning chorus feel familiar and welcome after that unexpected change.

choices.text: It makes the chorus sound different because the melody has changed.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage says the chorus always has the same melody — the melody does not change.

choices.text: It makes the listener forget the chorus because new words are used.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the bridge does not introduce new chorus words, and the chorus coming back after the bridge actually helps the listener remember it.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Music Without Words Has Form, Too

You might think only songs with words have a form. But instrumental music — music played by instruments alone, with no singing — follows a plan, too. A composer, the person who writes a piece of music, chooses exactly how to arrange each section.

One common plan is called AB form. Musicians also call it binary form, which means "two-part form." The A section introduces one musical idea. Then the B section introduces a contrasting idea — noticeably different, the way a sunny day feels different from a rainy one. Two ideas, two experiences.
stimulus.content_text:
Music Without Words Has Form, Too
You might think only songs with words have a form. But instrumental music — music played by instruments alone, with no singing — follows a plan, too. A composer, the person who writes a piece of music, chooses exactly how to arrange each section.
One common plan is called AB form. Musicians also call it binary form, which means "two-part form." The A section introduces one musical idea. Then the B section introduces a contrasting idea — noticeably different, the way a sunny day feels different from a rainy one. Two ideas, two experiences.

items.prompt: According to the text, what happens after the A section introduces one musical idea in AB form?

choices.text: The A section repeats the same idea again.
choices.feedback: The passage never says the A section repeats — it says the B section comes next with a different idea.

choices.text: The B section introduces a contrasting idea.
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says 'the B section introduces a contrasting idea' right after the A section.

choices.text: The composer adds words and singing.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because the passage explains that instrumental music has no singing at all.

choices.text: The B section comes before the A section.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because the passage clearly shows A comes first and B comes second, not the other way around.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
The Musical Sandwich

ABA form adds one more step. Musicians also call it ternary form, which means "three-part form." Imagine a peanut butter sandwich. The two slices of bread are the same. The peanut butter in the middle is different. ABA form works the same way. You hear the A section first. Then a contrasting B section arrives. Then the A section comes back.

That return of the A section gives you a warm, satisfied feeling — like coming home after a long trip. "Twinkle, Twinkle, Little Star" uses ABA form. The opening tune is A. The middle part ("Up above the world so high") is B. Then the opening tune returns. Sometimes the returning A sounds a little different — maybe louder, maybe with new instruments — but it is still A because the main melody is the same.
stimulus.content_text:
The Musical Sandwich
ABA form adds one more step. Musicians also call it ternary form, which means "three-part form." Imagine a peanut butter sandwich. The two slices of bread are the same. The peanut butter in the middle is different. ABA form works the same way. You hear the A section first. Then a contrasting B section arrives. Then the A section comes back.
That return of the A section gives you a warm, satisfied feeling — like coming home after a long trip. "Twinkle, Twinkle, Little Star" uses ABA form. The opening tune is A. The middle part ("Up above the world so high") is B. Then the opening tune returns. Sometimes the returning A sounds a little different — maybe louder, maybe with new instruments — but it is still A because the main melody is the same.

items.prompt: In the section, the author says, 'The two slices of bread are the same. The peanut butter in the middle is different.' Then the author writes, 'Then a contrasting B section arrives.' Based on comparing the sandwich to ABA form, what does the word 'contrasting' most likely mean?

choices.text: sounding louder than before
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage never says the B section is louder — louder is mentioned as something that might happen when A comes back, not B.

choices.text: being different from what came before
choices.feedback: B is correct because the sandwich clue shows the peanut butter (B section) is different from the bread (A sections), which matches the meaning of 'contrasting.'

choices.text: repeating the same melody again
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because contrasting means something different, not something repeated — repeating the same melody is what the A section does.

choices.text: coming back home after a trip
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because 'coming home after a trip' describes the warm feeling of hearing A return, not the meaning of the word 'contrasting.'

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Why Form Matters

Knowing about form changes the way you hear music. When a verse ends, you can feel the chorus coming. When the B section plays in an ABA piece, you can look forward to the return of A. Your brain loves these patterns. It loves guessing what comes next and being right.

Form also helps musicians learn their music faster. Instead of memorizing thousands of separate notes, they think in sections: "Here is A, here is B, and here A comes back." The sections become building blocks.

Every song you have ever loved was built from a plan.
stimulus.content_text:
Why Form Matters
Knowing about form changes the way you hear music. When a verse ends, you can feel the chorus coming. When the B section plays in an ABA piece, you can look forward to the return of A. Your brain loves these patterns. It loves guessing what comes next and being right.
Form also helps musicians learn their music faster. Instead of memorizing thousands of separate notes, they think in sections: "Here is A, here is B, and here A comes back." The sections become building blocks.
Every song you have ever loved was built from a plan.

items.prompt: In the article "How Songs Are Built: Musical Form," what does the author mean by the phrase "building blocks"?

choices.text: The thousands of separate notes
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says musicians think in sections *instead of* memorizing thousands of separate notes — so notes are the opposite of building blocks.

choices.text: The different sections of music
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly says 'The sections become building blocks,' meaning the A and B sections are the building blocks musicians use.

choices.text: The patterns inside your brain
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because brain patterns describe how listeners enjoy guessing what comes next, not what building blocks are.

choices.text: The plans behind every song
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because 'the plan' refers to the whole song put together, not the smaller sections that are used to build it.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: How is a verse different from a chorus in verse-chorus form?

choices.text: A verse has a melody but a chorus does not have one
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage says a verse has a melody — in fact, each verse uses 'the same melody' even though the words change.

choices.text: A verse changes its words each time but a chorus keeps the same words
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage clearly states that a verse changes its words each time while a chorus 'repeats with the same words and the same melody.'

choices.text: A verse is played by instruments but a chorus is sung by people
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage never says verses are played by instruments — verses and choruses are both sections of songs, not separated by who performs them.

choices.text: A verse sounds like the bridge but a chorus sounds like the A section
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage describes verses and choruses as their own unique sections and never says a verse sounds like a bridge or that a chorus sounds like an A section.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: To find out what causes a chorus to get stuck in your head and why your brain loves musical patterns, which two headings should you read?

choices.text: "A Song You Already Know" and "The Musical Sandwich"
choices.feedback: Option A is wrong because 'A Song You Already Know' only introduces the idea of musical form using Old MacDonald, and 'The Musical Sandwich' talks about ABA form — neither one explains why a chorus gets stuck in your head or why your brain loves patterns.

choices.text: "Verses and Choruses" and "Why Form Matters"
choices.feedback: Option B is correct because 'Verses and Choruses' explains that a chorus repeats the same words and melody so it gets stuck in your head, and 'Why Form Matters' explains that your brain loves musical patterns because it enjoys guessing what comes next.

choices.text: "Verses and Choruses" and "The Musical Sandwich"
choices.feedback: Option C is wrong because 'The Musical Sandwich' is about ABA form and peanut butter sandwiches — it doesn't talk about why a chorus gets stuck in your head or why your brain loves patterns.

choices.text: "A Song You Already Know" and "Why Form Matters"
choices.feedback: Option D is wrong because 'A Song You Already Know' only introduces what musical form is using Old MacDonald as an example, and doesn't explain anything about choruses getting stuck in your head.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: Which statement best explains the main idea of the article?

choices.text: A piece of music is built by putting different sections into a planned order.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the article explains that all music — songs with words and instrumental pieces — is built by putting different sections into a planned order called form.

choices.text: A piece of music is built by playing one catchy melody from start to finish.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the article describes music as having multiple different sections, like verses, choruses, and bridges, not just one melody repeated from start to finish.

choices.text: A piece of music is built by writing the words first and adding instruments later.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the article talks about instrumental music, which has no words, so music is definitely not always built by writing words first.

choices.text: A piece of music is built by letting musicians choose which part to play next.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the article says a composer chooses exactly how to arrange the sections, meaning the plan is decided ahead of time, not made up by musicians as they go.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: The author writes about a house's floor plan and a peanut butter sandwich. Based on the article, how are these two things alike?

choices.text: They both show how a song tells a new story.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because neither the floor plan nor the sandwich is used to explain how a song tells a story — they are both used to explain how music is organized.

choices.text: They both explain how pieces of music are organized.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the author uses both the floor plan and the peanut butter sandwich as comparisons to help explain how pieces of music are organized into sections.

choices.text: They both describe the feeling of returning back home.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the 'coming home' feeling is used to describe what ABA music sounds like, not what the sandwich comparison means.

choices.text: They both tell musicians which instruments to play next.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because neither the floor plan nor the sandwich has anything to do with telling musicians which instruments to play.

syllabus_metadata.title: How Songs Are Built: Musical Form

assessments.title: Meet the Orchestra: Families of Instruments

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Listening to the Orchestra: Four Families, One Sound

Eighty Musicians, One Downbeat

A woman in a black dress lifts a wooden stick called a baton. In that instant, eighty musicians breathe together and begin to play. A few stand behind giant drums, mallets raised. Somehow, all eighty people create one piece of music instead of a jumbled mess. The secret? An orchestra is organized into four families of instruments, and each family has its own job to do.
stimulus.content_text:
Listening to the Orchestra: Four Families, One SoundEighty Musicians, One Downbeat
A woman in a black dress lifts a wooden stick called a baton. In that instant, eighty musicians breathe together and begin to play. A few stand behind giant drums, mallets raised. Somehow, all eighty people create one piece of music instead of a jumbled mess. The secret? An orchestra is organized into four families of instruments, and each family has its own job to do.

items.prompt: The text says, "Somehow, all eighty people create one piece of music instead of a jumbled mess." In this sentence, what does the author contrast with a "jumbled mess"?

choices.text: A single wooden stick
choices.feedback: A wooden stick (the baton) is mentioned earlier in the passage, but it is not what the author is contrasting with a jumbled mess in that sentence.

choices.text: One piece of music
choices.feedback: This is correct! The sentence directly says the eighty musicians create 'one piece of music' instead of a 'jumbled mess,' so those two things are being contrasted.

choices.text: Four instrument families
choices.feedback: The four instrument families are mentioned later in the passage as the reason the orchestra works, but they are not part of the contrast in that sentence.

choices.text: Several giant drums
choices.feedback: The giant drums are described at the beginning of the passage, but they are not being compared to a jumbled mess in that sentence.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
The String Family: The Orchestra's Warm Heart

The largest group sits right at the front. These are the strings — violins, violas, cellos, and double basses. They make sound through vibrating strings. Vibrating means moving back and forth very quickly, the way a rubber band twangs when you pluck it. Players can drag a bow across the strings or pluck them with their fingers.

The violin plays the highest notes with a bright, singing voice. The double bass plays the lowest, with a deep rumble you can almost feel in your chest. Together, the strings act as a foundation — a base that holds everything up, like the floor of a house holds up the furniture.
stimulus.content_text:
The String Family: The Orchestra's Warm Heart
The largest group sits right at the front. These are the strings — violins, violas, cellos, and double basses. They make sound through vibrating strings. Vibrating means moving back and forth very quickly, the way a rubber band twangs when you pluck it. Players can drag a bow across the strings or pluck them with their fingers.

The violin plays the highest notes with a bright, singing voice. The double bass plays the lowest, with a deep rumble you can almost feel in your chest. Together, the strings act as a foundation — a base that holds everything up, like the floor of a house holds up the furniture.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, why do strings make sound when a player drags a bow across them?

choices.text: because the bow pushes air through the instrument
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage never mentions air being pushed through string instruments — that's how wind instruments make sound.

choices.text: because the strings move back and forth very quickly
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage explains that strings make sound by vibrating, which means moving back and forth very quickly.

choices.text: because the player plucks them with their fingers
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because plucking with fingers is a different way to play strings, not what happens when a bow is used.

choices.text: because the strings are the largest group in front
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because sitting at the front of the orchestra tells us where the strings are located, not why they make sound.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
The Woodwind Family: Not Always Wood

These instruments make sound when air vibrates inside a tube. The flute, clarinet, oboe, and bassoon all belong here. The flute, for example, is made of metal. It is still called a woodwind because of how it makes sound, not what it is made of.

Each woodwind has its own personality. The flute sounds bright and airy, like a bird calling. The clarinet sounds smooth and warm. The oboe has a rich, slightly nasal tone — nasal means it sounds a bit like someone singing through their nose. That sharp sound helps the oboe cut right through the rest of the orchestra.
stimulus.content_text:
The Woodwind Family: Not Always Wood
These instruments make sound when air vibrates inside a tube. The flute, clarinet, oboe, and bassoon all belong here. The flute, for example, is made of metal. It is still called a woodwind because of how it makes sound, not what it is made of.

Each woodwind has its own personality. The flute sounds bright and airy, like a bird calling. The clarinet sounds smooth and warm. The oboe has a rich, slightly nasal tone — nasal means it sounds a bit like someone singing through their nose. That sharp sound helps the oboe cut right through the rest of the orchestra.

items.prompt: According to the text, when do woodwind instruments make a sound?

choices.text: When air vibrates inside a tube
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage directly states that woodwind instruments 'make sound when air vibrates inside a tube.'

choices.text: When a bird calls in the forest
choices.feedback: B is wrong because a bird calling is only used as a comparison to describe how the flute sounds, not how woodwinds make sound.

choices.text: When a person sings a loud note
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the passage never mentions someone singing a loud note as the way woodwind instruments produce sound.

choices.text: When solid metal turns into wood
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the passage explains that woodwinds can be made of metal, like the flute, and nothing in the text talks about metal turning into wood.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
The Brass Family: Bold and Powerful

Farther back sit the brass instruments — trumpets, trombones, French horns, and tubas. To play one, a musician buzzes their lips into a cup-shaped mouthpiece. That buzzing travels through coiled metal tubes and comes out big and bold.

Brass instruments can sound triumphant — proud and victorious, like a fanfare announcing a king. A fanfare is a short, loud burst of brass music, the kind you hear at the start of a royal ceremony. But brass can also play softly, like a faraway horn echoing across a valley.
stimulus.content_text:
The Brass Family: Bold and Powerful
Farther back sit the brass instruments — trumpets, trombones, French horns, and tubas. To play one, a musician buzzes their lips into a cup-shaped mouthpiece. That buzzing travels through coiled metal tubes and comes out big and bold.

Brass instruments can sound triumphant — proud and victorious, like a fanfare announcing a king. A fanfare is a short, loud burst of brass music, the kind you hear at the start of a royal ceremony. But brass can also play softly, like a faraway horn echoing across a valley.

items.prompt: In the section "The Brass Family: Bold and Powerful," why does the author compare a loud fanfare to a soft, faraway horn?

choices.text: To show that brass instruments can play in very different ways.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the author uses both a loud fanfare and a soft faraway horn as examples to show that brass instruments can make very different kinds of sounds.

choices.text: To show that brass instruments are the loudest in the orchestra.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the passage never compares brass to other instrument families or says brass is the loudest in the whole orchestra.

choices.text: To show that brass instruments only play music for royal kings.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because kings and royal ceremonies are just one example in the passage, and brass instruments are not limited to only royal music.

choices.text: To show that brass instruments are made out of long metal tubes.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the metal tubes describe how brass instruments are made, which has nothing to do with why the author compares loud and soft sounds.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
The Percussion Family: Keeping the Beat

At the very back stand the percussion instruments. Percussion means anything you strike, shake, or scrape. Drums, cymbals, triangles, and xylophones all belong here. A thundering bass drum can shake the whole concert hall. A tiny tap on a triangle adds a small sparkle of sound.

An orchestra depends on balance — every part at the right volume, like ingredients in a recipe. Too much of one thing ruins the whole dish. It's easy to think the loudest instruments matter the most, but that triangle sparkle matters just as much as the booming drum.
stimulus.content_text:
The Percussion Family: Keeping the Beat
At the very back stand the percussion instruments. Percussion means anything you strike, shake, or scrape. Drums, cymbals, triangles, and xylophones all belong here. A thundering bass drum can shake the whole concert hall. A tiny tap on a triangle adds a small sparkle of sound.

An orchestra depends on balance — every part at the right volume, like ingredients in a recipe. Too much of one thing ruins the whole dish. It's easy to think the loudest instruments matter the most, but that triangle sparkle matters just as much as the booming drum.

items.prompt: According to the text, what does the word 'percussion' mean?

choices.text: anything you play by blowing air into it
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because blowing air into an instrument describes wind instruments, and the passage never connects percussion to blowing air.

choices.text: anything you strike, shake, or scrape
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly states 'Percussion means anything you strike, shake, or scrape' — the text gives us the definition word for word!

choices.text: anything that makes a very loud sound
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage shows that percussion instruments can be very quiet, like the triangle that adds just a 'small sparkle of sound.'

choices.text: anything found at the back of a stage
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because standing at the back of the stage is just where percussion instruments are located in an orchestra, not what the word 'percussion' actually means.

items.title: Section 6 - Guiding Question 5

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
How to Listen Like a Musician

Next time you listen to an orchestra, start by finding the strings. They are usually the easiest to find because there are so many of them. Then listen for a woodwind floating above, or a brass instrument punching through. Finally, find the percussion holding the beat steady underneath.

Once you can pick out each family, you are no longer just hearing music. You are reading a story told by eighty people at once — and every single voice in it matters.
stimulus.content_text:
How to Listen Like a Musician
Next time you listen to an orchestra, start by finding the strings. They are usually the easiest to find because there are so many of them. Then listen for a woodwind floating above, or a brass instrument punching through. Finally, find the percussion holding the beat steady underneath.

Once you can pick out each family, you are no longer just hearing music. You are reading a story told by eighty people at once — and every single voice in it matters.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, why does the author suggest finding the strings first when listening to an orchestra?

choices.text: because strings play louder than all other instruments
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage never says strings are the loudest — it says they are easy to find because there are so many of them, not because of their volume.

choices.text: because strings are the largest group in the orchestra
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage says strings are easiest to find 'because there are so many of them,' which means they are the largest group in the orchestra.

choices.text: because strings always start playing before other instruments
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage never mentions anything about which instruments start playing first.

choices.text: because strings hold the beat steady for everyone else
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage says percussion holds the beat steady, not the strings.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: Based on the text, how is the sound of the flute different from the sound of the double bass?

choices.text: The flute has a deep rumble, while the double bass has a bright voice.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because it has the descriptions backwards — the deep rumble belongs to the double bass, and the bright voice belongs to the flute, not the other way around.

choices.text: The flute sounds bright and airy, while the double bass has a deep rumble.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly says the flute sounds 'bright and airy' and the double bass has a 'deep rumble you can almost feel in your chest.'

choices.text: The flute has a sharp nasal tone, while the double bass sounds smooth and warm.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because 'nasal' describes the oboe and 'smooth and warm' describes the clarinet — neither of those descriptions is used for the flute or double bass.

choices.text: The flute sounds loud and triumphant, while the double bass adds a small sparkle.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because 'triumphant' is used to describe brass instruments like the trumpet, and 'small sparkle' describes the triangle, not the flute or double bass.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: According to the article, why does the author say that a tiny triangle sparkle matters just as much as a booming drum in an orchestra?

choices.text: because the triangle is louder than most other percussion instruments
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the article describes the triangle as making a tiny, small sound — not a loud one.

choices.text: because an orchestra needs every part at the right volume to sound good
choices.feedback: This is correct because the article explains that balance means every part plays at the right volume, so even a quiet triangle matters just as much as a loud drum.

choices.text: because percussion instruments are more important than string instruments
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the article never compares percussion instruments to string instruments or says one family is more important than another.

choices.text: because the triangle plays the melody while the drum keeps the beat
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the article never mentions the triangle playing a melody or the drum keeping the beat — those ideas are not in the passage.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: The main idea of the text is that you can hear how the whole orchestra works together by listening to its different parts. What order of steps does the author give to help you do this?

choices.text: First find the vibrating strings, next listen for the floating woodwinds, and finally find the steady beat.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage says to first find the strings, next listen for a woodwind floating above, and finally find the percussion keeping the steady beat — exactly that order.

choices.text: First find the steady beat, next listen for the vibrating strings, and finally find the floating woodwinds.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because it starts with the steady beat, but the passage tells you to find the strings first, not the beat.

choices.text: First find the loudest instruments, next listen for the steady beat, and finally find the vibrating strings.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage never tells you to start with the loudest instruments — it actually says every instrument matters equally, not just the loud ones.

choices.text: First find the wooden baton, next listen for the vibrating strings, and finally find the floating woodwinds.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because a baton is the stick the conductor waves, not something you listen for when following along with the orchestra.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: To compare the two instrument families that share the similarity of making sound using tubes, which two headings should a reader look under?

choices.text: "The String Family" and "The Percussion Family"
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because strings make sound with vibrating strings and percussion makes sound by striking or shaking — neither one uses tubes.

choices.text: "The Woodwind Family" and "The Brass Family"
choices.feedback: This is correct because both woodwinds and brass instruments make sound when air moves through tubes, as described in both of those sections.

choices.text: "The String Family" and "The Woodwind Family"
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the string family makes sound through vibrating strings, not tubes, so it doesn't share that similarity with woodwinds.

choices.text: "The Brass Family" and "The Percussion Family"
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the percussion family makes sound by striking, shaking, or scraping instruments — not by using tubes.

syllabus_metadata.title: Meet the Orchestra: Families of Instruments

assessments.title: Each Voice Is Unique: Getting to Know Individual Instruments

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Picking Out Voices in the Orchestra

A Stage Full of Sounds

Twelve feet of brass tubing, coiled into a circle, sits on a musician's lap. She presses her lips to the mouthpiece and blows. Out comes a sound so warm and golden it fills the entire concert hall. That instrument is the French horn, and its voice sounds nothing like any other instrument on stage.

Three things help you tell instruments apart: pitch (how high or low a sound is), volume (how loud or soft), and timbre — the special color or quality of a sound, the way a whisper sounds different from a shout even at the same volume.
stimulus.content_text:
Picking Out Voices in the OrchestraA Stage Full of Sounds
Twelve feet of brass tubing, coiled into a circle, sits on a musician's lap. She presses her lips to the mouthpiece and blows. Out comes a sound so warm and golden it fills the entire concert hall. That instrument is the French horn, and its voice sounds nothing like any other instrument on stage.

Three things help you tell instruments apart: pitch (how high or low a sound is), volume (how loud or soft), and timbre — the special color or quality of a sound, the way a whisper sounds different from a shout even at the same volume.

items.prompt: According to the text, which detail shows a contrast between the French horn and other instruments?

choices.text: It is made of brass tubing.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because saying it is made of brass tubing describes what the instrument is made of, not how it sounds different from other instruments.

choices.text: Its voice sounds unlike any other instrument.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly says the French horn's voice 'sounds nothing like any other instrument,' which shows a contrast between it and the rest.

choices.text: It fills the entire concert hall.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because filling the concert hall describes how loud the sound is, but it does not compare the French horn to other instruments.

choices.text: It rests on a musician's lap.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because resting on a musician's lap describes how the instrument is held, not how it is different in sound from other instruments.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Strings That Sing

The violin is the smallest member of the string family. It can sing sweet, soaring melodies or race through quick runs of notes. Orchestras have more violins than any other instrument, so their bright voice often rises above the rest. Violinists sit right at the front, closest to you.

The cello is much bigger, and its deep, warm tone sits far below the violin's sparkle. Listening for that difference between the violin's brightness and the cello's warmth is your first step in picking out string voices.
stimulus.content_text:
Strings That Sing
The violin is the smallest member of the string family. It can sing sweet, soaring melodies or race through quick runs of notes. Orchestras have more violins than any other instrument, so their bright voice often rises above the rest. Violinists sit right at the front, closest to you.

The cello is much bigger, and its deep, warm tone sits far below the violin's sparkle. Listening for that difference between the violin's brightness and the cello's warmth is your first step in picking out string voices.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, why does the violin's voice often rise above the rest in an orchestra?

choices.text: because it is the smallest string instrument
choices.feedback: Being the smallest instrument tells us about its size, but the passage never says that's why its voice rises above the rest.

choices.text: because there are more violins than other instruments
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage directly says orchestras have more violins than any other instrument, and that is the reason their voice rises above the rest.

choices.text: because violinists sit closest to the audience
choices.feedback: Sitting closest to the audience tells us where violinists are located, but the passage doesn't say that's why their sound rises above the other instruments.

choices.text: because its tone is deeper and warmer than the cello's
choices.feedback: This is actually describing the cello, not the violin — the passage says the violin has brightness and sparkle, while the cello has a deep, warm tone.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
The Piano — Two Families in One

The piano is hard to place in just one family. Inside its case, small hammers covered in soft felt strike metal strings when you press the keys. Because it has strings, you could call it a string instrument. But because those strings are struck, you could also call it a percussion instrument — an instrument you hit, shake, or scrape to make sound, the way you hit a drum. The piano stretches from rumbling bass notes all the way up to sparkling high ones. That huge range is one reason it is one of the most popular instruments in the world.
stimulus.content_text:
The Piano — Two Families in One
The piano is hard to place in just one family. Inside its case, small hammers covered in soft felt strike metal strings when you press the keys. Because it has strings, you could call it a string instrument. But because those strings are struck, you could also call it a percussion instrument — an instrument you hit, shake, or scrape to make sound, the way you hit a drum. The piano stretches from rumbling bass notes all the way up to sparkling high ones. That huge range is one reason it is one of the most popular instruments in the world.

items.prompt: According to the text, what happens right after you press the piano keys?

choices.text: The metal strings make the keys move.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage says pressing the keys makes the hammers strike the strings — the strings don't make the keys move.

choices.text: The small hammers strike the metal strings.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage clearly states that 'small hammers covered in soft felt strike metal strings when you press the keys.'

choices.text: The soft felt covers the piano case.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the soft felt covers the hammers, not the piano case.

choices.text: The bass notes turn into high notes.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage mentions bass and high notes to describe the piano's wide range of sounds, not as something triggered by pressing a key.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Brass and Thunder

Think back to that French horn from the opening. If you could unroll all its tubing and stretch it straight, it would reach over twelve feet — longer than most cars. All that tubing gives the horn its rich, full sound. It can roar like a hero or hum like a lullaby.

Now picture the timpani, also called kettledrums — large copper drums shaped like giant bowls. You might think drums just make noise and can't play different notes. Here is the surprising part: a timpani player can press a foot pedal to tighten or loosen the drumhead. That changes the pitch, letting the drums play real melodies, not just beats. A deep timpani roll can make the whole concert hall shake under your feet.
stimulus.content_text:
Brass and Thunder
Think back to that French horn from the opening. If you could unroll all its tubing and stretch it straight, it would reach over twelve feet — longer than most cars. All that tubing gives the horn its rich, full sound. It can roar like a hero or hum like a lullaby.

Now picture the timpani, also called kettledrums — large copper drums shaped like giant bowls. You might think drums just make noise and can't play different notes. Here is the surprising part: a timpani player can press a foot pedal to tighten or loosen the drumhead. That changes the pitch, letting the drums play real melodies, not just beats. A deep timpani roll can make the whole concert hall shake under your feet.

items.prompt: Why does the author compare the French horn's tubing to a car?

choices.text: To show how loud the horn can sound
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the author is not talking about how loud the horn is when comparing it to a car — the comparison is only about length.

choices.text: To show how long the tubing can stretch
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly compares the tubing's length to a car to help you understand how long twelve feet really is.

choices.text: To show how fast the music can play
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage never mentions anything about how fast the music plays when describing the tubing.

choices.text: To show how heavy the horn can feel
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage does not say anything about how heavy the horn is when making the car comparison.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
How to Really Listen

You pick your best friend's voice out of a noisy lunchroom every day. An orchestra is the same game — just with violins, horns, and kettledrums instead of people. A bright trumpet can blast a fanfare — a bold, attention-grabbing call — right through the full orchestra. The growling tuba anchors the group with its deep bass notes. Shimmering cymbals crash at exactly the right moment.

Other voices are quieter but just as powerful. A gentle flute can float a delicate melody above everything else. Sometimes the quietest moment in a piece is the most powerful — a single flute, barely breathing a melody, can hold an entire audience perfectly still.

Soon you will not hear one big wall of sound. You will hear dozens of voices talking, answering, and telling a story together that no single instrument could ever tell alone.
stimulus.content_text:
How to Really Listen
You pick your best friend's voice out of a noisy lunchroom every day. An orchestra is the same game — just with violins, horns, and kettledrums instead of people. A bright trumpet can blast a fanfare — a bold, attention-grabbing call — right through the full orchestra. The growling tuba anchors the group with its deep bass notes. Shimmering cymbals crash at exactly the right moment.

Other voices are quieter but just as powerful. A gentle flute can float a delicate melody above everything else. Sometimes the quietest moment in a piece is the most powerful — a single flute, barely breathing a melody, can hold an entire audience perfectly still.

Soon you will not hear one big wall of sound. You will hear dozens of voices talking, answering, and telling a story together that no single instrument could ever tell alone.

items.prompt: According to the text, what is a fanfare?

choices.text: a soft, sleepy call
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage describes a fanfare as bold and attention-grabbing, which is the opposite of soft and sleepy.

choices.text: a bold, attention-grabbing call
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly defines a fanfare as 'a bold, attention-grabbing call' right after the word appears.

choices.text: a deep, rumbling call
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because deep and rumbling describes the tuba in the passage, not a fanfare.

choices.text: a slow, floating call
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because slow and floating describes the gentle flute melody, not a fanfare.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: How is the timpani different from most other drums, according to the article?

choices.text: It is made of brass tubing coiled into a circle
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because brass tubing coiled into a circle describes the French horn, not the timpani.

choices.text: It can change pitch to play real melodies
choices.feedback: B is correct because the article explains that a timpani player uses a foot pedal to change the pitch, allowing the drums to play real melodies.

choices.text: It is the loudest instrument in the orchestra
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the article never says the timpani is the loudest instrument in the orchestra.

choices.text: It is struck by small hammers covered in felt
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because small hammers covered in soft felt are found inside the piano, not the timpani.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: Under which heading should a reader look to find out what causes a drum to play different notes?

choices.text: "A Stage Full of Sounds"
choices.feedback: 'A Stage Full of Sounds' introduces the French horn and explains pitch, volume, and timbre, but it doesn't say anything about drums playing different notes.

choices.text: "Strings That Sing"
choices.feedback: 'Strings That Sing' is all about violins and cellos, not drums, so you won't find information about drum pitch there.

choices.text: "The Piano — Two Families in One"
choices.feedback: 'The Piano — Two Families in One' explains how the piano works and why it fits in two families, but it doesn't mention drums or how they change pitch.

choices.text: "Brass and Thunder"
choices.feedback: 'Brass and Thunder' is correct because it includes the part about timpani drums and explains that pressing a foot pedal changes the drumhead's tightness, which changes the pitch so the drums can play real melodies.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: Which choice best shows the order the article follows to teach the main idea about hearing different instrument voices?

choices.text: It starts with where players sit, then names loud instruments, then explains pitch.
choices.feedback: A is wrong because the article doesn't start with where players sit — that's just one small detail mentioned about violinists, not the beginning of the whole article.

choices.text: It starts with sound clues, then gives instrument examples, then shows how to listen.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the article starts with sound clues (pitch, volume, timbre), then gives instrument examples (violin, cello, piano, French horn, timpani), and ends with a section on how to really listen.

choices.text: It starts with quiet flutes, then explains drums, then ends with the French horn.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the article actually starts with the French horn, not quiet flutes, and the flute only appears near the very end.

choices.text: It starts with piano keys, then compares cars, then lists the string family.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the article doesn't start with piano keys — the piano comes in the middle, and comparing tubing to a car's length is just one small detail about the French horn.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: Based on the article, how are the piano and the timpani similar?

choices.text: They both are hit to make sound and can play different notes.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the article says both the piano (hammers striking strings) and the timpani (a drum you hit) make sound by being struck, and both can play different pitches or notes.

choices.text: They both use long brass tubing to make a rich, golden sound.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because long brass tubing describes the French horn, not the piano or the timpani.

choices.text: They both are small string instruments that sit at the front.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because small string instruments at the front describes the violin, not the piano or the timpani.

choices.text: They both use a foot pedal to tighten a large copper drumhead.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because only the timpani uses a foot pedal and a copper drumhead — the piano uses felt hammers and metal strings, not a drumhead or foot pedal.

syllabus_metadata.title: Each Voice Is Unique: Getting to Know Individual Instruments

assessments.title: Music That Tells a Story: Program Music

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Music That Paints Pictures

An Elephant Dances on Stage

A huge instrument groans out low, heavy notes. The audience grins because they can hear it — an elephant, dancing. No one said the word "elephant." No singer sang about one. But the music made everyone see the same big, slow animal.

Think of it as a movie with no dialogue — the sounds do all the talking. It is a perfect example of program music — instrumental music written to tell a story, describe a scene, or bring an idea to life. This moment comes from Carnival of the Animals, a piece written in 1886 by the French composer Camille Saint-Saëns.

You might think music without words can't really tell you anything specific. But Saint-Saëns proved otherwise. He used a double bass — the biggest, deepest string instrument in the orchestra, taller than most grown-ups — to play that lumbering elephant dance. Lumbering means moving in a slow, clumsy, heavy way, the way you might walk if you carried a full backpack on each shoulder. For the swan, he chose the cello, which sang a smooth, gliding melody like a bird drifting across a still lake. For the kangaroo, he wrote bouncy piano notes that hop up and down. Each section brings a different animal to life through sound alone.
stimulus.content_text:
Music That Paints PicturesAn Elephant Dances on Stage
A huge instrument groans out low, heavy notes. The audience grins because they can hear it — an elephant, dancing. No one said the word "elephant." No singer sang about one. But the music made everyone see the same big, slow animal.

Think of it as a movie with no dialogue — the sounds do all the talking. It is a perfect example of program music — instrumental music written to tell a story, describe a scene, or bring an idea to life. This moment comes from Carnival of the Animals, a piece written in 1886 by the French composer Camille Saint-Saëns.

You might think music without words can't really tell you anything specific. But Saint-Saëns proved otherwise. He used a double bass — the biggest, deepest string instrument in the orchestra, taller than most grown-ups — to play that lumbering elephant dance. Lumbering means moving in a slow, clumsy, heavy way, the way you might walk if you carried a full backpack on each shoulder. For the swan, he chose the cello, which sang a smooth, gliding melody like a bird drifting across a still lake. For the kangaroo, he wrote bouncy piano notes that hop up and down. Each section brings a different animal to life through sound alone.

items.prompt: According to the text, how is the elephant's music different from the swan's music?

choices.text: The elephant's music is slow and heavy, but the swan's music is smooth and gliding.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage says the elephant's music is 'lumbering' (slow and heavy) and the swan's music is 'smooth and gliding,' which matches this answer perfectly.

choices.text: The elephant's music is bouncy and quick, but the swan's music is low and deep.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because it gives the elephant bouncy and quick music, but the passage says the elephant's music is slow and heavy — that's the opposite!

choices.text: The elephant's music is smooth and gliding, but the swan's music is slow and heavy.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because it has the descriptions switched — the swan's music is smooth and gliding, and the elephant's music is slow and heavy, not the other way around.

choices.text: The elephant's music is high and light, but the swan's music is bouncy and quick.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the passage never says the elephant's music is high and light — the double bass makes low, deep, heavy sounds, not light ones.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
A Wolf Made of French Horns

Another famous piece of program music is Peter and the Wolf by the Russian composer Sergei Prokofiev. He wrote it in 1936 to help children learn the instruments of the orchestra. Every character gets its own instrument. Peter is the bright, cheerful strings. The bird is a flute, trilling high notes. The duck is an oboe, playing wobbly, quack-like sounds. The cat is a clarinet, sneaking along in smooth, slinky notes. And the wolf? Three French horns, playing dark, scary chords — groups of notes sounded together, like a team of voices speaking at once.

A narrator — a person who reads the story aloud — speaks while the orchestra plays. But here is the surprising part: even without the narrator, you could follow the action. The music itself tells you when the cat is creeping and when the wolf is near.
stimulus.content_text:
A Wolf Made of French Horns
Another famous piece of program music is Peter and the Wolf by the Russian composer Sergei Prokofiev. He wrote it in 1936 to help children learn the instruments of the orchestra. Every character gets its own instrument. Peter is the bright, cheerful strings. The bird is a flute, trilling high notes. The duck is an oboe, playing wobbly, quack-like sounds. The cat is a clarinet, sneaking along in smooth, slinky notes. And the wolf? Three French horns, playing dark, scary chords — groups of notes sounded together, like a team of voices speaking at once.

A narrator — a person who reads the story aloud — speaks while the orchestra plays. But here is the surprising part: even without the narrator, you could follow the action. The music itself tells you when the cat is creeping and when the wolf is near.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, why is Peter and the Wolf a good example of the main idea that music can tell a story?

choices.text: Because a narrator reads the whole story so the audience understands it
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage says that even without the narrator, the music itself tells you what is happening.

choices.text: Because each instrument sounds like its character and shows the action
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage shows how each instrument matches its character's sounds and actions, which is exactly how music tells a story.

choices.text: Because Sergei Prokofiev wrote it in Russia a long time ago
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the fact that Prokofiev was Russian and wrote it long ago has nothing to do with music telling a story.

choices.text: Because French horns are the loudest instruments in the orchestra
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage never says French horns are the loudest — it describes them as sounding dark and scary.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Not All Music Tells a Story

It is easy to think every piece of orchestra music is trying to paint a picture. Actually, much of it is not. Some music exists just to sound beautiful. This kind is called absolute music — music meant to be enjoyed for its melody, rhythm, and shape, without representing any story or scene. A symphony — a long piece for a full orchestra, usually in several parts — is often absolute music. Think of "Twinkle, Twinkle, Little Star" played by instruments. It sounds lovely, but it is not trying to show you a star. That is the idea behind absolute music.
stimulus.content_text:
Not All Music Tells a Story
It is easy to think every piece of orchestra music is trying to paint a picture. Actually, much of it is not. Some music exists just to sound beautiful. This kind is called absolute music — music meant to be enjoyed for its melody, rhythm, and shape, without representing any story or scene. A symphony — a long piece for a full orchestra, usually in several parts — is often absolute music. Think of "Twinkle, Twinkle, Little Star" played by instruments. It sounds lovely, but it is not trying to show you a star. That is the idea behind absolute music.

items.prompt: According to the passage, what does the author explain first before describing absolute music?

choices.text: that a symphony has several parts
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the author mentions that a symphony has several parts after already explaining what absolute music is, not before.

choices.text: that some music just sounds beautiful
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because saying some music just sounds beautiful is part of explaining absolute music, not something said before it.

choices.text: that orchestra music paints a picture
choices.feedback: C is correct because the very first sentence says it's easy to think orchestra music paints a picture, and that idea comes right before the author introduces absolute music.

choices.text: that instruments play lovely melodies
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because instruments playing lovely melodies is mentioned near the end of the passage as an example, not before the description of absolute music.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Your Imagination Fills the Rest

Here is something worth knowing: two people can listen to the same program music and picture different things. One person hears those bouncy piano notes and sees a kangaroo in a field. Another person sees a kangaroo on a city street. Soft, flowing strings might mean a peaceful river to one listener and something else entirely to another. The composer sets the mood and the motion. Your mind adds the details.

That is what makes program music so powerful. It does not need a single word to make you feel something real. A French horn can become a wolf. They are an invitation — and your imagination is the one who decides what to see.
stimulus.content_text:
Your Imagination Fills the Rest
Here is something worth knowing: two people can listen to the same program music and picture different things. One person hears those bouncy piano notes and sees a kangaroo in a field. Another person sees a kangaroo on a city street. Soft, flowing strings might mean a peaceful river to one listener and something else entirely to another. The composer sets the mood and the motion. Your mind adds the details.

That is what makes program music so powerful. It does not need a single word to make you feel something real. A French horn can become a wolf. They are an invitation — and your imagination is the one who decides what to see.

items.prompt: In the section 'Your Imagination Fills the Rest,' two people listen to the same bouncy piano notes but picture different scenes. How are these two listeners similar in what they do?

choices.text: They both imagine the exact same details.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage clearly shows the two listeners picture different scenes — one sees a city street and the other sees a field.

choices.text: They both use their imaginations to add details.
choices.feedback: B is correct because both listeners use their imaginations to add details to the music, just as the passage says 'Your mind adds the details.'

choices.text: They both prefer piano music over other instruments.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage never mentions which type of instrument either listener prefers.

choices.text: They both need words to understand the music.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage says program music 'does not need a single word to make you feel something real.'

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: Based on the article, how is program music different from absolute music?

choices.text: Program music features single instruments, while absolute music features a large full orchestra.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the article never says program music only uses single instruments — in fact, both Carnival of the Animals and Peter and the Wolf use a full orchestra.

choices.text: Program music tells a specific story, while absolute music exists just to sound beautiful.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the article clearly states that program music tells a story or describes a scene, while absolute music is meant to be enjoyed just for its melody and beauty, with no story attached.

choices.text: Program music is written to teach children, while absolute music is written for grown-ups.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because only Peter and the Wolf was written to teach children about instruments — that is not the definition of all program music, and the article never says absolute music is just for grown-ups.

choices.text: Program music uses a speaking narrator, while absolute music uses a singing voice.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because not all program music uses a narrator — Carnival of the Animals has no narrator at all, and the article even points out that Peter and the Wolf's music tells the story even without the narrator speaking.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: How does the section "Your Imagination Fills the Rest" add to what we learn in "An Elephant Dances on Stage"?

choices.text: It shows that the listener's mind causes the musical picture to be completed.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage says the composer sets the mood, but your mind adds the details — so the listener's imagination completes the musical picture.

choices.text: It shows that the heavy instruments cause the animal dance to sound clumsy.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the last section never talks about heavy instruments or clumsy dancing — that was only in the elephant section.

choices.text: It shows that the helpful narrator causes the orchestra story to make sense.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the narrator is from the Peter and the Wolf section, and the last section is about how listeners use their imagination, not about narrators.

choices.text: It shows that the famous composer causes the beautiful song to be written.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the last section is about what listeners picture in their minds, not about how or why a composer writes music.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: Which sequence of details does the author use to support the main idea that music can tell a story?

choices.text: First, by describing how instruments sound like animals, and later, by explaining how our minds add the final details.
