choices.feedback: A is correct because the author first describes how instruments sound like animals (like the elephant and wolf), and then later explains that your imagination adds the final details — which is exactly the order in the passage.

choices.text: First, by explaining how our minds add the final details, and later, by describing how instruments sound like animals.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because it has the order backwards — the author talks about instruments sounding like animals before explaining how our minds fill in the details.

choices.text: First, by showing how absolute music sounds beautiful, and later, by explaining why a narrator must read the story.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because absolute music is about NOT telling a story, and the narrator is described as a fun surprise, not something the music needs.

choices.text: First, by listing the different parts of a full symphony, and later, by describing how a movie uses spoken dialogue.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the article never lists the parts of a symphony, and it uses the movie comparison to help explain program music, not to talk about dialogue.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: Based on the article, how are the musical pieces Carnival of the Animals and Peter and the Wolf alike?

choices.text: Both use a narrator to read a story to the audience.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because only Peter and the Wolf uses a narrator — the article says Carnival of the Animals has no singer or narrator, just instruments.

choices.text: Both use special instruments to show different animals.
choices.feedback: B is correct because both pieces use specific instruments to represent different animals, like the double bass for the elephant and French horns for the wolf.

choices.text: Both use absolute music to create a beautiful melody.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because both pieces are program music, not absolute music — they are meant to tell stories and paint pictures, not just sound beautiful.

choices.text: Both use the exact same instruments to play their songs.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the two pieces use different instruments — for example, Carnival of the Animals features the double bass and cello, while Peter and the Wolf features the flute, oboe, and French horns.

syllabus_metadata.title: Music That Tells a Story: Program Music

assessments.title: Songs for Our Country: America's Patriotic Music

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Songs That Make a Nation Sing Together

A Flag in the Smoke

Bombs exploded over a fort all night long. Fire and smoke filled the dark sky. A man named Francis Scott Key stood on a ship in Baltimore Harbor, watching. It was 1814, and the United States was at war with Britain. British warships were attacking Fort McHenry, firing over and over to destroy it. Key could not tell if the American soldiers inside were still alive. Then morning came. Key looked through the clearing smoke and saw the American flag still flying above the fort. The soldiers had survived.

Key was so moved that he wrote a poem about that moment. You might think he also wrote the music. He didn't. A song has two parts: the lyrics, which are the words, and the melody, which is the tune you hum. Key only wrote the lyrics. His poem was later matched to a melody from an older British song. Together, the words and music became "The Star-Spangled Banner." Here's a surprise: it did not become the national anthem — the official song of the country — until 1931. That was more than a hundred years after Key wrote it.
stimulus.content_text:
Songs That Make a Nation Sing TogetherA Flag in the Smoke
Bombs exploded over a fort all night long. Fire and smoke filled the dark sky. A man named Francis Scott Key stood on a ship in Baltimore Harbor, watching. It was 1814, and the United States was at war with Britain. British warships were attacking Fort McHenry, firing over and over to destroy it. Key could not tell if the American soldiers inside were still alive. Then morning came. Key looked through the clearing smoke and saw the American flag still flying above the fort. The soldiers had survived.

Key was so moved that he wrote a poem about that moment. You might think he also wrote the music. He didn't. A song has two parts: the lyrics, which are the words, and the melody, which is the tune you hum. Key only wrote the lyrics. His poem was later matched to a melody from an older British song. Together, the words and music became "The Star-Spangled Banner." Here's a surprise: it did not become the national anthem — the official song of the country — until 1931. That was more than a hundred years after Key wrote it.

items.prompt: According to the text, how were the words and the tune of 'The Star-Spangled Banner' created differently?

choices.text: Key wrote the words, and British soldiers wrote the tune.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the tune did not come from British soldiers — it came from an older British song, which is very different from soldiers writing it.

choices.text: Key wrote the words, but the tune came from an older song.
choices.feedback: B is correct because Key wrote only the words (lyrics), and the tune (melody) was borrowed from an older British song that already existed.

choices.text: Key wrote the tune, but someone else wrote the words later.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because it has it backwards — Key wrote the words, not the tune, and the tune came from an older song, not from someone who wrote it later.

choices.text: Key wrote both the words and the tune at the same time.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because Key did not write both parts — the passage clearly says he only wrote the lyrics and did not write the music.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
A View from a Mountaintop

In 1893, a college professor named Katharine Lee Bates climbed to the top of Pikes Peak, a tall mountain in Colorado. She looked out and saw something that took her breath away. Golden wheat fields stretched below. Huge mountains rose up with a purple glow in the distance. The sky seemed to go on forever.

Bates wrote a poem about what she saw. Her poem described "spacious skies," meaning skies that look wide and open. It described "amber waves of grain" — amber means golden-yellow, and grain means crops like wheat. It described "purple mountain majesties," meaning mountains so grand they look like kings and queens of the earth. Her poem was later matched to a melody composed by Samuel A. Ward. The words and music came from two different people, but they fit together perfectly. The song became "America the Beautiful."
stimulus.content_text:
A View from a Mountaintop
In 1893, a college professor named Katharine Lee Bates climbed to the top of Pikes Peak, a tall mountain in Colorado. She looked out and saw something that took her breath away. Golden wheat fields stretched below. Huge mountains rose up with a purple glow in the distance. The sky seemed to go on forever.

Bates wrote a poem about what she saw. Her poem described "spacious skies," meaning skies that look wide and open. It described "amber waves of grain" — amber means golden-yellow, and grain means crops like wheat. It described "purple mountain majesties," meaning mountains so grand they look like kings and queens of the earth. Her poem was later matched to a melody composed by Samuel A. Ward. The words and music came from two different people, but they fit together perfectly. The song became "America the Beautiful."

items.prompt: Based on the passage, what caused Katharine Lee Bates to write her poem?

choices.text: Because she wanted to teach her students about crops.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage never mentions Bates wanting to teach anyone about crops — she wrote the poem because of the beautiful view she saw.

choices.text: Because she saw a beautiful view from a mountain.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage clearly says she saw a breathtaking view from the top of Pikes Peak, and that is what inspired her to write her poem.

choices.text: Because she heard a wonderful melody by Samuel Ward.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because Samuel Ward composed the melody after Bates wrote her poem — the music and words came from two different people at different times.

choices.text: Because she wanted to meet kings and queens of the earth.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because 'kings and queens of the earth' was just a poetic description of the grand mountains, not real royalty she wanted to meet.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
A Borrowed Tune

One of the oldest patriotic songs in America has a funny secret. "My Country, 'Tis of Thee" uses the exact same melody as "God Save the King," the national anthem of Britain. In 1831, a man named Samuel Francis Smith heard the British tune and liked it. So he kept the melody but wrote brand-new lyrics about American freedom and liberty — the right of people to live and choose freely. Two different songs, two different countries, one shared tune. This shows you something interesting: a melody can carry very different meanings depending on the words you pair with it.
stimulus.content_text:
A Borrowed Tune
One of the oldest patriotic songs in America has a funny secret. "My Country, 'Tis of Thee" uses the exact same melody as "God Save the King," the national anthem of Britain. In 1831, a man named Samuel Francis Smith heard the British tune and liked it. So he kept the melody but wrote brand-new lyrics about American freedom and liberty — the right of people to live and choose freely. Two different songs, two different countries, one shared tune. This shows you something interesting: a melody can carry very different meanings depending on the words you pair with it.

items.prompt: According to the passage, what did Samuel Francis Smith do first before he wrote new lyrics about American freedom?

choices.text: He created a brand-new melody for the song.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because Smith did not make a brand-new melody — he liked the British tune and kept it.

choices.text: He heard the British tune and liked it.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage says Smith heard the British tune and liked it before he wrote any new words.

choices.text: He shared his song with people in Britain.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage never mentions Smith sharing his song with anyone in Britain.

choices.text: He wrote the words to "God Save the King."
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because Smith wrote the lyrics to the American song, not to 'God Save the King.'

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Why These Songs Still Matter

Picture a baseball stadium packed with thousands of people. The first notes of "The Star-Spangled Banner" play, and every single person stands up. Strangers take off their hats. For a few minutes, all those different people share the same song.

That is what patriotic songs — songs that express love for one's country — actually do. They give people a way to feel connected. Americans have sung these songs at joyful celebrations and at sad memorials, which are events held to remember people who have died. The songs carry real stories inside them: a flag surviving a battle, a view from a mountaintop, a melody borrowed and made new.

You are not just singing words. You are singing someone's true story — a moment when one person saw or felt something so powerful, they had to turn it into music.
stimulus.content_text:
Why These Songs Still Matter

That is what patriotic songs — songs that express love for one's country — actually do. They give people a way to feel connected. Americans have sung these songs at joyful celebrations and at sad memorials, which are events held to remember people who have died. The songs carry real stories inside them: a flag surviving a battle, a view from a mountaintop, a melody borrowed and made new.

You are not just singing words. You are singing someone's true story — a moment when one person saw or felt something so powerful, they had to turn it into music.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, how are the different people at the baseball stadium alike when the song begins?

choices.text: They all play in the big game.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage describes thousands of people in the stands watching, not playing in the game.

choices.text: They all share the same music.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly says 'all those different people share the same song,' which is what makes them alike.

choices.text: They all sing the words loudly.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage only mentions people standing and removing their hats — it never says they all sing loudly.

choices.text: They all know the true stories.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage doesn't tell us that the people in the stadium know the true stories; it just says the songs carry those stories inside them.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: Based on the text, how was the reason Francis Scott Key wrote his poem different from the reason Katharine Lee Bates wrote hers?

choices.text: Key watched a dark battle, but Bates saw a beautiful mountain.
choices.feedback: A is correct because Key was moved by a frightening battle scene, while Bates was inspired by a breathtaking, beautiful view from a mountaintop.

choices.text: Key wrote the song's tune, but Bates only wrote the poem's words.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because neither Key nor Bates wrote the melody — both of them only wrote the words (lyrics) to their poems.

choices.text: Key borrowed a British song, but Bates borrowed an American song.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because it was Samuel Francis Smith who borrowed a British tune, not Key or Bates.

choices.text: Key sang at a baseball game, but Bates sang at a sad memorial.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the baseball game and memorials are examples from the last section about where people sing these songs today, not reasons why Key or Bates wrote their poems.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: Based on the article, what caused both "The Star-Spangled Banner" and "My Country, 'Tis of Thee" to become complete songs?

choices.text: The writers matched their new poems to older British melodies.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the article says both songs were completed by matching new poems to older British melodies — Key's words were paired with a British tune, and Smith kept a British melody but wrote new American lyrics.

choices.text: The writers watched brave soldiers fight in long, dark battles.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because watching soldiers fight in a battle is only part of Francis Scott Key's story, and it has nothing to do with how 'My Country, 'Tis of Thee' became a complete song.

choices.text: The writers climbed tall mountains to look at beautiful views.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because climbing a mountain and seeing a beautiful view is the story behind 'America the Beautiful,' not 'The Star-Spangled Banner' or 'My Country, 'Tis of Thee.'

choices.text: The writers wanted to create official anthems for the country.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the article tells us 'The Star-Spangled Banner' didn't become the official national anthem until 1931, more than 100 years after it was written, so that was not the writers' original goal.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: Under which two headings can you find songs where the words were written first and the music was added later?

choices.text: "A Flag in the Smoke" and "A Borrowed Tune"
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because 'A Borrowed Tune' is actually the opposite — the music came first, and then new words were written to go with it.

choices.text: "A View from a Mountaintop" and "A Borrowed Tune"
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because 'A Borrowed Tune' doesn't fit, since Samuel Francis Smith heard the tune first and wrote the words second, not the other way around.

choices.text: "A Flag in the Smoke" and "A View from a Mountaintop"
choices.feedback: C is correct because both 'A Flag in the Smoke' and 'A View from a Mountaintop' clearly show that a poem was written first, and the melody was added later.

choices.text: "A View from a Mountaintop" and "Why These Songs Still Matter"
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because 'Why These Songs Still Matter' only talks about why patriotic songs are important — it doesn't describe how any song was created.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: Which statement best tells the main idea that both the battle story and the mountain story help explain?

choices.text: Patriotic songs come from real moments people wanted to share.
choices.feedback: A is correct because both Key's battle story and Bates's mountain story show that patriotic songs grew out of powerful real moments that people felt they had to share.

choices.text: Patriotic songs are mostly about places people visited alone.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the stories focus on the powerful feelings those moments created and the songs that came from them, not on the fact that the people were alone.

choices.text: Patriotic songs were written only to become national songs.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because neither Key nor Bates set out to write a national song — they just wrote poems about something that moved them.

choices.text: Patriotic songs sound the same because they use old tunes.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because 'The Star-Spangled Banner' and 'America the Beautiful' use different melodies, so the two stories don't support this idea at all.

syllabus_metadata.title: Songs for Our Country: America's Patriotic Music

assessments.title: Songs of the People: Folk Music and Rounds

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Songs That Nobody Wrote

Passed from Voice to Voice

A grandmother in the mountains of Virginia rocks a baby on her lap. She sings a song her own grandmother sang to her, long ago. That song has lived for over a hundred years — carried only by human voices.

Songs like this are called folk songs — traditional songs that belong to a whole community, not to one songwriter. Folk songs are some of the oldest and most cherished music in the world. You might think every song starts with one famous person who writes it, the way pop songs work today. But most folk songs are so old that nobody knows who made them up first. People learned them by ear. One person heard a song, remembered it, and taught it to someone else. A generation — all the people born around the same time — would pass the song to the next generation, the way you might pass a ball down a line of friends.

Over time, the songs grew into many different versions. You did not need to be a professional musician to sing a folk song.
stimulus.content_text:
Songs That Nobody WrotePassed from Voice to Voice
A grandmother in the mountains of Virginia rocks a baby on her lap. She sings a song her own grandmother sang to her, long ago. That song has lived for over a hundred years — carried only by human voices.

Songs like this are called folk songs — traditional songs that belong to a whole community, not to one songwriter. Folk songs are some of the oldest and most cherished music in the world. You might think every song starts with one famous person who writes it, the way pop songs work today. But most folk songs are so old that nobody knows who made them up first. People learned them by ear. One person heard a song, remembered it, and taught it to someone else. A generation — all the people born around the same time — would pass the song to the next generation, the way you might pass a ball down a line of friends.

Over time, the songs grew into many different versions. You did not need to be a professional musician to sing a folk song.

items.prompt: According to the text, how are folk songs different from pop songs today?

choices.text: Folk songs are written by one famous person
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage says pop songs today are written by one famous person — folk songs are the opposite of that.

choices.text: Nobody knows who first made up folk songs
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly says 'nobody knows who made them up first,' which is what makes folk songs different from pop songs.

choices.text: Folk songs are newer than pop songs today
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage says folk songs are some of the oldest music in the world, making them much older than today's pop songs.

choices.text: Folk songs can only be sung by professionals
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage clearly states that you did not need to be a professional musician to sing a folk song.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Songs from Many Lives

American folk songs tell the stories of many different groups of people. Pioneer songs described the hard times and big hopes of families moving west. Railroad songs honored the workers who laid tracks across the continent — the huge stretch of land from the Atlantic to the Pacific. Cowboy songs painted pictures of life on the open range. Immigrant songs carried the sadness and dreams of people who left their home countries to start over in America. Communities treasured these songs the way a family treasures an old photograph.
stimulus.content_text:
Songs from Many Lives
American folk songs tell the stories of many different groups of people. Pioneer songs described the hard times and big hopes of families moving west. Railroad songs honored the workers who laid tracks across the continent — the huge stretch of land from the Atlantic to the Pacific. Cowboy songs painted pictures of life on the open range. Immigrant songs carried the sadness and dreams of people who left their home countries to start over in America. Communities treasured these songs the way a family treasures an old photograph.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, what is the most likely reason why immigrant songs carried sadness?

choices.text: The people had to leave their home countries behind.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage directly tells us immigrants left their home countries, and leaving behind everything you know is a reason to feel sad.

choices.text: The people had to lay tracks across the continent.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because laying tracks across the continent was the work of railroad workers, not immigrants.

choices.text: The people faced hard times living on the open range.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because living on the open range was part of cowboy life, not immigrant life.

choices.text: The people lost their treasured family photographs.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because the passage uses family photographs as a comparison to explain how communities valued songs — it never says immigrants lost their photos.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Singing in a Round

Here is one of the most fun things you can do with a folk song: sing it as a round. A round is a song where two or more groups sing the exact same melody, but each group starts at a different time. Think of "Row, Row, Row Your Boat." Group one begins singing. After a few measures — short, equal sections of music — group two starts the same song from the very beginning.

You might think the groups are singing different songs. They are not. Everyone sings the same tune. But because the groups are at different places in the melody, the voices overlap. That overlapping creates harmony, which means sounds that blend together in a pleasing way. Just staggering the same melody is enough to make the music sound rich and full.
stimulus.content_text:
Singing in a Round
Here is one of the most fun things you can do with a folk song: sing it as a round. A round is a song where two or more groups sing the exact same melody, but each group starts at a different time. Think of "Row, Row, Row Your Boat." Group one begins singing. After a few measures — short, equal sections of music — group two starts the same song from the very beginning.

You might think the groups are singing different songs. They are not. Everyone sings the same tune. But because the groups are at different places in the melody, the voices overlap. That overlapping creates harmony, which means sounds that blend together in a pleasing way. Just staggering the same melody is enough to make the music sound rich and full.

items.prompt: According to the text, what happens right after group one begins singing a round?

choices.text: Both groups stop and start over together.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage never says the groups stop — they keep singing at different times, which is what makes it a round.

choices.text: Group two starts the same song from the beginning.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly states that after group one begins, group two starts the same song from the very beginning.

choices.text: Group two sings a completely different melody.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage says 'everyone sings the same tune,' not different melodies.

choices.text: Group one waits for group two to catch up.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because group one never waits — both groups keep moving forward through the song at their own starting points.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
What Is a Canon?

A canon is a close cousin of the round. In a canon, the second group also starts later than the first. But a canon can be trickier. The second voice might sing the melody at a different pitch — how high or low a sound is. The second voice might also make small changes to the tune. These layers create interesting musical textures, meaning the way the music feels to your ears — smooth or bumpy, thick or thin.
stimulus.content_text:
What Is a Canon?
A canon is a close cousin of the round. In a canon, the second group also starts later than the first. But a canon can be trickier. The second voice might sing the melody at a different pitch — how high or low a sound is. The second voice might also make small changes to the tune. These layers create interesting musical textures, meaning the way the music feels to your ears — smooth or bumpy, thick or thin.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, how is a canon different from a round?

choices.text: A canon has groups that all start singing at the same time
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage says the second group starts later than the first, not at the same time.

choices.text: A canon lets the second voice change the pitch or tune
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly says the second voice can sing at a different pitch or make small changes to the tune.

choices.text: A canon uses only one voice to sing the whole melody
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because a canon has multiple voices singing together, not just one voice alone.

choices.text: A canon always repeats the melody in the exact same way
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage explains that the second voice can change the pitch or tune, so it does not always repeat the melody exactly.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Music That Belongs to You

Rounds and canons need no instruments. They need no stage. They just need a few voices and a song everyone knows. That is the heart of folk music — ordinary people making something beautiful together. The next time you sing "Row, Row, Row Your Boat" with your friends, listen to the moment when the voices overlap. You are doing exactly what people have done for hundreds of years: keeping a song alive by opening your mouth and letting it out.
stimulus.content_text:
Music That Belongs to You
Rounds and canons need no instruments. They need no stage. They just need a few voices and a song everyone knows. That is the heart of folk music — ordinary people making something beautiful together. The next time you sing "Row, Row, Row Your Boat" with your friends, listen to the moment when the voices overlap. You are doing exactly what people have done for hundreds of years: keeping a song alive by opening your mouth and letting it out.

items.prompt: According to the text, what is the "heart of folk music"?

choices.text: Ordinary people making something beautiful together.
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage uses these exact words: 'ordinary people making something beautiful together' to describe the heart of folk music.

choices.text: Famous singers performing a song that nobody knows.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because the passage says folk music is for ordinary people singing songs everyone knows — not famous singers performing unknown songs.

choices.text: Quiet audiences listening to a very old performance.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because folk music is about everyone joining in and singing together, not about quiet audiences watching a performance.

choices.text: Trained musicians playing instruments on a big stage.
choices.feedback: This is wrong because the passage clearly says rounds and canons need no instruments and no stage — the opposite of what answer D describes.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: Based on the text, how is a canon different from a round?

choices.text: A canon can change the tune, but a round keeps the exact same tune.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage says a round keeps the exact same melody, but a canon can change the pitch or make small changes to the tune.

choices.text: A canon needs many instruments, but a round only uses human voices.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the passage says both rounds and canons need no instruments — just voices.

choices.text: A canon is sung by one person, but a round is sung by many people.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because both rounds and canons involve multiple groups of singers, not just one person.

choices.text: A canon starts at the same time, but a round starts a little later.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because it has it backwards — in both rounds and canons, the second group starts later than the first, not at the same time.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: Why does singing a folk song as a round cause the music to sound rich and full?

choices.text: Because each group sings a different melody at the same time
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage clearly says everyone sings the same tune in a round, not different melodies.

choices.text: Because the groups start at different times and their voices overlap
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage explains that groups start at different times, their voices overlap, and that overlapping creates harmony that makes the music sound rich and full.

choices.text: Because folk songs have been changed many times over the years
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because songs changing over many years is about how folk songs are passed down, not about what makes a round sound full.

choices.text: Because the singers use instruments to add extra sounds
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage says rounds and canons need no instruments — the rich sound comes from voices alone.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: Which two headings show where to read about the order in which groups start singing?

choices.text: "Passed from Voice to Voice" and "Songs from Many Lives"
choices.feedback: Both 'Passed from Voice to Voice' and 'Songs from Many Lives' are about the history and stories behind folk songs, not about the order groups start singing.

choices.text: "Songs from Many Lives" and "Singing in a Round"
choices.feedback: 'Songs from Many Lives' talks about pioneer, railroad, and cowboy songs — not about when singing groups start — so it doesn't belong with the correct answer.

choices.text: "Singing in a Round" and "What Is a Canon?"
choices.feedback: This is correct because both 'Singing in a Round' and 'What Is a Canon?' explain that the second group starts singing later than the first group.

choices.text: "Passed from Voice to Voice" and "What Is a Canon?"
choices.feedback: 'Passed from Voice to Voice' is about how folk songs were passed down through generations, not about the order groups start singing.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: What do folk songs and rounds have in common that shows the main idea of the passage?

choices.text: They both need famous pop musicians to write the new songs.
choices.feedback: A is wrong because the passage says folk songs have no known songwriter and are different from pop songs made by famous musicians.

choices.text: They both use ordinary people singing with just their voices.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage says both folk songs and rounds need only ordinary people singing with their voices — no instruments or famous musicians needed.

choices.text: They both tell sad stories about families moving to new lands.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because while some folk songs tell sad stories, rounds are about overlapping voices, not sad stories.

choices.text: They both change the high and low sounds of the exact melody.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because changing the pitch of a melody is something canons do, not rounds, and it has nothing to do with folk songs.

syllabus_metadata.title: Songs of the People: Folk Music and Rounds

assessments.title: Songs of Hope and Freedom: The Spirituals

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Songs with Secrets: The Story of Spirituals

Voices Rising from the Fields

Picture a group of people working under a hot sun, with no freedom and no pay. One person lifts a voice and begins to sing. Then another voice joins in, and another. The song fills the air like something alive. These were spirituals — songs created by enslaved African Americans that carried faith, sorrow, hope, and sometimes hidden messages inside their words.
stimulus.content_text:
Songs with Secrets: The Story of SpiritualsVoices Rising from the Fields
Picture a group of people working under a hot sun, with no freedom and no pay. One person lifts a voice and begins to sing. Then another voice joins in, and another. The song fills the air like something alive. These were spirituals — songs created by enslaved African Americans that carried faith, sorrow, hope, and sometimes hidden messages inside their words.

items.prompt: According to the text, the spirituals carried sorrow, but they also carried which opposite feeling?

choices.text: The feeling of anger
choices.feedback: Anger is incorrect because the passage never says the spirituals carried anger.

choices.text: The feeling of hope
choices.feedback: Hope is correct because the passage directly says spirituals carried 'sorrow, hope' — and hope is the opposite of sorrow.

choices.text: The feeling of fear
choices.feedback: Fear is incorrect because the passage does not mention fear as something the spirituals carried.

choices.text: The feeling of pain
choices.feedback: Pain is incorrect because even though the people were suffering, the passage does not list pain as one of the things the spirituals carried.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Music They Brought with Them

You might think enslaved people simply copied the music they heard in America. Actually, they were brilliant inventors. They arrived carrying rich musical traditions from Africa — traditions hundreds of years old. African music used call and response, a style where a leader sings a line and the group sings it back. Imagine your teacher saying "Good morning!" and your whole class answering "Good morning!" at the same time. That's call and response. African music also featured layered rhythms and powerful, emotional voices.

Enslaved people blended these African traditions with Christian hymns they heard in America. Spirituals didn't belong to Africa or to Europe.
stimulus.content_text:
Music They Brought with Them
You might think enslaved people simply copied the music they heard in America. Actually, they were brilliant inventors. They arrived carrying rich musical traditions from Africa — traditions hundreds of years old. African music used call and response, a style where a leader sings a line and the group sings it back. Imagine your teacher saying "Good morning!" and your whole class answering "Good morning!" at the same time. That's call and response. African music also featured layered rhythms and powerful, emotional voices.
Enslaved people blended these African traditions with Christian hymns they heard in America. Spirituals didn't belong to Africa or to Europe.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, what caused spirituals to become a new kind of music?

choices.text: Enslaved people copied the Christian hymns they heard in American churches
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage clearly says enslaved people did NOT just copy music — they were inventors who brought their own rich traditions with them.

choices.text: Enslaved people mixed African musical traditions with American hymns
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage says enslaved people 'blended' African musical traditions with Christian hymns, creating something new that belonged to neither Africa nor Europe.

choices.text: Enslaved people replaced European singing styles with African rhythms
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because enslaved people didn't replace one style with another — they combined both African and European elements together.

choices.text: Enslaved people forgot their African traditions after arriving in America
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage says the opposite: enslaved people arrived carrying African musical traditions that were hundreds of years old and kept using them.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Hidden Meanings in the Music

On the outside, they sounded like religious songs about heaven and Bible stories. But inside the words, singers hid messages about escaping slavery.

Many historians believe the song Follow the Drinking Gourd contained coded directions for running away to freedom. The "drinking gourd" meant the Big Dipper, a constellation — a group of stars that forms a shape in the night sky, like connect-the-dots made of light. The Big Dipper points toward the North Star. By following that star, enslaved people could travel north toward free states. Some historians debate whether this story is fully true, but the song has been passed down for generations.

Other spirituals used phrases like "crossing the Jordan River" to secretly mean crossing into free territory. Wade in the Water may have held practical advice — walking through streams could wash away a person's scent so tracking dogs lost the trail.
stimulus.content_text:
Hidden Meanings in the Music
On the outside, they sounded like religious songs about heaven and Bible stories. But inside the words, singers hid messages about escaping slavery.
Many historians believe the song Follow the Drinking Gourd contained coded directions for running away to freedom. The "drinking gourd" meant the Big Dipper, a constellation — a group of stars that forms a shape in the night sky, like connect-the-dots made of light. The Big Dipper points toward the North Star. By following that star, enslaved people could travel north toward free states. Some historians debate whether this story is fully true, but the song has been passed down for generations.
Other spirituals used phrases like "crossing the Jordan River" to secretly mean crossing into free territory. Wade in the Water may have held practical advice — walking through streams could wash away a person's scent so tracking dogs lost the trail.

items.prompt: According to the passage, what was the order of steps enslaved people followed to travel toward free states?

choices.text: They crossed the Jordan River, then walked through streams, then looked at the sky.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because crossing the Jordan River and walking through streams were separate ideas in the passage, not steps in a single journey toward the North Star.

choices.text: They found the Big Dipper, then located the North Star, then traveled north.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage says the Big Dipper points to the North Star, and following the North Star led enslaved people north toward freedom — exactly that order.

choices.text: They traveled north, then found the North Star, then looked for the Big Dipper.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because it reverses the order — people had to find the Big Dipper and North Star first before they could travel north.

choices.text: They located the North Star, then sang spirituals, then found the Big Dipper.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage never says singing spirituals was a step between locating the North Star and finding the Big Dipper.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
More Than Sadness

It's easy to think spirituals were only about pain. But many of these songs burst with strength and joy. Swing Low, Sweet Chariot expressed a longing to be carried to a better place. Go Down, Moses told the Bible story of Moses leading his people out of slavery in Egypt. It was also a bold message: African Americans believed they would be free one day, too.

When voices joined in a spiritual, people felt less alone. The songs were like a shared heartbeat, keeping hope alive even in terrible times.
stimulus.content_text:
More Than Sadness
It's easy to think spirituals were only about pain. But many of these songs burst with strength and joy. Swing Low, Sweet Chariot expressed a longing to be carried to a better place. Go Down, Moses told the Bible story of Moses leading his people out of slavery in Egypt. It was also a bold message: African Americans believed they would be free one day, too.
When voices joined in a spiritual, people felt less alone. The songs were like a shared heartbeat, keeping hope alive even in terrible times.

items.prompt: How are Swing Low, Sweet Chariot and Go Down, Moses alike?

choices.text: Both songs were only about sadness.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage actually says these songs 'burst with strength and joy,' not just sadness.

choices.text: Both songs shared hope for a better future.
choices.feedback: B is correct because both songs expressed a longing and belief for a better, freer future, which is a message of hope.

choices.text: Both songs told the same Bible story.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because only 'Go Down, Moses' told the Bible story — 'Swing Low, Sweet Chariot' was about longing for a better place.

choices.text: Both songs made people feel more alone.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage says when people sang spirituals together, they felt less alone, not more alone.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
A Sound That Kept Growing

Spirituals did not fade away after slavery ended. Their sound kept spreading into new kinds of music. Gospel music — joyful, powerful singing you can hear in churches across America — grew straight from spirituals. Listen to a gospel choir clap and sway, and you are hearing the echo of those first spiritual singers.

The blues, with its deep feeling and raw emotion, has roots in spirituals too. And jazz, with its layered rhythms and harmonies — notes sung or played together that blend into a richer sound, like mixing colors to make a new one — was also shaped by the spirit of these songs.
stimulus.content_text:
A Sound That Kept Growing
Spirituals did not fade away after slavery ended. Their sound kept spreading into new kinds of music. Gospel music — joyful, powerful singing you can hear in churches across America — grew straight from spirituals. Listen to a gospel choir clap and sway, and you are hearing the echo of those first spiritual singers.
The blues, with its deep feeling and raw emotion, has roots in spirituals too. And jazz, with its layered rhythms and harmonies — notes sung or played together that blend into a richer sound, like mixing colors to make a new one — was also shaped by the spirit of these songs.

items.prompt: According to the passage, what are harmonies?

choices.text: songs that are sung very loudly in churches
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because singing loudly in churches is how the passage describes gospel music, not harmonies.

choices.text: notes played together that blend into a richer sound
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly defines harmonies as 'notes sung or played together that blend into a richer sound.'

choices.text: rhythms that make people want to clap and sway
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because clapping and swaying is how the passage describes gospel choir singers, not harmonies.

choices.text: feelings of deep sadness found in blues music
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because deep feeling and raw emotion is how the passage describes the blues, not harmonies.

items.title: Section 6 - Guiding Question 5

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
Still Singing Today

People recognize them as masterpieces of American music. But here is what matters most: enslaved people had almost everything taken from them. Yet they created an entirely new kind of music so powerful that the whole world still sings it. Spirituals are proof that no one can take away a person's voice.
stimulus.content_text:
Still Singing Today
People recognize them as masterpieces of American music. But here is what matters most: enslaved people had almost everything taken from them. Yet they created an entirely new kind of music so powerful that the whole world still sings it. Spirituals are proof that no one can take away a person's voice.

items.prompt: Which detail from the passage best supports the idea that spirituals are powerful music?

choices.text: People had almost everything taken from them.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because it describes what was taken from enslaved people, not why the music itself is powerful.

choices.text: The whole world still sings spirituals today.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage says the whole world still sings spirituals today, which is strong proof that the music is powerful enough to last and spread everywhere.

choices.text: Spirituals are recognized as American music.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because being recognized as American music tells us where the music is from, not how powerful it is.

choices.text: Enslaved people created a new kind of music.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because it tells us the music was new and different, but it doesn't show us that the music is powerful.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: Based on the article, how does the author contrast what spirituals seemed to be on the outside with what they really were?

choices.text: They seemed to be simple religious songs about pain, but they actually held secret messages and joy.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the article says spirituals looked like simple religious songs on the outside, but secretly held hidden messages about freedom and were also filled with strength and joy.

choices.text: They seemed to be quiet tunes for working in fields, but they actually held loud warnings for owners.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because spirituals were not quiet working tunes meant to warn owners — they carried hidden messages of hope and escape for the enslaved people singing them.

choices.text: They seemed to be copied from American music, but they actually came straight from European churches.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the article says enslaved people did NOT copy American music — they blended their own rich African musical traditions with Christian hymns they heard in America.

choices.text: They seemed to be only about the night sky, but they actually gave directions for finding clean water.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the night sky and the Big Dipper were used as secret directions to travel north toward freedom, not as a way to find clean water.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: Why does the author include definitions for special words throughout the article?

choices.text: To show which words came from African languages
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the bolded words like 'harmonies' and 'constellation' are music and vocabulary terms, not words from African languages.

choices.text: To help readers understand important music ideas
choices.feedback: B is correct because the author bolds important terms like 'call and response' and 'harmonies' and immediately explains what they mean, helping readers understand key music ideas.

choices.text: To prove that spirituals were better than hymns
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the author never compares spirituals and hymns or says one is better than the other.

choices.text: To list every type of music mentioned in the article
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because only certain important words are bolded and defined, not every single type of music mentioned in the article.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: Which sequence of events best tells the main story of spirituals in the article?

choices.text: First, enslaved people created spirituals, next they used them to find hope, and finally the songs became new music.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the article first shows spirituals being created, then explains how they gave people hope and carried hidden messages, and finally describes how they grew into gospel, blues, and jazz.

choices.text: First, spirituals became new types of music, next enslaved people found hope, and finally they created the songs.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because it has the order backwards — the songs had to be created first before they could become new types of music or help people find hope.

choices.text: First, enslaved people looked at the stars, next they walked through streams, and finally they crossed the river.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because looking at stars, walking through streams, and crossing rivers are just small details about escaping, not the main story the whole article is telling.

choices.text: First, enslaved people learned American hymns, next they traveled to Africa, and finally they created jazz and blues.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because enslaved people never traveled to Africa — they already carried African musical traditions with them, and the article never says they made that journey.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: Based on the article, how are the songs "Follow the Drinking Gourd" and "Go Down, Moses" alike?

choices.text: They both shared a message about finding freedom.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the article shows that both songs shared a message about freedom — one with escape directions and one with a bold promise of freedom someday.

choices.text: They both gave secret directions to the North Star.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because only 'Follow the Drinking Gourd' talked about the North Star — 'Go Down, Moses' was about the Bible story of Moses, not star directions.

choices.text: They both told people to walk through the water.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because walking through water was connected to 'Wade in the Water,' not these two songs.

choices.text: They both grew into a new style called gospel.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the article says gospel music grew from spirituals in general, not specifically from these two songs.

syllabus_metadata.title: Songs of Hope and Freedom: The Spirituals

assessments.title: Chants and Hymns: Music in Medieval Monasteries

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
The Monks Who Sang All Day

Voices Before Dawn

The sky is black. The air is cold. Inside a stone building, a group of monks opens their mouths and begins to sing. Their voices blend together and bounce off the tall stone walls. The sound fills every corner of the room. This is how every single day begins.

These monks lived in monasteries — buildings where religious men lived, worked, and prayed together, like a school and a church combined into one home. They lived during medieval times, the period in European history roughly between the years 500 and 1500. And they sang constantly. They sang before sunrise. They sang at noon. They sang at sunset and again before bed. The music they sang has a name: Gregorian chant.
stimulus.content_text:
The Monks Who Sang All DayVoices Before Dawn
The sky is black. The air is cold. Inside a stone building, a group of monks opens their mouths and begins to sing. Their voices blend together and bounce off the tall stone walls. The sound fills every corner of the room. This is how every single day begins.

These monks lived in monasteries — buildings where religious men lived, worked, and prayed together, like a school and a church combined into one home. They lived during medieval times, the period in European history roughly between the years 500 and 1500. And they sang constantly. They sang before sunrise. They sang at noon. They sang at sunset and again before bed. The music they sang has a name: Gregorian chant.

items.prompt: The text says a monastery is like a school and a church. According to the text, what makes a monastery different from a regular school or church?

choices.text: It is a home where the men live together.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage uses the word 'home' and says the men 'lived, worked, and prayed together' there, which makes it different from a school or church you just visit.

choices.text: It is a place where the men only sing at noon.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the passage tells us the monks sang before sunrise, at noon, at sunset, and before bed — not just at noon.

choices.text: It is a room where the men stay awake all night.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the passage never mentions the men staying awake all night; that detail is not in the text.

choices.text: It is a building where the men live completely alone.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the passage says the monks lived together as a group, not that each man lived completely alone.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
What Gregorian Chant Sounded Like

Gregorian chant is named after Pope Gregory I, who led the Catholic Church around AD 600. He is said to have collected and organized the chants used in church services. The music itself grew and changed over hundreds of years. People also call it plainchant or plainsong.

This music sounded nothing like the songs on your playlist. The monks sang in Latin, the official language of the Catholic Church. There were no instruments at all. No drums. No guitars. No keyboards. Just human voices. And every monk sang the same melody at the same time. You might think that would sound flat and boring — like everyone talking in one dull tone. Actually, the melodies moved up and down in graceful, flowing lines. When dozens of voices sang together inside a stone room with tall, arched ceilings, the sound grew rich and full. The stone walls acted almost like a giant speaker, making the voices ring and echo.

The chants had no strong beat like a pop song does. Instead, the rhythm followed the natural rise and fall of the Latin words, the way ocean waves roll in and pull back. The effect was calm, peaceful, and deeply spiritual — like a prayer you could hear floating through the air.
stimulus.content_text:
