This music sounded nothing like the songs on your playlist. The monks sang in Latin, the official language of the Catholic Church. There were no instruments at all. No drums. No guitars. No keyboards. Just human voices. And every monk sang the same melody at the same time. You might think that would sound flat and boring — like everyone talking in one dull tone. Actually, the melodies moved up and down in graceful, flowing lines. When dozens of voices sang together inside a stone room with tall, arched ceilings, the sound grew rich and full. The stone walls acted almost like a giant speaker, making the voices ring and echo.

The chants had no strong beat like a pop song does. Instead, the rhythm followed the natural rise and fall of the Latin words, the way ocean waves roll in and pull back. The effect was calm, peaceful, and deeply spiritual — like a prayer you could hear floating through the air.

items.prompt: The main idea of this section is that Gregorian chants had a special sound. How does the detail about the stone walls acting "like a giant speaker" support this main idea?

choices.text: It explains what caused the monks to play loud instruments.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage clearly says the monks used no instruments — no drums, no guitars, nothing!

choices.text: It explains what caused the monks' voices to ring and echo.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly says the stone walls acted like a giant speaker, making the voices ring and echo.

choices.text: It explains what caused the rhythm to roll like ocean waves.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the ocean waves comparison is about the rhythm of the Latin words, not about the stone walls.

choices.text: It explains what caused the singers to talk in one dull tone.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage says the chanting sounded rich and full, not dull — the stone walls actually made it sound better!

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
A Day Filled with Song

Monks sang during religious services spread across the whole day. These services were called the Divine Office, a schedule of eight prayer times that stretched from before dawn to late at night. Singing was not just a musical activity. It was a form of devotion — deep love and dedication to God. The monks believed their voices were praising God directly.
stimulus.content_text:
A Day Filled with Song
Monks sang during religious services spread across the whole day. These services were called the Divine Office, a schedule of eight prayer times that stretched from before dawn to late at night. Singing was not just a musical activity. It was a form of devotion — deep love and dedication to God. The monks believed their voices were praising God directly.

items.prompt: According to the text, when did the last of the monks' eight prayer times happen?

choices.text: Before dawn
choices.feedback: Before dawn is when the prayer times started, not when they ended — it was the beginning of the schedule, not the last prayer time.

choices.text: In the morning
choices.feedback: The passage never says the last prayer time was in the morning — morning is in the middle of the day, not the end.

choices.text: During the day
choices.feedback: During the day is too vague and not what the passage says — the text gives us a specific ending time of late at night.

choices.text: Late at night
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says the schedule 'stretched from before dawn to late at night,' which tells us the last prayer time happened late at night.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Writing Music Down for the First Time

There were no staff lines or notes on a page. Monks memorized every chant and taught each one by singing it over and over to younger monks. But as the number of chants grew, no one could remember them all.

So monks invented an early form of musical notation — a system of written marks and symbols, like a secret code placed above the words. These marks showed whether the melody should go up or down. Over time, this system grew into the five-line staff and the round notes you see in music class today. Before these monks solved this problem, music lived only in people's memories. One forgotten melody was gone forever.
stimulus.content_text:
Writing Music Down for the First Time
There were no staff lines or notes on a page. Monks memorized every chant and taught each one by singing it over and over to younger monks. But as the number of chants grew, no one could remember them all.

So monks invented an early form of musical notation — a system of written marks and symbols, like a secret code placed above the words. These marks showed whether the melody should go up or down. Over time, this system grew into the five-line staff and the round notes you see in music class today. Before these monks solved this problem, music lived only in people's memories. One forgotten melody was gone forever.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, how is early musical notation similar to a secret code?

choices.text: They both are groups of written marks and symbols.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage directly says early musical notation was 'a system of written marks and symbols, like a secret code,' meaning both share written marks and symbols.

choices.text: They both are made of a five-line staff and notes.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because the five-line staff and round notes are the modern version of music writing that came later, not what early notation and secret codes share.

choices.text: They both are old chants sung over and over again.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because singing chants over and over was how monks memorized music before notation was invented, not a similarity between notation and secret codes.

choices.text: They both are memories that are forgotten forever.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because forgotten memories describe the problem monks faced without notation — notation was actually created to prevent melodies from being lost forever.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Still Singing After a Thousand Years

Even now, people around the world listen to recordings of Gregorian chant. They find its calm, flowing sound relaxing and beautiful. Those medieval monks gave us more than songs. They gave us a way to capture sound on paper — the same basic system every musician on Earth still uses when they read a piece of sheet music.
stimulus.content_text:
Still Singing After a Thousand Years
Even now, people around the world listen to recordings of Gregorian chant. They find its calm, flowing sound relaxing and beautiful. Those medieval monks gave us more than songs. They gave us a way to capture sound on paper — the same basic system every musician on Earth still uses when they read a piece of sheet music.

items.prompt: According to the text, what is 'sheet music'?

choices.text: a recording of Gregorian chant songs
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because a recording is sound saved on something like a CD or phone, but sheet music is written on paper, not recorded.

choices.text: a way to capture sound on paper
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage says the monks gave us 'a way to capture sound on paper,' and links that directly to what musicians use when they read sheet music.

choices.text: a calm and flowing singing style
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because 'calm and flowing' describes how Gregorian chant sounds when you listen to it, not what sheet music is.

choices.text: a type of song from medieval times
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because sheet music is a system for writing down music that is still used today by all musicians, not just a song from medieval times.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: Based on the article, how is the way people use Gregorian chants today different from how the medieval monks used them?

choices.text: People today listen to chants to relax, but monks sang them to pray to God.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the article says people today listen to chants to relax, while monks sang them as devotion — a way of praying and praising God.

choices.text: People today sing chants to praise God, but monks listened to them to relax.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because it has it backwards — it's people today who listen to relax, and the monks who sang to praise God, not the other way around.

choices.text: People today sing chants with a beat, but monks sang them with instruments.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the article says monks never used instruments, and it never says people today sing chants with a beat.

choices.text: People today read chants from a code, but monks read them from sheet music.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the monks invented the early written code for music, and today's musicians use sheet music — not the other way around.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: If you need to find the reasons why the monks sang and why they invented musical notation, which two headings should you look under?

choices.text: "What Gregorian Chant Sounded Like" and "Still Singing After a Thousand Years"
choices.feedback: These two headings talk about what the chant sounded like and how people listen to it today — not why monks sang or why they invented notation.

choices.text: "Voices Before Dawn" and "What Gregorian Chant Sounded Like"
choices.feedback: These headings introduce the monks and describe the sound of the music, but they don't explain the reasons behind the singing or the invention of written music.

choices.text: "A Day Filled with Song" and "Writing Music Down for the First Time"
choices.feedback: 'A Day Filled with Song' explains why monks sang (devotion to God), and 'Writing Music Down for the First Time' explains why they invented musical notation (too many chants to memorize), so this is the correct answer.

choices.text: "What Gregorian Chant Sounded Like" and "Writing Music Down for the First Time"
choices.feedback: 'What Gregorian Chant Sounded Like' describes how the music sounded, not why monks sang, so it doesn't answer the first part of the question.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: Which sequence of key details best supports the main idea of how monks changed music history?

choices.text: Monks sang chants from memory, the number of chants grew, and monks invented written marks.
choices.feedback: A is correct because it follows the exact order the passage describes — memorizing chants, running out of memory space, and then inventing written marks to solve the problem.

choices.text: Monks invented written marks, the number of chants grew, and monks sang chants from memory.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because it puts the events in the wrong order — monks invented written marks because of the memory problem, not before it.

choices.text: Monks sang chants before dawn, monks sang chants at noon, and monks sang at sunset.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because it only describes the monks' daily singing schedule, not how they changed music history.

choices.text: Monks read modern sheet music, monks learned the chants, and monks taught the younger monks.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because medieval monks did not read modern sheet music — they actually helped invent the early system that eventually became modern sheet music.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: Based on the article, how is the monks' early musical notation similar to the sheet music musicians use today?

choices.text: Both are systems used to capture musical sounds on paper.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the article directly connects the monks' early notation system to the sheet music musicians still use today — both are ways to capture musical sounds on paper.

choices.text: Both are secret codes made entirely of Latin church words.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because today's sheet music is made of notes, lines, and symbols — not Latin church words.

choices.text: Both are created only for voices singing without instruments.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because sheet music today is used for guitars, pianos, drums, and many other instruments, not just singing voices.

choices.text: Both are memorized by listening to older singers perform.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because writing music down was invented specifically so monks would NOT have to memorize everything by listening — that was the old way before notation existed.

syllabus_metadata.title: Chants and Hymns: Music in Medieval Monasteries

assessments.title: Songs of Knights and Ladies: Troubadours and Medieval Entertainment

items.title: Section 1 - Guiding Question 0

stimulus.title: Section 1
stimulus.content_html:
Singers, Storytellers, and Strings: The Fun Music of Medieval Europe

A Musician at the Castle Gate

A dusty traveler stands at the gate of a stone castle. He carries a wooden instrument under one arm. "I'll sing for my supper!" he calls to the guard. That night, the lord's family gathers close. The traveler plucks his strings and sings a wild tale about a knight fighting a dragon. Children and grown-ups alike lean in, tapping their feet. This was a regular evening in medieval Europe — the period from about the year 500 to 1500.

You might think all music back then was slow and serious. Monks did sing quiet prayers in monasteries — buildings where monks lived and worshipped together, like a combination of a church and a home. But outside those walls, a completely different kind of music filled the air. It was loud, funny, exciting, and full of stories.
stimulus.content_text:
Singers, Storytellers, and Strings: The Fun Music of Medieval EuropeA Musician at the Castle Gate
A dusty traveler stands at the gate of a stone castle. He carries a wooden instrument under one arm. "I'll sing for my supper!" he calls to the guard. That night, the lord's family gathers close. The traveler plucks his strings and sings a wild tale about a knight fighting a dragon. Children and grown-ups alike lean in, tapping their feet. This was a regular evening in medieval Europe — the period from about the year 500 to 1500.
You might think all music back then was slow and serious. Monks did sing quiet prayers in monasteries — buildings where monks lived and worshipped together, like a combination of a church and a home. But outside those walls, a completely different kind of music filled the air. It was loud, funny, exciting, and full of stories.

items.prompt: Based on the article 'Songs of Knights and Ladies,' how was the music outside the monasteries different from the music inside?

choices.text: It was slow, serious, and full of quiet prayers.
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because 'slow, serious, and full of quiet prayers' describes the music INSIDE the monasteries, not outside.

choices.text: It was loud, funny, and full of stories.
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage uses almost those exact words — 'loud, funny, exciting, and full of stories' — to describe music outside the monasteries.

choices.text: It was short, simple, and played without any words.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage never says outside music was short, simple, or without words — in fact, the traveler sang a story with words!

choices.text: It was wild, scary, and about a fighting dragon.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the dragon story was just one example of a song the traveler sang, not a description of all music outside monasteries.

items.title: Section 2 - Guiding Question 1

stimulus.title: Section 2
stimulus.content_html:
Troubadours: The Songwriter-Poets

Troubadours were poet-musicians who wrote and performed their own songs. Think of them like today's singer-songwriters, but without microphones or recording studios. Most troubadours lived in southern France during the 1100s and 1200s. Many came from noble or well-educated families.

Here's something that made them special. Instead of singing in Latin, the language of the Church, troubadours sang in the everyday language that people around them actually spoke. Their favorite subject? Love. They sang about the beauty of noble ladies, the sadness of lovers far apart, and the devotion — the deep, loyal care — that knights felt for the women they admired. They also wrote about nature, the changing seasons, and chivalry, which was the code of honor that knights promised to follow, like a set of rules about bravery and kindness.
stimulus.content_text:
Troubadours: The Songwriter-Poets
Troubadours were poet-musicians who wrote and performed their own songs. Think of them like today's singer-songwriters, but without microphones or recording studios. Most troubadours lived in southern France during the 1100s and 1200s. Many came from noble or well-educated families.
Here's something that made them special. Instead of singing in Latin, the language of the Church, troubadours sang in the everyday language that people around them actually spoke. Their favorite subject? Love. They sang about the beauty of noble ladies, the sadness of lovers far apart, and the devotion — the deep, loyal care — that knights felt for the women they admired. They also wrote about nature, the changing seasons, and chivalry, which was the code of honor that knights promised to follow, like a set of rules about bravery and kindness.

items.prompt: Based on the passage, why could more people understand troubadours' songs?

choices.text: because they sang about love and nature
choices.feedback: Singing about love and nature made their songs enjoyable, but the topic of a song doesn't help people understand the language it's sung in.

choices.text: because they sang in everyday language
choices.feedback: This is correct! The passage says troubadours sang in the everyday language people actually spoke, which means everyone around them could understand the words.

choices.text: because they came from noble families
choices.feedback: Coming from noble families tells us about their backgrounds, but it has nothing to do with why people could understand their songs.

choices.text: because they followed the rules of chivalry
choices.feedback: Chivalry was a code of honor for knights about bravery and kindness — it wasn't about the language troubadours used in their songs.

items.title: Section 3 - Guiding Question 2

stimulus.title: Section 3
stimulus.content_html:
Minstrels: Musicians on the Move

It's easy to mix up troubadours and minstrels, but they were different. Troubadours wrote their own music. Minstrels were traveling performers who played at castles, outdoor fairs, busy marketplaces, and taverns — places where adults gathered to eat and drink, like a restaurant and meeting hall combined. A minstrel might perform for a king one week and for farmers the next. Together, troubadours and minstrels kept the spirit of creativity alive during the Middle Ages.
stimulus.content_text:
Minstrels: Musicians on the Move
It's easy to mix up troubadours and minstrels, but they were different. Troubadours wrote their own music. Minstrels were traveling performers who played at castles, outdoor fairs, busy marketplaces, and taverns — places where adults gathered to eat and drink, like a restaurant and meeting hall combined. A minstrel might perform for a king one week and for farmers the next. Together, troubadours and minstrels kept the spirit of creativity alive during the Middle Ages.

items.prompt: According to the text, what might a minstrel do the week after performing for a king?

choices.text: They might perform for farmers.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the passage says directly that 'A minstrel might perform for a king one week and for farmers the next.'

choices.text: They might perform for troubadours.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because troubadours were fellow musicians, not an audience that minstrels performed for.

choices.text: They might write their own music.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because writing their own music was what troubadours did, not minstrels.

choices.text: They might eat at a restaurant.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because even though the passage mentions taverns, it never says a minstrel would go eat at a restaurant.

items.title: Section 4 - Guiding Question 3

stimulus.title: Section 4
stimulus.content_html:
Instruments That Sounded Strange and Wonderful

Medieval instruments did not sound like the ones you hear today. There were no pianos, no electric guitars, no trumpets with valves. The lute looked a bit like a guitar, but with a fat, rounded back. It made a softer, buzzier sound. The recorder was a simple wooden flute with a clear, sweet voice. The harp, one of the oldest instruments in the world, rang out bright notes at royal courts. Drums of many sizes kept the beat for dances.
stimulus.content_text:
Instruments That Sounded Strange and Wonderful
Medieval instruments did not sound like the ones you hear today. There were no pianos, no electric guitars, no trumpets with valves. The lute looked a bit like a guitar, but with a fat, rounded back. It made a softer, buzzier sound. The recorder was a simple wooden flute with a clear, sweet voice. The harp, one of the oldest instruments in the world, rang out bright notes at royal courts. Drums of many sizes kept the beat for dances.

items.prompt: In the sentence "The lute looked a bit like a guitar, but with a fat, rounded back," what does the word "rounded" tell the reader about the lute compared with a guitar?

choices.text: Its back was more curved.
choices.feedback: A is correct because 'rounded' describes a curved shape, and the passage says the lute had 'a fat, rounded back,' meaning its back curved outward instead of being flat like a guitar's.

choices.text: Its sound was more buzzy.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because 'rounded' describes the shape of the lute's back, not its sound — the buzzy sound is mentioned separately in the passage.

choices.text: Its wood was more polished.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the passage never says anything about the wood being polished or shiny.

choices.text: Its strings were more numerous.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because 'rounded' is a shape word and has nothing to do with how many strings the lute had.

items.title: Section 5 - Guiding Question 4

stimulus.title: Section 5
stimulus.content_html:
Songs for Fun, Not Just for Prayer

This entertaining, non-church music had a name: secular music. Secular means "not connected to religion." Secular songs told stories of brave knights, clever heroes outsmarting villains, and young lovers meeting in moonlit gardens. The whole point was to make people smile, gasp, or laugh.

Secular music also got people moving. At feasts and festivals, musicians played fast, lively tunes. Guests joined hands and danced in long lines and spinning circles. Music brought people together, turning an ordinary evening into a celebration.
stimulus.content_text:
Songs for Fun, Not Just for Prayer
This entertaining, non-church music had a name: secular music. Secular means "not connected to religion." Secular songs told stories of brave knights, clever heroes outsmarting villains, and young lovers meeting in moonlit gardens. The whole point was to make people smile, gasp, or laugh.
Secular music also got people moving. At feasts and festivals, musicians played fast, lively tunes. Guests joined hands and danced in long lines and spinning circles. Music brought people together, turning an ordinary evening into a celebration.

items.prompt: According to the passage 'Songs for Fun, Not Just for Prayer,' what does the word secular mean?

choices.text: played only at big feasts
choices.feedback: A is incorrect because the passage never says secular music is only played at feasts — it can be played anywhere, not just at big celebrations.

choices.text: not connected to religion
choices.feedback: B is correct because the passage directly states 'Secular means not connected to religion,' giving us the exact definition.

choices.text: sung by brave knights
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because brave knights are mentioned as characters in secular songs, but that is not what the word secular means.

choices.text: played on old instruments
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the passage never mentions old instruments when explaining the meaning of the word secular.

items.title: Section 6 - Guiding Question 5

stimulus.title: Section 6
stimulus.content_html:
Old Songs, Familiar Feelings

Those troubadours and minstrels lived over 800 years ago. The melodies sound strange to modern ears. But listen to the words, and you'll recognize everything — love, adventure, humor, heartbreak. The next time you hear your favorite song on the radio, remember: you and a medieval troubadour want the exact same thing from music.
stimulus.content_text:
Old Songs, Familiar Feelings
Those troubadours and minstrels lived over 800 years ago. The melodies sound strange to modern ears. But listen to the words, and you'll recognize everything — love, adventure, humor, heartbreak. The next time you hear your favorite song on the radio, remember: you and a medieval troubadour want the exact same thing from music.

items.prompt: Based on the section "Old Songs, Familiar Feelings," which detail shows that people today can connect with medieval music?

choices.text: The words share familiar feelings.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage says the words contain feelings like love, adventure, and heartbreak — feelings we still recognize and share today.

choices.text: The melodies sound very strange.
choices.feedback: B is incorrect because strange-sounding melodies actually make it harder to connect with the music, not easier.

choices.text: The singers lived long ago.
choices.feedback: C is incorrect because the fact that singers lived long ago is just a time detail — it doesn't show a connection between us and them.

choices.text: The music plays on the radio.
choices.feedback: D is incorrect because the radio is only mentioned to compare modern music to medieval music, not to show a connection with medieval songs.

items.title: Quiz Question 1
items.prompt: Based on the article, how were the monks different from the traveling performers called minstrels?

choices.text: Monks stayed in one building, but minstrels traveled to many places.
choices.feedback: This is correct! The article says monks lived in monasteries and sang quiet prayers there, while minstrels were traveling performers who went to many different places.

choices.text: Monks played very loud music, but minstrels played quiet, slow songs.
choices.feedback: This is backwards — the article says monks sang quiet prayers, while the music outside the monastery was described as 'loud, funny, and exciting.'

choices.text: Monks sang in everyday words, but minstrels sang only in Latin.
choices.feedback: This is also backwards — the article says troubadours (not monks) sang in everyday language, while the Church used Latin, which is where monks would have sung.

choices.text: Monks wrote their own music, but minstrels played on modern instruments.
choices.feedback: This is not right either — the article says troubadours (not monks) wrote their own music, and medieval instruments like the lute and recorder were definitely not modern instruments.

items.title: Quiz Question 2
items.prompt: If you want to find out what caused medieval people to tap their feet and dance, which two headings should you look under?

choices.text: "A Musician at the Castle Gate" and "Songs for Fun, Not Just for Prayer"
choices.feedback: This is correct because 'A Musician at the Castle Gate' shows people tapping their feet, and 'Songs for Fun, Not Just for Prayer' explains how fast music got people up and dancing.

choices.text: "Troubadours: The Songwriter-Poets" and "Minstrels: Musicians on the Move"
choices.feedback: These sections tell us about the difference between troubadours and minstrels, but they don't really explain what made people tap their feet and dance.

choices.text: "Minstrels: Musicians on the Move" and "Instruments That Sounded Strange and Wonderful"
choices.feedback: The minstrels section talks about where minstrels performed, and the instruments section describes what the instruments sounded like — neither one focuses on dancing.

choices.text: "Troubadours: The Songwriter-Poets" and "Old Songs, Familiar Feelings"
choices.feedback: The troubadours section is mostly about love songs and poetry, and 'Old Songs, Familiar Feelings' is about connecting old music to today — neither one is mainly about dancing.

items.title: Quiz Question 3
items.prompt: Based on the article, what is the correct order of events when a traveling musician visits a castle?

choices.text: First the musician arrives at the gate, next the musician plays a tune, and last the guests dance.
choices.feedback: A is correct because the passage clearly shows the traveler arrives at the gate first, then plays and sings, and then the family taps their feet to the music — in that exact order.

choices.text: First the musician plays a tune, next the guests dance, and last the musician arrives at the gate.
choices.feedback: B is wrong because the musician hasn't even arrived at the castle yet, so he couldn't possibly play music before getting there.

choices.text: First the musician arrives at the gate, next the monks pray, and last the guests dance.
choices.feedback: C is wrong because the monks praying in a monastery is a separate idea from the castle visit and doesn't happen as part of that story.

choices.text: First the guests dance, next the musician arrives at the gate, and last the monks pray.
choices.feedback: D is wrong because the guests need the musician to arrive and play first before there is any music to dance to.

items.title: Quiz Question 4
items.prompt: Based on the article, how are troubadours and today's singer-songwriters alike?

choices.text: They both perform in monasteries for monks.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because troubadours performed at castles and for regular people, not in monasteries for monks — that was a completely different kind of music.

choices.text: They both write and perform their own songs.
choices.feedback: This is correct because the article directly compares troubadours to 'today's singer-songwriters' and says they both write and perform their own songs.

choices.text: They both use lutes and recorders on stage.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because today's singer-songwriters use modern instruments, not the medieval lutes and recorders described in the article.

choices.text: They both sing only about knights and dragons.
choices.feedback: This is incorrect because troubadours sang about many topics like love, nature, and chivalry — knights and dragons were just one small part of medieval storytelling.

syllabus_metadata.title: Songs of Knights and Ladies: Troubadours and Medieval Entertainment
